[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 1.02_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-401B-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 1- Introductory Matters_ > _Topic 1- Definitions and Terminology_**

**§ 1.02 Parties' Labeling and Popular Usage Not Controlling**

**An agency relationship arises only when the elements stated in § 1.01 are present. Whether a relationship is characterized as agency in an agreement between parties or in the context of industry or popular usage is not controlling.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. In general._ Whether a relationship is one of agency is a legal conclusion made after an assessment of the facts of the relationship and the application of the law of agency to those facts. Although agency is a consensual relationship, how the parties to any given relationship label it is not dispositive. Nor does party characterization or nonlegal usage control whether an agent has an agency relationship with a particular person as principal. The parties' references to functional characteristics may, however, be relevant to determining whether a relationship of agency exists.

Many common legal relationships do not by themselves create relationships of agency as defined in § 1.01. These include relationships between suppliers and resellers of goods or property, franchisors and franchisees, lenders and borrowers, and parent corporations and their subsidiaries. For further discussion, see § 1.01, Comments _f(2)_ and _g._ Relationships like these are also relationships of agency when the elements stated in § 1.01 are present.

_b. Judicial acceptance or rejection of parties' characterization._ An agreement between or among parties may positively characterize their relationship as one of agency or assert a negation of agency. Statements of each kind raise somewhat different issues. If a particular relationship is one of agency, a consequence may be that an act otherwise illegal is legal. This is so when relevant legislation defines illegality in terms of the existence of an agreement, the transmission of information, or some other interaction between two economically distinct parties. It is essential to the common-law definition of agency that the party designated as principal has the right to control the party designated as agent and that the party designated as agent act on behalf of the party designated as principal. See § 1.01, Comments _f_ and _g._ These factors may also be important in statutory contexts, as may be the substance of the relationship, including the allocation of business risks and other indicia of entrepreneurship. It is appropriate for the court to consider whether the parties' characterization serves a function other than circumventing an otherwise-applicable statute, regulation, or rule of law, or invoking a statute, regulation, or rule of law to limit or prevent liability. On the relationships between statutes and common-law doctrines generally, see Introduction.

**Illustration:**

1\. P Corporation, a credit bureau, collects information about users of credit. A statute permits disclosure of such information within a bureau but limits the circumstances under which credit bureaus may provide information about an individual's credit history to a third party without the individual's consent. Responding to journalistic inquiries is not among the circumstances permitted by the statute. P Corporation enters into an agreement with Q Corporation, a publisher of newspapers, designating Q Corporation the agent of P Corporation for the purpose of collecting and analyzing information. A court is not bound by the parties' characterization of the relationship in determining whether P Corporation's transmission of individually identified credit histories to Q Corporation violates the statute.

The parties' agreement may negatively characterize the relationship as not one of agency, or as one not intended by the parties to create a relationship of agency or employment. Although such statements are relevant to determining whether the parties consent to a relationship of agency, their presence in an agreement is not determinative and does not preclude the relevance of other indicia of consent.

_c. Ambiguous and other nonlegal designations of agency relationships._ Whether an actor has a relationship of agency with a particular principal, with one possible principal as opposed to another, with multiple principals, or is a coagent or a subagent, or is not an agent at all, is resolvable only by applying the legal definition of agency to the facts of the relationship. The fact that it is in the interest of an agent who sells the principal's product or service to accommodate a customer and pay attention to the customer's needs does not in itself make a seller's agent the customer's agent as well. How the parties characterized the relationship is not dispositive, nor is popular usage. Such questions commonly arise with regard to service providers in the insurance, real-estate, and travel industries, in which agency terminology is often used without regard for its legal significance. Whether these providers are acting as agents and, if so, who are their principals depends on the context. For definitions of terms, see § 1.04. Section 3.15 deals more specifically with subagency.

_d. Burden of establishing existence of relationship of agency._ The party asserting that a relationship of agency exists generally has the burden in litigation of establishing its existence. The parties' agreement does not control whether a person is an employee for purposes of the respondeat superior doctrine stated in § 7.07. Nor does it do so for purposes relevant to statutes or administrative regulations, or to avoid vicarious liability, when the definition of employee in § 7.07(3) is an element in determining the person's status.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ This section constitutes a further elaboration on the basic point made in Restatement Second, Agency § 1, Comment _b:_ "Agency is a legal concept which depends upon the existence of required factual elements. . . ." Comment _a_ gives examples of common legal relationships that do not themselves create relationships of agency. Comparable points are made by Restatement Second, Agency §§ 14 J and 14 M. Section 14 O states that a creditor who assumes control of a debtor's business for their mutual benefit may become liable as principal for the debtor's acts and transactions in connection with the business. In this Restatement, see § 1.01, Comment _f(1)_ for discussion of debtor-creditor relationships and the common-law definition of agency.

_b. Judicial acceptance or rejection of parties' characterization._ For the general proposition that the parties' characterization is not conclusive, see [_Anderson v. Badger, 191 P.2d 768, 771 (Cal.App.1948)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S1W-KP90-003V-P3YM-00000-00&context=1530671) (contractual designation of person as "agent" will be disregarded if contract as a whole is one in which a person is engaged to produce a result by means solely of his own choosing or to sell goods at a stated price); [_Policy Mgmt. Sys. Corp. v. Indiana Dept. of State Revenue, 720 N.E.2d 20, 25 (Ind.Tax 1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XVY-8G90-0039-43MG-00000-00&context=1530671) (presence or absence of specific contract language does not control whether services received by taxpayer from its customers are taxable gross revenues or reimbursement in which taxpayer lacked any beneficial interest); [_Amerifirst Sav. Bank of Xenia v. Krug, 737 N.E.2d 68 (Ohio App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3Y2W-JSF0-0039-445P-00000-00&context=1530671) (record in case stemming from salesperson's promises and representations to retail customer presented genuine issue of material fact whether automobile dealer acted as lender's agent; use of term "agency" in agreement between dealer and lender not determinative, and no evidence submitted that relationship satisfied elements requisite to relationship of agency).

The test of whether the parties' characterization serves a function apart from circumventing an otherwise applicable legal requirement is articulated and applied in [_Morrison v. Murray Biscuit Co., 797 F.2d 1430 (7th Cir.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-2V20-0039-P3MW-00000-00&context=1530671) (agency relationship between cookie manufacturer and dealer had function apart from frustrating antitrust constraints on resale-price maintenance). On the relationship between agency and legal consequences under antitrust laws, compare [_United States v. General Elec. Co., 272 U.S. 476, 483-486 (1926)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-GDM0-003B-74W6-00000-00&context=1530671) (manufacturer's control over price at which del credere agent sold patented product to customer did not constitute illegal resale-price maintenance) with [_United States v. Masonite Corp., 316 U.S. 265, 276-277 (1942)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-5D20-003B-74B8-00000-00&context=1530671) (del credere structure does not prevent manufacturer's selling arrangements from running afoul of Sherman Act, nor does its patent on product) and [_Simpson v. Union Oil Co., 377 U.S. 13, 20, 23-24 (1964)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-GW00-003B-S4RH-00000-00&context=1530671) (arrangements like those in _General Electric_ constitute per se violations of antitrust law; court distinguishes _General Electric_ on ground that patents were involved; agents in _Simpson,_ who took gasoline on consignment, were "independent businessmen" having "all or most of the indicia of entrepreneurs, except price fixing") and [_United States v. General Elec. Co., 358 F. Supp. 731 (S.D.N.Y. 1973)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-JPN0-003B-3496-00000-00&context=1530671) (exact arrangement upheld by Supreme Court in 1926 constitutes per se violation of § 1 of Sherman Act).

Characterizations of agency may meet a warmer and less skeptical judicial welcome in the tax context than in a context in which a statute serves a specifically regulatory function. Recent tax authority focuses heavily on whether the formal aspects of the parties' characterization are consistent with agency. See [_Commissioner v. Bollinger, 485 U.S. 340, 349-350 (1988)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-FDG0-003B-452G-00000-00&context=1530671) ("genuineness of the agency relationship is adequately assured, and tax-avoiding manipulation adequately avoided" when fact that corporation is acting as its shareholders' agent with regard to asset is set forth in written agreement at time asset is acquired, corporation functions as agent with respect to asset for all purposes and is held out to third parties as agent, not as principal). But see [_National Carbide Corp. v. Commissioner, 336 U.S. 422 (1949)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-JR30-003B-S2DN-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporation is not true agent of principal if its relations with principal depend on fact that it is owned by principal; corporation's business purpose must be to carry on "normal duties" of an agent).

Provisions in agreements that purport to negate a relationship of agency are held not to be determinative in the context of claims asserted by third parties in [_Pistone v. Superior Court, 279 Cal.Rptr. 173, 177 (Cal. App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-HDG0-003D-J14Y-00000-00&context=1530671) (in action brought by customer, administrator of vehicle service contract could be found to be car dealer's agent, despite provision disaffirming agency in agreement between dealer and administrator); [_N & G Constr., Inc. v. Lindley, 384 N.E.2d 704, 706 (Ohio 1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-T3W0-0054-C1MF-00000-00&context=1530671) (provision in agreement between coal owner and coal shipper stating that shipper was an "independent contractor" not determinative of agency status in tax dispute based on assessment of coal severance tax against coal owner).

The restrictions on transmission of information presupposed by Illustration 1 stem from the Fair Credit Reporting Act, [_15 U.S.C. § 1681 et seq._](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8S9D-W4W2-8T6X-71PW-00000-00&context=1530671) See [_Trans Union Corp. v. FTC, 81 F.3d 228, 234 (D.C.Cir.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-2HM0-006F-M499-00000-00&context=1530671) (relevant to credit bureau's liability whether consumer reports disseminated consistently with purposes of statute).

As between the parties to an agreement, an assertion or negation of agency is not determinative. See, e.g., [_MJ & Partners Rest. Ltd. P'ship v. Zadikoff, 10 F.Supp.2d 922, 932 (N.D.Ill.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SRS-1PK0-0038-Y4J6-00000-00&context=1530671) (despite provisions in agreements among licensee of trademark, limited partnership designated to manage restaurant, and consulting firm with which partnership entered into contract for consulting and administrative services, individual who had significant responsibilities in running restaurant could be shown to be agent of trademark licensee, notwithstanding provision in consulting agreement characterizing firm as an independent consultant; "the existence of an agency relationship is determined on the actual practices of the parties, and not merely by reference to a written agreement"); [_Prudential Ins. Co. v. Eslick, 586 F. Supp. 763, 764 (S.D.Ohio 1984)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-DRM0-0054-52JX-00000-00&context=1530671) (action by insurer against former salesman alleging breach of fiduciary duty; although contract between insurance company and former salesman characterized salesman as an "independent contractor," nature of parties' relationship must be determined by comprehensive factual analysis; court denies insurer's motion for summary judgment on point that former salesman was its agent); cf. [_GAVCO, Inc. v. Chem-Trend, Inc., 81 F.Supp.2d 633, 644 (W.D.N.C.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YVD-MRG0-0038-Y28S-00000-00&context=1530671) (finding triable issue of fact as to whether parties intended relationship to remain one of exclusive dealing despite entering into agreement that contained a confidentiality provision but no covenant not to compete; parties were attempting to avoid characterization of relationship as employment for tax purposes); [_Old Republic Ins. Co. v. Hansa World Cargo Serv., Inc., 51 F.Supp.2d 457, 473 (S.D.N.Y.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WNV-V910-0038-Y0RD-00000-00&context=1530671) (contract language intended to protect undisclosed principal from liability for customs duties does not mean customs agent lacked actual authority to post bonds; customs agent may be able to establish claim for indemnity against undisclosed principal); [_Zajac v. Harris, 410 S.W.2d 593, 594 (Ark. 1967)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-YPJ0-003C-13B8-00000-00&context=1530671) (agreement characterizing plaintiff as employee of business owned by defendant not determinative of whether partnership existed; inferences that might ordinarily be drawn from facts that federal withholding and Social Security taxes were paid on plaintiffs share of firm's profits and that firm carried workers'-compensation insurance for plaintiff's protection effectively rebutted by undisputed fact that plaintiff was unable to read; "\[t\]here is no reason to believe that he appreciated the significance of the accounting practices now relied upon by \[defendant\]. They were unilateral"). See also South Sydney Dist. Rugby League Football League Ltd. v. News Ltd., \[2000\] 177 A.L.R. 611 (Austl.Fed.Ct.), rev'd on other grounds, \[2001\] 181 A.L.R. 188 (Austl.Fed.Ct.), rev'd by \[2003\] 215 C.L.R. 563 (Austl.) (although provision in merger agreement that created partnership between media company and football league stated that company created to conduct competition was not agent of partnership, company in fact conducted partnership's business as its agent in operating competition).

_c. Ambiguous and other nonlegal designations of agency relationships._ For the proposition that a sales agent's attentiveness to a customer's needs does not create an agency relationship with the customer, see [_Weisblatt v. Minnesota Mut. Life Ins. Co., 4 F.Supp.2d 371, 382 (E.D.Pa.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SD4-KFV0-0038-Y0N7-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurer's agent did not owe special duty to act in customer's exclusive benefit; agent's evident attentiveness to customer's needs stemmed from "a duty to his employer--and to his own self-interest--to sell its products as successfully as possible."). On travel agents, see _Afflerbach v. Cunard Line, Ltd., 14 F.Supp.2d 1260 (D.Wyo.1998)_; cf. [_Manes v. Coats, 941 P.2d 120, 124 (Alaska 1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SC9-JT80-003C-G2SP-00000-00&context=1530671) (accommodation referral service was not an agent of traveler who used service). On insurance brokers as insureds' agents, see [_Evvtex Co., Inc. v. Hartley Cooper Assocs. Ltd., 102 F.3d 1327, 1331-1332 (2d Cir.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-XWB0-006F-M4K9-00000-00&context=1530671).

_d. Burden of establishing existence of relationship of agency._ For the point that one asserting the existence of a relationship of agency has the burden of establishing it, see, e.g., [_Romak USA, Inc. v. Rich, 384 F.3d 979, 985 (8th Cir.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4DD3-DT40-0038-X24D-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Bridas S.A.P.I.C. v. Government of Turkmenistan, 345 F.3d 347, 356 (5th Cir. 2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:49H0-PSX0-0038-X1K9-00000-00&context=1530671); [_E.I. DuPont de Nemours & Co. v. Rhone Poulenc Fiber & Resin Intermediates, S.A.S., 269 F.3d 187, 198 (3d Cir.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4477-S3R0-0038-X44F-00000-00&context=1530671); [_United States v. Greer, 383 F.Supp.2d 861, 864-865 (W.D.N.C.2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4GST-WRD0-TVVT-Y227-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Steigerwald v. Bradley, 136 F.Supp.2d 460, 470 (D.Md. 2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4378-S4G0-0038-Y1W3-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Heise v. Rosow, 771 A.2d 190, 193 (Conn.App.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:42JY-8B40-0039-43BF-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Aladdin Constr. Co. v. John Hancock Life Ins. Co., 914 So. 2d 169, 177 (Miss.2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4HF9-YPM0-0039-416V-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Springfield Hydroelec. Co. v. Copp, 779 A.2d 67, 73 (Vt.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:43G4-3840-0039-41B5-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Acordia of Virginia Ins. Agency, Inc. v. Genito Glenn, L.P., 560 S.E.2d 246, 249-250 (Va.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4593-NN50-0039-426G-00000-00&context=1530671). The burden encompasses establishing that an actor served as an agent for a particular relevant purpose. See [_Spencer v. Doyle, 733 N.E.2d 1082, 1085 (Mass.App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4119-28T0-0039-43XS-00000-00&context=1530671) (relevant question is whether collection firm, which had agency relationship with investors in purchasing accounts receivable, also acted as their agent in hiring auditor; firm engaged auditor for routine audit of its financial statements, not as agent on behalf of accounts-receivables investors). When an agent arguably acts on behalf of more than one principal, the burden of establishing an agency relationship encompasses establishing the relationship with respect to a particular principal. See, e.g., [_Foisy v. Royal Maccabees Life Ins. Co., 356 F.3d 141, 151 (1st Cir.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4BHM-HS70-0038-X1VX-00000-00&context=1530671) (jury could reasonably find that insurance broker acted as insurer's agent in making representations to annuitant about terms of annuity contract, although "\[c\]ertainly, the jury could have determined otherwise"; by time of representation, broker had initiated relationship with insurer, "acting at least in part on its behalf in securing \[annuitant's\] business"). Courts may differ on how best to characterize the same conduct when the situation is equivocal. Compare [_Green v. H & R Block, Inc., 735 A.2d 1039, 1047-1055 (Md.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3X83-K140-0039-42JB-00000-00&context=1530671) (material issue of fact precluded summary judgment for tax-preparation firm; taxpayer signed application transmitted by firm to bank that made "rapid refund" loan to taxpayer but application authorized firm to share taxpayer's tax return, prepared by firm, with lender, which delivered loan check to firm to hold for taxpayer; firm's selfpromotion made it reasonable for customer to believe firm acted as customer's agent in relationship to lender and IRS) with [_Peterson v. H & R Block Tax Servs., Inc., 971 F.Supp. 1204, 1213 (N.D.Ill.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RH6-PNJ0-00B1-F0MT-00000-00&context=1530671) (dismissing claim that tax-preparation firm acted as taxpayer's agent; customer provided firm with information but did not provide instructions on how to prepare tax return) and _Basile v. H & R Block, Inc., 761 A.2d 1115, 1121-1122 (Pa.2000)_ (relationship between taxpayer and tax-preparation firm was not relationship of agency because firm lacked power to bind taxpayer and only facilitated "rapid refund" loans; taxpayer's authorization and signature requisite for both tax return and loan application).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 1.03_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-401C-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 1- Introductory Matters_ > _Topic 1- Definitions and Terminology_**

**§ 1.03 Manifestation**

**A person manifests assent or intention through written or spoken words or other conduct.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Cross-references._ A manifestation of the principal's assent to the agent is an element of the definition of a relationship of agency in § 1.01. Actual authority as defined in § 2.01 requires a manifestation from the principal to the agent. The scope of actual authority, addressed in § 2.02, is based upon, although not wholly defined by, the principal's manifestation to the agent. A manifestation of assent by the principal is requisite to creating actual authority under § 3.01. The definition of apparent authority in § 2.03 requires that the third party's belief that an actor has authority as an agent to act on behalf of a person be traceable to a manifestation made by that person. See also § 3.03, under which a principal creates apparent authority through a manifestation of authority. A manifestation by principal or agent is effective to revoke or renounce actual authority when the other has notice of the manifestation. See § 3.10. A person may ratify another's act by a manifestation of assent as stated in § 4.01(2)(a). As stated in § 6.07(1), the principal may pay or otherwise settle an account with a third party by payment or other settlement with the agent when the principal reasonably relies upon a manifestation by the third party, not induced by misrepresentation by the agent, that the agent has settled the account with the third party. A principal's manifestation to a third party is a basis on which the third party may rely on an agent's false representation concerning the agent's authority. See § 6.11(1). An agent's duty to furnish information to the principal as stated in § 8.11 is subject to any contrary manifestation by the principal.

_b. In general._ A manifestation is conduct by a person, observable by others, that expresses meaning. It is a broader concept than communication. The relevant state of mind is that of the person who observes or otherwise learns of the manifestation. See Comment _d._ Conduct often incorporates more than one manifestation, made to different people and carrying different legal consequences. The relevant audience for a manifestation depends on the consequence that the manifestation is alleged to carry. For example, the presence of actual authority depends upon whether the principal has made a manifestation to the agent. See §§ 2.01 and 3.01. In contrast, a manifestation sufficient to establish apparent authority may be, but need not necessarily be, directed toward an identified person as its audience. See §§ 2.03 and 3.03.

Expressive conduct is not limited to spoken or written words, although it often takes those forms. Silence may constitute a manifestation when, in light of all the circumstances, a reasonable person would express dissent to the inference that other persons will draw from silence. Failure then to express dissent will be taken as a manifestation of affirmance. This point is discussed further in Comment _e._ See also § 3.03, Comment _b,_ and § 4.01, Comment _f._ If a person makes no manifestation, as defined in this section, that another has authority as an agent, the person may nonetheless be estopped to deny the existence of a relationship of agency under the circumstances specified in § 2.05.

A principal's manifestations of assent or intention to an agent may differ from the principal's manifestations to third parties and carry different legal consequences. If the principal places a person in a position or office with specific functions or responsibilities, from which third parties will infer that the principal assents to acts by the person requisite to fulfilling the specific functions or responsibilities, the principal has manifested such assent to third parties. For further discussion, see § 3.03, Comments _b_ and _d._ The principal's manifestation to the agent, on the other hand, may be different, expressing assent in narrower or otherwise different terms.

Moreover, an agent is sometimes placed in a position in an industry or setting in which holders of the position customarily have authority of a specific scope. Absent notice to third parties to the contrary, placing the agent in such a position constitutes a manifestation that the principal assents to be bound by actions by the agent that fall within that scope. A third party who interacts with the person, believing the manifestation to be true, need not establish a communication made directly to the third party by the principal to establish the presence of apparent authority as defined in § 2.03.

**Illustration:**

1\. P Corporation produces musical recordings and employs A to engage performers. A's counterparts in the recording industry have authority to make unconditional contracts with performers, but B, who is A's superior within P Corporation, directs A to condition all payments to performers on the sales revenues that P Corporation receives from their work. B's direction to A is not known outside P Corporation. P Corporation has made a manifestation to performers with whom A deals on its behalf that it assents to be bound by contracts made by A on terms that are standard in the industry. P Corporation's manifestation to A is that it assents to be bound by A's acts only if they conform to the terms stated by B. On an agent's duties to act only within the scope of the agent's actual authority and to comply with the principal's reasonable instruction, see § 8.09.

A principal may also place an agent in a position from which the reasonable inference may be drawn that the agent has authority of a particular scope even though the particular business is novel.

**Illustration:**

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that P Corporation markets a novel computer-based technology that effects simultaneous translation of broadcast and recorded music. P Corporation employs A to engage performers to appear in live television broadcasts demonstrating the technology, and again P directs A to condition all payments to performers on sales revenues received by P Corporation. P Corporation has made a manifestation to performers with whom A deals that it consents to be bound by contracts made by A on terms that are standard when performers are engaged for live televised appearances.

_c. Manifestations by organizations._ A juridical person that is an organization manifests its assent to be bound by the acts of individuals through the observable connections between the individual and the organization. An organization manifests assent to an individual by appointing that person to a position defined by the organization. Such appointment often occurs through the acts of superior agents. See § 1.04(9), which defines superior and subordinate coagents. The terms defining the position may be specific, singling out specific tasks to be accomplished, or general, prescribing responsibilities in broad terms that contemplate the substantial use of discretion. Legally defined structures that constitute vehicles for organized activity enable the appointment of individuals to positions within an organization that carry certain powers. This capacity is a common feature of public as well as private organizations, of business firms however organized, and of not-for-profit as well as for-profit organizations. Within publicsector organizations, the authority associated with a position is often defined by legislation or by administrative regulation.

Organizations generally operate by subdividing work or activities into specific functions that are assigned to different people. The legal treatment of organizations often recognizes this characteristic. For example, under U.C.C. § 2-104(1), an organization may be treated as a "merchant" that deals in goods of a particular kind, even when such dealing is not the organization's purpose, if the organization employs a purchasing department or business personnel who are authorized to take the requisite action and who have knowledge and skill peculiar to the goods involved in a transaction. Another functional necessity for an organization is a routine for internal communication so that relevant information reaches those assigned a particular function. This characteristic underlies U.C.C. § 1-202(f), which deems an organization's receipt of notice or knowledge to be effective for a particular transaction when brought to the attention of the individual conducting the transaction, "and, in any event, from the time it would have been brought to the individual's attention if the organization had exercised due diligence," defined as maintaining "reasonable routines for communicating significant information" plus "reasonable compliance with the routines."

An organization may create a routine to communicate with those external to the organization, designating people with authority to speak as to particular types of matters. When the designated person speaks as to a matter that is one of the types specified, and in a manner that conforms to the established routine, a third party is justified in believing that the person speaks for the organization. The representation of reliability made through the manifestation cascades over the designated person's statements. The organization may also develop a channel of communications by which information about decisions made within the organization is communicated to persons external to it. An organization's channel of communications may under some circumstances constitute a manifestation of its assent to be bound in accordance with the communication. See § 3.03, Comment d.

In private-sector organizations, actual authority, as defined in § 2.01, originates both with the statute through which the organization achieves a legally recognized form and with the organization's constitutional documents. These provide the skeleton or essential architecture for beginning to determine which acts committed by human actors should be attributed to the organization. An organization's charter, certificate of incorporation, articles, and bylaws typically specify, among other basic structural matters, certain key positions within the organization and authority to act on behalf of the organization that is associated with each position. Private-sector organizations formed as corporations have a governing body ordinarily known as a board of directors (sometimes called a board of governors or board of trustees) that, by statute, must take specified types of actions and that also, by statute, is assigned ultimate supervisory responsibility for the corporation's business and affairs. Directors commonly appoint officers for the corporation. Officers hold defined executive positions carrying titles; the nature and scope of each officer's authority may be defined by statute, by the corporation's constitutional documents, by specific resolutions adopted by the directors, and by custom associating specific functions with a particular position.

Albeit of foundational importance, these formal characteristics do not capture either the practical or the legal reality of how most corporations and other business organizations operate. The ability to delegate with a reasonably reliable sense of the legal consequences is the essence of corporate management. What is delegated often includes the right to make manifestations to subordinates within the organization or to persons external to it. Delegation does not generally constitute abdication by the corporation's board or its officers. An officer granted broad managerial authority to manage a corporation's affairs impliedly has authority to delegate to subordinate agents acting under the officer's supervisory control. In a large corporation, where matters are complex, the implied authority to delegate extends to matters that are discretionary and not wholly or even substantially ministerial. The actual authority of a corporation's employees traces to its officers' power to hire employees and is defined in part by their terms of employment, which may describe authority with varying degrees of breadth or narrowness and specificity or generality. In many corporations, additionally, functions are delegated informally through oral instructions from a superior agent to an inferior one. It is not unusual for paperwork documenting a decision by a superior agent to follow rather than accompany the making of the decision.

Many titles within corporations are indeterminate and denote little of specific content about the holder's actual authority. Depending on the particular corporation, an individual's title may cast light on the extent of the individual's actual authority. A "general manager" is likely to have been granted broad powers to manage ordinary business. On the other hand, an executive whose title specifies a particular function, such as Vice President for X, may have broad powers, but ones delimited by the scope of the named function.

An ownership interest in a corporation ordinarily does not create any capacity to act on behalf of the corporation or to bind it. Some legislation specific to closely held corporations enables such capacity to be created if the corporation's organizational documents eliminate a board of directors.

In contrast, general-partnership legislation characterizes each partner as an agent of the partnership for purposes of carrying on its ordinary business in the usual way. General-partnership legislation, like the common law of partnership but in contrast to corporation statutes, links ownership with agency because it makes each partner an agent of the partnership for the purpose of its business. A partner's act binds the partnership if the act appears to carry on partnership business in the usual way. However, if the third party with whom the partner deals knows that the partner lacks authority, the partnership is not bound by the partner's act. The third party's knowledge means that the partner did not act with apparent authority. See § 3.03, Comment _e(2)._

A limited-liability partnership (LLP) is a statutory variation on general partnership that enables partners' individual liability to be limited or eliminated upon compliance with statutory requirements. Statutes vary in the degree of insulation from liability that may be achieved. While under some statutes partners are shielded against all liability except that stemming from their own wrongdoing or negligent supervision of others, other statutes create shields only against vicarious liability for malpractice and other torts committed by fellow partners and other representatives of the firm.

In a limited partnership, a statutorily authorized form in which partners' roles are differentiated, only the general partner or general partners under ordinary circumstances have authority to bind the partnership because the limited partners occupy only a passive investment role. In a limited-liability company (LLC), the operating agreement usually reflects whether the LLC has chosen to be managed by its members, by a manager, or by both. The operating agreement may also contain further specifications of authority.

An organizational principal's manifestations of assent to third parties may exceed the organization's manifestations to the individuals who act on its behalf, as in Illustration 1. Apparent authority, as defined in § 2.03, is applicable when a third party's reasonable belief that the organization has consented is traceable to a manifestation of the organization. See § 2.03, Comment _d._ Organizations often create apparent authority by situating people within a hierarchy of positions with defined responsibilities. Observing a systematic hierarchy, a third party might reasonably infer that the organization is represented by a particular agent whose acts and statements are compatible with the agent's situation within the organization. Questions of apparent authority in this context often turn on the interplay between general definitions of the authority associated with specific positions and observed characteristics of how the organization actually functions. If an individual occupies a position that customarily carries specific authority, a third party who knows of the individual's position will properly infer that the principal consents to the individual's exercise of such authority. If the organization's manifestation to the individual is narrower because it has carved out elements of the customary authority, but the third party is unaware that authority has been withheld, the organization's broader manifestation of assent to the third party connects the individual's acts to the organization. For further discussion of manifestations and apparent authority in the organizational context, see § 3.03, Comments _c, d,_ and _e._

_d. Unintended manifestations._ If a principal voluntarily manifests assent or intention, the manifestation is effective although it is made negligently or is otherwise in error. The principal's manifestation of assent determines whether a relationship of agency arises, not the principal's unexpressed intention if otherwise unknown to the agent. See § 1.01, Comment _d._ An agent's reasonable understanding of the principal's intention determines whether the agent has acted with actual authority as stated in §§ 2.01-2.02. A manifestation that is the result of duress or fraud does not reflect the uncoerced intention of the person who makes it.

_e. Manifestations understood in context._ A manifestation does not occur in a vacuum, and the meaning that may reasonably be inferred from it will reflect the context in which the manifestation is made. Assent and intention may be expressed explicitly, but often they are inferred from surrounding facts and circumstances. For example, if an organization hires a person as its purchasing manager, the organization's act expresses assent that the person undertake the manager's role as defined by the organization. Such assent would be communicated by someone acting and speaking on behalf of the organization. If a person is in a particular industry, industry custom and practice shape how others in that industry will understand manifestations that the person makes. In the absence of manifestations to the contrary, engaging an agent to accomplish an objective that in a particular industry requires that particular actions be taken would manifest assent to those actions.

Between particular persons, prior dealings or an ongoing relationship frame the context in which manifestations are made and understood. For example, an agent may know, based on prior dealings with the principal, that the principal uses language idiosyncratically or that a particular statement by the principal does not accurately reflect the principal's intention. The operative meaning of the principal's manifestation to the agent is that which the agent has reason to know the principal intends.

**Illustrations:**

3\. P says to A, "Sell my horse for me." A knows that what P intends to sell is P's cow. A has actual authority to sell only the cow. See §§ 2.01-2.02. Whether A has apparent authority to sell the cow, or the horse, or neither, is governed by § 2.03.

4\. P, a dealer in used books, retains A to buy books on P's behalf. P directs A to buy only "first editions." A knows that by "first editions," P means first editions published in the United Kingdom. As to A, P has not manifested assent to A's purchase on P's behalf of first editions published outside the United Kingdom.

In some circumstances, a manifestation may be made by conduct alone or by conduct that carries meaning at odds with words expressed previously or simultaneously. For example, if a lawyer accepts a retainer and files a complaint on behalf of a person, a client-lawyer relationship results although the lawyer has disclaimed in writing any intention to have such a relationship. Actions may speak louder than words. Likewise, in some circumstances, failure to object may properly be understood as a manifestation of intention or consent. In Illustration 1, the principal's failure to communicate the limit on A's authority constitutes a manifestation, to the performers with whom A deals, that A has authority standard in the industry. P has made a manifestation but not objected to inferences that others will draw from it. As between the agent and the principal, an unexplained failure to object may also in appropriate circumstances constitute a manifestation of assent or intention.

**Illustration:**

5\. P employs A to sell items manufactured by P, requiring A to submit a weekly report. A states in a weekly report that A had a favorable opportunity to close a sale and did so, stating the terms of the sale. A requests in the report that P fax or telephone, at A's expense, if P objects. P neither faxes nor telephones A, nor does P otherwise communicate with A. No extraneous circumstances explain P's silence. P's silence constitutes a manifestation to A that P intends to affirm the transaction.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ Restatement Second, Agency, does not define manifestation, although the concept and term are used at several points. See, e.g., § 1(1) (definition of agency), § 7 (definition of authority), § 8 (definition of apparent authority), § 15 (creation of agency relation), § 26 (creation of authority), § 33 (general principle applicable to agent's interpretation of principal's manifestation), § 77 (authority to delegate), § 83 (definition of affirmance of unauthorized act), § 95 (necessity to communicate affirmance), § 106 (manifestation of principal to agent determines whether authority terminates because another has accomplished act), § 110 (principal's manifestation regarding destruction of subject matter of agency), § 118 (revocation and renunciation of authority), § 119 (manner of renunciation and revocation), § 134 (material manifestation contrary to notice of when authority terminates), § 135 (notice to third party of termination of authority), and § 146 (agent's manifestation determines parties to transaction). Other examples of usage include Restatement Second, Contracts § 2 (definition of promise as a "manifestation of intention to act or refrain from acting in a specified way . . . ."), § 3 (definition of agreement as a "manifestation of mutual assent on the part of two or more persons"), §§ 19-20 (specifying when conduct manifests assent); Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 14(1) (relationship of client and lawyer arises when "a person manifests to a lawyer the person's intent that the lawyer provide legal services for the person; and either (a) the lawyer manifests to the person consent to do so; or (b) the lawyer fails to manifest lack of consent to do so, and the lawyer knows or reasonably should know that the person reasonably relies on the lawyer to provide the services. . . ."); and Restatement Third, Trusts § 2 (defining trust as a "fiduciary relationship with respect to property, arising from a manifestation of intention to create that relationship . . . .").

The definition of manifestation in this section is intended to be broader than that assumed to be operative at points in the Restatement Second of Agency. One consequence of this breadth is to eliminate the rationale for a distinct doctrine of inherent-agency power applicable to disclosed principals when an agent disregards instructions or oversteps actual authority. See Restatement Second, Agency § 8 A, Comment _b_ (noting that in some situations to which the doctrine of inherent agency power is applicable, "the courts have rested liability upon the ground of 'apparent authority' . . . . If the meaning of the term \[apparent authority\] is restricted, as is done in Section 8, to those situations in which the principal has manifested the existence of authority to third persons, the term does not apply to the above situations"). In this Restatement, conduct may constitute a manifestation sufficient to create apparent authority even though it does not use the word "authority" and even though it does not consist of words targeted specifically to a third party. For an early critique of inherent agency power and recognition that a broader definition of manifestation better explains the liability of disclosed principals, see Steven A. Fishman, Inherent Agency Power--Should Enterprise Liability Apply to Agents' Unauthorized Contracts, 19 Rutgers L.J. 1, 41-43 (1987). On the treatment of manifestation in Restatement Second, Agency, see Aaron Lipson, Whose Fool Is It Anyway: Updating Apparent Authority for Today's Business World, [_33 Ga. L. Rev. 1219, 1247 (1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3YGC-D1Y0-00CW-G09P-00000-00&context=1530671) (noting absence of definition of manifestation).

_b. In general._ Illustrations 1 and 2 are based on the factual scenario in [_Kidd v. Thomas A. Edison, Inc., 239 F. 405 (S.D.N.Y.1917)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-R670-003B-W1W8-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_242 F. 923 (2d Cir.1917)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-46B0-003B-K46F-00000-00&context=1530671). The Illustrations differentiate between situations in which an agent operates in an established industry in which the authority associated with a position is defined by custom or convention, and situations in which, as in _Kidd_ itself, the industry is novel. In _Kidd,_ the court held the recording company liable to the performer on the terms of the contract made by its employee. The agent in _Kidd_ was authorized to engage singers to perform "tone test" recitals of their live voices versus recordings. Said the court: "while it is fair to remember that the 'tone test' recitals were new, in the sense that no one had ever before employed singers for just this purpose . . . they were not new merely as musical recitals; for it was, of course, a common thing to engage singers for such recitals. When, therefore, an agent is selected . . . to engage singers for musical recitals, the customary implication would seem to have been that his authority was without limitation of the kind imposed, which was unheard of in the circumstances. The mere fact that the purpose of the recitals was advertisement, instead of entrance fees, gave no intimation to a singer dealing with him that the defendant's promise would be conditioned upon so unusual a condition as was actually imposed." [_239 F. at 406_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-R670-003B-W1W8-00000-00&context=1530671). As to apparent authority, the court said, "If estoppel be, therefore, the basis of all apparent authority, it existed here. . . . It is only a fiction to say that the principal is estopped, when he has not communicated with the third person and thus misled him." [_Id. at 407_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-R670-003B-W1W8-00000-00&context=1530671). The court, instead, justified the outcome on the basis of the agency relation itself: "It makes no difference that the agent may be disregarding his principal's directions, secret or otherwise, so long as he continues in that larger field measured by the general scope of the business intrusted to his care. . . . Once a third person has assured himself widely of the character of the agent's mandate, the very purpose of the relation demands the possibility of the principal's being bound through the agent's minor deviations." [_Id. at 407-408_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-R670-003B-W1W8-00000-00&context=1530671).

On an agent's appointment to a particular position as a manifestation by the principal, see, e.g., [_Peoples Heritage Sav. Bank v. Pease, 797 A.2d 1270, 1276 (Me.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45XC-9P90-0039-42S7-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[a\]n employee has apparent authority to do what an employee in his position would customarily do, if the third party knows of his position with the principal, but not of what the employer actually authorized him to do in that position"; genuine issue of material fact whether mortgagee's employee acted with apparent authority in negotiating terms with mortgagors to pay off loans). On appointment to a position as a basis for apparent authority in a particular context, see Grace M. Giesel, Enforcement of Settlement Contracts: The Problem of the Attorney Agent, [_12 Geo. J. Legal Ethics 543, 561 (1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3XY7-XGJ0-00CV-90DP-00000-00&context=1530671) (noting that some courts use appointment of an agent to a position as a basis on which to find a third party's belief in the agent's authority to be based on a manifestation by the principal; noting variety of doctrines applied to determine whether client is bound to settlement agreement on basis of lawyer's assent to it).

A representation or manifestation by conduct is not made by conduct whose meaning is equivocal or by conduct not attributable to the person whose alleged manifestation is at issue. See, e.g., Lukoil v. Tata, \[1999\] 2 Lloyd's L. Rep. 129, 138 (Ct. App.) (representation by conduct that hirer of vessels had authority on behalf of shipowner to enter into towage contract that made ship's owner party to towage contract not established when owner lacked any knowledge of terms on form of contract).

_c. Manifestations by organizations._ For examples of the operation of organizations' channels of communication, see [_Miller v. Mason-McDuffie Co., 739 P.2d 806 (Ariz.1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-WKJ0-003F-T1R4-00000-00&context=1530671) (branch manager's authority to communicate with loan applicants, solicit loan applications, accept commitment fees, and deliver loan commitments supported finding that branch manager acted with apparent authority in telling applicant that loan had been approved); [_Pipkin v. Thomas & Hill, Inc., 236 S.E.2d 725 (N.C.App. 1977)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-1D00-003G-04BY-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd in part and rev'd in part on other grounds, [_258 S.E.2d 778 (N.C.1979)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-Y970-003G-04WD-00000-00&context=1530671) (branch manager of mortgage broker who did not have actual authority to commit institution to permanent financing had apparent authority to represent to plaintiff's interim lender that permanent financing had been arranged; nothing in defendant's loan application indicated it limited its business to loanbrokerage service, letterhead and business cards indicated defendant was in business of making mortgage loans, and branch manager would usually have authority necessary to conduct business of office); First Energy (UK) Ltd. v. Hungarian Int'l Bank Ltd., \[1993\] 2 Lloyd's Rep. 194 (Ct. App.) (manager of Manchester office had ostensible authority to communicate that bank's head office in London had approved loan facility; bank liable for breach of contract when manager had authority to sign and send letters on behalf of bank and borrower was not informed of any limits on such authority). See also [_Jones v. Federated Fin. Reserve Corp., 144 F.3d 961, 966 (6th Cir. 1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SS0-5N40-0038-X01W-00000-00&context=1530671) (plaintiff's ex-spouse discovered plaintiffs address when ex-spouse's friend, an employee of defendant, requested plaintiff's credit history from credit bureau; plaintiff may hold defendant liable for breach of Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) "if the trier of fact finds that \[defendant\] created an appearance of authority that caused the credit reporting agency reasonably and prudently to believe that \[defendant's employee\] had made a proper request for a permissible purpose, and that there was reliance on \[defendant's employee's\] apparent authority"); [_Kanavos v. Hancock Bank & Trust Co., 439 N.E.2d 311 (Mass.App.1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-G120-003C-V456-00000-00&context=1530671), remanded for retrial on other grounds, [_479 N.E.2d 168 (Mass.1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-50F0-003C-V2XS-00000-00&context=1530671) (bank's executive vice-president had apparent authority to amend repurchase option in workout deal when bank's president permitted executive vicepresident to exercise general executive responsibilities and encouraged borrower to deal with executive vicepresident); [_Diversified Dev. & Inv., Inc. v. Heil, 889 P.2d 1212 (N.M. 1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W1F0-003D-D18X-00000-00&context=1530671) (lawyer who represented grantor of option had apparent authority to tell grantee that grantor had extended deadline for exercise of option although grantee knew that lawyer lacked authority to grant extension).

For an illustration of formal and informal intra-organizational dealing, see [_Gabelmann v. NFO, Inc., 571 N.W.2d 476, 480 (Iowa 1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RFX-XXF0-0039-40S7-00000-00&context=1530671) (manager had actual authority to offer housing and moving allowances to employee based on oral approval of organization's president; paperwork to document decision followed, as was routine, but was not initialed by president).

In social-science literature, cascade effects concern situations in which perceptions increase in plausibility because of antecedent or subsequent behaviors. For applications in legal scholarship, see Timur Kuran & Cass Sunstein, Availability Cascades and Risk Regulation, [_51 Stan. L. Rev. 683 (1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3WCR-Y330-00CW-804W-00000-00&context=1530671); Eric Talley, Precedential Cascades: An Appraisal, [_73 S. Cal. L. Rev. 87 (1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3YCK-M0F0-00CV-50SF-00000-00&context=1530671).

Statutory statements as to partners' actual authority are Unif. Partnership Act § 9(1) (1914) and Rev. Unif. Partnership Act § 301(1) (1997). These provisions are substantially similar, but under § 301(1) a partner's appearance of authority pertains to "apparently carrying on in the ordinary course the partnership business or business of the kind carried on by the partnership. . . ." Section 6(1) explicitly encompasses only the partnership's business, not "business of the kind." Rev. Unif. Partnership Act § 301, comment 2.

On the actual authority of partners, see [_Sadelmi Joint Venture v. Dalton, 5 F.3d 510, 513 (Fed.Cir.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-MXN0-003N-4376-00000-00&context=1530671) (creation of management committee for joint venture did not divest partners of authority to conduct business of joint venture); [_FDIC v. Enventure V, 868 F.Supp. 870, 874 (S.D.Tex.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-7J70-003B-V423-00000-00&context=1530671), rev'd on other grounds, [_77 F.3d 123 (5th Cir.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-36M0-006F-M4W7-00000-00&context=1530671) (limited partnership bound by note executed by its general partner unless lender had knowledge of general partner's lack of authority as defined by partnership agreement; federal law bars efforts to defeat FDIC's enforcement of note when defense is based on written agreement not found in records of financial institution related to transaction and, under [_12 U.S.C. § 1823(e)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SF8-7342-D6RV-H1V6-00000-00&context=1530671), when agreement was not executed by financial institution and obligor contemporaneously with execution of note); C-4 Corp. v. E.G. [_Smith Constr. Prods., 894 S.W.2d 242, 245 (Mo.App.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-4R70-003F-C51Y-00000-00&context=1530671) (partnership bound by partner's execution of note as consideration for settlement agreement).

On variations in the shield permitted by limited-liability-partnership legislation, compare Del. Code Ann., tit. 6, § 1515(b) (providing that individual partners shall not be liable for debt or liability chargeable to partnership, excepting liability "for the partner's own negligence, wrongful acts or misconduct or that of any person under his direct supervision and control") with Tex. Civ. Stats. Art. 6132b-3.08(a)(2) (partner in registered limited-liability partnership is not liable "for debts and obligations of the partnership arising from errors, omissions, negligence, incompetence, or malfeasance committed . . . by another partner or a representative of the partnership not working under the supervision or direction of the first partner unless the first partner (A) was directly involved in the specific activity in which the errors, omissions, negligence, incompetence, or malfeasance were committed by the other partner or representative; or (B) had notice or knowledge of the errors, omissions, negligence, incompetence, or malfeasance . . . and then failed to take reasonable steps to prevent or cure the errors, omissions, negligence, incompetence, or malfeasance").

Examples of statutes that enable shareholders, as such, to manage the corporation if the organizational documents eliminate the board of directors are Del. Code Ann., tit. 8, § 351; Model Stat. Close Corp. Supplement § 21(C)(1).

Within corporations, a distinction can be drawn between primary and general rules of attribution. Primary rules, found in corporation law and the individual corporation's constitutional documents, "are obviously not enough to enable a company to go out into the world and do business. Not every act on behalf of the company could be expected to be the subject of a resolution of the board or a unanimous decision of the shareholders." Meridian Global Funds Mgmt. Asia Ltd. v. Securities Comm'n, \[1995\] 2 A.C. 500, 506 (P.C.) (appeal taken from New Zealand). A corporation must supplement primary rules of attribution with general rules, equally available to natural persons, drawn from the law of agency. The essence of management is stated to be the ability to delegate in [_Schoonejongen v. Curtiss-Wright Corp., 143 F.3d 120, 127 (3d Cir.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SKF-VRR0-0038-X04F-00000-00&context=1530671).

For a general statement on the creation of actual authority in a corporation, see Principles of Corporate Governance: Analysis and Recommendations § 3.01, Reporter's Note. See also State Bank & Trust Co. v. Boat "D.J. Griffin," [_755 F. Supp. 1389, 1399 (E.D.La.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-G9Y0-001T-74PD-00000-00&context=1530671). For the proposition that actual authority is specific to a particular corporation in a multi-corporation group, see [_Algie v. RCA Global Commc'ns., Inc., 60 F.3d 956, 960-961 (2d Cir.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-D500-001T-D1DC-00000-00&context=1530671) (when employee benefit plan reserves amendment power to a corporation, only that corporation may amend the plan; corporation that retains identity following its sale remains relevant actor for purposes of amendment, not purchasing corporation).

The term "accordion title" is used in [_Commission for Ecumenical Mission & Relations of the United Presbyterian Church v. Roger Gray, Ltd., 318 N.Y.S.2d 726, 730 (N.Y.1971)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-VV00-003C-C3R5-00000-00&context=1530671), to characterize the indeterminacy of the title "managing agent." In contrast, a "general manager" is said to have broad authority to do acts in the ordinary course of the corporation's business, including hiring employees, in [_Anderson v. McAllister Towing & Transp. Co., 17 F.Supp.2d 1280, 1289 (S.D.Ala.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3TJJ-4TS0-0038-Y0KT-00000-00&context=1530671). It is much less likely that an executive has authority to undertake a matter that does not match with functional attributes in the executive's title. See _Orient Overseas Container Line v. Kids Int'l Corp., 1999 A.M.C. 961 (S.D.N.Y. 1998)_ (officer with title "Director of Imports" lacked actual authority to execute \$ 1 million guarantee on behalf of third party to induce shipping company's general agent to release merchandise in absence of original bills of lading).

_e. Manifestations understood in context._ For the point that an agent's engagement to accomplish an objective may constitute a manifestation of assent to the agent to take actions customary in the industry, see [_Old Republic Ins. Co. v. Hansa World Cargo Serv., Inc., 51 F.Supp.2d 457, 472-473 (S.D.N.Y.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WNV-V910-0038-Y0RD-00000-00&context=1530671) (holding that customhouse broker may have acted with actual authority in posting bonds on behalf of importer when broker was retained as agent for the arrival and delivery of steel into the United States from Brazil; custom in importing business requires posting of surety bonds and responsibility for arrival and delivery of goods encompasses such posting).

Illustration 3 is a variation on Restatement Second, Contracts § 20, Illustration 5.

On a principal's failure to object to inferences that others reasonably will draw, see [_McWhorter v. Sheller, 993 S.W.2d 781, 787 (Tex.App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WDK-0HB0-0039-451P-00000-00&context=1530671) (holder of nontransferable federal firearms license, who allowed agents to use it to buy and sell firearms for profit, knew that plaintiff gave funds to agents to buy firearms for purpose of selling them at gun shows, but did not tell investor that agents were acting outside their relationship with him as to transactions involving investor's money and firearms bought with it; evidence at trial sufficient to sustain verdict for damages against holder of license resulting from fraud committed by agents). See also [_Gruenwald v. Advanced Computer Applications, Inc., 730 A.2d 1004, 1011 (Pa.Super.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WH6-DSN0-0039-4275-00000-00&context=1530671) (failure of corporation's president, also a director, to object to his demotion to vice-president at board meeting when demotion occurred manifests assent); [_Trustees of Am. Fed. of Musicians & Employers' Pension Fund v. Steven Scott Enters., Inc., 40 F.Supp.2d 503, 511 (S.D.N.Y.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3W43-0WJ0-0038-Y4GV-00000-00&context=1530671) (pension fund affirmed appearance of authority of fund trustee who was also union agent by failing to object when he cashed 13 checks that were expressly purported to constitute full settlement of all pension contributions owed to fund on account of performances given by union members).

On the formation of a client-lawyer relationship when actions speak louder than words, see Catherine J. Lanctot, Attorney-Client Relationships in Cyberspace: The Peril and the Promise, [_49 Duke L.J. 147, 190-191 (1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3YBY-K8J0-00CV-50S3-00000-00&context=1530671) (lawyer's written disclaimer does not prevent formation of client-lawyer relationship when lawyer accepts a retainer and performs service); see also Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 14, Comment _e_ (lawyer may indicate consent to creating client-lawyer relationship by performing services requested by client).
