The definition of apparent authority in this section includes the concepts termed "usual authority" and "customary authority" insofar as the principal's appointment or apparent appointment of an agent to a position is a substantial basis for a third party's belief that the agent has authority to do acts typical of an agent in such a position. Some English commentary uses the term "usual authority" in a related but somewhat different sense to mean the authority an agent normally possesses in specific circumstances, whether or not the agent has actual authority. The definition in this section requires that the third party reasonably believe the agent to be authorized and that such belief be traceable to a manifestation of the principal's manifestation as defined in § 1.03. See id., Comment c, for discussion of manifestations made by organizations, including by appointing an individual to a position.

It is consistent with contemporary usage in statutory drafting and in judicial opinions to treat apparent authority as a form of authority rather than as a distinct instance of an agent's ability to affect a principal's legal position. For example, U.C.C. § 1-201(41) defines an "unauthorized" signature as one "made without actual, implied, or apparent authority. . . ." In contrast, Restatement Second, Agency, characterized apparent authority as "entirely distinct from authority, either express or implied." Restatement Second, Agency § 8, Comment a.

_c. Rationale._ Apparent authority holds a principal accountable for the results of third-party beliefs about an actor's authority to act as an agent when the belief is reasonable and is traceable to a manifestation of the principal. As to the third person, apparent authority when present trumps restrictions that the principal has privately imposed on the agent. The relevant appearance is that the principal has conferred authority on an agent. Apparent authority, when present, often has the effect of reinforcing the legal effect of actual authority when actual authority has been conferred by a principal but is not readily provable by a third party. Apparent authority is distinct from the circumstances of an agency relationship known to agent and principal, which may not be observable by a third party, even though the course of dealing between agent and principal will often be observed at least in part by third parties. A principal may not choose to act through agents whom it has clothed with the trappings of authority and then determine at a later time whether the consequences of their acts offer an advantage.

The doctrine stated in this section applies to any set of circumstances under which it is reasonable for a third party to believe that an agent has authority, so long as the belief is traceable to manifestations of the principal. A principal's conduct does not occur in a vacuum. A third party's reasonable understanding of the principal's conduct will reflect general business custom as well as usage that is particular to the principal's industry and prior dealings between the parties. A belief that results solely from the statements or other conduct of the agent, unsupported by any manifestations traceable to the principal, does not create apparent authority unless, as explained below, the agent's conduct has been directed by the principal. An agent's success in misleading the third party as to the existence of actual authority does not in itself make the principal accountable.

Manifestations as defined in § 1.03 may take many forms. These include explicit statements that a principal makes directly to a third party, as well as statements made by others concerning an actor's authority that reach the third party and are traceable to the principal. For example, a principal may make a manifestation about an agent's authority by directing that the agent's name and affiliation with the principal be included in a listing of representatives that is provided to a third party. The principal may make a manifestation by directing an agent to make statements to third parties or directing or designating an agent to perform acts or conduct negotiations, placing an agent in a position within an organization, or placing the agent in charge of a transaction or situation. See § 3.03, Comments _b, c, d,_ and _e._

Apparent authority often coincides with actual authority. In such situations, the agent has acted consistently with what the agent reasonably believes the principal wishes the agent to do. This provides actual authority, and the principal's manifestations are the basis for the third party's reasonable belief that the principal has authorized the agent's acts. An agent may have actual authority to make statements concerning the agent's own authority. If a principal directs an agent to make a statement to a third party about the agent's authority and the agent's statement of authority is consistent with a reasonable belief on the agent's part about the principal's wishes, the agent's statement to the third party is within the scope of the agent's actual authority. The principal has no claim against the agent because the agent acted with actual authority. If the third party reasonably believes that the agent is authorized and that the principal has authorized the agent to make the statement, apparent authority is present as well. In contrast, if a principal grants actual authority to an agent and for some reason directs the agent to deny authority, the agent's statement to the third party denying that the agent has authority precludes the presence of apparent authority because it is not reasonable for the third party to believe the agent to be authorized. The third party's ability to hold the principal to a transaction entered into on the principal's account by the agent will then depend on the third party's ability to prove the existence of actual authority. Sections 6.10 and 6.11 address other aspects of agents' statements as to their own authority.

**Illustrations:**

1\. P owns a granary and employs A to manage it. A's employment agreement with P states that A's authority to purchase grain is limited to transactions that do not exceed \$ 5000; larger purchases require P's express approval. This limit is unusual in the granary business. P directs A to tell T, a seller of grain, that A's unilateral authority to purchase is unlimited because P believes this will induce T to give priority to orders placed by A. A represents to T that A's authority to purchase is unlimited and enters into a contract on P's behalf with T to buy \$ 10,000 of grain. P is bound by the contract with T. A has actual authority to make the representation to T. A has apparent authority to enter into the contract with T because T reasonably believes A has authority to bind P to a contract to buy the grain.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that T has sold grain to P before and has previously been told by A that A's unilateral purchasing authority is limited to \$ 5000 transactions. At P's direction, A tells T that A's unilateral authority to purchase is unlimited and enters into a contract on P's behalf to buy \$ 10,000 of grain. A has actual authority to make the representation to T. P's direction to A creates apparent authority as well if it is reasonable for T to believe that A now has unlimited purchasing authority on the basis of A's representation to that effect. A has actual authority to enter into the contract if A reasonably believes P wishes A to do so.

3\. P employs A as the manager of P's property. A is not authorized to sell or otherwise deal in or dispose of P's property. P executes and dates a power of attorney designating B as P's agent with full power to sell, give, and otherwise transfer P's property. A and B share the same first name. P places the power in a locked drawer in P's office desk. One week later, A picks the lock and steals the drawer's contents, including the power of attorney. The next day, A displays the power to T, to whom A offers to sell property owned by P, explaining that the last name on the power does not reflect A's current marital status. A trier of fact may conclude that A's theft and possession of the power do not create apparent authority because T did not reasonably believe A to be authorized by P. A's theft and subsequent display of the power do not constitute manifestations of P. On estoppel to deny the existence of a relationship of agency, see § 2.05.

More generally, a principal may permit an agent to acquire a reputation of authority in an area or endeavor by acquiescing in conduct by the agent under circumstances likely to lead to a reputation. Third parties may continue reasonably to believe that the agent is authorized even after the agency relationship has been terminated if they are unaware of the termination. See § 3.11.

Apparent authority is distinct from estoppel, defined in §§ 2.05-2.06. Comment _e_ discusses proof of reliance in connection with apparent authority.

Apparent authority has been said to be "based upon the principle which has led to the objective theory of contracts, namely, that in contractual relations one should ordinarily be bound by what he says rather than by what he intends, so that the contract which results from the acceptance of an offer is that which the offeree reasonably understands, rather than what the offeror means." Restatement Second, Agency § 8, Comment _d._ The principal's manifestation as to the agent's authority empowers the agent to affect the principal's legal relations. Restrictions on an agent's authority that are known only to the principal and the agent do not defeat or supersede the consequences of apparent authority for the principal's legal relations, apart from the principal's legal relations with the agent. In the principal's relations with third parties, restrictions that the principal has placed on the agent's authority are inoperative if apparent authority is present just as, by analogy, an offeror's unexpressed meaning that is unknown to the offeree is inoperative as to the offeror's contractual relations with the offeree. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 19, Comment c. To establish apparent authority, it is not necessary for a third party to establish fault on the part of the principal, just as it is not necessary for an offeree to establish fault on the part of an offeror to hold the offeror to the manifested intention to enter into a bargain with the offeree. If apparent authority is present, it is irrelevant that its presence or continued presence eluded the principal's exercise of due care to prevent or defeat it.

**Illustration:**

4\. P Corporation owns several breweries, one of which is managed by A. P Corporation's executives tell vendors that A has authority to buy supplies for the brewery "as A determines necessary, based on A's superior knowledge of local conditions." Disenchanted with A's decisions but not wishing to terminate A, P Corporation's officers send a letter to all 2000 known vendors to the brewery stating that A's authority to purchase supplies in excess of a stated amount now requires the approval of P Corporation's newly appointed Vice President for Production Management. P Corporation sends the letter through the U.S. Postal Service and, additionally, faxes a copy of the letter to all known vendors. The posted letter and the fax reach all vendors with the exception of T, a bottle vendor. Neither reaches T as a result of the postal authorities' misdirection of the posted letter and an unnoticed and inexplicable malfunction in telephonic transmission of the fax. T does not otherwise have notice of the limit placed on A's unilateral authority. A enters into a contract with T to buy bottles in a magnitude in excess of A's unilateral authority. P Corporation is bound by the contract. T reasonably believed that A had unrestricted authority to contract for supplies. Even though P Corporation used reasonable care in attempting to communicate the limit, A's appearance of authority as to T is not defeated by P Corporation's unsuccessful efforts to communicate a limit on A's authority.

_d. Reasonable belief._ It is usually a question for the trier of fact whether a reasonable person in the position of a third party would believe that an agent had the authority or the right to do a particular act. It is a separate but related question of fact whether such a belief is traceable to a manifestation of the principal. Although many factual circumstances can be relevant to these determinations, basic points about apparent authority provide an underlying framework for analysis.

Apparent authority is based on a third party's understanding of signals of all sorts concerning the actor with whom the third party interacts. When the third party knows the actor is an agent and knows the identity of the principal, the presence of apparent authority turns on the tie between these signals and the principal in the mind of the reasonable third party. The third party observes the agent in a particular context, one that may be defined in part by interactions, dealings, or relationships between the principal and the agent that the third party has observed in the past, by an organization, by an industry and its customs; by a type of transaction that is conventionally done in a particular way; or, if in a new context, by reasonable expectations based on analogous situations and other relevant circumstances. On agents operating in novel contexts, see § 1.03, Comment _b._ Of course, the reasonable third party should also assess the credibility of assertions made directly by the agent. If the agent tells the third party that a transaction is beyond the agent's authority, it is not reasonable for the third party to believe thereafter that the agent has such authority absent a credible change in circumstances that augment the agent's authority.

If a third party knows that the actor in question is an agent and knows the identity of the principal, the third party should assess what is observed of the agent in light of the agent's position as a fiduciary with a duty to use authority on behalf of the principal. In a transactional context, the agent's position as a fiduciary should prompt doubt in the mind of the reasonable third party when the agent appears to be using authority to bind the principal to a transaction that will not benefit the principal. Commercial legislation reflects this insight. Suppose a negotiable instrument, taken for payment or collection from a person known to be a fiduciary, is payable to the fiduciary in that capacity or to the person represented by the fiduciary. If the instrument is taken in a transaction known to be for the fiduciary's personal benefit, including in payment of or as security for a debt known to be the fiduciary's personal debt, or if the instrument is deposited into an account other than an account of the represented person or an account of the fiduciary in that capacity, the person who takes the instrument is treated by U.C.C. § 3-307(b)(2) as having notice of a breach of fiduciary duty. To be sure, it is logically possible that the represented person might decide to compensate the fiduciary by permitting the fiduciary so to use negotiable instruments, but it is not normal practice for instruments payable to a represented person or a fiduciary in that capacity to be used for the fiduciary's personal benefit. Id., Official Comment 3.

An agent may also represent a principal in a context that is not oriented toward the pursuit of economic advantage. In such a context, the relevant questions for a third party who interacts with the agent are whether it is reasonable to believe that the principal has authorized, consented to, or acquiesced in the agent's actions and whether the scope of the principal's consent encompasses the agent's conduct.

A third party should give considerable weight to written statements from the principal specifying what the agent is authorized to do. The third party should additionally consider whether the proposed transaction appears to be reasonably related to the principal's known business. If material doubt arises on either score, ordinarily it is reasonable to inquire further about the extent of the agent's authority. Absent circumstances that should raise questions in the mind of a reasonable third party, as a general matter there is no requirement that the third party inquire into the scope of an agent's authority.

The specific context in which a third party observes an agent is of course relevant to the reasonableness of the third party's belief. The customs or practices of a particular industry shape that context, as well as bearing upon the reasonableness of any belief that a particular agent has been authorized by the principal to do any particular act.

Industry custom, moreover, defines the context in which a third party assesses the fit between the position in which the principal has placed an agent and the particular act. In considering the significance of custom, judicial opinions often conflate the concept of apparent authority with the concept of implied actual authority. This conflation results from the fact that custom shapes an agent's reasonable interpretation of the principal's instructions just as it shapes a reasonable third party's understanding of what the agent has been authorized to do by the principal based on the principal's conduct. See § 2.02, Comment _e._ The fact that it is customary for participants in an industry to be represented by agents does not invest an agent with apparent authority to do acts other than those customary to the agent's position. Thus, agents customarily represent toy designers in presenting designs to manufacturers and customarily execute on the designer's behalf some sort of disclosure form, but designers' agents do not customarily sign away all of the designer's intellectual-property rights prior to presenting the design to a manufacturer. A well-known industry custom may also make it reasonable for a third party to believe that an agent has authority even though, unbeknownst to the third party, the principal has restricted the agent's authority, shrinking it below the industry norm. Such a custom does not supersede inferences that a reasonable third party would draw about a particular agent's authority on the basis of facts known to the third party.

**Illustrations:**

5\. P Corporation, a manufacturer of specialty food items, retains A, a commodity food broker, to purchase a large quantity of an expensive commodity, Q, for P Corporation's manufacturing needs. A enters into a contract on P Corporation's behalf to purchase a large quantity of Q from T. P Corporation does not itself communicate directly or indirectly with T concerning A's authority. It is customary in the Q trade for a broker to obtain the principal's consent before fixing a binding contract for Q because its price fluctuates sharply. It is also customary for a third party to confirm with the principal or someone else other than the broker that a broker has the principal's consent. A assures T that it will not be necessary for T to contact P Corporation because A has already done so. A does not have apparent authority to bind P Corporation to the contract, nor is it reasonable for T to believe that A has authority to represent that T has consented to the transaction.

6\. Same facts as Illustration 5, except that the custom in the Q trade is that brokers have authority to fix binding contracts for principals whom the broker has previously represented so long as the contracts are consistent in amount and in payment and delivery terms with the principal's prior purchases of Q. P Corporation, which has previously purchased Q through A, tells A not to commit it to any contract without P Corporation's consent. This limitation is known only to personnel at P Corporation and A. Disregarding P Corporation's instruction, A enters into a contract on P Corporation's behalf with T for a quantity of Q that is known to T to be consistent with P Corporation's prior purchases. A has apparent authority to make the contract. Thus, P Corporation is bound to the contract but has a claim against A for any loss suffered due to A's actions beyond A's actual authority. See § 8.09, Comment _b._

7\. Same facts as Illustration 6, except that T learns that it is widely said in the industry that P Corporation has "placed A on a short leash." A does not have apparent authority because it would not be reasonable for T to rely on industry custom in light of specific information concerning A's authority.

Some transactions by their nature should strike a dissonant chord for a reasonable third party, given the situation in which an agent has been placed, the nature of the principal or its activities, or what the third party knows of the agent's position within an organization. A basic circumstance is whether the transaction is itself legal. Industry custom also varies in the diffusion and depth of agency authority throughout an organization, that is, in the degree to which employees at lower levels commonly have authority to commit their principal to types of transactions or to any transaction. A transaction that lacks any evident connection with a principal's interests, such as a guarantee of indebtedness incurred by another party that is unrelated to any business or activity of the principal, is on its face inconsistent with the fiduciary character of the relationship between principal and agent. Similarly, a reasonable third party should realize that a transaction with content fundamental to the principal's future operations or existence requires the assent of the principal's governing board, members, or shareholders. Courts often term such transactions "extraordinary" or "novel"; what this terminology reflects is an excessive distance between the transaction and what reasonably appears to be the agent's authority to bind the principal unilaterally.

Separately, a transaction may be "novel" even if it bears some relationship to the principal's interests if the type of transaction is unprecedented in light of the principal's position, business, or activity. The question is whether the transaction is so fundamental or unusual for the type of principal that a reasonable third party would question the agent's broad discretion, although the transaction may appear to serve the principal's interests. Apart from these common-law doctrines, statutes require assent by an organization's shareholders, members, or directors for specified fundamental transactions.

**Illustration:**

8\. A is the Chief Executive Officer of P Corporation, which conducts manufacturing operations out of two comparably sized plants. A enters into a contract with a developer of theme parks to convert one of the plants into an amusement park to be operated by P Corporation. P Corporation has no comparable business operations, nor do other members of its industry. A does not have apparent authority to bind P Corporation to the contract.

_e. Reliance._ To establish that an agent acted with apparent authority, it is not necessary for the plaintiff to establish that the principal's manifestation induced the plaintiff to make a detrimental change in position, in contrast to the showing required by the estoppel doctrines stated in §§ 2.05, 2.06, 3.02, and 4.08.

Many cases stating that apparent authority requires "reliance" on the part of the plaintiff do not articulate what specifically must be shown. Successful assertions of apparent authority by third parties as the basis on which to bind a principal follow action or forbearance on the part of a third party as a result of the agent's action and the principal's manifestation. Establishing that a plaintiff took an action as a result of the principal's manifestation may also help to establish that the person to whom the manifestation was made believed it to be true. Moreover, the underlying substantive cause of action on which the third party sues the principal may require proof that the plaintiff took a specific type of action. For example, if the underlying cause of action is fraud, it is necessary for the plaintiff to establish that the defendant's misrepresentation led to a detrimental change in position.

**Illustration:**

9\. P Corporation obtains insurance on its property from I Corporation, an insurance company. P Corporation states to I Corporation that A is its agent with authority to convey all directions from P regarding the insurance policy. P directs A not to cancel the insurance. A states to I Corporation that P wishes to cancel the policy. I Corporation may treat the policy as cancelled by P Corporation. Actions taken internally within I Corporation, such as noting that the policy has been cancelled, suffice to meet any showing of reliance by I Corporation on A's apparent authority.

If a third party enters into a contract with the principal, the third party's measure of recovery is not limited to reliance damages. When an agent acts with apparent authority, its effect on the principal's legal relations with third parties is identical to the effect of actual authority.

**Illustration:**

10\. P owns a bail-bond business licensed to write bonds throughout a state. P engages agents, including A, to represent it in regions of the state. P tells A to write bonds solely for judicial proceedings in one county, Placid County. P prepares a written statement that A has authority to write bonds on its behalf in Placid County and the adjacent county, Tranquil County. P files the statement with judicial authorities in both Placid and Tranquil counties. A does not have notice that the statement includes Tranquil County. A writes a bond with a penal amount of \$ 100,000, assuring the appearance at trial of F, who has been charged with a felony in Tranquil County. As a consequence, F is released pending trial by Tranquil County authorities. F absconds and does not appear for trial. P must pay the penal amount of the bond to Tranquil County. P manifested to relevant officials of the county that A had authority to write bonds in Tranquil County. Tranquil County's recovery is not limited to costs incurred in attempting to apprehend F.

Additionally, when an agent acting with apparent authority enters into a contract with a third party that purports to bind the principal, the principal may enforce the contract against the third party. The principal's ability to enforce the contract does not depend on showing that the third party has made a detrimental change in position. The third party, by entering into the contract, has manifested assent to be bound by its terms to the principal. Both the principal and the third party are bound although the principal does not subsequently ratify the agent's conduct. See § 4.01, Comment _b._

**Illustration:**

11\. A, a literary agent, represents P, who holds the copyrights in books written by P's father, D. A's authority has been restricted by P to entering into contracts for the publication of D's nonfiction works. P manifests to T, a publisher, that A has authority with regard to all of D's works, including fiction as well as nonfiction. A enters into a contract with T for the publication of a novel written by D. T breaches the publication contract. P has a claim against T for breach of contract.

In some situations in which an agent has acted with apparent authority, the principal did not make the manifestation of authority to the party who suffered injury. In these cases, the plaintiff is a fourth party who is injured as a result of action taken by a third party, when the third party took action traceable to the principal's manifestation regarding an actor's authority as an agent.

**Illustrations:**

12\. A owns a business that manufactures a component used in commercial heating equipment. A is also a nonemployee officer of an organization, P, that sets and promulgates safety standards for heating equipment. P receives an inquiry from T as to the safety of a component manufactured by F. T plans to replace the heating equipment in its premises. The component manufactured by F is directly competitive with that manufactured by A. F's component is not deficient from a safety standpoint. Using P's letterhead, A replies to T's inquiry, stating that the component manufactured by F is unsafe. T reasonably believes that A's response to the inquiry is authorized by P. After receiving the letter, T purchases the component manufactured by A. F sues P, alleging that A acted with apparent authority and that A's actions violate the antitrust laws. It is not necessary for F to show that F relied on the appearance of authority on A's part.

13\. P Corporation is in the business of making small business loans. A, an employee of P Corporation, in the course of A's work, responds to inquiries from third parties concerning the status of individual loan accounts. A receives an inquiry from T Corporation concerning the account of Q, who has a loan outstanding with P Corporation and has applied for a separate loan from T Corporation. A has responded to prior comparable inquiries from T Corporation. A holds a grudge against Q, having invested and lost money in a business that Q started. A falsely tells T Corporation that Q's loan from P Corporation is in default, causing T Corporation to turn down Q's loan application. A has apparent authority to respond to T Corporation's inquiry. Q has a claim against P Corporation for loss suffered due to A's false statement.

_f. Undisclosed and unidentified principals._ When a third party interacts with an actor known to be someone's agent, but the third party does not know the principal's identity, the third party does not know whom the agent represents and whose legal position the agent's acts may affect. See § 1.04(2)(c), which defines unidentified principal. If the agent occupies a position customarily carrying authority to do specific types of acts on behalf of a principal, it is reasonable for the third party to believe that the agent possesses such authority even though the third party does not know the identity of the principal. The principal's identity is irrelevant to the reasonableness of a third party's belief that a principal has consented to be bound by the acts of a conventional type of agent. Appointing an agent, given a customary definition of the agent's authority, constitutes a manifestation as defined in § 1.03 to those who deal with the agent.

In contrast, apparent authority is not present when a third party believes that an interaction is with an actor who is a principal. See § 1.04(2)(b), which defines undisclosed principal. If a third party believes that an actor represents no one else's interests, the third party does not have a reasonable belief in the actor's power to affect anyone else's legal position. If the third party is in doubt about whether the actor represents only the actor's own interests, the third party does not have a basis reasonably to believe that the actor has the power to affect anyone else's legal position. What is reasonable for a third party in such circumstances is ambivalence about whether the actor has such power. If the third party's only reasonable belief is one of ambivalence, the principal's manifestations have not caused the third party reasonably to believe the agent has power to affect the legal position of another person.

**Illustrations:**

14\. A, a partner in a law firm, Q, has long represented T, a real-estate developer, in many matters. A withdraws from Q and becomes a partner in another law firm, P. When A tells T that A is now a partner in a different firm, T does not inquire further. The Practice Committee of P directs A not to undertake any new matter, including new matters for existing clients, without the prior approval of the Committee. At T's request, A undertakes to represent T in a new real-estate project. A does not seek the prior approval of P's Practice Committee. A's representation of T in connection with T's new real-estate project is apparently carrying on, in the usual way, law-firm business. As to T, A has apparent authority, and A's acts in A's representation of T bind P.

15\. A, a customhouse broker, is retained by P, an importer, to import steel that P has ordered to be shipped from Brazil. It is customary for customhouse brokers to take steps necessary to ensure that goods move through customs, including posting security for customs duties. A arranges with T, an insurance company, to post a surety bond on customary terms for the duties on P's steel. A does not disclose P's identity to T, nor does P communicate directly with T. As to T, A has apparent authority to arrange for T to post the bond. P placed A in a situation in which a reasonable person in T's position, familiar with practices relevant to importing, would be justified in assuming that A had authority to arrange a bond on behalf of A's principal.

_g. Governmental actors._ The doctrine of apparent authority generally does not apply to sovereigns and entities that have been created by sovereigns to achieve governmental ends. Unless a sovereign or entity can be estopped to deny the agent's lack of authority, such principals are bound only by acts within the scope of the agent's actual authority. The underlying principle is that a sovereign has the exclusive ability to prescribe what its creations and its agents may do; third parties who deal with national governments, quasi-governmental entities, states, counties, and municipalities take the risk of error regarding the agent's authority to a greater degree than do third parties dealing through agents with nongovernmental principals. This principle may work hardships when the third party is unaware of the nature of the principal. The principle is subject to several qualifications. First, a sovereign may waive its right to be bound only by actually authorized acts. Second, if the sovereign benefited through the third party's performance, in some jurisdictions a third party may recover the value it has conferred on the sovereign if the sovereign would otherwise be unjustly enriched. Third, in some jurisdictions estoppel is available against a sovereign or an entity created by a sovereign. More narrowly, some states estop municipal corporations from defending on the basis of an agent's lack of authority when substantial injustice would otherwise be the consequence.

If the sovereign is foreign, subject-matter jurisdiction exists in U.S. courts only as provided in the Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act. The Act provides that a foreign sovereign is not immune from jurisdiction when the action is based on a "commercial activity of the foreign state," which appears to contemplate activity in which the state itself is engaged, either directly (as through legislative or executive action) or through the acts of its agents that the state itself has authorized. See Restatement Third, The Foreign Relations Law of the United States § 453(2). Even if a sovereign benefited as a result of the agent's conduct, its unjust enrichment does not overcome its immunity to suit and create jurisdiction. However, the agent would be liable to the third party for breaching the agent's express or implied warranty of authority and because the agent's unauthorized act is not an act of the foreign state, the agent would not be immune to suit, unless the agent itself is a foreign state within the meaning of the Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act. On an agent's implied warranty of authority, see § 6.10.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency, and codifications._ Restatement Second, Agency, defines apparent authority in § 8 as "the power to affect the legal relations of another person by transactions with third persons, professedly as agent for the other, arising from and in accordance with the other's manifestations to such third persons." Under § 27, "\[e\]xcept for the execution of instruments under seal or for the conduct of transactions required by statute to be authorized in a particular way, apparent authority to do an act is created as to a third person by written or spoken words or any other conduct of the principal which, reasonably interpreted, causes the third person to believe that the principal consents to have the act done on his behalf by the person purporting to act for him." In this Restatement, the definition of apparent authority incorporates by reference the definition of manifestation in § 1.03. The consequence intended is to eliminate any inference that, to create apparent authority, a principal's manifestation must be directed to a specific third person in a communication made directly to that person. "Professedly as \[an\] agent for the other" has been deleted because its connotation is too narrow when applied to agents who hold positions within organizations. Sections 1.03 and 3.03 make explicit that a principal may create apparent authority by appointing a person to a particular position.

Cal. Civil Code § 2300 defines "ostensible agency" as arising "when the principal intentionally, or by want of ordinary care, causes a third person to believe another to be his agent who is not really employed by him." Cal. Civ. Code § 2300 (1985 & Supp. 2005). Section 2317 defines "ostensible authority" as "such as a principal, intentionally or by want of ordinary care, causes or allows a third person to believe the agent to possess." Under § 2330, "\[a\]n agent represents his principal for all purposes within the scope of his actual or ostensible authority, and all the rights and liabilities which would accrue to the agent from transactions within such limit, if they had been entered into on his own account, accrue to the principal." The Georgia code does not contain counterpart definitions, but states in § 10-6-50 that "\[t\]he agent's authority shall be construed to include all necessary and usual means for effectually executing it. Private instructions or limitations not known to persons dealing with a general agent shall not affect them. In special agencies for a particular purpose, persons dealing with the agent should examine his authority." Ga. Code Ann. § 10-6-50 (1996 & Supp. 2005). La. Civil Code Art. 3021 (Supp. 2005) states that "\[o\]ne who causes a third person to believe that another person is his mandatary is bound to the third person who in good faith contracts with the putative mandatary."

See also Aaron Lipson, Whose Fool Is It Anyway: Updating Apparent Authority for Today's Business World, [_33 Ga. L. Rev. 1219, 1243 (1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3YGC-D1Y0-00CW-G09P-00000-00&context=1530671) (noting uniformity and consistency in formulations of apparent authority among jurisdictions studied).

_b. Terminology._ On the usage of "usual authority" in England and Commonwealth, see Francis M.B. Reynolds, Bowstead & Reynolds on Agency 92-94 (17th ed. 2001).

_c. Rationale._ For the basic proposition that a principal is not accountable simply because the agent has succeeded in misleading the third party about the extent or nature of the agent's authority, see [_Dr. Beck & Co. v. General Elec. Co., 210 F.Supp. 86, 90 (S.D.N.Y.1962)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-J4H0-003B-20TV-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_317 F.2d 538 (2d Cir.1963)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-2NC0-0039-Y1Y8-00000-00&context=1530671). A principal may create apparent authority by knowingly permitting an agent to exercise authority, without directly communicating with the third party. See [_Century Fin. Servs. Group, Ltd. v. First Bank, 996 S.W.2d 92, 94 (Mo.App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WST-KJK0-0039-40NM-00000-00&context=1530671). For the proposition that a principal is not liable on the basis of apparent authority if the agent steals indicia of authority or obtains them through fraud, see [_Minskoff v. American Express Travel Related Servs. Co., 98 F.3d 703, 709 (2d Cir.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-06C0-006F-M1VP-00000-00&context=1530671) (apparent authority in connection with use of holder's credit card inapplicable when card transferred without holder's consent, including instances of loss, theft, and fraud; cardholder subject to liability for only those fraudulent charges incurred prior to reasonable opportunity to examine billing statement). Cf. [_DBI Architects, P.C. v. American Express Travel-Related Servs. Co., 388 F.3d 886, 894 (D.C.Cir.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4DRX-3R20-0038-X531-00000-00&context=1530671) (remanding for determination of when fraudulent user of holder's credit card acted with apparent authority in card use; cardholder paid thousands of dollars each month with consequence that "at some point the cardholder led the card issuer reasonably to believe that the fraudulent user had authority to use its card").

For an example of an undisclosed limit on an agent's actual authority, see [_State v. Korecky, 777 A.2d 927, 936 (N.J.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:43NW-W7S0-0039-44N8-00000-00&context=1530671) (court that continued bail subject to conduct condition had no reason to suspect that surety had limited bail bondsman's authority to amend bond to add imposition of condition unrelated to court appearance; bondsman had apparent authority to agree to amendment).

The first use of the language "reputation of authority" may have been in [_Hawaiian Paradise Park Corp. v. Friendly Broad. Co., 414 F.2d 750, 756 (9th Cir.1969)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-T1G0-0039-Y3B3-00000-00&context=1530671). An agent's reputation of authority may be based on prior similar conduct in which the principal acquiesces, as observed by the third party. See, e.g, [_Powell v. MVE Holdings, Inc., 626 N.W.2d 451, 457 (Minn.App.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4327-8Y50-0039-425V-00000-00&context=1530671) (employee could reasonably believe that corporation's president had authority in 1997 to agree to redeem employee's stock when president redeemed stock of 6 other employees at the same price in 1996); [_Indosuez Int'l Fin. B.V. v. National Reserve Bank, 774 N.E.2d 696, 700-701 (N.Y.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45SR-VNR0-0039-406F-00000-00&context=1530671) (plaintiff could reasonably rely on unilateral authority of chairperson of defendant bank to enter into forward currency-exchange transaction when chairperson had executed 5 prior transactions with plaintiff's parent corporation).

On the contractual irrelevance of an offeror's unexpressed intention, see E. Allan Farnsworth, Changing Your Mind: The Law of Regretted Decisions 31 (1998) ("\[i\]n deciding whether an expression is one of commitment, courts commonly use an objective and not a subjective standard of interpretation, asking not what the promisor actually intended by the words in question but what a reasonable promisee would have understood by those words. Others are entitled to judge by appearances, and even if you do not mean to make a commitment you may be bound if a reasonable person in the circumstances would have understood you to be making one.").

_d. Reasonable belief._ For the proposition that the perspective of the third party governs when the question is the presence of apparent authority, see [_AgriStor Leasing v. Farrow, 826 F.2d 732, 738 (8th Cir. 1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-88V0-001B-K075-00000-00&context=1530671) (genuine issue of material fact present as to apparent authority of sales person for equipment dealer to contract on behalf of equipment lessor; lessor gave dealer its forms for lease application, lessor's direct representative accompanied dealer's salesperson to lessee's farm prior to lessor's approval of lease application, lessor's letter to lessee stated dealer would make contact concerning necessary documentation, and dealer's salesperson in fact reappeared with lessor-provided forms to be signed).

On federal admiralty doctrine, see [_Karavos Compania Naviera, S.A. v. Atlantica Exp. Corp., 588 F.2d 1, 10 (2d Cir.1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-X5C0-0039-M288-00000-00&context=1530671) (dictum that inquiry must be made into authority). It is an interesting question whether the admiralty rule mirrors industry custom or has shaped it. See _Herlofson Mgmt. A/S v. Ministry of Supply, Kingdom of Jordan, 765 F.Supp. 78, 89 (S.D.N.Y.1991)_ (observing that custom in shipping industry requires broker to obtain principal's consent before fixing a binding contract); cf. [_Karavos, 588 F.2d at 10_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-X5C0-0039-M288-00000-00&context=1530671) (noting that "not every employee of a trading company has authority to fix a time charter, let alone a series of such charters"; as a matter of law, actions of trading company insufficient to create apparent authority in nonemployee agent when trading company permitted agent to work in its office, receive telexes there, and use telephone in effort to find vessel for trading company to charter). Alternatively, _Herlofson_ may reflect, not a special admiralty doctrine as such, but circumstances in which it is reasonable to make further inquiry.

A third party's knowledge that an agent lacks actual authority to bind the principal to a transaction defeats the reasonableness of believing the agent to be authorized in [_Hatfield v. Control Sys. Int'l, 21 F.Supp.2d 546, 550 (D.S.C.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3TNH-P8G0-0038-Y1VB-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _155 F.3d 558 (4th Cir.1998)_ (third parties could not reasonably have relied on agent's representations of right to sell equipment when third parties had actual knowledge agent lacked any such authority). See also [_Bayless v. Christie, Manson & Woods Int'l, Inc., 2 F.3d 347, 353 (10th Cir.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-DKX0-003B-P23F-00000-00&context=1530671) (auction house aware through terms of consignment agreement that partner's authority to sell painting owned by partnership was limited to effecting transaction from which partnership received sale proceeds and nothing in record suggests partnership enhanced partner's authority; partner acted without apparent authority in directing auction house to pay sale proceeds to him individually); [_Sphere Drake Ins. Ltd. v. All Am. Life Ins. Co., 300 F.Supp.2d 606, 619-620 (N.D.Ill.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4BDM-Y800-0038-Y038-00000-00&context=1530671) (although customary practice in reinsurance does not expect third parties to monitor whether reinsurer's agent exceeds premium limit, ceding insurer's awareness that agent had exceeded known premium limit triggered duty to inquire into extent of agent's authority); [_Sipes v. Kinetra, L.L.C., 137 F.Supp.2d 901, 907-908 (E.D.Mich.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:42VM-8RN0-0038-Y3PG-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_55 Fed.Appx. 322 (6th Cir. 2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:47TW-BG40-0038-X192-00000-00&context=1530671) (LLC's CEO acted without apparent authority in granting equity to executive when executive had reviewed copy of LLC's formation agreement, under which CEO clearly lacked unilateral authority to grant equity); _Streetman v. Benchmark Bank, 890 S.W.2d 212 (Tex.App.1994)_ (bank executive did not have apparent authority to promise customers that bank would honor all overdrafts on customers' account when customers knew that banks have lending limits and thus that executive's authority would be subject to limits). The best-known illustration is Armagas Ltd. v. Mundogas S.A. \[1986\] 1 A.C. 717, 777 (H.L.), in which the third party knew that the agent, a corporate vice president, lacked authority to bind the corporation to a ship charter to run for three years. The court held that the third party could not reasonably rely on the vice president's claim (which was false) that he had been granted such authority, and no representation to augment the appearance of authority was made for which the corporation's senior management was accountable. [_Id. at 778_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-88V0-001B-K075-00000-00&context=1530671).

For a recent and exceptional case reaching a contrary result, see [_Menard, Inc. v. Dage-MTI, Inc., 726 N.E.2d 1206 (Ind.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:402S-YK00-0039-40FT-00000-00&context=1530671). In _Menard,_ the third party dealt with a corporation through its president whom the third party knew had required specific authorization from the board of directors to commit the corporation to prior comparable transactions. Nonetheless, the third party entered into a real-estate transaction although the president lacked such authorization and although the third party had no notice of any circumstance suggesting that the corporation had augmented the scope of its president's authority to take action unilaterally. A majority of the _Menard_ court, while acknowledging that the president acted without either actual or apparent authority, subjected the corporation to liability on the basis of the president's inherent agency power. Menard's application of inherent agency power appears to outrun formulations of the concept in Restatement Second, Agency. Under § 8A, Comment _b,_ an instance of inherent agency power subjects a principal to liability when "a general agent does something similar to what he is authorized to do, but in violation of orders." Under the specific formulation in § 161, a principal is subject to liability "for acts done on his account which usually accompany or are incidental to transactions which the agent is authorized to conduct if, although they are forbidden by the principal, the other party reasonably believes that the agent is authorized to do them and has no notice that he is not so authorized." Even assuming that the transaction to which the president committed the corporation in _Menard_ could be characterized as one usually accompanying or incidental to authorized actions, prior dealings between the third party and the president appear to have placed the third party on notice of limits on the president's unilateral authority. _Menard_ effectively shifts to the principal the burden of notifying third parties that it has not removed restrictions on its agents' actual authority. For a critique of inherent agency power and _Menard,_ see John Dwight Ingram, Inherent Agency Powers: A Mistaken Concept Which Should Be Discarded, [_29 Okla. City U. L. Rev. 583 (2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4G4N-36Y0-00CV-209X-00000-00&context=1530671).

Custom works against the presence of apparent authority as to a third party who is a regular participant in the industry in [_FASA Corp. v. Playmates Toys, Inc., 892 F.Supp. 1061 (N.D.Ill.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RV4-96N0-001T-50C0-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent representing toy designer did not have apparent authority to sign waiver of all claims against toy manufacturer that might arise from intellectual-property disputes prior to making presentation to manufacturer; signing away all intellectual-property rights not standard behavior for designers' agents despite custom that agents executed some form of disclosure prior to making presentation). See also _Herlofson, 765 F.Supp. at 89_ (custom in shipping industry that broker cannot fix binding contract without principal's approval); [_Maurice Elec. Supply Co. v. Anderson Safeway Guard Rail Corp., 632 F.Supp. 1082, 1090 n.13 (D.D.C.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:426H-H1D0-0038-Y24F-00000-00&context=1530671) (customary practice of other agents in field relevant to apparent authority of manufacturer's agent to bind manufacturer to contract); [_Flame Cut Steel Prods. Co. v. Performance Foams & Coatings, Inc., 46 F.Supp.2d 222, 230 (E.D.N.Y.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WDC-VP30-0038-Y34V-00000-00&context=1530671) (noting widespread practice of dealers extending manufacturers' warranties to customers; reasonable customer would not normally infer from receipt of manufacturer's warranty that manufacturer is responsible for dealer's oral representations concerning warranty). See also H. Edwin Anderson, III, Shipbrokers' Authority and Ability to Bind Principals: At the Juncture of Chartering and Agency, [_31 J. Mar. L. and Com. 89, 103 (2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3YDP-44Y0-00CV-J15M-00000-00&context=1530671) (custom in charter-party negotiations is that principals rarely communicate directly with each other, reducing likelihood that shipbroker will act with apparent authority as to terms of charter).

Custom supports the presence of apparent authority when an agent's act is within the limit of authority standard for agents in similar position and endeavors. See [_In re Victory Corrugated Container Corp., 183 B.R. 373 (Bankr.D.N.J.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-N9S0-003B-X149-00000-00&context=1530671) (auctioneer has apparent authority to sell equipment in sale after auction in which no bids were received even when actual authority was restricted to selling through auction because auctioneers commonly sell items in private sales following auction; person acquainted with business of auctioneering and unaware of any restriction on auctioneer's authority would assume auctioneer had post-auction sale authority). _Victory Corrugated Container_ notes that the party who placed the equipment with the auctioneer was familiar with auction practices. It does not appear that that person expressed any dissent to the possibility of a private postauction sale, either to the auctioneer or in the tags it placed on the equipment to be sold, which the court treats as general manifestations of the auctioneer's authority to sell, without regard to the mode of sale.

Industry custom is among the factors supporting the presence of apparent authority in [_Essco Geometric, Inc. v. Harvard Indus., Inc., 46 F.3d 718, 726 (8th Cir.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-H4D0-001T-D34F-00000-00&context=1530671) (apparent authority of purchasing manager to bind manufacturer to exclusive non-cancelable requirements contract; reasonable jury could conclude from appearance of authority created by industry custom that manager had authority to bind, combined with evidence of manager's position and prior acts); [_Property Advisory Group, Inc. v. Bevona, 718 F.Supp. 209, 211-212 (S.D.N.Y.1989)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-BMS0-0054-42G8-00000-00&context=1530671) (managing agent for office building had apparent authority to bind principal to collective bargaining agreement; position of managing agent also relevant to presence of apparent authority). Custom that associates differentiated degrees of authority with agents in particular positions undermines the reasonableness of believing that an agent has authority to a degree exceeding that customary with the agent's position. See, e.g., [_General Am. Life Ins. Co. v. AmSouth Bank, 100 F.3d 893, 898 (11th Cir.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-YK40-006F-M2J1-00000-00&context=1530671) (evidence supporting existence of custom that general insurance agents have authority to issue insurance policies does not support proposition that general insurance agent had apparent authority for all purposes, in particular to indorse checks made payable to insurer). Custom is not a substitute for some manifestation traceable to the principal. For an example where none is evident in the court's analysis, see [_Seacoast Realty Co. v. West Long Branch Borough, 14 N.J. Tax 197, 205 (Tax 1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SF5-PS30-000H-S05D-00000-00&context=1530671) (person familiar with litigation of local property-tax matters could reasonably assume counsel had authority to settle in light of routine practice of settlement through telephonic discussion among counsel; relevant also that counsel represented principal throughout litigation, engaged in prior settlement negotiations with third party's counsel, and telephoned to inquire as to status of prior offer of settlement).

For examples of the conflation of apparent authority with implied actual authority, see, e.g., [_Property Advisory Group, 718 F.Supp. at 212-213_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-BMS0-0054-42G8-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Ebasco Servs., Inc. v. Pennsylvania Power & Light Co., 402 F.Supp. 421, 449-450 (E.D.Pa.1975)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-MF50-0054-64KM-00000-00&context=1530671).

Many cases also tie the significance of custom closely to apparent authority based on the position occupied by the agent. See, e.g., [_Laborers Int'l Union v. HSA Contractors, 728 F.Supp. 519, 526 (E.D.Wis.1989)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-9PN0-0054-44T1-00000-00&context=1530671) (absence of custom in labor relations that could lead employer to believe local union generally acts as agent for international organization); [_Epstein v. Corporacion Peruana de Vapores, 325 F.Supp. 535 (S.D.N.Y.1971)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-PX00-003B-327V-00000-00&context=1530671) (lack of custom to support apparent authority of ship's captain to buy cigarettes and liquor in excess of ship's necessities; no evidence to support custom of ship's captain's authority to buy necessities for a company's other ships); [_Johnson v. Shenandoah Life Ins. Co., 281 So. 2d 636, 640 (Ala.1973)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-1T60-003C-92BS-00000-00&context=1530671) (not customary in insurance industry for agent to lend money for insurance company); [_Nichols v. Prudential Ins. Co., 851 S.W.2d 657, 664 (Mo.App. 1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-6SH0-003F-C010-00000-00&context=1530671) (absence of evidence of custom in insurance industry that "sales manager" has authority of "general manager"); [_Costco Wholesale Corp. v. World Wide Licensing Corp., 898 P.2d 347, 353 (Wash.App.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-WXS0-003F-W3TG-00000-00&context=1530671) (absence of evidence that broker customarily has authority to negotiate and settle warranty dispute by agreeing to a substantial refund).

Legality of a proposed transaction is treated as relevant to, but not dispositive of, an agent's apparent authority in [_Orchard Group, Inc. v. Konica Med. Corp., 135 F.3d 421, 427 (6th Cir.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-R950-0038-X1DP-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_First Valley Bank of Los Fresnos v. Martin, 144 S.W.3d 466, 471 (Tex.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4D7M-1W70-0039-455B-00000-00&context=1530671) (director of bank did not act with apparent authority to waive bank's lien on collateral by helping debtor to move collateral; oral release of collateral or forgiveness of debt would violate federal banking regulations); [_Lehman Bros. Commercial Corp. v. Minmetals Int'l Non-Ferrous Metals Trading Co., 179 F.Supp.2d 118, 148 (S.D.N.Y. 2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:41NS-RRY0-0038-Y2V5-00000-00&context=1530671) (question of fact whether employee of trading conglomerate acted with apparent authority in investment transactions violative of Chinese law).

For an example of a transaction characterized as "extraordinary," see [_General Overseas Films, Ltd. v. Robin Int'l, Inc., 542 F.Supp. 684 (S.D.N.Y.1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-CRM0-0039-S0TY-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _718 F.2d 1085 (2d Cir.1983)_ (corporation's treasurer lacked apparent authority to execute guarantee of indebtedness incurred by corporation with no evident relation to treasurer's corporation; bylaw stating treasurer's authority conferred power to sign "evidences of indebtedness," does not encompass guarantees, which are collateral to a debt itself). The prospect of an "extraordinary" transaction that only directors or shareholders could authorize appears in [_Lee v. Jenkins Bros., 268 F.2d 357, 370 (2d Cir.1959)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-SRR0-003B-024B-00000-00&context=1530671) (promise by corporation's president to pay pension to newly hired manager "regardless of what happens" was not extraordinary; existence of apparent authority beyond an "extraordinary act" is "simply a matter of fact").

Most transactions that courts term "novel" are also characterized as "extraordinary." The distinction suggested in this Comment is supported by _FDIC v. Providence Coll., 115 F.3d 136 (2d Cir.1997)_. In _Providence College,_ the college's Vice President for Business Affairs executed a guarantee for loans made to a building contractor who was removing asbestos from college buildings. Although the court concluded that the purpose of the guarantee may have served a vital interest of the college, the court held the lender could not have reasonably believed that the vice president had authority to guarantee loans to the contractor. No evidence demonstrated that the college had ever before guaranteed loans to contractors working on campus or that nonprofit educational institutions regularly or occasionally made such guarantees. Additionally, the guarantee in _Providence College_ may have been "extraordinary" as a result of its breadth, which encompassed all indebtedness, up to the amount of the guarantee, including later-incurred debt, regardless of the use the borrower made of the loan proceeds.

For an illustration in a novel context of the basic point that a third party's belief is not reasonable when the content of a principal's manifestation does not encompass an agent's conduct, see [_CSX Transportation, Inc. v. Recovery Express, Inc., 415 F.Supp.2d 6 (D.Mass.2006)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4J5N-0VJ0-TVVD-N2PB-00000-00&context=1530671) (unreasonable as a matter of law for third party to believe individual had authority to bind corporation to purchase out-of-service railcars on basis that individual communicated using an e-mail address assigned by corporation with its domain name; "\[g\]ranting an e-mail domain name, by itself, does not clothe the recipient with carte blanche authority to act on behalf of the \[grantor\]. Were this so, every subordinate employee with a company e-mail address--down to the night watchman--could bind a company to the same contracts as the president.").

_e. Reliance._ Some codifications of common-law agency include third-party reliance as a defining element of liability based on apparent (or "ostensible") authority. See, e.g., Cal. Civ. Code § 2334 ("\[a\] principal is bound by acts of his agent, under a merely ostensible authority, to those persons only who have in good faith, and without want of ordinary care, incurred a liability or parted with value, upon the faith thereof"); Ala. Code Ann. § 8-2-6 (same); Mont. Code Ann. § 28-10-606 (same); N.D. Cent. Code § 3-03-03 (same); S.D. Code § 59-6-3 (same). The Georgia Code does not explicitly define a doctrine of apparent or ostensible authority and does not refer to reliance. Cf. Ga. Code Ann. § 10-6-50 ("\[t\]he agent's authority shall be construed to include all necessary and usual means for effectually executing it. Private instructions or limitations not known to persons dealing with a general agent shall not affect them. In special agencies for a particular purpose, persons dealing with the agent should examine his authority").

Cases that explicitly follow the distinction in this Restatement and Restatement Second, Agency, between the elements requisite to apparent authority and those requisite to estoppel include _Tedesco v. Gentry Dev., Inc., 540 So. 2d 960, 964 (La. 1989)_ (noting that apparent authority may be available theory although claimant is unable to show a change in position); [_Wiggins v. Barrett & Assocs., 669 P.2d 1132, 1140 (Or.1983)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-X4W0-003F-Y3W8-00000-00&context=1530671) (noting that apparent authority "does not include all of the usually stated elements of equitable estoppel, particularly with regard to the requirement of change of position. . . ."). Some cases treat apparent authority, when present, as a circumstance that creates a "right to rely." See, e.g., [_Tricat Indus., Inc. v. Harper, 748 A.2d 48, 57 (Md.App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YSD-2630-0039-44YC-00000-00&context=1530671).

Cases in many jurisdictions refer to a required showing of reliance when a third party asserts an agent acted with apparent authority. This was so as well in the decades prior to Restatement Second, Agency. See Restatement Second, Agency § 8, Reporter's Note. The analysis in this Restatement, as in Restatement Second, Agency, does not follow these cases. A representative sample includes: [_Doxsee Sea Clam Co. v. Brown, 13 F.3d 550, 553-554 (2d Cir. 1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-9JD0-003B-P0KP-00000-00&context=1530671) (issue whether claims adjuster had apparent authority to accept notice of claim in worker's-compensation case under maritime law; court states that principal is bound by agent's conduct "if a third party reasonably relies" on conduct by the principal that gives the appearance agent has authority); [_Blackman v. Hustler Magazine, Inc., 620 F.Supp. 1501, 1513 (D.D.C.1984)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-C990-0054-50J0-00000-00&context=1530671), rev'd in part on other grounds, [_800 F.2d 1160 (D.C.Cir.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-1M90-0039-P1YB-00000-00&context=1530671) (contract claim by third party must fail due to lack of detrimental reliance; "to rely on the doctrine of apparent authority, it must be proved that a party relied to its detriment on the appearance that an agent was authorized to act. . . . Nothing exists save sheer speculation as to whether there was damage and the extent of that injury, if any"); [_Billops v. Magness Constr. Co., 391 A.2d 196, 198 (Del.Supr.1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRT-7VB0-003C-K2M1-00000-00&context=1530671) (tort action based on alleged extortion by agent with apparent authority; "a litigant must show reliance on the indicia of authority originated by the principal" to establish chain of liability to principal); [_Steen v. G.S. Andrews, 223 Miss. 694, 698, 78 So. 2d 881, 882 (1955)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRN-10H0-000H-33H7-00000-00&context=1530671) (apparent authority requires reliance by third party and "a change of position by the third person to his detriment").

A plaintiffs lack of "reliance" is sometimes taken to indicate that the plaintiff did not reasonably believe an agent to be authorized to bind the principal. See, e.g., [_Veranda Beach Club, Ltd. P'ship v. Western Sur. Co., 936 F.2d 1364, 1378 (1st Cir.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-CD20-008H-V0DC-00000-00&context=1530671). Reliance is an element of estoppel and relevant when the plaintiffs theory of liability is grounded in estoppel. See, e.g., [_General Am. Life Ins. Co. v. AmSouth Bank, 100 F.3d 893, 899-900 (11th Cir.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-YK40-006F-M2J1-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 9 is based on _Chemical Bank v. Affiliated FM Ins. Co., 169 F.3d 121, 129 (2d Cir.)_, vacated in part and superseded on other grounds, [_196 F.3d 373 (2d Cir.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XVG-K1K0-0038-X16G-00000-00&context=1530671).

If a plaintiff alleges breach of contract based on a contract made by an agent who reasonably appeared to be authorized, the claim is a contract claim, not a tort claim, although the agent's assertion of authority is false. See [_LTX Corp. v. Daewoo Corp., 979 F.Supp. 51, 55 (D.Mass.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRH-7Y30-0038-Y1BW-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _141 F.3d 1149 (1st Cir.1998)_.

Cases that articulate a "reliance" requirement often make interchangeable use of terminology that refers to reliance and terminology that refers to tracing. For a leading example, see [_Ford v. Unity Hosp., 346 N.Y.S.2d 238, 244 (N.Y.1973)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-VJY0-003C-C1DP-00000-00&context=1530671). The opinion states that "the existence of 'apparent authority' depends on a factual showing that the third party relied upon the misrepresentations of the agent because of some misleading conduct on the part of the principal--not the agent." Id. This sentence is followed immediately, however, by a quotation from a treatise stating that "'the principal can be held liable under the doctrine only where he was responsible for the appearance of authority . . . \[t\]he apparent authority for which the principal may be held liable must be traceable to him. . . .'" Id., quoting 2 N.Y. Jur., Agency § 89, at 253.

It is not clear within some jurisdictions what it is that must be shown to establish "reliance." Authority conflicts as to whether New York, in particular, requires a showing of detrimental reliance to establish apparent authority. Compare [_General Overseas Films, Ltd. v. Robin Int'l, Inc., 542 F.Supp. 684, 688 n.2 (S.D.N.Y.1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-CRM0-0039-S0TY-00000-00&context=1530671) (stating that "the doctrine of apparent authority appears to require detrimental reliance--that is a change in position--by the plaintiff on the basis of the agent's misrepresentation of his authority," noting some variation as to whether New York authority requires the detrimental reliance to be, additionally, on the principal's conduct as well as the agent's conduct) with _Chemical Bank v. Affiliated FM Ins. Co., 169 F.3d 121, 129 (2d Cir.1999)_, vacated in part and superseded on other grounds, [_196 F.3d 373 (2d Cir.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XVG-K1K0-0038-X16G-00000-00&context=1530671) (stating that under doctrine of apparent authority, principal may be bound by conduct of agent "where a third party reasonably relies on such acts" of principal that reasonably give appearance of agent's authority to conduct a particular transaction). The practical consequences of this disparity have not been the subject of judicial scrutiny. See, e.g., [_Lawton v. Nyman, 62 F.Supp.2d 533, 538 (D.R.I.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XB0-GF10-0038-Y2BG-00000-00&context=1530671) (in action alleging federal securities fraud, court states that "reliance upon the appearance of authority is a necessary element in establishing apparent authority"; in actions alleging breach of contract, plaintiff's prospective loss of benefit of bargain if principal is not bound to contract made by apparently authorized agent satisfies requirement that plaintiff show a loss will be suffered). In _General Overseas Films,_ the third party did not establish that its belief in the agent's authority was reasonable. In _Chemical Bank,_ the third party, an insurer, canceled the principals' policy when their insurance broker informed the insurer that substitute coverage had been procured. It is not evident how the insurer's action would constitute reliance that is detrimental to the insurer, although it surely was an action taken as a result of the agent's communication to the insurer.

Illustration 10 is based on [_State v. Delacruz, 977 S.W.2d 95 (Mo.App. 1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3TVF-VMM0-0039-430R-00000-00&context=1530671). On the availability of an expectation-based measure of damages, see [_Linkage Corp. v. Trustees of Boston Univ., 679 N.E.2d 191 (Mass.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RHC-FC90-003C-V00W-00000-00&context=1530671).

A principal's ability to use apparent authority to bind the third party is supported by, e.g., [_Equitable Variable Life Ins. Co. v. Wood, 362 S.E.2d 741 (Va.1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-VV00-003D-51NG-00000-00&context=1530671) (decedent acted consistently with belief that insurance agent had apparent authority to receive notice cancelling policy; decedent's beneficiary bound by decedent's act). Restatement Second, Agency § 8, Comment _d,_ explicitly treats apparent authority as a basis on which "the principal becomes immediately a contracting party, with both rights and liabilities to the third person. . . ." Accord, id. § 292.

Illustration 12 is based on [_American Soc'y of Mech. Eng'rs v. Hydrolevel Corp., 456 U.S. 556 (1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-5J60-003B-S04K-00000-00&context=1530671).

In a tort action based on misrepresentation of fact, New York requires that the person injured by a misrepresentation and the person who relies on it be one and the same. See, e.g., [_Cement & Concrete Workers Dist. Council Welfare Fund v. Lollo, 148 F.3d 194, 196 (2d Cir.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3T3R-WN10-0038-X1JW-00000-00&context=1530671). But see [_Randi W. v. Muroc Joint Unified School District, 60 Cal.Rptr.2d 263 (Cal.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RJN-2DM0-0039-44GP-00000-00&context=1530671) (student who alleges sexual assault by school administrator need not allege that she personally relied on misrepresentations in letters of recommendation written by administrator's former employer, but only that injury resulted from action taken by recipient of misrepresentations); [_Davis v. Board of County Comm'rs of Dona Ana County, 987 P.2d 1172 (N.M.App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3X6B-43G0-0039-43GR-00000-00&context=1530671) (adopting principle of Restatement Second, Torts § 311, court holds that employer who elects to give references to former employees owes duty of care to persons who foreseeably may be harmed by negligent misrepresentation or misleading half-truth).

For a situation comparable to Illustration 13, see [_Jones v. Federated Fin. Reserve Co., 144 F.3d 961, 966 (6th Cir.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SS0-5N40-0038-X01W-00000-00&context=1530671) (plaintiffs ex-spouse discovered plaintiffs address when ex-spouse's friend, an employee of defendant, requested plaintiffs credit history from credit bureau; plaintiff may hold defendant liable for breach of Fair Credit Reporting Act, [_15 U.S.C. § 1681 et seq._](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8S9D-W4W2-8T6X-71PW-00000-00&context=1530671), "\[i\]f the trier of fact finds that \[defendant\] created an appearance of authority that caused the credit reporting agency reasonably and prudently to believe that \[defendant's employee\] had made a proper request for a permissible purpose, and that there was reliance on \[defendant's employee's\] apparent authority").

Although many cases state that a plaintiff must have relied on the appearance of authority, few appear to involve a situation in which the immediate audience for the agent's statement was someone other than the plaintiff. For an example, see [_Jaeger v. Western Rivers Fly Fisher, 855 F. Supp. 1217, 1220 (D.Utah 1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-H6V0-003B-V4GG-00000-00&context=1530671) (plaintiff unable to establish apparent authority of alleged agent for undisclosed principal; plaintiff was guest on a fishing expedition organized by plaintiffs friend and plaintiff sustained physical injuries due to fishing guide's negligence, but no representations as to relationship between guide and tour outfitter were made to plaintiff).

_f. Undisclosed and unidentified principals._ A few cases refer to the "apparent authority" of an agent for an undisclosed principal. See [_Department of Banking & Fin. v. Davis, 416 N.W.2d 566, 569-570 (Neb. 1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-5C30-003D-B40M-00000-00&context=1530671) (undisclosed principal, the assignee of a mortgage, granted "the apparent authority to act as its agent" to original mortgagee by allowing original mortgagee "to represent itself as mortgagee with full ownership of the mortgage and the secured debt and full authority to service the debt"); [_McDaniel v. Hensons', Inc., 493 S.E.2d 529, 530 (Ga.App.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S69-BJB0-0039-41XH-00000-00&context=1530671) (jury question whether agent had either actual or apparent authority on behalf of undisclosed principal in contracting for improvements to land owned by principal); [_Handy v. C.I.T. Corp., 197 N.E. 64 (Mass.1935)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-C460-003C-T0P4-00000-00&context=1530671) (automobile dealer who was in fact agent of undisclosed principal had apparent authority as to transactions in usual and ordinary course of business conducted with third party who reasonably believed agent to be owner).

Illustration 15 is based on [_Old Republic Ins. Co. v. Hansa World Cargo Serv. Inc., 51 F.Supp.2d 457, 475 (S.D.N.Y.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WNV-V910-0038-Y0RD-00000-00&context=1530671). See also _Lake Charles Stevedores, Inc. v.
