Professor Vladimir Popov M/V, 199 F.3d 220, 228 (5th Cir.1999)_ (apparent authority may be present when third party knows that actor acts for another but does not know its identity; stevedoring service had no reason to, and did not, believe that shipper of rice was acting on behalf of vessel's owner, or anyone separate from itself, when it retained stevedoring service to load rice on vessel).

_g. Governmental actors._ The principle insulating the federal government from contract claims based on an agent's apparent authority extends to corporations created by Congress "for defined ends." See [_Federal Crop Ins. Corp. v. Merrill, 332 U.S. 380 (1947)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-JTH0-003B-S4X7-00000-00&context=1530671). The _Merrill_ doctrine has been held to protect entities created by Congress that have since been privatized. See [_DuPuis v. Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corp., 879 F.Supp. 139 (D.Me.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-VTJ0-001T-50HW-00000-00&context=1530671) (noting that stock of defendant trades on New York Stock Exchange). The _Merrill_ doctrine has been extended beyond contract claims in [_Mendrala v. Crown Mortgage Co., 955 F.2d 1132 (7th Cir. 1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-5XP0-008H-V33C-00000-00&context=1530671) (quiet-title claim); [_Deerman v. Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corp., 955 F.Supp. 1393 (N.D.Ala.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-H9H0-00B1-F28C-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _140 F.3d 1043 (11th Cir.1998)_ (claim of deceptive trade practices under state statute); see also [_Hinton v. Federal Nat'l Mortgage Ass'n, 945 F.Supp. 1052, 1057-1059 (S.D.Tex. 1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-7XC0-006F-P4CG-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _137 F.3d 1350 (5th Cir. 1998)_ (broad statements denying rights to mortgagor on basis of servicing guide between lender and purchaser of loan and state deceptive-trade-practices act). In at least two circuits, federal-government corporations (like the defendant in _DuPuis)_ are not subject to the Federal Tort Claims Act if the court determines that the corporation was not acting primarily as an instrumentality or agency of the [_United States. Mendrala v. Crown Mortgage Co., 955 F.2d 1132 (7th Cir.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-5XP0-008H-V33C-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Goddard v. District of Columbia Redev. Land Agency, 287 F.2d 343, 345 (D.C.Cir.)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-4YC0-0039-Y0VS-00000-00&context=1530671), cert. denied, _366 U.S. 910 (1961)_. But see [_Lewis v. United States, 680 F.2d 1239 (9th Cir.1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-3BM0-003B-G4DP-00000-00&context=1530671) (Federal Reserve banks are not federal instrumentalities for purposes of FTCA). A governmental corporation that is foreign, i.e., not a creation of the government of the United States, is not comparably entitled to immunity unless it is an organ of the foreign state or at least 50 percent of it is owned by a foreign state. See [_28 U.S.C. § 1603(b)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SG9-5HW2-D6RV-H0M4-00000-00&context=1530671) (limiting sovereign immunity). For a critical treatment of these doctrines as they apply to U.S. governmental corporations, see Michael Froomkin, Reinventing the Government Corporation, [_1995 U. Ill. L. Rev. 543_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S3T-WBV0-00CW-507P-00000-00&context=1530671).

In actions against states and municipalities, some courts hold the defendant liable only if the agent's conduct or statements were actually authorized. See [_Miller v. McKinnon, 124 P.2d 34 (Cal.1942)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-91K0-003D-W0K9-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Healey v. Town of New Durham Zoning Bd. of Adjustment, 665 A.2d 360 (N.H. 1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-4GW0-003G-B09X-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Wagner v. Armbruster, 671 N.E.2d 630 (Ohio App.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-WYV0-003C-8122-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Daniels v. City of Goose Creek, 431 S.E.2d 256 (S.C.App.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RVJ-CFN0-003G-C216-00000-00&context=1530671); [_School Bd. of Amherst County v. Burley, 302 S.E.2d 53 (Va.1983)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-VX10-003D-52HF-00000-00&context=1530671). Cf. [_Lakeside Equip. Corp. v. Town of Chester, 865 A.2d 422 (Vt.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4D63-9230-0039-42V9-00000-00&context=1530671) (apparent-authority doctrine has only limited applicability against municipality; court notes absence of conduct or communication by municipality on basis of which vendor could conclude that operator of waste-water plant had authority to purchase replacement clarifiers for plant). But see _Enron Equip. Procurement Corp. v. M/V Titan 2, 82 F.Supp.2d 602, 609 (W.D.La.1999)_ (holding that agent of foreign sovereign acted with actual authority on basis that sovereign's representatives acquiesced in representations as to agent's authority to grant lien and security interest made in their presence; agent's authority also implicit because it was necessary to negotiation of agreement to repair ship, as to which authority was explicitly granted to agent).

Several courts appear to permit the use of estoppel when an agent lacked actual authority, although the standards articulated vary considerably. On estoppel when the state has made an affirmative misrepresentation, see [_Arkansas Dept. of Human Servs. v. Estate of Lewis, 922 S.W.2d 712 (Ark.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-X070-003C-1023-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Naegele Outdoor Adver., Inc. v. Minneapolis Cnty. Dev. Agency, 551 N.W.2d 235 (Minn.App.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-5BN0-003F-V490-00000-00&context=1530671). On estoppel to prevent manifest injustice, see [_Simpson v. Department of Land & Natural Resources, 791 P.2d 1267 (Haw.App.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-1WD0-003F-G3VC-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Communications Workers of Am. v. Treffinger, 677 A.2d 295 (N.J.Supr.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W6G0-003C-P2XN-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Southern Nevada Mem'l Hosp. v. State Dept. of Human Res., 705 P.2d 139 (Nev.1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXR-3BS0-003D-C0YF-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Branca v. Board of Educ., 657 N.Y.S.2d 445 (N.Y.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S2R-5380-003V-B53D-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Smith v. Neville, 539 N.W.2d 679 (S.D.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S71-2KJ0-003G-D1WW-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Pirtle v. Spokane Pub. School Dist. No. 81, 921 P.2d 1084 (Wash.App. 1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-WW30-003F-W37Y-00000-00&context=1530671).

The Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act is the exclusive basis for subject-matter jurisdiction in actions against foreign governments and diplomats. See [_28 U.S.C. § 1604 et seq._](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SG9-5HW2-D6RV-H0M5-00000-00&context=1530671) In general, the Act excepts from immunity contractual and other liabilities that stem from a "commercial activity," see id. § 1605(a)(2). A commercial activity is one "concerned with production, sale, or purchase of goods; hiring or leasing of property; borrowing or lending of money; performance of or contracting for the performance of services; and similar activities of the kind that are carried on by natural or juridical persons." Restatement Third, The Foreign Relations Law of the United States § 453, Comment _b._ Even when the ultimate objective of a contract is to serve a public function, its commercial nature places a suit upon the contract within the exception to immunity. Contracts to renovate embassy premises, like contracts to provision an embassy's operation, have been characterized by courts as commercial. See [_De Sanchez v. Banco Central De Nicaragua, 515 F.Supp. 900, 903 (E.D.La.1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-SWC0-0039-S45S-00000-00&context=1530671) (dictum), aff'd, [_770 F.2d 1385 (5th Cir.1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-DVS0-0039-P523-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Liberian E. Timber Corp. v. Government of Republic of Liberia, 659 F. Supp. 606, 610 (D.D.C. 1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-D1M0-003B-60RD-00000-00&context=1530671) (dictum). To be attributed to a foreign sovereign, the "commercial activity" must be that of its agent, or treating the actor as an entity apart from the state must work fraud or injustice. See _Transamerica Leasing, Inc. v. La Republica de Venezuela, 200 F.3d 843, 848 (D.C.Cir. 2000)_. See also _id. at 850_ (stating that court doubts whether "a case of merely apparent authority falls within the agency exception," but noting that "in an appropriate case a court might attribute the acts of the instrumentality to the sovereign under the exception for fraud or injustice"). In theory, diplomatic agents may not waive their own diplomatic immunity without their state's consent; in practice they often purport to do so, such that their authority to waive is widely assumed or the state is estopped to deny such authority if a third party relies on the purported waiver. Restatement Third, The Foreign Relations Law of the United States § 464, Reporters' Note. Courts recognize juridical separation among a foreign sovereign's various instrumentalities for waiver purposes. See [_Chuidian v. Philippine Nat'l Bank, 912 F.2d 1095, 1103-1105 (9th Cir.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-2W80-003B-51YN-00000-00&context=1530671) (waiver by one foreign instrumentality does not waive sovereign immunity for all other instrumentalities of small state).

A foreign sovereign's liability under the "commercial activity" exception is limited to acts by agents with actual authority in [_Phaneuf v. Republic of Indonesia, 106 F.3d 302, 308 (9th Cir.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-JDX0-00B1-D3WT-00000-00&context=1530671). In [_First Fid. Bank, N.A. v. Government of Antigua & Barbuda-Permanent Mission, 877 F.2d 189, 194-196 (2d Cir.1989)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-BKD0-003B-52NN-00000-00&context=1530671), the court assumes without discussion that the commercial-activity exception could be based on apparent authority. An agent's personal liability for an unauthorized transaction is recognized in [_Chuidian, 912 F.2d at 1106_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-2W80-003B-51YN-00000-00&context=1530671) (stating that "plainly \[defendant\] would not be entitled to sovereign immunity for acts not committed in his official capacity. . . . \[s\]overeign immunity . . . will not shield an official who acts beyond the scope of his authority.").

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 2.04_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-401K-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 2- Principles of Attribution_ > _Topic 3- Respondeat Superior_**

**§ 2.04 Respondeat Superior**

**An employer is subject to liability for torts committed by employees while acting within the scope of their employment.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Terminology and cross-references._ This Restatement does not use the terminology of "master" and "servant." Section 7.07(3) defines "employee" for purposes of this doctrine. Section 7.07(2) states the circumstances under which an employee has acted within the scope of employment. Section 7.08 states the circumstances under which a principal is subject to vicarious liability for a tort committed by an agent, whether or not an employee, when actions taken with apparent authority constituted the tort or enabled the agent to conceal its commission.

_b. In general._ This Comment is a brief discussion of the operation of respondeat superior and the justifications for it. The doctrine of respondeat superior is fundamental to the operation of the tort system in the United States. The doctrine establishes a principle of employer liability for the costs that work-related torts impose on third parties. Its scope is limited to the employment relationship and to conduct falling within the scope of that relationship because an employer has the right to control how work is done. This right is more detailed than the right of control possessed by all principals, whether or not employers.

Functionally tied though the doctrine is to tort law, it has long been classified as an element of agency doctrine. In early times, a master's servants were treated as part of the household and their relation to the master made their acts his responsibility as the head of the household. Blackstone's Commentaries refer not to agents but to types of servants, "such as _stewards, factors,_ and _bailiffs:_ whom, however, the law considers as servants _pro tempore,_ with regard to such of their acts, as affect their master's or employer's property." William Blackstone, 1 Commentaries \*427 (1765). In the first treatise on agency published in the United States, William Paley stated, as a principle of agency law, that "\[a\] master is responsible for the negligence or unskillfulness of a servant acting in the prosecution of his service, though not under his immediate direction." William Paley, A Treatise on the Law of Principal and Agent, Chiefly with Reference to Mercantile Transactions 126 (3d ed. 1840). As the location of work moved outside the household and into mercantile and industrial settings, an employer's responsibility for harm caused by employee activities followed the employer's right to control how work is done.

Viewed as a doctrine within the law of agency, respondeat superior is a basis upon which the legal consequences of one person's acts may be attributed to another person. Most often the doctrine applies to acts that have not been specifically directed by an employer but that are the consequence of inattentiveness or poor judgment on the part of an employee acting within the job description. Most cases applying the doctrine involve negligence resulting in physical injury to a person or to property. But respondeat superior is not the exclusive basis on which an employer may be vicariously liable for torts committed by employees. Many employees have jobs in which they interact with third parties, as do nonemployee agents, by making transactions and statements on the employer's behalf. This activity is transactional and communicative in nature. When it is misused, for example to perpetrate a fraud for the employee's sole benefit, the employer's responsibility is often determined by whether the party injured reasonably believed the employee's activity to be authorized. The fraud is associated with a transaction, in contrast to the negligent physical actions to which respondeat superior is conventionally applied. Many cases apply the doctrine of apparent authority to determine whether an employer is liable for employee torts associated with such transactions and statements. See § 7.08. The application of apparent-authority doctrine, and not respondeat superior, may be a consequence of the generalization that employees work with things but agents deal with people. Many employees and agents, of course, do both, and "things" are often instrumentalities for communicating with others.

Respondeat superior is inapplicable when a principal does not have the right to control the actions of the agent that makes the relationship between principal and agent performing the service one of employment as defined in § 7.07(3). In general, employment contemplates a continuing relationship and a continuing set of duties that the employer and employee owe to each other. Agents who are retained as the need arises and who are not otherwise employees of their principal normally operate their own business enterprises and are not, except in limited respects, integrated into the principal's enterprise so that a task may be completed or a specified objective accomplished. Therefore, respondeat superior does not apply.

Respondeat superior assigns responsibility to an employer for the legal consequences that result from employees' errors of judgment and lapses in attentiveness when the acts or omissions are within the scope of employment. See § 7.07(2). A firm or organization that employs individuals usually structures their work to limit the scope of discretion and individual action, thus limiting the occasions when unreasonable decisions are likely to be made. Impulsive conduct is not typical of firms or organizations. The firm as a principal may always act more rationally and reasonably than would most individuals acting by themselves because different individuals are assigned different tasks, often monitoring and checking each other. Respondeat superior creates an incentive for principals to choose employees and structure work within the organization so as to reduce the incidence of tortious conduct. This incentive may reduce the incidence of tortious conduct more effectively than doctrines that impose liability solely on an individual tortfeasor.

Respondeat superior also reflects the likelihood that an employer will be more likely to satisfy a judgment. Moreover, an employer may insure against liability encompassing the consequences of all employees' actions, whereas individual employees lack the incentive and ability to insure beyond any individual's liability or assets.

Despite the general scope of the doctrine, there are significant exceptions to the applicability of respondeat superior to tortious conduct committed by employees. The doctrine is inapplicable when the question is municipal liability for money damages for most acts of local-government employees in actions brought under [_42 U.S.C. § 1983_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SHT-0712-D6RV-H526-00000-00&context=1530671). Additionally, respondeat superior is inapplicable when an employee's tortious conduct does not fall within the scope of employment as stated in § 7.07(2). Finally, when the question is an employer's vicarious liability for punitive damages stemming from the employee's tort, a majority of jurisdictions impose liability on the employer when the employee acted with apparent authority or within the scope of employment, while a sizable minority of jurisdictions require some showing of the employer's direct culpability or, in some circumstances, impose liability when the employee was a manager. See § 7.03, Comment _e._

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency, and codifications._ The rule stated in this section is substantively the same as that stated by Restatement Second, Agency § 219(1). This Restatement does not use the "master-servant" terminology used in Restatement Second, Agency, and Restatement Second, Torts §§ 314B, 317, and 909.

Broad statements of a principal's responsibility in codified statements of agency would encompass the doctrine stated in this section. See Cal. Civ. Code § 2338 (1985 & Supp. 2005) ("\[u\]nless required by or under the authority of law to employ that particular agent, a principal is responsible to third persons for the negligence of his agent in the transaction of the business of the agency, including wrongful acts committed by such agent in and as a part of the transaction of such business, and for his wilful omission to fulfill the obligations of the principal"); Mont. Code § 28-10-602 (same) (2003); S.D. Code Ann. § 59-6-9 (same) (1993 & Supp. 2003); N.D. Cent. Code § 3-03-09 (1987 & Supp. 2003) (same, with specific exclusions). See also Ga. Code Ann. § 10-6-60 (1996 & Supp. 2004) ("\[t\]he principal shall be bound for the care, diligence, and fidelity of his agent in his business, and hence he shall be bound for the neglect and fraud of his agent in the transaction of such business").

_b. In general._ The most comprehensive treatment of vicarious liability and the doctrine of respondeat superior, focused on cases from England and the Commonwealth, is Patrick S. Atiyah, Vicarious Liability in the Law of Torts (1967). For a critique of the history, logic, and social philosophy of respondeat superior, see T. Baty, Vicarious Liability: A Short History of the Liability of Employers, Principals, Partners, Associations and Trade-Union Members with a Chapter on the Laws of Scotland and Foreign States (1916). The argument for the superior rationality of firms is developed in Steven P. Croley, Vicarious Liability in Tort: On the Sources and Limits of Employee Reasonableness, [_69 S. Cal. L. Rev. 1705, 1733-1737 (1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S41-5XC0-00CV-51YY-00000-00&context=1530671). A suggestion that liability rules may influence the conduct of institutional defendants more effectively than they shape the choices of "ordinary people plaintiffs" is made in Gary T. Schwartz, The Hidden and Fundamental Issue of Employer Vicarious Liability, [_69 S. Cal. L. Rev. 1739, 1763 (1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S41-5XD0-00CV-5200-00000-00&context=1530671). On the history and scope of agency doctrine in the United States, see Agency-United States Civil Law, in Oxford Encyclopedia of Legal History (forthcoming 2007).

For an incisive critique of "working with things versus dealing with people" as a basis on which to categorize agents, see John J. Slain, Charles A. Thompson and Freda F. Bein, Agency, Partnership and Employment: A Transactional Approach ix-x (1980).

The leading case holding respondeat superior inapplicable in the context of municipal liability for money damages under [_42 U.S.C. § 1983_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SHT-0712-D6RV-H526-00000-00&context=1530671) is [_Monell v. City of New York, 436 U.S. 658 (1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-8SP0-003B-S1RH-00000-00&context=1530671). On vicarious liability for punitive damages, see [_Kolstad v. American Dental Assoc., 527 U.S. 526 (1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WSC-DW90-004B-Y01S-00000-00&context=1530671). It is a separate question whether damages should be characterized as "punitive." For a recent illustration, see [_United States v. Southern Maryland Home Health Servs., Inc., 95 F.Supp.2d 465, 472-473 (D.Md.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:408S-2B90-0038-Y223-00000-00&context=1530671) (holding that civil penalty under False Claims Act should be characterized as punitive damages for purposes of determining whether employer is liable for damages assessed due to violations of low-level employee, based on relatively large amount of penalty relative to actual losses; court acknowledges its holding represents a minority position). See, e.g., [_United States v. O'Connell, 890 F.2d 563, 567-569 (1st Cir.1989)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-81P0-003B-50SD-00000-00&context=1530671) and [_United States v. Hangar One, Inc., 563 F.2d 1155, 1158 (5th Cir.1977)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-0360-0039-M50W-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying respondeat superior to question of vicarious liability for penalty under False Claims Act).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 2.05 Introductory Note_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-401M-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 2- Principles of Attribution_ > _Topic 4- Related Doctrines_**

**Restat 3d of Agency, § 2.05 Introductory Note**

Agency doctrine does not operate in isolation from other doctrines developed within the common-law tradition. Contract law, for example, defines many of the rights that a principal, an agent, and a third party may assert against each other and provides the remedies available for breach. Doctrines distinctive to agency that attribute the legal consequences of one person's acts to another are also supplemented by the operation of other bodies of doctrine, in particular estoppel and restitution.

_Estoppel._ Estoppel, a general principle of broad applicability, protects justifiable and detrimental reliance on promises and factual representations when and to the degree necessary to avoid injustice. Courts sometimes use the word "reliance" to refer to any kind of action or inaction that is caused by another person's manifestation. The term "detrimental reliance" is used here to emphasize that estoppel in this context requires that a third party undergo a change of position involving an expenditure of money, or labor, an incurrence of loss, or a subjection to liability.

Estoppel is relevant to agency when a representation is made that one person has authority to act on behalf of another. In this Restatement, when doctrines other than estoppel are applicable to a situation, those doctrines govern. Unlike many cases, but like Restatement Second, Agency, this Restatement treats apparent authority as a doctrine distinct from estoppel. It does so to clarify the law and to make clear when and why it is necessary for a plaintiff to show detrimental reliance.

Although cast in a secondary role, estoppel is relevant to agency in several respects. Under the circumstances stated in § 2.05, estoppel may impose liability on a person who has not made a manifestation, as defined in § 1.03, that another has authority as an agent. Estoppel imposes liability when a person is responsible for a third party's belief that an actor is an agent and when the belief induces a third party's justifiable and detrimental reliance. A person who has not made a manifestation that an actor is an agent would be responsible for a third party's belief that the actor is an agent when the person carelessly or intentionally causes the belief or when, knowing of the belief and the likelihood that it might induce third parties to change position, the person fails to take reasonable steps to make the facts known to the third party. Additionally, estoppel may prevent a person alleged to be the principal of an agent from asserting as a defense the fact that formalities have not been complied with or that a claim is not timely. See § 3.02. A separate form of estoppel is applicable when an undisclosed principal permits an agent to operate in circumstances in which a reasonable third party would not have cause to question the extent of the agent's authority if the third party had notice the actor was an agent acting on behalf of a principal. Section 2.06 states this form of estoppel. Additionally, an undisclosed principal is subject to liability to a third party who is justifiably induced to make a detrimental change in position by an agent when the principal, having notice of the agent's conduct and that it might induce others to change their positions, did not take reasonable steps to notify third persons of the facts. Finally, under the circumstances stated in § 4.08, a principal may be estopped to deny ratification of an unauthorized act or transaction.

Estoppel doctrine enables the court to engage in a situation-specific assessment of the demands of justice in particular circumstances, including a determination of the appropriate remedy. The requirement that the third party's change in position be justifiable invites a flexible and contextual assessment. It is not necessary for a plaintiff to establish that the person responsible for a representation acted wrongfully or that making a representation itself constituted a tort.

Estoppel is distinct from apparent authority and ratification in its consequences because the party who is estopped does not acquire reciprocal rights merely due to the estoppel. A principal made liable as a consequence of estoppel may, however, acquire rights against the party asserting the estoppel by ratifying what the agent or actor has done. See § 4.02 for the consequences of ratification. The principal may also have rights against the agent. Estoppel is distinct from both the creation and the appearance of actual authority because estoppel is available only when the person asserting it has justifiably and detrimentally changed position.

_Restitution._ The principle requiring restitution to avoid or redress unjust enrichment is, like estoppel, one of broad applicability. It is triggered by instances of unjustified enrichment in which one party has benefited from a transfer that lacks an adequate legal basis. More precisely, this means a transfer that cannot be explained by the terms of a valid and enforceable exchange transaction, by the donative intention of the transferor, or by the transferor's legal duty to the transferee. For a comprehensive treatment, see Restatement Third, Restitution and Unjust Enrichment.

Within the common law of agency, this principle underlies a number of doctrines, including the principal's right to recover profits improperly made by the agent, see § 8.02, Comment _e,_ as well as the duties of the principal to indemnify the agent under stated circumstances. See § 8.14. The principle requiring restitution is also applicable when a principal has benefited through an agent's action and the principal would otherwise be unjustly enriched, even though the agent acted without actual or apparent authority. Section 2.07 applies to these situations. Restitution is not required when the person who conferred the benefit acted officiously in doing so. In the situations covered by § 2.07, the conferral is a nonofficious consequence of the relationship between the agent and the principal and the duties the agent owes the principal. In contrast with the estoppel doctrines stated in §§ 2.05-2.06, the principal's duty to make restitution as stated in § 2.07 is applicable only when the principal has benefited from actions taken by the agent or by the agent and third parties.

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 2.05_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-401N-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 2- Principles of Attribution_ > _Topic 4- Related Doctrines_**

**§ 2.05 Estoppel to Deny Existence of Agency Relationship**

**A person who has not made a manifestation that an actor has authority as an agent and who is not otherwise liable as a party to a transaction purportedly done by the actor on that person's account is subject to liability to a third party who justifiably is induced to make a detrimental change in position because the transaction is believed to be on the person's account, if**

**(1) the person intentionally or carelessly caused such belief, or**

**(2) having notice of such belief and that it might induce others to change their positions, the person did not take reasonable steps to notify them of the facts.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope._ This section does not presuppose the existence of a relationship of agency as defined in § 1.01. When apparent authority, defined in § 2.03, is present, § 2.03 governs whether the principal is bound to the legal consequences of the agent's actions. Section 4.08 governs estoppel to deny ratification. Section 1.03 defines manifestation.

_b. Terminology._ The doctrine in this section encompasses definitions of "ostensible authority" that hold a principal accountable for an appearance of authority arising solely from the principal's failure to use ordinary care. Some statutes and cases so define "ostensible authority," while others use it as a synonym for "apparent authority" as defined in § 2.03. "Detrimental change of position" means an expenditure of money or labor, an incurrence of a loss, or subjection to legal liability, not the loss of the benefit of a bargain.

_c. In general._ The estoppel stated in this section protects third parties who justifiably rely on a belief that an actor is an agent and who act on that belief to their detriment. The doctrine is applicable when the person against whom estoppel is asserted has made no manifestation that an actor has authority as an agent but is responsible for the third party's belief that an actor is an agent and the third party has justifiably been induced by that belief to undergo a detrimental change in position. Most often the person estopped will be responsible for the third party's erroneous belief as the consequence of a failure to use reasonable care, either to prevent circumstances that foreseeably led to the belief, or to correct the belief once on notice of it. If the scope of the actor's power to bind the person estopped is in issue, the doctrine of apparent authority, stated in § 2.03, governs. The operative question is whether a reasonable person in the position of the third party would believe such an agent, as the actor appears to be, to have authority to do a particular act.

_d. Rationale._ The agency-law doctrines of actual authority and apparent authority attribute the legal consequences of an actor's actions to a person when the person has made a manifestation of authority, either to the actor, thereby creating actual authority under § 2.01, or to a third party, thereby creating apparent authority under § 2.03. Estoppel links the legal consequences of the actor's actions to the person estopped on different bases. These are the third party's justifiable and detrimental change in position and the third party's belief in the actor's relationship to the person estopped, which induced the change in position for which the person estopped is held responsible.

Estoppel is analyzed with doctrinal elements similar to those applicable to a claim of negligent misrepresentation. See Restatement Second, Torts § 552. The principal's failure to use care enables the agent, or an actor who purports to be an agent, to misrepresent the agent's authority or to masquerade as an agent. If the third party is unreasonable in believing that an agency relationship exists and that the actor has authority, the third party should not recover, just as a plaintiff's contributory negligence in relying on a negligent misrepresentation bars recovery or reduces it under a rule of comparative negligence. See Restatement Second, Torts § 552A. The third party must prove a reasonable and detrimental change of position and the damages recoverable are those necessary to compensate for the loss caused by that reliance, as in an action for negligent misrepresentation. See Restatement Second, Torts § 552B.

When a relationship of agency exists, a principal is often estopped to deny the existence of authority when the origin of the third party's belief, or the explanation for it, demarcates between the elements requisite to estoppel and those requisite to proving apparent authority as defined in § 2.03. Apparent authority is not present unless the third party's belief is traceable to the principal's own manifestations, which may include placing the agent in a position that leads third parties to believe the agent has authority consistent with the position. See § 3.03, Comment _b._ Estoppel does not require as close a fit between affirmative acts of the principal and the third party's belief. Instead, it protects third parties who reasonably believe an actor to be authorized as an agent when the belief cannot be shown to follow directly or indirectly from the principal's own manifestations.

**Illustration:**

1\. P has two coagents, A and B. P has notice that B, acting without actual or apparent authority, has represented to T that A has authority to enter into a transaction that is contrary to P's instructions. T does not know that P's instructions forbid A from engaging in the transaction. T cannot establish conduct by P on the basis of which T could reasonably believe that A has the requisite authority. T can, however, establish that P had notice of B's representation and that it would have been easy for P to inform T of the limits on A's authority. T detrimentally changes position in reliance on B's representation by making a substantial down payment. If it is found that T's action was justifiable, P is estopped to deny B's authority to make the representation.

Estoppel to deny authority may also arise in close proximity to circumstances that establish that the principal has ratified the agent's acts. See § 4.01. Estoppel's operation is distinct from ratification because it requires that the plaintiff establish a detrimental change of position.

The doctrine stated in this section may estop a person from denying the existence of an agency relationship with an actor when the third party would be unable to establish either the existence of a relationship of agency between the actor and the person estopped as defined in § 1.01, or a manifestation of authority sufficient to create apparent authority as defined in § 2.03.

**Illustration:**

2\. P owns a large retail furniture store, known as "P's Furniture Emporium." P, who is often absent from the premises, does not otherwise maintain surveillance over the store's sales force. T, a prospective customer, enters the store and is approached by A, whose demeanor and attire lend A the appearance of a salesperson. After examining floor samples, T purchases several items of furniture for cash, giving the cash to A. A gives T a receipt written on a standard-looking form, with "P's Furniture Emporium" printed at the top. A explains to T that the items purchased are not presently in inventory but will be delivered to T's home within two weeks. A is an imposter who is not an employee or other agent of P. A does not remit any of the cash paid by T to P. No furniture is delivered to T. T's change of position is justified by T's belief that A is what A purports to be, a salesperson with authority to sell from P's inventory. Whether P may deny A's authority is a question for the trier of fact.

Although the doctrine may estop a person from denying the existence of a relationship of agency with an actor, it does not determine the scope of the actor's power to affect the person's legal relations. Section 2.03 is applicable.

**Illustration:**

3\. Same facts as Illustration 2, except that A tells T that P's Furniture Emporium is about to go out of business, after T professes a long-standing admiration for the store. A agrees to sell to T all of P's inventory in P's Furniture Emporium, taking a cash down payment from T. P is not bound to T on the agreement made by A, and P is not liable to T for the amount of the cash down payment. It is not reasonable for T to believe that A, who purports to be a retail salesperson, would be authorized to agree to sell all of the store's inventory.

_e. Apparent ownership distinguished._ A representation that an actor owns something, or an appearance of ownership, does not create apparent authority to act as an agent. On appearance of ownership when a principal is undisclosed, see § 2.06, Comment c.

**Illustration:**

4\. A, who is impecunious, borrows an expensive necklace from P. Wearing the necklace, A enters an art gallery owned by T. T believes the necklace to be P's, having seen photographs of P wearing it. A purports to buy an expensive painting on credit on P's behalf. A takes the painting and disappears with it. T sues P to recover the purchase price of the painting, consistent with the credit terms to which A agreed. P is not estopped to deny that A was acting as P's agent. The fact that T believed A was wearing jewelry belonging to P does not warrant a belief that A has authority to act as P's agent.

Some recurrent situations that are outside the operation of agency doctrines are addressed by legislation. For example, statutes in some states make a vehicle owner vicariously liable for the negligence of any person, whether or not a member of the owner's family, who drives the vehicle with the owner's consent. As a result, it is unnecessary for an injured person to establish that the driver acted as the owner's agent or that a representation of agency, on which the injured person somehow relied, should be inferred from the driver's use of the car. Likewise, if a person who sells goods regularly deals in goods of the kind, U.C.C. § 2-312(2) implies a warranty that the goods are delivered free of any other person's rightful claim to them.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ This section corresponds in operative respects to Restatement Second, Agency § 8 B(1). The definition of "change of position" in Comment _b_ is drawn from Restatement Second, Agency § 8 B(3). Restatement Second, Agency § 8 B(2) has no counterpart in this Restatement because it does not represent a doctrine within the common law of agency. Restatement Second, Agency § 8 B(2) states: "An owner of property who represents to third persons that another is the owner of the property or who permits the other so to represent, or who realizes that third persons believe that another is the owner of the property, and that he could easily inform the third persons of the facts, is subject to the loss of the property if the other disposes of it to third persons who, in ignorance of the facts, purchase the property or otherwise change their position with reference to it."

_c. In general._ Restatement Second, Agency § 8 B, Comment _a,_ characterizes estoppel as "fundamentally a doctrine in the law of torts. . . ." Id., Comment a. This assertion is not entirely consistent with the cases, which do not require that the representation of agency or authority be tortious. Nonetheless, the relevance of concepts such as reasonable care links estoppel doctrine as it operates in agency to concepts familiar in tort law. See Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 3 (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005) (specifying elements relevant to ascertaining whether conduct lacks reasonable care). The link to tort law is also present in the analogous operation of estoppel and the doctrine of negligent representation.

_d. Rationale._ For the proposition that a person may be estopped from denying an actor's authority when the person has notice of a third party's belief, and fails to take steps to correct it, see [_American Cas. Co. v. Krieger, 181 F.3d 1113, 1121-1123 (9th Cir.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WWD-2VG0-0038-X4DF-00000-00&context=1530671) (triable issue of fact when promoter of bungee-jumping contests relied on certificate of insurance issued by broker who did not act as agent of insurer when insurer had knowledge that broker used standard-form certificates to add insureds to policies); _McNeil Real Estate Fund XXVI, L.P. v. Matthew's, Inc., 112 F.Supp.2d 437, 443-444 (W.D.Pa. 2000)_ (company estopped to deny authority of Chief Financial Officer to enter into guarantee agreement with landlord when company's owner signed lease that expressly incorporated guarantee, which placed owner on notice of guarantee); [_Patrick v. Miss New Mexico-USA Universe Pageant, 490 F. Supp. 833, 840 (W.D.Tex.1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4P-3740-0039-S50X-00000-00&context=1530671) (officers of national beauty pageant had affirmative duty to correct misinformation about eligibility on which contestants might rely provided by other agents, in light of fact that same question as to eligibility had arisen before); [_Ercanbrack v. Crandall-Walker Motor Co., 550 P.2d 723, 725 (Utah 1976)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-64C0-003G-F05S-00000-00&context=1530671) (holding principal cannot be estopped by failing to take positive action about matter absent showing it had notice; evidence did not establish that superior coagents had notice of salesman's representation to customer).

For the proposition that estoppel requires reliance whereas ratification does not, see [_Dempsey-Tegeler & Co. v. Irwin, 415 F.2d 1348, 1351 (7th Cir.1969)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-T050-0039-Y34F-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Trimble-Weber v. Weber, 695 N.E.2d 344 (Ohio App. 1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RJN-2X90-0039-41T6-00000-00&context=1530671) (law firm not subject to liability for actions of lawyer listed as "of counsel" when plaintiff's injuries resulted from lawyer's intentional torts unrelated to any reliance by plaintiff on representation of lawyer's relationship with law firm). Contrary to the distinction drawn by this Restatement and by Restatement Second, Agency, some courts equate elements requisite to estoppel with those requisite to apparent authority. See, e.g., [_Malmberg v. American Honda Motor Co., 644 So. 2d 888, 890 (Ala.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-WTJ0-003C-91H9-00000-00&context=1530671) ("'While some suggestion has been made that a distinction exists between apparent authority and authority grounded on estoppel . . . our cases and authority generally base the two upon the same elements,'" quoting [_Pearson v. Agricultural Ins. Co., 25 So. 2d 164, 167 (Ala.1946))_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40M5-TNH0-0046-80PM-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Fike v. Board of Trustees, Teachers' & State Employees' Ret. Sys., 279 S.E.2d 910, 912 (N.C.App.1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-1470-003G-046J-00000-00&context=1530671) (law of estoppel as applied to agency is that "'\[w\]here a person by words or conduct represents or permits it to be represented that another person is his agent, he will be estopped to deny the agency as against third persons who have dealt, on the faith of such representation, with the person so held out as agent, even if no agency existed in fact,'" quoting [_Daniel Boone Complex, Inc. v. Furst, 258 S.E.2d 379, 388 (N.C.App.1979))_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-1850-003G-01KN-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 2 is based on [_Hoddeson v. Koos Bros., 135 A.2d 702 (N.J.Super.1957)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRH-SKJ0-003D-S2NY-00000-00&context=1530671). In the Illustration, the customer receives a receipt from the imposter. In _Hoddeson,_ although the customer neither requested nor received a receipt, the court ordered a new trial to enable the customer to establish that the store's failure to exercise reasonable surveillance breached a duty owed her as a customer. For an analogous fact pattern, see [_Luken v. Buckeye Parking Corp., 68 N.E.2d 217, 220 (Ohio App.1945)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-XJC0-003C-62YX-00000-00&context=1530671) (trial raised questions for jury as to liability of owner of public parking lot to customer for value of automobile destroyed in collision when driven by imposter who masqueraded as lot attendant and gave customer a ticket for car, stating that he was owner's agent; while owner's acts and omissions might cause reasonably prudent person to believe imposter was owner's agent, unusual aspects of imposter's conduct might have aroused suspicion of reasonably prudent person). A later case, [_Raclaw v. Fay, Conmy & Co., 668 N.E.2d 114, 118 (Ill.App. 1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2670-003D-H4V2-00000-00&context=1530671), suggests that the authority of _Hoddeson_ and _Luken_ is limited to "transactions in a commercial retail setting in which a member of the general public enters a retail business establishment or store, which is open to the public and known to sell specific products or provide specific services . . . \[and\] is approached by an imposter whom the customer assumes to be an agent or employee of the business, authorized to offer the services or the products displayed in the store. Under these circumstances courts have held retail business establishments responsible for any fraud perpetrated on its \[sic\] customers by such imposter agents." The defendant in _Raclaw,_ an accounting firm, did not market investment products to the general public. The court held that it was not liable for the fraud of an alleged agent who was allowed to use the firm's address, office, and telephone number. The plaintiffs neither visited the firm's office nor investigated whether it actually marketed the investment product.

For an early and broad articulation of the potential operation of estoppel in an agency context, see [_MacAndrews & Forbes Co. v. United States, 23 F.2d 667 (3d Cir.1928)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-0CG0-003B-K4G2-00000-00&context=1530671). In _MacAndrews & Forbes,_ a shipper of a cargo of fox skins appointed a firm of custom-house brokers to handle entry of the skins into port and delivery of the skins to the shipper. The brokerage firm filed a bill of lading with the customs house designating a particular trucking firm to receive and deliver the skins. The brokerage firm also wrote a letter addressed to the ship's delivery clerk directing delivery of the skins to "our truckmen." The letter, taken by a representative of the brokerage firm to the office of the trucking firm, was then taken to the public garage where trucks were housed and placed in an unlocked drawer for the assigned trucker to find. Instead, the letter was purloined and then presented to the ship's delivery clerk, who delivered the fox skins to the individual who presented the letter. The court held that the shipper was estopped, as against the ship's owner and operator, to deny that the individual who presented the letter was authorized to receive the skins because "the fraud was made possible by those acting for the \[shipper\]. The ship brokers were entitled to receive the shipment and, had they applied to the delivery clerk, they would have received it. Instead of so doing, they signed a written order . . . left in an unlocked drawer in a public garage, where it was evidently found and abstracted by some person, who was thus by the lack of care of the \[shipper's\] agent--and lack of due care is negligence--put in a position to wrongfully obtain the goods," clothing him with a "semblance of authenticity." [_Id. at 667-668_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-0CG0-003B-K4G2-00000-00&context=1530671). In contrast, the ship's delivery clerk evidenced no lack of care, delivering goods upon presentation of a valid order in the press of off-loading the ship's cargo. The underlying principle allocates a third party's fraud, as between two innocent parties, on the party who made it possible to perpetrate the fraud. [_Id. at 667_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-0CG0-003B-K4G2-00000-00&context=1530671).

For a more recent articulation of estoppel as a broad basis for a principal's liability, see Pacific Carriers Ltd. v. BNP Paribas, (2004) 218 C.L.R. 451 (Austl.). In _Pacific Carriers,_ the manager of a bank's indemnity department, who lacked authority herself to bind the bank to a guarantee or indemnity, stamped two letters of indemnity with the stamp used to authenticate letters of credit. The stamped letters, presented by a shipper to a carrier who delivered cargo in reliance on the letters, carried no indication that the manager's authority to affix the authenticating stamp extended only to letters of credit. The signatures on the letters were illegible. The court found that the presence of the stamp on the letters was likely more significant to the carrier than the signatures and that, having situated the departmental manager in a position to sign and stamp documents, the bank was accountable for the consequences of its failure to impose any internal check on how the manager used the stamp. The outcome in _Pacific Carriers,_ like that in _MacAndrews & Forbes,_ rests on a comparison of the accountability of two parties linked by an unreliable or rogue actor. For critical assessments of _Pacific Carriers,_ see F.M.B. Reynolds, Apparent Authority and Illegible Signatures, 121 L.Q. Rev. 55 (2005); Peter Watts, The Creep of Negligence into Agency Law in Australia, 26 Austl. Bar Rev. 185 (2005).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 2.06_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-401P-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 2- Principles of Attribution_ > _Topic 4- Related Doctrines_**

**§ 2.06 Liability of Undisclosed Principal**

**(1) An undisclosed principal is subject to liability to a third party who is justifiably induced to make a detrimental change in position by an agent acting on the principal's behalf and without actual authority if the principal, having notice of the agent's conduct and that it might induce others to change their positions, did not take reasonable steps to notify them of the facts.**

**(2) An undisclosed principal may not rely on instructions given an agent that qualify or reduce the agent's authority to less than the authority a third party would reasonably believe the agent to have under the same circumstances if the principal had been disclosed.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Cross-references._ This section applies only to undisclosed principals as defined in § 1.04(2)(b). An undisclosed principal may ratify a prior act by an agent. See § 4.03, Comment _b._ For discussion of a principal's receipt or retention of benefit generated by an agent's unauthorized act as a means through which the act may be ratified, see § 4.01, Comment _g._ Comment _f_ to § 2.03 discusses the apparent authority that an agent for an unidentified principal may hold and explains why agents for undisclosed principals do not hold apparent authority.

_b. Terminology._ The situation encompassed by this rule is more precisely one of "undisclosed agency" because the third party does not know that the actor in question is acting as an agent, not a principal. The rule stated in this section is an instance of "inherent agency power," a term used in Restatement Second, Agency § 8 A. In this Restatement, the term "inherent agency power" is not used.

_c. Rationale._ The principle stated in this section will result in liability in a relatively small number of cases in which a third party deals with an agent but has no notice that the agent represents the interests of another. Under subsection (1), a principal is subject to liability to a third party who is justifiably induced to make a detrimental change in position by the conduct of an agent acting without actual authority when the principal has notice of the agent's conduct and its likely impact on third parties and fails to take reasonable steps to inform them of the facts. The underlying principle is consistent with the estoppel doctrine stated in § 2.05. Under subsection (2), a principal is subject to liability to a third party when the third party would have no reason to inquire into the scope of the agent's authority if the third party knew the agent acted on behalf of a principal. The principal may not rely on qualifications or reductions to the agent's authority when the doctrine of apparent authority would make the qualification or restriction ineffective as to an agent acting on behalf of a disclosed or an unidentified principal. In such cases, the principal has chosen to place the agent in a position in which it is reasonable for third parties to believe that the agent has authority consistent with the position.

The rule stated in this section, like the doctrine of apparent authority, protects third parties by backstopping actual authority when circumstances might otherwise permit the principal opportunistically to speculate at the expense of third parties. See § 2.03, Comment _c._ Apparent authority does not arise when a principal is undisclosed because no manifestation has been made that the actor with whom the third party interacts has authority to act as an agent.

A further rationale for the doctrine stated in this section is protection of the reasonable expectations of third parties who deal with an ongoing enterprise following an undisclosed sale to a new owner, whether or not affiliated in some manner with the former owner, when the former owner continues to operate the enterprise as agent on behalf of the new owner. The enterprise's new owner, as undisclosed principal, would or reasonably should expect that third parties will continue to deal with the now-agent as if no change had occurred. If the agent operates a business under the agent's name, the appearance to the third party may be that the agent owns the business and that its assets will be available to satisfy business-related obligations incurred by the agent. The opportunity for speculation provided the principal would emerge when the principal has limited the agent's authority and the agent enters into a contract that exceeds the agent's actual authority. Then, if the contract is advantageous to the principal, neither the principal nor the agent will raise the agent's lack of authority and the third party will not know of it. On the other hand, if the contract then is disadvantageous to the principal, the principal may assert lack of authority as a defense.

The doctrine allocates to the principal the risk that the agent will deviate from the principal's instructions while doing acts that are consistent with the apparent position the agent occupies, which are acts that third parties would anticipate an agent in such a position would have authority to do and may well be acts that are foreseeable to the principal. Such acts are especially likely to be foreseeable when they are consistent with the agent's position although they contravene the principal's instructions.

A third party who deals with an actor, lacking notice that the actor is someone's agent, does not expect the liability of any person in addition to the immediate actor with whom the third party deals. Estoppel's perspective is broader but less well defined. The question is whether it is unjust, in particular circumstances, to permit a principal who has chosen to deal through an agent but to remain undisclosed to have the benefit of restrictions on the agent's authority. In the instance in which the rule is applicable, it is unlikely that the third party will inquire into the status of the immediate actor or seek to identify the existence of restrictions on that actor's right to interact with legal consequences for the third party. The need to make such inquiries, in the circumstances to which this doctrine applies, is unlikely to occur to a reasonable third party, and making the inquiry may be difficult or its cost may seem excessive relative to the magnitude of the particular transaction. When a third party previously dealt with the agent as the owner of the business now managed by the agent, the third party may assume that inquiry is unnecessary for transactions similar to prior transactions. Separately, if an undisclosed principal has notice of an agent's unauthorized actions and notice that a third party will as a consequence be induced to make a detrimental change in position, the undisclosed principal may be estopped to deny the agent's authority. The undisclosed principal may avoid estoppel by disclosing the agent's status and the extent of the agent's authority.

The doctrine encompasses deviations from actual authority of a sort that would not lead a reasonable third party to inquire separately into the scope of the agent's authority. Such deviations are foreseeable to the principal and are likely not to be contested by any principal, whether or not undisclosed, when they appear likely to benefit the principal.

**Illustrations:**

1\. P buys a medical-supplies business owned and run for many years by A as a sole proprietor. P employs A as general manager, telling A to keep P's ownership a secret. P tells A to sell no supplies at a discount except with P's prior approval. As is customary in the trade, A offers a standard discount in exchange for a large order to T Hospital, a long-standing customer. T Hospital accepts the offer made by A. P is bound to sell to T Hospital on the terms to which A agreed. Had T known that A acted as an agent on behalf of a principal, T would reasonably believe that an agent in A's position had authority to offer the standard discount.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that A offers T Hospital a requirements contract at fixed prices, terms unprecedented in the industry. T Hospital accepts the offer made by A. P is not bound to sell to T Hospital on the terms offered by A. A's offer was extraordinary, and had T known that A acted as an agent on behalf of a principal, T's belief in A's authority to offer such terms would not be reasonable.

3\. A owns and operates a carpet-cleaning business doing business under the name "A's Cleaner Carpets." A purchases cleaning supplies each week from T on terms standard in the trade that require payment in cash within 30 days. A sells the business to P, who employs A to manage it. P continues to do business under the name "A's Cleaner Carpets." T is unaware that A no longer owns the business. P directs A to buy two specific types of cleaning solution only from P. A continues to purchase all supplies from T, using all in the business. P directs A not to pay T for the two solutions that A bought contrary to P's instructions. P must make restitution to T for the cleaning solutions that A receives from T even when P has directed that they be purchased only from P. See § 2.07. Alternatively, P may return unused supplies to T if such is feasible.

4\. Same facts as Illustration 3, except that A enters into a contract to sell A's Cleaner Carpets to T. P is not subject to liability to T on the contract. A lacked actual authority to make the contract. A lacked apparent authority because P's existence was undisclosed. Had T known that A acted as an agent for P, it would not have been reasonable for T to believe that A had authority as manager to contract to sell P's business. T is not entitled to restitution under § 2.07 because P did not benefit from the contract.

5\. P, who owns Blackacre, coinhabits it with A, who manages Blackacre on P's behalf. T, who wishes to purchase Blackacre, mistakenly believes that A has authority to sell it on the basis of A's representation that A has such authority. P is aware of T's error and could easily inform T of the facts. P is subject to liability to T if T justifiably makes a detrimental change in position as a consequence of A's conduct.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTE**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ Subsection (1) of this section has no precise counterpart in Restatement Second, Agency. The coverage of subsection (2) corresponds to Restatement Second, Agency §§ 194 and 195. Section 194 provides that "\[a\] general agent for an undisclosed principal authorized to conduct transactions subjects his principal to liability for acts done on his account, if usual or necessary in such transactions, although forbidden by the principal to do them." Section 195 provides that "\[a\]n undisclosed principal who entrusts an agent with the management of his business is subject to liability to third persons with whom the agent enters into transactions usual in such businesses and on the principal's account, although contrary to the directions of the principal." Section 194, Comment _a,_ characterizes the section as an example of inherent agency power.

_c. Rationale._ On conduct of the undisclosed principal that leads to the third party's belief, see [_Frohlich & Newell Foods, Inc. v. New Sans Souci Nursing Home, 441 N.Y.S.2d 335, 338 (Civ.1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-G420-003C-F51C-00000-00&context=1530671) (by allowing agents to run nursing home with no notice of principals' ownership during 3 years of continuous dealing with supplier, undisclosed principals "invested the agents with the indicia of property and thus placed them in a position to deceive the plaintiff about their true status"). Some academic commentary instead stresses the appearance of ownership that the principal has created. Professor Barnett observes of these relationships, "what \[the undisclosed principal\] has done is to create the appearance of an entitlement- or ownership-transfer, and outsiders are entitled to rely on this appearance. Conversely, where \[the undisclosed principal\] has created no such appearance, the unauthorized acts of an agent will not bind an undisclosed principal." Randy E. Barnett, Squaring Undisclosed Agency Law with Contract Theory, [_75 Calif. L. Rev. 1969, 1996 (1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S41-2700-00CV-60W4-00000-00&context=1530671).

The nature of the business entrusted to the agent is relevant to the undisclosed principal's liability. See [_Morris Oil Co. v. Rainbow Oilfield Trucking, Inc., 741 P.2d 840 (N.M.App.1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-X590-003D-D4NH-00000-00&context=1530671) (undisclosed principal liable for agent's acts, even if forbidden, that are usual or necessary or occur in usual course of business conducted by agent). A separate basis for an undisclosed principal's liability is its retention of benefit from the transaction. See [_N. K. Parrish, Inc. v. Southwest Beef Indus. Corp., 638 F.2d 1366, 1371 (5th Cir.1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-4920-0039-W0D5-00000-00&context=1530671) (investors in cattle-feeding program liable to seller on grain contract if agent who purchased grain had actual authority or if investors retained benefit of grain transaction). On acceptance of benefit more generally, see § 2.07.

Whether an actor is in charge of an established place of business affects the inferences a third party may reasonably draw about the actor's right to do particular acts. For a leading example, see [_Senor v. Bangor Mills, 211 F.2d 685, 688 (3d Cir.1954)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-Y000-003B-04VG-00000-00&context=1530671). The range, duration, and scope of the matters entrusted to an agent by the undisclosed principal are relevant to whether a third party reasonably believes an actor has the right to do a particular act, just as these factors are relevant to the reasonableness of belief in the authority of an agent for a fully or partially disclosed principal. See, e.g., [_Master Commodities, Inc. v. Texas Cattle Mgmt. Co., 586 F.2d 1352, 1359 (10th Cir.1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-X6H0-0039-M2HX-00000-00&context=1530671) (looking to use of agent in context of particular case, apparent authority exists for "acts which might reasonably be expected to be performed by such an agent").

The best known among a small universe of cases applying this doctrine is Watteau v. Fenwick \[1893\] 1 Q.B. 346. The agent in _Watteau_ previously owned a pub but sold it to a brewing company. The former owner operated the pub as manager for the brewer, which remained undisclosed as principal. The manager contravened the owner's instructions by buying selected inventory items from other sources. Those vendors sued and recovered from the owner. In _Watteau,_ the court held the principal liable on the ground that the agent acted within the authority usually given to agents of the kind. Of the case it has been said: "a case with such little subsequent judicial history has done remarkably well in exciting the imagination of academic writers who have striven hard to find a theoretical basis for it." B.S. Markesinis & R.J.C. Munday, An Outline of the Law of Agency 28 (1986). The authority of the case has been rejected in Canada. See Sign-O-Lite Plastics v. Metropolitan Life Ins. Co., \[1991\] 73 D.L.R. (4th) 541 (B.C.App.1990). The reasoning of the court's opinion has been questioned as inconsistent with the precedent stated by the court, Edmunds v. Bushell & Jones (1865) 1 Q.B. 97, in which there may have been apparent authority. See F.M.B. Reynolds, Bowstead & Reynolds on Agency 349-351 (17th ed. 2001). In Restatement Second, Agency § 195, _Watteau_ is the basis for Illustration 1.

An alternative is to treat the resolution in _Watteau_ as more closely analogous to doctrines of vicarious liability than to any form of usual or apparent authority. See G.H. Treitel, The Law of Contract 634 (1995). Doing so would ground the principal's liability in the risk of injury to third parties foreseeably undertaken by the principal's decision to create an appearance of ownership in the agent. The injury to the third party, though, may well be mitigated by the fact that the agent is liable. To be sure, that outcome does not result in precisely what the third party anticipated in _Watteau,_ which is the liability of a party who owns the business. Another analysis, critical of the court's reasoning, emphasizes the misleading impression of ownership, but queries the relationship between the liabilities that an agent might incur that are usual or necessary to operating the business and the appearance of ownership. See J. Dennis Hynes & Mark J. Loewenstein, Agency, Partnership and the LLC: The Law of Unincorporated Business Enterprises 372-373 (6th ed. 2003). A related but separate approach considers what the principal reasonably should have foreseen. See Melvin A. Eisenberg, An Introduction to Agency and Partnership 16 (2d ed. 1995) (benchmark for inherent agency power is reasonable foreseeability from principal's viewpoint). A final strand of academic commentary focuses on the benefit received by the principal through the delivery over several years of inventory to the pub, coupled with sales out of inventory in the pub's business. Invoices presumably accompanied the inventory when the agent received it, and the agent's knowledge that the supplier expected to be paid would be imputed to the principal. See Merton Ferson, Bases for Master's Liability and for Principal's Liability to Third Persons, 4 Vand. L. Rev. 260, 280 (1951). Viewed from a more general perspective, the outcome in _Watteau_ has been justified as an application of the least-cost avoider principle: the costs incurred by a third-party vendor to investigate an agent (especially when the third party assumes it deals with the owner of a business) would exceed costs incurred by the undisclosed principal in controlling the agent. See Eric Rasmussen, Agency Law and Contract Formation, 6 Am. L. & Econ. Rev. 369 (2004).

Two recent cases featuring undisclosed principals involve the assignment of mortgages without notice to the mortgagor under terms that appoint the originating financial institution as a servicing agent for the assignee. See [_Department of Banking & Fin. v. Davis, 416 N.W.2d 566, 569-570 (Neb.1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-5C30-003D-B40M-00000-00&context=1530671) (assignee bound by servicing agent's acceptance of payoff amount and release of mortgage); [_DuPuis v. Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corp., 879 F.Supp. 139 (D.Me. 1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-VTJ0-001T-50HW-00000-00&context=1530671) (under principles of commonlaw agency, assignee would be bound by terms of servicing agent's dealings with mortgagor, although contrary to assignee's instructions; assignee not bound because as entity created by Congress it is bound only by acts of agent having actual authority).

In some circumstances, an undisclosed principal may be estopped when it does not disclose an agent's lack of authority to a third party. See [_Bailey v. Worton, 752 So. 2d 470 (Miss.App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3Y2W-KMY0-0039-449N-00000-00&context=1530671) (undisclosed principal who owned property estopped to deny authority of person who appeared to be owner of property to direct that paving work be done; paving contractor reasonably relied to its detriment on "apparent authority" of person who appeared to be owner). The most extensive analysis of the underlying principles appears in Spiro v. Lintern, \[1973\] 1 W.L.R. 1002 (App.) (owner of property had duty to disclose to third party, who believed he purchased property from owner's spouse and that she was the owner, that spouse lacked authority to sell property; without making disclosure, owner acquiesced in third party undertaking renovation and landscaping work on property). Illustration 5 is derived from a simplified version of the facts of _Spiro._
