[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 2.07_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-401R-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 2- Principles of Attribution_ > _Topic 4- Related Doctrines_**

**§ 2.07 Restitution of Benefit**

**If a principal is unjustly enriched at the expense of another person by the action of an agent or a person who appears to be an agent, the principal is subject to a claim for restitution by that person.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. In general._ Restitution supplements actual and apparent authority when neither is present at the time an agent or apparent agent takes action and the principal accepts the benefit of the action. Restitution does not protect persons who confer benefits officiously. See Restatement Third, Restitution and Unjust Enrichment § 2(4) and Comment _f_ (Discussion Draft, 2000). The rule stated in this section creates restitutionary claims against a principal that may be asserted by third parties.

_b. Restitution to third party._ The rule stated in this section requires a principal to make restitution when the principal has accepted a benefit resulting from the actions of an agent or apparent agent and the principal's benefit is at the expense of a third party. When this section is applicable, the principal has a duty to make restitution even though the agent acted without actual authority and the doctrine of apparent authority stated in § 2.03 is unavailable.

**Illustration:**

1\. A is the head of procurement for P, a subdivision of the federal government. A's superiors within P have limited A's authority to purchase goods to a specified dollar amount. A contracts with T to buy goods in an amount that exceeds the limit imposed on A. T delivers the goods and all are put to use within P. T has a claim in restitution against P.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ The principle stated in this section is more focused and explicitly connected to agency than the counterpart in Restatement Second, Agency. Section 8 C in Restatement Second, Agency, states that "\[a\] person who has been unjustly enriched at the expense of another is required to make restitution to the other." See also Restatement Second, Contracts § 370 ("\[a\] party is entitled to restitution under the rules stated in this Restatement only to the extent that he has conferred a benefit on the other party by way of part performance or reliance").

_a. In general._ For further discussion of the components of unjust enrichment, see Lord Goff & Gareth Jones, The Law of Restitution 11-75, 586-591 (1998). See also George E. Palmer, The Law of Restitution (1978).

_b. Restitution to third party._ Illustration 1 applies the doctrine of [_Campbell v. TVA, 421 F.2d 293 (5th Cir.1969)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-SMW0-0039-Y120-00000-00&context=1530671), as analyzed in 3 George E. Palmer, The Law of Restitution 216-217 (1978). Apparent authority is not a basis on which to hold P because P is a subdivision of the federal government. See § 2.03, Comment _g._ On the acceptance of benefit by an agent, see [_Matarese v. Moore-McCormack Lines, 158 F.2d 631, 635 (2d Cir. 1946)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-3T90-003B-0394-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Butcher & Willis Consulting Eng'rs, Planners & Architects v. Smith, 643 P.2d 1156 (Kan.App. 1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-4KD0-003F-D0WJ-00000-00&context=1530671) (act of attorney for estate in demanding and accepting land survey bound estate in quasi-contract to pay for services rendered; mere appointment of attorney for estate created apparent authority to contract for routine services).

Restatement of the Law, Third, Agency

Copyright (c) 2006, The American Law Institute

**End of Document**

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 3.01_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-401S-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 3- Creation and Termination of Authority and Agency Relationships_ > _Topic 1- Creating and Evidencing Actual Authority_**

**§ 3.01 Creation of Actual Authority**

**Actual authority, as defined in § 2.01, is created by a principal's manifestation to an agent that, as reasonably understood by the agent, expresses the principal's assent that the agent take action on the principal's behalf.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Cross-references._ Section 1.03 defines manifestation. Section 2.01 defines actual authority. Whether an agent's understanding is reasonable is determined as stated in § 2.02. Section 3.02 governs formalities that may be requisite to establishing actual authority. A principal may ratify an agent's acts when the agent did not act with actual authority, thereby replicating the effects of actual authority, as stated in §§ 4.01 and 4.02.

_b. Manifestation an essential requirement._ As defined in § 2.01, an agent's actual authority originates with expressive conduct by the principal toward the agent by which the principal manifests assent to action by the agent with legal consequences for the principal. A principal's unexpressed willingness that another act as agent does not create actual authority. The principal must make a manifestation, as defined in § 1.03, that expresses this willingness. The manifestation may be made directly by the principal to the agent or may reach the agent through a more circuitous route. The principal's unexpressed reservations and qualifications do not reduce the agent's actual authority.

**Illustrations:**

1\. P, a producer and bottler of beverages, retains A as P's agent with authority to purchase tea leaves on P's behalf. P executes a power of attorney that authorizes A to purchase ingredients and containers of all kinds. After executing the power, P places it in a desk drawer and does not mention it to A, nor does P in any other way indicate to A that P has expanded A's authority. A enters into a contract that purportedly binds P to purchase bottles from T. A lacks actual authority to make the contract. P is not bound by the contract unless apparent authority, defined in § 2.03, is present, or unless P ratifies the contract under rules stated in §§ 4.01-4.08. Section 6.10 states A's liability to T on A's implied warranty of authority.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that P mails the power of attorney to A. A does not receive it until after A enters into the contract that purportedly binds P to purchase bottles from T. A lacked actual authority to make the contract. P is not a party to a contract with T unless apparent authority, defined in § 2.03, is present or unless P ratifies the contract under rules stated in §§ 4.01-4.08. Section 6.10 states A's liability to T on A's implied warranty of authority.

3\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that P, intending to mail the power of attorney to A, misaddresses the envelope to A's friend B. When B opens the envelope, B realizes the error and gives the power of attorney to A. A then enters into the contract purportedly binding P to purchase bottles from T. A has actual authority to make the contract.

Actual authority may exist although there is no contract between a principal and agent; a relationship of agency does not require that the principal or the agent receive consideration from the other. See § 1.01, Comment _d._ It is not necessary to the existence of actual authority that an actor promise or otherwise undertake to act as agent. However, an actor is not an agent as defined in § 1.01 unless the actor manifests assent or otherwise consents to a relationship of agency. The agent's manifestation need not be made explicitly, nor be made directly to the principal.

**Illustration:**

4\. P, a dealer in used books, writes to A, who purchases books on P's behalf, stating the titles of specific books and telling A to purchase one copy of each and ship it to P immediately. A does as directed. A acts with actual authority.

A person may request an action from another, indicating that no further communication should precede the action. If the action is taken it is inferred that the actor consented to act as an agent unless the actor manifests a contrary intention or unless the circumstances so indicate. The inference is strengthened if the action is one that only an agent with actual authority could properly do.

**Illustration:**

5\. Same facts as Illustration 4, except that P tells A to draw on P's bank account to pay for the books before shipping them to P. A makes the purchase in A's own name, pays with funds from P's account, then dies. No other facts demonstrate A's intention. It is inferred that A purchased the books as P's agent.

_c. Statutory variations._ Some statutes ascribe the legal consequences of agency to relationships that fall outside the common-law definition of agency. See § 1.01, Comment _d._ Additionally, statutes applicable to relationships that are within the definition in § 1.01 may vary the common-law requisites for creating actual authority. For example, the Soldiers' and Sailors' Civil Relief Act of 1940 provides an automatic extension for powers of attorney previously executed by service members that expire on their own terms while a service member is in missing status, effective for the period the service member is missing. The effect of the statute is to extend the agent's actual authority beyond the term specified in the principal's manifestation.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ This section is comparable in substance to Restatement Second, Agency §§ 15-16 and 26.

_b. Manifestation an essential requirement._ Illustration 2 makes the same point as Restatement Second, Agency § 26, Illustration 1. The point is rarely raised directly in litigated cases because, as in Illustrations 1 and 2, it would be difficult for T to establish that P executed the power. A rare reported case raising the point is [_Ruggles v. American Cent. Ins. Co., 114 N.Y. 415 (1889)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRK-P170-003D-N4R8-00000-00&context=1530671), in which an insurer sent its agent a letter increasing the agent's authority to that which third parties who dealt with the agent reasonably believed the agent to have. The letter did not arrive until after the agent issued the policy involved in the case. The court held that the position in which the principal placed the agent created apparent authority. Alternatively, the court held actual authority to be present. The alternate holding in _Ruggles_ is not consistent with the treatment of actual authority in this section and in Restatement Second, Agency § 26 because the agent issued the policy prior to the arrival of the principal's letter and the principal made no other manifestation to the agent augmenting the agent's authority. _Ruggles_ itself does not address whether the insurer would have claims against the agent for acting without actual authority. The principal's letter did not ratify the agent's act in _Ruggles_ because the principal lacked knowledge of the act. See § 4.06, Comment _b._ However, an agent and a principal may develop a practice in which the principal does not object to actions by the agent that exceed prior conferrals of authority, and on that basis the agent may reasonably believe that the principal wishes the agent to take action. See § 2.02, Comments _e_ and _f._ See also 1 Floyd R. Mechem, A Treatise on the Law of Agency § 732, at 516-517 n.52 (1914) (citing _Ruggles_ to support proposition that secret limits on agent's authority when insurance company "puts an agent into the field with apparent authority to accept applications, issue policies, receive payment of premiums, and the like . . . are designed merely to control the manner of acting, and a disobedience of them, while it might make the agent liable to the company, would not relieve the company of liability to a third person who has dealt with the agent in ignorance of them").

For a representative statement as to the basis of actual authority, see [_AgriStor Leasing v. Farrow, 826 F.2d 732, 737 (8th Cir.1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-88V0-001B-K075-00000-00&context=1530671) (determination "of an express or implied agency focuses on communications and contacts between the principal and the agent"). See also [_Senior Commodity Co. v. Econo-Rail Corp., 2000 WL 350508 (Tex.App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4002-3J40-0039-44F0-00000-00&context=1530671) (actual authority not present where actor denied agency relationship with defendants and plaintiff was unable to establish any actions taken by defendants that could manifest defendants' intention to actor that he act as their agent; testimony by third party's representative that he perceived actor as agent for defendants did not establish that actor had actual authority because it did not identify any actions taken by defendants that would circumstantially indicate defendants granted authority to actor).

On the irrelevance of a principal's unexpressed reservations and qualifications, see [_Pohl v. United Airlines, Inc., 213 F.3d 336 (7th Cir.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:407D-KTR0-0038-X2M9-00000-00&context=1530671) (lawyer had actual authority to settle plaintiff's claim against employer although plaintiff believed he had ultimate authority to approve and disapprove settlement and that settlement was not final until he signed off on it; terms of retainer agreement did not reflect plaintiff's belief and granted law firm power of attorney to execute all documents including settlement agreements, and lawyer continually communicated proposed settlement terms to plaintiff, who registered no objection).

When a principal's manifestation of an agent's authority is stated in a power of attorney, interpretation of the power is generally subject to contractual rules of interpretation, including the principle that any single provision in an instrument should be read in the context of the entire instrument. See, e.g., [_Schmitz v. Firstar Bank, 658 N.W.2d 442, 448-449 (Wis.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4876-J7N0-0039-43JT-00000-00&context=1530671) (limited power of attorney held by financial advisor did not authorize advisor's endorsement and deposit of checks from investment company payable to client when power did not explicitly authorize such transactions and client's holdings with investment company were not listed in power; single sentence at end of power referring to other investments and accounts of client should be read in context of provisions in power requiring listing of specific investments held by client, indicating that advisor's authority was limited to listed investments).

_c. Statutory variations._ The provision in the Soldiers' and Sailors' Civil Relief Act extending the effective date of powers of attorney is 50 App. U.S.C. § 591 (2000), _54 Stat. 1178_. The provision is limited to powers that designate as agent a spouse, parent, or other relative. The provision is inapplicable if the power expressly provides that the agent's authority shall expire even though the service member is missing at the time. See 50 App. U.S.C. § 591(b) (2000).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 3.02_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-401T-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 3- Creation and Termination of Authority and Agency Relationships_ > _Topic 1- Creating and Evidencing Actual Authority_**

**§ 3.02 Formal Requirements**

**If the law requires a writing or record signed by the principal to evidence an agent's authority to bind a principal to a contract or other transaction, the principal is not bound in the absence of such a writing or record. A principal may be estopped to assert the lack of such a writing or record when a third party has been induced to make a detrimental change in position by the reasonable belief that an agent has authority to bind the principal that is traceable to a manifestation made by the principal.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Cross-references._ Section 2.01 defines actual authority. Section 3.01 states how actual authority is created.

_b. Equal-dignity requirements._ Creating actual authority under § 3.01 does not require a writing or other formality. However, legislation in many states imposes what is often termed an "equal dignity" requirement for the creation of authority or agency applicable to specific types of agreements. As with the Statute of Frauds more generally, the purpose is to prevent fraud and perjury by safeguarding the principal against agreements made by an agent who lacks authority. Under an equal-dignity rule, if a transaction is unenforceable unless the party to be charged has agreed in writing to be bound, an agent's authority to enter into such a transaction on behalf of a principal must likewise be in writing. Such rules also apply to ratification. See § 4.02, Comment _e._ If an agent acted with apparent authority as defined in § 2.03 but no writing or record evidences the agent's authority, the principal is not bound to contracts or other transactions to which an equal-dignity rule applies.

A writing includes "any intentional reduction to tangible form," see Restatement Second, Contracts § 131, Comment _d._ A record includes an electronic record that is given the legal effect of a writing. Electronic records are given the legal effect of paper-and-ink writings by legislation, including the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act § 7 (1999) and the federal Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act § 101(a) (2000). A writing is signed by the principal by affixing a symbol that evidences intention to authenticate the writing. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 134. Similarly, an electronic record is signed by attaching to it an electronic sound, symbol, or process that is adopted with the intention of signing the record. See Uniform Electronic Transactions Act § 2(8).

Equal-dignity rules are either elements in a statute of frauds or are stated in separate statutes. Equal-dignity rules apply when a statute of frauds requires the underlying agreement to be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged, including agreements for the sale or lease of land and suretyship agreements. On the scope of contracts to which statutes of frauds apply, see Restatement Second, Contracts § 110. Equal-dignity rules are inapplicable when a writing is required by agreement but not by law. However, a contract may not be binding when a contractual provision itself requires that the principal sign personally.

Equal-dignity rules have been applied with some stringency. For example, when one spouse acts as the agent of the other who owns property, or as an agent when both spouses own property jointly, the equal-dignity rule requires the agent-spouse's authorization to be in writing. If a principal executes a contract but leaves blanks that are completed by the agent, the contract may not be enforceable against the principal if the third party has notice that the agent may fill in the blanks only when the agent is authorized in writing to do so.

Nevertheless, the application of equal-dignity rules should not outrun their purpose. Equal-dignity rules do not apply when an action on a contract is brought by the principal against a third party. An equal-dignity rule may be satisfied by a writing or record executed by a principal following the agent's execution of the contract or transaction. Moreover, if an agent has acted without actual authority, as well as without prior written authorization, the principal would be able to ratify the transaction by a signed writing, see § 4.01, Comment _e,_ so long as the principal ratifies prior to a manifestation from the third party that the third party has withdrawn from the transaction, see § 4.05(1). Additionally, an equal-dignity rule does not create a defense that an agent may assert against the third party, such as when the agent misrepresents authority. On an agent's warranties of authority, see § 6.10. On an agent's representations, see § 6.11.

_c. Exceptions._ Exceptions to equal-dignity rules fall into two general categories. First, a principal may be estopped from asserting lack of written authorization as a defense when the principal has made a manifestation of the agent's authority that induces detrimental reliance by a third party. See Comment _d._ Second, equal-dignity rules are inapplicable to specific types of principals and agents.

Most equal-dignity statutes, like most cases, distinguish between individual and incorporated principals and do not require written authorization for a corporate officer to enter into a transaction that requires a writing to be enforceable against the corporation. The rationale for the exception is that a corporation can act only through individuals authorized to take action on its behalf. Officers receive written authorization when their offices are created through the corporation's charter, bylaws, or a board resolution. This exception is sometimes termed applicable to "executive officers," to differentiate duly appointed officers from the retention of a nonofficer agent to enter into a contract to which the Statute of Frauds applies. An "executive officer" for this purpose is not necessarily one for the distinct purposes of the federal securities laws. See Principles of Corporate Governance: Analysis and Recommendations § 1.27, Comment _c._ Some authority applies the exception for "executive officers" to partnerships. The equal-dignity principle is also inapplicable when an agent acts only as an amanuensis who signs at the principal's request. The narrow role played by an amanuensis, plus the principal's position of control, suffice to protect the principal.

_d. Estoppel to assert lack of writing or record._ The court has discretion to deny a principal the benefit of a defense, created by statute, that bars enforcement of a contract or transaction made by an agent when the agent's authority is not evidenced by a writing or record that satisfies the statute. When a principal makes a manifestation about the agent's authority, albeit in a form that does not satisfy the statute, the manifestation may induce a detrimental change in position by the third party. There is potential for injustice if a principal, by making a manifestation as to an agent's authority, could induce a third party to commit to a transaction and to change position in reliance on the appearance of the principal's commitment, all the while holding in reserve the option of defeating enforcement if alternatives to the transaction with the third party become more attractive. For purposes of this doctrine, a detrimental change in position occurs when the third party acts or forbears to act and, as a result, transfers money or other assets, incurs liability, or loses an opportunity of value. Such a change of position does not require part performance of the transaction with the principal.

**Illustrations:**

1\. The applicable statute requires a writing signed by the principal to establish that an agent has authority to bind the principal to a lease of real estate for a term of more than one year's duration. P owns Blackacre, a farm. In a manner that does not satisfy the statute, P tells T that A is P's property manager with authority to execute all leases, regardless of their term. A enters into a five-year lease of Blackacre to T. Thereafter, before T makes any payments to P or A or otherwise detrimentally changes position, P notifies T that A lacked authority to bind P and that P repudiates the lease of Blackacre. P will not be estopped to deny A's authority.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that T, after entering into the lease but before receiving notice from P that A lacks authority and that P repudiates the lease, enters into a five-year lease of farming equipment for which T has no other use. P may be estopped to deny A's authority.

The estoppel doctrine stated in this section operates simply to bar a defense otherwise available to a principal on the basis of an agent's lack of written authorization. The fact that a principal is so estopped does not preclude the assertion of other defenses. The court has discretion to determine the appropriate remedy in light of all relevant circumstances, comparable to the discretion stated in Restatement Second, Contracts § 139 and Comments _b_ and _d._

As defined in § 1.03, a manifestation may be made in many different ways. It is not necessary to establish a direct communication from the principal to the third party, or an affirmatively stated explicit assertion of the agent's authority, if the principal has notice that the third party may infer from the principal's conduct that the principal consents to the agent's action.

**Illustration:**

3\. P, the owner of Blackacre, learns that T is interested in purchasing Blackacre, and that T plans to fund the purchase with money borrowed from B. P tells B that A is P's property manager with authority to sell property. P is aware that B may repeat P's statement to T. If B does so, P has made a manifestation to T as to A's authority.

_e. Contracts between principal and agent._ In general, equal-dignity rules are inapplicable to the relationship between principal and agent themselves. For example, if an agent sues the principal to recover commissions from a transaction that is enforceable against the principal only if the agent's authorization is in writing, the lack of written authorization in itself will not defeat the agent's claim. However, in many states, statutes of frauds impose a writing requirement on contracts to pay a commission to a real-estate broker or business-opportunity broker. Many statutes of frauds also impose a writing requirement on contracts that are not capable of performance within one year from the making of the contracts. See § 4.04, Comment c, for discussion of this requirement when a corporation adopts a contract purportedly made on its behalf prior to its existence.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ The first sentence in this section corresponds in substance to Restatement Second, Agency § 30(2). Statutes of frauds that impose writing requirements on contracts between principal and agent are covered in Restatement Second, Agency § 468(2) and (3). The second sentence in this section is comparable in substance to § 31(1). However, this section makes explicit that the third party's change in position must be detrimental, while § 31(1) refers only to a change in position "in reasonable reliance. . . ." Other treatments of estoppel in Restatement Second are not entirely consistent. Section 8 B(3) defines "change of position" to mean "payment of money, expenditure of labor, suffering a loss or subjection to legal liability." Comment _d_ to § 8 states that "one basing his claim upon the rules of estoppel must show not merely reliance . . . but also such a change of position that it would be unjust for the speaker to deny the truth of his words."

_b. Equal-dignity requirements._ For the point that the purpose of equal-dignity statutes is the prevention of fraud and perjury, see [_Deal v. Dickson, 209 S.E.2d 214, 214 (Ga. 1974)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-4990-003F-J550-00000-00&context=1530671) (court declines to order specific performance of sales agreement as to one-half of undivided interest in property owned by spouse, whose spouse entered into agreement in his own name to sell property but lacked written authorization to sell property interest of spouse; plaintiff did not go into possession and make improvements, and nonagent spouse did not accept any benefit from contract).

For a useful discussion of electronic records and signatures, see Robert A. Wittie & Jane K. Winn, Electronic Records and Signatures under the Federal E-Sign Legislation and the UETA, [_56 Bus. Law. 293 (2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=legalnews&id=urn:contentItem:429G-T6P0-0027-B080-00000-00&context=1530671).

Equal-dignity statutes fall into two general patterns. Some equal-dignity requirements are embodied in a statute of frauds. See, e.g., N.Y. Gen. Oblig. Law § 5-703(2) (agreement for sale or lease of land not binding on principal unless subscribed by "lawful agent authorized in writing"). Statutes comparable in form include Ala. Code § 8-9-2; Del. Code Ann., tit. 6, § 2714(a); Ill. Comp. Stat., ch. 740, § 80(2); Miss. Code Ann. § 15-3-1; N.H. Rev. Stat. Ann. §§ 506(1); 477(9); Okla. Stat. tit. 15, § 136(5); 33 Penn. Cons. Stat. § 1. Louisiana law incorporates an equal-dignity rule for some classes of contracts, although it technically never adopted the English Statute of Frauds. See [_Fleet Fin., Inc. v. Loan Arranger, Inc., 604 So. 2d 656 (La.App.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-3SF0-008T-X457-00000-00&context=1530671). The other pattern of statutes is a general and freestanding embodiment of an equal-dignity rule. Examples include Cal. Civ. Code § 2309 (providing that authority to enter into a contract that is required by law to be in writing can only be given by written instrument) and Ga. Code § 10-6-2 (where exercise or performance of agency is by written instrument, agency must be created by written instrument).

When a state does not have a separate "equal dignity" statute, and the general statute of frauds does not require that an agent be authorized in writing, it is generally held that oral authorization is sufficient. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 135, Comment _b._ Some statutes of frauds expressly contemplate that a writing that satisfies the statute may be signed by the party to be charged or an agent authorized to do so, but do not require written authorization. For example, U.C.C. § 2-201 requires signature "by the party against which enforcement is sought or by the party's authorized agent or broker."

For the proposition that no equal-dignity rule applies to writing requirements imposed by agreement, see [_Ripani v. Liberty Loan Corp., 157 Cal.Rptr. 272 (Cal.App.1979)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S11-T3F0-003C-R0MM-00000-00&context=1530671). The principal may not be bound when the contract requires that the principal sign personally. See 20/[_20 Vision Ctr., Inc. v. Hudgens, 345 S.E.2d 330 (Ga. 1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-2DD0-003F-J0X1-00000-00&context=1530671).

For cases applying the equal-dignity rule to spouses acting as agents on behalf of property owned by the other spouse or jointly, see, e.g., [_O'Banion v. Paradiso, 393 P.2d 682 (Cal. 1964)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRK-MNG0-003C-H27R-00000-00&context=1530671); [_East Piedmont 120 Assocs., L.P. v. Sheppard, 434 S.E.2d 101 (Ga. App.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX8-BFW0-003G-P1VH-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Cippitelli Bros. Towing & Collision, Inc. v. Rosenfeld, 566 N.Y.S.2d 950 (App.Div.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S2R-8RY0-003V-B31D-00000-00&context=1530671).

On the application of an equal-dignity rule when an agent lacking written authorization fills in blanks in a contract executed by the principal, see [_Monte Carlo Motors, Inc. v. Volkswagenwerk, G.M.B.H, 1 Cal. Rptr. 920 (Cal.App.1960)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRK-M2W0-003C-334P-00000-00&context=1530671) (automobile manufacturer not estopped to assert statute of frauds as defense against enforcement of alleged oral wholesale distributorship agreement made by plaintiff with manufacturer's agent when agent filled in plaintiff's name in blank on manufacturer's printed-form distributorship agreement that had already been signed by manufacturer's general agent in Germany; plaintiff knew signature on agreement preceded completion of blanks).

For the proposition that noncompliance with an equal-dignity rule creates a defense for the principal but not the third party, see [_Mitchell v. Locurto, 179 P.2d 848 (Cal.App.1947)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S1W-KHY0-003V-P13B-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Fiorito v. Yaskulski, 228 N.Y.S.2d 598 (App.Div.1962)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRT-2J70-003C-C1P9-00000-00&context=1530671). But cf. [_Aubry v. Gates, 199 N.Y.S.2d 80 (Sup.1959)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRT-3MN0-003C-C3CS-00000-00&context=1530671) (vendor seeks damages from vendees for breach of alleged contract to sell property, when agreement signed by spouse as agent for other spouse, lacking written authorization; court holds that damages are not recoverable because vendee was entitled to a formal contract, properly signed by all parties or their properly authorized agents, and that such contract was a condition precedent to defendants' obligation to perform; unclear whether such entitlement stems exclusively from binder agreement).

On the impact of equal-dignity rules on claims as between the agent and the third party, the statement in Comment _b_ is counter to [_Nalley v. Whitaker, 115 S.E.2d 790 (Ga.App. 1960)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-BMT0-003F-J3WB-00000-00&context=1530671) (third party had duty to inquire whether special agent had written authorization to enter into 15-year lease; failure to inquire bars third party's claim against agent for misrepresentation of authority). But cf. 20/[_20 Vision Center, Inc. v. Hudgens, 345 S.E.2d 330, 334 n.4 (Ga.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-2DD0-003F-J0X1-00000-00&context=1530671) (stating that doctrine in _Nalley_ should be understood as creating question of fact in each case whether plaintiff's negligence bars claim that agent acted without apparent authority when plaintiff did not inquire into written authority). See also [_Mott v. Kaldes, 135 A. 764 (Pa.1927)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRH-VS00-003G-X1CF-00000-00&context=1530671) (third party barred from recovery against agent when third party knows agent acts with only oral authorization).

_c. Exceptions._ As to corporate agents, see [_Rohm & Haas v. Gainesville Paint & Supply Co., 483 S.E.2d 888, 891 (Ga.App.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SG2-MGD0-003G-P32H-00000-00&context=1530671) (equal-dignity rule bars enforcement of guarantee signed by employee of affiliate corporation in name of corporation's president at president's oral direction; corporation did not have practice or normal course of orally authorizing nonemployees to sign for corporation, and no evidence supported president's authority to appoint agents orally); [_Commission on Ecumenical Mission & Relations of United Presbyterian Church v. Roger Gray, Ltd., 267 N.E.2d 467, 469-470 (N.Y.1971)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-VV00-003C-C3R5-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporate officer is not an "agent" for purposes of equal-dignity statute; employee characterized by corporation as a "managing agent" is not a corporate officer). See also [_Whiteway Neon-Ad, Inc. v. Opportunities Industrialization Ctr., 252 S.E.2d 604, 605 (Ga.1979)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-3JN0-003F-J159-00000-00&context=1530671) (under Georgia Code Ann. § 4-105, corporation may create agents through its "usual mode of transacting business--i.e. shareholder action in the adoption of charters, by-laws, resolutions and similar conduct vesting corporate agents with authority to act"; corporate agents' authority to execute sealed document need not be conferred by sealed document). For the exception for "executive officers," see [_Kagel v. First Commonwealth Co., 409 F.Supp. 1396, 1399 (N.D.Cal.1973)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-J900-003B-324J-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_534 F.2d 194 (9th Cir.1976)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-23G0-0039-M0BD-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Jeppi v. Brockman Holding Co., 206 P.2d 847, 850-851 (Cal.1949)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRK-RV50-003C-H008-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Commission on Ecumenical Mission, 267 N.E.2d at 469-470_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-VV00-003C-C3R5-00000-00&context=1530671). For an application of the exception to a managing agent, see [_Showers v. Rober, 267 P. 884 (Cal. App.1928)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S1W-NBR0-003V-P48K-00000-00&context=1530671).

Some authority supports extension of the exception to partnerships, treating any partner as the equivalent of a corporate officer for purposes of an equal-dignity requirement. See [_Cinema North Corp. v. Plaza at Latham Assocs., 867 F.2d 135 (2d Cir.1989)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-D8V0-003B-50NY-00000-00&context=1530671) (predicting that New York courts would extend exception for corporate officers to a partner acting within scope of partnership's usual business); cf. [_Maretz v. 595 Corporate Circle, 780 A.2d 43, 50 (Conn.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:442K-DB70-0039-4213-00000-00&context=1530671) (statutory requirement that deed-like document authorize signing of listing agreement inapplicable when principal can act only through its agents; all general partners of partnership signed listing agreement as agents of partnership). Other authority resolves the question by applying principles of apparent authority in the partnership context. See [_Wyss v. Albee, 532 N.W.2d 444 (Wis.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-VJY0-003G-307K-00000-00&context=1530671) (under partnership statute, partner with apparent authority who acts in partnership name may bind partnership by execution of "any instrument"; court interprets statute-of-frauds provision requiring agent to be expressly authorized to be inapplicable to partner who apparently acts in ordinary course of partnership business in transactions with third party who does not know that partner is acting beyond scope of authority).

On the exception for an agent who signs as the principal's amanuensis, see [_In re Estate of Stephens, 49 P.3d 1093 (Cal.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:46CB-PKS0-0039-42P6-00000-00&context=1530671).

_d. Estoppel to assert lack of writing or record._ On the basis for estoppel, see, e.g., [_Augusta Surgical Ctr., Inc. v. Walton & Heard Office Venture, 508 S.E.2d 666 (Ga.App.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3VPY-PDJ0-0039-44VS-00000-00&context=1530671) (principal's conduct may have led person of ordinary prudence to believe agent had authority to execute contract to buy property when principal's communications with other party reflected its intention to proceed with transaction); [_Chleboun v. Varilek, 136 N.W.2d 348 (S.D.1965)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S71-44S0-003G-D08K-00000-00&context=1530671) (jury could properly determine whether plaintiff estopped to deny spouse's authority to enter into lease for 5 years with defendant when plaintiff led defendant to believe spouse had such authority by communicating satisfaction that defendant would be in residence after learning of lease term).

Some jurisdictions permit actions grounded in implied contract although the underlying contract does not comply with a statute of frauds. See, e.g., [_T.G. Slater & Son, Inc. v. Donald P. & Patricia A. Brennan LLC, 385 F.3d 836, 844-845 (4th Cir. 2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4DFC-7V20-0038-X2PT-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Virginia law; complaint stated claim that real-estate broker performed valuable services for prospective purchaser of property with expectation of payment). On other issues under statutes imposing writing requirements on brokerage contracts, see [_Rosbach v. Industry Trading Co., 81 F.Supp.2d 522 (S.D.N.Y.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YJY-K1W0-0038-Y4KH-00000-00&context=1530671) (N.Y. Gen. Obl. L. § 5-701 applicable to oral agreement in which defendants agreed to discontinue suit against plaintiff in exchange for plaintiff's finding an investor to lend \$ 80,000 to defendants; statute requires writing for contract "to pay compensation for services in negotiating a loan. . . ."); [_Reiss v. GAN, S.A., 78 F.Supp.2d 147, 156-157 (S.D.N.Y.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3X3B-DF80-0038-Y544-00000-00&context=1530671), vacated in part on other grounds, [_235 F.3d 738 (2d Cir.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:420S-8RC0-0038-X377-00000-00&context=1530671) (exemption for "duly licensed" real-estate agents created by N.Y. Gen. Obl. § 5-701(a)(10) applicable to transactions involving sales of businesses unaccompanied by a transaction in real estate).

For illustrative instances of conduct alleged to constitute a detrimental change in position, see [_Cinema North Corp., 867 F.2d at 144_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-D8V0-003B-50NY-00000-00&context=1530671) (plaintiff made expenditures and lost alternate business opportunity); [_Kerner v. Hughes Tool Co., 128 Cal.Rptr. 839 (Cal.App.1976)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S11-V1F0-003C-R014-00000-00&context=1530671) (plaintiff made expenditures in time and money); [_Augusta Surgical Center, 508 S.E.2d at 669_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3VPY-PDJ0-0039-44VS-00000-00&context=1530671) (plaintiff ceased advertising for leased space and failed to seek renewal of mortgage); [_Chleboun, 136 N.W.2d at 350_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S71-44S0-003G-D08K-00000-00&context=1530671) (plaintiff bought farm equipment on credit).

On the importance of understanding the principal's manifestation in context, see, e.g., [_Cinema North Corp., 867 F.2d at 141_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-D8V0-003B-50NY-00000-00&context=1530671) (holding trial court could exercise its discretion to decline to enforce statute of frauds when person who signed lease on behalf of partnership as its Vice President explained he did so carelessly; plaintiff may be able to establish that partnership's only general partner knew that purported Vice President was so holding himself out, knew that purported Vice President lacked written authority to bind partnership, and intended that plaintiff rely on purported Vice President's misrepresentations). The cases are not entirely consistent as to the specificity necessary for the principal's manifestation. Compare [_Kerner, 128 Cal.Rptr. 839_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S11-V1F0-003C-R014-00000-00&context=1530671) (third party reasonably relied on routine contracting without written authorization by an agent to whom third party was referred by principal's managing director; third party could reasonably conclude from principal's conduct either that agent had written authority or that principal would not object to its absence) with [_Dweck v. Altman, 208 N.Y.S.2d 294 (Sup.1960)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-CR70-003C-D13W-00000-00&context=1530671) (no estoppel when principal never represented that agent had written authority or that principal would not object to lack of written authority). See also [_McGirr v. Gulf Oil Corp., 115 Cal.Rptr. 902 (Cal.App.1974)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-8HF0-003C-J0CH-00000-00&context=1530671) (third party's change of position estopped principal from asserting statute-of-frauds defense as to underlying oral contract to lease service station for 5 years, but lack of any manifestation from principal as to agent's authority defeated estoppel challenge to application of equal-dignity rule; oral contract made by agent, who was not authorized in writing, unenforceable when principal did not knowingly accept any benefit stemming from contract).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 3.03_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-401V-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 3- Creation and Termination of Authority and Agency Relationships_ > _Topic 2- Creating Apparent Authority_**

**§ 3.03 Creation of Apparent Authority**

**Apparent authority, as defined in § 2.03, is created by a person's manifestation that another has authority to act with legal consequences for the person who makes the manifestation, when a third party reasonably believes the actor to be authorized and the belief is traceable to the manifestation.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Cross-references._ Section 1.03 defines manifestation. Section 2.03, Comment _d,_ discusses the requirement that the third party's belief be reasonable.

_b. Manifestation an essential requirement._ As defined in § 2.03, an agent's apparent authority originates with expressive conduct by the principal toward a third party through which the principal manifests assent to action by the agent with legal consequences for the principal. Apparent authority is present only when a third party's belief is traceable to manifestations of the principal. The fact that one party performs a service that facilitates the other's business does not constitute such a manifestation. For example, by clearing securities trades for another firm, a securities broker does not make a manifestation to customers of the firm sending the orders that it acts with the authority of the clearing firm.

A principal may make manifestations regarding an agent's authority in many ways. In some settings, the principal's acts speak so loudly that explicit verbal communication is unnecessary. Similarly, an indirect route of communication between a principal and third party may suffice, especially when it is consistent with practice in the relevant industry.

**Illustration:**

1\. Senior officers of P Corporation have been involved in complicated negotiations for the purchase of custom-designed machinery, to be specially designed by T and manufactured by S Corporation. P Corporation's CEO receives a joint letter from T and the CEO of S Corporation stating that they have scheduled a meeting to finalize terms and that P Corporation should send a representative with authority to commit it. P Corporation's CEO sends A, the Executive Vice President of P Corporation. As to S Corporation and T, P Corporation has manifested that A has authority to commit it to the terms of the transaction.

A principal may also make a manifestation by placing an agent in a defined position in an organization or by placing an agent in charge of a transaction or situation. Third parties who interact with the principal through the agent will naturally and reasonably assume that the agent has authority to do acts consistent with the agent's position or role unless they have notice of facts suggesting that this may not be so. A principal may make an additional manifestation by permitting or requiring the agent to serve as the third party's exclusive channel of communication to the principal. See also § 1.03, Comments _c_ and _e._

A principal may also create apparent authority by actually or apparently authorizing an agent to make representations to third parties concerning the agent's own authority or position, even though the agent's representations by themselves would be insufficient. The determinative question is whether a third party can establish a linkage between statements of authority by the agent and a manifestation of assent by the principal to the making of such statements. If no such linkage can be established, the third party may be able nonetheless to estop the principal from denying the agent's authority under the doctrine stated in § 2.05 if the principal is responsible for the agent's representation, typically because the principal did not exercise reasonable care to prevent the agent from making the representation or to inform the third party of its falsity. Section 6.10, Comment _d,_ and § 6.11(1) address other aspects of agents' statements as to their own authority.

A principal's inaction creates apparent authority when it provides a basis for a third party reasonably to believe the principal intentionally acquiesces in the agent's representations or actions. See § 1.03, Comment _e._ If the third party has observed prior interactions between the agent and the principal, the third party may reasonably believe that a subsequent act or representation by the agent is authorized because it conforms to the prior pattern observed by the third party. The belief is thus traceable to the principal's participation in the pattern and failure to inform the third party that no inferences about the agent's authority should be based upon it. The principal's acquiescence may also be effective to ratify the agent's acts or representations. See § 4.01, Comment _f._

If a principal has given an agent general authority to engage in a class of transactions, subject to limits known only to the agent and the principal, third parties may reasonably believe the agent to be authorized to conduct such transactions and need not inquire into the existence of undisclosed limits on the agent's authority. The agent's apparent authority does not disappear when, unbeknownst to the third party, the agent errs in performing tasks requisite to completing a transaction. Similarly, if a principal has authorized an agent to communicate the principal's decisions to a third party, the third party may reasonably believe the agent's reports. Reasonable belief is not defeated by the third party's knowledge that the agent could not unilaterally make the decision that is the focus of the communication to the third party. See Comment _d_ infra. For the circumstances under which the principal is bound by statements the agent makes that are incidental to transactions in which the agent is authorized to engage, see § 6.11(2) and (3).

In contrast, some courts define apparent authority more narrowly or deny its efficacy altogether when the question is whether a client is bound by a settlement negotiated on the client's behalf by a lawyer. Narrower definitions of apparent authority require a direct manifestation from the client to the opposing party. By retaining a lawyer in a litigated matter, a client does not by that conduct alone create actual or apparent authority for the lawyer to enter into a settlement without the client's assent. See Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 22. A client may create actual or apparent authority for a lawyer to bind the client to a settlement. See id. § 27, Comment _d._ Apparent authority to effect a settlement that binds the client is present when, as in transactions of various sorts involving agents who are not lawyers, the opposing party or lawyer reasonably believes that the lawyer has actual authority to effect a settlement and that belief is traceable to manifestations of the client.

_c. Organizational principals._ Apparent authority is an essential adjunct to actual authority in enabling third parties to deal effectively with organizations. Actual authority, however it may be created within or by an organization, is often less important to those who interact with the organization than is the regular-looking appearance of authority. Apparent authority enables persons who interact with the organization to treat the agent's act or statement as dispositive, without further inquiry directed elsewhere within the organization, in the absence of circumstances suggestive of self-dealing or other irregularity.

**Illustrations:**

2\. P Corporation claims that T Corporation owes it \$ 10,000; T Corporation disputes the debt. P Corporation sends T Corporation a letter stating that all communications regarding disputed debts, including instruments tendered in full satisfaction, are to be sent to the attention of A. A has actual authority to endorse and deposit checks to P Corporation's account. A reports to P Corporation's Chief Financial Officer, who tells A that A does not have authority to resolve disputed claims without approval from the Chief Financial Officer. T Corporation sends a check for \$ 5000 to P Corporation, addressed to the attention of A, which A receives. The check is marked conspicuously "Tendered in Full Satisfaction of Claim." A endorses the check and deposits it in P Corporation's bank account. A's deposit of the check is effective under U.C.C. § 3-311 to discharge T Corporation's claim against P Corporation.

3\. Same facts as Illustration 2, except that T Corporation sends the check with the payment slip for P Corporation's monthly bill to the regional office to which P's bill states payment should be made. The envelope is opened by B, a clerk, who endorses and deposits the check to a bank account of P Corporation. B's actions do not discharge P Corporation's claim against T Corporation because T Corporation did not send the check to the person designated by P Corporation under U.C.C. § 3-311(c)(1).

To create actual authority to act on behalf of an organization is usually more cumbersome than doing so for an individual principal. An organization could do an act through the specific decision of its governing body or, in some instances, its members; directors of a business corporation could, for example, adopt a resolution binding the corporation to a contract. Most organizational activity takes place less formally, through the individual acts of the organization's agents--its officers, managers, and employees. To create actual authority within an organization implicates formal procedures of institutional governance, as does evidencing that authority to third parties. An officer's authority is often created directly and explicitly by an organizational bylaw applicable to the office holder. Like a natural person, an organizational principal may certify an agent's actual authority by making a specific statement to a third party about the nature or extent of an agent's authority. In an organizational setting, such a statement could come from the organization's governing body. If the organization is a corporation, a formal route through which its board of directors could create actual authority is to adopt a resolution authorizing an officer or other agent to enter into a transaction. For purposes of properly evidencing the officer's or agent's authority, the corporation's secretary certifies that the resolution has been duly adopted and affixes the corporate seal to the resolution. A third party may rely on the statement of authority in such a resolution that has been adopted by the organization's governing body and need not inquire further into the agent's authority. More commonly, and less formally, superior agents authorize subordinate coagents. For definitions of superior and subordinate coagents, see § 1.04(9). A superior agent's authority to authorize may itself have been conferred by the organization's governing body or by a higher superior agent. Comment _e_ addresses more specific issues regarding the apparent authority of corporate officers and contrasts the consequences of the corporate form with those of partnership and limited-liability companies.

In large organizations, direct statements from the governing board that have immediate operational impact occur relatively infrequently. Most organizations are operated by their officers and managers; the governing body often plays no direct role in the myriad of decisions and transactions required for the organization's routine operation. As a consequence, delegation of authority is significant in the organizational context because it reflects a sensible accommodation of agency doctrine to the common operating practices of organizations. Most people proceed on many matters on the assumption that ordinary-looking instances of delegation are effective. For example, it is not necessary to see an exemplified resolution of the board of directors of a car-rental company reasonably to conclude that a counter agent has authority to bind the company to the rental agreement.

Moreover, the fact that any given organization has a particular legal form does not mean that its actual operational practices conform perfectly to legal form. Organizations vary widely in the degree to which they operate informally or formally in making decisions and in interacting with third parties external to the organization. Organizations also vary in the degree to which authority is associated solely with a position within an organizational hierarchy or is personalized to a particular individual regardless of the organization's formal association of authority with defined positions.

**Illustrations:**

4\. A is the Dean of the medical school of P University. When A was appointed, P University's board of trustees issued a press release stating, "We are confident in A's ability to improve the quality of the medical school's faculty and will trust A's judgment in all employment decisions. This is unusual in a university, but the school needs bold leadership." T, a faculty member at another medical school, reads the press release, which is widely publicized. T knows that four of T's colleagues received and accepted offers of employment from A and joined the medical-school faculty of P University. T receives an offer of employment from A. As to T, A has apparent authority to make the offer. It is reasonable for T to believe that A has authority. Although a dean's unilateral possession of such authority in a university would be unusual, the institution's governing board has explicitly stated that the dean has authority.

5\. Same facts as Illustration 4, except that, unbeknownst to T, the president of P University directs A to make no more offers of employment without the President's approval. The bylaws of P University provide that all deans are subject to the "general executive authority" of the University's President. A makes an offer to T, who has no notice of the bylaw provision. A has apparent authority to make the offer, although A lacks actual authority to do so. Extending an offer to T contravenes a directive from A's organizational superior as to a matter that, under P's bylaws, falls within the superior's province of authority.

6\. Same facts as Illustration 5, except that T has notice of the President's directive and also has notice that A hired four of T's colleagues without obtaining prior consent from the President. Whether T's belief in A's authority is reasonable under the circumstances is a question for the trier of fact.

Apparent authority in an organizational setting may also arise from the fact that a person occupies a type of position that customarily carries specific authority although the organization has withheld such authority from that agent. See Comment _b_ to § 1.03. If a third party knows that an agent is an agent for an organization but is unaware of the agent's position, or unaware of the authority conventionally associated with the position, the principal is not accountable for the third party's belief in the agent's authority. Moreover, it is important to distinguish between a third party's opinion of the organization itself, its credibility, and its reliability, and the basis for the third party's belief that the agent is authorized to commit the organization. The third party may have a reasonable ground to believe that the organization is reputable and will honor its commitments but lack a basis reasonably to believe that the agent is authorized to bind the organization. Such a basis would be lacking, for instance, if the third party is unaware of the agent's position within the organization.

The reasonableness of a third party's belief in an agent's authority is often a matter of degree. That is, the fact that an organization has authorized an agent to do specific acts does not always make it reasonable for a third party to believe that the agent is authorized to do other acts as well. The nature of the agent's act, or the subject matter of a transaction to which the agent purports to commit the organization, is often a dispositive circumstance. The magnitude or scope of the agent's actual authority is also relevant. If the transaction is of a type that typically requires the approval of the organization's governing body, a third party would not be reasonable in believing that the agent has complete authority to bind the organization. For example, the fact that an organization has entrusted an officer with keys to a building does not warrant a third party's belief that the officer has been authorized to sell the building.

Complex organizations such as large corporations and research universities often assign some matters to a formal process of decisionmaking that is subject to detailed written rules. Managerial employees may assure third parties with whom they interact that the rules are a mere formality and that the manager may unilaterally commit the organization. The gravity of the matter from the standpoint of the organization is relevant to whether a third party could reasonably believe that the manager has authority to proceed unilaterally. For example, a dean in a university, attempting to recruit a prospective faculty member, may assure the faculty prospect that compliance with the university's written procedures for granting tenure is but a formality. If the faculty prospect knows that the rules repose discretion in persons other than the dean--in the university's trustees, president, or provost--the prospect's belief in the dean's unilateral authority is not reasonable; conduct by the dean's organizational superiors would be necessary to make reasonable a belief that the university organization has conferred on the dean an unusual degree of unilateral authority over a matter fraught with enduring consequences for the institution.

_d. Branch and functional managers._ A circumstance that lends weight to an appearance of authority is the fact that an organization has placed an agent in charge of a geographically distinct unit or branch or of a specific function or activity. As with questions of apparent authority generally, it is difficult to state categorical generalizations due to the inescapable importance of particular factual circumstances.

Although a few cases find apparent authority to be present simply because an agent is a branch manager, most cases treat that fact as a factual element to be considered along with others. Of initial significance are the nature of the organization and the nature of the transaction. The nature of an organization's business or activity is relevant to whether a third party could reasonably believe that a branch manager is authorized to commit the organization to a particular transaction. The reasonableness of the third party's belief is enhanced if managers who are in charge of an office engaged in a type of business or activity customarily have the authority that the third party believes the particular manager possesses. In contrast, if a branch manager purports to commit the organization to a contract that is unusual or extraordinary, the third party's belief that the branch manager as such has authority ordinarily is not reasonable. Additionally, it is significant how the organization has structured its interactions with third parties; a third party who observes that the branch manager makes decisions and directs activity without checking elsewhere in the organization may reasonably believe that the branch manager has authority to commit the organization in a comparable transaction with the third party. It is also significant whether the organization itself gives the third party any reason (beyond placing the branch manager in his or her position) to believe that the branch manager has or lacks unilateral authority as to the transaction. For example, if the organization gives the branch manager a set of contract forms that do not indicate that some other officer within the organization must approve a type of transaction, the third party may reasonably believe that the branch manager is authorized to bind the organization using the contract form.

As in other situations governed by apparent authority, the reasonableness of a third party's belief is often a matter of degree, such that a third party may reasonably believe a manager to be authorized to do some things but not others. So situated, a branch manager's apparent authority to communicate on behalf of the principal may exist although the manager may lack authority to commit the principal to a transaction. Even if a third party knows that a branch manager lacks final authority to commit an organization to a transaction, factual circumstances may make it reasonable to believe that the branch manager has authority to communicate a decision made by central authority. It is relevant to the reasonableness of the third party's belief whether the organization has structured itself to designate the branch manager as its exclusive channel for communications with its customers.

**Illustration:**

7\. P Corporation, in the business of making loans, is organized into regional offices. P Corporation directs applicants for loans less than \$ 500,000 to the branch manager of each office. The branch manager interviews all such applicants, telling them that the decision whether to extend a loan is made in P Corporation's central office but that the results of the decision will be communicated by the branch manager. Any loan applicant who communicates directly with P Corporation's central office for a decision on an application is directed to the branch manager. A, a branch manager, erroneously tells T, an applicant for a \$ 250,000 loan, that P Corporation's central office has approved T's application. It is reasonable for T to believe that A's statement is accurate and that the loan will be made. P Corporation is subject to liability to T if it does not make the loan.

A functional manager has been placed in charge of a specific category of matters, such as sales, inventory, human resources, or accounts receivable or payable. A functional manager's apparent authority is defined, first, by the area of assigned responsibility, and second, by the ordinary scope of responsibility of comparable functional managers. See also Comment _e(4),_ discussing the apparent authority of functionally designated vice presidents.

_e. Organizational executives and corporate officers_

_(1). Organizational executives--corporations._ The corporate form of organization makes it necessary that a corporation act through agents as well as undertaking commitments that are expressly authorized by resolutions adopted by its board of directors. Corporate legislation contemplates that a corporation's directors and in some instances its shareholders have power to and will appoint officers as agents of the corporation. Some contemporary statutes are flexible and do not prescribe titles for holders of offices. Officer positions are often specified in bylaws but may be designated by directors acting in accord with the bylaws. The term "officer" usually means an executive has been appointed directly by the board to the position held. Persons hired by officers are typically employees of the corporation, not subagents of the officers themselves. See § 1.04(8), which defines subagent. In a corporation of any size, many managerial agents will not hold statutorily defined positions as officers. It is not always clear whether a person is an officer, and the answer to the question may vary with the context in which it is asked. For purposes of the federal securities laws, "executive officer" is a defined term encompassing officers who perform policy-making functions.

Questions of apparent authority turn on the reasonableness of a third party's belief in an executive's authority and whether these beliefs are traceable to conduct of the corporation, in particular, conduct by its board of directors. In the absence of a link to conduct of the corporation, a third party's belief in an executive's authority is not encompassed by apparent authority if it is based solely on the executive's own representations of authority.

When present, apparent authority expands an executive's effective ability to bind the corporation, typically because the third party is unaware when the executive's actual authority is limited in a manner not customary for an executive in a comparable position. Less typically, apparent authority expands an executive's authority when the corporation's directors acted in a manner that led a third party reasonably to believe that the directors acquiesced in conduct by the executive that had not been authorized by the board.

It is not reasonable for a third party to believe a corporate executive to be authorized simply on the basis of the physical grandeur of the executive's work space or other atmospherics. In contrast, apparent authority may be based on the third party's observations of how the executive customarily functions and the scope of the executive's evident responsibility in dealing with third parties and directing corporate business. It may not be reasonable to believe that an executive has authority to speak as to a matter that is within the province of others' functionally defined responsibilities.

If an executive proposes a transaction that will generate proceeds inuring solely to the executive's personal benefit, the fact that the third party is shown what purports to be a resolution from the corporation's board authorizing such a transaction does not in itself establish the reasonableness of the third party's belief in the executive's authority. It is a question of fact whether, under all relevant circumstances, a reasonable third party would inquire further into the propriety of the transaction, the genuineness of the resolution, or the confirmation of authority.

It is, of course, possible for the same individual to serve as an agent of more than one corporation or, for example, to be both an officer of a corporation and a partner in a partnership. The authority that such an agent may have in connection with one such association does not in itself create apparent authority as to the other association.

A corporation may create an appearance of authority by acquiescing in the continuing exercise of a corporate officer's functions by an individual who has never been duly appointed or elected to office. Such an individual is often characterized as a "de facto" corporate officer to protect third parties who deal with the corporation through the purported officer, reasonably believing the appearance that the individual has the status of an officer. For these purposes, a shareholder is not a "third party" but is a member of the corporate principal itself. The question is whether the third party had a reasonable basis to believe that the officer with whom the interaction occurred had authority to bind the firm, a basis that might be present if the other shareholders led the third party to believe the officer had such authority.

_(2). Organizational executives--partnerships and other unincorporated associations._ As an organizational structure, a general partnership is flatter than a structure defined by the corporate form. A general partnership results when two or more persons associate for the purpose of carrying on as co-owners a business for profit, unless they structure their association pursuant to some other organizational form. General partnership is thus the legal structure applicable to an ongoing business that extends beyond a single project. An association limited to a single project is a joint venture. A joint venture is treated as a form of partnership, in which duties and authority are limited by the scope of the venture. Partnership legislation characterizes all general partners as agents of the partnership "for the purpose of its business . . . ." Partnership thus automatically links ownership with agency, which the corporate form does not. Under the Uniform Partnership Act § 9(1), the partnership is bound by "the act of every partner . . . for apparently carrying on in the usual way the business of the partnership" unless the partner lacks authority so to act and the person with whom the partner deals "has knowledge of the fact that \[the partner\] has no such authority." Under the Revised Uniform Partnership Act § 301, a partner's act binds the partnership "for apparently carrying on in the ordinary course the partnership business or business of the kind carried on by the partnership" unless the partner lacks authority so to act and the person with whom the partner deals "knew or had received a notification that the partner lacked authority." Under both formulations, a partner's apparent authority is linked, not to a specific manifestation by the partnership about the particular partner, but to the scope or nature of the partnership's business and whether the partner's particular act carries it on in "the usual way" or "in the ordinary course." What is determinative is the connection between the act and the business in which the partnership is engaged, as well as the connection between the act and the type of activities typical or characteristic of the business.

The consequences of defined position within a hierarchical organizational structure do not affect appearances of authority in a general partnership to the degree they shape such appearances in a corporate context. Apparent authority in the partnership context is a question of the reasonableness of the third party's belief about authority as defined by the partnership's business and by known limits on a partner's actual authority. A third party may reasonably believe that the partnership has acquiesced or consented to a partner's expansion of the nature of "the business of the partnership" if the third party observes dealings or a pattern of conduct between the individual partner and the partnership supportive of that belief.

**Illustrations:**

8\. A, a partner in P, an accounting firm, regularly prepares tax returns for T, who receives bills from P for A's services. At T's request, A agrees to collect accounts receivable and make cash disbursements for businesses owned by T. Accountants do not ordinarily perform such work for clients. T receives bills from P, prepared by P's central billing office, that include a specifically itemized charge for A's collection and disbursement work. A disburses cash to persons to whom T does not owe money. P is subject to liability to T for loss caused T by A. It is reasonable for T to believe that P has authorized A to undertake the work in the course of P's business.

9\. Same facts as Illustration 8, except that A tells T that the fact that A is doing the additional work must remain a private matter between them, and that accordingly A will bill T separately for the work, to be paid to A individually. P is not subject to liability to T for the loss caused by A. It is not reasonable for T to believe that P has authorized A to undertake the work in the course of P's business.

Unlike general-partnership legislation, limited-partnership statutes do not invest all partners with authority to bind the partnership. Limited-partnership statutes invest management functions in the general partner; if limited partners assume to act on behalf of the limited partnership, they risk being recharacterized as general partners. General partners are subject to liability for partnership obligations, while a limited partner's liability is limited to the amount the limited partner has contributed or agreed to contribute to the partnership. The authority of general partners is defined by the partnership agreement, read in a context in which the general partner has a fiduciary duty to use its power as specified by the agreement for the purpose of benefiting the limited partnership. Thus, a broad grant of authority to enter into contracts does not create actual or apparent authority to commit the partnership to transactions that do not promise it any chance to benefit, such as guarantees of the debts of other obligors when the limited partnership itself does not appear to benefit through the guarantee. This principle of interpretation may be altered, for example, by a specific grant of authority in the partnership agreement.

Apparent-authority doctrine as applied in this context also reflects the principle of interpreting the partnership agreement as a whole. Acts by a general partner that exceed authority as specified when the agreement is read as a whole are unlikely to be consistent with authority that a third party would reasonably believe the general partner possesses. A third party who is aware of restrictions imposed on the general partner's authority by the partnership agreement would not act reasonably in relying on broad language elsewhere in the agreement.

Legislation permitting the formation of limited-liability companies (LLCs) contemplates that the operating agreement for the LLC will usually reflect a choice between management by members and management by a manager. Hybrid management structures are also possible. When an LLC functions without an operating agreement, the statute controls on the question of managerial authority. In a manager-managed LLC, members ordinarily lack apparent authority to bind the LLC. In a member-managed LLC, in contrast, authority is a consequence of membership, as in a general partnership.

Additionally, partnerships and LLCs may hire employees and, like all principals, may appoint nonemployee agents. The actual and apparent authority of such employees and nonemployee agents are governed by the rules stated in §§ 2.01-2.03 and 3.01-3.02.

_(3). Corporate officers: president or chief executive officer._ The apparent authority of a president or chief executive officer encompasses transactions falling within the ordinary course of the corporation's business.
