When a third party has notice that approval by the corporation's board of directors is requisite to bind the corporation to a transaction of a particular type, it is not reasonable for the third party to believe that the president or chief executive officer (CEO) may bind the corporation through unilateral action. In smaller firms, the president's operational responsibilities are likely to encompass more detail than in larger firms. In smaller firms, it is not unusual for the president to function as the "general manager," a term used in many cases involving smaller businesses and intended to describe a person with authority to bind an organization in all matters within the ordinary scope of its business. The ordinary scope of business does not include transactions for which shareholder approval is requisite under corporation statutes, such as a sale of all or substantially all of the corporation's assets. In most small companies the president is the chief executive officer, while in many larger companies the president is only the chief operating officer, who reports to the CEO. The president's position encompasses apparent authority to hire and fire employees and fix their compensation.

Two types of transactions are usually characterized as "extraordinary." First, a transaction is extraordinary if by its terms the corporation cannot benefit from it. A guarantee or other transaction is less likely to be "extraordinary" when the obligor is an affiliated corporation, especially when the enterprises are run as interdependent or interrelated businesses. Second, an agreement to sell real estate owned by the corporation when its business purpose is other than buying and selling real estate, may be extraordinary when the sale, like a sale of operating assets, calls into question the corporation's ability to continue operations after the sale. In both instances, the transactions are extraordinary because their effect is well beyond the corporation's ordinary operation.

Contemporary authority supports the proposition that the CEO of a corporation has authority by virtue of the position to defend or commence litigation against or on behalf of the corporation. However, many courts limit the president's litigation-related authority when the context is an intra-corporate dispute among groups of investors or individuals in a closely held corporation. It is in this context that most litigated disputes over the president's litigation authority surface. Moreover, regardless of context, even if a CEO has expressly stated authority from the corporation's directors to take such legal action as is appropriate in the CEO's sole discretion, the CEO lacks actual or apparent authority to settle a lawsuit on terms that unconditionally commit the corporation to a transaction requiring shareholder consent.

_(4). Corporate officers: vice president._ The office of vice president by itself does not carry actual or apparent authority to bind the corporation. The designation of a person as a vice president does not have a standardized or customary meaning associating particular functions or authority with the position. Normally, however, designating a person as "Vice President for    ," such as Sales, Manufacturing, Industrial Relations, Public Relations, or Finance, means that the holder of the title is the ranking executive in charge of that particular function, who reports to an Executive Vice President, a Chief Operating Officer, a President, or a Chief Executive Officer. All are executives whose responsibilities are broader than those of the functionally specified Vice President. A person designated "Vice President for    " has a customary level of authority over the specified functional area analogous to that of a branch manager over the affairs of the branch. See Comment _d,_ supra.

Moreover, not all vice presidents are executive officers of the corporation, a category significant under the federal securities laws, because they do not perform policy-making functions. Vice presidents of divisions and branches have titles but do not hold general corporate offices. In some industries, promotion of a sales person to "vice presidential" status is simply a reward for excellent job performance and effects no change in job function. In contrast, some corporations define the vice president's position to carry full authority to act on behalf of the corporation when the president is unavailable. If a corporation permits a vice president to exercise significant transactional functions and to make or appear to be in control of operational decisions, it creates a basis on which actual or apparent authority may arise. If the corporation reasonably appears to have validly entered into a contract that specifies acts to be performed by a vice president, it is reasonable for a third party to believe that the vice president has authority to do the specified acts.

_(5). Corporate officers: secretary, treasurer, and secretary-treasurer._ In itself, the office of secretary is ministerial and does not support apparent authority to transact business on behalf of a corporation. However, a corporation could create transactional or discretionary authority in a secretary, either individually or in conjunction with other officers. The customary authority of a secretary is limited to certifying that a resolution has been duly adopted by the corporation's directors or by its shareholders, and to certifying the signatures of the other corporate officers. The secretary's certification provides the linkage between any corporate filing with the state of incorporation that lists the officers' names and titles and the signatures on a particular document. The secretary's customary authority also includes affixing the corporate seal to evidence due execution of a resolution. Once a resolution is duly certified, a third party has a right to rely on it unless the third party knows facts suggesting that the resolution was spurious or was never adopted.

Likewise, the office of treasurer does not in itself entail actual or apparent authority in the office holder to bind the corporation on all matters within the ordinary scope of its business. A treasurer's customary role is to receive and disburse money belonging to the corporation, consistent with instructions received from directors or from officers with general managerial authority. In many large corporations, the treasurer's functions are executed by an officer designated the "Chief Financial Officer." It is not unusual for a corporate bylaw to specify the scope of the treasurer's authority. Internal bylaws, like corporation statutes in some states, often specify that the secretary and the treasurer are officers who have authority to countersign written instruments signed by another officer, such as the president, board chairman, or a vice president. Like the secretary's authority, that of the treasurer may be enlarged by the corporation.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ The black letter of this section corresponds in substance to that of Restatement Second, Agency § 27. Restatement Second, Agency, does not deal specifically with organizational principals, discussed in Comments c, _d,_ and _e._ Restatement Second, Agency § 73 addresses authority to manage a business in the context of an agent given charge of an entire business unit.

_b. Manifestation an essential requirement._ For examples of the basic point that apparent authority requires some sort of holding out by the principal that is salient in light of the agent's act, see [_Secura Ins. Co. v. Saunders, 227 F.3d 1077, 1080-1081 (8th Cir.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:417V-CCB0-0038-X1C0-00000-00&context=1530671) (fact that insurer permitted applicant's agent to issue temporary protection in form of binder, and furnished agent with blank application forms, did not constitute appearance of authority to issue insurance); [_Auvil v. Grafton Homes, Inc., 92 F.3d 226, 230 (4th Cir.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-1F60-006F-M142-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying West Virginia law) (no manifestation or holding out by principal on basis of which third party or its attorneys could reasonably believe attorney for principal had authority to agree to specific settlement of dispute when manifestation indicated at most that lawyer had only authority to negotiate and clients were not present when lawyer announced settlement had been reached and did not thereafter acquiesce in announcement); _Steele v. Ellis, 961 F.Supp. 1458, 1464-1465 (D.Kan.1997)_ (mechanic from whom plaintiff purchased car never represented to plaintiff that he acted on behalf of automobile dealership that allowed mechanic to obtain new title to car in name of dealership; by allowing retitling in its name, dealership did not hold out mechanic as having authority to act on its behalf); [_Landis & Staefa (UK) Ltd. v. Flair Int'l Corp., 60 F.Supp.2d 14, 21 (E.D.N.Y.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3X6S-1YY0-0038-Y164-00000-00&context=1530671) ("confirmatory conduct" on part of principal is essential to apparent authority; no evidence that principal in any manner indicated agent had authority to bind it to major transaction). See also [_Insurance Co. of N. Am. v. Morris, 981 S.W.2d 667, 673 (Tex.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3T5G-DWF0-0039-454T-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurance company permitted its name to be used in private-placement memoranda for program of surety-bond financed partnership-investment product and permitted agents to take applications and collect premiums for bonds at same time investors were presented with partnership units, which did not create apparent authority for agents' statements regarding quality of investment program).

For the proposition that performing normal clearing functions does not constitute a manifestation by the clearing firm that the originating or "introducing" firm acts with its authority, see [_Riggs v. Schappell, 939 F.Supp. 321, 328-330 (D.N.J.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-D8V0-006F-P4MK-00000-00&context=1530671). See also John M. Bellwoar, Bar _Baron_ at the Gate: An Argument for Expanding the Liability of Securities Clearing Brokers for the Fraud of Introducing Brokers, [_74 N.Y.U. L. Rev. 1026-1028, n.92 (1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3XKD-FNB0-00CW-70DV-00000-00&context=1530671) (arguing that clearing brokers' actions could create apparent authority if acts in addition to normal clearing functions can be shown; noting that to reduce risk that an unscrupulous introducing broker will use its name to reassure customers, some well-known clearing brokers use a name for their clearing subsidiary that is dissimilar to well-known name firm uses for its own brokerage business).

An example of an indirect route of communication between a principal and a third party is for the principal to provide the agent with authentic-looking powers of attorney executed by the principal, which the agent in turn affixes to instruments purportedly executed on the principal's behalf and then sends to the third party. See [_Herbert Constr. Co. v. Continental Ins. Co., 931 F.2d 989, 997-998 (2d Cir.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-DN40-008H-V14S-00000-00&context=1530671) (affirming denial of insurer's motion for summary judgment, court observes that agent's practice of affixing power-of-attorney forms to performance-bond documents "may provide a sufficient communication by the principal to all parties to a transaction, whether present at the signing of the documents or not, that the holder of the power has authority to bind the principal").

For an illustration of an agent's apparent authority based on the principal's permission to the agent to make statements to third parties, see [_Ditty v. CheckRite, Ltd., 973 F.Supp. 1320, 1334-1335 (D.Utah 1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S0D-X850-00B1-F313-00000-00&context=1530671) (collection agency allowed law firm to state in letter that it represented collection agency and had authority to settle dispute regarding dishonored checks in exchange for a covenant not to sue). An illustration of an observed prior pattern of interaction is [_Hawaiian Paradise Park Corp. v. Friendly Broad. Co., 414 F.2d 750, 756 n.4 (9th Cir.1969)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-T1G0-0039-Y3B3-00000-00&context=1530671), in which the principal and the agent communicated primarily by telephone. The agent received telephone instructions from the principal and sought confirmation by telephone as necessary. Having observed this pattern, the third party had, according to the district court's finding, a right to believe that the agent had authority to sign an agreement revising the terms of a prior agreement for the sale of a television station. The Ninth Circuit noted that "\[p\]erhaps another trial judge could have found, on the basis of this evidence, that \[the agent\] did not have apparent authority" but concluded that "a reasonable person in the same circumstances could" reach the same conclusion as did the third party. [_Id. at 756_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-T1G0-0039-Y3B3-00000-00&context=1530671).

For illustrations of apparent authority based on the principal's acquiescence in prior similar transactions between the agent and the third party, see [_Utley Lumber Co. v. Bank of the Bootheel, 810 S.W.2d 610, 613-614 (Mo.App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-86Y0-003F-C4C4-00000-00&context=1530671) (officers of corporation knew bookkeeper drew checks on corporate account, drawn to order of bank, to buy money orders); [_Haywood, Jordan, McCowan of Dallas, Inc. v. Bank of Houston, 835 S.W.2d 738, 742 (Tex.App.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-1N30-003C-24W7-00000-00&context=1530671) (firm and officers knew that president endorsed checks payable to firm). On the principal's acquiescence combined with an agent's prior acts, see [_Lynch v. Helm Plumbing & Elec. Contractors, Inc., 108 S.W.3d 657, 659-661 (Mo.App. 2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:46WW-J1X0-0039-43NV-00000-00&context=1530671) (motel's groundskeeper had apparent authority to direct plumbing company's placement of pipeline on motel property; groundskeeper spoke on owner's behalf on matters related to motel property in past and accompanied owner to meeting with plumbing company, where groundskeeper acted "as if he possessed no less authority" than owner to make decision on placement).

Apparent authority based on the situation in which the agent has been placed by the principal is illustrated by [_Chilsan Merch. Marine Co. v. M/V K Fortune, 110 F.Supp.2d 492, 498 (E.D.La.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:413C-VG00-0038-Y2JV-00000-00&context=1530671) (principal created apparent authority to settle claim by authorizing employee to make inquiry into how to resolve arrest of vessel); [_Nogales Serv. Ctr. v. Atlantic Richfield Co., 613 P.2d 293 (Ariz.1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-YN70-003F-T0HY-00000-00&context=1530671) (truck-stop operator could reasonably believe manager of truck-stop marketing for gasoline company had authority to grant across-the-board discount on gasoline purchases); [_Menard & Co. Masonry Bldg. Contractors v. Marshall Bldg. Sys., Inc., 539 A.2d 523 (R.I.1988)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-Y1W0-003D-F2WP-00000-00&context=1530671) (second-tier masonry subcontractor reasonably believed that first-tier subcontractor's project manager had authority to agree to payment of overtime rates despite provision in subcontract requiring changes to be in writing; significant that project manager was individual with whom second-tier subcontractor entered into contract).

On agents with general authority over a class of transactions, see [_Butler v. Maples, 76 U.S. 766, 775 (1869)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-JSK0-003B-H1WH-00000-00&context=1530671). The agent's error in executing its authority did not defeat the third party's claim in [_Pacific Tall Ships Co. v. Kuehne & Nagel Inc., 76 F.Supp.2d 886, 891 (N.D.Ill.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3Y2M-P4G0-0038-Y514-00000-00&context=1530671) (shipper alleged cause of action against carrier for damage to cargo caused by advice given by carrier's shipping agent; agent had obligation to inform shipper of customs and regulations, which agent erroneously stated required fumigation, leading to damage to cargo). _Pacific Tall Ships_ characterizes the agent's advice as "within the scope of its actual authority," perhaps because the written agency agreement between the carrier and the agent required the agent to furnish the principal with information concerning regulations, which the agent then provided to the shipper on the carrier's behalf. So characterizing the agent's error is inconsistent with the definition of actual authority in § 2.01 because nothing suggests the agent reasonably believed that the carrier wished it to give erroneous advice. Moreover, if the agent acted with actual authority, the carrier would have no claim against it for the loss its erroneous advice caused the carrier.

On an agent's communications of decisions made by the principal, see [_Diversified Dev. & Inv., Inc. v. Heil, 889 P.2d 1212, 1221 (N.M.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W1F0-003D-D18X-00000-00&context=1530671).

On apparent authority as a basis for holding a client to a settlement agreement to which counsel consented, compare [_Farris v. JC Penney Co., 176 F.3d 706, 712 (3d Cir.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WH5-FV30-0038-X26M-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Pennsylvania law) (apparent authority applicable only when facts show clients as principals communicated directly with opposing counsel, "making representations that would lead \[opposing\] counsel to believe . . . attorney had authority to settle the case"; district court based finding of apparent authority on facts that clients were seen conferring with lawyer during settlement negotiations and clients were silent when court announced settlement and dismissal of case) with [_Carr v. Runyan, 89 F.3d 327, 332 (7th Cir.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RK4-CH20-006F-M2BY-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Indiana law) (apparent authority present when lawyer sent by client to mediation under order requiring attendance by party and representatives with "full settlement authority"). But cf. [_Columbus-America Discovery Group v. Atlantic Mut. Ins. Co., 203 F.3d 291, 298 (4th Cir.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YHN-S7K0-0038-X0FM-00000-00&context=1530671) (stating "general rule" that "counsel of record have the apparent authority to settle litigation on behalf of their client"; stating also that record clearly shows lawyer had actual express authority to execute settlement agreement). The authority of _Carr_ as an interpretation of Indiana agency doctrine is open to question. In [_Koval v. Simon Telelect, Inc., 693 N.E.2d 1299, 1304-1305 (Ind.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SCP-KH40-0039-421G-00000-00&context=1530671), the court analyzed an attorney's ability to bind a client to the consequences of the attorney's actions in court as an instance of inherent agency power, extending the analysis to encompass settlement procedures governed by ADR rules. The _Koval_ court rejects apparent authority as a basis, stating that retaining a lawyer in itself tells third parties little. [_Id. at 1304_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SCP-KH40-0039-421G-00000-00&context=1530671). In the court setting, a lawyer has a "unique role . . . \[a\] role that did not enable an attorney to bind a client to in court action would impede the efficiency and finality of courtroom proceedings. . . ." But when an attorney represents a client out of court, "custom does not create an expectation of settlement or compromise without the client's signing off." [_Id. at 1306_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SCP-KH40-0039-421G-00000-00&context=1530671). A client's conferral of actual authority may be proven through the client's acquiescence, such as by permitting the lawyer to believe that the client has authorized the lawyer to settle. See [_Federal Land Bank v. Sullivan, 430 N.W.2d 700, 702 (S.D.1988)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S71-2HX0-003G-D1D4-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Malave v. Carney Hosp., 170 F.3d 217, 221 (1st Cir.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3W0K-0X00-0038-X498-00000-00&context=1530671) (stating "salutary proposition" that "the decision to settle litigation belongs to the client, not the lawyer . . . a settlement agreement entered into by an attorney is ineffective if the attorney did not possess actual authority to bind the client, and the cases so hold."). _Malave_ appears to overstate the current state of the law. For an analysis and critique of the varied approaches, see Grace M. Giesel, Enforcement of Settlement Contracts: The Problem of the Attorney Agent, [_12 Geo. J. Legal Ethics 543 (1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3XY7-XGJ0-00CV-90DP-00000-00&context=1530671).

_c. Organizational principals._ A rich literature explores issues raised by organizations in general. Kenneth J. Arrow identifies differential information among agents as a "prerequisite for the existence of nontrivial organizations in a rational world." Kenneth J. Arrow, Scale Returns in Communication and Elite Control of Organizations, 7 J. L. Econ. & Orgs. 1, 3 (1991) (Special Issue). Other prerequisites for the creation and survival of formal organizations are the object of rigorous analyses in James S. Coleman, Foundations of Social Theory (1991).

The interplay between customary or prior authority, a separate directive restricting authority, and observed practice within an organization underlies Illustrations 4-6 and is based on the analysis in [_Linkage Corp. v. Trustees of Boston Univ., 679 N.E.2d 191 (Mass.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RHC-FC90-003C-V00W-00000-00&context=1530671) (apparent authority of university's vice president for external programs to renew contract not countermanded by directive requiring review by specified senior officers when third party observed university make payments in disregard of policy). See also [_Earl v. St. Louis Univ., 875 S.W.2d 234, 239 (Mo.App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-5HJ0-003F-C20B-00000-00&context=1530671) (employee could reasonably believe that medical center's vice president had authority to negotiate and execute a severance agreement with employee on terms comparable to those negotiated by other employees with their supervisors; although university's president testified he sent letter to vice president instructing him that severance agreements required president's approval, vice president testified he never received letter and nothing indicated employee knew or should have known of president's directive).

Situations in which the third party was unaware of a known agent's position within an organization include [_Whitten v. Harold Austin Constr., Inc., 935 S.W.2d 579 (Ark.App.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RJN-2BH0-0039-42V8-00000-00&context=1530671) (apparent authority not present when third party unaware that person was the president of corporation); cf. _FDIC v. Providence Coll., 115 F.3d 136 (2d Cir.1997)_ (lender unaware of officer's title prior to making initial loan in series of 5; lender's officer would have reviewed entire file prior to authorizing subsequent loans, which would result in seeing letterhead containing title of officer of borrower).

On the principle that apparent authority is a matter of degree, compare [_Yugoslav-American Cultural Ctr., Inc. v. Parkway Bank & Trust Co., 682 N.E.2d 401 (Ill.App.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RJN-39H0-0039-44DB-00000-00&context=1530671) (officers' possession of keys to building and of organization's minute books and list of creditors and members did not constitute holding out by organization that officers were authorized to sell building) with [_Lundberg v. Church Farm, Inc., 502 N.E.2d 806 (Ill.App.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-48P0-0054-H429-00000-00&context=1530671) (by designating manager in public advertisement as person with whom members of public should deal in purchasing syndication shares in racehorse, owner of horse created situation in which buyers might reasonably believe manager had authority to modify terms of sale on preprinted form contract).

A superior agent directs a third party to deal with a subordinate agent in [_Kanavos v. Hancock Bank & Trust Co., 439 N.E.2d 311 (Mass.App. 1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-G120-003C-V456-00000-00&context=1530671), remanded for retrial on other grounds, [_479 N.E.2d 168 (Mass.1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-50F0-003C-V2XS-00000-00&context=1530671) (bank's executive vice president had apparent authority to amend repurchase option in workout deal when bank's president permitted executive vice president to exercise general executive responsibilities and encouraged borrower to deal with executive vice president).

For the significance of well-known institutional practices of the sort embodied in formal rules and practices, see [_Johns Hopkins Univ. v. Ritter, 689 A.2d 91 (Md.App.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-8K40-003G-2473-00000-00&context=1530671); cf. [_Perry v. Sindermann, 408 U.S. 593 (1972)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-D510-003B-S237-00000-00&context=1530671) (recognizing claim of de facto tenure when university lacked formal written tenure policy); [_Soni v. Board of Trustees of Univ. of Tennessee, 513 F.2d 347 (6th Cir.1975)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-42J0-0039-M2T4-00000-00&context=1530671) (recognizing possibility of de facto tenure despite existence of explicit tenure system and formally articulated tenure process). See also [_Anderson v. Int'l Union, United Plant Guard Workers of Am., 150 F.3d 590, 593 (6th Cir.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3T8W-YSX0-0038-X4D8-00000-00&context=1530671) (remanding for determination of whether former officers of union knew or should have known of requirement that full union membership must ratify grant of early retirement benefits agreed to by other union officers).

_d. Branch and functional managers._ The court treated the fact that an agent was a branch manager as sufficient in itself to establish apparent authority to transact ordinary business in [_Shahan-Taylor Co. v. Foremost Dairies, 233 S.W.2d 885 (Tex. Civ.App.1950)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S11-V620-003D-R386-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Holland v. Landsdowne-Moody Co., 269 S.W.2d 478 (Tex.Civ.App.1954)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S11-TTK0-003D-R1K8-00000-00&context=1530671), and [_Lindstrom v. Minnesota Liquid Fertilizer Co., 119 N.W.2d 855 (Minn.1963)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-47H0-003G-V074-00000-00&context=1530671). Other cases treat an agent's position as a branch manager as a factor relevant to a determination of whether apparent authority existed. See [_Lanier v. Alenco, 459 F.2d 689 (5th Cir.1972)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-5N50-0039-X4G0-00000-00&context=1530671) (branch manager had apparent authority to hire sales manager for fixed term; nothing in company policy and procedures manual would have led plaintiff to suspect otherwise of branch manager of plant in which he was to work); [_Fletcher v. Tri-State Mill Supply Co., 609 F.Supp. 150 (M.D.La.1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-FRX0-0039-R3T7-00000-00&context=1530671) (branch manager had apparent authority to enter into employment contract specifying salesman's commission rates; contract form provided by corporation did not indicate that approval of corporation's president was necessary, and branch manager had actual authority to hire and fire employees and set salaries); [_Smith v. Saginaw Sav. & Loan Ass'n., 288 N.W.2d 613 (Mich.App.1979)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-K1X0-003D-64WB-00000-00&context=1530671) (branch manager had apparent authority to bind bank with promise to issue two-party checks; manager was in complete charge of office and was bank's sole representative in dealings with most customers, sat at desk with sign designating him an assistant vice president, customer draws on mortgage funds were directed to him, and manager distributed checks to contractors).

Apparent authority is not likely to be present when a branch manager purports to authorize an unusual or extraordinary transaction or to exercise a power not normally within the scope of actual authority of a person in the branch manager's position. See [_Management P'ship, Inc. v. Crumlin, 423 A.2d 939 (D.C.1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRT-9RD0-003G-10MM-00000-00&context=1530671) (lessee of apartment lacked reasonable basis to believe that apartment building's office manager had authority to give lessee an oral release of obligations remaining under lease; in support of apparent authority, lessee offered only evidence that agent was office manager, who testified she lacked actual authority to modify lease; lessee offered no evidence of usual or normal conduct of agent, prior dealings between lessee and agent, customary practice of other similarly situated agents, or representations allegedly made by agent); [_Hassett v. Swift & Co., 388 N.W.2d 55 (Neb.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-5JJ0-003D-B4R8-00000-00&context=1530671) (regional sales manager did not have apparent authority to waive provisions of company's pension plan for plant manager whom he rehired).

If a branch manager is provided with documentation that discloses no limit on the branch manager's authority, and requires no countersignature by headquarters, a third party may reasonably believe the manager to be authorized. See [_Obras Civiles, S.A. v. ADM Sec., Inc., 32 F.Supp.2d 1018, 1021 (N.D.Ill.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3VRC-KYC0-0038-Y0K9-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 7 involves a branch manager's apparent authority to communicate the results of decisions made elsewhere in an organization. The outcome stated is supported by [_Pipkin v. Thomas & Hill, Inc., 236 S.E.2d 725 (N.C.App.1977)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-1D00-003G-04BY-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd in part and rev'd in part on other grounds, [_258 S.E.2d 778 (N.C.1979)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-Y970-003G-04WD-00000-00&context=1530671) (branch manager of mortgage broker who did not have actual authority to commit institution to permanent financing had apparent authority to represent to plaintiff's interim lender that permanent financing had been arranged; nothing in defendant's loan application indicated it limited its business to loan-brokerage service, letterhead and business cards indicated defendant was in business of making mortgage loans, and branch manager would usually have authority necessary to conduct business of office) and First Energy (UK) Ltd. v. Hungarian Int'l Bank Ltd., \[1993\] 2 Lloyd's Rep. 194 (Ct. App.) (manager of Manchester office had ostensible authority to communicate that bank's head office in London had approved loan facility; bank liable for breach of contract when manager had authority to sign and send letters on behalf of bank and borrower was not informed of any limits on such authority). Other authority finds liability for misrepresentation based on comparable facts. See [_Miller v. Mason-McDuffie Co., 739 P.2d 806 (Ariz.1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-WKJ0-003F-T1R4-00000-00&context=1530671) (branch manager's authority to communicate with loan applicants, solicit loan applications, accept commitment fees, and deliver loan commitments supported finding that branch manager acted with apparent authority in telling applicant that loan had been approved). Cf. [_Diversified Dev. & Inv., Inc. v. Heil, 889 P.2d 1212 (N.M.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W1F0-003D-D18X-00000-00&context=1530671) (lawyer who represented grantor of option had apparent authority to tell grantee that grantor had extended deadline for exercise of option although grantee knew that lawyer lacked authority to grant extension).

_e. Organizational executives and corporate officers_

_(1). Organizational executives--corporations._ On the sensitivity to context of determinations of "officer" status, see Robert Charles Clark, Corporate Law § 3.3 (1986). For the treatment of officers in corporation legislation, see, e.g., Model Bus. Corp. Act § 8.40-.8.44. Although the New York statute appears to contemplate that officers be chosen only by directors, the Delaware statute and the Model Business Corporation Act would permit shareholder election of officers. Compare N.Y. Bus. Corp. Law § 715(a) ("\[t\]he board may elect or appoint a president, one or more vice presidents, a secretary and a treasurer, and such other officers as it may determine, or as may be provided in the by-laws") with Del. Code Ann., tit. 8, § 142(b) ("\[o\]fficers shall be chosen in such manner and shall hold their offices on such terms as are prescribed in the by-laws or determined by the board of directors. . . .") and Model Bus. Corp. Act § 8.40(a) ("\[a\] corporation has the officers described in its bylaws or appointed by the board of directors in accordance with the bylaws"). An amendment to § 8.40(a) substitutes the term "offices" for "officers." See Committee on Corporate Laws, Changes in the Model Business Corporation Act Pertaining to the Standards of Conduct for Officer; Inspection Rights and Notices--Final Adoption, [_54 Bus. Law. 1229, 1247 (1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=legalnews&id=urn:contentItem:3X09-V140-0027-B03X-00000-00&context=1530671). Section 8.40(c) requires that the board assign responsibility to an officer for preparing minutes of directors' and shareholders' meetings and for maintaining and authenticating records of the corporation. Similarly, § 142(a) in the Delaware statute provides that "\[o\]ne of the officers shall have the duty to record the proceedings of the meetings of the stockholders and directors in a book to be kept for that purpose." Under the Model Business Corporation Act, an officer is defined to be an employee of the corporation. See Model Bus. Corp. Act § 1.40(8). Not all states with corporation legislation patterned on the Act adopted this treatment. For an example, see N.C. Gen. Stat., § 1.40, Amended North Carolina Commentary (iv) (deleting Model Act definition of "employee" as "unnecessary and undesirable"). In several states, it is a question of fact whether an officer is also an employee. An officer would, for example, additionally be an employee if the duties and incidents of employment are separate from those of the office. See [_Haft v. Dart Group Corp., 841 F.Supp. 549, 569-572 (D.Del. 1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-8WV0-001T-60DS-00000-00&context=1530671) (predicting that Delaware Supreme Court would hold that, under Delaware law, whether an officer is an employee depends on the incidents of the officer's relationship to the corporation).

For purposes of the federal securities laws, an "executive officer" of a corporation means "its president, any vice president . . . in charge of a principal business unit, division or function (such as sales, administration or finance), any other officer who performs a policy making function or any other person who performs similar policy making functions . . . Executive officers of subsidiaries may be deemed executive officers of the \[parent\] if they perform such policy making functions for the \[parent\]." [_17 C.F.R. § 240.3b-7_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=administrative-codes&id=urn:contentItem:603K-8JY1-DYB7-W3YH-00000-00&context=1530671). Some requirements are specifically applicable to executive officers. See, e.g., Regulation S-K, Item 402(a)(3), [_17 C.F.R. § 229.402(a)(3)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=administrative-codes&id=urn:contentItem:67YH-2KB3-GXF6-82GJ-00000-00&context=1530671) (mandating disclosure in proxy statement of compensation of chief executive officer and 4 most highly compensated executive officers other than CEO).

The possibility of apparent authority based on a corporation's prior apparent acquiescence or consent is recognized in [_Black v. Harrison Home Co., 99 P. 494 (Cal.1909)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-BS60-003D-W2N5-00000-00&context=1530671); _Tuttle v. Junior Bldg. Corp., 46 S.E.2d 313 (N.C.1948)_.

On the apparent authority of dual officers, compare [_Veranda Beach Club Ltd. P'ship v. Western Sur. Co., 936 F.2d 1364, 1378 (1st Cir.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-CD20-008H-V0DC-00000-00&context=1530671) (no apparent authority to bind one corporation when officer made it clear for particular transaction that he was acting on behalf of other corporation) with [_Baxter Porter & Sons Well Servicing Co. v. Venture Oil Corp., 488 So. 2d 793, 795 (Miss.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-6TN0-003G-74X4-00000-00&context=1530671) (third party could reasonably believe dual vice president authorized to bind defendant corporation when both corporations shared office suite, had same president and vice president, and vice president told third party work was being done for defendant corporation and invoices should be addressed to defendant corporation).

On the insignificance of title and trappings by themselves, see [_Kanavos v. Hancock Bank & Trust Co., 439 N.E.2d 311, 315 (Mass.App.1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-G120-003C-V456-00000-00&context=1530671), remanded for trial on other grounds, [_479 N.E.2d 168 (Mass.1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-50F0-003C-V2XS-00000-00&context=1530671). On the existence of a duty to inquire further, see [_Schmidt v. Farm Credit Servs., 977 F.2d 511 (10th Cir.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-0N60-008H-V54H-00000-00&context=1530671); [_In re Branding Iron Motel, 798 F.2d 396 (10th Cir.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-2PX0-0039-P3GB-00000-00&context=1530671).

For the proposition that executives may not have apparent authority as to matters falling within others' functionally defined responsibilities, see [_Rodowicz v. Massachusetts Mut. Life Ins. Co., 192 F.3d 162, 177 (1st Cir. 1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XFK-VWM0-0038-X006-00000-00&context=1530671) (employees could not reasonably believe executives had authority to speak on behalf of corporation with regard to retirement benefits when employees were aware that corporation's designated retirement counselors were representatives with authority to provide information and guidance on retirement benefits).

For discussion of the "de facto" officer phenomenon, see [_Community Collaborative of Bridgeport, Inc. v. Ganim, 698 A.2d 245, 252 n.6 (Conn. 1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RR5-G440-003D-8044-00000-00&context=1530671) (noting that doctrine in Connecticut had previously been applied in the context of public officers, not private corporations); [_H & H Press, Inc. v. Axelrod, 638 N.E.2d 333, 339 (Ill.App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2J20-003D-H1H2-00000-00&context=1530671) (persons exercising corporate office "under color and claim of authority" are de facto officers; an officer's authority may be proved by prior exercise of power as agent or officer with corporation's approval or recognition); [_Baggett v. Cyclopss Med. Sys., Inc., 935 P.2d 1265, 1269-1270 (Utah App.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-6S70-003G-F317-00000-00&context=1530671) (doctrine validates acts of officers who do not have actual authority as officers de jure; doctrine inapplicable to internal disputes involving shareholders, including challenges to purported officer's transactions with shareholders). On the apparent authority of officers in informally run small corporations, see [_Fentress v. Triple Mining, Inc., 635 N.E.2d 102 (Ill.App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2M00-003D-H1VP-00000-00&context=1530671) (treating statements of corporation's officer-shareholders to third party as sufficient to create apparent authority on part of secretary/treasurer to bind corporation by executing promissory note).

For broad-ranging discussion of these issues based on Australian and English statutes and cases, see Larelle Chapple & Phillip Lipton, Corporate Authority and Dealings with Officers and Agents (2002).

_(2). Organizational executives--partnerships and other unincorporated associations._ The Uniform Partnership Act (UPA) (1914) defines partnership in § 6. The Revised Uniform Partnership Act (RUPA) defines partnership in similar terms in § 301(1). RUPA § 303 permits a partnership to file a statement of partners' authority with the state. The statement may designate partners' authority and any limits on it. Section 303 creates conclusive rights to rely on such statements in specified instances. It deems a third party to have knowledge of a limitation on a partner's authority only as the limits apply to authority to transfer real estate and only when the limitation is filed with real-property records. For a concise critique of RUPA's provisions, see J. Dennis Hynes, Notice and Notification Under the Revised Uniform Partnership Act: Some Suggested Changes, [_2 J. Small & Emerging Bus. L. 299 (1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3VX9-NTX0-00CW-5021-00000-00&context=1530671).

Whether a transaction is within the scope of partnership business is a question of fact. See [_First W. Bank v. Livestock Yards Co., 466 N.W.2d 853, 857 (S.D.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S71-2FX0-003G-D0W3-00000-00&context=1530671). For examples of activity unrelated to partnership business, see [_Sheinkopf v. Stone, 927 F.2d 1259, 1267 (1st Cir.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-FP90-008H-V0KJ-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Massachusetts law) (law firm not liable for partner's acts when partner used law-firm office, secretary, telephone, and stationery in transaction for real-estate-development business; investor not a client of law firm, firm not in business of soliciting investments, and fellow partners unaware of activity); [_United States v. Acorn Tech. Fund, L.P., 295 F.Supp.2d 494, 508 (E.D.Pa.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:49VF-58V0-0038-Y45J-00000-00&context=1530671) (president of general partner of limited partnership had neither actual nor apparent authority to execute guaranty of debt incurred by corporation controlled by president; partner who executes a guaranty in partnership name "is engaging in a transaction that is uncommon to the business of the partnership and will be binding on the partnership only if that partner has the express authority of the entire partnership"); [_Risk v. Schilling, 569 N.E.2d 646 (Ind.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-21D0-003F-X1S3-00000-00&context=1530671) (partnership not liable for acts of partner in connection with hobby of tractor restoration pursued on farm partnership's property).

Evidence of course of dealing between the parties defeats a limitation on a partner's authority in a written partnership agreement in [_Harper v. Delaware Valley Broadcasters, Inc., 743 F.Supp. 1076, 1090 (D.Del.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-8630-0054-426M-00000-00&context=1530671). In the absence of such evidence, or other evidence of consent to the transaction from fellow partners, a written limitation on authority in a partnership agreement in the possession of a third party defeats a third party's attempt to invoke apparent authority for a partner's act in contravention of the limit. See [_Schrammeck v. FDIC, 853 P.2d 702 (Mont. 1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-3S90-003G-81N7-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Evans v. Pioneer Bank of Evanston, 809 P.2d 251 (Wyo.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RVJ-BJP0-003G-J1H0-00000-00&context=1530671).

A partner's ability to expand the scope of partnership business turns on whether the partner had actual authority to expand or whether a third party would reasonably believe that the partnership itself has authorized the expansion. See [_Heine v. Colton, Hartnick, Yamin & Sheresky, 786 F.Supp. 360, 368 (S.D.N.Y.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-N730-008H-F21W-00000-00&context=1530671). A third party may reasonably believe the expansion in scope to be authorized although participants in the partnership's original business would view the expansion as atypical because the third party's belief is based on the observed interactions of the partner with the partnership. Moreover, a reasonable third party may well have a perspective on what activities are typical or atypical that differs from that of participants in the partnership's original business. See [_Croisant v. Watrud, 432 P.2d 799 (Or. 1967)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-XKB0-003F-Y2T8-00000-00&context=1530671) (client of accounting firm's branch manager could reasonably believe authorized services encompassed collecting and disbursing funds). Illustrations 8 and 9 are variants on the facts of _Croisant._ The analysis in this section treats the situation as governed by apparent authority and partnership law. The _Croisant_ court applied the doctrine of inherent agency power articulated in Restatement Second, Agency § 8 A (power derived "solely from the agency relation and exists solely for the protection of persons harmed by or dealing with a servant or other agent.").

Limited-partnership legislation dating from 1916 originally provided that a limited partner would become liable as a general partner if the limited partner "takes part in the conduct of the business" in addition to exercising a limited partner's rights and powers. Uniform Limited Partnership Act § 7 (ULPA). ULPA § 10 specified limited partners' rights narrowly and exclusively, while the concept of "control" was itself undefined. The Revised Uniform Limited Partnership Act (RULPA) in its 1976 version specified a "safe harbor" of acts that by themselves would not constitute participation in control. RULPA § 303(b). RULPA § 303(a), additionally, limits third-party claims against limited partners based on the appearance of management authority by providing that "if the limited partner's participation in the control of the business is not substantially the same as the exercise of the power of a general partner," the limited partner has personal liability only to persons who transact business having "actual knowledge of his participation in control." RULPA § 303(a) was amended in 1985 to drop the "substantially the same" language and shift from a test of third parties' knowledge to a test of reasonable belief; a limited partner who participates in control of the business is liable as a general partner only to third parties who transact business "reasonably believing, based on the partner's conduct, that the limited partner is a general partner."

For principles of interpretation applicable to limited-partnership agreements, see [_Tsakos Shipping & Trading, S.A. v. Juniper Garden Town Homes, Ltd., 15 Cal.Rptr.2d 585, 595 (Cal.App.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-GNC0-003D-J356-00000-00&context=1530671) (general partner lacks authority to commit limited partnership to contract of guarantee despite presence in partnership agreement of broad language confirming authority to enter into contracts); see also [_In re Sec. Group, Inc., 926 F.2d 1051, 1054 (11th Cir.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-FHK0-008H-V00F-00000-00&context=1530671) (partnership agreement may alter anti-guarantee norm, for example by language authorizing general partner to arrange "financing for corporation or other entities"). A third party's right to rely on broad language stating the general partner's authority is undone by terms restricting the breadth of authority in [_In re Fox Hill Office Investors, Ltd., 926 F.2d 752, 754 (8th Cir.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-FYH0-008H-V1YS-00000-00&context=1530671) (partnership agreement provided that persons dealing with limited partnership were "entitled to rely conclusively on the power and authority of the General Partner as set forth in this Agreement \[and\] \[i\]n no event shall any person dealing with the General Partner . . . be obligated to ascertain that the terms of this Agreement have been complied with" and that each instrument executed by General Partner "shall be conclusive evidence in favor of any and every person relying thereon . . ."; agreement elsewhere prohibited recourse loans, prohibited commingling funds, and required each partnership to have separate bank account); see also [_In re West Tech Ltd., 104 B.R. 176, 179 (Bankr. W.D.Mo.1988)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-M170-0039-K3TV-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_882 F.2d 323 (8th Cir.1989)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-9TP0-003B-53FJ-00000-00&context=1530671) (agreement stating conclusive right to rely on instruments executed by general partner also required transactions to be "reasonably related to the achievement of the purposes of the Partnership").

For a comprehensive treatment of limited-liability companies, see Larry E. Ribstein & Robert E. Keatinge, Limited Liability Companies (1996). State legislation varies on some points relevant to questions of authority. The Delaware statute is unusual in holding out the possibility that a restriction on members' authority placed in an LLC agreement would bind a third party who was unaware of the restriction. The effective breadth of such a restriction may be limited by the fact that the statutory provision does not expressly refer to apparent authority. See Del. Code Ann., tit. 6, § 18-402 (2005) ("\[u\]nless otherwise provided in a limited liability company agreement, each member and manager has the authority to bind the limited liability company"). Commentators suggest that in a manager-managed LLC, members retain authority to amend its articles of organization "or take other important actions" without the managers' approval unless the LLC agreement provides otherwise. See Ribstein & Keatinge at 8-35.

_(3). Corporate officers: president or chief executive officer._ For apparent authority as to employment-related commitments, compare [_Lee v. Jenkins Bros., 268 F.2d 357, 365-367 (2d Cir.1959)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-SRR0-003B-024B-00000-00&context=1530671) (within president's authority to hire employee and agree to pay pension "whatever may happen"; in magnitude, pension commitment was not comparable to lifetime contract of employment) with _Sacks v. Helene Curtis Indus., Inc., 91 N.E.2d 127 (Ill.App.1950)_ (contract to pay salesman a percentage of corporation's profits is beyond corporation's usual and ordinary business).

For examples of other transactions falling within the ordinary scope of the corporation's business, see [_Ullman-Briggs, Inc. v. Salton, Inc., 754 F.Supp. 1003 (S.D.N.Y.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-G430-001T-73W6-00000-00&context=1530671) (president's authority to engage in ordinary business includes contracting authority and, in particular, encompasses 2-year written representation contract); [_Buchanan Contracting Co. v. Denson, 80 So. 2d 614 (Ala.1955)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRH-JKM0-0046-83V4-00000-00&context=1530671) (general manager had authority to lend company's vehicle to night watchman); [_Monteleone v. Southern California Vending Corp., 70 Cal.Rptr. 703 (Cal. App.1968)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-9J20-003C-J1XB-00000-00&context=1530671) (general manager had authority to ratify lease); [_J. Perez, S.A. v. Louisiana Rice Growers, Inc., 139 So. 2d 247 (La.App.1962)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-6CN0-0044-C04N-00000-00&context=1530671) (general manager of rice grower, having actual authority to sell rice, had apparent authority to give refund for rice); [_Burnett's Lumber & Supply Co. v. Commercial Credit Corp., 51 So. 2d 54 (Miss.1951)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRR-2JB0-000H-3563-00000-00&context=1530671) (general manager had authority to endorse and assign notes from transaction in which corporation sold vehicles pursuant to conditional sales contract); [_Diederich v. Wisconsin Wood Prods., Inc., 19 N.W.2d 268 (Wis.1945)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRR-2V90-003V-H0G6-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporation's president and general manager was clothed with apparent authority to enter into requirements contract by letter signed by president and sent to supplier by corporation's secretary).

Guarantee transactions characterized as "extraordinary" include [_Johnson v. Witkowski, 573 N.E.2d 513 (Mass.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-BW30-003C-V1D9-00000-00&context=1530671) (president lacks authority to execute unlimited corporate guarantee); [_In re Union City Milk Co., 46 N.W.2d 361 (Mich.1951)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-Y3T0-003D-711V-00000-00&context=1530671) (manager lacked authority to use milk trucks as collateral for loan made to manager's child); [_Molasky Enters. Inc. v. Carps, Inc., 615 S.W.2d 83 (Mo.App.1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-FYH0-003F-C0XW-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporation's president and executive vice-president acted outside scope of corporation's business in signing for corporation as endorser of loan made personally to officers; corporation's commitment was of no benefit to it, despite officers' claim that purpose of loan was to repay debt they owed to corporation so it would not appear on corporation's annual report); [_Burlington Indus., Inc. v. Foil, 202 S.E.2d 591 (N.C.1974)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-YHF0-003G-02YC-00000-00&context=1530671) (president acted outside ordinary course of business of textile company in executing guarantee on behalf of another corporation). Guarantees on behalf of affiliates feature in [_Anderton v. Certainteed Corp., 411 S.E.2d 558 (Ga.App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX8-BWK0-003G-P01G-00000-00&context=1530671) (in absence of president, vice president had at least apparent authority to bind corporation to guarantee of affiliate corporation's debt); [_Bell Atl. Tricon Leasing Corp. v. DRR, Inc., 443 S.E.2d 374 (N.C.App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-03Y0-003G-00S2-00000-00&context=1530671) (president could bind corporation to guarantee lease of affiliate corporation incidental to corporation's normal course of business); [_Foote & Davies, Inc. v. Arnold Craven, Inc., 324 S.E.2d 889 (N.C.App.1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-0V10-003G-042P-00000-00&context=1530671) (guarantee of debt incurred by subsidiary in interest of corporation). An additional question as to guarantee transactions is whether the corporation itself has power to engage in the transaction. See, e.g., Model Bus. Corp. Act § 2.02(7) (unless corporation's articles provide otherwise, corporation has power to make contracts and guarantees).

"Extraordinary" transactions in corporate property include [_Rothberg v. Manhattan Coil Co., 66 S.E.2d 390 (Ga.App.1951)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-7960-003V-C22D-00000-00&context=1530671) (sale of corporation's physical property not in normal course of business; chairman of corporation lacked apparent authority to contract with real-estate broker to locate offeror for property); [_Pee Dee Nursing Home, Inc. v. Florence Gen. Hosp., 419 S.E.2d 834, 837 (S.C.App. 1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RVJ-CJ90-003G-C2FN-00000-00&context=1530671) (hospital administrator and president lacked apparent authority to contract to sell portion of facility to nursing home; relevant that prior negotiations for related transaction with same party included specific authorization by board); [_Mosell Realty Corp. v. Schofield, 33 S.E.2d 774 (Va. 1945)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S12-01V0-003D-00PF-00000-00&context=1530671) (even when 2 or 3 directors knew of and approved sale, president lacked authority to sell without a formal directors' resolution and thus authority to contract with broker). See also [_Black v. Harrison Home Co., 99 P. 494 (Cal.1909)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-BS60-003D-W2N5-00000-00&context=1530671) (authority of president of corporation in business of acquiring and selling land is subject to resolution of board that sales must be jointly done by president and secretary/treasurer). An exceptional case reaching an outcome at odds with the bulk of authority is [_Menard Inc. v. Dage-MTI, 726 N.E.2d 1206 (Ind.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:402S-YK00-0039-40FT-00000-00&context=1530671) (although third party knew that corporation's president had required specific authorization from board of directors for prior comparable transactions in corporation's property, corporation bound by president's agreement to sell property although president lacked specific authorization and third party had no notice of any manifestation by corporation that president's authority had been augmented by board). A majority of the _Menard_ court, although recognizing that the president lacked apparent authority, base the outcome on the president's "inherent agency power" as stated in Restatement Second, Agency § 8 A.

On a president's litigation-related authority, see [_Ono v. Itoyama, 884 F. Supp. 892 (D.N.J.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-MS30-001T-52TV-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _79 F.3d 1138 (3d Cir.1996)_ (applying New York law) (president and 50% shareholder did not have presumptive authority to bring action for corporation against other 50% shareholder); [_Squaw Mountain Cattle Co. v. Bowen, 804 P.2d 1292 (Wyo.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RVJ-BK80-003G-J1JD-00000-00&context=1530671) (despite directors' authorization of president to take such legal action as he deemed appropriate in his sole discretion, president lacked authority to settle; settlement agreement would have involved sale of corporation's assets without shareholder consent, which was required by corporation statute). Professor Goebel's study of earlier cases found support for litigation authority in the president in the face of active opposition from directors and in the absence of a manifestation to third parties from directors that would support apparent authority. See Roger J. Goebel, The Authority of the President Over Corporate Litigation: A Study in Inherent Agency, 37 St. John's L. Rev. 29 (1962). Most cases since 1962 involve either small business corporations or not-for-profits. The president of a corporation has presumptive power to act to protect the corporation's interests in the absence of prior restriction in the corporation's certificate of incorporation or bylaw and in the absence of prohibition by the board. See [_Joseph Polchinski Co. v. Cemetery Floral Co., 433 N.Y.S.2d 825 (App.Div.1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-BP80-003C-F02R-00000-00&context=1530671); see also [_First Cmty. State Bank of Savannah v. Pemberton, 493 S.W.2d 692 (Mo.App.1973)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-J000-003F-C1BY-00000-00&context=1530671) (bank president declared in bylaws to be principal executive officer and to have general control of property and business of corporation had inherent power to institute action against state officer who seized bank). Acting alone, and without separate authorization from the corporation's board of directors, the president may institute suit on behalf of a corporation. [_Gemstar Ltd. v. Ernst & Young, 917 P.2d 222, 230-231 (Ariz.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-W8B0-003F-T015-00000-00&context=1530671); accord, [_American Ctr. for Educ., Inc. v. Cavnar, 145 Cal.Rptr. 736, 750 (Cal.App. 1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S11-TFR0-003C-R2G3-00000-00&context=1530671) (dictum that corporation may sue on authorization of directors or initiative of president or managing officer). The president may retain counsel when the board lacks a sufficient number of disinterested directors to otherwise ratify or deny the retention. See [_Smaha v. Landy, 638 A.2d 392, 399 (Pa.Commw.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-0XV0-003C-S48R-00000-00&context=1530671). The president may not maintain a judicial proceeding that the president has instituted in the corporation's name contrary to subsequent directives from the board. [_TJI Realty, Inc. v. Harris, 672 N.Y.S.2d 386, 388 (App.Div.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SMF-PK90-0039-409M-00000-00&context=1530671). Other officers may also have presumptive authority over litigation. See [_Gemstar, 917 P.2d at 231_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-W8B0-003F-T015-00000-00&context=1530671) (vice president would have authority to institute suit "under certain circumstances," including circumstance that president is not acting); _Happy Banana, Ltd. v. Tishman Constr. Corp., 578 N.Y.S.2d 574, 575 (App.Div.1992)_ (corporation's secretary, alone in conducting its business affairs, had authority to institute action against an outsider). In contrast, cases from Texas require an affirmative grant of authority in the corporation's bylaws or in a resolution adopted by the directors. See [_Kaspar v. Thorne, 755 S.W.2d 151, 154 (Tex. App.1988)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-34D0-003C-20WN-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Valley Int'l Props., Inc. v. Brownsville Sav. & Loan Ass'n, 581 S.W.2d 222, 227 (Tex.Civ.App.1979)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-5BC0-003C-213G-00000-00&context=1530671); but cf. [_Square 67 Dev. Corp. v. Red Oak State Bank, 559 S.W.2d 136, 138 (Tex.Civ.App.1977)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-5JC0-003C-23M8-00000-00&context=1530671) (although president lacks authority to employ attorney to conduct litigation on behalf of corporation in absence of express authority established by bylaw or resolution of directors, president may have "implied authority (such as entrustment with broad powers of management of the corporate business)"). Louisiana authority is scant, but may be consistent with the Texas rule. See [_Eastlake Trading Co. v. Iberia Trading Co., 398 So. 2d 1146 (La.App. 1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRN-0GK0-003G-M3Y0-00000-00&context=1530671) (suit brought by president dismissed when no showing president authorized by charter, bylaw, or resolution adopted by directors; statute authorizing president of foreign or domestic corporation doing business in state to institute suit inapplicable because corporation could not prove it did business within state). The president's inherent power under Florida law appears to trump even an express bylaw requiring board authorization for instituting suit. See [_Conlee Const. Co. v. Cay Const. Co., 221 So. 2d 792 (Fla.Dist.App.1969)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-87W0-003C-W51T-00000-00&context=1530671) (bylaw requiring approval of majority of board to institute suit invalid insofar as it prevented president from instituting suit against defendant director and spouse who controlled onehalf of board and allegedly usurped corporate opportunity).

Recent authority in New York and California disapproves of a shareholder/officer's suit in the corporation's name against the corporation's sole other shareholder, requiring instead the institution of a shareholder's derivative action. See [_Stone v. Frederick, 666 N.Y.S.2d 294, 295 (App.Div. 1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RHR-WDF0-0039-43TS-00000-00&context=1530671) (by permitting 2-shareholder corporation to have 2-director board, legislature has implicitly recognized "'the desire for equal control in some corporations'" and permitting exercise of presidential power to resolve deadlock "'disregards fundamental rules of agency law,'" quoting [_Sterling Indus. v. Ball Bearing Pen Corp., 84 N.E.2d 790, 793 (N.Y.1949)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-XMR0-003C-C30G-00000-00&context=1530671); proper remedy is shareholder derivative action when corporation has 2 50% shareholders); [_Executive Leasing Co. v. Leder, 594 N.Y.S.2d 217, 218-219 (App.Div.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S2R-7K60-003V-B117-00000-00&context=1530671). Accord, [_Anmaco, Inc. v. Bohlken, 16 Cal.Rptr.2d 675 (Cal. App.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-GM20-003D-J2YM-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Glisson Coker, Inc. v. Coker, 581 S.E.2d 303 (Ga.App.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:484K-YX80-0039-40DF-00000-00&context=1530671); _L.W. Kent & Co. v. Wolf, 533 N.Y.S.2d 119, 120 (App.Div.1988)_; cf. [_Innovative Therapy Prods., Inc. v. Roe, 1998 WL 405049, at \*4 (E.D.La. 1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3T7V-9J40-0038-Y2GY-00000-00&context=1530671) (holding de facto president lacks authority to file suit on behalf of corporation against other 50% shareholder; recognizing split in authority).

_(4). Corporate officers: vice president._ For the basic proposition that the office of vice president is not a position with customary power to bind the corporation, see [_Barnes v. Treece, 549 P.2d 1152 (Wash.App. 1976)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-Y2V0-003F-W3WB-00000-00&context=1530671). Disputes over the actual authority or position of a vice president arise with some frequency under Securities Exchange Act § 16(b). The section applies to short-swing trading by defined types of corporate insiders including "officers" of reporting companies. See, e.g., [_Merrill Lynch v. Livingston, 566 F.2d 1119 (9th Cir. 1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-YX50-0039-M41J-00000-00&context=1530671) (broker awarded title of "Vice President" had same duties after award of title as before and lacked access to inside information concerning employer that would make him an insider for purposes of statute).

On the apparent authority of a vice president when the president is unavailable, see [_Anderton v. Certainteed Corp., 411 S.E.2d 558 (Ga.App. 1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX8-BWK0-003G-P01G-00000-00&context=1530671) (court summarily states vice president of family corporation had apparent authority to sign guarantee to benefit affiliated corporation when president was out of town). For an illustration of contract language creating apparent authority, see [_Master Consol. Corp. v. BancOhio Nat'l Bank, 575 N.E.2d 817, 822 (Ohio 1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-KXS0-008T-Y455-00000-00&context=1530671) (vice president had apparent authority to request and direct distribution of bond proceeds when language of bond agreement and communications from corporate secretary supported existence of authority). A vice president assumed general operational responsibility in [_Kanavos v. Hancock Bank & Trust Co., 439 N.E.2d 311 (Mass.App.1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-G120-003C-V456-00000-00&context=1530671).

_(5). Corporate officers: secretary, treasurer, and secretary-treasurer._ On a secretary's lack of managerial, transactional, and discretionary authority, see [_Ideal Foods, Inc. v. Action Leasing Corp., 413 So. 2d 416 (Fla.Dist.App.1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-B7K0-003C-X0WR-00000-00&context=1530671) (secretary-treasurer of corporation lacked actual or apparent authority to bind corporation to a lease); [_Constructors' Ass'n. v. Furman, 87 A.2d 801 (Pa.Super.1952)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRN-1130-003C-M09J-00000-00&context=1530671) (testimony as to declaration of secretary not admissible as admission against interest of corporation due to secretary's lack of substantive authority). Cf. _Burkett v. Finger Lake Dev. Corp., 336 N.E.2d 628 (Ill. App.1975)_ (corporation failed to allege and prove facts to show that secretary lacked authority to execute cognovit notes; such notes are prima facie valid if executed by persons recognized by law as proper persons to do so). Instances in which corporations created transactional authority in a secretary include [_Black v. Harrison Home Co., 99 P. 494 (Cal.1909)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-BS60-003D-W2N5-00000-00&context=1530671) (directors' resolution required that sales or purchases of land be done jointly by president and secretary/treasurer); [_First Nat'l Bank of Clayton v. Frisco Park Realty Co., 510 S.W.2d 59 (Mo.App.1974)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-HR70-003F-C042-00000-00&context=1530671) (resolution authorized secretary to borrow funds). On a secretary's authority to certify, see [_Associates Disc. Corp. v. Tobb Co., 50 Cal.Rptr. 738 (Cal.App. 1966)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-9YB0-003C-J00S-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Condor Corp. v. Cunningham, 162 P.2d 21 (Cal.App.1945)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S1W-KN70-003V-P38C-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Contel Credit Corp. v. Central Chevrolet, Inc., 557 N.E.2d 77 (Mass.App.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-C530-003C-V29M-00000-00&context=1530671) (secretary tricked into certifying general manager's authority, pursuant to corporate resolutions never presented to directors; third party has right to rely on certification, lacking knowledge of manager's dishonesty).

On a treasurer's customary authority, see Robert Charles Clark, Corporate Law § 3.3.1 (1986). A treasurer with broad authority to commit a corporation in financial dealings reasonably related to the corporation's business does not have apparent authority to commit the corporation to an extraordinary transaction. See [_General Overseas Films, Ltd. v. Robin Int'l, Inc., 542 F.Supp. 684, 691 (S.D.N.Y.1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-CRM0-0039-S0TY-00000-00&context=1530671) (treasurer did not have apparent authority to execute guarantee of debt of unrelated corporation). See also [_Varney Bros. Sand & Gravel, Inc. v. Champagne, 703 N.E.2d 721, 725 (Mass.App.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3VD0-WXT0-0039-41Y2-00000-00&context=1530671) (treasurer lacked apparent authority to bind corporation to agreement to permit his nurse to reside rent-free in house owned by corporation, for period of time to be determined in nurse's discretion, with corporation to pay utility bills and real-estate taxes).

A comptroller, like a treasurer, generally does not have authority to contract on a corporation's behalf. See [_Lavoie v. Safecare Health Serv., Inc., 840 P.2d 239, 248 (Wyo.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RVJ-BDJ0-003G-J159-00000-00&context=1530671), citing Harry G. Henn & John R. Alexander, Laws of Corporations 595-599 (3d ed. 1983).

On statutory requirements that an instrument be signed by two corporate officers, see [_Snukal v. Flightways Mfg., Inc., 3 P.3d 286, 305-309 (Cal.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40RT-3H80-0039-42X4-00000-00&context=1530671) (interpreting Cal. Corp. Code § 313; signature of one person suffices to bind corporation when person holds separate corporate offices in categories prescribed by statute, which court characterizes as "operational" and "record-keeping or financial").
