[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 3.04_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-401W-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 3- Creation and Termination of Authority and Agency Relationships_ > _Topic 3- Capacity to Act as Principal or Agent_**

**§ 3.04 Capacity to Act as Principal**

**(1) An individual has capacity to act as principal in a relationship of agency as defined in § 1.01 if, at the time the agent takes action, the individual would have capacity if acting in person.**

**(2) The law applicable to a person that is not an individual governs whether the person has capacity to be a principal in a relationship of agency as defined in § 1.01, as well as the effect of the person's lack or loss of capacity on those who interact with it.**

**(3) If performance of an act is not delegable, its performance by an agent does not constitute performance by the principal.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Cross-references._ Person is defined in § 1.04(5). A person has capacity to act as an agent as stated in § 3.05. Comment _c_ to § 1.03 describes the general legal frameworks applicable to various types of business organizations. A principal's loss of capacity has the consequences stated in § 3.08.

_b. Capacity of individual persons._ The capacity to do a legally consequential act by means of an agent is coextensive with the principal's capacity to do the act in person. The dispositive question is whether, as to an agent's action, the principal's legal rights and obligations would have been affected had the action instead been done by the principal in person. Agency law reflects in this respect a principle of neutrality or transparency because an agent's legal capacity neither augments nor reduces the principal's capacity. The principal's capacity is requisite to a relationship of agency because the agent's actions within the scope of the relationship affect the principal's legal position. A person may have capacity in some respects but not in others. A person who lacks capacity in a particular respect may, upon regaining or acquiring capacity, elect to be bound by transactions made during the period capacity was lacking. In the sense used here, minors lack full capacity, as do those persons who suffer from mental disease or disturbance, or a physical condition that reduces awareness. A person so afflicted may lack capacity prior to any adjudication of legal incompetence or the appointment of a guardian.

**Illustration:**

1\. P is 16 years old. Under the statute applicable to minors in the jurisdiction, P lacks capacity to make a binding contract because P may disaffirm contracts made prior to reaching the age of majority. P asks A, who is 18 years old, to contract on P's behalf to buy a car. P does not have capacity to authorize A to make a contract on P's behalf that will bind P. If A makes such a contract on P's behalf, it is voidable by P as if P made the contract in person.

_c. Delegability._ A person may delegate performance of an act if its legal consequences for that person are the same whether the act is performed personally or by another. If personal performance is required, performance by an agent does not constitute performance by the principal.

**Illustration:**

2\. P, a lawyer, is subject to a regulatory rule in the jurisdiction in which P is admitted to law practice that expressly requires P personally to read a statement of the rules applicable to maintaining client trust accounts. The regulatory rule also requires P personally to sign a certification that P has read the trust-account rules. P does not have capacity to delegate performance to A, an associate in P's law firm. If, at P's direction, A reads the trust-account rules and signs P's name, A's actions do not satisfy the regulatory rule applicable to P.

_d. Capacity of organizations and other persons that are not individuals._ A person that is not an individual, such as a sovereign state or a corporation, cannot act in the physical world except through actions of individual persons. This basic fact distinguishes such persons from individuals as principals. The legal capacity of a person that is not an individual is governed by the legal regime by virtue of which such person exists and functions.

Legal capacity is a function of three distinct but inter-related concepts: existence, power, and disenablement or suspension of power. For example, a corporation comes into existence when its organizers file articles or a certificate of incorporation with the state of incorporation. Dissolution of a corporation does not end its existence until the time specified by statute. Filing a petition under the federal bankruptcy statute does not effect dissolution of a corporation or end its existence although, if the court appoints a bankruptcy trustee, the trustee's powers include operating the corporation's business unless the court orders otherwise. Postdissolution, a corporation's powers are limited to performing acts appropriate to winding-up and liquidating. Predissolution, a corporation's powers may be suspended as a sanction for noncompliance with statutory requirements, such as payment of franchise taxes and filing required reports. Some statutes sanction noncompliance through administrative dissolution, providing as well for reinstatement on stated conditions. Additionally, courts may supplement or qualify the consequences specified by organizational statutes. For example, if the parties deal as if a corporation existed when the legal requisites of incorporation have not been fulfilled, many courts recognize the "de facto" corporation as the functional equivalent of a de jure corporation.

**Illustrations:**

3\. A, representing to T that A is the CEO of P Corporation, enters into a contract on behalf of P Corporation to purchase 5000 customized semiconductors from T. At the time of the contract, P Corporation does not exist because the requisite organizational documents have not been filed. No activities, apart from A's, are carried on purportedly by or on behalf of P Corporation. A is not P Corporation's agent because P Corporation does not exist. If P Corporation thereafter comes into existence, it may adopt the contract with T. See § 4.04, Comment c.

4\. A enters into a contract on behalf of P Corporation. At the time of the contract, P Corporation has been administratively dissolved by the state in which it was incorporated as a consequence of its nonpayment of franchise taxes. The consequences of A's action are governed by the organizational law applicable to P Corporation.

Organizational statutes specify the legally operative powers that an entity created pursuant to the statute shall have. Such statutes often provide that powers granted by the statute are subject to conditions and limitations stated in organizational documents. An entity lacks capacity to authorize an agent to do an act that is not within the entity's powers conferred by statute.

Likewise, the legally operative actions that may be taken by governments and subdivisions of governments are often specified by statute, constitution, or charter. This in turn limits a governmental principal's capacity to authorize agents.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ The rule stated in subsection (1) of this section is consistent with the treatment in Restatement Second, Agency § 20. The material in subsection (2) does not have a counterpart in Restatement Second, Agency, because the treatment in § 20 is confined to individual persons. Restatement Second, Agency, treats delegability, the focus of subsection (3), in §§ 17 and 18.

_b. Capacity of individual persons._ Capacity to do an act through an agent is characterized as "coextensive" with legal capacity to do the act oneself in Francis M.B. Reynolds, Bowstead & Reynolds on Agency 34 (17th ed. 2001). For the proposition that a person's capacity to do an act through an agent is governed by the person's capacity to do the act in person, see [_United States v. Craig, 896 F.Supp. 85, 89 (N.D.N.Y.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-FG10-001T-50PS-00000-00&context=1530671) (defendant, who under state law lost civil right to possess firearms due to criminal conviction, may not direct that firearms subsequently seized in law-enforcement raid be released to spouse because defendant may not delegate to another a right he does not possess himself); Addario v. Sandquist, 1998 WL 161176 (Conn.Super.1998) (minor who acted through adult agent to purchase automobile may avoid contract because use of an agent cannot alter principal's legal capacity); [_Couch v. Couch, 824 S.W.2d 65, 71 (Mo.App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-7RV0-003F-C2V6-00000-00&context=1530671) (person whose mental capacity has declined to point that appointment of a guardian would be warranted may not execute durable power of attorney appointing agent); [_Estate of Peters, 336 N.Y.S.2d 712 (Sur.1972)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-9VG0-003C-D3CJ-00000-00&context=1530671) (minor who lacks capacity to appear in Surrogate's court in person may not appoint attorney-in-fact as agent to appear on her behalf but may appear only through guardian of infant's property or guardian ad litem appointed by court).

On the capacity of minors to contract, see Robert H. Mnookin & D. Kelly Weisberg, Child, Family and State 1081-1085 (4th ed. 2000) (majority rule is that contracts made by minor are voidable, but statutes and cases in some jurisdictions modify rule, for example to require minor to make restitution for benefits received). See also Warren A. Seavey, Agency Since the Restatement, 23 A.B.A. J. 503 (1936), reprinted in Studies in Agency 161, 169 (1949) (noting that "\[a\] person who acts as an agent of an infant takes great risks," and criticizing [_Schroeder v. Hitt, 260 N.Y.S. 2 (App.Div.1932)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRR-23T0-003D-T1GK-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _185 N.E. 786 (N.Y.1933)_, in which stock broker who bought shares for minor, acting on minor's instructions given when market price was high, was ordered to repay purchase amount to minor, who disaffirmed contract once price fell).

_c. Delegability._ On acts that require personal performance on the part of an individual principal, see [_Heine v. Newman, Tannenbaum, Helpern, Syracuse & Hirschtritt, 856 F.Supp. 190, 194 n.4 (S.D.N.Y.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-GKH0-003B-V373-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _50 F.3d 2 (2d Cir.1995)_ ("\[a\]cts which require personal performance are those acts that center around areas of personal concern such as marriage or divorce"; attorney-in-fact appointed by general power of attorney may do all acts on behalf of principal, excepting those requiring personal performance). On matters that an organization's governing board may not delegate to an agent, see, e.g., [_Wells Fargo Bank v. Superior Court, 811 P.2d 1025, 1032-1035 (Cal.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-8930-003D-J2V0-00000-00&context=1530671) (under National Bank Act, [_12 U.S.C. § 24_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SF8-7342-D6RV-H087-00000-00&context=1530671), bank directors may not delegate function of dismissing officers who serve at pleasure of board).

_d. Capacity of organizations and other persons that are not individuals._ Corporation statutes vary on these points in terminology, procedures, and substance. Compare Del. Code Ann., tit. 8, §§ 106 (corporate existence commences upon filing with Secretary of State of certificate of incorporation duly executed and acknowledged, unless a delayed effective date not to exceed 90 days is specified under § 103(d)); 278 (dissolved corporation shall continue to exist for 3 years or such longer time directed by Court of Chancery for purpose of prosecuting and defending suits and concluding business but not for purpose of continuing business) with Model Bus. Corp. Act §§ 2.03(a) (corporate existence begins when articles of incorporation are filed, unless a delayed effective date is specified); 14.05(a) (dissolved corporation continues to exist but may not carry on business except that appropriate to wind up and liquidate its business and affairs). Sections 14.20 to 14.22 govern administrative dissolution as a sanction for noncompliance with the requirements specified in § 14.20. Section 14.21(c) provides that an administratively dissolved corporation continues its existence but may not carry on any business except that requisite to winding up and liquidating. In contrast, the Delaware statute does not use the term "administrative dissolution." Section 284 gives the Court of Chancery authority to revoke a corporation's charter for "abuse, misuse or nonuse of its corporate powers, privileges and franchises." In some states, the statutory sanction for failure to pay franchise taxes is external to the corporation statute itself. See, e.g., Cal. Rev. & Tax. Code § 23301 (corporate powers of domestic corporation may be suspended, and exercise of powers in state by foreign corporation may be forfeited, as a result of nonpayment of any tax, penalty, or interest).

Under the federal bankruptcy code, a bankruptcy trustee has power to operate the debtor's business unless the court orders otherwise. [_11 U.S.C. § 1108_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8S8T-0CK2-8T6X-73PW-00000-00&context=1530671). If no trustee is appointed, the debtor in possession performs this function, subject to conditions imposed by the court. See [_11 U.S.C. § 1107(a)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8S8T-0CK2-8T6X-73PV-00000-00&context=1530671).

For the proposition that existence is a prerequisite to capacity as a principal, see, e.g., [_Jean Claude Boisset Wines U.S.A., Inc. v. Newton, 830 P.2d 1134, 1135 (Colo.App.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2RN0-003D-94T8-00000-00&context=1530671) (where no steps taken to incorporate corporation at time defendant wrote check on purported corporation's account, defendant individually liable for goods purchased; defendant also liable for treble damages on basis of statute applicable to issuance of bad checks).

On the general consequences of dissolution of a corporation, see 3 James D. Cox, Thomas L. Hazen and F. Hodge O'Neal, Corporations § 26.7 (1995). On de facto corporations, see id. § 6.3 (de facto doctrine generally applicable except in direct action by state). On the effect of administrative dissolution and suspension, see, e.g., [_Kirby v. Coastal Sales Ass'n., Inc., 82 F.Supp.2d 193, 196 (S.D.N.Y.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YHV-X0X0-0038-Y44R-00000-00&context=1530671) (under California law, suspended corporation lacks capacity to enter into contracts; contract that president entered into on behalf of suspended corporation is voidable by corporation); [_Equipto Div. Aurora Equip. Co. v. Yarmouth, 950 P.2d 451 (Wash.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S83-X3W0-0039-4148-00000-00&context=1530671) (under Washington statute, corporation continues in existence after administrative dissolution to enable it to wind up its affairs but may not conduct other business; statute imposing liability on persons who assume to act as or on behalf of a corporation requires actual knowledge that no corporation existed). See also [_Penasquitos, Inc. v. Superior Court, 812 P.2d 154 (Cal.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-8810-003D-J2M6-00000-00&context=1530671) (under California corporation statute, post-dissolution claims may be maintained against dissolved corporation for an indefinite period).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 3.05_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-401X-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 3- Creation and Termination of Authority and Agency Relationships_ > _Topic 3- Capacity to Act as Principal or Agent_**

**§ 3.05 Capacity to Act as Agent**

**Any person may ordinarily be empowered to act so as to affect the legal relations of another. The actor's capacity governs the extent to which, by so acting, the actor becomes subject to duties and liabilities to the person whose legal relations are affected or to third parties.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Cross-references._ Person is defined in § 1.04(5). A person has capacity to act as a principal as stated in § 3.04.

_b. Capacity to affect the legal relations of another._ As a general matter, any person able to act may do so with actual authority as defined in § 2.01 or apparent authority as defined in § 2.03. An agent's power to affect the principal's legal relations is usually limited only by the agent's ability to take action. If an agent is an individual, ability to act is a function of physical and mental ability. However, in some contexts the requisites for capacity to act as an agent are more exacting. Representing another person as a lawyer requires that the representative be a lawyer; thus, a defunct corporation may not be "represented" except by a lawyer. Additionally, a more restrictive rule governs whether a person has capacity to be a holder of a durable power of attorney. See Restatement Third, Property (Wills and Other Donative Transfers) § 8.2, Comment _f_ (minor lacks capacity to be holder of durable power of attorney).

If an agent is not an individual, ability to act is a function of the law through which the agent has legal personality. It is not necessary for an agent, as to the action taken, to have capacity to hold legal rights or be subject to liabilities.

**Illustration:**

1\. P permits A, P's 12-year-old child, to use P's computer, for which P has arranged Internet access. P has configured the computer such that, once the user is inside the website of T.com, a retailer of books, it is possible to order any particular book title by clicking on a button labeled "Buy It Now." Clicking on "Buy It Now" transmits the order to T.com, along with P's e-mail address, credit-card number, and password, all of which P has registered with T.com.

With P's consent, A enters an order for many books from T.com. P is bound to a contract with T.com, although A lacks capacity to make contracts that are enforceable against A because A is a minor. The computer itself is an instrumentality that P has chosen to use in interacting with T, and not P's agent, because it is not a person. See § 1.04(5).

In Illustration 1, A acted with actual authority as defined by § 2.01. In contrast, if A uses the computer to order books from T.com without P's consent, A acts without actual authority. By permitting A to have access to the computer, loaded as it is with the "Buy It Now" feature, P has created the risk that A will use the feature without P's consent and without actual authority. Some uses of the facility that A may make may be so extravagant that human agents of T.com, or even T.com's own computer system, would be able to recognize that the new purchases depart from P's established purchasing pattern. Such a recognition is not compatible with the presence of apparent authority, as defined in § 2.03. On the other hand, P affirmatively chose to enable the "Buy It Now" feature, which made A's unauthorized spree much easier for A to effect. Whether A acted with actual or apparent authority in so using P's computer, the contract that results between P and T may be subject to rescission if P returns the books to T and requests a refund. See Restatement Third, Restitution and Unjust Enrichment § 16, Comment _h_ (Tentative Draft No. 1, 2001).

Power is a broader concept than authority. One who holds power is not necessarily an agent as defined in § 1.01. For example, holding an irrevocable power as defined in § 3.12 does not make the holder an agent as defined in § 1.01. Likewise, a person who acts with apparent authority as defined in § 2.03 has power to affect the legal relations of another person even though actual authority as defined in § 2.01 is not present. A former agent, all of whose actual authority has been revoked by the principal, may act with apparent authority as defined in § 2.03. See § 3.11, Comment c, for further discussion of the apparent or "lingering" authority of former agents.

Capacity is not identical to legality, in that a person may have power to bind another although in exercising the power the person acts illegally. For example, a securities broker whose license has lapsed who buys securities on behalf of a customer affects the customer's legal position by making the customer the owner of the securities. Likewise, the agent of one party to a transaction does not lack capacity to act as agent for the other party as well. Doing so without consent from both principals breaches the agent's duty as stated in § 8.03 and may result in a transaction that either principal may avoid. See § 8.03, Comment _d._

_c. Extent of duties and liabilities._ Persons who act as agents owe duties toward their principals and become subject to liabilities to their principals if they breach those duties. See §§ 8.01-8.12. A person acting as an agent may also become subject to duties and liabilities owed to third parties. On an agent's liability stemming from contracts, see §§ 6.01-6.04 and §§ 6.10-6.11. On an agent's tort liability, see § 7.01.

A minor who acts as an agent lacks legal capacity to be a trustee of an express trust or to make an enforceable contract. Such incapacity is not by itself a defense to a claim for restitution asserted against the minor by the principal or a third party. Although a minor may disaffirm a contract, an agent's minority does not in itself immunize from liability conduct that is tortious or otherwise wrongful.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ The rules stated in this section follow in substance the black letter of Restatement Second, Agency § 21. Restatement Second, Agency § 19 deals with appointment to commit an illegal act.

_b. Capacity to affect the legal relations of another._ The proposition that any person may act on another's behalf appears to be well-established but does not appear in recent authority. For codified statutory statements, see Cal. Civ. Code § 2296 ("any person may be an agent"); Ga. Code, ch. 6, § 10-6-3 ("\[a\]ny person who is of sound mind may be appointed an agent; so a principal shall be bound by the acts of his infant agent"); Mont. Code § 28-10-104 ("any person may be an agent"); N.D. Cent. Code § 3-01-04 (same). For case support applying comparable concepts under Louisiana law, see [_Prelow v. Dorian, 53 So. 2d 467, 470 (La.App.1951)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-84C0-0044-C2JS-00000-00&context=1530671) (minor who is incapable of entering into valid contract may accept contract of mandate).

For an application of this general principle to tort liability, see [_Comeau v. Lucas, 455 N.Y.S.2d 871 (App.Div. 1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-27P0-003D-G468-00000-00&context=1530671) (parents of 16-year-old left in charge of property liable for assault committed by intoxicated musician on guest at party; applicable standard of care is reasonable expectation of children of like age, intelligence, and experience).

Actions taken by computers and other machines, without immediate direction, can bind the party who chose to transact via computer. See, e.g., [_State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co. v. Bockhorst, 453 F.2d 533, 536 (10th Cir.1972)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-7M50-0039-X1NP-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurance company bound as to damage done during period of time policy lapsed because computer, incorrectly according to insurer, permitted policy to apply retroactively once premium was paid); [_McEvans v. Citibank, 408 N.Y.S.2d 870, 872 (Civ. 1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-GN40-003C-F3C6-00000-00&context=1530671) (ATM machine, although unable to create debtor-creditor relationship with bank customer who used machine, created bailment relationship when ATM was to hold funds of customer until bank accepted deposit; bank liable for lost funds because customer unable to verify steps of transaction, as is possible by observing actions of a human teller).

_c. Extent of duties and liabilities._ Comment c to Restatement Second, Agency § 21 states that "\[a\]lthough all persons have legal capacity to hold powers, not all persons have legal capacity to be subject to fiduciary duties. Thus, a person without capacity to bind himself because lacking in mentality may have a power to bind one who appoints him to act, but he is not subject to the duties or liabilities which result from a fiduciary relationship. Likewise, an infant is not subject to the liabilities to which an adult agent would be subject." Minors and legally incompetent adults cannot act as trustees, at least until the disability is removed, because their transfers and contracts are voidable. See Restatement Third, Trusts § 32, Comment c. On minors and alternate structures for holding property, see Robert H. Mnookin & D. Kelly Weisberg, Child, Family and State 359-363 (4th ed. 2000). A duty to make restitution may be applicable to persons lacking legal capacity. See Restatement of Restitution § 139 ("\[i\]ncapacity to enter into a contract or to incur liability in tort is not in itself a defense in an action for restitution"); Francis M.B. Reynolds, Bowstead & Reynolds on Agency 39 (17th ed. 2001) (observing that "there is no reason why" a minor should not be subject to liability as a constructive trustee). See also Re Vinogradoff \[1935\] W.N. 68 (minor transferee of security held security as trustee of resulting trust when transferor, minor's grandmother, transferred security into joint names of herself and her granddaughter).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 3.06 Introductory Note_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-401Y-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 3- Creation and Termination of Authority and Agency Relationships_ > _Topic 4- Termination of Agent's Power_**

**Restat 3d of Agency, § 3.06 Introductory Note**

This Topic deals with events that have the effect of terminating an agent's actual authority and apparent authority. Different rules, also covered by this Topic, govern the termination of powers given as security and other irrevocable powers created pursuant to legislation, chiefly irrevocable proxies to vote securities. See § 3.13. Although the creation of such a power does not create a relationship of agency as defined in § 1.01, the result is a close cognate of agency because the legal consequences of the holder's acts within the scope of the power are attributed to its creator.

This Topic does not deal specifically with the consequences of termination. In general, once an agent's actual authority has terminated, the agent does not have the right to act on behalf of the principal. See § 8.09(1) for the agent's duty to the principal to act only within the scope of actual authority. However, an agent may have apparent authority as to a third party following the termination of the agent's actual authority. See § 3.11. If a principal is subject to liability to a third party because an agent acted with apparent authority following termination of the agent's actual authority, the agent's action is wrongful as to the principal. The principal has a claim against the agent for any loss that the principal incurs as a result of the agent's conduct. See § 8.09, Comment _b._ If the termination breaches a contract between a principal and agent, the nonbreaching party has a claim against the party whose termination breached the contract. See §§ 8.07 and 8.13.

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 3.06_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-4020-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 3- Creation and Termination of Authority and Agency Relationships_ > _Topic 4- Termination of Agent's Power_ > _Title A- Termination of Actual Authority_**

**§ 3.06 Termination of Actual Authority--In General**

**An agent's actual authority may be terminated by:**

**(1) the agent's death, cessation of existence, or suspension of powers as stated in § 3.07(1) and (3); or**

**(2) the principal's death, cessation of existence, or suspension of powers as stated in § 3.07(2) and (4); or**

**(3) the principal's loss of capacity, as stated in § 3.08(1) and (3); or**

**(4) an agreement between the agent and the principal or the occurrence of circumstances on the basis of which the agent should reasonably conclude that the principal no longer would assent to the agent's taking action on the principal's behalf, as stated in § 3.09; or**

**(5) a manifestation of revocation by the principal to the agent, or of renunciation by the agent to the principal, as stated in § 3.10(1); or**

**(6) the occurrence of circumstances specified by statute.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. General overview._ An agent's actual authority is premised upon a manifestation of assent made by the principal, in accordance with which the agent reasonably believes the principal wishes the agent to take action. See § 3.01. Several distinct types of events terminate an agent's actual authority. This Comment outlines the salient features of each route toward termination and the relationships among them and describes how the treatment in this Restatement differs from that in Restatement Second, Agency. First, a principal and an agent may agree to terminate the agent's actual authority either as of the time of agreement or upon the future occurrence of specified circumstances. See § 3.09. Second, notwithstanding any agreement between principal and agent, either may terminate the agent's actual authority by a manifestation to the other. See § 3.10. Third, an individual principal's death or loss of legal capacity terminates the agent's actual authority, as does cessation of existence or suspension of powers of a nonindividual principal. See § 3.07(2) and (4) and § 3.08(1) and (3). An agent's death or cessation of existence or suspension of powers also terminates the agent's actual authority. See § 3.07(1) and (3). Finally, a statute may provide that an agent's actual authority will terminate upon the occurrence of circumstances specified by the statute, including the impact of divorce upon a spouse's actual authority to act on behalf of the other spouse. Such statutes are beyond the scope of this Restatement.

_b. Agreement between principal and agent and changes in circumstances._ The basis for actual authority is a manifestation of assent made by a principal to an agent. See § 3.01. When the manifestation is embodied in an agreement that specifies circumstances under which the agent's actual authority shall terminate, occurrence of a specified circumstance effects termination of the principal's expressed assent. Regardless of whether the initial agreement contains a termination provision, mutual agreement to terminate the agency relationship is always effective to terminate the agent's actual authority. See § 3.09.

Following the principal's manifestation of assent to an agent, circumstances may change such that, at the time the agent takes action, it is not reasonable for the agent to believe that the principal at that time consents to the action being taken on the principal's behalf even though the principal has not manifested dissent to the action by that time. For example, the agent may become insolvent and have notice that it is important to the principal to be represented by a solvent agent. The agent may lose capacity to bind itself by a contract or to become subject to other obligations and have notice that it is important to the principal that the agent retain such capacity. Events that are totally outside the control of the agent or the principal may also make it unreasonable for the agent to believe that the principal consents to the agent's action. For example, if the principal retains the agent to sell goods in a particular geographically defined market, the occurrence of war or widespread civil unrest may so impair the value of the agent's efforts to the principal that the agent would not be reasonable in believing the principal wishes the agent to sell into the territory. The agent then lacks actual authority so to act but would have acted with actual authority had the agent acted prior to the change in circumstances. The focal point for determining whether an agent acted with actual authority is the time of action, not the time of the principal's manifestation, which may be earlier. See §§ 2.01 and 2.02.

An alternate analysis of the impact of changed circumstances, as set forth in Restatement Second, Agency, requires the use of at least two distinct focal points. First, it is posited that an agent has actual authority up until the agent receives notice of the changed circumstances. Notice that circumstances have changed, beyond those operative when the principal manifested consent to the agent, then implicitly modifies either the principal's manifestation of consent or the actual authority it engenders on the basis of the agent's reasonable belief in the principal's consent. See Restatement Second, Agency, Introductory Note to Chapter 5. Moreover, if circumstances thereafter revert to those present originally, the agent's actual authority, having once existed but thereafter having been terminated, is again revived. See Restatement Second, Agency § 108(2). This Restatement does not follow this alternate pattern of analysis because it is unnecessary in light of the nature of actual authority and the rules that determine whether an agent acted with actual authority. See §§ 2.01-2.02.

_c. Manifestation of dissent--revocation and renunciation._ In a relationship of agency, the principal or the agent has power to terminate the agent's actual authority. The power is exercised through a manifestation of dissent to the other through which the principal revokes the agent's authority or the agent renounces it. See § 3.10. The power to terminate does not depend upon the existence of or conformity with an agreement between the agent and the principal. Exercising the power to terminate may constitute a breach of contract. The power to terminate stems from the character of actual authority, which assumes ongoing and manifest assent that is operative when an agent takes action.

_d. Death, cessation of existence, suspension of powers, and loss of capacity._ Agency is a relationship between two persons in which one acts on behalf of the other. This is so even when the principal or the agent, or both, are not individuals. When a principal ceases to exist, the expression of assent in the principal's manifestation to the agent becomes inoperative because it is no longer that of a person, presently in existence, on whose behalf the agent acts. Likewise, when an agent ceases to exist, actual authority terminates because the principal's manifestation of assent was to a particular person who no longer can take action. Under the rule stated in Restatement Second, Agency § 120, the principal's death terminates the agent's actual authority even when the agent, or a third party with whom the agent dealt, had no notice of the principal's death. Apparent authority terminates likewise even when the third party had no notice of the principal's death. Restatement Second, Agency § 120, Comment c.

This Restatement treats the consequences of an individual principal's death differently, following the lead of commercial legislation and § 4(a) of the Uniform Durable Power of Attorney Act. In general, the principal's death terminates the agent's actual authority. See § 3.07(2). However, subject to provisions in other bodies of law that make the agent's action ineffective to bind the principal's estate, the termination of actual authority is not effective until the agent has notice of the principal's death, so long as the agent's act would be effective as that of an agent acting with actual authority, were the principal then alive. A principal's manifestation of assent to an agent, on which the agent's actual authority is premised by §§ 2.01 and 3.01, remains a basis on which an agent may reasonably and in good faith determine to act, notwithstanding the fact of the principal's death, when the agent does not have knowledge of the principal's death. See Restatement Third, Property (Wills and Other Donative Transfers) § 8.1, Comment _l._ Likewise, the principal's manifestation to a third party, on which the agent's apparent authority is premised under § 2.03, is undiminished in effect by the principal's death unbeknownst to the third party. See § 3.07, Comment _e,_ and § 3.11, Comment _b._

Under Restatement Second, Agency § 122, the principal's loss of capacity likewise terminated the agent's actual authority and apparent authority. No position was taken as to the effect of temporary incapacity due to a principal's mental disease. This Restatement treats the consequences of an individual principal's loss of capacity differently, again following the lead set by statutes of broad applicability. In general, the principal's loss of capacity terminates the agent's actual authority, unless the principal has executed a valid durable power of attorney. See § 3.08(2). However, subject to provisions in other bodies of law that make the agent's action ineffective to bind the principal, the termination of actual authority is not effective until the agent has notice that the principal has been adjudicated to lack capacity, or that the principal's loss of capacity is permanent, so long as the agent's act would be effective as the act of an agent acting with actual authority, if the principal then had full legal capacity. The principal's loss of capacity does not terminate an agent's apparent authority, which under § 2.03 is based on a manifestation made to a third party by the principal when the principal had capacity.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

Several sections in Restatement Second, Agency, analyze the consequences of changed circumstances by positing that actual authority, which the agent once had, is thereby terminated, as opposed to focusing more simply on whether the agent had actual authority at the time of acting. See Restatement Second, Agency §§ 105 (lapse of time); 106 (accomplishment of authorized act); 107 (happening of specified events); 108 (happening of unspecified events or changes); 109 (change in value or business conditions); 110 (loss or destruction of subject matter); 111 (loss of qualification of principal or agent); 112 (disloyalty of agent); 113 (bankruptcy of agent); 114 (bankruptcy of principal); 115 (war); 116 (change of law); and 124 (impossibility).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 3.07_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-4021-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 3- Creation and Termination of Authority and Agency Relationships_ > _Topic 4- Termination of Agent's Power_ > _Title A- Termination of Actual Authority_**

**§ 3.07 Death, Cessation of Existence, and Suspension of Powers**

**(1) The death of an individual agent terminates the agent's actual authority.**

**(2) The death of an individual principal terminates the agent's actual authority. The termination is effective only when the agent has notice of the principal's death. The termination is also effective as against a third party with whom the agent deals when the third party has notice of the principal's death.**

**(3) When an agent that is not an individual ceases to exist or commences a process that will lead to cessation of existence or when its powers are suspended, the agent's actual authority terminates except as provided by law.**

**(4) When a principal that is not an individual ceases to exist or commences a process that will lead to cessation of its existence or when its powers are suspended, the agent's actual authority terminates except as provided by law.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Cross-references._ Section 2.01 defines actual authority. For the impact of a principal's death on the agent's apparent authority, see Comment _e_ and § 3.11, Comment _b._ Notice is defined in § 1.04(4).

_b. Death of individual agent._ An agent's death terminates the agent's actual authority because the person to whom, and concerning whom, the principal manifested assent no longer exists. The agent's actual authority is not transferable to the agent's estate or heirs. In contrast, a power held as security as defined in § 3.12 survives the death of the holder and passes into the holder's estate, unless the power otherwise provides or the holder's death terminates the interest secured by the power. The death of the holder of an irrevocable proxy may terminate the proxy, but does not do so under all circumstances. See § 3.12, Comment _d._ The impact of the death of an appointing agent on the actual authority of a subagent is as stated in § 3.15, Comment _e._

_c. Cessation of existence or suspension of powers when agent or principal is not an individual._ The circumstances under which a person that is not an individual ceases to exist are governed by the legal regime by virtue of which the person has legal personality. Organizational statutes often specify the degree to which there is authority to take actions on behalf of an organization that has commenced the process that will culminate in cessation of its existence. See § 3.04, Comment _d._

_d. Death of individual principal._ An individual principal's death terminates the agent's actual authority because it negates the existence of the person on whose behalf the agent acts. The rule stated in subsection (2) specifies when the termination becomes effective in a manner that protects agents and third parties who act without knowledge of the principal's death. It is consistent with the position taken in Restatement Third, Property (Wills and Other Donative Transfers) § 8.1, Comment _l,_ but contrary to the position taken in Restatement Second, Agency § 120.

In contrast, its creator's death does not terminate a power granted as security unless the power so provides or the creator's death discharges the obligation secured by the power. Although actions taken by the holder of the power may affect the creator's legal relations, the holder is not an agent as defined by § 1.01 because the holder does not act solely on the grantor's behalf. See §§ 3.12-3.13. The principal's death terminates the actual authority of an agent whom the principal has appointed pursuant to a durable power of attorney. See § 3.08(2). Statutes that designate agents by operation of law do so to protect the interests of third parties. Such a designation does not require the consent and manifestation by the principal requisite to a relationship of agency as defined in § 1.01. Thus, the effect of these designations is not undone by the principal's death.

The impact of the principal's death on an agent's actual authority has the potential to create harsh consequences because the agent, unaware of the principal's death, may continue to act in good faith following it. The agent risks claims from third parties that the agent breached express or implied warranties of authority. See § 6.10. Third parties risk the loss of transactions to which the agent committed the principal. Legislation has long mitigated the common-law result. Widespread adoption of consistent legislation of general applicability is a reliable measure of contemporary policy. The residual common-law rule should reflect the policy judgments reflected in legislation such as the Uniform Commercial Code, the Uniform Durable Power of Attorney Act, and contemporary partnership statutes. Banks are protected by the provisions of U.C.C. § 4-405, which continue a bank's actual authority to pay or otherwise handle items when the bank does not know that its customer has died. Under § 4(a) of the Uniform Durable Power of Attorney Act, when a principal has executed any power of attorney, whether or not durable (see § 3.08(2)), the principal's death does not revoke the power of an agent whose authority is stated in the power and as to other persons, if they deal under the power in good faith and without actual knowledge of the principal's death. A majority of states have adopted statutes based on or comparable in effect to § 4(a).

Contemporary partnership statutes, such as the Revised Uniform Partnership Act, are in marked contrast with the 1914 Uniform Partnership Act. Under the 1914 Act, a partner's death automatically dissolved the partnership, thereby terminating the authority of all partners to act for the partnership except to complete transactions already begun and to wind up partnership affairs. See Uniform Partnership Act §§ 31(4) and 33. Under § 601(7)(i) of the Revised Uniform Partnership Act, while a partner's death dissociates the partner from the partnership, dissolution of the partnership does not follow automatically. Dissolution follows only if at least half of the surviving partners decide to wind up partnership business. See Revised Uniform Partnership Act § 801(2)(i). Moreover, under § 804(2), a partnership is bound by a partner's act following dissolution, even if the act is not incident to winding-up, if the act would have bound the partnership prior to dissolution and the other party to the transaction did not have notice of the dissolution.

The policy choice reflected in these statutes is to reduce risks imposed by the common-law rule upon agents and third parties who deal in a manner consistent with the principal's prior manifestations of assent. The common-law rule, in contrast, emphasizes the personal relationship that agency creates. The principal's death eliminates the principal's capacity to exercise control over an agent when the agent acts, as well as the distinct legal personality on whose behalf the agent acts. An allied doctrine in contract law terminates an offeree's power of acceptance when the offeror dies or loses legal capacity, albeit without notice to the offeree. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 48. Both the agency and contract doctrines may reflect the now-outmoded view that formation of a contract required a meeting of the parties' minds, as opposed to the contemporary view "that a manifestation of assent is effective without regard to actual mental assent." Restatement Second, Contracts § 48, Comment a; see Restatement Second, Agency § 120, Comment a.

The qualification to the common-law rule stated in subsection (2) departs from Restatement Second, Agency § 120, to which recent cases continue to adhere. The qualification is justified by the logic and fairness of the choice reflected in the statutes discussed above. Under subsection (2), a principal's death is not effective to terminate actual authority until the agent has notice of it. An agent who acts unreasonably, given the principal's prior instructions, is not protected by subsection (2) because an agent who so acted would lack actual authority, regardless of the principal's death. See §§ 2.01 and 2.02. Under subsection (2), if an agent lacks notice of the principal's death, the termination of actual authority is effective as against a third party who has notice of the death. If the agent has notice of the death but the third party does not, the agent does not act with actual authority. However, the third party may be protected by the doctrine of apparent authority because the principal's death does not automatically terminate an agent's apparent authority. See Comment _e_ and § 3.11, Comment _b._

The rule stated in subsection (2) is subject to other bodies of law, such as the law applicable to decedents' estates, that render the agent's action ineffective as against the principal's estate. Additionally, specific types of agency relationships may warrant different rules that are less protective of the agent. For example, a client's death terminates a lawyer's actual authority. See Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 31.

_e. Impact of principal's death on agent's apparent authority._ An agent's apparent authority terminates as provided in § 3.11. The principal's death does not by itself or automatically terminate the agent's apparent authority. See § 3.11, Comment _b._

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ The counterpart sections in Restatement Second, Agency, are §§ 120 and 121, which deal with individual principals and agents. Subsection (2) is contrary to the treatment of an individual principal's death in Restatement Second, Agency § 120. Restatement Second, Agency, does not deal with nonindividual agents and principals.

_b. Death of individual agent._ For the proposition that a testator may not transfer actual authority through a will because it terminates upon the testator's death, see [_Bowman v. Bowman, 210 N.E.2d 920, 922 (Ohio Prob. 1965)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-YGF0-003C-61N1-00000-00&context=1530671). An agent's authority does not constitute a property interest that is transferable to the agent's estate. See Restatement Third, Property (Wills and Other Donative Transfers) § 1.1 (defining "probate estate" as consisting of "property owned by the decedent at death and property acquired by the decedent's estate at or after the decedent's death"). See also Restatement of Property, Division I, Chapter 1, Introductory Note ("The word 'property' is used in this Restatement to denote legal relations between persons with respect to a thing. The thing may be an object having physical existence or it may be any kind of an intangible such as a patent right or a chose in action. The broader meanings of the word 'property,' which include any relationship having an exchange value, are not used"). Partnership law draws a distinction between a partner's share of profits and losses and right to receive distributions, which are transferable personal property, and the partner's other interests in the partnership such as management rights, which are not transferable. See Uniform Partnership Act § 26 (partner's interest in partnership consists of share of profits and surplus); Revised Uniform Partnership Act §§ 101(9) (defining "partnership interest") and 502 (defining partner's transferable interest in partnership).

_c. Cessation of existence or suspension of powers when agent or principal is not an individual._ For the basic proposition, see Barbados # 7, [_Bajan Servs., Inc., Tax Matters Partner v. Commissioner, 92 T.C. 804, 811 (T.C.1989)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SGP-JV40-003B-D07D-00000-00&context=1530671) (dissolution of corporate general partner, caused under Utah law by its bankruptcy, terminated all its authority to act as agent for limited partnership, and also dissolved limited partnership; under Utah law partner has authority postdissolution to complete transactions begun but not yet completed and to do acts necessary to winding up partnership affairs). See also [_Dorn v. _d. Death of individual principal._ For representative statements of the basic proposition that the principal's death terminates an agent's authority, see, e.g., [_United States v. Chin, 848 F.2d 55, 57 (4th Cir.1988)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-0XM0-001B-K3P3-00000-00&context=1530671) (holding that lawyer who represented decedent did not have standing to move to abate decedent's criminal conviction); [_Smith v. H.C. Bailey Cos., 477 So. 2d 224, 235 (Miss.1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-74B0-003G-70F1-00000-00&context=1530671) (death of owner of property instantaneously revoked authority of real-estate agent with whom property was listed for sale; questions of fact whether agent's entitlement to commission arose before owner's death and whether owner's devisees ratified entitlement within reasonable time following his death). On the impact of the client's death on a lawyer's actual authority, see Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 31(2)(b). The principal's death does not affect the validity of a transaction when the agent has completed all necessary actions prior to the principal's death. See [_In re Estate of Capuzzi, 684 N.W.2d 677, 680 (Mich. 2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4CS5-WX90-0039-41JW-00000-00&context=1530671).

The principal's death does not terminate the form of agency created by statute, which specifies persons on whom process may be served to effect service following the death or loss of capacity of a nonresident motorist. See [_Peterson v. Wade, 152 S.E.2d 745, 748 (Ga.1966)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-53T0-003F-J44C-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Toczko v. Armentano, 170 N.E.2d 703, 706 (Mass.1960)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-9300-003C-T1WF-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Leighton v. Roper, 91 N.E.2d 876 (N.Y.1950)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-XM40-003C-C2TR-00000-00&context=1530671).

U.C.C. § 4-405(a) provides that "\[n\]either death nor incompetence of a customer revokes \[a bank's\] authority to accept, pay, collect, or account until the bank knows of the fact of death or of an adjudication of incompetence and has reasonable opportunity to act on it." Under U.C.C. § 4-405(b), even with knowledge of a customer's death, a bank "may for 10 days after the date of death pay or certify checks drawn on or before that date unless ordered to stop payment by a person claiming an interest in the account."

The critique of the common-law rule stated in Restatement Second, Contracts § 48, Comment a, was cited with approval in [_Swift & Co. v. Smigel, 279 A.2d 895, 898 (N.J.Super.1971)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRR-2KD0-003C-N3GH-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_289 A.2d 793 (N.J. 1972)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-XJ30-003C-N3J5-00000-00&context=1530671) (holding that offeror's loss of capacity does not revoke offer when offeree lacks notice). But cf. _J.L. Lester & Son, Inc. v. Smith, 280 S.E.2d 732 (Ga.1981)_ (decedent's estate not liable for open account indebtedness to wholesaler incurred after death; wholesaler had notice of death, its principals having attended decedent's funeral).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 3.08_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-4022-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 3- Creation and Termination of Authority and Agency Relationships_ > _Topic 4- Termination of Agent's Power_ > _Title A- Termination of Actual Authority_**

**§ 3.08 Loss of Capacity**

**(1) An individual principal's loss of capacity to do an act terminates the agent's actual authority to do the act. The termination is effective only when the agent has notice that the principal's loss of capacity is permanent or that the principal has been adjudicated to lack capacity. The termination is also effective as against a third party with whom the agent deals when the third party has notice that the principal's loss of capacity is permanent or that the principal has been adjudicated to lack capacity.**

**(2) A written instrument may make an agent's actual authority effective upon a principal's loss of capacity, or confer it irrevocably regardless of such loss.**

**(3) If a principal that is not an individual loses capacity to do an act, its agent's actual authority to do the act is terminated.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Cross-references._ Section 2.01 defines actual authority. On the capacity requisite to act as agent, see § 3.05. If an agent loses capacity to become bound by legal obligations, the agent's actual authority is terminated if the agent has notice that its capacity to bind itself is important to the principal. See § 3.06, Comment _b._ On the capacity requisite to act as principal, see § 3.04. Notice is defined in § 1.04(4).

_b. Individual principal's loss of capacity._ An individual principal's loss of capacity does not automatically terminate an agent's actual authority. Likewise, a principal's loss of capacity does not automatically or by itself terminate an agent's apparent authority. See § 3.11, Comment _b._ This is contrary to the position taken in Restatement Second, Agency § 122(1), which equated the consequences of a principal's loss of capacity to the consequences of the principal's death. Section 120 terminated actual and apparent authority automatically upon the principal's death. See § 120, Comment c. This rule voided all transactions made by the agent on the principal's behalf, even when neither the agent nor the third party had notice of the principal's loss of capacity and regardless of the identity of the party who asserted the principal's loss of capacity. A Caveat to § 122 explicitly took no position on "the effect of the principal's temporary incapacity due to a mental disease."

The rule stated in § 122 should no longer be followed. This is so for two distinct groups of reasons. First, the rule made transactions on behalf of a principal automatically void, thereby denying the principal, or the principal's estate, the benefit of transactions that advance the principal's interests in conformity with the agent's reasonable understanding of them. Moreover, loss of legal capacity, unlike death, is not always final and its occurrence is often not precisely associated with a particular moment or event. When no judicial proceeding has determined a principal's lack of capacity, its loss may be difficult for the agent, the principal, or the third party to ascertain. Finally, the rule of automatic voidness can operate to benefit parties to the detriment of the principal's estate, in ways directly contrary to the principal's expressed intentions. For example, a rule making transactions automatically void disqualifies the principal's estate from benefiting from tax advantages associated with financial instruments that confer estate-tax benefits on the owner's estate if the instruments were purchased by an agent at a time when, in retrospect, it could be demonstrated that the principal lacked capacity.

Second, the rule making such transactions automatically void creates substantial risks for agents acting in good faith, as well as for third parties. Widespread adoption of legislation mitigating such risks suggests that it is not necessary or desirable to treat as automatically void the transactions of an agent on behalf of a principal who lacks capacity at the time of the transaction. Several types of legislation are relevant. U.C.C. § 4-405 protects banks when a customer loses capacity but the bank does not have actual knowledge of an adjudication of incompetence. Under partnership legislation, a partner's loss of capacity does not automatically dissolve the partnership and terminate fellow partners' authority. Statutes that permit the creation of durable powers of attorney enable a principal to address, in advance of disability, the need to take legally consequential action should disability occur, when it is attractive to the principal to be able to do so without anticipating the appointment of a guardian or conservator. Separate from the creation of a durable power, statutes in many states provide, in substantial conformity with § 4(b) of the Uniform Durable Power of Attorney Act, that the disability or incapacity of a principal who has previously executed a power of attorney that is not a durable power does not terminate the agency as to the holder of the power or other person who deals in good faith without actual knowledge of the principal's disability or incapacity. A majority of states have adopted statutes in substantial conformity with § 4(b).

Most recent cases reject the rule stated in Restatement Second, Agency § 122. Instead, they treat a principal's loss of capacity as a condition comparable to minority that does not destroy legal person-hood but justifies an election whether to be bound by transactions. Under this approach, transactions are voidable, not void, unless the principal's loss of capacity is known at the outset to be permanent or a court has adjudicated the principal to lack capacity. If the principal recovers capacity, the principal may exercise the right to disaffirm or ratify transactions or the right may be exercised by the principal's guardian if and when one is appointed.

The drawback to this approach is that it does not necessarily protect agents and third parties who have acted reasonably in light of the principal's manifestations of assent and who lack notice of the principal's incapacity. Loss of capacity is not fully analogous to minority because, absent a judicial determination of incapacity, it is less ascertainable by lay persons on the basis of objectively observable criteria, including such official indicia of age as birth certificates and drivers' licenses. If the principal elects to disaffirm a transaction upon recovering capacity, the agent risks liability to the third party for breaching the agent's express or implied warranty of authority. See § 6.10. Moreover, if the third party did not have notice of the principal's loss of capacity, the third party would not have notice that the transaction might later be disaffirmed by the principal. Finally, lacking notice of the principal's loss of capacity, the third party lacks occasion to attempt on that basis to take advantage of the principal. None of the cases that treat such transactions as voidable involve these consequences.

The rule stated in subsection (1), based on the policy of the statutes discussed above, does not make such transactions voidable. Instead, it provides that the termination of actual authority that follows a principal's loss of legal capacity is not effective when the agent did not have notice of the principal's loss of capacity. If the agent did not have such notice, but the third party had notice of the principal's incapacity, the termination of actual authority is effective as against the third party. If the agent had such notice, but the third party did not have notice, the principal may be bound by the doctrine of apparent authority. See § 3.11, Comment _b._ For purposes of subsection (1), notice of a principal's incapacity requires notice of an adjudication to that effect, or notice of circumstances establishing that the principal's loss of capacity is permanent.

Although the rule in subsection (1) does not permit a principal to disaffirm transactions if the principal regains legal capacity, it protects the principal because the principal is not bound by transactions that would exceed the agent's actual authority, if the principal had capacity at the time the agent acted. If the principal is bound to the third party because the agent acted with apparent authority, the principal has a claim against the agent.

The rule stated in subsection (1) does not undermine the reasons for a principal to execute a durable power of attorney, as provided in subsection (2). The formal written character of a durable power provides much greater certainty for the principal, the agent, and third parties, and its effect need not turn on whether it can be shown the agent or third party lacked notice of the principal's loss of legal capacity. Subsection (1) is subject to other bodies of law, such as those prescribing the consequences of judicial appointment of a guardian for the principal.

_c. Durable powers of attorney._ Section 1.04(7) defines power of attorney. A durable power constitutes a written designation of an attorney-in-fact. The power must contain language expressing the principal's intention that the power shall not be affected by the principal's subsequent disability or loss of capacity, or that the power shall become effective upon the principal's subsequent disability or incapacity. The power states the authority that the holder shall possess to act on the principal's behalf. Many states provide statutory forms that may be used for this purpose, which delineate the types of acts that the agent may be authorized to do based on the principal's explicit choices. To be effective, a durable power must comply with any applicable statute, which may limit the scope of the authority that a durable power may create. See Restatement Third, Property (Wills and Other Donative Transfers) § 8.1, Comment _l._ The principal may revoke the power so long as the principal has capacity. See § 3.10. State statutes govern the relationship between the authority of an agent who holds a durable power of attorney and the authority of a guardian or other legal representative appointed by a court.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ Loss of capacity is dealt with in Restatement Second, Agency § 122. Restatement Second, Agency, does not contain a counterpart to subsections (2) and (3). The rule stated in § 122(1) is counter to the position taken herein in subsection (1).

_b. Individual principal's loss of capacity._ For the proposition that the principal's loss of capacity does not necessarily terminate the agent's authority, see [_Trepanier v. Bankers Life & Cas. Co., 706 A.2d 943, 944 (Vt.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RJC-WJT0-0039-40M7-00000-00&context=1530671) (principal's descent into coma did not terminate agent's authority; agent's act at most voidable); [_TransWorld Airlines, Inc. v. Summa Corp., 394 A.2d 241, 246 (Del.Ch. 1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRT-92G0-003C-K4WP-00000-00&context=1530671) (alleged incompetence of corporation's controlling shareholder, who had not been adjudicated to be incompetent, did not terminate authority of lawyer to make binding admissions); see also [_Blackhurst v. Transamerica Ins. Co., 699 P.2d 688, 692 (Utah 1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-58T0-003G-F287-00000-00&context=1530671) (incompetent principal had no general guardian; insurer estopped to claim son lacked authority to negotiate settlement on behalf of incompetent parent when insurer knew of parent's incapacity and knew no general guardian had been appointed; guardian subsequently ratified settlement terms). For authority contra, see [_Estate of Berry, 329 N.Y.S.2d 915 (Sur.1972)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-9Y40-003C-D46R-00000-00&context=1530671) (principal's descent into comatose or semicomatose state would terminate agent's authority to transfer funds; transfer voided at instance of principal's intestate distributees and their assignees).

The implications of the doctrine stated in Restatement Second, Agency § 122(1) were most sharply illustrated in cases involving whether a decedent was an owner of a flower bond at the time of death. Flower bonds, issued by the U.S. Treasury between 1918 and 1971, had a long maturity, a relatively low coupon rate, and "blossomed" or "bloomed" to their full principal amount, plus any accrued interest, for the purpose of paying federal estate taxes due on the owner's estate. In effect the flower-bond feature created an option in the owner's estate to put the bond to the Treasury and a duty in the Treasury to redeem the bond for the purpose of paying estate taxes, if the owner's estate exercised the option. See [_Weld v. United States, 55 F.3d 623, 625 (Fed. Cir.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-M8G0-003N-4386-00000-00&context=1530671). The doctrine stated in Restatement Second, Agency § 122(1) was advanced by the Internal Revenue Service as a reason to disallow the use of a flower bond to pay estate taxes when the bond had been purchased by an agent on behalf of an incapacitated principal who had not been adjudicated to be incompetent. This argument was rejected in cases holding that the principal's incapacity made the purchase voidable, not void. See [_United States v. Manny, 645 F.2d 163 (2d Cir.1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-3N30-0039-W4WD-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Campbell v. United States, 657 F.2d 1174 (Ct. C1.1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-54W0-003B-84G3-00000-00&context=1530671).

For English authority rejecting the proposition that a principal may, upon regaining capacity, disaffirm transactions that were made on the principal's behalf while the principal lacked capacity, see Drew v. Nunn \[1879\] 4 Q.B.D. 661 (Ct. App.) (third-party tradesman who supplied goods, upon order of principal's wife who was held out by principal as agent with authority to deal and draw checks on his behalf, was unaware of principal's insanity; upon regaining sanity, principal may not disaffirm transactions made by wife-agent).

U.C.C. § 4-405(a) provides that "\[n\]either death nor incompetence of a customer revokes \[a bank's\] authority to accept, pay, collect, or account until the bank knows of the fact of death or of an adjudication of incompetence and has reasonable opportunity to act on it."

Partnership legislation has not followed the common-law rule by automatically dissolving a partnership upon a partner's loss of capacity. Under § 32(1)(a) of the 1914 Uniform Partnership Act, the fact that a partner "has been declared a lunatic in any judicial proceeding or is shown to be of unsound mind" is a basis on which a court shall decree dissolution on an application by or for a partner. Under § 601(7)(ii) of the Revised Uniform Partnership Act, a partner is dissociated from a partnership by the appointment of a guardian or general conservator, and under § 601(7)(iii) dissociation follows a "judicial determination that the partner has otherwise become incapable of performing the partner's duties under the partnership agreement. . . ." Dissociation leads to dissolution under § 801(2) only when at least half of the remaining partners decide to wind up partnership business.

Section 4(b) of the Uniform Durable Power of Attorney Act provides:

The disability or incapacity of a principal who has previously executed a written power of attorney that is not a durable power does not revoke or terminate the agency as to the attorney in fact or other person, who, without actual knowledge of the disability or incapacity of the principal, acts in good faith under the power. Any action so taken, unless otherwise invalid or unenforceable, binds the principal and his successors in interest.

Statutes based on § 4(b), or to the same substantive effect, are: Ala. Code § 26-1-2(d)(2) (2004); Alaska Stat. § 13.26.356 (2004); Ariz. Rev. Stat. Ann. § 14-5504(B) (West 2005); Cal. Prob. Stat. § 551D-4(b) (Lexis 2000 & Supp. 2005); Idaho Code § 15-5-504(2) (2001); Iowa Code Ann. § 633.706(1) (West 2003); Kan. Stat. Ann. § 58-613(b) (1994); Ky. Rev. Stat. Ann. § 386.093(4) (2005); Me. Ann. § 506:6 III (Supp. 2005); N.M. Stat. Ann. § 45-5-504(B) (1995 & Supp. 2002); N.D. Cent. Code § 30.1-30-04(2) (1996 & Supp. 2005); Okla. Stat. Ann. § 243.07(4)(b) (2001 & Supp. 2005). The Alaska, Colorado, and Maryland statutes are structured to be applicable when the principal has executed any power, whether or not durable. Under the Texas statute, the qualification of a guardian for the principal's estate does not terminate the agent's authority as to a person who lacked actual knowledge of the appointment and acted in good faith under or in reliance on the power. See Texas Probate Code Ann. § 486(a) (2003).

_c. Durable powers of attorney._ On revocation of a durable power, see [_Matter v. Williams, 832 F.Supp. 244, 247-248 (C.D.Ill.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-F660-001T-618M-00000-00&context=1530671) (competent principal revoked durable health-care power; holder of revoked power did not have claim either under statute requiring notice or for tortious interference with prospective economic advantage).

The applicable statute governs the effectiveness of the power. See [_In re Trust of Franzen, 955 P.2d 1018, 1021 (Colo.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SG0-9050-0039-43T4-00000-00&context=1530671) (durable-power-of-attorney statute expressly inapplicable to instruments executed prior to its effective date).
