[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 3.09_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-4023-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 3- Creation and Termination of Authority and Agency Relationships_ > _Topic 4- Termination of Agent's Power_ > _Title A- Termination of Actual Authority_**

**§ 3.09 Termination by Agreement or by Occurrence of Changed Circumstances**

**An agent's actual authority terminates (1) as agreed by the agent and the principal, subject to the provisions of § 3.10; or (2) upon the occurrence of circumstances on the basis of which the agent should reasonably conclude that the principal no longer would assent to the agent's taking action on the principal's behalf.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Cross-references._ Manifestation is defined in § 1.03. On the creation and termination of powers given as security and irrevocable proxies, see §§ 3.12 and 3.13.

_b. Mutual consent._ An agent's actual authority stems from a manifestation of assent by the principal to the agent. See §§ 2.01 and 3.01. The manifestation encompasses the terms of any agreement between agent and principal dealing with termination. For example, the agreement may provide that the agent's authority shall end as of a fixed date or interval of time, upon notice of intention to terminate given by one party to the other, upon the parties' later mutual agreement to terminate their relationship, or upon the happening of some other event. In determining whether parties have complied with provisions requiring notice of termination, courts assess whether the purpose of the provision was served by attempts made in good faith to give notice. Additionally, regardless of whether principal and agent have agreed on a time or a mechanism for termination, their mutual consent at any time is effective to terminate the agent's actual authority. If a third party is unaware of the termination, the agent may continue to act with apparent authority. See § 3.11. An agreement between principal and agent is ineffective to terminate a power or an irrevocable proxy given as security for the benefit of a third party. See § 3.13, Comment _b._

Additionally, an agent's actual authority may terminate upon the occurrence of circumstances under which the agent should reasonably conclude the principal would no longer assent to the agent's taking action on the principal's behalf. If the principal has engaged the agent for a particular task, its completion is such a circumstance. See Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 31(2)(e). Circumstances outside the control of the agent may also change in such drastic fashion that it is not reasonable for the agent to conclude that the principal, if aware of those circumstances, would wish the agent to act on the principal's behalf.

_c. Whether exercise of right is subject to good-faith standard._ A principal and agent may provide by agreement that either or both may terminate their relationship and thus the agent's actual authority. Such a provision does not categorically protect termination under all circumstances. In particular, having been terminated, a former agent may claim that the principal's exercise of the contractual right contravened the implied contractual covenant of good faith and fair dealing, alleging that the termination was the product of improper motives. Every contract imposes on each party "a duty of good faith and fair dealing in its performance and its enforcement." Restatement Second, Contracts § 205. When an agent is not an employee, courts are divided on whether the implied covenant is applicable at all to the decision to exercise an explicitly specified right to terminate authority. As Comment _e_ discusses, it is not necessary to invoke the implied covenant to protect an agent's right to receive commissions earned prior to the termination of authority. Most courts that have considered the question hold that the implied covenant is inapplicable to the exercise of a right to terminate in accord with terms of an agreement between a nonemployee agent and the principal. Recent authority applying the implied covenant in this context does so to enable the court to examine the consequences of the termination on the agent, in particular whether the termination appears to be a pretext to defeat the agent's right to commissions already earned. On the rights of nonemployee lawyers whose representation is terminated by the client before services have been completed, see Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 40.

_d. Duration of authority._ If the parties do not specify a duration for the agent's actual authority, it terminates after a reasonable period of time. What is a reasonable time is an issue for the trier of fact. Ongoing interactions between principal and agent may extend the duration of the agent's actual authority by making it reasonable for the agent to believe that the principal continues to consent to the agent's taking action on the principal's behalf.

**Illustrations:**

1\. P retains A, a real-estate agent, to sell Blackacre, specifying no limit on the duration of A's authority. Ten years pass with no communication between P and A. A then enters into a contract binding P to sell Blackacre to T. A's actual authority has terminated by reason of the passage of time.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that throughout the 10 years A reports to P on A's efforts to date and their results. P gives no indication to A that A's authority has terminated. A has actual authority to bind P to sell Blackacre to T.

_e. Impact on agent's commission._ The termination of actual authority does not extinguish the agent's right to be paid commissions that the agent has earned as of the time of termination. On the agent's rights to commissions and other compensation generally, see § 8.13, Comments c and _d._

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ The rule stated in this section is consistent with the substance of Restatement Second, Agency § 117.

_c. Whether exercise of right is subject to good-faith standard._ For the proposition that courts look to whether good-faith efforts to give notice have occurred, see [_Schwartz v. Fortune Magazine, 89 F.Supp.2d 429, 433 (S.D.N.Y.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3Y5M-8N90-0038-Y0T3-00000-00&context=1530671).

On the applicability of the implied covenant to the principal's termination of a nonemployee agent, see, e.g., [_Burnette Techno-Metrics, Inc. v. TSI Inc., 44 F.3d 641, 643 (8th Cir. 1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-YX90-003B-P0SV-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Minnesota law, court holds implied covenant inapplicable to manufacturer's decision to terminate sales agent pursuant to provision in agreement permitting either party to terminate upon 60 days' notice to the other; court also holds extrinsic evidence of oral assurances that no termination would be made without good cause to be inadmissible because barred by parol-evidence rule); [_Trionic Assocs., Inc. v. Harris Corp., 27 F.Supp.2d 175 (E.D.N.Y.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3V6W-K8N0-0038-Y13K-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _198 F.3d 235 (2d Cir.1999)_ (when agreement authorized termination for convenience of either party, implied covenant inapplicable to manufacturer's termination of sales representative); [_Ostrander v. Co., 851 P.2d 946, 950 (Idaho 1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-1CP0-003D-30VB-00000-00&context=1530671) (covenant inapplicable when plaintiff did not challenge status as nonemployee independent contractor; agreement provided that either party could cancel at any time, whether with or without cause); [_Melnick v. Co., 749 P.2d 1105 (N.M.1988)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W570-003D-D4MC-00000-00&context=1530671) (absent showing of improper motive, insurance agent may be terminated with no showing of good cause). For authority holding the covenant to be applicable, see [_Premier Technical Sales, Inc. v. Digital Equip. Corp., 11 F.Supp.2d 1156, 1161 (N.D.Cal.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3V86-9PK0-0038-Y1D5-00000-00&context=1530671), affd in part and rev'd in part on other grounds, _202 F.3d 279 (9th Cir.1999)_ (court examines consequences of termination for agent); _Seaward Yacht Sales, Ltd. v. Murray Chris-Craft Cruisers, Inc., 701 F.Supp. 766 (D.Or. 1988)_ (applying Oregon law; although implied covenant applicable, no bad faith shown). Some cases applying the covenant have been modified by subsequent authority. Compare [_Gambar Enters., Inc. v. Kelly Servs., Inc., 418 N.Y.S.2d 818 (App.Div.1979)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-CFS0-003C-F317-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Michigan law) with [_Cloverdale Equip. Co. v. Simon Aerials, Inc., 869 F.2d 934, 938 (6th Cir.1989)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-CTP0-003B-53HX-00000-00&context=1530671) (recognizing that _Gambar_ limited to situations in which party lacked good faith at time of bargaining for termination rights); and [_Hall v. Farmers Ins. Exch., 713 P.2d 1027 (Okla.1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RVB-WGG0-003G-628N-00000-00&context=1530671) with [_Devery Implement Co. v. J.I. Case Co., 944 F.2d 724, 728-729 (10th Cir. 1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-8R00-008H-V19G-00000-00&context=1530671) (recognizing subsequent limitation of _Hall_ to particular facts).

_d. Duration of authority._ For the proposition that an agent has actual authority for a reasonable time if none is specified in an agreement between the agent and the principal, see [_McCoy v. H.N.R. Inv. Group, L.P., 437 S.E.2d 355, 357 (Ga.App. 1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX8-BDJ0-003G-P19X-00000-00&context=1530671). Actual authority terminates upon the expiration of any time fixed by the parties. See [_Love v. Gamble, 448 S.E.2d 876, 881 (S.C.App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RVJ-C9X0-003G-C1BC-00000-00&context=1530671).

_e. Impact on agent's commission._ An agent's right to commissions already earned survives termination of actual authority in [_Fryer v. Boyett, 978 S.W.2d 304, 306 (Ark.App.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3V1G-BGH0-0039-44HV-00000-00&context=1530671) (actual authority of nonemployee sales representative terminated by principal pursuant to contractual provision giving principal election to terminate upon giving 30 days' written notice to agent; agent entitled to commissions earned prior to date of termination because contract implicitly distinguished between time at which right to commission could be earned and time at which commission would be payable).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 3.10_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-4024-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 3- Creation and Termination of Authority and Agency Relationships_ > _Topic 4- Termination of Agent's Power_ > _Title A- Termination of Actual Authority_**

**§ 3.10 Manifestation Terminating Actual Authority**

**(1) Notwithstanding any agreement between principal and agent, an agent's actual authority terminates if the agent renounces it by a manifestation to the principal or if the principal revokes the agent's actual authority by a manifestation to the agent. A revocation or a renunciation is effective when the other party has notice of it.**

**(2) A principal's manifestation of revocation is, unless otherwise agreed, ineffective to terminate a power given as security or to terminate a proxy to vote securities or other membership or ownership interests that is made irrevocable in compliance with applicable legislation. See §§ 3.12-3.13.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Cross-references._ Section 1.03 defines manifestation. Section 3.13 governs the termination of powers given as security and irrevocable proxies. A durable power of attorney, which makes the agent's actual authority irrevocable when or although the principal has lost legal capacity, is not a power given as security. See § 3.08(2) and Comment _c._ Therefore, this section, not § 3.13, applies to the termination of a durable power of attorney by a competent principal.

_b. Power to revoke or renounce actual authority._ A principal has power to revoke an agent's actual authority and the agent has power to renounce it. The power is not extinguished because an agreement between principal and agent states that the agent's actual authority shall be irrevocable or shall not be revoked except under specified circumstances. See § 3.09. Revocation or renunciation may terminate all of an agent's actual authority, or only a portion of it. Exercising the power to revoke or renounce may constitute a breach of contract. On contracts between agents and principals, see §§ 8.07 and 8.13. Statutes may vary these basic rules. For example, business-organization statutes may give delayed effect to the resignation of an agent for service of process. Moreover, the right of some agents to renounce authority is subject to restrictions. A lawyer's withdrawal from the representation of a client is rightful only if specific conditions are present. See Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 32(3).

The rationale for the power to revoke or renounce is that agency is a consensual relationship. Whether the principal or the agent consents to the relationship is a relevant question on an ongoing basis throughout the duration of the relationship. A manifestation of nonconsent, or dissent, to the other party is determinative. Agency thus differs from the consensual relationships created by contract law and trust law in which manifestations and conduct at the outset of the relationship determine the legal consequences that may follow, moving through time. The power to revoke or renounce actual authority, notwithstanding the terms of an agreement, is comparable in this respect to a partner's power under the Uniform Partnership Act (1914) to cause dissolution of a partnership by express will but in contravention of the partnership agreement. Under the Revised Uniform Partnership Act, notice to the partnership of such an expression of will is effective to dissociate the partner but the partnership itself does not dissolve if a majority of the remaining partners do not determine to wind up partnership business. Dissociation, however, terminates the dissociated partner's power to bind the partnership as well as the dissociated partner's liability to third parties arising from postdissolution transactions unless a statutorily defined form of apparent authority is present.

The justification for the power to revoke or renounce reflects the nature and legal consequences of agency. From the standpoint of a principal, enforcement of an agent's exercise of actual authority affects the principal's legal relations with third parties. A principal thus has the power to call back what could be characterized as a reflection or an extension of the principal's own legal personality. Moreover, a principal's power to control an agent's use of actual authority will seldom be perfect. Although the agent will be subject to liability to the principal if the agent does not act as instructed, and although the agent owes fiduciary duties to the principal, the agent holds ongoing power to act with adverse consequences for the principal. The principal's power to revoke the agent's authority reduces the principal's risk in retaining the agent because exercising the power does not require a showing that the agent has breached a duty owed to the principal. Exercising the power to revoke enables the principal to reclaim property that the agent will not rightfully possess following the revocation. See §§ 8.05 and 8.12. Exercising the power to revoke also enables the principal to avoid incurring the costs of heightened monitoring that might be warranted were the agent to remain in place. In contrast, the principal's ability to assert claims against the agent, whether for breach of contract or breach of fiduciary duty, is subject to the delays, costs, and other vagaries of litigation, a fact that would be open to exploitation by an agent who could not be removed.

Accordingly, if a principal's revocation of authority constitutes a breach of contract, the agent's remedies against the principal do not include specific performance. This is so even though the principal is a nonindividual person, such as a corporation, and even though an order of specific performance would not burden the court in enforcing the order or supervising the agent's performance because the contract allocates discretion in performance to the agent. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 366.

From the standpoint of an agent, the power to renounce authority enables the agent to act on the agent's own behalf or on behalf of other principals, freed of ongoing duties to the original principal. For exceptions involving confidential information possessed by former agents, see § 8.05, Comment c. The agent's potential freedom reduces the costs of becoming an agent because attractive opportunities, which the agent may not rightfully pursue due to the duties owed the principal, become available once the agent terminates the relationship. Accordingly, a court will not grant an order of specific performance against a former agent, although the principal may seek and receive an award of money damages. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 367.

**Illustrations:**

1\. P retains A to sell Blackacre in exchange for a percentage commission payable from the sale price. A agrees to advertise the property and devote energy to finding a buyer. In exchange, P grants A a power of attorney authorizing A to sell Blackacre, which states that it shall be irrevocable for one year. A does nothing. After three months, P tells A that P revokes A's authority. A's actual authority is terminated.

2\. Same fact as Illustration 1, except that A spends substantial sums advertising Blackacre. After three months, P tells A that P revokes A's authority. A's actual authority is terminated. A may be able to recover the advertising costs in an action against P. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 349.

3\. P, who owns a hotel, retains A Corp. to manage it. P and A Corp. enter into an agreement providing that, in exchange for A Corp.'s management services, A Corp. will receive a commission equal to five percent of the hotel's gross revenues. The agreement also provides that the hotel shall be renamed using a trade name owned by A Corp. The agreement further provides that, in exchange for the use of A Corp.'s trade name, A Corp.'s authority shall be irrevocable by P for a period of 10 years. Two years later, P revokes. A Corp.'s actual authority is terminated. Although A Corp. may have claims against P for breach of contract, specific performance is not an available remedy. A Corp.'s continued occupancy or other possession of the hotel is not rightful as to P.

_c. Manifestation of revocation or renunciation._ Many manifestations of revocation and renunciation are made explicitly and directly to the other party. However, in this context as in others, manifestations may be made indirectly as well as directly and implicitly as well as explicitly. See § 1.03. A manifestation of revocation or renunciation is not effective until the other party has notice of it. See § 1.04(4), which defines notice. When such notice is given is a question of fact.

**Illustration:**

4\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that P's statement revoking A's authority is made only in a letter, which P mails to A. Before the letter reaches A, and before A otherwise has notice of P's change of heart, A enters into a contract binding P to sell Blackacre to T. The contract binds P and T. A acted with actual authority because A did not have notice of P's revocation of authority.

It is not necessary that a manifestation of revocation or renunciation be in a particular form. An oral manifestation of revocation or renunciation is effective even when a writing evidencing the agent's authority is necessary to bind the principal. See § 3.02. However, formal requirements may apply to a manifestation from a principal that creates or expands an agent's authority.

**Illustration:**

5\. P executes a power of attorney authorizing A to sell Blackacre and Whiteacre. A statute of frauds is applicable to authority to sell the land of another. P orally tells A not to sell Whiteacre. A does not have actual authority to sell Whiteacre.

_d. Consequences of termination._ A revocation or renunciation may constitute a breach of contract if it contravenes the terms of an agreement between principal and agent. As to claims for breach of contract as between principal and agent, see §§ 8.07 and 8.13. A principal's revocation of authority does not extinguish the agent's right to commissions or other compensation already earned by the agent. See § 3.09, Comment _e._ Outside the employment context, a principal's revocation of authority may be actionable by the agent when the principal's motives or the consequences for the agent constitute a tort, such as wrongful interference with prospective economic advantage. See Restatement Second, Torts § 766B. An agent's renunciation of authority, like an employee's resignation, does not privilege postassociational conduct that is wrongful, such as wrongful use of confidential information. See § 8.04, Comment c, and § 8.05, Comment c.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency._ The black letter of this section is consistent with the substance of Restatement Second, Agency §§ 118-119. Sections 118 and 119 characterize the manifestation as one of "dissent" to the continuance of authority.

_b. Power to revoke or renounce actual authority._ Examples of business-organization statutes giving delayed effect to resignation of an agent for service of process are Unif. Limited Liability Co. Act § 110(c) (agency terminated on 31st day after copy of statement of resignation is filed in office of Secretary of State); Ariz. Rev. Stat. Ann. § 29-304(A)(2) (resignation of agent for service of process on limited partnership is effective when new agent is appointed or when notice of resignation is received by Secretary of State, whichever occurs first); Del. Code Ann., tit. 8, § 136(a) (if registered agent for corporation resigns without appointing a successor, resignation does not become effective until 30 days after certificate of resignation is filed with Secretary of State).

On a partner's power to dissolve a partnership in contravention of the partnership agreement, see Unif. Partnership Act § 31(2) (dissolution caused by express will of any partner at any time in contravention of partnership agreement when circumstances do not permit dissolution under any other provision in statute). Under the Revised Uniform Partnership Act, notice to the partnership of a partner's express will effects dissociation under § 601(1). The dissociation is wrongful under § 602(b)(1) if it breaches an express provision of the partnership agreement, and wrongful under § 602(b)(2) if it precedes the expiration of the term set by agreement for the partnership or the completion of a specified undertaking unless preceded within 90 days by another partner's dissociation. Section 801(2) provides that a partnership for a definite term or particular undertaking dissolves following a partner's wrongful dissociation if it is the express will of at least half of the remaining partners to wind up the partnership's business.

On the effect of dissolution on a partner's authority and liability, see Unif. Partnership Act §§ 33 and 35; Rev. Unif. Partnership Act §§ 702 and 703.

English partnership law, in contrast, does not empower a partner to dissolve the partnership unless the partnership agreement so permits when the partnership is for a fixed term and that term has not yet expired, or when the partnership is for a single "adventure or undertaking" that has not yet been completed. Partnership Act 1890 § 32(a) and (b). If the partnership has an undefined term, it is dissolved by any partner giving notice of intention to dissolve to the other partners. Id. § 32(c). A comprehensive study of English partnership law recommends that the giving of notice be treated as effective only to dissolve the relationship between the outgoing partner and the other partners, not the partnership itself. This proposal would constitute a default rule, subject to contrary provision in the partnership agreement. See The Law Commission and The Scottish Law Commission, Partnership Law: A Joint Consultation Paper PP 6.16-6.19 (Law Commission Consultation Paper No. 159; Scottish Law Commission Discussion Paper No. 111) (2000).

The point that the principal, having created actual authority, may always call it back appears in Francis Reynolds, When is an Agent's Authority Irrevocable? 259, 259 in Making Commercial Law: Essays in Honour of Roy Goode (Ross Cranston ed., 1997) ("\[t\]he reasoning is that the principal is conferring on the agent the power to commit him: the power is such a strong one that the principal must be free to call it back.").

The factual scenario and result in Illustration 3 are based on [_Government Guarantee Fund v. Hyatt Corp., 95 F.3d 291 (3d Cir.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-1030-006F-M1HW-00000-00&context=1530671). For applications of the same doctrine, see [_Pacific Landmark Hotel, Ltd. v. Marriott Hotels, Inc., 23 Cal.Rptr.2d 555 (Cal.App.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-GBY0-003D-J1Y0-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Woolley v. Embassy Suites, Inc., 278 Cal.Rptr. 719 (Cal. App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-HDX0-003D-J170-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Government Guarantee Fund v. Hyatt Corp., 5 F.Supp.2d 324, 326 (D.V.I.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SR3-BH00-0038-Y4C4-00000-00&context=1530671) (hotel manager refused to vacate premises following owner's revocation; manager had burden to make accounting to show that it managed and handled funds properly, for which manager's existing accounting system was inadequate); 2660 Woodley Rd. Joint Venture v. ITT Sheraton Corp., 1998 WL 1469541 (D.Del.1998) (granting hotel owner's motion for preliminary injunction terminating manager's operation of hotel when owner had revoked authority; owner made sufficient showing of irreparable harm on basis of argument that manager could no longer be trusted to act in owner's best interests and was in position to divert business to other hotels it managed).

_c. Manifestation of revocation or renunciation._ For the proposition that a manifestation of dissent may be made implicitly, through conduct inconsistent with continued consent, see [_Security Servs., Inc. v. K Mart Corp., 996 F.2d 1516, 1523 (3d Cir. 1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-FNX0-003B-P3D7-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_511 U.S. 431 (1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S65-JWF0-003B-R1BX-00000-00&context=1530671) (carrier as principal participated in distance tariff filed by carriers' bureau to govern component determining rate to be charged per mile; bureau renounced authority vested in it by carrier by canceling carrier's participation in tariff; carrier revoked bureau's authority by failing to pay required fee to bureau when bureau's practice was to terminate service following reasonable period of time after nonpayment of fee).

For the point that the occurrence of notice of revocation is a question of fact, see [_Gornicki v. M & T Bank, 617 N.Y.S.2d 448, 450 (Sup.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S2R-6NJ0-003V-B32V-00000-00&context=1530671) (factual questions precluded grant of summary judgment; plaintiff executed written revocation of power of attorney 1 hour before agent entered same branch of bank and cashed check on plaintiffs account payable to himself).

On the effectiveness of a revocation that is not in writing, see [_Coldwell Banker Residential Real Estate Servs., Inc. v. Jepsen, 527 N.E.2d 79, 82 (Ill.App.1988)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-55N0-003D-H0S7-00000-00&context=1530671) (dictum; owner's statement to broker that owner wished to cancel listing would have revoked broker's authority); [_Dave Zerwas Co. v. James Hamilton Constr. Co., 876 P.2d 653, 655 (N.M. 1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W1V0-003D-D1MH-00000-00&context=1530671) (although exclusive listing contract for real estate was within Statute of Frauds, broker consented to cancellation through conduct, including failing to object when competing broker removed listing broker's sign from property and undertook to market property). For authority contra, see [_Givens v. Dougherty, 671 S.W.2d 877 (Tex.1984)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-WDR0-003C-22RS-00000-00&context=1530671) (holding that in general, oral rescission is ineffective to revoke a written listing agreement; oral rescission may be effective in limited circumstances, such as to extend time under agreement). See also Restatement Second, Contracts § 148 (notwithstanding Statute of Frauds, unperformed duties under enforceable contract may be discharged by oral agreement to rescind, but Statute may apply to contract to rescind transfer of property). On expansions in authority, see [_Estate of Swanson v. United States, 46 Fed.Cl. 388, 392 (2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YV5-DYC0-0004-S046-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_10 Fed.Appx. 833 (Fed. Cir. 2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:436D-X010-003B-92GT-00000-00&context=1530671) (durable power created pursuant to California statute did not expressly authorize agent to make gifts; competent principal's subsequent oral manifestation of assent to particular gifts, made prior to gifts, did not expand agent's authority because not in writing, requisite because statute required original power to be in writing to be valid and effective).

_d. Consequences of termination._ For the proposition that revocation of authority does not extinguish an agent's right to already-earned commissions, see [_Rellinger v. Bremmeyr, 448 N.W.2d 49, 51 (Mich.App.1989)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-CWJ0-003D-63RB-00000-00&context=1530671) (dictum; agent had no claim to commissions, having not promised to make any specific efforts to sell defendants' property, or otherwise furnished consideration; no showing revocation motivated by desire to defeat plaintiff's claim to commission as opposed to selling property to purchaser who was more creditworthy and who could close purchase more expeditiously).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 3.11_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-4025-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 3- Creation and Termination of Authority and Agency Relationships_ > _Topic 4- Termination of Agent's Power_ > _Title B- Termination of Apparent Authority_**

**§ 3.11 Termination of Apparent Authority**

**(1) The termination of actual authority does not by itself end any apparent authority held by an agent.**

**(2) Apparent authority ends when it is no longer reasonable for the third party with whom an agent deals to believe that the agent continues to act with actual authority.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Cross-references._ Section 2.03 defines apparent authority. A principal creates apparent authority as stated in § 3.03. Actual authority terminates as stated in §§ 3.06-3.10.

_b. Principal's death or loss of capacity._ A principal's death or loss of capacity does not by itself or automatically end the agent's apparent authority. This is contrary to the position taken in Restatement Second, Agency § 120, Comment c. Change is warranted for several reasons, including the nature of apparent authority and various indications of policy contrary to the position taken in § 120.

An agent may act with apparent authority following the principal's death or loss of capacity because the basis of apparent authority is a principal's manifestation to third parties, coupled with a third party's reasonable belief that the agent acts with actual authority. See §§ 2.03 and 3.03. Neither element requires that the principal consent or manifest assent at the time the agent takes action. When third parties do not have notice that the principal has died or lost capacity, they may reasonably believe the agent to be authorized.

Moreover, the rule that the principal's death terminates apparent authority is inconsistent with the effect of contemporary legislation, which in many respects is protective of third parties who act without knowledge of the principal's death or loss of capacity. Over half of the states have adopted statutes based on § 4 of the Uniform Durable Power of Attorney Act, providing that the death, disability, or incapacity of a principal who has previously executed any power of attorney, whether or not it is a durable power, does not terminate the agency as to the holder of the power or other person who deals in good faith without actual knowledge of the principal's death or incapacity. See § 3.07, Comment _d,_ and § 3.08, Comment _b._ As to third parties who deal with an agent without notice of the principal's death or incapacity, these statutes reflect a policy broad enough to encompass situations in which the principal has made a manifestation to third parties as to the agent's authority, but has not executed a written power of attorney. The executed power constitutes a manifestation of the principal's assent, made to the agent and to third parties as well when the power is displayed to them.

Few decided cases support the traditional rule as to apparent authority, and a well-reasoned recent case rejects the doctrine as illogical. The consequence of the rule stated in this section is that the principal or the principal's estate is bound by transactions of an agent acting with apparent authority as defined in § 2.03. The few decided cases holding otherwise should not be followed.

_c. Lingering apparent authority._ An agent's apparent authority may survive or linger after the termination of actual authority because apparent authority is present when a third party reasonably believes that the agent is authorized to take action and the belief is traceable to a manifestation made by the principal. See § 2.03. Once such a manifestation has been made, it remains operative independent of manifestations as between principal and agent that terminate actual authority, see § 3.10, or the occurrence of circumstances that otherwise operate to terminate actual authority, including the terms of any agreement between principal and agent, see § 3.09, the death of the principal, see § 3.07, and the principal's loss of capacity, see § 3.08. Apparent authority protects third parties who interact with an agent on the basis of the principal's prior manifestation and who lack notice that the agent's actual authority has terminated.

Often termed "lingering authority," the doctrine stated in this section recognizes that it is reasonable for third parties to assume that an agent's actual authority is a continuing or ongoing condition, unless and until a third party has notice of circumstances that make it unreasonable so to assume. These circumstances include notice that the principal has revoked the agent's actual authority, that the agent has renounced it, that the agent's authority was limited in duration or to a specific undertaking, or that circumstances otherwise have changed such that it is no longer reasonable to believe that the principal consents to the agent's act on the principal's behalf.

**Illustrations:**

1\. P Corporation, in the recycling business, retains A as a purchasing agent to buy recyclable material on its behalf. A is authorized by P Corporation to buy on terms that commit P Corporation to pay for the material when it arrives at P Corporation's recycling facility. A has purchased recyclables many times from T, who is in the business of building demolition. P Corporation terminates A's actual authority. T has no notice of the termination. As to T, A continues for a reasonable period of time to possess apparent authority to purchase from T on terms comparable to those on which A has made prior purchases on P Corporation's behalf.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that the local newspaper publishes a story about A's termination on its front page. As to T, A's apparent authority has ended.

3\. P Corporation, a manufacturer of semiconductors, retains A as a sales representative. A has often sold semiconductors manufactured by P Corporation to T. P Corporation is headquartered in the nation of B, which adopts a regulation prohibiting the export of semiconductors into the nation of C, where T is located. T is aware of the regulation. In all of T's prior dealings with P Corporation, including those through A, P Corporation has meticulously complied with legal regulations, including prior prohibitions on export. Following B's adoption of the export ban and while it is in effect, A does not have apparent authority to bind P Corporation to a contract to sell semiconductors to T.

4\. Same facts as Illustration 3, except that the senior officer of P Corporation tells A to disregard the export ban but, when asked, to deny that such an instruction has been given. Although A lacks apparent authority as to T, A has actual authority to bind P Corporation to a contract with T.

Circumstances that create lingering apparent authority may overlap with circumstances in which the principal disputes whether at any time the agent acted with actual authority. Such a dispute is irrelevant to whether the agent has acted with apparent authority.

**Illustration:**

5\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that when P Corporation retained A, A was instructed not to buy more than \$ 5000 in recyclable material from the same vendor without the prior approval of P Corporation's Vice President for Recyclable Operations. Ignoring this instruction, A often bought more than \$ 5000 in recyclables from T, and P Corporation paid T with no notice to T that the purchases exceeded any limit P Corporation imposed on A. If, as in Illustration 1, P Corporation terminates A's actual authority and T has no notice of the termination, A continues to have apparent authority to purchase on terms comparable to those on which A has made prior purchases from T on P Corporation's behalf.

Lingering apparent authority may be present when it is the agent who has terminated actual authority by making a manifestation to the principal that renounces the agent's actual authority. The agent's renunciation of actual authority does not defeat the presence of lingering apparent authority as to a third party who reasonably believes the agent acts with actual authority and who has no notice that the agent has renounced actual authority.

_d. Indicia of authority._ A principal may furnish an agent with a writing, such as a power of attorney, that states the extent and nature of the agent's actual authority. Providing the agent with such a writing enables third parties to deal with the agent as to matters within the scope of the stated authority without confirming the agent's authority directly with the principal. When actual authority is terminated, the agent has a duty to return indicia of authority to the principal. See § 8.05, Comment _b._ If the agent does not return the indicia to the principal, the principal bears the risk that the agent will use them to deceive third parties who do not have notice that the agent's actual authority has been terminated.

**Illustrations:**

6\. P gives A a written power of attorney that authorizes A to sell Blackacre upon terms stated in the power. Later, P tells A that A's authority is revoked but P does not procure the surrender of the power of attorney from A. The following day, A exhibits the power of attorney to T, who has no notice of the revocation of A's authority. A makes a contract to sell Blackacre in accordance with the terms stated in the power. A has apparent authority as to T.

7\. P Corporation, an insurance company, appoints A its agent in city B and authorizes A to issue payment and performance surety bonds. P Corporation furnishes A with a general power of attorney, blank policy-application forms bearing the logo "P Corporation," and corporate seals for P Corporation. P Corporation revokes A's authority but does not secure the return of the policy forms, the power of attorney, and the seals. A issues a performance bond to T, displaying P Corporation's power of attorney and affixing P Corporation's seal. T does not have notice that P Corporation has revoked A's actual authority. A has apparent authority as to T.

Not everything that a principal gives an agent constitutes an indicium of authority. The question is whether a reasonable person would understand the item in the agent's possession to express the principal's assent to be bound by actions taken by the agent. It is necessary that the item reflect authority to bind the principal. Thus, the fact that a former agent retains a customer list provided by the principal and uses it to sell on another principal's account does not create apparent authority as to the former principal. In Illustrations 6 and 7, the principal's power of attorney constitutes a written manifestation of the principal's assent. In contrast, the agent's possession of blank application forms in Illustration 7 may contribute to a third party's impression that the agent has authority to bind the principal but, when completed, the application forms by themselves are insufficient to bind the principal to a contract with the applicant. Thus, the fact that an agent possesses only the application forms does not constitute a manifestation by the principal to third parties expressing the principal's assent to be bound by the agent. Similarly, an agent's possession of the principal's letterhead stationery does not in itself constitute such a manifestation, but may constitute evidence relevant to showing apparent authority.

_e. Notice of termination of authority._ Apparent authority is not present when a reasonable person in the position of a third party would not believe that the principal consents to the agent's or other actor's conduct. If a third party has notice of facts that call the agent's authority into question, and these facts would prompt a reasonable person to make inquiry of the principal before dealing with the agent, the agent does not act with apparent authority. See § 2.03, Comment _d._ This general principle is applicable to determining whether and when an agent acts with the lingering appearance of authority after the agent's actual authority has terminated. Lingering authority does not survive a statement that the agent's authority has terminated, made by the principal to the third party with whom the now-former agent deals. The principal's statement is effective even though the agent succeeds in persuading the third party to disregard it.

**Illustration:**

8\. P Corporation, a manufacturer, retains A as its exclusive sales agent for a territory that includes T. P Corporation so notifies T. Later, P Corporation terminates A's authority. P Corporation sends a letter to T's headquarters informing T that A is no longer authorized. The letter arrives at T's headquarters at 9:00 a.m. A's apparent authority ends as soon as T or T's office force has an opportunity to read and act upon the mail.

This general doctrine is subject to modification by statute. Under § 704 of the Revised Uniform Partnership Act, after a partner dissociates the partnership may make a public filing of a statement of dissociation. Persons not partners are deemed to have notice of the dissociation 90 days after the statement is filed. If the partnership does not file a statement of dissociation, for two years after the dissociation under § 702(a) the partnership is bound by acts of the dissociated partner that would have bound the partnership prior to dissociation when the other party reasonably believed the dissociated partner continued to be a partner and lacked notice of the dissociation.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency._ This section covers the material included in Restatement Second, Agency §§ 124A-133. However, this section does not include the rule stated in § 125(c), which provides that apparent authority terminates when the third person has notice of "facts, the failure to reveal which, were the transaction with the principal in person, would be ground for rescission by the principal." Such a fact would include the third party's knowledge, not also known to the principal or the agent, that the principal has authorized the agent to sell goods based on a fundamental mistake as to the nature of the goods that would result in a sale at a grossly low price. See Restatement Second, Agency § 125, Illustration 9. This is not a problem for which the doctrine of apparent authority, or its termination, is analytically useful. On the impact of unilateral mistake on contract formation, see Restatement Second, Contracts § 153.

The position taken in Comment _b,_ that a principal's death or loss of capacity does not automatically terminate the agent's apparent authority, is contrary to the position taken in Restatement Second, Agency § 120, Comment c.

Restatement Second, Agency § 120 includes a Caveat stating that "\[n\]o inference is to be drawn from the rule stated in this Section that an agent does not have power to bind the estate of a deceased principal in transactions dependent upon a special relation between the agent and the principal … or in transactions in which special rules are applicable, as in dealings with negotiable instruments." The first Restatement stated in § 120 that "\[t\]he death of the principal terminates the authority of the agent." The Comment included the Caveat but made no reference to apparent authority.

_b. Principal's death or loss of capacity._ The proposition that the agent may have apparent authority following the principal's death is supported by [_Schock v. United States, 56 F.Supp.2d 185, 193 (D.R.I.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3X2G-MSV0-0038-Y4T9-00000-00&context=1530671) (criticizing contrary position in Restatement Second, Agency § 120 as "illogical," and holding that under Rhode Island law, death of holder of bank account did not automatically terminate apparent authority of agent to withdraw funds from account) and by Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 31(3), Comment _i._ See also [_Iovino v. Waterson, 274 F.2d 41, 51 (2d Cir.1959)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-SJG0-003B-00TF-00000-00&context=1530671) (although recognizing general proposition that client's death revokes a lawyer's actual authority, court states "an attorney … appearing \[in courts of this Circuit\] must assure himself of the continuing existence of his client before he takes affirmative steps in the client's behalf; … in the absence of knowledge to the contrary, his adversary may properly assume that he has done this; … and … no advantage may be taken by the client's personal representative of the attorney's failure to ascertain the fact of the client's death."). For contrary authority, see _Gallup v. Barton, 47 N.E.2d 921 (Mass.1943)_ (death of purchaser of cashier's check terminated actual and apparent authority of payee of check to distribute proceeds in accordance with purchaser's instructions, although payee lacked notice of purchaser's death); [_In re Estate of Kelly, 547 A.2d 284, 287 (N.H.1988)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-4V90-003G-B1T9-00000-00&context=1530671) (actual and apparent authority of executrix's attorney to settle will contest terminated when executrix died prior to occurrence of condition precedent in settlement agreement).

_c. Lingering apparent authority._ The classic statement of the rationale is 1 Floyd R. Mechem, A Treatise on the Law of Agency § 628 (2d ed. 1914): "Where a general authority is once shown to have existed, it may be presumed to continue until it is shown to have been revoked, and persons who have dealt with the agent as such, or who have had knowledge of his authority and are therefore likely to deal with him, may very properly expect that if the authority be withdrawn, reasonable and timely notice of that fact will be given and they may lawfully presume, in the absence of such notice, that the authority still continues."

For applications of the general doctrine; see, e.g., [_Hall-Brooke Found., Inc. v. City of Norwalk, 752 A.2d 523, 527-528 (Conn.App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40J7-WK90-0039-44YN-00000-00&context=1530671) (substance-abuse hospital could reasonably assume that city social workers remained authorized, as in past, to refer clients for treatment when hospital had no knowledge of city's internal policy that discontinued referrals to such providers as hospital); [_Harrah v. Home Furniture, Inc., 214 P.2d 1016 (Nev.1950)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-YMM0-000H-F167-00000-00&context=1530671) (husband, who had credit account with furniture store, was liable for large purchases charged to account by his wife on the day the couple separated; husband had previously paid for two smaller purchases made by wife and store had no reason to know that wife no longer had authority to make purchases on husband's account); [_Montana Reservoir & Irrigation Co. v. Utah Junk Co., 228 P. 201 (Utah 1924)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WKX-M3Y0-00KR-D41N-00000-00&context=1530671) (officers of corporation's subsidiary, who previously dealt with purchasing agent of junk company while they were officers of parent corporation and who lacked notice that agent's authority had been terminated, could assume that agent continued to be authorized); Petron Tankers v. Gotco, Ltd. (The GULF PACIFIC), Soc'y of Mar. Arbitrator Award Decisions, No. 3036, 1993 US Awds LEXIS (final award on liability) (managing director of shipping joint venture appeared to continue to have authority to exercise options to extend charter, despite owner's sale of interest in venture).

For an illustration of overlapping circumstances involving lingering apparent authority as well as disputed actual authority at any time, see [_Federal Land Bank of Omaha v. Union Bank & Trust Co. of Ottumwa, 290 N.W. 512 (Iowa 1940)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YTH-X8C0-00KR-F08R-00000-00&context=1530671) (plaintiffs attorney endorsed checks made payable to plaintiff, deposited check into attorney's account with defendant, and remitted proceeds to plaintiff by personal check, a practice in which plaintiff acquiesced; attorney acted with apparent authority as to defendant in endorsing and depositing check after plaintiff, with no notice to defendant, insisted that attorney cease practice).

On lingering apparent authority that may follow an agent's renunciation of actual authority, see [_Panzino v. City of Phoenix, 999 P.2d 198, 202 (Ariz.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4090-3XK0-0039-41XX-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent's "abandonment" of principal does not affect agent's ability to bind principal to a third party, absent notice to the third party; client bound by lawyer's actions in filing 2 suits but not actively pursuing them despite lawyer's abandonment of client, leaving client with claim against lawyer but no relief from judgments dismissing her claims; court declines to adopt "positive misconduct rule," which permits court to relieve client of consequences of lawyer's conduct when the lawyer's conduct is so egregious that it amounts to abandonment of client). The positive-misconduct rule, followed by a few courts, appears to have originated with [_Daley v. County of Butte, 38 Cal.Rptr. 693 (Cal.App.1964)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-B5F0-003C-J1XM-00000-00&context=1530671). See also Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 27, Comment _f_ (noting that when client is bound by act of lawyer with apparent but not actual authority, lawyer is liable to client for resulting damage, and may be subject to professional discipline); id. § 29(2) (in deciding whether to relieve a person from a criminal or civil ruling, default, or judgment, tribunal may consider otherwise admissible evidence to prove or disprove that lawyer represented client inadequately or contrary to client's instructions).

_d. Indicia of authority._ For the point that a power of attorney enables a third party to deal with an agent without seeking independent verification from the principal, see [_Heine v. Newman, Tannenbaum, Helpern, Syracuse & Hirschtritt, 856 F.Supp. 190, 195 (S.D.N.Y.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-GKH0-003B-V373-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _50 F.3d 2 (2d Cir.1995)_.

Illustration 7 and the discussion of blank application forms are based on [_Herbert Constr. Co. v. Continental Ins. Co., 931 F.2d 989, 995 (2d Cir. 1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-DN40-008H-V14S-00000-00&context=1530671).

On a former agent's retention of customer lists, see [_Herbert v. Banc One Brokerage Corp., 638 N.E.2d 161, 166 (Ohio App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-YP10-003C-80TJ-00000-00&context=1530671) (sales person retained customer list following termination of employment by brokerage firm and subsequently used it on behalf of associate who had scheme to sell worthless unregistered securities; brokerage firm not liable for sales person's postemployment activity).

For the proposition that possession of letterhead stationery does not in itself create an appearance of authority, see [_Drake v. Maid-Rite Co., 681 N.E.2d 734, 738 (Ind.App.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-50S0-003F-X062-00000-00&context=1530671).

_e. Notice of termination of authority._ For the proposition that it is the reasonableness of the third party's belief that is determinative, see [_Herbert Constr. Co., 931 F.2d at 996-997_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-DN40-008H-V14S-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurer's failure to give notice to client that agent's authority had been terminated does not in itself establish presence of apparent authority if client did not reasonably believe agent to be authorized). An alternative formulation of the same point is whether circumstances would place a reasonable person in the third party's position on notice to make inquiry concerning the agent's authority. See [_id. at 996_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-DN40-008H-V14S-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Bernstein v. Centaur Ins. Co., 644 F.Supp. 1361, 1369 (S.D.N.Y.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-8G70-0039-R1CV-00000-00&context=1530671). A principal's duty is said to be one to take "appropriate steps" to destroy the lingering appearance of authority. See [_Johnson v. Nationwide Gen. Ins. Co., 971 F.Supp. 725, 731-732 (N.D.N.Y.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-GMX0-00B1-F0WH-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _162 F.3d 1148 (2d Cir.1998)_ (agent terminated by insurance company after discovery of fraud perpetrated against clients continued to act with apparent authority when insurer did not contact clients or otherwise bring fact of termination to their attention). In _Johnson,_ there was no showing that the plaintiff had notice of the termination from sources other than the insurer itself. An alternative formulation is that the principal must have given notice in a manner that "brought home" to the third party the termination of the agent's actual authority. See [_Harrah v. Home Furniture, Inc., 214 P.2d 1016, 1019 (Nev.1950)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-YMM0-000H-F167-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Sherman, Taff & Bangert, P.C. v. Clark Equip. Co., 133 S.W.3d 125, 127 (Mo. App.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4BV4-FWH0-0039-417W-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporation had duty to notify outside counsel of spinoff transaction that terminated authority of associate general counsel to retain outside counsel on behalf of corporation; although outside counsel noticed that associate general counsel's letterhead changed to name of spunoff business unit, lawyer was unaware of reorganization and nothing else appeared to change in relationship). Notice that an agent's employment has terminated constitutes notice of termination of the agent's authority to make statements on the principal's behalf in Alan, Sean & Koule, Inc. v. S/V "Corsta V," [_286 F.Supp.2d 1367, 1374 (S.D.Ga.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:49WH-18X0-0038-Y4J5-00000-00&context=1530671) (shipowner bound only by statements of former ship captains made before ship repairer had notice of termination of captains' employment; repairer's motion for summary judgment denied because record unclear when captain told repairer he would be paid in full for all work, which captain said had been done in workmanlike fashion).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 3.12_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-4026-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 3- Creation and Termination of Authority and Agency Relationships_ > _Topic 4- Termination of Agent's Power_ > _Title C- Irrevocable Powers_**

**§ 3.12 Power Given as Security; Irrevocable Proxy**

**(1) A power given as security is a power to affect the legal relations of its creator that is created in the form of a manifestation of actual authority and held for the benefit of the holder or a third person. This power is given to protect a legal or equitable title or to secure the performance of a duty apart from any duties owed the holder of the power by its creator that are incident to a relationship of agency under § 1.01. It is given upon the creation of the duty or title or for consideration. It is distinct from actual authority that the holder may exercise if the holder is an agent of the creator of the power.**

**(2) A power to exercise voting rights associated with securities or a membership interest may be conferred on a proxy through a manifestation of actual authority. The power may be given as security under (1) and may be made irrevocable in compliance with applicable legislation.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Cross-references._ Comment _c_ discusses the doctrine of power coupled with an interest. A power given as security or an irrevocable proxy terminates as stated in § 3.13. A durable power of attorney does not constitute a power given as security. See § 3.08, Comment c. Section 2.01 defines actual authority.

_b. Distinguished from agency and actual authority._ A power given as security creates neither a relationship of agency as defined in § 1.01 nor actual authority as defined in § 2.01, although the power enables its holder to affect the legal relations of the creator of the power. The power arises from a manifestation of assent by its creator that the holder of the power may properly create liability against the creator, or dispose of property or other interests of the creator, or perfect or otherwise protect a title already held by the holder of the power or the person for whose benefit the holder is to act. If the power is given as security for the performance of a duty, it must be supported by consideration, but consideration is not necessary if the power is given to facilitate transfers of title to the power holder.

The rights created by a power given as security, or by an irrevocable proxy, entitle the holder to take specific actions. If the creator of a validly created power given as security purports to revoke the holder's authority contrary to the agreement pursuant to which the creator granted the power, specific enforcement of the holder's rights is an appropriate remedy, subject to the court's discretion in granting an equitable remedy. Likewise, specific enforcement may be warranted to protect the rights of the holder of a validly created irrevocable proxy. In both cases, it will often be difficult or impossible for the holder to prove quantifiable damages or to obtain a substitute performance. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 360, Comments _b_ and c.

A power given as security does not create a relationship of agency as defined in § 1.01 because it is neither given for, nor exercised for, the benefit of the person who creates it. The holder is not subject to the creator's control and the holder does not owe fiduciary duties to the creator. An agent's right to act, created by actual authority, does not constitute a power given as security although the agent has an interest in earning a commission by performing services and the principal has contracted with the agent not to revoke the agent's actual authority. An agent's interest in being paid a commission is an ordinary incident of agency and its presence does not convert the agent's authority into a power held for the agent's benefit. In contrast, cases interpreting statutes applicable to irrevocable proxies hold that a proxy holder's interest in an employment agreement with the corporation suffices to support irrevocability. See Comment _d._ However, if a power holder has a distinct interest in the subject matter of an agency relationship, separate from acting as an agent, a power given to protect that interest is a power given as security, as is a power given to protect a distinct relationship, separate from the agency relationship, between the agent and the principal. A power may be granted irrevocably for the benefit of its creator as well as the holder.

A power given as security may also be created and held for the benefit of a third party, other than the holder of the power. The creator of the power and the holder have the ability to create an enforceable right in a third party to benefit from the power, just as two parties to a contract have the ability to create a right in a third-party beneficiary. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 304, Comment _b._

**Illustrations:**

1\. P owns Blackacre, which is situated next to Whiteacre, on which P operates a restaurant. To finance renovations and expansions, P borrows money from A. A written agreement between P and A provides that A shall irrevocably have P's authority to transfer ownership of Blackacre to A in the event P defaults on the loan. A has a power given as security.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that A is a corporation and the agreement with P provides that M, an officer of A, shall have P's authorization to transfer ownership of Blackacre. M has a power given as security. The power was given by P for the benefit of A.

3\. P owns a resort hotel. P engages A to manage the hotel for a term of 10 years, in an agreement that expressly provides that P may not revoke A's authority except pursuant to mutual agreement. The agreement states that P's promise not to revoke A's authority constitutes security to A for A's interest in receiving the management fee specified in the agreement, which is three percent of gross revenues for the first five years, and five percent for the second five years. A does not have a power given as security. If P revokes A's authority, A will not have a specifically enforceable right to continue to manage the hotel. A may claim that P's revocation is a breach of contract and seek money damages from P.

4\. P develops a mechanical invention and engages A to patent the invention and arrange for its commercial manufacture. P authorizes A to file patent documents on P's behalf and to enter into contracts with suitable manufacturers. P agrees to pay A's expenses and agrees to pay A half of P's profits. Even if A's authority is stated to be irrevocable, A does not hold a power given as security.

5\. Same facts as Illustration 4, except that P grants A a one-half ownership interest in the invention and P's rights in it, and A agrees to cover the expenses of A's efforts. It may be found that A has a power given as security. A will exercise the power for A's benefit as well as P's.

6\. Same facts as Illustration 5, except that P, at A's request, grants the one-half ownership interest to C, to whom A is indebted. It may be found that A has a power given as security, to be exercised for the benefit of C.

_c. Power coupled with an interest._ Some jurisdictions follow a test narrower than that stated in this section, under which it is necessary that a power holder possess a proprietary interest in the "subject matter of the agency itself." This test also requires that the power and the proprietary interest be united in the same person. The narrower test has a distinguished lineage, beginning with Chief Justice Marshall's opinion in [_Hunt v. Rousmanier's Administrators, 21 U.S. (8 Wheat.) 174 (1823)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-KPM0-003B-H095-00000-00&context=1530671). Rousmanier had given Hunt a power of attorney to sell a brig and a schooner owned by Rousmanier, to be exercisable if Rousmanier defaulted in repaying loans he owed to Hunt. After Rousmanier died insolvent, following his default on the loans, the court held that the power was not enforceable. The court held that the power created only a revocable agency, which perished with Rousmanier's death, as opposed to "a power coupled with an interest" that survives its creator's death and may be exercised after it. [_21 U.S. at 203_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-KPM0-003B-H095-00000-00&context=1530671). To create such a power, the holder must be vested with an interest or estate that accompanies the power. The focus of the court's opinion is the demise of Rousmanier's legal personality with his death, not the commercial function of the power, which the parties intended as a substitute for a mortgage on the ships that might not have been enforceable.

The difference between the test for irrevocability derived from _Hunt,_ and that stated in this section, has practical consequences in two situations. First, under the "power coupled with an interest" approach, the power P granted to A in Illustration 1 is revocable because it does not accompany any proprietary interest of A in Blackacre itself. More generally, if a power must be coupled with a property interest to be made irrevocable, granting a power of sale as to property owned by the debtor, in which a creditor has no proprietary interest, will not effectively protect the creditor's interests. Unsurprisingly, the law applicable to loan transactions has itself evolved to overcome this obstacle. For example, a mortgage lender by statute has the right in every jurisdiction to cause the sale of the mortgaged property through judicial foreclosure. Additionally, in about 60 percent of the states, statutes authorize a mortgagee to sell pursuant to a power of sale, created by the mortgagor by conveying the property to a trustee who holds the property and the power to sell it as a fiduciary for the benefit of the mortgagee-beneficiary. The trustee exercises the power of sale. Separately, in secured financing under U.C.C. Article 9, it is irrelevant whether a lender obtains title to the collateral. A secured lender's rights concerning collateral, including the right to dispose of it after the borrower's default, are specified and regulated by the Code.

Second, the test derived from _Hunt_ requires that the same person hold both the interest and the power. As a consequence, it does not recognize the irrevocability of a power created in one person when the creator transfers the requisite proprietary interest to another person, even if the holder and the transferor are closely related, such as affiliated corporations in the same group. Illustration 6 presents such a situation. The rule stated in this section permits an irrevocable power to be held for the benefit of a third party, which implies that the power holder and the interest-holder may be distinct persons.

Distinguished lineage aside, the quest for an interest to which a power has been coupled is not a useful exercise when it is clear that the power has been created for the benefit of a person other than the creator, as in _Hunt_ itself. It is unnecessary to impose further limits on the creator's range of choices.

_d. Irrevocable proxies._ A right to vote associated with securities may be delegated by the owner to an agent. Likewise, many organizational statutes contemplate that members of not-for-profit corporations may delegate voting rights to agents, as may members of limited-liability companies (LLCs). In such statutes, the treatment of voting by proxy is less fully developed than in business-corporation statutes.

Designating a proxy to vote creates a relationship of agency as defined in § 1.01 unless the proxy is irrevocable. Thus, the principal may revoke a proxy holder's actual authority. The principal lacks power to revoke the proxy holder's authority to vote when such authority is expressly made irrevocable and the holder receives the proxy as a power given as security as defined in § 3.12(1), or the proxy is supported by an interest that suffices to make it irrevocable under the applicable organizational statute. Revocation requires a manifestation from the principal, which may be made by conduct from which dissent to the proxy holder's authority may be inferred. See Comment _c._ The death of the proxy holder revokes a proxy that has expressly been made irrevocable only when the proxy was given to support an interest that terminates with the holder's death. See § 3.13.

An irrevocable proxy separates voting power from stock ownership; even when the proxy holder owns stock, the ability to vote stock owned by another augments the proxy holder's voting power. An irrevocable proxy thereby distorts the allocation of voting power to or among a corporation's residual economic claimants, who own its stock. The proxy holder's interests may not always be aligned with those of stockholders. For example, if the holder owns no stock but has an interest in employment by the corporation or an interest in commercial transactions with it, the proxy holder's interests may diverge from the stockholders' interests.

This potential for divergence in interests explains why, in many cases, courts refused to enforce irrevocable proxies. Courts enforced irrevocable proxies only when the holder had a proprietary interest in the shares themselves, as would a pledgee who has lent money on the security of the shares to their owner. Even when coupled with such an interest, an irrevocable proxy always operates with regard to two subjects: first, the shares; and, as a secondary subject, the corporation. The holder's vote affects the interests, but not the legal relations, of all of the corporation's shareholders. The spillover effect from the principal to the corporation and its other shareholders may also explain why irrevocable proxies attracted skepticism. An irrevocable proxy affects the interests of all shareholders because, if the holder has majority control, the holder ordinarily has power to choose the corporation's directors and holds veto power over fundamental transactions that require a shareholder vote.

Judicial skepticism lessened in some jurisdictions. Irrevocability was a practical necessity to combinations among shareholders because it enabled the coalescence of voting power in a predictable and enforceable manner. Only specific enforcement of the proxy by its terms, and not money damages, can provide control over voting. In response, some courts recast the terms of analysis to examine the grantor's motive or objective in granting the proxy. Such cases focus on whether the grantor had the pursuit of purely personal gain as an objective, as opposed to the good of all shareholders. This test has proven difficult to state or apply with precision. Business-corporation statutes also evolved to permit stockholders to form voting trusts, or to enter into specifically enforceable voting agreements, both devices that separate control over voting from stock ownership.

Most business-corporation statutes now address the question of irrevocability, albeit against the background sketched above. The statutes, and recent cases interpreting them, broaden the circumstances in which a proxy may be made irrevocable well beyond those applicable more generally to powers given as security, defined in § 3.12(1). Most business-corporation statutes provide that a proxy shall be irrevocable if it expressly so states and if the proxy is "coupled with an interest." The statutes vary in the extent to which they specify what shall suffice as an interest and in whether the statute makes the specification exclusive. Many statutes specify a nonexclusive list of relationships that suffice as an interest. The typical statement is that the appointment of a proxy is coupled with an interest when the appointee is a pledgee, a person who has purchased or agreed to purchase the shares, a creditor who extended credit to the corporation under terms requiring the appointment, an employee whose employment contract with the corporation requires the appointment, or a party to a voting agreement created pursuant to the statute. In some statutes, including those of New York and California, the list of specified interests is exclusive. Several statutes, including those of Delaware and Massachusetts, use broader language without specifying particular interests. Under the Delaware statute, "\[a\] duly executed proxy shall be irrevocable if it states that it is irrevocable and if, and only as long as, it is coupled with an interest sufficient in law to support an irrevocable power. A proxy may be made irrevocable regardless of whether the interest with which it is coupled is an interest in the stock itself or an interest in the corporation generally." Del. Code Ann., tit. 8, § 212(e) (2005).

Recent Delaware authority holds that an interest in employment constitutes "an interest in the corporation generally." This possibility is present as well under statutes that define interest to include an employment contract that requires the irrevocable proxy. So treating an interest in employment is at odds with the cases defining the more general doctrine of power given as security, stated in § 3.12(1). An agent's interest in receiving commissions or other compensation is insufficient to support such a power because it is an ordinary incident of the agency itself.

Competing policies apply to the treatment of interests in employment. The policy factors are especially pertinent when the relevant organizational statute authorizes voting by proxy but does not specify when a proxy may be made irrevocable. For example, some LLC statutes, like some not-for-profit corporation statutes, authorize voting by proxy but are silent on irrevocability. A proxy holder's self-interest in employment could lead to the election of directors, or to votes on fundamental transactions, that disserve the interests of most shareholders or members. This risk, however, may not warrant a stricter rule or at least not a categorically stricter rule. The risk will not materialize unless the holder's vote controls the voting outcome. Even when a proxy holder has effective control, other legal doctrines regulate how that control may be used. For example, corporate law regulates the use of voting control through doctrines specifically applicable to shareholders who use control to benefit themselves at the expense of other shareholders. See Principles of Corporate Governance: Analysis and Recommendations §§ 5.10-5.14. Moreover, a corporation's directors, regardless of how or by whom elected, owe fiduciary duties to the entire corporation, which encompasses the interests of its noncontrolling shareholders. See id. §§ 5.02-5.09. The terms of the employment contract between a proxy holder and the organization may also mitigate the risk, for example by tying the holder's compensation to performance-related criteria, and thereby aligning the holder's economic interests more closely to those of stockholders or members.

In general, irrevocable proxies to vote sufficient shares to constitute control are much more likely in closely held corporations or other business organizations than in publicly held corporations. In closely held enterprises, it is also more likely that most members will assent to, or at least be aware of, the circumstances that led to the proxy holder's irrevocable appointment.
