[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 3.15_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-4029-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 3- Creation and Termination of Authority and Agency Relationships_ > _Topic 5- Agents with Multiple Principals_**

**§ 3.15 Subagency**

**(1) A subagent is a person appointed by an agent to perform functions that the agent has consented to perform on behalf of the agent's principal and for whose conduct the appointing agent is responsible to the principal. The relationships between a subagent and the appointing agent and between the subagent and the appointing agent's principal are relationships of agency as stated in § 1.01.**

**(2) An agent may appoint a subagent only if the agent has actual or apparent authority to do so.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ Comment _b_ contrasts subagency with coagency. Comment _c_ discusses how relationships of subagency are created. Comment _d_ summarizes their legal consequences. Comment _e_ discusses termination of actual or apparent authority held by a subagent. Comment _f_ discusses subagency and other forms of agency relationships in the context of transactions in real estate.

_b. Subagency contrasted with coagency._ Agency creates a personal relationship between principal and agent; an agent's delegation of power to another person to act on behalf of the principal is inconsistent with the undertaking made when a person consents to act as agent on behalf of a principal. However, a principal may empower an agent to appoint another agent to act on the principal's behalf. The second agent may be a subagent or a coagent. See Comment _d_ for discussion of the consequences that follow if the second agent is characterized as a subagent.

An agent who appoints a subagent delegates to the subagent power to act on behalf of the principal that the principal has conferred on the agent. A subagent acts subject to the control of the appointing agent, and the principal's legal position is affected by action taken by the subagent as if the action had been taken by the appointing agent. Thus, a subagent has two principals, the appointing agent and that agent's principal. Although an appointing agent has the right and duty to control a subagent, the interests and instructions of the appointing agent's principal are paramount. See Comment _d._

In contrast, an agent who appoints a coagent does not delegate power held by the agent to the coagent. By empowering an agent to appoint a coagent, the principal creates a mechanism through which to generate additional relationships of agency between the principal and persons chosen by the agent. Coagents, although they may occupy dominant and subordinate positions within an organizational hierarchy, share a common principal.

A person appointed by an agent to act on behalf of the agent's principal is a subagent if the appointing agent has agreed with the principal that the appointing agent shall be responsible to the principal for the agent's conduct. Such agreement may be express or implied. For example, employees of an appointing agent whom the agent designates to act on behalf of the appointing agent's principal are presumed to be subagents, not coagents of the appointing agent. When an agent is itself a corporation or other legal person, its officers, employees, partners, or members who are designated to work on the principal's account are subagents. In contrast, when a manager within an organization hires persons as employees of the organization, those hired are presumed to be the hiring manager's coagents and not subagents. Employees of a single organization are presumed to be coagents of that organization, not subagents.

**Illustrations:**

1\. A is the Chief Financial Officer of P Corporation. A hires S as P Corporation's comptroller. Although S reports to A, A and S are coagents of P Corporation. If A has the right, conferred by P Corporation, to direct S, A is S's superior and S is a subordinate coagent. See § 1.04(9), which defines the terms superior and subordinate coagent. S is not an agent of A or a subagent of P Corporation.

2\. P Corporation retains A Corporation to manage its investment portfolio. S, a senior vice-president of A Corporation, is placed in charge of the management of P Corporation's portfolio. S is P Corporation's subagent.

_c. Creation of subagency._ An agent has actual authority to create a relationship of subagency when the agent reasonably believes, based on a manifestation from the principal, that the principal consents to the appointment of a subagent. For the definition of actual authority, see § 2.01. For the means by which a principal creates it, see § 3.01.

A principal's consent to the appointment of a subagent may be express or implied. For example, a principal's consent to the appointment of subagents may be implied when the principal retains an individual as agent to carry out a number of transactions and has notice that the amount of work involved exceeds the agent's individual capability or requires action that the agent may not legally perform, such as action for which the law requires a license. Implied consent to appoint subagents is also present when an agent is itself a person that is not an individual, such as a corporation.

Subagents may be appointed in series. This would occur when a subagent has actual or apparent authority to appoint another for whose conduct the appointing subagent is responsible.

**Illustration:**

3\. Same facts as Illustration 2. P Corporation tells S to take all measures that S believes necessary to improve the performance of P Corporation's portfolio, including delegation to another portfolio manager. S retains V Associates, a firm specializing in certain types of debt securities, to buy and sell such securities for P Corporation's portfolio. V Associates is a subagent of P Corporation, appointed by S, and also a subagent of A Corporation.

An agent may have apparent authority to appoint a subagent if the principal has made a manifestation to third parties that the agent has such authority. Section 1.03 defines manifestation. Section 2.03 defines apparent authority; § 3.03 states how it is created. A principal may make a manifestation that an agent has actual authority to appoint subagents by placing the agent in a position in which agents customarily have such authority without notice to third parties that the agent's authority does not include the appointment of subagents. A subagent appointed by an agent acting with only apparent authority may act on behalf of the principal with actual authority. See Comment _d._

**Illustrations:**

4\. P retains A, a real-estate broker, to sell Blackacre. P knows that A employs salespeople to show property to prospective purchasers and to state the terms on which the property is for sale. The salespeople are A's employees, not P's employees. The salespeople are also P's subagents.

5\. Same facts as Illustration 4, except that P tells A that no one other than A may work on the Blackacre project. A employs S to work on the sale of Blackacre. S is unaware of P's direction to A and has no reason to believe that P wishes that A do the work alone. It is customary, as P and S know, for real-estate brokers like A to employ salespeople. S is P's subagent. If A's appointment of S and S's work on the sale of Blackacre cause loss to P, A is subject to liability to P. See § 8.09, Comment _b._

If an agent acts without actual or apparent authority in purporting to appoint a subagent, the person so appointed is the agent solely of the appointing agent and is not the principal's subagent unless the principal ratifies the appointment. On the requisites for ratification, see Chapter 4.

In emergencies and other unforeseen circumstances, when communication between agent and principal is not feasible, an agent's actual authority to take action to protect the principal's interests may permit the agent to appoint a subagent although the principal has not previously manifested consent to the agent to make such an appointment. See § 2.02, Comment _d._

**Illustration:**

6\. Same facts as Illustration 5, except that after retaining A and telling A that no one else may work on the Blackacre project, P tells A that it is important that Blackacre be sold within the next four months. P then leaves the country and cannot be contacted by A for six months. A becomes ill and is unable to work. A has actual authority to employ S to work on the sale of Blackacre. It is reasonable for A to believe, based on manifestations made to A by P, that P consents to A taking reasonable action to accomplish the sale of Blackacre.

A person may be appointed as a subagent even though the person does not expect to be compensated for actions taken.

**Illustrations:**

7\. P Corporation, in the consumer-finance business, employs A to manage the repossession of vehicles in which P Corporation has a security interest. P Corporation permits A to determine how best to effect repossessions and tells A to arrange assistance at A's expense if and when necessary. A retains S to assist in the repossession of an automobile owned by D in which P Corporation has a security interest because D is in default on D's car loan. A promises to pay S \$ 100 for S's assistance. Mistakenly, S seizes an automobile owned by T in which P Corporation also has a security interest, but T is not in default on T's car loan. P Corporation is subject to liability to T. S acted as P Corporation's subagent.

8\. Same facts as Illustration 7, except that S agrees to help A and does not expect to receive any compensation from A or from P Corporation. S acted as P Corporation's subagent.

_d. Consequences of subagency._ As between a principal and third parties, it is immaterial that an action was taken by a subagent as opposed to an agent directly appointed by the principal. In this respect, subagency is governed by a principle of transparency that looks from the subagent to the principal and through the appointing agent. As to third parties, an action taken by a subagent carries the legal consequences for the principal that would follow were the action instead taken by the appointing agent. Thus, in Illustrations 7 and 8, S's mistaken repossession of T's automobile subjects P Corporation to liability to T, just as would a mistaken repossession effected by A.

When a subagent works on a principal's account, notifications received by the subagent are effective as notifications to the principal to the same extent as if the principal had appointed the subagent directly. Likewise, notice of facts the subagent knows or has reason to know is imputed to the principal to the same extent as if the principal had appointed the subagent directly. Thus, in Illustrations 7 and 8, P Corporation is charged with notice of material facts that S knows or has reason to know, just as P Corporation would be charged with notice of the same material facts if A knows or has reason to know them. See §§ 5.01-5.04.

As among a principal, an appointing agent, and a subagent, the fact that action is taken by a subagent may carry different or additional legal consequences than if the action were taken directly by the appointing agent or by a coagent of the appointing agent. The legal consequences of subagency reflect the distinct significance of (1) the relationship between an appointing agent and a subagent; (2) the relationship between a principal and an appointing agent; and (3) the relationship between a principal and a subagent. An appointing agent is responsible to the principal for the subagent's conduct. This may subject an appointing agent to liability for loss incurred by the principal as a consequence of misconduct by a subagent. A contract between the principal and the appointing agent that requires or permits the appointment of subagents will often delineate the extent of the appointing agent's liability and will require indemnification by the principal of the appointing agent. In contrast, an agent is subject to liability stemming from a coagent's conduct only when the agent's own conduct subjects the agent to liability. See § 7.01, Comments _b_ and _d._

**Illustration:**

9\. Same facts as Illustration 7. T recovers a judgment against P Corporation for S's mistaken repossession of T's automobile. P Corporation cannot show any fault on the part of A in hiring or supervising S. A is subject to liability to indemnify P Corporation.

Statutes may change the results that would otherwise follow when a subagent takes action. For example, under Article 4 of the Uniform Commercial Code, when the owner of an item deposits it with a bank for collection, the depositary bank is treated as the agent of the item's owner. If the depositary bank sends the item for collection to another bank, the collecting bank is treated as the owner's subagent. See U.C.C. § 4-201(a). However, under U.C.C. § 4-202, although a depositary bank has a duty to use ordinary care in selecting intermediaries and in giving proper instructions to them, it is not liable for another bank's "insolvency, neglect, misconduct, mistake, or default . . . ."

Although an appointing agent is responsible for a subagent's action and has the right and duty to control the subagent, the principal's interests and instructions are nonetheless paramount. Several specific doctrines reflect this general point. If a subagent acts without actual or apparent authority, only the principal may ratify the subagent's action; ratification by an appointing agent is effective only when the principal has authorized the appointing agent to ratify on its behalf. Moreover, a subagent is a fiduciary, as is any agent. See § 1.01. A subagent owes duties of loyalty to the principal as well as to the appointing agent. See §§ 8.01-8.06, which state the specifics of these duties. A subagent owes a duty of obedience to the principal as well as to the appointing agent. See § 8.09(2). However, the principal's rights as to the subagent are superior to rights of the appointing agent, even in the event of conflict or disagreement between principal and appointing agent.

**Illustration:**

10\. Same facts as Illustration 7, except that S correctly effects repossession of the automobile owned by D. S complies with a direction from A that S retain possession of the automobile. P has a claim against S for the automobile. A is subject to liability to P. See § 8.09(2).

If a subagent is appointed by an agent who acts with only apparent authority, the subagent is privileged to act on behalf of the principal because the subagent, on the basis of the principal's manifestation, reasonably believes that the appointing agent acted with the principal's consent in appointing the subagent. As a consequence, the principal is subject to liability to the subagent to indemnify the subagent as stated in § 8.14. The appointing agent, having acted without actual authority in appointing the subagent, is subject to liability to the principal as stated in § 8.09(1). The subagent is not subject to liability to third parties on the basis that the subagent breached warranties of authority. See § 6.10.

A principal's duties to a subagent are those owed to any agent, but the principal is not subject to duties created by agreement between the appointing agent and the subagent, including the appointing agent's agreement to compensate the subagent. See § 8.13, Comment _d._ A subagent may have set-off rights against the principal. See § 6.06.

_e. Termination of subagent's actual and apparent authority._ Whether a subagent acts with actual authority depends on three separate consensual relationships: (1) between principal and appointing agent; (2) between appointing agent and subagent; and (3) between principal and subagent. A subagent's actual authority terminates upon notice to the subagent that any of these relationships is severed.

Additionally, circumstances that terminate an agent's actual authority by operation of law likewise terminate a subagent's authority. Thus, notice of the death of a principal or of an appointing agent terminates a subagent's actual authority, as does notice that the principal has revoked an appointing agent's actual authority. See §§ 3.07(2) and 3.10(1). If the relationship between an appointing agent and a subagent is severed, for example by notice of the appointing agent's death or loss of capacity, the subagent no longer acts under the control of the appointing agent who had responsibility for the subagent's acts. If a subagent is a lawyer who represents a client having diminished capacity, the lawyer's duties are as stated in Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 24.

**Illustration:**

11\. P gives a durable power of attorney to A authorizing A to take all action on P's behalf that P could take if acting personally. P loses capacity to manage P's own affairs. A retains S, an investment manager, to manage P's portfolio of investments. A dies. S has notice of A's death. S does not have actual authority to continue to act on behalf of P. S may continue to act with apparent authority. See § 3.11, Comment _b._ Any claim that S may have for the value conferred on P by services performed by S after S had notice of A's death is as stated in Restatement Third, Restitution and Unjust Enrichment §    .

Additionally, a principal may terminate a subagent's actual authority by making a manifestation of revocation directly to the subagent or to the appointing agent. A manifestation revoking a subagent's actual authority made by the principal to the appointing agent should not be effective, in most circumstances, as to the subagent until the subagent has notice of it. A principal has rights and responsibilities of ultimate control, including ultimate control over chains of command and channels of communication. A principal may choose how best to communicate with a subagent, including how best to convey notice that the subagent's actual authority has been revoked. If the principal chooses to rely exclusively on the appointing agent as the channel of communication with the subagent, the principal at least ordinarily should bear the risk that the appointing agent will not duly deliver notice of revocation to the subagent.

**Illustrations:**

12\. P Corporation, in the business of manufacturing toys, retains A Corporation as its sales representative for the United States. A Corporation designates an employee, S, as the representative for P Corporation's account for the southwestern United States. Dissatisfied with S's performance, P Corporation sends written notice to S that P Corporation terminates S's authority, effective upon S's receipt of the notice. S receives the notice. S no longer has actual authority to act on behalf of P Corporation.

13\. Same facts as Illustration 12, except that the vice-president for sales of P Corporation tells A, the CEO of A Corporation, that P Corporation no longer consents to representation by S in the southwestern United States. A promises to remove S from P Corporation's account immediately. A does not do so. S has no notice from any source that P Corporation no longer consents to representation by S. Until S receives such notice, S continues to act with actual authority.

14\. P lists Blackacre for sale with A, a real-estate brokerage firm. In the listing agreement, P states a minimum acceptable price; consents to A's appointment of subagents to obtain a buyer for Blackacre; and agrees to pay a commission of six percent of the sale price to be paid either to A alone if A obtains a buyer for Blackacre acting alone, or to be split equally between A and any subagent who obtains a buyer for Blackacre. A contacts S, another real-estate broker, who receives an offer from T to buy Blackacre at P's listing price. S accepts T's offer on P's behalf but before S notifies A or P, P learns that V is willing to buy Blackacre at a price higher than the listing price. P notifies A that P revokes A's authority, and the authority of any subagent appointed by A, to sell at the listing price. S acted with actual authority in accepting T's offer. S had no notice that P no longer consented to the sale of Blackacre at the price at which P listed it for sale with A.

In contrast, under the rule stated in Restatement Second, Agency § 137, Comment _b,_ when a principal terminates a subagent's authority by giving notice of termination to the appointing agent, "the subagent's authority terminates only when the agent has had an opportunity to communicate with him." If the appointing agent, having had the opportunity to communicate with the subagent, does not do so, or does not do so effectively, the rule as stated in Restatement Second allocates to the subagent the risk of acting without actual authority, although the subagent lacks notice that the principal wishes to terminate the subagent's authority. The notice requirement stated in this Restatement instead allocates to the principal the risk that notice of revocation communicated only to the appointing agent may not reach the subagent. So allocating the risk is supported by a well-reasoned case in the real-estate context. The principal should bear this risk because the principal has consented to the appointment of subagents and because a subagent should be under no greater risk than is an agent of unwittingly taking an unauthorized action following the termination of actual authority.

A subagent may continue to act with apparent authority following termination of the subagent's actual authority when third parties reasonably believe that the subagent continues to act with actual authority on the basis of manifestations traceable to the appointing agent or the principal. See § 3.11.

**Illustration:**

15\. Same facts as Illustration 12. After S receives notice from P Corporation terminating S's authority to represent P Corporation, S enters into a contract to sell toys manufactured by P Corporation to T. S has previously sold toys manufactured by P Corporation to T on comparable terms. T has no notice that P Corporation has revoked S's authority. S acted with apparent authority in entering into the contract with T. P Corporation is subject to liability on the contract, as is T. See § 6.01(2). S and A Corporation are subject to liability to P Corporation. See § 8.09, Comment _b._

_f. Transactions in real estate._ Under the common-law rule in most states, a real-estate broker or salesperson associated with a broker who successfully markets property to a prospective buyer (hereinafter the "showing broker") is treated as the subagent of the broker with whom a prospective seller has listed the property (hereinafter the "listing broker"), even if the showing broker is not the listing broker's employee or associate. Conventional terminology applies the term "broker" or "real-estate broker" to a person holding a license to provide brokerage services. A broker may use the services of salespeople, who may be employees of the broker or the broker's nonemployee associates. Except where the context otherwise requires, the remainder of this Comment refers to all as "brokers."

The better rule, followed in a minority of states, characterizes a showing broker as the agent of the prospective buyer, and not the seller's subagent, when the terms of the relationship between the showing broker and the prospective buyer reasonably reflect the buyer's expectation that the showing broker represents the buyer. The minority rule is consistent with the policy reflected in legislation in a majority of states that explicitly permits a prospective buyer to appoint a real-estate broker to act as the buyer's agent.

A showing broker may be characterized as: (1) the seller's subagent, regardless of whether the broker has a relationship with the buyer that constitutes a relationship of agency as defined by § 1.01; (2) the buyer's agent when the relationship between buyer and broker constitutes a relationship of agency as defined by § 1.01; or (3) neither the agent nor the subagent of buyer or seller. If a showing broker is characterized as acting as agent for both buyer and seller, or as agent for buyer and subagent for seller, the agent is in a position of dual agency because the showing broker represents adverse parties to the same transaction. See § 8.03.

As the seller's subagent, a showing broker is presumed not to have a relationship of agency with the buyer and does not owe an agent's fiduciary duties to the buyer. It is well-settled, as a matter of industry practice and regulation as well as common-law agency, that a broker in a single real-estate transaction may not represent both buyer and seller unless both know of and consent to the dual representation. Unless such consent is given, a broker who represents both buyer and seller may forfeit any commission otherwise payable to the broker. See § 8.03, Comment _d._ In a transaction involving the same property, buyer and seller have interests that are adverse. The benefit that a seller will receive through a higher sales price will be paid by the buyer. Moreover, a broker who represents a prospective seller or buyer of property may have material information that, if disclosed to the other party, will disadvantage the broker's client. For example, information about the seller's reservation price--the lowest price at which the seller may be willing to sell--is material to price negotiations between the seller and prospective buyers. For further treatment of dual agency, see § 8.03.

The mechanism through which a relationship of subagency arises is an offer of a unilateral contract of subagency, made by the listing broker to showing brokers, that is accepted by the showing broker who produces a buyer able and willing to purchase the property at the listing price and terms or at other terms acceptable to the seller. Such an offer is often made when a listing broker lists property with a multiple listing service ("MLS"). A multiple listing service is an agreement among brokers in a particular geographic market to pool information about their listings. A showing broker may also become a subagent in the absence of MLS participation through a relationship of cooperation with the listing broker. When a showing broker produces an offer from a buyer to purchase at the seller's stated listing price and on terms otherwise consistent with the listing agreement, conventional MLS terms require that the listing broker split the commission to be paid by the seller with the successful showing broker.

The legal consequences of how a showing broker is characterized carry corresponding advantages as well as disadvantages from the standpoint of buyers and sellers of real estate. Characterizing a showing broker as the seller's subagent orients the showing broker's duties exclusively to the seller, with the implication that the broker must disclose to the seller all material information the broker learns about the buyer. This disadvantages a buyer if the showing broker discloses information the buyer has provided to the seller or the listing broker, such as the maximum price the buyer is willing to pay to acquire property. However, this characterization may be advantageous to the buyer if the showing broker induces the buyer to purchase a property through fraud because the buyer may rescind the transaction. For the circumstances under which a misrepresentation makes a contract voidable, see Restatement Second, Contracts § 164(1). To be sure, a seller may not perceive this consequence to be fair when the seller has had no contact with a showing broker who induces a purchase through fraud. In contrast, if a showing broker acts as a buyer's agent, the buyer will be limited to remedies against the broker if the seller is innocent of the fraud and has in good faith given value or changed position in reliance on the transaction. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 164(2). Neither buyer nor seller benefits if a showing broker is not characterized as either the seller's subagent or the buyer's agent because the duties of a nonagent showing broker are indeterminate. However, principals in real-estate transactions may benefit if nonagent showing brokers charge lower commissions than brokers who act as agents and subagents.

The common-law rule applicable to agency relationships in this context should reflect the reasonable expectations of prospective buyers and sellers of property and the real-estate professionals who act on their behalf. If a broker has agreed to assist a prospective buyer, it is reasonable for the prospective buyer to expect that the broker does not owe duties of loyalty to the seller of property that the buyer purchases. Treating a showing broker as the seller's subagent is likely to conflict with the expectations of a buyer to whom the showing broker has given guidance and, in many instances, advice. Moreover, a seller may reasonably expect that, as the seller's subagent, the showing broker will be loyal to the seller's interests and will not compromise them through advice or information that the showing broker may share with buyers. If a showing broker is treated as a buyer's agent, although the buyer loses the availability of rescission as a remedy in some situations, the buyer gains the advantages of representation by a professional whom the buyer is free to choose. Real-estate professionals are best served by rules that clarify the identity of parties to whom particular duties are owed and that do not create the risk of inadvertently acting as a dual agent for buyer and seller.

Empirical results confirm the significance to buyers of retaining the services of an agent who has clearly defined duties to represent the buyer, even when the agent is compensated by splitting a commission that will be payable from the proceeds of a sale and that will be augmented the greater the sale price. In Georgia, following the enactment of a statute that encourages buyers to retain their own agents, the average time required to sell a house fell, as did reported sale prices of expensive houses. Additionally, a large majority of buyers chose to be represented by their own agent.

Statutes in many states expressly permit prospective buyers to retain licensed real-estate brokers as agents. Since 1993, the Realtors' Association has permitted listing brokers to offer showing brokers, as an alternative to a relationship of subagency, a relationship of "cooperation and compensation" that is compatible both with a showing broker who acts as a buyer's agent as well as with a showing broker who acts as the seller's subagent.

Unless a statute so requires, a writing should not be necessary to establish that a prospective buyer and a real-estate broker have consented to create a relationship of agency. See § 3.02, Comment _e,_ for discussion of statutes that impose a writing requirement on agreements to pay commissions to brokers. Statutes in some states require a written agreement to create a relationship of agency with either a listing broker or a buyer's agent.

If a prospective buyer does not create a relationship of agency as defined in § 1.01 with a showing broker, the showing broker should be treated as the seller's subagent if the seller has expressly or impliedly consented to the appointment of subagents by the listing broker. The showing broker should not be treated as the buyer's agent when no relationship of agency as defined in § 1.01 has been created between the buyer and the showing broker, nor should the showing broker be treated as an intermediary who acts as no one's agent, unless a statute so requires.

If a prospective buyer consents to a relationship of agency as defined in § 1.01 with a particular real-estate broker, it is incompatible with the duties thereby created for that broker additionally to act as subagent for the seller of property that the buyer purchases unless the buyer knows of the subagency and consents to it and unless the seller knows of and consents to the broker's relationship with the buyer. On consent by a principal to conduct by an agent that would otherwise breach the agent's duties of loyalty to the principal, see § 8.06. A broker retained by a prospective buyer would become a dual agent if the broker also acts as subagent on behalf of the seller of the property purchased by the buyer who is represented by the broker. This would occur if the property's owner lists it with the buyer's broker or if the buyer's broker is also characterized as the subagent of the property's owner. If the property in which a buyer is interested is listed in a MLS under terms that designate a successful showing broker as the seller's subagent, that designation should not be operative without the consent of a buyer who has previously retained the showing broker as the buyer's agent.

However, an agent is not a dual agent if there is no substantial risk that the agent's action on behalf of one principal will materially and adversely affect the agent's action on behalf of another principal. See § 8.03. An agent retained by a buyer does not become a dual agent by taking ministerial actions on behalf of a seller. Dual agency, as noted above, results when the broker whom a buyer has retained to act as the buyer's agent also serves as listing agent for the property purchased by the buyer, whether at the outset of the buyer's relationship with the broker or thereafter if the owner of the property lists it for sale with that broker. Under the common-law rule in most states, a broker has a relationship of agency with each of the broker's clients although the clients have agreed to be represented by individuals among the broker's cohort of employees and associates. Thus, a broker would be characterized as a dual agent when one employee or associate serves as the designated agent of a buyer and another serves as the seller's designated agent. Statutes in some states explicitly permit the practice of designated dual agency and impose specific writing and disclosure requirements. In contrast, a few statutes categorically prohibit dual agency in transactions but permit the use of nonagent intermediaries, as described below.

Relationships among brokers, sellers, and buyers have been recharacterized by statutes enacted by many states since the late 1980s. Statutes in many states expressly permit prospective buyers to retain licensed real-estate brokers to act as their agents. However, these statutes are disparate in specifying the default rule that characterizes a showing broker when a buyer has not appointed that broker as agent. Several statutes provide that subagency shall not be deemed to be created automatically or through participation in a MLS. Some statutes provide that, if no written agreement establishes a relationship of agency or subagency, a broker is treated as a "transaction broker," "transaction coordinator," or "facilitator" who does not act as either party's agent or subagent. Some statutes that permit the use of such nonagent intermediaries specify the duties owed by the intermediary. Many statutes make subagency the default characterization, either explicitly or implicitly by not specifying an alternative. Other statutes require specific types of disclosure, such as disclosure to a prospective buyer that a seller's agent represents the seller. Several statutes permit the parties to create a relationship of "designated dual agency" not subject to the usual consequences of dual agency, in which one sales associate in a real-estate brokerage represents the seller of a property and the other its buyer. Some statutes characterize such brokers as "limited agents," specifying their duties. Some statutes explicitly prohibit "designated dual agency." Statutes in a few states explicitly abrogate the common law of agency.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency, and codifications._ The substance of the black letter is comparable to its counterparts in Restatement Second, Agency, except that Restatement Second includes, as a separate category of subagents, "subservants." See Restatement Second, Agency §§ 5(1) (defining subagent); 5(2) (defining subservant); 77-81 (stating when agent has authority to appoint subagent and when servant has authority to appoint subservant); 137 (stating when authority or apparent authority of subagent or subservant shall terminate); 142 (stating general doctrine of principal's liability for act of subagent or subservant); 428 (stating duties that subagent owes to principal); 458 (stating duties that principal owes to subagent).

Restatement Second, Agency, also states in § 5 that an appointing agent "agrees" to be primarily responsible for the subagent's conduct. The "agreement" element has been omitted from the black letter because an appointing agent's refusal to be responsible for a subagent's conduct does not relieve an appointing agent of responsibility for the subagent's conduct, if the relationship is otherwise one of subagency, unless an agreement between principal and agent allocates financial responsibility for the consequences of the subagent's conduct.

Most codified treatments of agency contain provisions that deal explicitly with subagency. The California Civil Code provides that "\[a\]n agent, unless specially forbidden by his principal to do so, can delegate his powers to another person in any of the following cases, and in no others: 1. When the act to be done is purely mechanical; 2. When it is such as the agent cannot himself, and the subagent can lawfully perform; 3. When it is the usage of the place to delegate such powers; or, 4. When such delegation is specially authorized by the principal." Cal. Civ. Code § 2349 (1985 & Supp. 2005). Under § 2351, "\[a\] sub-agent, lawfully appointed, represents the principal in like manner with the original agent; and the original agent is not responsible to third persons for the acts of the subagent." Under § 2350, "\[i\]f an agent employs a sub-agent without authority, the former is a principal and the latter his agent, and the principal of the former has no connection with the latter." Comparable provisions appear in the codes of Montana, North Dakota, and South Dakota. See Mont. Code Ann. §§ 28-10-501 to -503 (2003); N.D. Cent. Code §§ 3-02-13 to -15 (1987 & Supp. 2003); S.D. Codified Laws §§ 59-3-15 to -17 (1993 & Supp. 2003).

In the Louisiana Civil Code, the terminological counterpart to "subagent" is "substitute." Article 3006 provides that "\[i\]n the absence of contrary agreement; the mandatary is bound to fulfill the mandate himself. Nevertheless, if the interests of the principal so require, when unforeseen circumstances prevent the mandatary from performing his duties and he is unable to communicate with the principal, the mandatary may appoint a substitute." La. Civ. Code Art. 3006 (Supp. 2005). Under Article 3007, "\[w\]hen the mandatary is authorized to appoint a substitute, he is answerable to the principal for the acts of the substitute only if he fails to exercise diligence in selecting the substitute or in giving instructions. When not authorized to appoint a substitute, the mandatary is answerable to the principal for the acts of the substitute as if the mandatary had performed the mandate himself. In all cases, the principal has recourse against the substitute."

These Code provisions impose less responsibility on the appointing agent for the legal consequences of the subagent's acts than does the rule stated in Restatement Second, Agency § 362 that "\[t\]he liability of an agent to third persons for the conduct of his servants, subservants and other subagents is the same as that of a master or other principal for the conduct of his servants and other agents."

The Georgia Code states that "\[w\]hatever one may do by himself may be done by an agent, except such personal trusts in which special confidence is placed on the skill, discretion, or judgment of the person called in to act; so an agent may not delegate his authority to another unless specially empowered to do so." Ga. Code § 10-6-5 (1996 & Supp. 2004).

_c. Creation of subagency._ The most comprehensive treatment of subagency remains Warren A. Seavey, Subagents and Subservants, 68 Harv. L. Rev. 658 (1955), reprinted in Restatement Second, Agency § 5, Reporter's Notes (Appendix vol. 3).

For applications of the basic definition of subagency, see [_Estate of Smith v. Underwood, 487 S.E.2d 807, 815-816 (N.C.App.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RHV-X9S0-0039-44G3-00000-00&context=1530671) (holding that accountant who prepared and filed tax returns for affiliated corporations and trusts was agent of principals, not subagent of lawyer who advised principals on tax matters and acted as general business manager; although lawyer hired accountant, accountant did not work solely for lawyer and was paid directly by principals, and no evidence indicated that lawyer assumed responsibility for accountant); [_Richardson v. John F. Kennedy Mem'l Hosp., 838 F.Supp. 979, 986 (E.D.Pa.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-CYP0-001T-64RT-00000-00&context=1530671) (issues of fact present whether person designated as "Special Assistant" to his father, who was hospital's president and executive director, was hospital's subagent; Special Assistant signed contract with plaintiff that under hospital's bylaws president and executive director had exclusive authority to enter into on hospital's behalf; hospital would be bound as principal by contract made by subagent if appointing agent had authority to make contract).

Unless a principal has explicitly or implicitly directed the retention of a subagent, an agent who is hired by an agent to carry out the principal's work remains the hiring agent's agent alone. See [_Estate of Smith, 487 S.E.2d at 815_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RHV-X9S0-0039-44G3-00000-00&context=1530671) (dictum). Moreover, a principal is not bound by any hiring done by its agent when the power to hire is a nondelegable function of the principal's governing board. See, e.g., [_McCormick v. Lake Washington School Dist., 992 P.2d 511, 514-515 (Wash.App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3Y27-KMJ0-0039-40T5-00000-00&context=1530671).

For the point that an agent's authority to engage a subagent may be implied from the nature of the work to be performed, see, e.g., [_Chevron Oil Co. v. Sutton, 515 P.2d 1283, 1287 (N.M.1973)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W9P0-003D-D34H-00000-00&context=1530671) (if master-servant relationship existed between oil company and lessee of service station, lessee would have authority to hire assistants because it would have been impracticable for lessee to operate service station alone; contract between oil company and station lessee itself contemplated that assistants might be employed). A relationship of subagency may also be based on a principal's awareness that another person will do work to discharge an agent's duty. See [_Royal Mortgage Corp. v. Montague, 41 S.W.3d 721, 735 (Tex.App. 2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4258-N850-0039-42KB-00000-00&context=1530671) (to establish sufficient presence in state for court's personal jurisdiction over principal, court holds that principal's awareness that employee of debt-purchasing firm would negotiate terms of actual contract made employee principal's subagent).

For the point that an appointing agent may impliedly agree to be responsible for a subagent's acts, see [_Colony Assocs. v. Fred L. Clapp & Co., 300 S.E.2d 37, 40 (N.C.App.1983)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-10Y0-003G-0219-00000-00&context=1530671) (defendant whom plaintiff contacted to arrange loan refinancing directed plaintiff to make deposit check payable to mortgage company whose involvement defendant needed to obtain refinancing; although defendant and mortgage company agreed to split the fee on a 50-50 basis, court holds defendant liable to plaintiff for entire amount of deposit check after financing fails to materialize on basis that agent is liable to principal for subagent's conduct unless otherwise agreed).

For the point that, absent ratification, a subagent appointed by an agent acting without actual or apparent authority is solely the agent of the appointing principal, see [_Pope v. Rice, 2005 WL 613085, at \*\*8-9 (S.D.N.Y.2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4FR5-5C20-TVW3-P2F9-00000-00&context=1530671) (cocounsel relationship among lawyers did not create subagency relationship; lawyer had neither actual nor apparent authority to appoint subagent).

The terms of agreement between principal and agent are relevant to establish whether an agent is responsible for the acts of persons argued to be subagents. See [_Zeigler v. Elmore County Health Care Auth., 56 F.Supp.2d 1334, 1337-1338 (M.D.Ala. 1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3X3K-6J50-0038-Y01H-00000-00&context=1530671) (terms of agreement between 2 hospitals contained express disclaimer of responsibility by hospital that assumed management functions, which included hiring and firing, for actions of persons working in healthcare facility and provided that managed hospital remained primarily responsible for most workers; court finds as matter of law that employees of managed hospital were not subagents of hospital that provided management services). On delegation of functions in the context of real-estate transactions, compare [_Lawyers Title Ins. Corp. v. Phillips Title Agency, 361 F.Supp.2d 443, 448-449 (D.N.J. 2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4FPJ-8HW0-TVVX-S33N-00000-00&context=1530671) (primary title agency delegated performance of closings to another agency; primary agency subject to liability for delegate's negligence in conducting closing as if delegate were its own employee when agreement with title insurer did not permit delegation of "'any right, interest, or duty arising under'" agreement without insurer's written consent) with [_Lawyers Title Ins. Corp. v. Groff, 808 A.2d 44, 48-50 (N.H.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:46SP-10G0-0039-44WV-00000-00&context=1530671) (lawyer who functioned as settlement agent not subject to liability to title insurer for negligence of title abstractor in performing title search; lawyer's agreement with insurer limited his liability to his own acts and omissions, foreclosing argument that abstractor was lawyer's subagent).

On a principal's liability for acts done by persons acting gratuitously under the direction of employee agents, compare [_Althorf v. Wolfe, 22 N.Y. 355 (1860)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3W1P-PFY0-00KR-F09S-00000-00&context=1530671) (employer liable for injuries inflicted on passerby by snow and ice thrown from roof by volunteer assisting employee; employer gave employee general directions to throw snow from roof of his house but suggested no mode of so doing and placed employee under no restriction against procuring assistance in doing the work) with [_Kosick v. Standard Props., Inc., 177 A. 428, 429 (N.J.1935)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XG1-F7P0-004J-M1MY-00000-00&context=1530671) (employer not liable for injuries inflicted on passerby by snow thrown from yard by janitor's son, who was gratuitously assisting his father; employer liable as a consequence of work done by employee's delegate only when employee made delegation with employer's direct or implied authority). In _Kosick,_ the court held that authority to delegate may be implied "when the work to be done is so far beyond the power of the servant as to indicate the necessity for assistance." [_Id. at 428_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XG1-F7P0-004J-M1MY-00000-00&context=1530671). Many cases follow the more stringent test stated in _Kosick_ explicitly or, without much discussion, hold that employers are not liable on the basis of work that employees have unilaterally decided to delegate to others. See, e.g., [_Higgins v. Investors Acceptance Co., 287 So. 2d 724, 726 (Fla.Dist.App.1974)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-74M0-003C-W4HM-00000-00&context=1530671) (employer of bill collector not liable for plaintiff's injuries when collector's companion sprayed mace in plaintiffs face following plaintiff's resistance to collector's nighttime attempt to enter plaintiff's residence; employer not liable for acts of employee's helper unless employer directed that helper be hired or could be considered to have authorized or expected hiring from nature of work, and no showing of a general custom for employees to utilize bodyguards in making collections or that employer "should have been aware from the nature of his business that an employee would hire such a subemployee"); [_White v. J.E. Levi & Co., 73 S.E. 376, 377 (Ga. 1911)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WMR-V8P0-00KR-D4SW-00000-00&context=1530671) (employer who directed employee to bring automobile to employer's place of business for repairs not liable for injury to plaintiff caused by accident when young child chosen by employee was operating automobile; unless employer ratified employment of child, employer not liable when employee acted outside scope of authority in delegating work to child). See also [_Standard Oil Co. v. Soderling, 42 N.E.2d 373, 377-378 (Ind.App.1942)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3X52-76D0-00KR-C02B-00000-00&context=1530671) (lessee of gas station, if treated as lessor's special agent for purpose of receiving shipment of air compressor consigned by lessor to station in care of lessee, lacked authority to direct employee who worked at gas station to unload compressor; lessor not liable to driver of truck on which compressor had been delivered who was injured by station employee's alleged negligence in unloading compressor because doctrine of respondeat superior is inapplicable to act of assistant retained by employee unless employee has actual or implied authority to employ help).

For extensive analysis of an appointing agent's liability to a principal for acts of a subagent, the subagent's duty to indemnify the appointing agent, and possible conflicts in interest as between the appointing agent and the principal, see [_Kramer v. No-wak, 908 F.Supp. 1281, 1289-1292 (E.D.Pa.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-6K20-001T-520J-00000-00&context=1530671) (holding that under New Jersey law, associate employed by law firm is subject to liability to firm for negligence in performing work for client; client earlier recovered arbitration award against firm). Subagency is a basis on which a law firm may be subject to liability for the actions of lawyers outside the firm who act as cocounsel or in similar arrangements. See Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 58, Comment _e._ See also [_Pollack v. Lytle, 175 Cal.Rptr. 81, 85-86 (Cal. App.1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S11-SG60-003C-R2TT-00000-00&context=1530671) (holding that lawyer subsequently retained as cocounsel may be subject to liability to initial counsel for breach of fiduciary duty arising from cocounsel's competition to be named sole trial counsel, which led to loss at trial and malpractice action that cocounsel induced client to file against initial counsel).

Illustration 4 appears as Illustration 5 to Restatement Second, Agency § 5. See also [_Moss v. Ole South Real Estate, Inc., 933 F.2d 1300, 1312 (5th Cir.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-BV40-008H-V3KY-00000-00&context=1530671) (citing Illustration 5, court holds that principal who owned property also controlled real-estate agency and through it agency's employees who performed their duties for owner's benefit).

Illustration 7 is based on a variation on the facts in [_Waggaman v. General Fin. Co., 116 F.2d 254, 258 (3d Cir.1940)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-W1N0-003B-T2XJ-00000-00&context=1530671) (employee of finance company had authority to hire driver to transport repossessed vehicles to site directed by finance company; finance company liable to plaintiff injured by negligent driving of driver hired by finance company's employee).

_d. Consequences of subagency._ On the duty of care that a subagent owes the principal, see [_United Capitol Ins. Co. v. Kapiloff, 155 F.3d 488, 498 (4th Cir.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3TKM-STW0-0038-X4FG-00000-00&context=1530671) (under Maryland law, evidence supported jury finding that surplus-lines insurance broker who placed insurance on behalf of plaintiffs acted as plaintiffs' agent or subagent after plaintiffs' request for insurance was referred to surplus-lines broker by insurance broker whom plaintiffs initially retained; as plaintiffs' agent or subagent who knew of plaintiffs and acted on their behalf, surplus-lines broker owed plaintiffs a duty of reasonable care in procuring insurance).

Official Comment 4 to U.C.C. § 4-202(c) states that at common law (termed the "New York rule") the initial bank was subject to liability for actions of subsequent banks in the collection chain. The drafters instead adopted the "Massachusetts rule," which made each bank liable for only its own negligence, subject to a duty to select proper intermediaries.

Statutes may also expand a principal's liability beyond its common-law boundaries. For example, under the Australian Insurance (Agents and Brokers) Act 1984, as amended, an insurer may be subject to liability for the conduct of a subagent even though the agreement between the appointing agent and the insurer forbade the appointment of the subagent and the subagent acted beyond the scope of authority. See Insurance (Agents and Brokers) Act 1984 § 11 (1H) (1999).

On ratification of a subagent's act, see [_Booker v. United Am. Ins. Co., 700 So. 2d 1333, 1337 (Ala.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RG8-34H0-0039-4071-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[i\]t is the principal's ratification, not the agent's, that is controlling"; court finds that principal was incapable of ratifying alleged subagent's actions because it was unaware of them); [_Bamber Contractors, Inc. v. Morrison Eng'g & Contracting Co., 385 So. 2d 327, 331 (La.App.1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRR-2TH0-003G-M3D1-00000-00&context=1530671) (evidence insufficient to show that general contractor ratified lease of earth-moving equipment entered into by subcontractor on construction project; general contractor received no benefit from equipment and evidence did not establish any clear manifestation of assent by general contractor); cf. [_Carter v. Bishop, 76 S.E.2d 784, 789 (Ga.1953)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-6R40-003V-M0FX-00000-00&context=1530671) (no showing that employer ratified act of employee in engaging subemployee).

In English law, the lack of privity of contract between principal and subagent has been more prominent in judicial reasoning, and in particular appears to have made direct actions by principals against subagents more difficult. For an illustration, see Prentis Donegan & Partners Ltd. v. Leeds & Leeds Co., \[1998\] 2 Lloyd's Rep. 326 (Q.B.). See also Francis M.B. Reynolds, Bowstead & Reynolds on Agency 142-145 (17th ed. 2001). For criticism of the English position, see Andrew Tettenborn, Principals, Subagents and Accountability, 115 L.Q. Rev. 655 (1999).

On subagency in other European systems, see Principles of European Contract Law 211-213 (Ole Lando & Hugh Beale, eds., 2000).

Canadian authority holds that whether a subagent owes a fiduciary duty to a principal depends on whether the principal can establish that it relied upon or reposed trust and confidence in the subagent. See Knoch Estate v. Jon Picken Ltd., 4 O.R. (3d) 385, 401 (1991).

_e. Termination of subagent's actual and apparent authority._ A subagent's authority may terminate by operation of law. See [_Friedman v. Irving Trust Co., 300 N.Y.S. 51, 55 (Mun.Ct.1937)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-2JS0-0044-F30H-00000-00&context=1530671) (authority of collecting bank terminated by operation of law by insolvency of forwarding bank).

On a principal's power to terminate a subagent, see [_Marine & Indus. Healthcare Servs., Inc. v. Lavoie, 2003 WL 446819, at \*4 (E.D.La.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4812-61V0-0038-Y1XC-00000-00&context=1530671) (vessel owners had "absolute right" to terminate or suspend subagent, retained by local agent, to arrange medical treatment for seamen).

The outcome stated for Illustration 11 is consistent with the statement in Restatement Second, Agency § 137, Comment _b,_ that "\[t\]he authority of the subagent may terminate because of the death or incapacity of the principal, the agent, or himself." The facts of Illustration 11 are based on those of [_In re Khou-besserian, 695 N.Y.S.2d 312 (App. Div.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XDW-84G0-0039-41R6-00000-00&context=1530671). However, the subagent in _In re Khoubesserian_ was a lawyer, not a financial manager. The outcome stated for Illustration 11 is not explicitly supported by the case. In _In re Khoubesserian,_ a lawyer continued to pursue a claim against an incompetent principal's former employer for two years after learning of the death of the appointing agent, who held the principal's durable power of attorney. After hiring the lawyer, the appointing agent executed a power of attorney delegating to him the powers earlier delegated to her by the now-incompetent principal. The claim resulted in payment of \$ 100,000 due the principal under a pension plan, from which the lawyer deducted 33 percent as his fee, pursuant to agreement reached with the appointing agent. The principal's guardian, appointed two years after the appointing agent's death, challenged the lawyer's fee as an unauthorized disbursement from the principal's assets. The court held that the lawyer's retention of the fee was justified because the principal would not have netted the remainder of the recovery but for his efforts and because there was no evidence of overreaching or unreasonable use of the principal's power of attorney. The court also stated that "\[w\]e do not rule, as a matter of law, that a power of attorney necessarily survives beyond the death of the principal." [_Id. at 315_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XDW-84G0-0039-41R6-00000-00&context=1530671). The court does not discuss whether it is advisable to permit a lawyer to continue to act on behalf of a client when no one can make decisions that would otherwise be made personally by a competent client. See also Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 24, Comment _e_ (stating that lawyer whose client's capacity is severely diminished may seek appointment of guardian or other representative when "no other practical method of protecting the client's best interests is available. . . .").

The statement in Restatement Second concerning when a principal's notice of revocation to an agent is effective to terminate a subagent's actual authority is at odds with the suggestion in Warren A. Seavey, Agency 98 (1964) that "\[i\]t is not unthinkable that an innocent subagent would be protected in acting if the agent failed to notify him, since, after all, his employer was the agent of the principal for whose conduct the principal is responsible." Supporting the position stated in this Restatement, and the stated outcome for Illustration 14, is [_Stortroen v. Beneficial Fin. Co., 736 P.2d 391, 402 (Colo.1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-1120-003D-9524-00000-00&context=1530671) (listing agent's communication to showing agent revoking offer of unilateral contract of subagency ineffective when listing agent left messages on showing agent's residential and office answering machines but, unaware of messages, showing agent received notice of acceptance of counteroffer to sell property from its would-be purchasers; court states "a principal's revocation of agency authority terminates only upon notice to the agent."). _Stortroen_ does not discuss when a principal's communication is effective to give notice to an agent or subagent if the agent or subagent does not have actual knowledge of the communication.

_f. Transactions in real estate._ For the majority rule characterizing a showing broker as the seller's subagent, see, e.g., [_Fennell v. Ross, 711 S.W.2d 793 (Ark.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-XPW0-003C-12V7-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Stortroen, 736 P.2d at 396_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-1120-003D-9524-00000-00&context=1530671) (stating that rule is "widely accepted"); [_Snider v. State ex rel. Oklahoma Real Estate Comm'n, 987 P.2d 1204, 1209 (Okla.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WNV-4T30-0039-41XT-00000-00&context=1530671); David A. Thomas, 11 Thompson on Real Property 513-514 (1994). Cases not following the majority rule are [_Mead v. Hummel, 121 P.2d 423, 425 (Ariz.1942)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRH-GPN0-003F-S3FF-00000-00&context=1530671) (real-estate dealers who approached owner of land stating they had buyers for it characterized as buyer's agents although owner promised to pay dealers a commission if sale consummated); [_Wise v. Dawson, 353 A.2d 207 (Del.Super.1975)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRT-BVV0-003C-K26N-00000-00&context=1530671) (in absence of evidence to contrary, MLS listing does not create subagency relationship); [_Menzel v. Morse, 362 N.W.2d 465, 475 (Iowa 1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2JN0-003G-5087-00000-00&context=1530671) (evidence that broker sought out purchasers sufficient to create relationship of agency; broker not subagent of sellers when broker never told purchasers he represented sellers); and [_Pumphrey v. Quillen, 141 N.E.2d 675 (Ohio App.1955)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-WGP0-003C-63ST-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_135 N.E.2d 328 (Ohio 1956)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-TDV0-003C-64CN-00000-00&context=1530671) (showing broker not agent of sellers).

On developments in real-estate brokerage relationships, see Ronald Benton Brown et al., Real Estate Brokerage: Recent Changes in Relationships and a Proposed Cure, [_29 Creighton L. Rev. 25 (1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S3T-9VC0-00CV-74KG-00000-00&context=1530671); Kathleen A. Pancak et al., Real Estate Agency Reform: Meeting the Needs of Buyers, Sellers, and Brokers, 25 Real Estate L.J. 345 (1997). For discussion of the origins of buyer's brokerage and issues it may raise, see John G. Cameron, Jr., Buyer Brokering--More Complicated Than It Sounds (with Form), 12 Prac. Real Estate Law. 13 (1996).

For an illustration of the perils for a buyer who is not represented in a transaction, see [_McAdams v. Dorothy Edwards Realtors, Inc., 604 N.E.2d 607 (Ind.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-1WY0-003F-X18W-00000-00&context=1530671) (buyers who were not represented either by counsel or by broker purchased property subject to two liens, as buyers learned at closing, in a contract sale under terms requiring periodic payments from buyers to sellers; closing proceeds used to discharge second but not first lien on property on assumption that sellers would continue to make payments to satisfy mortgage but sellers thereafter left state after failing to pay off mortgage; court held that sellers' agent owed no duty to advise buyers to consult with counsel before signing contract that contained no provision assuring that sellers would use payments from buyer to pay off mortgage). See also In re Opinion No. 26 of the [_Comm. on the Unauthorized Practice of Law, 654 A.2d 1344, 1350-1351 (N.J.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-VG10-003C-P38D-00000-00&context=1530671) (upholding practice of having seller's broker conduct real-estate closings without presence of lawyers to represent buyer and seller but requiring disclosure of conflicting interests of broker and title insurer; court noted that position of legally unrepresented buyer may be systematically worse than that of seller because, upon closing, seller can ascertain whether payment is received from settlement proceeds, whereas buyer "wants something that is largely incomprehensible to almost all buyers, good and marketable title, one that will not result in problems in the future"; although title insurer has interest aligned with that of buyer, title insurer and mortgagee may be willing to take risk to complete transaction when risk is not one buyer should be willing to take if buyer knew of risk, and unrepresented buyer may not comprehend explicit exceptions from coverage in title-insurance policy).

A staff report conducted by the Federal Trade Commission in 1983 surveyed purchasers of residential property and found that almost threequarters believed that the showing broker represented them as buyers. Very few consumers grounded their stated beliefs in assumptions about the law; they assumed that whoever worked with them represented them. However, findings in the same study also suggest that showing brokers may not act consistently with the expectations that a seller would have in light of the duties imposed on a showing broker as the seller's subagent. Sixty-two percent of buyers reported that the showing broker told them how low a price the broker believed the seller would accept. Sellers may also find it an unwelcome surprise that misrepresentations made by a showing broker carry legal consequences for them as principals in an extended chain of agency relationships that culminates in a subagent with whom a seller may have no direct contact. See Gerard R. Butters, Consumers' Experiences with Real Estate Brokers: A Report on the Consumer Survey of the Federal Trade Commission's Residential Real Estate Brokerage Investigation (Nov. 1983). When two different brokers were involved in a transaction, 74.2 percent of buyers of previously occupied homes reported a belief that the broker who handled the transaction represented them. Only 8.2 percent reported a belief that the broker represented the seller. When only one broker was involved in a transaction, a 34.15 percent plurality of buyers reported a belief that the broker represented the buyer, while 30.6 percent stated that the broker represented both buyer and seller, 18.8 percent stated that the broker represented only the seller, and 16.5 percent stated that the broker represented only the broker. Sixty-two percent of buyers either "strongly agreed" or "agreed" with the statement that: "My agent told me how low he thought the seller would go." The study did not include a question about the confidentiality of sellers' reservation prices. The author observes that "\[t\]hese statistics . . . demonstrate the potential for breaches of confidentiality, but fall short of hard evidence." Id. at 28.

The study of Georgia transactions reflecting the impact of the use of buyer's brokers is Christopher Cur-ran & Joel Schrag, Does It Matter Whom an Agent Serves? Evidence from Recent Changes in Real Estate Agency Law, [_43 J. Law & Econ. 265 (2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:40J7-FSD0-00CV-T0SM-00000-00&context=1530671). The statute is Ga. Code, tit. 10, ch. 6A (1996 & Supp. 2004). Section 10-6A-7 specifies the duties owed by a buyer's broker. A buyer's broker breaches no duty owed a buyer by showing properties to other purchasers, see § 10-6A-7(d), and the buyer's broker may provide specified types of ministerial assistance to the seller, see §§ 10-6A-7(C) and 10-6A-14. For a study suggesting that sellers' agents may take actions that reduce returns to sellers, see Royce R. de Barondes & V. Carlos Slawson, Jr., Examining Compliance with Fiduciary Duties: A Study of Real Estate Agents, [_84 Or.L.Rev. 681 (2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4JCB-BJG0-00CW-C01P-00000-00&context=1530671).

One relatively recent case imposes a writing requirement to establish a relationship of agency between a showing broker and a buyer, at least in a residential transaction. See [_Stortroen v. Beneficial Fin. Co., 736 P.2d 391, 400 (Colo.1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-1120-003D-9524-00000-00&context=1530671) ("Since the multiple listing service is a fact of life in modern real estate practice and since a real estate broker or salesperson operating within a multiple listing service is clearly in a chain of agency to the seller and is prohibited from representing both parties to a real estate sale unless there is a written disclosure of a dual agency and written consent of the parties, we conclude that a real estate broker or salesperson who wishes to act as the agent of a prospective purchaser of a home must establish that agency by a written agreement with the purchaser.").

Statutes expressly permitting the appointment of a licensed real-estate broker as a buyer's agent include: Ala. Code § 34-27-81(15)(a) (1975 & Supp. 2004); Alaska Stat. § 08.88.396 (2004); Cal. Civ. Code § 2079.17 (1993 & Supp. 2005); Colo. Rev. Stat. § 12-62-802(4)(a) and § 12-61-805 (2003 & Supp. 2005); Conn. Gen. Stat. § 20-325a (1999 & Supp. 2005); Fla. Stat. Ann. § 475.278(1) (2005); Ga. Code § 10-6A-7 (2004); Idaho Code § 54-2050(2) (2003 & Supp. 2005); 225 Ill. Comp. Stat. 454/15-15(c) (2005); Ind. Code Ann. § 25-34.1-10-11 (2004); Kan. Stat. Ann. § 58-30, 107 (2004); 1998 Ky. Rev. Stat. & R. Serv. 324.121 (2004); La. Rev. Stat. § 9:3893 (2005); Me. Rev. Stat. Ann. tit. 32, § 13274 (1998 & Supp. 2004); Md. Code Ann. Business, Occupations & Professions § 17-530 (2004 & Supp. 2004); Minn. Stat. Ann. § 82.196 (1999 & Supp. 2005); Mo. Rev. Stat. § 339-710 (2001 & Supp. 2005); Neb. Rev. Stat. § 76-2414 (2003); N.M. Ann. Stat. § 61-29-10.1 (1999 & Supp. 2003); N.Y. Real Prop. L. § 443(1)(c) (2005); Ohio Rev. Code Ann. § 4735.51(o) (2003 & Supp. 2003); Okla. Stat. Ann. tit. 59, § 858-351 (2000 & Supp. 2005); Or. Rev. Stat. § 696.810 (2003); Pa. Stat. Ann. tit. 63, § 455.606 (1996 & Supp. 2005); R.I. Gen. Laws § 5-20.6-1 (2004 & Supp. 2005); S.C. Code Ann. § 40-57-137 (2001 & Supp. 2004); S.D. Cod. Laws § 36-21A-131 (2004 & Supp. 2005); Va. Code § 54.1-2130 (2002 & Supp. 2004); Wash. Rev. Code Ann. § 18.86.010 (2004); Wyo. Stat. Ann. § 33-28-304 (2003). In some states, buyer's agency is permitted by administrative rules. See Ark. Real Estate Comm'n Rules & Regs., Rule 8 (2004); Hawaii Admin. Rules § 16-99-3.1(h) (2004); N.H. Code Admin. R. Ann. Reg. 701.01 (2004); N.C. Admin. Code tit. 21, R. 58A.0104 (2005); N.D. Admin. Code § 70-02-03-15.1 (2005); Vt. Real Estate Comm'n Rules 1.8 (2004); W. Va. Real Estate Comm'n Legis. Rule 174-1-29.2 (2004).

Statutes that require a writing to create a relationship of agency include: Ala. Code § 34-27-82(b) (1975 & Supp. 2005) (providing that after initial contact between consumer and real-estate licensee, licensee will not be considered agent of consumer until parties enter into specific written agreement); Idaho Code §§ 54-2050 and -2084 (2003 & Supp. 2005); Minn. Stat. § 82.195(1) and .196(2) (1999 & Supp. 2005); and N.M. Laws § 61-29-10.1 (1999 & Supp. 2003).

Statutes that provide that subagency shall not follow automatically or shall not be the result of participation in MLS include: Conn. Gen. Stat. § 20-325f (2004) (seller's written consent required to establish subagency with cooperating broker); Georgia, Ga. Code § 10-6A-13 (2004); Haw. Admin. Rules § 16-99-3.1(b)(1) (2004) (after broker obtains listing, seller's authorization is necessary to appoint subagents); Ill. Comp. Stat. 454/15-10 (2005) (broker considered to be representing consumer with whom broker works unless written agreement between that consumer and broker provides otherwise); Ind. Code Ann. § 34.1-10-17 (2004) (subagency created through MLS eliminated); Iowa Code Ann. § 543B.63 (1997 & Supp. 2005) (licensee not to be considered subagent simply as result of participation in MLS; no offers of subagency to be made through MLS); La. Rev. Stat. Ann. § 9:3898 (2005) (subagency may be created only through written agreement; statute does not specify identity of parties to agreement); Neb. Rev. Stat. § 76-2416 (2003) (licensee considered to be limited agent of buyer unless licensee has subagency agreement with another licensee); Ohio Rev. Code Ann. § 4735.64 (2003 & Supp. 2003) (offer of subagency to broker not party to original listing agreement requires prior consent of seller); Tenn. Code Ann. § 62-13-401 (1997 & Supp. 2004) (only written bilateral agreement may "establish the terms" of agency or subagency); Wash. Rev. Code § 18.86.020 (2004) (subagency requires written agreement between listing broker and showing broker). In California, most MLS organizations provide that a selling broker who acts as a cooperating broker shall be treated as the buyer's agent as opposed to the seller's subagent. See Brown et al. at 54-55.

Statutes providing that a showing broker shall, in the absence of a written agreement establishing otherwise, be treated not as an agent or subagent, but as a transaction broker, facilitator, or coordinator include: Ala. Code § 34-27-82 (1975 & Supp. 2004); Colo. Rev. Stat. § 12-61-803 (2003 & Supp. 2005); Kan. Stat. Ann. § 58-30,103 (2004); Mo. Rev. Stat. § 339.710 (2000 & Supp. 2005); Okla. Stat. Ann., tit. 59, § 858-352 (2000 & Supp. 2005).

Statutes that define and permit designated dual agency include: Conn. Gen. Stat. § 20.325a (1999 & Supp. 2005); Ga. Code § 10-6A-12 (1999 & Supp. 2005); Ill. Comp. Stat. 454-15-10 (2005); Kan. Stat. Ann. § 58-30,109 (2004); Ky. Rev. Stat. Ch. 324.121 (2004); La. Rev. Stat. Ann. § 9:3891(8) (2005); Me. Rev. Stat. Ann. § 13271(7) (1999 & Supp. 2004); Md. Code Ann. Bus. Occ. & Prof. § 17-530(4) (2004 & Supp. 2004); N.M. Ann. Stat. § 61-29-10.1 (1999 & Supp. 2003); Pa. Stat. Ann. Tit. 63, § 455.606 (1996 & Supp. 2005); Va. Code Ann. § 54.1-2130 (2002 & Supp. 2004).

Statutes treating a showing broker as seller's subagent include Minn. Stat. Ann. § 82.197(4) (1999 & Supp. 2005); N.Y. Real Prop. L. § 443(1)(h) (2005); N.C. Admin. Code tit. 21, r. 58A.0104 (2005); N.D. Admin. Code § 70-02-03-15.1 (2005); Or. Rev. Stat. § 696.830 (2003) (disclosure statement); R.I. Gen. Laws § 5-20.6-1,2 (2004 & Supp. 2005).

Statutes requiring brokers to disclose whom they represent include Ala. Code § 34-27-82 (1975 & Supp. 2005); Ark. Code Ann. § 17-42-108 (2001 & Supp. 2003); Del. Code Ann. Tit. 18, § 2931 (1999 & Supp. 2004); Me. Rev. Stat. Ann. tit. 32, § 13274 (1999 & Supp. 2004); Mich. Comp. Laws Ann. § 339.2517 (2004 & Supp. 2005); Neb. Rev. Stat. § 76-2415 (2003); Ohio Rev. Code Ann. § 4735.57 (2003 & Supp. 2003); Pa. Stat. Ann. tit. 63, § 455.606 (1996 & Supp. 2005); S.D. Cod. Laws § 36-21A-143 (2004 & Supp. 2005); Va. Code § 54.1-2138 (2002 & Supp. 2004); Wyo. Stat. Ann. § 33-28-301 (2003).

Statutes specifying duties of transaction brokers and other nonagent counterparts include Ala. Code § 34-27-84 (1975 & Supp. 2004); Colo. Rev. Stat. § 12-61-807 (2003 & Supp. 2005); Fla. Stat. Ann. § 457.278(2) (2001 & Supp. 2005); Ga. Code Ann. § 10-6A-3(14) (2004); Kan. Stat. Ann. § 53-30,113 (2004); La. Rev. Stat. § 9:384 (2005); N.H. Code, Admin. R. Ann. Reg. 701.01 (2004); Okla. Stat. Ann. tit. 59, § 858-353 (2000 & Supp. 2005); Pa. Stat. Ann. tit. 63, § 455.606f (1996 & Supp. 2005); S.D. Cod. Laws § 36-21A-144 (2003); Wash. Rev. Code Ann. § 18.86.030 (2004); Wis. Stat. Ann. § 452.133 (1995 & Supp. 2004); Wyo. Stat. Ann. § 33-28-305 (2003). See [_Sussman v. Stoner, 143 F.Supp.2d 1232, 1239 (D. Colo. 2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:437X-PDN0-0038-Y234-00000-00&context=1530671) (holding that purpose of statutory specification of transaction broker's duties in Colo. Rev. Stat. § 12-61-807 would be undermined by judicial imposition of duty to inform seller of rising market value of water shares).

Statutes that mention nonagent actors but do not define their duties include Mich. Comp. Laws Ann. § 339.2517 (1992 & Supp. 2005) ("transaction coordinator"); Minn. Stat. Ann. § 82.197 (1999 & Supp. 2005); N.M. Ann. Stat. § 61-29-10.3 (1999 & Supp. 2003) (duties to be stated by another commission).

On "limited" agents, statutes using the term in the context of dual agents include Ala. Code § 34-27-81(11) (1975 & Supp. 2004); Idaho Code § 54-2088 (2003 & Supp. 2005); Ind. Code Ann. § 25-34.1-10-7 (2004); Neb. Rev. Stat. § 76-2411 (2003). All agents appear to be limited agents under Tenn. Code Ann. § 62-13-402 (1997 & Supp. 2004). A subagent is a limited agent with defined duties under Colo. Rev. Stat. §§ 12-62-804(5)(b) and 12-61-804(1), (2), (3), and (4) (2005 & Supp. 2005) (defining duties). See also Mo. Rev. Stat. §§ 339-730(5) (2001 & Supp. 2005) (subagent working for seller may be a limited agent) and 339-740.1 (buyer's agent is a limited agent); and Fla. Stat. Ann. § 475.278(2)(a) (2005) (transaction agent is a limited agent). On statutes that abrogate the common law of agency, see Ala. Code § 34-27-87 (1975 & Supp. 2004) ("supersedes" common law); 225 Ill. Comp. Stat. 454/15-5 (2005) (applies "to the exclusion of the common law"); S.D. Cod. Laws § 36-21A-149 (2004 & Supp. 2005) (replaces common law only where specified); Va. Code § 54.1-2144 (2002 & Supp. 2004); Wis. Stat. Ann. § 452.139 (1998 & Supp. 2004) (changes common law as specified).

On "designated dual agency," the common-law rule in a majority of jurisdictions is that salespersons and associates who work for a common broker are also agents of the broker's principal. See Pancak et al., 25 Real Estate L.J. at 366. In a few states, the common-law rule permits designated dual agency by treating distinctly the duties of each salesperson. See [_Moser v. Bertram, 858 P.2d 854, 856 (N.M.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W2G0-003D-D24T-00000-00&context=1530671).
