[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 4.02_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-402F-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 4- Ratification_**

**§ 4.02 Effect of Ratification**

**(1) Subject to the exceptions stated in subsection (2), ratification retroactively creates the effects of actual authority.**

**(2) Ratification is not effective:**

**(a) in favor of a person who causes it by misrepresentation or other conduct that would make a contract voidable;**

**(b) in favor of an agent against a principal when the principal ratifies to avoid a loss; or**

**(c) to diminish the rights or other interests of persons, not parties to the transaction, that were acquired in the subject matter prior to the ratification.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Cross-references._ Section 2.01 defines actual authority. A ratification is not effective unless it concerns an act that may be ratified, see § 4.03, and unless the principal has knowledge of material facts as specified in § 4.06. Additionally, the time of the ratification must precede the occurrence of circumstances specified in § 4.05. The principal may be estopped to deny ratification as stated in § 4.08.

_b. Impact and irrevocability._ Ratification has an immediate effect on legal relations between the principal and agent, the principal and the third party, and the agent and the third party. Ratification recasts those legal relations as they would have been had the agent acted with actual authority. Legal consequences thus "relate back" to the time the agent acted. Ratification creates claims not otherwise present, giving the principal and the third party enforceable rights against each other and giving the agent a claim for the commission or compensation to which the agent would have been entitled, had the agent acted with actual authority. Once the principal has ratified the agent's act, the agent is subject to a fiduciary duty to account to the principal as if the agent had acted with actual authority. The impact of a principal's ratification may be limited to a single act or transaction, in contrast to acquiescence by the principal that may be understood by the agent as a manifestation by the principal creating actual authority for similar acts in the future. See § 1.03, Comment _e,_ and § 2.02, Comment _e._

Moreover, with the exception stated in subsection (2)(b), ratification extinguishes claims that the principal would otherwise have against the agent by exonerating the agent. This consequence of ratification is related to the doctrine stated in § 4.07, under which a transaction or act must be ratified in its entirety or not at all. A principal may not, for example, ratify a sale that the agent has made while disaffirming the unauthorized representations made by the agent to effect the sale. The burdens created by the legal consequences of the transaction accompany its benefits. Similarly, legal relations as between agent and principal are affected by whether the agent has acted with actual authority, as are legal relations as between principal and third party. Were the rule otherwise, a principal could ratify a transaction to bind the third party, while reserving the possibility of asserting a claim against the agent for loss if the transaction subsequently becomes disadvantageous to the principal.

**Illustration:**

1\. P, in the business of making loans, employs A to process loan documentation and loan payments. Acting without actual or apparent authority, A tells T, a debtor of P's, that P will file an involuntary bankruptcy petition against T unless T makes all past-due loan payments to P within the next three days. T voluntarily files a petition in bankruptcy. P would be able to prove that, but for A's statement to T, P would have recovered more of the debt that T owes to P. If P ratifies A's conduct, P has no claim against A for the loss A has caused P.

If a ratification occurs, the principal does not have the privilege of unilaterally revoking it. Ratification thus differs from actual authority before the act of an agent, which the principal has unilateral power to revoke, even when the exercise of that power breaches a contract between the principal and the agent. See § 3.10. The reason for the difference is that ratification follows action by the agent or other actor in an interaction with third parties. The effect of actual authority, which the ratification provides, relates to an action that the agent has already taken. In contrast, when a principal revokes actual authority, the principal's withdrawal of consent relates to actions that the agent has not yet taken. Moreover, if the principal and third party agree to rescind the ratification, the rescission does not diminish or eliminate any right the agent has to compensation from the principal, nor does it make the agent liable to the third party. An agreement between principal and agent to rescind a prior ratification made by the principal does not affect rights or obligations of the third party.

_c. Ratification induced by fraud or by conduct that would make a contract voidable._ The exception stated in subsection (2)(a) reflects the general principle that a person who acquires rights through misrepresentation, or through the exercise of duress or undue influence, may not enforce them against the person whose assent was thereby affected or impaired. See Restatement Second, Contracts §§ 164, 175, and 177.

**Illustration:**

2\. Acting without actual or apparent authority, A purchases Blackacre for P from T. T persuades P to affirm the transaction by misrepresenting to P that Blackacre has a valuable stand of hardwood trees. P is not bound to T.

An otherwise-effective ratification eliminates claims that the principal has against the agent, including claims for breach of fiduciary duty. If the principal ratifies a transaction, having the knowledge required by § 4.06, the principal ratifies the agent's conduct in effecting the transaction unless the principal must ratify to avoid a loss. See Comment _d._ The principal thereby consents to the agent's acts, including acts that would otherwise constitute breaches of fiduciary duty. Claims for breach of fiduciary duty are extinguished by the principal's consent to the agent's actions.

_d. Principal ratifies to avoid a loss._ Section 4.01(2) defines broadly the actions that may constitute ratification. When a person's ratification is not indicative of consent, the person responsible for the ratification may not claim the benefit of it against the ratifier. Thus, under subsection (2)(a), ratification is not effective on behalf of one who induces it by conduct that would make a contract voidable. The same general principle underlies subsection (2)(b). In situations governed by this rule, an agent's unauthorized act has placed the principal in a position in which the principal must take affirmative steps to avoid loss. The principal's action is operative as to the principal's legal relations with third parties but, as to legal relations between the principal and the agent, does not exonerate the agent or constitute consent to the agent's actions.

**Illustrations:**

3\. Acting without actual or apparent authority, A lends P's money to T. T becomes insolvent, and P files a claim in T's bankruptcy proceeding for the money lent by A. By filing the claim, P does not release A from liability to P.

4\. P appoints A as a financial manager. Without actual or apparent authority, A invests P's funds in overseas property. To protect the value of the property, P files a protest against a proposed governmental taking of the property. The filing does not release A from liability to P.

_e. Rights or interests of persons not parties to transaction._ Ratification does not operate to destroy rights created between the time of the agent's act and the principal's affirmance except the third party's claim against the agent for breach of the warranties of authority, which ratification eliminates. See § 6.10. An affirmance that results in ratification is subject to rights acquired by other persons prior to the affirmance.

**Illustration:**

5\. P owns Blackacre. A forges P's name on a lease of Blackacre to T that contains an option to purchase. P then enters into a contract to sell Blackacre to S. Thereafter, P ratifies A's forgery of P's name on the lease and T wishes to exercise the purchase option. S's contract to buy Blackacre is specifically enforceable. P may be liable to T for money damages for breach of contract.

If other law provides rules for priority of rights, that other law governs. See, e.g., U.C.C. §§ 9-322 and 9-509 and Comment 3 to § 9-509 (last sentence).

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ Subsection (1) is comparable in substance to Restatement Second, Agency §§ 100 and 100 A. Subsection (2)(a) is a broader formulation than Restatement Second, Agency § 101(a), which denies the benefit of ratification to one who causes an affirmance "by misrepresentation or duress." The counterpart to subsection (2)(b), Restatement Second, Agency § 101(b), bars the effect of ratification "in favor of the agent against the principal if the principal is obliged to affirm in order to protect his own interests." Subsection (2)(b) uses instead the formulation, "when the principal ratifies to avoid a loss" in the interest of clarity. The revocation of a ratification, addressed in Comment _b,_ is given the same substantive treatment by Restatement Second, Agency § 102.

_b. Impact and irrevocability._ For the distinction between the effect of ratification and the creation of actual authority through a principal's acquiescence, see [_United States v. Fulcher, 188 F.Supp.2d 627, 636 (W.D.Va. 2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4592-B7V0-0038-Y24C-00000-00&context=1530671).

On the impact of ratification on claims the principal would otherwise have against an agent, see Cathay Pac. Airways, Ltd. v. Fly & See [_Travel, Inc., 3 F.Supp.2d 443, 455 (S.D.N.Y.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SND-D960-0038-Y3TF-00000-00&context=1530671) (by knowingly accepting benefit of practice, airlines ratified agent's unauthorized practice of issuing tickets using a fictitious point of origin to obtain favorable currency conversion rates; agent not subject to liability to principal); [_National Inspection & Repair, Inc. v. Valley Forge Life Ins. Co., 56 P.3d 807, 820-823 (Kan.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:474W-S560-0039-42CG-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurer ratified agent's unauthorized acceptance of application and premium for policy of key-man life insurance; agent not subject to liability to insurer); [_Barta v. Kindschuh, 518 N.W.2d 98, 100-101 (Neb.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-4DS0-003D-B0YJ-00000-00&context=1530671) (sellers of property ratified agent's written misrepresentations concerning condition of roof, releasing agent from liability to sellers for damage stemming from purchasers' suit against them).

_c. Ratification induced by fraud or by conduct that would make a contract voidable._ Illustration 2 is a simplified version of Restatement Second, Agency § 101, Illustration 1. Cf. [_Harstad v. Frol, 704 P.2d 638, 642 (Wash.App.1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-XH00-003F-W2D3-00000-00&context=1530671) (owner of building did not ratify actions of real-estate broker who did not disclose his ownership interest in prospective purchaser; when that purchaser was unable to obtain financing, broker induced owner's assent to sale to different purchaser, telling owner it would be necessary to pay debt incurred by initial purchaser to renovate building; court holds owner affirmed to protect own interests and that broker's failure to disclose his interest precluded broker from relying on estoppel theory against owner).

For the proposition that a principal's informed ratification of a transaction is effective as to claims for breach of fiduciary duty the principal may have against the agent in connection with the transaction, see [_Jaksich v. Thomson McKinnon Secs., Inc., 582 F.Supp. 485, 502 (S.D.N.Y. 1984)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-GYS0-0054-513S-00000-00&context=1530671) (broker's customer accepted benefit of unauthorized transaction with knowledge of relevant facts; customer may not assert claim of breach of fiduciary duty, based on alleged churning of account, against broker); cf. [_Bullock v. State, 966 P.2d 1215, 1220 (Utah App.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3TWG-W3R0-0039-41SY-00000-00&context=1530671) (partner who ratified unauthorized sale of partnership property, arranged by fellow partners, released claim against fellow partners for their breach of partnership agreement). Contrary authority may be localized to Texas. See _Spangler v. Jones, 861 S.W.2d 392 (Tex.App.1993)_; [_William B. Roberts, Inc. v. McDrilling Co., 579 S.W.2d 335, 339 (Tex.App.1979)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-5BV0-003C-2199-00000-00&context=1530671). See also _Spangler, 861 S.W.2d at 400_ (McGarry, C.J., dissenting) (key question is whether principal had full knowledge of agent's fraudulent acts at time of ratification).

_d. Principal ratifies to avoid a loss._ For the basic proposition, and facts comparable to those of Illustration 3, see _Pacific Vinegar & Pickle Works v. Smith, 93 P. 85 (Cal.1907)_. See also [_Bingham v. Zolt, 823 F. Supp. 1126, 1133 (S.D.N.Y.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-MD60-001T-6397-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_66 F.3d 553 (2d Cir.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXN-WVV0-001T-D184-00000-00&context=1530671) (ratification of unauthorized actions of legal and financial advisors did not occur; principal took actions only to protect its own interests and avoid further loss); [_Williams v. National Housing Exch., Inc., 949 F.Supp. 646, 650 (N.D.Ill.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-6K30-006F-P27M-00000-00&context=1530671) (receiver for purchaser of collateralized mortgage debentures and indenture trustee did not ratify servicer's unauthorized appointment of subservicer of mortgages securing debentures by accepting remittances of mortgage payments from subservicer when receiver and trustee had right and obligation to accept payments); [_Apple Glen Crossing, LLC v. Trademark Retail, Inc., 784 N.E.2d 484, 489 (Ind.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4840-2GG0-0039-4345-00000-00&context=1530671) (payment by LLC's majority owner of obligation to construction contractor, incurred by LLC's manager without authority did not waive LLC's claims against manager when necessary to protect LLC's interests).

There is no precise definition of action necessary to protect one's own interests or avoid loss. See, e.g., [_Delano v. Kitch, 663 F.2d 990, 999 (10th Cir.1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-YDJ0-0039-W2D2-00000-00&context=1530671), cert. denied, _456 U.S. 946 (1982)_ (minority shareholders did not ratify action of corporate attorney who extracted 3% finder's fee for arranging sale of shares when minority learned of sale contract only after majority shareholders agreed to it, had only 10 days to decide, and if they failed to sell would become minority shareholders in company controlled by newspaper conglomerate as opposed to shareholders in family corporation with no one controlling shareholder).

_e. Rights or interests of persons not parties to transaction._ Only rights of third parties are relevant for this purpose. See [_Swain v. Wiley Coll., 74 S.W.3d 143, 151 (Tex.App. 2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45K1-JX00-0039-402H-00000-00&context=1530671) (college's president was not "third party" to ratification by college's board of directors of its prior decision not to extend president's contract; date on which president's right accrued to automatic renewal of contract preceded ratification). Illustration 5 is based on [_Lissau v. Smith, 138 A.2d 381, 386 (Md.1958)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S12-2T60-003D-Y345-00000-00&context=1530671). In _Lissau,_ the court held that it was not possible for the principal to ratify the initial transaction, following the intervening contract. The lessees in _Lissau_ knew at the time they attempted to exercise the option to purchase that the lessors had contracted to sell the property to others. The lessees thus were not bona fide purchasers who acquired rights. [_Id. at 386_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S12-2T60-003D-Y345-00000-00&context=1530671). In the insurance context, another application of the underlying principle prohibits ratification by an insured of a nonpermitted use of a vehicle following the occurrence of a loss. See [_Allied Mut. Ins. Co. v. Webb, 111 Cal.Rptr.2d 426, 430 (Cal.App.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:43VS-C9Y0-0039-424Y-00000-00&context=1530671).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 4.03_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-402G-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 4- Ratification_**

**§ 4.03 Acts That May Be Ratified**

**A person may ratify an act if the actor acted or purported to act as an agent on the person's behalf.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Cross-references._ Ratification may create a relationship of agency when none existed before. See § 4.01, Comment _b._

_b. Acting or purporting to act as an agent._ A person may ratify the act of an agent or the act of a person who purported to be an agent but was not. When an actor is not an agent and does not purport to be one, the agency-law doctrine of ratification is not a basis on which another person may become subject to the legal consequences of the actor's conduct. Other bodies of law govern the circumstances under which such a consequence might occur.

The formulation in this section does not distinguish among disclosed principals, unidentified principals, and undisclosed principals. It is contrary to the rule in Restatement Second, Agency § 85(1), which states that an act may be ratified only if the actor purported to act as an agent. That rule limited ratification to situations in which the principal was disclosed or unidentified. For definitions of these terms, see § 1.04(2). In contrast, under the formulation in this section, an undisclosed principal may ratify an agent's unauthorized act. This is consistent with the result reached in a number of relatively recent cases but inconsistent with the often-stated (but rarely directly dispositive) proposition that it is a requisite for ratification that the actor have purported to act as an agent.

It is not necessary so to limit the availability of ratification. First, the limitation is not necessary to prevent a person who is a stranger to a transaction from attempting to claim its benefit by ratifying. An undisclosed principal is not a stranger to the agent. Second, whether to ratify is a choice an undisclosed principal should be free to make. An undisclosed principal's ratification creates rights and obligations applicable to principal, agent, and third party, subject to the limits generally applicable to protect third parties stated in §§ 4.05 and 4.07. Finally, the fact that a third party does not expect the undisclosed principal's liability at the time the agent acts is no more persuasive an argument against ratification than it is against the general proposition that a third party may hold an undisclosed principal liable. See § 6.03, Comment _b._ The third party is not disadvantaged by recognizing ratification by an undisclosed principal. When a principal is disclosed or unidentified, and the agent's act does not bind the principal, the principal's ratification may result in the loss of claims the third party could otherwise assert against the agent for breach of warranties of authority. See § 4.01, Comment _b._ On claims for breach of warranties of authority, see § 6.10. Offsetting the loss is the principal's assent to be bound by the agent's act. When a principal is undisclosed, the third party has no claims for breach of warranties of authority against the agent. An undisclosed principal's ratification does not eliminate the agent's liability to the third party on the transaction because an agent for an undisclosed principal has such liability when acting with actual authority. See § 6.03. The effect of ratification is to add the undisclosed principal as a party with rights and obligations stemming from the transaction. See § 6.03.

**Illustration:**

1\. P is the owner of a residential property. P's spouse, A, has actual authority from P to negotiate terms to lease the property without disclosing the fact of P's ownership. A negotiates such terms with T. Under applicable state law, the Statute of Frauds is inapplicable to the lease transaction. Acting without actual authority, A agrees to lease the residence to T on those terms. Thereafter, prior to the formal closing contemplated by the agreement, T visits the residence and meets P but does not realize that P owns the property. P acquiesces in T undertaking renovation and landscaping work on the property. Prior to the scheduled closing with T, P agrees to lease the property to S. By permitting T to undertake work on the property, P ratified the agreement to lease made by A.

_c. Forgeries._ One underpinning of the "purported to act" requirement was the doctrine that a forgery could not be ratified because a forger purports to act, not for someone else, but as someone else. The doctrine was not uniformly supported by the cases, with some cases holding ratification by a principal to be effective although it did not relieve the forger of criminal liability. U.C.C. § 3-403(a) resolves the question by providing that for all purposes under Article 3, an unauthorized signature may be ratified, even that of a forger. Official Comment 3 acknowledges that a forger is not an agent. However, the person whose name is signed may retroactively adopt the forger's signature as the person's own. The retroactive adoption carries the consequences of ratification. Like ratification, it is a unilateral expression of a person's consent, and like ratification it does not require consideration to be enforceable. It may be in the principal's interest to ratify a forgery to obtain the benefit of a transaction not otherwise available.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ The rule stated in this section is contrary to the rule stated in Restatement Second, Agency § 85(1).

_b. Acting or purporting to act as an agent._ Cases supporting the rule stated in this section include [_Coyle v. Smith, 300 So. 2d 738 (Fla.Dist.App. 1974)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-6VR0-003C-W2GV-00000-00&context=1530671) (parents bought property from son without recording deed; after son sold property on parents' behalf but under his own sign and seal, sellers rescinded when Disney bought property located nearby; court holds principals will be bound if principals ratified and remands for determination whether ratification occurred); [_Young & Rubicam, Inc. v. Ticket Holding Mktg., Inc., 1989 WL 4210 (N.D.Ill. 1989)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-D780-0054-4083-00000-00&context=1530671) (ratification occurred when principal with knowledge of facts of unauthorized transaction took position inconsistent with nonaffirmance; principal's relationship with and control over persons who dealt with third party appears not to have been known at time of dealings); [_Acuri v. Figliolli, 398 N.Y.S.2d 923 (Dist.1977)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-GV70-003C-F4NG-00000-00&context=1530671) (son sold car owned by mother to plaintiff without disclosing mother's ownership; court holds mother's receipt of checks from son ratified transaction). For scholarly support, see Arnold Rochvarg, Ratification and Undisclosed Principals, 34 McGill L.J. 287 (1989).

The rule stated in this section is not formally consistent with the requirement, stated in some cases, that an act may not be ratified "unless it appears that the act was performed for and on behalf of another, and not on account of the actor himself." [_Dominion Invs. v. Yasechko, 767 F.Supp. 1460, 1468 (N.D.Ind.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-CD40-001T-72TW-00000-00&context=1530671) (dictum; majority shareholder was acting for and on behalf of corporation when he entered into agreement to purchase real estate).

Illustration 1 is based on a variation on the facts of Spiro v. Lintern, \[1973\] 1 W.L.R. 1002 (App.1973). Spiro involved a sale of property, not a lease as in the Illustration. The opinion assumes, on the authority of Keighley, Maxsted & Co. v. Durant \[1901\] A.C. 240, that an undisclosed principal may not ratify an agent's unauthorized transaction. The court resolves the problem in _Spiro_ by holding that the owner-spouse was under a duty to disclose that the agent-spouse acted without authority to the person who believed he purchased the property from the agent-spouse. His failure so to disclose "amounted to a representation by conduct that she had that authority." \[1973\] 1 W.L.R. at 1012. Since the agent-spouse did not represent herself to be an agent at all, the owner-spouse's conduct does not materially appear to make any implicit representation about the other spouse's authority as an agent.

_c. Forgeries._ The leading authorities holding that a forged signature may be ratified are [_Rakestraw v. Rodrigues, 500 P.2d 1401 (Cal.1972)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRK-HH60-003C-H0GG-00000-00&context=1530671) and [_President, Directors & Co. of Greenfield Bank v. Crafts, 86 Mass. 447 (1862)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WFN-80Y0-00KR-F1CW-00000-00&context=1530671). For authority contra, see [_Funds for Bus. Growth, Inc. v. Woodland Marble & Tile Co., 278 A.2d 922, 925 (Pa.1971)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-T6H0-003C-M1TR-00000-00&context=1530671) (forgery of nonnegotiable instrument not ratifiable because it is a crime). Most contemporary courts recognize that a forgery may be ratified because pretending to be the principal is not so different from pretending to have authority to act on behalf of the principal. See J. Dennis Hynes & Mark J. Loewenstein, Agency, Partnership, and the LLC: The Law of Unincorporated Business Enterprises 440 (6th ed. 2003) ("from the third party's perspective, acting _as_ the victim is not so different from purporting to act _on behalf of_ the victim").

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 4.04_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-402H-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 4- Ratification_**

**§ 4.04 Capacity to Ratify**

**(1) A person may ratify an act if**

**(a) the person existed at the time of the act, and**

**(b) the person had capacity as defined in § 3.04 at the time of ratifying the act.**

**(2) At a later time, a principal may avoid a ratification made earlier when the principal lacked capacity as defined in § 3.04.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Cross-references._ "Person" is defined in § 1.04(5).

_b. Capacity to be a principal._ Ratification under §§ 4.01 and 4.02 replicates the legal consequences of actual authority after the fact of action taken by an agent or a person who purported to be an agent. See § 4.03. Capacity to ratify requires that the would-be ratifier have capacity to act as a principal in a relationship of agency, which under § 3.04 requires capacity, as to delegable acts and transactions, to have legal relations affected by the act if done in person. The agent's capacity neither augments nor diminishes that of the principal. Under § 3.04, the relevant time to determine whether the principal has capacity is the time the agent acts. In contrast, the relevant time to determine whether the principal has capacity to ratify is the time of ratification. This is because ratification requires an externally observable indication of assent to have legal relations affected by the act done earlier and to give such assent requires capacity.

It is not necessary for ratification that the principal have had capacity as well at the time of the act that the ratification concerns. See Comment _c_ for discussion of principals not in existence at the time of the act. For example, under subsection (1)(b) a minor may ratify contracts when the minor attains legal majority. This is so even though the minor lacked capacity to enter into a binding contract at the time the agent acted. Likewise, if a personal representative has been appointed for a principal, the personal representative may ratify on behalf of the principal although the principal lacked capacity at the prior time of the act that is ratified.

Earlier statements of ratification doctrine were more stringent on this score, requiring that the principal have had capacity at the time of the original act as well as at the time of ratification. See Restatement Second, Agency § 84(1). There were two rationales for the restriction. The first was that ratification's legal consequences relate back to the time of the original act. See id., Comment a. Contemporary cases do not support restricting ratification on this basis. The second rationale was that, if an act carried no legal consequences because it was illegal, it could not be ratified because it could not have been initially authorized by the principal. See id. § 86(1). In contrast, within this Restatement, whether an action taken by an agent is illegal is not treated as a question of the principal's capacity.

_c. Nonexistent principals, including corporations yet to be formed._ Under the rule stated in subsection (1)(a), a person not in existence at the time of an act or transaction may not subsequently ratify it. Instead, a person may elect to become bound under such circumstances by adopting what was done prior to the person's existence. Adoption operates analogously to ratification because it requires assent or affirmance on the part of the ratifier. Unlike ratification, adoption does not have a relation-back effect. Additionally, an adoption, unlike a novation, does not itself release obligors from liabilities created by the original transaction.

**Illustration:**

1\. A, acting on behalf of P Corporation, which is not yet incorporated, enters into an oral employment contract for a term of 18 months with B. Once formed, P Corporation adopts the contract 13 months after A made the contract with B. The applicable Statute of Frauds requires that contracts not to be performed within one year of their making, including contracts of employment, be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. B's contract is enforceable notwithstanding the Statute of Frauds because P Corporation's adoption does not relate back to the time of the original contract.

This limit on ratification has a long lineage in disputes involving transactions made by promoters on behalf of corporations that have not yet been formed. Comparable questions may arise concerning promoters' transactions on behalf of not-yet-formed limited partnerships and limited-liability companies. A corporation should not be bound by the terms of a contract made prior to its existence until its own mechanisms of governance are in place and able to assess the merits of the transaction. A promoter's interests are often not identical to the interests of those who own equity in a corporation once it is formed.

If a promoter enters into a contract with a third party on behalf of a corporation that has not yet commenced legal existence, the corporation itself cannot be a party to the contract prior to its existence. This is so whether the contract purports to be made on behalf of a corporation characterized as one yet to be formed or characterizes the corporation as one presently in existence. If the contract is to bind the third party prior to the existence of the corporation, unless the relationship is structured as an option that may be exercised by the corporation when formed, either the promoter or someone else must be liable on the contract. The parties' intention must often be determined on the basis of inferences to be drawn from the circumstances, which include whether the promoter and the other party are aware that the corporation does not yet exist. A third party may agree in a contract made by a promoter to release the promoter's liability when and if the corporation adopts the contract. In the absence of an express agreement by the third party to release the promoter, some cases permit the promoter to establish that the parties intended that the promoter would not be personally liable. A corporation may adopt a contract made by a promoter by accepting its benefits with knowledge of its terms.

This doctrine has the potential to carry harsh consequences for promoters when a third party does not agree to release a promoter's individual liability when and if the corporation adopts the contract. The third party may determine that the corporation's ability to perform under the contract looks risky and decline to release the promoter or, regardless of its assessment of prospects of the corporation's performance, the third party may prefer to have more rather than fewer obligors on the contract. When a third party knows at the time of contracting with a promoter that the corporation has not yet been formed, the promoter's conduct has not deceived the third party. Moreover, even if the corporation agrees to indemnify the promoter against loss stemming from the promoter's individual liability, the indemnity will not protect the promoter if the corporation is insolvent and cannot pay when the promoter seeks to be indemnified.

Viewed in a broader perspective, the risk of individual liability may be beneficial because it encourages promoters to complete the formal requisites for incorporation sooner rather than later. The process requisite to incorporation under contemporary corporation statutes is not cumbersome. Moreover, duly completing the process of forming a corporation creates a publicly accessible document, filed with the state, that furnishes useful albeit minimal information, including the corporation's name and the name and address of the agent it has designated to receive service of process. Such a formal record provides a source of information that enables the state to enforce applicable law, as well as enabling private parties to pursue legal process.

Provisions in corporation statutes reflect different judgments about how contractual risk should be allocated as between promoter and third party when a contract purports to be made on behalf of a not-yet-formed corporation as if it had present existence. Several states have adopted verbatim the language of § 2.04 of the Model Business Corporation Act, which provides that "\[a\]ll persons purporting to act as or on behalf of a corporation, knowing there was no incorporation under this act, are jointly and severally liable for all liabilities created while so acting." In a few states, a promoter must have "actual knowledge" that there was no incorporation, while in several other states, the promoter is liable whether or not the promoter knows that the corporation has not yet been incorporated. A few states excuse a promoter from liability when the promoter believes in good faith that incorporation has occurred or do not permit a person "who also knew that there was no incorporation" to hold the promoter liable.

A court may determine that a third party should be estopped from seeking a promoter's personal liability. For example, if a promoter is reluctant to enter into a contract personally but a third party persuades the promoter to contract in the name of a nonexistent corporation, the third party has induced the promoter to take action the promoter would likely not have taken otherwise.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ This section covers the subject matter of Restatement Second, Agency §§ 84(1) and 86(1). This section differs in substance because it does not require that a principal have had capacity at the time of the act to be ratified as well as at the time of ratifying it. Restatement Second, Agency § 326 also deals specifically with preincorporation contracts.

_b. Capacity to be a principal._ For the proposition that a minor may ratify an unauthorized transaction upon attaining majority, see [_Sabourin v. LBC, Inc., 731 F.Supp. 1151, 1155 (D.R.I.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-99J0-0054-417N-00000-00&context=1530671) (father lacked authority to agree to settlement and release of claims on behalf of son because mother had custody of son; settlement agreement, which was voidable by son upon attaining majority, was ratified by son). On ratification following recovery from incapacitation, see [_Wilcox Mfg. Group, Inc. v. Marketing Servs. of Indiana, Inc., 832 N.E.2d 559, 563 (Ind.App.2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4GV9-5YC0-TVTW-B22X-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporation's president initially signed loan agreement while experiencing impaired cognitive function due to lithium toxicity; agreement ratified when corporation continued to make loan payments after president "had recovered his ability to understand the nature of the contract upon which his company was making payments").

On subsequent ratification by a personal representative, see [_Henritzy v. General Elec. Co., 451 N.W.2d 558, 561-562 (Mich.App.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-CP00-003D-62KH-00000-00&context=1530671) (personal representative of deceased principal could ratify act of attorney who entered into settlement agreement on behalf of principal, not knowing she had died); [_Blackhurst v. Transamerica Ins. Co., 699 P.2d 688 (Utah 1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-58T0-003G-F287-00000-00&context=1530671) (special administrator of estate ratified settlement previously reached on behalf of principal by her attorney and son, who may have lacked authority).

Some cases state in dictum that ratification requires capacity at the time of the act as well as at the time of ratification. See [_Franklin Sav. Assoc. v. Director of Office of Thrift Supervision, 740 F.Supp. 1535, 1539 (D.Kan.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-8D50-0054-43KP-00000-00&context=1530671) (dictum that public officer who lacked power to appoint conservator at time of appointment may not ratify appointment at later time; court holds that public officer was not principal and lacked power to ratify in any event); [_In re New York State Med. Transporters Ass'n v. Perales, 566 N.E.2d 134, 138 (N.Y.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S2R-8XH0-003V-B3F6-00000-00&context=1530671) (principal cannot ratify contract that it could not authorize because contract is illegal). Some earlier authority requires only capacity at the time of ratification. For example, it is stated that "\[u\]pon the same principles which govern the creation of agency in the first instance, there can be no ratification of the unauthorized act of an agent if, at the time of the alleged ratification, the principal is insane." 1 Floyd R. Mechem, A Treatise on the Law of Agency 273 (2d ed. 1914). The next sentence states that "\[o\]n the other hand, one on whose behalf an act has been done during his insanity, may after his sanity is restored, ratify the act." Id.

_c. Nonexistent principals, including corporations yet to be formed._ Illustration 1 is based on [_McArthur v. Times Printing Co., 51 N.W. 216, 217 (1892)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WSM-5M20-00KR-C24F-00000-00&context=1530671).

For the proposition that a corporation may adopt preincorporation transactions, see [_Illinois Controls, Inc. v. Langham, 639 N.E.2d 771, 780 (Ohio 1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-Y030-003C-82R3-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Madeja v. Olympic Packer, LLC, 155 F.Supp.2d 1183, 1204 (D.Hawaii 2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:43PR-RG90-0038-Y2MG-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_310 F.3d 628 (9th Cir.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4743-KG70-0038-X3HG-00000-00&context=1530671) (holding that charter agreement was not void because made prior to incorporation of company that chartered vessel; either corporation or individual who signed charter on its behalf as president would be required to perform terms of charter).

Cases holding that a corporation once formed may "adopt" but not ratify promoters' contracts include [_Commissioners of Lewes v. Breakwater Fisheries Co., 117 A. 823 (Del.Ch. 1922)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XT0-X110-003C-K1PH-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Davis & Rankin Bldg. & Mfg. Co. v. Hillsboro Creamery Co., 37 N.E. 549 (Ind.App.1894)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3X0T-1WC0-00KR-C515-00000-00&context=1530671); [_McCrillis v. A & W Enters., Inc., 155 S.E.2d 281 (N.C.1967)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-YRB0-003G-00G3-00000-00&context=1530671). The sole authority to the contrary appears to be [_Stanton v. New York & E.Ry. Co., 22 A. 300 (Conn.1890)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WM5-2KS0-00KR-C1CY-00000-00&context=1530671).

Some opinions use the terms "ratification" and "adoption" interchangeably when no operative consequences follow. See [_Yost v. Early, 589 A.2d 1291, 1300 (Md.App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RVM-0DN0-003G-201R-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Eden Temp. Servs., Inc. v. House of Excellence, Inc., 704 N.Y.S.2d 239, 240 (App.Div.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YS3-VJ30-0039-449D-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Coastal Shutters & Insulation, Inc. v. Derr, 809 S.W.2d 916, 920 (Tex.App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-22V0-003C-249H-00000-00&context=1530671).

Cases holding that a promoter retains individual liability despite the corporation's adoption of the contract include [_Allen Steel Supply Co. v. Bradley, 402 P.2d 394 (Idaho 1965)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-29K0-003D-310M-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Jacobson v. Stern, 605 P.2d 198 (Nev. 1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXR-3TW0-003D-C28V-00000-00&context=1530671). This gives the third party "double security." See [_Carle v. Corhan, 103 S.E. 699, 702 (Va.1920)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W3N0-003D-5512-00000-00&context=1530671); Eddie Flores, The Case for Eliminating Promoter Liability on Preincorporation Agreements, 32 Ariz. L. Rev. 405, 407 (1990).

Cases looking to the parties' intention to determine whether a promoter is personally liable include [_Quaker Hill, Inc. v. Parr, 364 P.2d 1056 (Colo. 1961)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-WX60-0040-011P-00000-00&context=1530671) (no intention to hold promoters personally liable when contract signed in name of corporation and other party knew corporation not in existence); [_Tin Cup Pass Ltd. P'ship v. Daniels, 553 N.E.2d 82 (Ill.App. 1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-4H60-003D-H221-00000-00&context=1530671) (holding promoters not liable when lease signed in name of corporation that both parties knew had not been formed); [_Company Stores Dev. Corp. v. Pottery Warehouse, Inc., 733 S.W.2d 886, 887 (Tenn.App.1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-5S20-003V-D4GM-00000-00&context=1530671) (holding promoter not liable on lease when lessor agreed to look only to named lessee as "'a corporation to be formed'").

The burden of showing intention is allocated to the promoter in [_Goodman v. Darden, Doman & Stafford Assocs., 670 P.2d 648, 651 (Wash.1983)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W6Y0-003F-W2B5-00000-00&context=1530671).

The Official Comment to Model Business Corporation Act § 2.04 indicates that its drafters intended to cover a variety of situations in which action is taken purportedly on behalf of a corporation that does not yet exist, including (1) cases in which those acting on behalf of the corporation honestly and reasonably--but erroneously--believe it to exist; (2) cases in which those acting on behalf of the corporation have mailed organizational documents to the state that are not filed in timely fashion; (3) cases in which the other party to the contract knows the corporation does not yet exist but nonetheless presses for execution of the contract in the corporation's name; (4) cases in which the other party to the contract deals with one who represents that the corporation exists, knowing that it does not; and (5) cases in which passive investors furnish funds to a promoter with the caution not to commence doing business until incorporation has been completed.

Statutes representing verbatim adoptions of § 2.04 of the Model Business Corporation Act include Ala. Code § 10-2B-2.04 (1999 & Supp. 2004); Ark. Code Ann. § 4-27-204 (2001 & Supp. 2003); Conn. Gen. Stat. Ann. § 33-638 (1997 & Supp. 2005); Ga. Code Ann. § 14-2-204 (1996 & Supp. 2004); Ind. Code Ann. § 23-1-21-4 (1999 & Supp. 2004); Iowa Code Ann. § 490.204 (1999 & Supp. 2005); Ky. Rev. Stat. Ann. § 271B.2-040 (2003 & Supp. 2004); Miss. Code Ann. § 79-4-2.04 (1999 & Supp. 2004); Mo. Ann. Stat. § 351.053 (2001 & Supp. 2005); Nev. Rev. Stat. § 21-2020 (2003); N.H. Rev. Stat. Ann. § 293-A:2.04 (1999 & Supp. 2004); Or. Rev. Stat. § 60.054 (2003); Utah Code Ann. § 16-10a-204 (2001 & Supp. 2004); Vt. Stat. Ann. tit. 11A, § 2.04 (1997 & Supp. 2004); Wyo. Stat. Ann. § 17-16-204 (2003 & Supp. 2004).

Statutes requiring that the promoter have "actual knowledge" that there was no incorporation are Ariz. Rev. Stat. § 10-204 (2005); Fla. Stat. Ann. § 607.0204 (2001 & Supp. 2005).

Statutes with no knowledge requirement at all are Alaska Stat. § 10.06.220 (2004); Idaho Code § 30-1-204 (2005). In a few states, the statute provides that "\[a\]ll persons who assume to act as a corporation without the authority to do so shall be jointly and severally liable for all debts and liabilities incurred or arising therefrom." This language comes from § 146 of the 1969 Model Business Corporation Act. 805 Ill. Comp. Stat. Ann. 5/3.20 (2004 & Supp. 2005); N.M. Stat. Ann. § 53-18-9 (2001 & Supp. 2003); N.D. Cent. Code § 10-19.1-29 (2001 & Supp. 2003); R.I. Gen. Laws § 7-1.1-13 (repealed eff. July, 2005).

Statutes providing that a promoter shall not be liable "to any person who also knew that there was no incorporation" are Tenn. Code Ann. § 48-12-104 (2002 & Supp. 2004); Va. Code § 13.1-622 (1999 & Supp. 2005); Wash. Rev. Code Ann. § 23B.02.040 (2004).

This doctrine also has a long lineage in England, beginning with Kelner v. Baxter, (1866) L.R. 2 C.P. 174, which held that a promoter who contracted, in his own name expressly for and on behalf of a corporation to be formed, had personal liability on the contract. However, Newborne v. Sensolid (Great Britain) Ltd., \[1954\] 1 Q.B. 45 (C.A.) held that no contract at all was formed when a promoter signed the proposed name of the company on the contract, adding his own name to authenticate the signature. English law changed with the United Kingdom's entry into the European Community. To implement Article 7 of the Community's First Company Law Directive, Parliament enacted what is now § 36c of the Companies Act 1985. It provides that "\[a\] contract which purports to be made by or on behalf of a company at a time when the company has not been formed, has effect, subject to any agreement to the contrary, as one made with the person purporting to act for the company or as agent for it, and he is personally liable on the contract accordingly." The legislation, like the Directive, does not address how a company may adopt a contract made on its behalf by a promoter. A formal novation appears to be necessary. See Paul L. Davies, Gower's Principles of Modern Company Law 144 (6th ed. 1997) (noting that "\[a\]t present, the only way in which the company can adopt \[contract\] is by entering into a post-incorporation agreement in the same terms. Even if the company does so, that will not relieve the promoters of personal liability (at any rate while the new agreement remains executory) unless they are parties to the new agreement which expressly relieves them of liability under the pre-incorporation agreement.").

Ontario's Business Corporation Act contains a more elaborate treatment of preincorporation contracts. Under § 21(1), a person who enters into an oral or written contract on behalf of a corporation prior to its existence "is personally bound by the contract and is entitled to the benefits thereof," except as provided otherwise in the section. After a corporation comes into existence, under § 21(2) within a reasonable time it may adopt any prior contract made in its name or on its behalf; the corporation then "is bound by the contract and is entitled to the benefits thereof as if the corporation had been in existence at the date of the contract and had been a party thereto," and the person who made the contract for the corporation "ceases" to be bound by it or entitled to its benefits, except as provided in subsection (3). Under subsection (3), a party to a contract may apply to a court for an order "fixing obligations under a contract" as joint or joint and several, or apportioning liability, between the corporation and the person who purported to act on its behalf. Under subsection (4), a person who purports to act on behalf of a corporation-yet-to-be-formed "is not in any event bound by the contract or entitled to the benefits thereof" if the contract expressly so provides. The Ontario statute and cases applying it raise a number of questions, including when language in a contract constitutes an express disclaimer of a promoter's individual liability under subsection (4) and the legal consequences of a successful disclaimer by a promoter in the period prior to a corporation's adoption of a prior contract made on its behalf. See Wilfred M. Estey, Pre-Incorporation Contracts: The Fog is Finally Lifting, 33 Can. Bus. L.J. 3 (2000); Jacob Ziegel, Preincorporation Contracts: A Further Comment on 1394918 Ontario Ltd. v. 1310210 Ontario Inc., 37 Can. Bus. L.J. 445 (2002).

On estoppel against a third party who persuades a promoter to execute a contract immediately in a corporation's name, knowing that it has not been formed, see [_Quaker Hill, Inc. v. Parr, 364 P.2d 1056 (Colo.1961)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-WX60-0040-011P-00000-00&context=1530671). See also Rev. Model Bus. Corp. Act § 2.04, Official Comment (1999) (stating that "\[w\]hile no special provision is made in section 2.04, the section does not foreclose the possibility that persons who urge defendants to execute contracts in the corporate name knowing that no steps to incorporate have been taken may be estopped to impose personal liability on individual defendants. This estoppel may be based on the inequity perceived when persons, unwilling or reluctant to enter into a commitment under their own name, are persuaded to use the name of a nonexistent corporation, and then are sought to be held personally liable under section 2.04 by the party advocating that form of execution.").

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 4.05_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-402J-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 4- Ratification_**

**§ 4.05 Timing of Ratification**

**A ratification of a transaction is not effective unless it precedes the occurrence of circumstances that would cause the ratification to have adverse and inequitable effects on the rights of third parties. These circumstances include:**

**(1) any manifestation of intention to withdraw from the transaction made by the third party;**

**(2) any material change in circumstances that would make it inequitable to bind the third party, unless the third party chooses to be bound; and**

**(3) a specific time that determines whether a third party is deprived of a right or subjected to a liability.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Cross-references._ Section 4.01(2) states how ratification is effected.

_b. Temporal limits on effectiveness of ratification._ The rules stated in this section limit the effectiveness of ratification in the interest of fairness to third parties by requiring that it precede specific circumstances. The rationale for these limits is fairness to third parties, in light of the unilateral power ratification gives a principal to elect to be bound in light of knowledge obtained after the fact of an agent's action.

Doctrines beyond those imposed by agency law also limit the effectiveness of ratification. For example, a ratification is ineffective as to an unauthorized offer that has already lapsed.

_c. Withdrawal by third party._ Consistent with the rule stated in prior Restatements, subsection (1) permits a third party to withdraw from a transaction when the third party is not bound because the agent acted without actual or apparent authority, so long as the third party manifests an intention to withdraw prior to the principal's ratification. The manifestation is effective when either the principal or the agent has notice of it. The relatively small number of cases on point either accept this doctrine or find other grounds for decision without rejecting the doctrine. English cases, however, have long declined to permit the third party to withdraw.

The American doctrine has been explained as analogous to the contract-law treatment of offerors and offerees. An offeror is free to withdraw by revoking the offer prior to the offeree's acceptance, subject to doctrines that restrict the power to revoke once the offeree has commenced performance in response to an offer for a unilateral contract. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 45(1). The third party could be analogized to an offeror and the principal to an offeree. The principal has the power to bind the third party by affirming the unauthorized transaction, but until that time the third party, like an offeror, may withdraw. See Restatement Second, Agency § 88, Comment a. This analogy is incomplete, however, because it does not encompass or explain the relation-back effect of ratification. That is, a contract is formed at the time an offeree accepts an offer, but ratification relates back to the time of the agent's action.

A stronger justification focuses on fairness to the third party in the third party's relationship to the principal and agent. If the agent duped the third party into believing the agent had authority, it is fair to permit the third party to withdraw. The third party's mistake is comparable to a mistaken belief that makes the transaction voidable. See Restatement Second, Agency § 88, Reporter's Notes (Appendix vol. 3). The principal's ratification, however, "cures" the mistake, giving the third party what was anticipated originally. If the third party realizes that the agent lacked authority to bind the principal, permitting the third party to withdraw prior to the principal's ratification is consistent with the third party's initial expectations. It is fair to release a third party who manifests an intention to withdraw from an indefinite period of uncertainty about whether and when the principal may ratify. See id.

Permitting the third party to withdraw prior to ratification by the principal creates an incentive for the principal to ratify sooner rather than later, rather than delaying to see whether the transaction itself turns out to be to the principal's advantage. This reduces the risk created by ratification, which is that the principal will use it to speculate at the expense of the third party.

**Illustration:**

1\. P retains A to identify properties that P might purchase. Lacking actual or apparent authority to do so, A enters into a contract that purports to commit P to purchase Blackacre, which is owned by T. T notifies P that T withdraws from the transaction. If P subsequently manifests assent to affirming the contract made by A, P's manifestation does not effect a ratification. T is not bound by the transaction.

_d. Material change in circumstances._ If a material change in circumstances prior to the principal's ratification would make it inequitable to bind the third party, the third party may avoid liability. This appears to be a well-settled proposition, despite the scarcity of recent cases providing direct support.

**Illustration:**

2\. Purporting to act for P but without power to bind P, A contracts to sell Blackacre with a house thereon to T. The next day the house burns. P's later ratification does not bind T. T may elect to be bound by the contract.

Circumstances may also change materially as a result of action taken by the third party, typically after discovering that the agent lacked authority. The third party may change position before learning that the principal has ratified. For example, if the third party makes costly alternate arrangements and it would be inequitable to hold the third party to the transaction, the third party may avoid the effects of the principal's ratification.

_e. Ratification after rights have crystallized._ Consistent with the rule stated in prior Restatements, subsection (3) provides that a ratification is not effective to deprive a person of a right fixed or "crystallized" prior to the ratification or to create a liability against a person past the time set to determine such a liability.

**Illustration:**

3\. P holds an option written by T that sets July 1 as the last date on which it may be exercised. Acting without actual or apparent authority, A purports to exercise the option on P's behalf on June 30. P ratifies on July 2. P's ratification does not bind T.

This doctrine is in some respects diffuse and difficult to define, in part because it is applicable to a wide range of circumstances, including a party's right to bring a lawsuit following an agent's unauthorized and injurious act. Although some courts treat the doctrine as a basis for limiting defenses that may be asserted in litigation, the conceptual basis for so doing is not clear.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ This section corresponds in substance to Restatement Second §§ 88, 89, 90, and 95, Comment _b._

_c. Withdrawal by third party._ Illustration 1 is based on [_Bardusch v. Hofbeck, 51 A.2d 231 (N.J.Super.Ch.1947)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S11-XP80-004J-M3C4-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Estate of Alexander, 248 N.W.2d 475, 483 (Wis. 1977)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-WCB0-003G-33CH-00000-00&context=1530671) (deceased's affirmance of forgery of her signature by financial manager ineffective to ratify guaranty transaction when lender withdrew from transaction by cutting off line of credit upon discovering forgery). But cf. _P.D. 2000, L.L.C. v. First Fin. Planners, Inc., 998 S.W.2d 108, 111 (Mo.App.1999)_ (court determines it is not necessary to address whether agency-law doctrine permits third party to withdraw from contract made with LLC prior to its formal inception, when third party manifested intention to withdraw prior to LLC's adoption of contract; third party estopped to deny existence of LLC, having known that it was in the process of formation at time contract was made).

On the English rejection of the doctrine permitting the third party to withdraw, see Francis M.B. Reynolds, Bowstead & Reynolds on Agency 76-77 (17th ed. 2001) (criticizing English position as one that can only work fairly if it is subject to exceptions that are "extremely difficult to formulate").

_d. Material change in circumstances._ Illustration 2 comes from Restatement Second, Agency § 89, Illustration 1.

For support for the doctrine, see _Pape v. Home Ins. Co., 139 F.2d 231, 234 (2d Cir.1943)_ (fire-insurance policy covered damage to insured's cotton in Spain, except that resulting from action of duly constituted authorities; insured's rights stemming from seizure of cotton by committee without official standing during civil war not destroyed by subsequent alleged ratification by government). See also [_Allied Mut. Ins. Co. v. Webb, 111 Cal.Rptr.2d 426, 430 (Cal.App. 2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:43VS-C9Y0-0039-424Y-00000-00&context=1530671) (insured's ratification of employee's unauthorized use of vehicle following occurrence of loss ineffective as against insurer; "'allowing ratification permits the insured to examine the alternatives after the loss has occurred, which is clearly unfair to the insurer, and has the potential to foster collusion between the insured and the permittee,'" quoting 8 Couch on Insurance § 113:3 (3d ed. 1997)).

_e. Ratification after rights have crystallized._ Illustration 3 is taken from J. Dennis Hynes & Mark J. Loewenstein, Agency, Partnership and the LLC: The Law of Unincorporated Business Enterprises 442 (6th ed. 2001). For comparable facts, see Dibbins v. Dibbins \[1896\] 2 Ch. 348. See also [_Lund v. Chemical Bank, 760 F.Supp. 51, 54 (S.D.N.Y.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-F120-001T-746P-00000-00&context=1530671) (statutory requirement that payee have received a check in order to sue bank for paying against a forged endorsement; ratification of forger's act in taking delivery of check under a false power of attorney would protect bank for paying on forger's later endorsement of the check under same power of attorney).

On the use of the doctrine to limit the assertion of defenses in litigation, compare [_Wagner v. City of Globe, 722 P.2d 250, 255 (Ariz.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-WMP0-003F-T1WX-00000-00&context=1530671) (allowing ratification would deprive plaintiff of "right of defense" in derogation of his right to bring cause of action) with [_City of Trenton v. Fowler-Thorne Co., 154 A.2d 369 (N.J.Super.1959)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRH-SCT0-003D-S216-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_160 A.2d 131 (N.J.1960)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-Y5T0-003C-N0DC-00000-00&context=1530671) (statute of limitations ran before principal's ratification of agent's act in filing lawsuit; ratification effective and action not barred). See also [_Gemstar Ltd. v. Ernst & Young, 901 P.2d 1178, 1190-1191 (Ariz.App.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-X710-003F-T4CP-00000-00&context=1530671), vacated on other grounds, [_917 P.2d 222 (Ariz.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-W8B0-003F-T015-00000-00&context=1530671) (ratification of filing of lawsuit ineffective when ratification occurs after applicable statute of limitation has run). For English authority on the impact of ratification, see Presentaciones Musicales SA v. Secunda, \[1994\] Ch. 271 (ratification of issue of writ after limitation period had run was effective and related back to date on which writ issued).
