[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 5.04_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T910-00YG-402T-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 5- Notifications and_**

**§ 5.04 An Agent Who Acts Adversely to a Principal**

**For purposes of determining a principal's legal relations with a third party, notice of a fact that an agent knows or has reason to know is not imputed to the principal if the agent acts adversely to the principal in a transaction or matter, intending to act solely for the agent's own purposes or those of another person. Nevertheless, notice is imputed**

**(a) when necessary to protect the rights of a third party who dealt with the principal in good faith; or**

**(b) when the principal has ratified or knowingly retained a benefit from the agent's action.**

**A third party who deals with a principal through an agent, knowing or having reason to know that the agent acts adversely to the principal, does not deal in good faith for this purpose.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ Comment _b_ discusses the circumstances under which notice is not imputed to a principal because an agent acts adversely to the principal. Comment _b_ also discusses the rationales for this exception to imputation. Comment _c_ discusses when an agent acts adversely. Comment _d_ discusses the consequences of a principal's retention of a benefit from an agent's adverse action.

"Notice" is defined in § 1.04(4). Section 5.01(3) states when a person has notice of a fact. For further discussion, see § 5.03, Comment _b._

On the significance of notice in the context of status as a bona fide purchaser or creditor, see Restatement Third, Restitution and Unjust Enrichment §§ 65-67 (forthcoming).

_b. Exception to imputation--in general._ The doctrine stated in this section is an exception to the general rule, stated in § 5.03, that notice is imputed to a principal of a fact that an agent knows or has reason to know if knowledge of the fact is material to the agent's duties to the principal and to the principal's legal relations with third parties. The exception stated in this section is often termed the "adverse interest" exception. There is no imputation if an agent acts adversely to the principal. The adverse-interest exception is subject to two exclusions or exceptions. First, as stated in subsection (a), notice is imputed to the principal of facts that an agent who acts adversely knows or has reason to know when necessary to protect the rights of a third party who has dealt with the principal in good faith. Second, as stated in subsection (b), notice is imputed to a principal when the principal ratifies the agent's action or knowingly retains a benefit from the agent's action, although the agent acted adversely to the principal.

A third party who knows or has reason to know that an agent acts adversely to the principal, and who deals with the principal through the agent, has not dealt in good faith and may not rely on the adverse-interest exception. Thus, imputation protects innocent third parties but not those who know or have reason to know that an agent is not likely to transmit material information to the principal.

The rule stated in this section, like the rule stated in § 5.03, is applicable only for purposes of determining a principal's legal relations with a third party. Thus, notice is not imputed for purposes of determining rights and liabilities as between principal and agent. As a consequence, imputation does not furnish a basis on which an agent may defend against a claim by the principal.

If a principal ratifies action taken by an agent, the principal is bound by the legal consequences of that action as if the principal had knowledge of all the facts known by the agent. Section 4.01(2) states how a principal may ratify an agent's action, including by retaining a benefit produced by the agent's action. See Comment _d_ for further discussion.

This section states a well-established doctrine. Partnership legislation has long incorporated the principle that a partnership is not charged with the knowledge of a partner who acts adversely to it. Under § 12 of the Uniform Partnership Act (1914) and § 102(f) of the Uniform Partnership Act (1997), a partnership is charged with a partner's knowledge, "except in the case of a fraud on the partnership committed by or with the consent of that partner."

However, as stated above, in order to protect third parties who deal with the principal in good faith, common-law agency doctrine encompasses exclusions to the exception to imputation when an agent acts adversely. As stated in subsection (a), the adverse-interest exception to imputation does not defeat the rights of a third party who dealt in good faith with the principal through an agent. Common-law agency also recognizes that a principal may elect to ratify the actions of an adversely acting agent. Taken as a whole, the common-law doctrine reflects a balance among factors that, if pressed in isolation to their respective extremes, would lead to divergent outcomes.

Ordinarily, an agent's failure to disclose a material fact to a principal does not defeat imputation, nor does the fact that the agent's action otherwise constitutes a breach of a duty owed the principal. See § 5.03, Comment _b._ For an agent's duty to provide information to the principal, see § 8.11. A principal's opportunity to monitor an agent and create incentives for the proper handling of information warrant imputing an agent's knowledge to the principal even when the agent has breached duties of disclosure to the principal. Moreover, imputation does not rest on an identification between principal and agent but rather on the fact that a principal's agents link it to the external world for purposes of obtaining and conveying information as well as taking action.

When an agent acts for the agent's own purposes or those of another person, the principal may be subject to liability to third parties because the agent may reasonably appear to be acting as the principal's representative with actual authority. See § 2.03. Likewise, notice of material facts known to an agent is imputed to the principal when the agent deals with a third party who reasonably believes the agent to be authorized so to act for the principal. However, this section does not protect a third party who knows or has reason to know that an agent acts adversely to the principal. If the third party colludes with the agent against the principal or otherwise knows or has reason to know that the agent is acting adversely to the principal, the third party should not expect that the agent will fulfill duties of disclosure owed to the principal.

_c. When an agent acts adversely to a principal._ The term "adversely" is not free of ambiguity. However, for purposes of common-law agency, the fact that an action taken by an agent has unfavorable results for the principal does not establish that the agent acted adversely. Beyond this basic point, it is helpful to distinguish between the meaning and consequences of "adversely" in connection with the duties an agent owes to a principal as opposed to the principal's legal relations with third parties. As stated in § 8.03, an agent acts adversely to a principal in conducting a transaction between the agent and the principal or between the principal and another principal on whose behalf the agent also acts. Unless the principal consents as stated in § 8.06, the agent's conduct breaches the agent's duty to the principal. Moreover, when an agent takes action in dealing with third parties, intending solely to benefit the agent or another person, the agent's action breaches the agent's most basic duty to the principal as stated in § 8.01.

Although an agent's action may breach duties the agent owes to the principal, the agent's action may also affect the principal's legal relations with third parties. For example, an agent acts with apparent authority when the third party with whom the agent deals reasonably believes that the agent acts with actual authority. Section 2.03 defines "apparent authority"; § 3.03 states how a principal may create apparent authority. The rule stated in this section is consistent with the general operation of apparent authority. Under subsection (a), notice is imputed to the principal of facts known to the agent, although the agent takes action intending to benefit only the agent or another person, when necessary to protect the rights of a third party who dealt with the principal in good faith.

In many cases, courts determine whether an agent acted adversely to a principal by assessing the motives with which the agent acted. The formulation in Restatement Second, Agency § 282 focuses on whether an agent acted "entirely for his own or another's purposes . . . ." Id. § 282(1). Most cases read this formulation to entail a subjective test that turns on the agent's motives at the time of taking action. The agent's own testimony is not determinative. Ascertaining the motives with which an agent acted is often a fact-intensive exercise.

The analysis in other cases extends beyond the agent's motives to consider whether the principal benefited through the agent's actions. This analysis calls into question how and from what point in time the principal's realization of a benefit should be assessed. It also is in tension with the basic point that, when an agent acts with actual or apparent authority, the principal is bound by the legal consequences of the agent's actions even though they were not beneficial to the principal.

It is helpful to view questions about imputation from the perspective of risk assumption, taking into account the posture of the third party whose legal relations with the principal are at issue. A principal assumes the risk that the agents it chooses to interact on its behalf with third parties will, when actual or apparent authority is present, bind the principal to the legal consequences of their actions. This is because the principal chooses its agents, has the right to control them, and determines how to characterize its agents' positions and indicia of authority in manifestations made to third parties. See § 1.03, which defines "manifestation." A principal's incentive systems and other practices may also have the effect of discouraging agents from reporting information that, after the fact, it would have been advantageous for the principal to have known.

In contrast, when the third party whose legal relations with the principal are at issue has not dealt with the principal (either directly or through an agent) or has not dealt in good faith, the principal does not bear the risk that its agent may withhold relevant information to serve the agent's own purposes or those of another person. A principal should not be held to assume the risk that an agent may act wrongfully in dealing with a third party who colludes with the agent in action that is adverse to the principal. That is, the third party should not benefit from imputing the agent's knowledge to the principal when the third party itself acted wrongfully or otherwise in bad faith. The circumstances surrounding a transaction, including the magnitude of benefit it will confer on the agent who arranges it, may place a reasonable third party on notice that the agent will withhold material information from the principal.

**Illustrations:**

1\. Prior to B's employment by P, B obtains a surety bond for P's benefit, issued by T, that guarantees B's honesty. After B's employment by P begins, A, who manages P's business, learns that B has stolen from P. A has a duty to report B's theft to P but does not do so because A expects to demand payment from B as the price of concealment. Thereafter B again steals from P. P sues T on the surety bond. P may not recover on the bond for the second theft if P knew of the first theft and did not inform T before the second theft occurred. Notice of the fact of B's first theft, known by A, is not imputed to P because A acted adversely to P.

2\. P retains A to negotiate terms for the purchase of Blackacre for P, promising to pay A a percentage commission of the purchase price if A can persuade Blackacre's owner, T, to agree to sell and if A finds no defects in T's title to Blackacre. In investigating T's title, A discovers that L has an unrecorded equitable interest in Blackacre. A does not reveal L's interest to P, fearing that P will not otherwise agree to buy Blackacre and thus that A would not receive a commission. P agrees to buy Blackacre from T. Although A acted adversely to P, notice of the fact of L's interest in Blackacre is imputed to P. T acted in good faith, and imputation is necessary to protect T's rights. T may not enforce the contract against P if T knew or had reason to know of A's failure to disclose the existence of L's interest to P.

3\. Same facts as Illustration 2, except that A does not reveal L's interest in Blackacre because T bribes A not to do so. Notice of the fact of L's interest in Blackacre is not imputed to P. In accepting the bribe from T, A acted for A's own or T's own purposes. T did not act in good faith.

In situations in which notice of a fact known to an agent is imputed to a principal, such as Illustration 2, the principal's own incentive systems for the agent may help explain why the agent was reticent in disclosing material information to the principal. In Illustration 2, A will receive compensation from P only upon concluding an agreement with T to sell Blackacre to P, assuming no defects are found in T's title to Blackacre. If A discloses what A knows of L's equitable interest to P, A risks losing any right to compensation because P may decide not to purchase Blackacre. If, following disclosure by A, P remains interested in purchasing Blackacre, P is likely to wish to do so at a lower price that reflects the economic impact of L's interest. If A anticipates that P may so react, A may withhold the information about L's interest from P because P has agreed to pay A a percentage commission of the price P pays T for Blackacre.

In contrast, in situations in which notice of a fact known to an agent is not imputed to a principal, such as Illustrations 1 and 3, the principal's own incentive system does not explain the agent's reticence. In Illustration 1, the prospect of payment by B induces A not to reveal B's initial theft to P. In Illustration 3, the fact of payment by T induces A not to reveal the fact of L's equitable interest in Blackacre to P.

The identity of the other party whose interests are at issue is also relevant to whether notice is imputed to a principal when an agent has acted adversely. In Illustrations 2 and 3, L, who is innocent of collusion with A, has not dealt with P. As between L and P, A's knowledge of L's unrecorded lien is not imputed to P unless P ratifies A's action.

When a third party deals with a principal through an agent, and does so reasonably and in good faith, imputation protects the third party's rights against the principal.

**Illustrations:**

4\. A, the chief financial officer of P Corporation, withholds material financial information from T, P Corporation's auditor. T does not independently discover the information and certifies materially inaccurate financial statements for P Corporation. Relying reasonably on P Corporation's financial statements, which A furnishes to S, and acting in good faith, S enters into a transaction with P Corporation. S suffers loss when the true facts about P Corporation's financial condition become evident. P Corporation is subject to liability to S for the loss suffered by S. A's knowledge that P Corporation's financial statements, as certified by T, do not reflect P Corporation's true financial condition is imputed to P Corporation. S dealt with P Corporation in good faith. A is subject to liability to P as stated in §§ 8.01, 8.08, and 8.09 and to S as stated in §§ 7.01 and 7.02.

5\. Same facts as Illustration 4, except that T knows or has reason to know that A has withheld material information from T. P Corporation sues T, claiming that T is subject to liability to P for loss suffered by P Corporation due to its inaccurate financial statements. T may not assert, as a defense to P Corporation's claim, that A's knowledge of P Corporation's true financial condition is imputed to P Corporation. T has not dealt with P Corporation in good faith.

Illustrations 4 and 5 do not specify whether T's failure independently to discover the information withheld by A is the consequence of common-law negligence on T's part; whether T's failure is the consequence of a breach of an independent professional or legal obligation owed by T as an auditor; or whether T has violated other legal requirements applicable to auditors, such as requirements of independence, prohibitions on conflicts of interest, or prohibitions on improper influence of a chief financial officer on the conduct of an audit. These issues are not relevant to P Corporation's liability to S, the question in Illustration 4. These issues are, however, relevant when the legal relations in question are those between P Corporation and T, as in Illustration 5. The nature of T's duties to P Corporation may subject T to liability to P Corporation, independently of whether A's knowledge is imputed to P Corporation. For further discussion, see § 5.03, Comment _b._

Illustrations 4 and 5 also do not specify the motive with which A acted. A's motive is not relevant to the question addressed by Illustration 4, which is P Corporation's liability to S, a third party who dealt with P Corporation in good faith. The duties of T to S, if any, are beyond the scope of both this Restatement and this section.

If the third party with whom an adversely acting agent interacts does not reasonably believe that the agent acts with actual authority, the principal is not bound by the agent's actions because the agent has acted without apparent authority. See § 2.03, which defines "apparent authority." Moreover, in connection with the principal's legal relations with that third party, notice of facts that the agent knows or has reason to know is not imputed to the principal. However, if the principal ratifies the agent's actions, the principal is charged with knowledge of material facts that the agent knows or has reason to know.

**Illustrations:**

6\. P Corporation, a dealer in industrial furnaces, retains A to sell furnaces for it on terms to be negotiated by A. As is customary in such transactions, P Corporation authorizes A to state the capacity and condition of each furnace to prospective purchasers. To T, a prospective purchaser, A falsely states that a particular furnace has been used only for demonstration purposes, which is material to the value of the furnace. A knows that the statement is false and makes it because A wishes to damage P Corporation's business reputation. T purchases the furnace. In T's claim against P Corporation, P Corporation is charged with notice of the fact that the furnace has been used other than for demonstration purposes. T dealt with P Corporation in good faith.

7\. Same facts as Illustration 6, except that P Corporation authorizes A to sell furnaces only at prices set by P Corporation on price lists that P Corporation furnishes to A and to prospective customers, including T. The price lists also describe the condition and capacity of each furnace. Wishing to damage P Corporation's reputation, A tells T that a particular furnace has been used only for demonstration purposes although it is otherwise described on P Corporation's price list. A tells T that the price stated on the price list is mistakenly low and that the correct price is 50 percent higher. A also tells T that T should not check the furnace's condition directly with P Corporation because checking with its sales department will delay the sale and A has other prospective purchasers for the furnace. T agrees to buy the furnace on these terms. In T's claim against P Corporation, P Corporation is not charged with A's knowledge. A did not act with apparent authority in so dealing with T. It was not reasonable for T to believe that A had actual authority so to deal on P Corporation's behalf.

_d. Benefit of agent's action knowingly retained by principal._ The adverse-interest exception serves to shield a principal against imputation of notice of facts known to an agent who acts adversely to the principal. The exception should not serve as a sword that enables a principal knowingly to retain the benefits of its agent's wrongdoing. Closely related principles apply to a principal's ratification of an agent's conduct. See §§ 4.01 and 4.07. Whether a principal has retained a benefit is at least in part a question of fact.

**Illustrations:**

8\. Same facts as Illustration 7, except that T pays P Corporation for the furnace, paying 50 percent more than the list price, as demanded by A. After the furnace is delivered to T, T discovers that its condition is as stated on the price list. T demands that P Corporation return the payment made by T in exchange for the furnace. P Corporation is charged with notice of the fact known to A that the condition of the furnace was as stated on the price list.

9\. P retains A as manager of P's investment portfolio. A purchases securities issued by S Corporation for P's account from T at a bargain price, falsely representing to T that S Corporation has lost the account of its major customer. A does this because A wishes to damage T, a competitor of A's. P learns of the purchase and refuses to return the securities to T after T learns that A's statement about S Corporation was false. In a claim by T against P, notice is imputed to P of the true facts known to A.

However, a principal's retention of a benefit does not result in imputation to the principal of notice of facts known to the agent when the principal changes position before knowingly retaining the benefit. A principal changes position as stated in Restatement Third, Restitution and Unjust Enrichment, Chapter 8, Defenses to Restitution (forthcoming).

As discussed in § 5.03, Comment _b,_ notice is not imputed to a principal of facts known to an agent when the agent deals with the principal on the agent's own account, as by selling property to the principal. Thus, if an agent sells property to the principal that the agent has obtained wrongfully from a third party, the principal as purchaser is not charged with notice of the agent's wrongdoing. However, if the agent controls the principal's decisionmaking, the principal is charged with notice of the agent's wrongdoing. This rule, often termed the "sole actor doctrine," treats principal and agent as one.

**Illustration:**

10\. A is the Chief Executive Officer and Chairman of the Board of Directors of P Corporation. A persuades T to lend gold ingots to A personally by falsely representing material facts about A's personal finances. A proposes to borrow money from P Corporation and to use the ingots borrowed from T as collateral for the loan. At A's request, P Corporation's other directors agree to the transaction. T discovers the true facts about A's personal finances and A's loan transaction with P Corporation. T demands that P Corporation return the ingots to T. P Corporation refuses. P Corporation is charged with notice of A's knowledge of A's misrepresentations to T.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ In Restatement Second, Agency, the counterpart to this section is § 282, which states:

(1) A principal is not affected by the knowledge of an agent in a transaction in which the agent secretly is acting adversely to the principal and entirely for his own or another's purposes, except as stated in Subsection (2).

(2) The principal is affected by the knowledge of an agent who acts adversely to the principal:

(a) if the failure of the agent to act upon or to reveal the information results in a violation of a contractual or relational duty of the principal to a person harmed thereby;

(b) if the agent enters into negotiations within the scope of his powers and the person with whom he deals reasonably believes him to be authorized to conduct the transaction; or

(c) if, before he has changed his position, the principal knowingly retains a benefit through the act of the agent which otherwise he would not have received.

This Restatement does not treat the situation in § 282(2)(a) in this Chapter because it does not involve imputing notice to a principal of facts that an agent knows or has reason to know. This Restatement states explicitly that the adverse-interest exception is inoperative when the third party with whom an agent deals knows or has reason to know that the agent acts adversely to the principal. This Restatement simplifies the underlying point in § 282(2)(b), which is to protect third parties who deal with a principal in good faith. Comment _c_ discusses third parties who deal with agents who lack apparent authority.

_b. Exception to imputation--in general._ Relevant provisions in partnership legislation, although varying in other respects, identically exclude imputation when a partner commits a fraud on the partnership or consents to a fraud on it. Section 12 of the Uniform Partnership Act (1914) provides that "\[n\]otice to any partner of any matter relating to partnership affairs, and the knowledge of the partner acting in the particular matter, acquired while a partner or then present to his mind, and the knowledge of any other partner who reasonably could and should have communicated it to the acting partner, operate as notice to or knowledge of the partnership, except in the case of a fraud on the partnership committed by or with the consent of that partner." Section 102(f) of the Uniform Partnership Act (1997) provides that "\[a\] partner's knowledge, notice, or receipt of a notification of a fact relating to the partnership is effective immediately as knowledge by, notice to, or receipt of a notification by the partnership, except in the case of a fraud on the partnership committed by or with the consent of that partner." Section 103(h) of the Revised Uniform Limited Partnership Act (2001) provides that "\[a\] general partner's knowledge, notice, or receipt of a notification of a fact relating to the limited partnership is effective immediately as knowledge by, notice to, or receipt of a notification by the limited partnership, except in the case of a fraud on the limited partnership committed by or with the consent of the general partner. A limited partner's knowledge, notice, or receipt of a notification of a fact to the limited partnership is not effective as knowledge by, notice to, or receipt of a notification by the limited partnership." The English Partnership Act (1890) provides in § 16 that "\[n\]otice to any partner who habitually acts in the partnership business of any matter relating to partnership affairs operates as notice to the firm, except in the case of a fraud on the firm committed by or with the consent of that partner."

In English law, the "adverse interest" exception is broader in scope. For extensive discussion and critique, see Peter Watts, Imputed Knowledge in Agency Law--Excising the Fraud Exception, 117 L.Q. Rev. 300 (2001).

The test for imputation as against a partnership, when a partner is not acting to defraud the partnership, is whether the partner acted within the scope of partnership affairs and within his authority. See, e.g., [_Affiliated FM Ins. Co. v. Kushner Cos., 627 A.2d 710, 716 (N.J.Super.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-WCD0-003C-P4RB-00000-00&context=1530671) (partner's knowledge of fire loss sustained by partnership prior to issuance of insurance policy imputed to partner responsible for maintaining insurance, voiding coverage under policy; partner who knew of loss, although not directly involved in applying for insurance, was involved in supplying information concerning insurance application). On imputation when a partner acts fraudulently toward the partnership, see [_Invest Almaz v. Temple-Inland Forest Prods. Corp., 243 F.3d 57, 74-75 (1st Cir.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:42KM-7880-0038-X0WW-00000-00&context=1530671). But see [_Grassmueck v. American Shorthorn Ass'n, 402 F.3d 833, 838 (8th Cir.2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4FV4-YYM0-0038-X2MM-00000-00&context=1530671) (fraud of general partner of investment partnerships imputed to partnerships under "sole actor" doctrine, although language of partnership statute codifies adverse-interest exception and does not include sole-actor doctrine).

In jurisdictions with codified treatments of agency law, the adverse-interest exception may be applicable although the code is silent on the question. See, e.g., [_People v. Park, 151 Cal.Rptr. 146 (Cal.App.1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S11-T8W0-003C-R1KB-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Witty v. Clinch, 279 P. 799 (Cal.1929)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-9MY0-003D-W00C-00000-00&context=1530671). Under Ga. Code § 10-6-59 (2000 & Supp. 2005), a principal is not charged with knowledge of facts acquired by an agent "where an agent shall conspire with the other party...."

_c. When an agent acts adversely to a principal._ For analysis of the competing rationales underlying imputation doctrine and of risk allocation as the most compelling rationale, see Marin R. Scordato, Evidentiary Surrogacy and Risk Allocation: Understanding Imputed Knowledge and Notice in Modern Agency Law, [_10 Fordham J. Corp. & Fin. L. 129 (2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4F7F-SB90-00SW-405V-00000-00&context=1530671). See also Deborah A. DeMott, When is a Principal Charged With an Agent's Knowledge?, [_13 Duke J. Comp. & Int'l L. 291 (2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:49N3-1MF0-00CV-S0HW-00000-00&context=1530671). Illustration 1 is in substance derived from Illustration 1 to Restatement Second, Agency § 282.

Illustration 2 is in substance derived from Illustration 4 to Restatement Second, Agency § 282.

For a hypothetical counterpart to Illustration 3, see Scordato, [_10 Fordham J. Corp. & Fin. L. at 134_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4F7F-SB90-00SW-405V-00000-00&context=1530671) ("imagine that in the course of a sales transaction, a car salesman working for a dealership learns from a potential customer that the customer must receive a certain minimum amount in trade for his current car in order to be willing to purchase a new car from the dealership through the salesman. Upon direct inspection, the salesman learns of defects in the customer's current car that would make it impossible to give the customer the minimum trade-in value that he seeks. Nevertheless, badly wanting the commission on the sale, the salesman prepares a report of the inspection as if the defects did not exist and credits the customer the desired amount of value in trade for his current car. When the dealership subsequently seeks a remedy against the customer for failing to fully disclose defects in the car of which the customer was fully aware at the time of the transaction as required by the sales contract, the customer is not likely to be able to employ the imputed-knowledge rule to bind the dealership to the knowledge of the salesman regarding the condition of the car, because the agent, the salesman, was operating adversely to the interests of the principal, the dealership, in the transaction in question."). The result would be otherwise, however, when imputation is at issue as between the dealership and a party who dealt with it in good faith. Suppose the dealership next resells the trade-in car to another customer who deals with the dealership in good faith, but that the dealership does not disclose the car's defects. Under subsection (a), notice of the defect is imputed to the dealership for the purpose of determining that customer's legal relations with the dealership.

Representative cases in which a principal is held to be subject to liability for the fraudulent misrepresentations of an agent, acting with apparent authority, are [_In re Atlantic Fin. Mgmt., Inc., 784 F.2d 29, 31 (1st Cir.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-9500-0039-P37V-00000-00&context=1530671); In re Tyco Int'l, Ltd., 2004 WL 2348315, at \*6 (D.N.H.2004); [_Grease Monkey Int'l, Inc. v. Montoya, 904 P.2d 468 (Colo.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-0040-003D-924F-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_DGM Invs., Inc. v. New York Futures Exch., Inc., 265 F.Supp.2d 254, 262 (S.D.N.Y.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48PV-95V0-0038-Y0M6-00000-00&context=1530671) (adverse-interest exception inapplicable when chairman of exchange, acting within scope of duties, intentionally created artificial settlement prices and disregarded proper settlement procedures; chairman's bad faith imputed to exchange and to settlement committee). If an agent acts outside the scope of the agent's actual and apparent authority, the agent's knowledge of the falsity of representations made by the agent to a third party is not imputed to the principal. See [_United States v. President and Fellows of Harvard Coll., 323 F.Supp.2d 151, 201 (D.Mass.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4CRP-MBT0-0038-Y3TH-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Massachusetts law).

Cases in which notice is imputed to a corporation, in fact patterns involving financial misconduct by a corporate insider and the corporation's legal relations with its outside auditor, include [_Cenco Inc. v. Seidman & Seidman, 686 F.2d 449, 456 (7th Cir.1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-3K00-003B-G042-00000-00&context=1530671) (notice imputed to corporation when management falsely overstated value of inventories, creating defense for auditor in negligence action brought by corporation; court states that "\[f\]raud on behalf of a corporation is not the same thing as fraud against it. Fraud against the corporation usually hurts just the corporation; the stockholders are the principal if not the only victims; their equities vis-a-vis a careless or reckless auditor are therefore strong. But the stockholders of a corporation whose officers commit fraud for the benefit of the corporation are beneficiaries of the fraud."); [_Mid-Continent Paper Converters, Inc. v. Brady, Ware & Schoenfeld, Inc., 715 N.E.2d 906, 911-912 (Ind. App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3X8F-R4S0-0039-430T-00000-00&context=1530671) (notice imputed to corporation when chief financial officer falsified financial records, barring corporation's negligence counterclaim against auditor's action for unpaid fees; not clearly erroneous for trial court to find officer intended to and did benefit corporation by facilitating commitment from lender); _Seidman & Seidman v. Gee, 625 So. 2d 1, 3 (Fla.Dist.App.1992)_ (fraud of corporate officer imputed to corporation when it was "intended to and did" benefit corporation, creating absolute defense to negligence action brought by corporation's liquidator against auditor; officer falsely represented that corporation in insurance and reinsurance business was backed by \$ 10 million certificate of deposit, which served as premise for corporation's marketing program and benefited corporation by enabling it to continue in business).

The analysis in _Cenco_ may be modified by the Seventh Circuit's later opinion in [_Schacht v. Brown, 711 F.2d 1343, 1347 (7th Cir.1983)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-0CX0-003B-G107-00000-00&context=1530671), in which the court characterized _Cenco_ as a case in which the management misconduct "clearly benefited the corporation to the detriment of outside creditors, stock purchasers and insurers...." In _Schacht,_ the consequence of the misconduct was to continue the corporation's existence past the point of insolvency, enabling corporate insiders to loot business and income from the corporation. The court held that since prolonging the corporation's existence benefited only its managers and their conspirators, analysis under _Cenco_ was not triggered. [_Id. at 1350_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-0CX0-003B-G107-00000-00&context=1530671). Other cases follow _Schacht_ in recognizing a doctrine of "deepening insolvency." See, e.g., [_Official Comm. of Unsecured Creditors v. R.F. Lafferty Co., 267 F.3d 340 (3d Cir.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:445R-YT50-0038-X37X-00000-00&context=1530671). Moreover, a court may determine that claims premised on a third party's negligence are sufficiently distinct from claims premised on management fraud to constitute claims of fault that are independent of management's knowledge imputed to a corporate principal. See [_In re CBI Holding Co., 311 B.R. 350, 375 (S.D.N.Y.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4CS5-WWH0-0038-Y462-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[plaintiff\] alleges that \[auditor\] is independently at fault for committing errors in auditing \[corporation\]. According to \[plaintiffs\] negligence claims, a reasonable accountant would not have made the auditing errors that \[auditor\] made, even if the culpable \[corporate\] officials had tried to hide their fraud from that (reasonable) accountant."). _CBI_ relies on [_Shearson Lehman Hutton, Inc. v. Wagoner, 944 F.2d 114 (2d Cir.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-8FH0-008H-V024-00000-00&context=1530671), and [_Hirsch v. Arthur Andersen & Co., 72 F.3d 1085 (2d Cir.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-9330-001T-D23K-00000-00&context=1530671), for the proposition that claims may be distinct from "facts concerning any participation by the third party in management's fraud." [_311 B.R. at 374_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4CS5-WWH0-0038-Y462-00000-00&context=1530671). See [_Wagoner, 944 F.2d at 119_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-8FH0-008H-V024-00000-00&context=1530671) (claim that broker churned company's account distinct from claim that broker participated in management fraud); [_Hirsch, 72 F.3d at 1089-1090_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-9330-001T-D23K-00000-00&context=1530671) & 1094 n.6 (allegations of malpractice against accounting and law firms closely tied to alleged participation in management fraud). However, on rehearing, the _CBI_ court determined that the claims were not distinct. See [_318 B.R. 761, 766 (S.D.N.Y.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4FFG-TKS0-TVW3-P2GR-00000-00&context=1530671). Creditors' interests become salient to directors' duties when a corporation becomes insolvent or trends irretrievably toward insolvency, see [_Geyer v. Ingersoll Publ'ns., 621 A.2d 784, 789 (Del.Ch.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRT-8J20-003C-K1T7-00000-00&context=1530671) (director owes fiduciary duty to creditors when corporation is insolvent, even though no formal insolvency proceeding has been commenced); [_In re Kingston Square Assocs., 214 B.R. 713, 735 (Bankr. S.D.N.Y.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RY4-RF50-003B-X11V-00000-00&context=1530671) (noting "universal" agreement that directors' fiduciary duties expand to include general creditors "when a corporation approaches insolvency or actually becomes insolvent"); Matthew G. Dore, Presumed Innocent? Financial Institutions, Professional Malpractice Claims, and Defenses Based on Management Misconduct, [_1995 Colum. Bus. L. Rev. 127, 198-200 (1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S3T-VYR0-00CW-301G-00000-00&context=1530671). The fact of insolvency does not, under Delaware law, change the nature of a claim stemming from harm to the corporation caused by directors' breach of duties owed to the corporation, which remains a claim belonging to the corporation. See [_Production Res. Group, L.L.C. v. NCT Group, Inc., 863 A.2d 772, 792 (Del.Ch.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4DYN-3BC0-TVSY-W293-00000-00&context=1530671).

The Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 contains provisions relevant to independent auditors' duties. See [_15 U.S.C. §§ 7233(b)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8S9D-W4W2-8T6X-7379-00000-00&context=1530671) (requirement of auditor independence) and 7242(a) (prohibition on exercise of improper influence on audit by corporation's chief financial officer, in contravention of rules to be prescribed by SEC). Under § 7242(b), the SEC has exclusive jurisdiction in any civil proceeding to enforce this prohibition. See also [_15 U.S.C. § 78j-1(g)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8S9D-W4W2-8T6X-700S-00000-00&context=1530671) (prohibitions on auditor's provision of specified nonaudit services to audit client).

On the definition of when an agent acts adversely, see [_Beck v. Deloitte & Touche, 144 F.3d 732, 734 (11th Cir.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3TJB-49K0-0038-X445-00000-00&context=1530671) (facts as alleged in complaint brought by trustee of corporation against accounting firm would establish that its directors acted adversely to corporation and would delay running of statute of limitations applicable to action for professional negligence; complaint alleged that directors of bank purchased savings and loan institution, already placed in receivership by FDIC, for sole purpose of making their bank an unattractive target for any attempted takeover, and that directors had "no belief or intention that the acquisition … might benefit \[the corporation\] in any legitimate way"; accounting firm allowed directors to account for acquisition, contrary to generally accepted accounting practices, using pooling method as opposed to purchase method, with consequence that regulatory approval for acquisition was obtained and danger that it presented to bank's health was concealed); [_In re Parmalat Sec. Litig., 383 F.Supp.2d 587, 597-598 (S.D.N.Y.2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4GV4-4BM0-0038-Y35F-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying North Carolina law; relevant actions and conduct imputed to corporation when officers acted for corporation as well as for themselves; immaterial that actions did not benefit corporation as "the question is not whether the officers' actions ultimately were good or bad for the corporation, but whether the officers were conducting their own business or the corporation's"); [_Allard v. Arthur Andersen & Co., 924 F.Supp. 488, 495 (S.D.N.Y.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-R0M0-006F-P4J1-00000-00&context=1530671) (predicting that Michigan court would follow test as stated in New York and Restatement Second, Agency § 282; adverse-interest rule not triggered "if agent is acting at least in part to further the principal's interest"); [_Saks v. Charity Mission Baptist Church, 110 Cal.Rptr.2d 45, 63-64 (Cal.App.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:43K3-D1K0-0039-42RD-00000-00&context=1530671) (notice not imputed to church of facts about property transaction known to minister when minister did not intend to benefit church through transaction although ownership of property placed in church's name; sole purpose of placing property in church's name was to obtain public-development funds and then immediately transfer property to private partnership from which minister would potentially benefit). See also Matthew G. Dore, Presumed Innocent? Financial Institutions, Professional Malpractice Claims, and Defenses Based on Management Misconduct, [_1995 Colum. Bus. L. Rev. 127, 161 (1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S3T-VYR0-00CW-301G-00000-00&context=1530671) (analysis of cases establishes that agent's motives are determinative as opposed to outcome of agent's actions).

A court may also define when an agent acts adversely by characterizing when an agent's actions are not to be understood as adverse. For example, in [_In re Rent-Way Sec. Litig., 209 F.Supp.2d 493, 522 (W.D.Pa. 2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:468B-KPG0-0038-Y12M-00000-00&context=1530671), the plaintiff alleged that the defendants misstated the corporation's earnings with the intention of benefiting the company by raising its stock price "and, in turn, to fund the company's rapid growth through acquisitions." Observed the court, "\[t\]hese allegations are clearly distinguishable from, for example, allegations that a corporate officer misappropriated corporate funds for his or her own use, a clear example of conduct adverse to the interests of the employer." Id.

It may be difficult to determine the motive with which an agent acted in the context of a motion for summary judgment. See, e.g., [_In re Phar-Mor, Inc. Sec. Litig., 900 F.Supp. 784, 787 (W.D.Pa.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-KFK0-001T-509R-00000-00&context=1530671) (denying summary judgment on behalf of auditor when court unable to conclude as matter of law that acts of corporation's officers were intended to benefit it; while motive behind overstatements of corporation's financial performance may have been to provide time for it to resolve underlying business problems, motive may instead have been to preserve officers' "employment, salaries, emoluments and reputations, as well as their liberty" at expense of corporation); [_In re Crazy Eddie Sec. Litig., 802 F.Supp. 804, 818 (E.D.N.Y.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-GSK0-008H-F3HK-00000-00&context=1530671) (denying auditor's motion for summary judgment on negligence and breach-of-contract claims when jury must determine what motivated management of corporation to scheme to inflate price of corporation's shares to mislead investors; although evidence before court on motion for summary judgment was "certainly susceptible of the interpretation that any short-term benefit to \[corporation\] was intended to redound to advantage of only \[officers and directors\] and their conspirators," auditor may be able to persuade jury that actions were taken in part to benefit corporation and existing shareholders through infusion of funds from public offerings of shares).

Cases stating alternative tests include _Seidman & Seidman v. Gee, 625 So. 2d 1, 3 (Fla.Dist.App.1992)_ (notice imputed to corporation when corporate officer "intended to and did benefit the corporation"); [_Fowler v. Latham, 38 S.E.2d 732, 737 (Ga.1946)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-7JV0-003V-M314-00000-00&context=1530671) (notice or knowledge not imputed when agent's relations to subject matter or previous conduct make it uncertain whether agent will disclose information to principal); [_Sequa Corp. v. Cooper, 128 S.W.3d 69, 77 (Mo. App.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4B37-B0R0-0039-43GH-00000-00&context=1530671) (knowledge of lawyer who represented corporation, if acting as an agent on corporation's behalf in transaction, would not be imputed to corporation when lawyer "was in a position to benefit financially" from the transaction, a sale of a subsidiary's assets to purchaser in which lawyer had 5% ownership interest; lawyer knew that transaction had been mischaracterized). See also [_Golden Door Jewelry Creations, Inc. v. Lloyds Underwriters Non-Marine Ass'n, 117 F.3d 1328, 1339 (11th Cir. 1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-DTN0-00B1-D4TD-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Florida law, court holds that officer of corporation perpetrated theft and embezzlement from corporation "solely for his own benefit" and acted directly antagonistically to interests of corporation as insured under insurance policy that insured all jewelry products against all risks, including theft). For the characterization of situations to which the doctrine applies as ones in which the agent is unlikely to communicate relevant information to the principal, see [_Northern Assurance Co. v. Summers, 17 F.3d 956, 965 (7th Cir.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-8F90-003B-P51R-00000-00&context=1530671) (court is "hard-pressed to imagine a case in which it is more clear that an agent will not inform her principal of the facts"; insurer's agent knew that automobile to be covered by insurance policy carrying retroactive date had already been involved in accident within policy's term).

For the point that an agent does not act adversely for purposes of this doctrine because achieving benefit for the principal is not the agent's sole or primary objective, see [_Williams v. Continental Stock Transfer & Trust Co., 1 F.Supp.2d 836, 844 (N.D.Ill. 1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SB6-SSG0-0038-Y4Y1-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent does not have "adverse interest" merely because agent has conflict of interest or does not act primarily for principal); [_Allard v. Arthur Andersen & Co., 924 F.Supp. 488, 495 (S.D.N.Y.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-R0M0-006F-P4J1-00000-00&context=1530671). But cf. River City Ranches # [_1 Ltd. v. Commissioner, 401 F.3d 1136, 1143 (9th Cir.2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4FSX-4630-0038-X26M-00000-00&context=1530671) (managing partner of sheep-breeding partnerships, knowing of his own thefts from partnerships, had motive to offer any concession to Internal Revenue Service to forestall day of reckoning with fellow partners, creating interests that "appear to have run in the opposite direction" from interests of other partners in extension of limitation periods within which Internal Revenue Service could issue adjustments for theft losses; remanded for further discovery on whether managing partner executed extensions "while disabled by conflicts of interest").

For the point that the doctrine is inapplicable to a principal's claims against an agent, see [_Eastern Trading Co. v. Refco, Inc., 229 F.3d 617, 624 (7th Cir.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:41DB-R520-0038-X3N3-00000-00&context=1530671) (characterizing as "obviously wrong" reasoning that knowledge of partner involved in unauthorized speculative trading is imputed to partnership that necessarily therefore approved of trading because argument would "provide a legal immunity for anyone who assisted one partner to defraud another"); [_Nerbonne, N.V. v. Lake Bryan Int'l Props., 685 So. 2d 1029, 1032 (Fla. Dist.App.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-3JC0-003F-301K-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent who defrauded principal may not benefit from imputation of knowledge of fraud to principal; doctrine imputing agent's knowledge to principal when agent is sole actor in transaction with third party applicable only to controversy between principal and innocent third party); [_Marine Midland Bank v. John E. Russo Produce Co., 405 N.E.2d 205, 212-213 (N.Y.1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-9V80-003C-F1JX-00000-00&context=1530671) (individual liability of corporate officer in check-kiting scheme is separate from question of whether knowledge is imputed to corporation to establish corporate liability). But see [_Banco Latino Int'l v. Gomez Lopez, 95 F.Supp.2d 1327, 1335-1336 (S.D.Fla.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:405S-G6V0-0038-Y0S9-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Florida law, court holds that directors of bank had absolute defense to bank's action against them based on their scheme to deceive government regulators absent evidence that directors benefited personally from scheme to detriment of bank because directors' knowledge of transactions involved in scheme was imputed to bank).

On dual agents, see [_Nat'l Credit Union Admin. v. Ticor Title Ins. Co., 873 F.Supp. 718, 727-728 (D.Mass. 1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-VSK0-001T-50DF-00000-00&context=1530671) (when dual agent acts adversely as to both principals, notice of agent's knowledge is imputed to neither); [_Lane v. Oustalet, 873 So. 2d 92, 97 (Miss.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4CCP-B0Y0-0039-4016-00000-00&context=1530671) (declining to establish bright-line rule of automatic imputation to both principals of dual agent, court states that proper analysis requires recognizing that "in a dual agency two distinct agencies are vested in the agent with separate duties and responsibilities as to each principal"; question of seller's liability for breach of contract should have been submitted to jury when real-estate agent and lawyer who represented both seller and purchaser received termite-inspection report and transmitted it to seller but not to buyer, because determinative question is whether seller acted reasonably in believing that dual agents would properly inform buyer); [_Williams v. State Med. Oxygen & Supply, Inc., 874 P.2d 1225, 1229 (Mont.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-3M80-003G-816B-00000-00&context=1530671) (whether knowledge of common officer of two corporations should be imputed "depends on the circumstances"; when corporation that owned truck dealt through common officer with corporation that used truck for moving supplies, officer's knowledge of how he would manage and supervise moving project should be imputed to corporation that owned truck); [_GXG, Inc. v. Texacal Oil & Gas, Inc., 977 S.W.2d 403, 410 (Tex. App.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3T10-72T0-0039-437B-00000-00&context=1530671) (knowledge of dual agent's fraudulent conduct toward one principal not automatically imputed to other principal; "\[t\]he evidence must demonstrate an affirmative link, not mere suspicion.").

In [_Farr v. Newman, 199 N.E.2d 369 (N.Y.1964)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-WMY0-003C-C00V-00000-00&context=1530671), in which buyer and seller used the same lawyer to effect a real-estate transaction, the lawyer learned of another claim against the property, asserted by the plaintiff. The buyer, having paid cash for the property and properly recorded his interest, would ordinarily have had priority over the plaintiffs claim. However, the court held that the lawyer's receipt of notice of the claim should be imputed to the buyer because "\[n\]othing can alter the fact that the attorney was held out as a proper person to whom notice of outstanding equities was to be given, and that his receipt of such notice from plaintiff was within his authority … Once the attorney received plaintiff's notification, as authorized by defendant, even a fraudulent or self-serving concealment of that fact from the defendant would no more extinguish plaintiff's protection than would a debtor's debt be revived where he had paid his creditor's authorized agent for collection, who thereafter embezzled the money collected." [_199 N.E.2d at 373_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-WMY0-003C-C00V-00000-00&context=1530671). However, the facts in _Farr,_ unlike those in [_Oustalet, 873 So. 2d 92_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4CCP-B0Y0-0039-4016-00000-00&context=1530671), do not raise the question of imputed knowledge as a basis for a defense against a principal's failure to discharge a duty owed by one principal to another.

For the point that the adverse-interest doctrine is unavailable to a third party who knows that an agent is acting adversely to a principal, see [_Ash v. Georgia-Pacific Corp., 957 F.2d 432, 436 (7th Cir.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-5BC0-008H-V4WH-00000-00&context=1530671); cf. [_Eastern Trading Co. v. Refco, Inc., 229 F.3d 617, 624 (7th Cir.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:41DB-R520-0038-X3N3-00000-00&context=1530671) (stating that doctrine applies when agent defrauds third party); [_Jensen v. IHC Hosps., Inc., 82 P.3d 1076, 1091 n.13 (Utah 2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4B12-SWJ0-0039-41VN-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[a\]lthough typically an agent's knowledge will not be imputed to its principal when an agent is involved in fraud or other adverse dealings, an exception exists for innocent third parties. Under agency law, 'the adverse dealings or fraud of an agent cannot alter the legal effect of his knowledge … with respect to his principal in regard to third persons who had _no connection whatever with such agent in relation to the perpetration of the adverse dealings or fraud.'")_ (quoting 3 Am. Jur. 2d Agency § 290, at 796 (1986)).

On imputation of notice to a corporation of facts known to its sole shareholder, see [_FDIC v. Ernst & Young, 967 F.2d 166, 171 (5th Cir. 1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-2220-008H-V2MS-00000-00&context=1530671) (sole shareholder dominated corporation's board of directors).

The doctrine applies to sole shareholders who exercise control over actions taken by a corporation. See [_In re The Mediators, Inc., 105 F.3d 822, 827 (2d Cir.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-JGY0-00B1-D4Y9-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[t\]his rule imputes the agent's knowledge to the principal notwithstanding the agent's self-dealing because the party that should have been informed was the agent itself albeit in its capacity as principal"; adverse-interest exception inapplicable when sole shareholder is alleged to have stripped corporation of its assets). It is also applicable to a person who exercises control who is not a corporation's sole shareholder. [_Demoulas v. Demoulas, 703 N.E.2d 1149, 1171-1172 (Mass.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3VN8-RMK0-0039-41JT-00000-00&context=1530671) (effect of doctrine is to impute to corporation knowledge of shareholder who exercised complete dominance over corporation).

The adverse-interest exception is inapplicable when all of an organization's relevant decisionmakers are involved in the wrongful conduct at issue. This point is often termed the "sole actor" rule, conceptualizing principal and agent as one. See [_Official Comm. of Unsecured Creditors of Color Tile, Inc. v. Coopers & Lybrand, LLP, 322 F.3d 147, 164-165 (2d Cir.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:480T-B0Y0-0038-X3C4-00000-00&context=1530671) (misconduct and knowledge of corporation's controlling shareholders in connection with transactions properly imputed to corporation; 3 directors on 5-member board who were not affiliates of controlling shareholders approved transactions knowing that both purchase price and debt to be incurred by corporation were excessive).

For an alternative statement of the doctrine, see [_Invest Almaz v. Temple-Inland Forest Prods. Corp., 243 F.3d 57, 74 (1st Cir.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:42KM-7880-0038-X0WW-00000-00&context=1530671) (dictum; stating that "\[i\]n the majority of jurisdictions, the law has evolved towards a recognition that information given to even a fraudulent agent should normally be imputed to the principal, unless the third party providing the information has notice that the agent is acting adversely or otherwise colludes with the faithless agent."). The court's opinion in _Invest Almaz_ calls into question the continuing vitality of a New Hampshire precedent, _Boucouvalas v. John Hancock Mut. Life Ins. Co., 5 A.2d 721 (N.H. 1939)_. In _Boucouvalas,_ the court did not impute to a life insurer its agent's knowledge that an applicant for insurance had a serious illness. The agent deliberately omitted relevant information from the insurer's application form, which the agent completed for the applicant, who was illiterate. _Invest Almaz_ notes that other courts are "virtually unanimous" in imputing notice of an agent's knowledge to the insurer unless the applicant has notice of the agent's fraud. See [_243 F.3d at 74_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:42KM-7880-0038-X0WW-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Schmidt v. Fortis Ins. Co., 349 F.Supp.2d 1171, 1199-1200 (N.D.Iowa 2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4F5R-3F60-TVTR-T2JW-00000-00&context=1530671) (under Iowa law, agent's knowledge of insured's health history imputed to insurer, "the principal he purported to serve"; no evidence insured participated in plan with agent to defraud insurer).

On the practical impact of the doctrine, see [_In re The Mediators, Inc., 105 F.3d at 826_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-JGY0-00B1-D4Y9-00000-00&context=1530671) (trustee or unsecured creditor's committee, asserting claims that corporation could have instituted prior to its bankruptcy, lacks standing to assert aiding-and-abetting claims against third parties when corporation's sole agent initiated misconduct); see also [_Shearson Lehman Hutton, Inc. v. Wagoner, 944 F.2d 114, 120 (2d Cir.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-8FH0-008H-V024-00000-00&context=1530671) (claim against third party alleging fraud against corporation belongs to corporation's creditors, not to guilty corporation itself, on basis that managerial misconduct is normally imputed to corporation); [_Wight v. BankAmerica Corp., 219 F.3d 79, 87 (2d Cir.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40BT-XXG0-0038-X0CP-00000-00&context=1530671) (recognizing applicability of adverse-interest exception to imputation when claim brought by liquidator).

A plaintiff may attempt to sidestep the operation of the "sole actor" doctrine by alleging the existence of at least one member of management who, with knowledge, could have prevented the fraud. Some courts reject this argument categorically. See [_Cenco, 686 F.2d at 454-456_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-3K00-003B-G042-00000-00&context=1530671) (emphasizing importance of maintaining proper incentives for shareholders, directors, and members of management to police conduct of others); [_CBI, 311 B.R. at 372_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4CS5-WWH0-0038-Y462-00000-00&context=1530671) (accord; "misconduct by those given authority to make decisions on behalf of a company should be imputed to the company even if innocent members of management could and would have prevented the fraud had they been aware of it."). Other courts recognize or do not reject the possibility of an "innocent insider" exception, while finding the exception inapplicable on the facts. See [_Official Comm. of Unsecured Creditors of Color Tile, Inc. v. Coopers & Lybrand, 322 F.3d 147 (2d Cir.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:480T-B0Y0-0038-X3C4-00000-00&context=1530671) (dismissing claim against auditor brought by creditors' committee when all members of corporation's board of directors, along with controlling shareholders, approved of problematic transaction). See also [_FDIC v. Ernst & Young, 967 F.2d 166, 171 (5th Cir.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-2220-008H-V2MS-00000-00&context=1530671) (in action brought by FDIC as receiver against auditor, claim barred on basis that knowledge of corporation's sole shareholder is imputed to corporation; court dismisses FDIC's argument that properly done audit would have alerted corporation's creditors or regulators to rescue corporation because "it is not an appropriate argument for \[corporation\], or its assignee, to make. \[Corporation\] cannot claim it should recover from \[auditor\] for not being rescued by a third party for something \[corporation\] was already aware of and chose to ignore. . . . The FDIC in its own capacity or \[corporation's\] creditors might be able to make this claim, but the FDIC brought this suit only on \[corporation's\] behalf"). For a case accepting the argument, see _Wechsler v. Squadron, Ellenoff, Plesent & Sheinfeld, L.L.P., 212 B.R. 34, 36 (S.D.N.Y.1997)_ (fraud is imputed to corporation and bankruptcy trustee is barred from bringing suit only when _"all_ relevant shareholders and/or decisionmakers are involved in the fraud") (emphasis in original). See also [_In re Bennett Funding Group, Inc., 336 F.3d 94, 101 (2d Cir.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:492X-3R00-0038-X1FJ-00000-00&context=1530671) (facts of case make it unnecessary to resolve question "of whether the presence of innocent directors would provide the trustee with standing where fewer than all shareholders are implicated in the fraud....").

Cases that place a trustee or receiver in larger shoes than those of the bankrupt corporation itself include [_Schacht v. Brown, 711 F.2d 1343, 1348 (7th Cir.1983)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-0CX0-003B-G107-00000-00&context=1530671) (officer's fraud is not imputed to corporation when action against auditor brought by liquidator; no evidence that wrongdoers themselves would benefit from recovery or that corporation had large shareholders who could have conducted an independent audit); [_Welt v. Sirmans, 3 F.Supp.2d 1396, 1402-1403 (S.D.Fla.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SK7-TS20-0038-Y2W0-00000-00&context=1530671) (predicting course of Florida law, court declines to impute officer's fraud to corporation in action brought by trustee in bankruptcy against corporation's outside law firm). See also [_Comeau v. Rupp, 810 F.Supp. 1127, 1141 (D.Kan. 1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-FS10-008H-F4V8-00000-00&context=1530671) (court analogizes FDIC to creditor and does not impute wrongful managerial acts to FDIC in FDIC's action against auditor on basis that public should not pay for wrongs it was in no position to prevent).

A receiver's position may turn on the sequence of wrongful acts as well as the identity of defendants. Compare [_Scholes v. Lehmann, 56 F.3d 750 (7th Cir.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-F730-001T-D3PP-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporation's receiver has standing under fraudulent-conveyance statute to recover funds from transferees who received funds from perpetrator of Ponzi scheme) with [_Knauer v. Jonathon Roberts Fin. Group, Inc., 348 F.3d 230, 236 (7th Cir.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:49V0-9M30-0038-X4SD-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporate receiver lacks standing to pursue claim for tort damages against entities allegedly to blame for agents' embezzlements integral to Ponzi scheme when entities derived no benefit from embezzlements).

_d. Benefit of agent's action knowingly retained by principal._ For the basic point, see [_National Union Fire Ins. Co. v. Bonnanzio, 91 F.3d 296 (2d Cir.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-1M70-006F-M3MV-00000-00&context=1530671) (in part question of fact whether bankrupt individual benefited from tax fraud resulting from transactions structured by her investment advisor); [_National Credit Union Admin. v. Ticor Title Ins. Co., 873 F.Supp. 718, 727 (D.Mass.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-VSK0-001T-50DF-00000-00&context=1530671) (when officers acted adversely to credit union in loan transaction and failed to disclose prior interest as against property held by sellers, notice of officer's knowledge of prior interest should be imputed to lender when otherwise lender would acquire value from issuer of title-insurance policy, an innocent party).

On ratification and the adverse-interest exception, see [_In re Bennett Funding Group, Inc., 336 F.3d 94, 100-101 (2d Cir.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:492X-3R00-0038-X1FJ-00000-00&context=1530671) (sole shareholders of closely held family corporation were aware of and acquiesced in fraudulent actions of corporation's CFO, who exercised "unfettered control" over corporation's financial operations with "full approval" of sole shareholders; immaterial that corporation's board included innocent members when they lacked any power to stop fraud).

In [_In re Payroll Express Corp., 186 F.3d 196, 208-209 (2d Cir.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XG0-YTH0-0038-X0BN-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying New Jersey law), the court held that the adverse-interest exception to imputation was inapplicable when an action against an insurer was brought by the corporation's trustee in bankruptcy, noting that at least one court permitted a bankruptcy trustee to invoke the adverse-interest exception to enforce an insurance policy on comparable facts. See [_Shields v. National Union Fire Ins. Co., 153 B.R. 677 (E.D.Pa.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-V5C0-001T-6158-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Pennsylvania law). In contrast to the insurance applications in _Payroll Express,_ some applications specify that false statements made by the person who signs the application for the insured are not imputed to the insured. See [_Phoenix Sav. & Loan, Inc. v. Aetna Cas. & Sur. Co., 427 F.2d 862, 870-871 (4th Cir.1970)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-KY90-0039-X39H-00000-00&context=1530671). Questions of construction are also relevant. See, e.g., [_Golden Door Jewelry Creations, Inc. v. Lloyds Underwriters Non-Marine Assoc., 117 F.3d 1328, 1337-1339 (11th Cir.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-DTN0-00B1-D4TD-00000-00&context=1530671) (court determines, on basis of reading insurance policy as a whole, that chief executive officer who stole from insured corporation was not an "employee" for purposes of language in policy of theft insurance excluding loss resulting from dishonest act of insured or its employee; policy elsewhere distinguished between officers and employees). The court permits an insured to assert the adverse-interest exception as against an insurer on facts comparable to [_Payroll Express in Puget Sound Nat'l Bank v. St. Paul Fire & Marine Ins. Co., 645 P.2d 1122, 1126-1128 (Wash.App.1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-XPN0-003F-W1VM-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 10 is based on [_Munroe v. Harriman, 85 F.2d 493 (2d Cir. 1936)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-TS80-003B-K504-00000-00&context=1530671).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 6.01_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8W0-00YG-4001-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 6- Contracts and Other Transactions with Third Parties_ > _Topic 1- Parties to Contracts_**

**§ 6.01 Agent for Disclosed Principal**

**When an agent acting with actual or apparent authority makes a contract on behalf of a disclosed principal,**

**(1) the principal and the third party are parties to the contract; and**

**(2) the agent is not a party to the contract unless the agent and third party agree otherwise.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section states the basic principle that when an agent enters into a contract on behalf of a disclosed principal, the principal and the third party are parties to the contract. A principal is disclosed when the third party has notice that an agent is acting for a principal and has notice of the principal's identity. See § 1.04(2)(a). An agent does not become a party to a contract made on behalf of a disclosed principal unless the agent and the third party so agree. Comment _b_ discusses the bases for these doctrines. Comment _c_ discusses the circumstances relevant to determining whether an agent has entered into a contract on behalf of a disclosed principal. Comment _d_ discusses agreements between an agent and a third party that may make the agent a party to a contract and other circumstances that may subject the agent to liability. Comment _e_ discusses the position of an agent who is a party to a contract. Comment _f_ discusses sealed and negotiable instruments.

Section 1.04(2) defines the terms "disclosed principal," "unidentified principal," and "undisclosed principal." Sections 2.01 and 2.02 define "actual authority." Section 3.01 states how a principal may create actual authority. Section 2.03 defines "apparent authority." Section 3.03 states how a principal may create apparent authority.

_b. Bases and consequences of contractual liability when agent acts on behalf of disclosed principal._ An agent acts on behalf of a disclosed principal when the third party with whom the agent deals has notice that the agent acts for a principal and also has notice of the principal's identity. See § 1.04(2)(a). An agent has power to make contracts on behalf of the agent's principal when the agent acts with actual or apparent authority. This power is consistent with the bargain principle in contemporary contract law under which "the formation of a contract requires a bargain in which there is a manifestation of mutual assent to the exchange and a consideration." Restatement Second, Contracts § 17(1). An agent enters into a contract on behalf of the agent's principal by manifesting assent to an exchange that constitutes valid consideration. The third party manifests assent to the exchange to the agent, having notice that the agent acts on behalf of a particular principal.

A principal may manifest assent to the agent's power to commit the principal either to the agent or to third parties. If the principal's manifestation is made to the agent and the agent acts reasonably in accordance with the manifestation, the agent acts with actual authority. See §§ 2.01, 2.02, and 3.01. If the third party reasonably believes that the agent acts with authority and that belief is traceable to a manifestation made by the principal to the third party, the agent acts with apparent authority. See §§ 2.03 and 3.03. Actual authority does not require that apparent authority be present.

**Illustration:**

1\. P, a wine merchant, engages A to dispose of portions of P's inventory. P directs A to sell all of P's inventory of French wines to T, another wine merchant. A makes an offer to T on P's behalf. T replies: "I do not believe that you have authority to sell all of P's French wine, but I will chance it and accept the offer." There is a contract between P and T. A had actual authority to bind P, although T doubts whether this is so.

A principal may also be subject to liability as a consequence of an agent's promise, unsupported by consideration, that should reasonably be expected to induce action or forbearance on the part of a third party. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 90(1), which states that "\[a\] promise which the promisor should reasonably expect to induce action or forbearance on the part of the promisee or a third person and which does induce such action or forbearance is binding if injustice can be avoided only by enforcement of the promise." If a third party is induced by an agent's promise to act or to forbear from acting, the principal is subject to liability when the agent acted with actual or apparent authority. The court may award such remedy as justice requires. Id.

**Illustration:**

2\. T, who owns and operates a bakery, desires to go into the supermarket business. T approaches A, who is the district manager of P Corporation, a franchisor of supermarkets. A tells T that for \$ 18,000 P Corporation will establish T in a supermarket. A also advises T to move to another town and buy a small grocery to gain experience. T does so. Representatives from P Corporation's home office visit T's grocery operation and inspect T's financial records. Later A advises T to sell both the bakery and the grocery, saying "Everything is ready to go. Get your money together and we are set." T complies, incurring a capital loss on each sale and forgoing expected profits from the summer tourist trade. Still later, A tells T that considerably more than an \$ 18,000 investment will be needed. Negotiations among T, A, and A's superiors at P Corporation collapse. At the point of collapse, many details of the proposed agreement between T and P Corporation are unresolved. P Corporation is subject to liability to T if A acted with actual or apparent authority in A's dealings with T. P Corporation's liability may be limited to T's actual losses sustained in reasonable reliance on promises made by A, including loss from the sale of the grocery and the bakery and T's expenses for moving and temporary accommodations.

An agent who enters into a contract on behalf of a disclosed principal does not become a party to the contract and is not subject to liability as a guarantor of the principal's performance unless the agent and the third party so agree. Thus, in the absence of such agreement, an agent for a disclosed principal who enters into a contract on the principal's behalf is not subject to liability if the principal fails to perform obligations created by the contract. As a consequence, the agent is not a necessary party to breach-of-contract litigation between a disclosed principal and the third party to a contract made by the agent on the principal's behalf.

Through ratification, a person may become a party to a contract purportedly made on that person's behalf by another who acted without actual or apparent authority. See Chapter 4.
