If parties to a contract, a principal and third party have the same rights, liabilities, and defenses against each other as if the principal had made the contract directly, subject to §§ 6.05-6.09. A disclosed principal may assert against the third party all defenses that arise out of the contract itself and all defenses that are personal to the principal. The principal may not assert defenses that would have been personal to the agent had the agent been a party to the contract. On the defenses available to an agent who is a party to a contract, see Comment _e._

If a disclosed principal is not subject to liability on a contract purportedly made on the principal's behalf by an agent who lacked power to bind the principal, the agent may be subject to liability to the third party for breaching the agent's warranty of authority as stated in § 6.10. However, the fact that an agent acted without power to subject the principal to liability does not make the agent a party to the contract. This is because an agent who only purports to bind a disclosed principal to a contract does not promise to render any of the performance purportedly required from the principal.

A principal who deals with third parties through an agent ordinarily expects to become a party to transactions entered into on the principal's behalf by the agent. When the third party knows the identity of the principal, the third party ordinarily expects that the principal, and not the agent, will become a party to the transaction. This is because third parties do not ordinarily rely on agents' solvency or ability to perform the promises required to consummate a transaction. An agent and a third party, however, may agree otherwise. Comment _d_ discusses circumstances under which an agent may become subject to liability as a party to a contract.

An agent who acts on behalf of a disclosed principal may enter into a contract with a third party that by its terms excludes the principal as a party. Although the agent may, by entering into the contract, have breached the agent's duties to the principal by not making the principal a party to it, the principal is not a party to the contract. This is because the third party has not manifested assent to an exchange with the principal.

An agent may enter into a contract on behalf of a disclosed principal and, additionally, enter into a separate contract on the agent's own behalf with the same third party. The agent's failure to perform the separate contract does not affect the principal's liability under the contract made by the agent on behalf of the principal.

**Illustration:**

3\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that A tells T that, if T buys P's inventory of French wines, A will sell wine racks to T. T accepts. A fails to deliver the wine racks. A's failure to perform does not affect P's liability to T, or T's liability to P, on the contract for the wine.

It is also possible that the same actor's status in one transaction may differ in relation to different third parties, such that one third party may be entitled to treat the actor as a principal while the other third party is entitled to treat the actor as only an agent on behalf of a disclosed principal. For example, within the shipping industry, non-vessel-operating common carriers ("NVOCCs") typically operate as middlemen who retrieve small shipments from different shippers and consolidate the resulting load for shipment by a vessel or vessel-owning common carrier. As to each shipper, an NVOCC is a common carrier but, with respect to the vessel and her owners, an NVOCC is characterized as an agent of the shipper. As a consequence, a shipper who uses an NVOCC's services has privity of contract with a vessel and her owner through a bill of lading issued to the NVOCC for carriage of goods belonging to the shipper.

_c. Contract made on behalf of a disclosed principal._ If an agent makes a contract in the name of a principal or a description in the contract is sufficient to identify the principal, the principal is a disclosed principal and is a party to the contract. When a disclosed principal is a corporation or other organization with separate legal personality, the corporation becomes a party to contracts made on its behalf by its agents. Corporate or other organizational agents, like agents for other disclosed principals, are not parties to a corporate contract unless the agent and the third party so agree. See Comment _d(2)_ for further discussion.

Additionally, a principal may be disclosed even though the contract does not name or identify the principal; it is sufficient that the third party has notice of the principal's identity. Compare § 1.04(2)(a) with § 1.04(2)(c). Unless the contract explicitly excludes the principal as a party, parol evidence is admissible to identify a principal and to subject the principal to liability on a contract made by an agent. The parol-evidence rule does not bar proof that an agent made a contract on behalf of a principal. In contrast, the rule bars proof offered to delete a person identified in a contract as a party to that contract. Thus, if a contract explicitly makes a principal a party, parol evidence is not admissible to show agreement that the principal would not become a party.

**Illustrations:**

4\. P, a farmer, incorporates the farm business under the name "P Farms Corp." P operates the business of P Farms Corp. as its chief executive officer. On behalf of and in the name of P Farms Corp., P purchases supplies on credit from T. P is not a party to, and is not subject to liability on, contracts between P Farms Corp. and T.

5\. Same facts as Illustration 4, except that P purchases the supplies for delivery to the farm but does not specify whether P buys them in the name of P Farms Corp. Parol evidence would be necessary to resolve the ambiguity whether P and T intended P personally or P Farms Corp. to be the party to the contract.

6\. Same facts as Illustration 4, except that P does not incorporate the farming business. Instead, P leases the farm land to S, P's son, who operates the farm business using the assumed name "P Farms." P continues to live in a residence on the farm. S purchases supplies for the farm on credit from T, which T delivers to the farm. All of S's purchases are pursuant to orders signed "P Farms--S." P occasionally signs receipts for the supplies when T delivers them. Unless parol evidence so establishes, P is not a party to contracts between S and T. S is a party to contracts with T.

7\. P, who owns and operates a farm, retains A to buy livestock, instructing A not to disclose P's name or identity if A is able to buy the livestock without doing so. A attempts to buy livestock from T, saying that A is acting on behalf of a principal. T refuses to sell without knowing the identity of A's principal. A names P. T replies, "P will be good for the price." T and A draft a sales contract that names A as the purchaser. T delivers the livestock to A, who delivers them to P. It may be inferred that P is a party to the contract although the contract names A and not P as the purchaser.

If a statute of frauds is applicable to a contract, the contract may not be enforced against a party unless there is a written memorandum signed by that party or an applicable exception exists. A writing or other record may satisfy the Statute of Frauds if it is "signed by or on behalf of the party to be charged. . . ." See Restatement Second, Contracts § 131. However, an agent's authority to sign on a principal's behalf may itself require a writing or record signed by the principal. See § 3.02. To satisfy the Statute of Frauds, a memorandum must also be sufficient to identify the parties to the contract. A memorandum that identifies a party's agent sufficiently refers to the party, whether or not the agent is also a party to the contract. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 131, Comment _f._

Whether the existence and identity of any principal have been disclosed are questions of fact. The third party with whom an agent deals does not have a burden of inquiry. A third party's failure to inquire whether an agent acts on behalf of a principal is not tantamount to disclosure of the principal's existence and identity. A third party will be treated as having notice of an agency relationship if the third party has actual knowledge of it or reason to know of it or if the third party has been given a notification of it. See § 1.04(4), which defines these terms. See § 5.02 for further discussion of notifications.

A principal has not been disclosed simply because a third party could conceivably have discovered that the agent acted as the principal's representative. Practical reasons justify placing the burden of disclosure on the agent, who has both the means of informing the third party and the motivation to do so.

Absent explicit disclosure to the third party, an agent may argue that the circumstances surrounding the agent's dealings with the third party gave the third party reason to know that the agent dealt only as the principal's representative. It is a question of fact whether facts known by a third party gave the third party reason to know that person with whom it dealt acted as agent on behalf of a disclosed principal. An agent's use of or disclosure of a trade name does not by itself constitute disclosure of a principal. If the trade name has been registered to comply with a statute requiring registration of assumed names, the fact of registration in itself should not be treated as sufficient to give notice of the identity of an agent's principal unless the registration statute explicitly so provides. Likewise, if an agent, in fulfilling a contractual commitment, pays a creditor using a check drawn on the account of a corporate or other organizational principal, it is a question of fact whether, in subsequent transactions between the parties, the creditor should be deemed to have received sufficient notice that the agent dealt with the creditor only as the organization's agent.

Ordinarily, to avoid personal liability on a contract, an agent must disclose the principal's identity at the time of making the contract. However, an agent may avoid personal liability when performance under the contract is divisible and the third party has not yet rendered portions of its performance. If the third party receives sufficient notice of the principal's identity while a divisible contract is still executory in part and the agent and the third party have not agreed to the contrary, the agent is not subject to liability if the third party thereafter continues to perform. The third party, having received notice of the principal's identity, may choose how subsequent dealings should be conducted, whether with the agent or with the principal. Continuing to deal with notice that it is the principal who will be liable, a third party may no longer argue that its performance was induced by reliance on the agent's credit or other ability to undertake the obligations of performance.

**Illustration:**

8\. A manages commercial buildings on behalf of the limited partnerships that own them. A enters into a contract with T under which T will provide janitorial services for three years to the buildings in exchange for monthly payments, but A does not disclose to T that A acts only as the representative of the buildings' owners. Several months later, A tells T that A acts only as the buildings' manager and discloses the identity of their owners. T continues to perform janitorial services. A is not personally liable for the monthly payments that became due to T after A disclosed the identity of the buildings' owners.

The result in Illustration 8 is fair to T. If P's credit quality is less than A's, T may refuse to continue performing if A does not agree to remain liable.

A person who wishes to become a party to a contract, acting as an undisclosed or unidentified principal, may direct an agent not to disclose the principal's identity to those with whom the agent deals on the principal's behalf. If the agent then discloses the principal's identity notwithstanding the principal's instructions, or the principal's identity otherwise becomes known, the principal is subject to liability on a contract that the agent makes on the principal's behalf if the agent acts with actual or apparent authority. Third parties are not bound by the principal's wish and instructions that its identity not be disclosed. A principal, however, is not subject to liability on a contract that by its terms excludes the principal as a party. See § 6.03(1).

If an agent acts with actual or apparent authority in making a contract on behalf of a principal, the principal should be bound by the contract even though the principal claims that maintaining secrecy of the principal's identity was essential to achieving the principal's objectives. This statement differs from Restatement Second, Agency § 163, which contains a caveat taking no position on whether a principal is subject to liability on a contract when disclosure of the principal's identity or existence may defeat the principal's purposes.

Although a principal may believe that knowledge of the principal's identity will affect the price at which third parties are willing to deal or their willingness to deal on any terms, an agent nevertheless may act with actual authority in ignoring the principal's instructions that the principal's identity not be divulged. For example, an agent may disregard such an instruction when the agent reasonably believes, on the basis of manifestations made by the principal, that the principal wishes the agent so to act. See § 2.02, Comment _f._ If so, the agent has acted with actual authority.

In these kinds of cases, moreover, liability may be imposed on a principal even though an agent acts without actual authority in disclosing the principal's identity or in making a contract with a third party, knowing that the third party has otherwise learned the principal's identity. On the basis of a manifestation traceable to the principal, a third party may reasonably believe that an agent acts with actual authority. See § 3.03, Comment _b._ If so, the agent acts with apparent authority. If a third party has notice that the principal wishes the agent to make a contract with the third party only if the principal's identity remains unknown, it is not likely that the third party would reasonably believe that the agent acts with actual authority. See § 2.03, Comment _d._ Moreover, if a principal has notice that third parties may deal with the agent and change position in the belief that the agent has authority to bind the principal, the principal may be estopped to deny the agent's authority. See § 2.05. A principal may also ratify an agent's actions in dealings with third parties if, having knowledge of them, the principal fails to object. See § 4.01, Comment _f._

If a principal may disregard contracts made by an agent on the basis that disclosure of the principal's identity or existence defeated the principal's purposes, a principal may be tempted to speculate after the fact at the expense of third parties. A principal is not likely so to claim, regardless of instructions actually given to an agent, when a contract made by an agent turns out to be advantageous for the principal. A third party is not in a position to monitor interactions between principal and agent when, it is claimed, such an instruction was given.

_d. Agreement that agent shall be a party to contract_

_(1). In general._ When an agent enters into a contract on behalf of a disclosed principal, the agent is not a party to the contract unless the agent and the third party so agree. The third party has the burden of showing a manifestation of assent by the agent to such an agreement. Loose usage by an agent of such terms as "I," "me," "mine," "we," or "ours" in referring to a business does not, standing alone, constitute a manifestation of assent to be bound personally but may corroborate other evidence.

**Illustrations:**

9\. P, a dealer in rare books, retains A to buy books on P's account. A negotiates to buy a book on P's account from T, who knows that A represents P. A says, "I will buy the book for \$ 200." In the absence of other facts, A is not a party to the contract.

10\. Same facts as Illustration 9, except that in negotiations with A, T is represented by T's agent, B. B says to A, "I warrant that the book is a first edition." In the absence of other facts, neither A nor B is a party to the warranty. On whether legal relations between P and T are affected by B's representation, see § 6.11(2) and (3).

An agent is not a party to a contract if any portion of the parties' writing makes clear that the agent acts solely in a representative capacity on behalf of a disclosed principal. It is not necessary that this point recur throughout the writing nor, if the writing otherwise makes this point, is it necessary that the agent sign the writing in a representative capacity.

A third party's knowledge of an agent's capacity, obtained from prior transactions, is deemed to continue for subsequent transactions of the same character and between the same parties. Unless a third party has notice that circumstances have changed, it is reasonable for a third party to assume that an agent's relationship with a principal is an ongoing condition and that the agent's capacity remains operative for subsequent similar transactions among the same parties. See § 3.11, Comment _c,_ for discussion of the related doctrine of lingering apparent authority.

**Illustrations:**

11\. P, a professional baseball player, retains A Corporation as his agent to represent him in making contracts to provide product endorsements, make commercials, and license the use of his name and image. Represented by A, the President of A Corporation, P makes two contracts with T Corporation under which, in exchange for royalties, P's name, facsimile signature, or image will be affixed to various products. Under the first contract, P's facsimile signature and, where appropriate, image will be affixed to numerous products, which include stamps, medals, and baseball cards. Under the second contract, executed nine months later, P's name will be affixed to teddy bears. P signs all three contracts in P's individual capacity. A signs the first agreement "A for A Corporation." A signs the second contract "A" without making reference to A Corporation. Neither contract elsewhere mentions A. Neither A nor A Corporation is a party to either contract. A's failure to sign the second agreement "A for A Corporation" does not constitute a manifestation by A of agreement to become a party to the agreement, nor does A's failure so to sign make the second contract ambiguous.

12\. Same facts as Illustration 11, except that the two contracts also include covenants that neither A Corporation nor P shall grant licenses to any other persons for any products covered by the agreements without T Corporation's consent. A Corporation is bound by those covenants.

The manner in which an agent's name appears in a contract is often relevant to establishing whether the agent agreed to become a party to the contract because it may establish whether the agent has manifested assent to become a party to the contract. If a contract names both the principal and the agent, in the absence of a manifestation to the contrary the agent is not a party to the contract if the contract indicates that the agent is named only as agent. Such an indication may be made in many ways. These include statements of: (1) the principal's name followed by the agent's name preceded by a preposition such as "by" or "per"; and (2) the agent's name followed by the principal's name, separated by a word or phrase such as "for," "agent of," "agent for," "as agent of," or "on behalf of." See also Comment _d(2),_ which discusses organizational executives and agents.

The manner in which an agent signs a document is also relevant to establishing whether the agent has manifested assent to be a party. Using language that simply describes a person as an agent is insufficient to indicate that the person acts only in a representative capacity and does not agree to become a party. Language that only describes a person as an agent, like language that states a person's address, occupation, or marital status, does not indicate whether the person agrees to become a party to a contract.

If an agent does not become a party to a contract and is not subject to liability on the contract as an individual, the agent should not be able to assert rights as an individual derived from the contract in the absence of indicia that the parties to the contract so intended. Of course, as against the principal, the agent may be entitled to payment for services performed as an agent although the agent does not become a party to a contract made on behalf of the principal.

**Illustration:**

13\. T, an inventor of underground-shelter technology, incorporates T Corporation to manufacture underground storage tanks and personal shelters. T negotiates an agreement with A under which A Corporation, a corporation formed by A, will acquire the shares of T Corporation from its shareholders. A signs the acquisition agreement as President of A Corporation. The agreement provides that "disputes under this Agreement shall be resolved by arbitration." T sues A asserting personal claims against A that do not arise under the acquisition agreement. A may not compel T to arbitrate these claims. A as an individual is not a party to the acquisition agreement and may not assert rights derived from the agreement. Moreover, T's claims against A do not arise under the agreement.

Notwithstanding the general rule, in some situations an agent is subject to liability on a contract made on behalf of a disclosed principal although the agent has not expressly assumed liability. Custom and practice in particular industries make an agent primarily liable, and the principal secondarily liable, for goods or services provided by third parties who are likely to rely on the agent's credit, not the principal's. A lawyer who contracts with a third party for goods or services used by lawyers is subject to liability to the third party when the lawyer knows or reasonably should know that the third party relies on the lawyer's credit although the third party knows the identity of the lawyer's client, unless at the time of contracting the lawyer has disclaimed such liability. See Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 30(2)(b). Such a service provider who deals regularly with lawyers is likely to rely on their credit and may find it too costly to investigate the credit of clients. Moreover, if the lawyer uses the same provider's services in work for many clients, absent the lawyer's liability the service provider will be compelled to pursue many separate claims. In a litigation context, third parties like court reporters may provide repetitive services, relying on the credit of the lawyer who requests the service. In a transactional context, a real-estate lawyer may regularly use the services of appraisers who rely on the lawyer's credit. Additionally, established maritime practice sometimes makes a freight forwarder personally liable to an ocean carrier for freight charges, notwithstanding the fact that the carrier knows the identity of the shipper. In both instances, the agent--the lawyer or the freight forwarder--may recover the third party's charges from its principal. See § 8.14 for an agent's right to indemnity from the principal.

Of course, such contracts made by agents on behalf of principals differ from a contract that an agent makes solely on the agent's own behalf so that the agent may do work on behalf of a principal. Statutes may also impose liability on agents for their principals' contractual obligations.

_(2). Organizational executives and other agents._ The basic principles discussed in Comment _d(1)_ are applicable when a principal is an organization, such as a corporation or limited-liability company. An organizational executive does not become subject to personal liability on a contract as a consequence of executing a document in the executive's organizational capacity or as a consequence of holding that office. An executive's agreement to become a party to a contract made on behalf of the organization may be shown by language in the agreement itself that names the officer individually as a party. Other indicia of intention may also be relevant, such as business records maintained by the third party that indicate whether it was intended that the individual would be a party in addition to or instead of the organization. The nature of the parties' contract may also establish whether an organizational executive agreed to be individually liable.

**Illustration:**

14\. A, the president of P Corporation, executes a lease on behalf of P Corporation as lessee with T as lessor. The body of the agreement states that "in the event the net worth of P Corporation is reduced in excess of ten percent, it is agreed that A shall guarantee P Corporation's obligations hereunder to the extent of the shortfall." The lease is signed "P Corporation, by A, President." A is individually liable as a guarantor to the extent of the shortfall. Although A's signature indicates that A signs in a representative capacity only, limiting the effect of A's signature would nullify the effect of the language in the body of the lease stating that A individually guarantees P Corporation's net worth.

_e. Agent's position as party to contract._ An agent for a disclosed principal who agrees to be a party to a contract made on behalf of the principal is subject to duties of performance as stated in the contract. For example, in Illustration 12, A Corporation's duty to T Corporation is to refrain from granting licenses to any other persons to use P's name, facsimile signature, or image for products covered by the contracts without T Corporation's consent. An agent may also agree to become subject to liability to the third party if the principal does not satisfy duties of performance under the contract.

In an action against an agent who is a party to a contract, the agent may assert all defenses that arise out of the contract itself and all defenses that are personal to the agent. However, a principal may not assert defenses that are personal to the agent. See Comment _b._

An agent who enters into a contract on behalf of a disclosed principal may have actual or apparent authority to sue on the contract in the principal's name on behalf of the principal. An agent who enters into a contract on behalf of a disclosed principal may not sue on the contract in the agent's own name, even if so authorized by the principal, unless the agent does so in another capacity, for example, as a party to the contract or an assignee. If an agent is a party to a contract, the agent may sue on it in the agent's name, the principal's name, or both. In maritime affairs, the practice is that agents may sue in their own names on their principal's behalf.

_f. Sealed and negotiable instruments._ Unless a statute provides otherwise, whether a principal is subject to liability on a contract is not affected by whether the contract is in the form of a sealed instrument. This is so whether the principal is disclosed, unidentified, or undisclosed. See § 6.03, Comment _f,_ for discussion of this point as it concerns undisclosed principals. U.C.C. § 2-203 makes law respecting sealed instruments inapplicable to contracts for the sale of goods or offers for such contracts. In contrast, Restatement Second, Agency § 151 provided that, in the absence of a statute providing otherwise, a disclosed or partially disclosed principal "is not a covenantor or grantor in a sealed contract or conveyance" unless the principal appears upon the instrument as such.

Negotiable instruments are governed by U.C.C. Article 3. Under U.C.C. § 3-401(a), a person is not liable on an instrument unless the person signed the instrument or "the person is represented by an agent or representative who signed the instrument and the signature is binding on the represented person under Section 3-402." Under § 3-402(a), a person is bound by a representative's signature "to the same extent the represented person would be bound if the signature were on a simple contract." This is so whether the representative signs the instrument using either the name of the represented person or the name of the signer. In contrast, under Restatement Second, Agency § 152, a disclosed or partially disclosed principal was not liable as a party to a negotiable instrument that did not name the principal.

Under U.C.C. § 3-402(c), a representative who signs the representative's name as drawer of a check that is payable from an account of a represented person who is identified on the check is not liable on the check if the signature is an authorized signature of the represented person. This is so even when the representative signs the check without indication of the representative status. The rationale is that virtually all contemporary checks are in a personalized form that identifies the person on whose account the check is drawn. If an agent or other representative signs without affixing a separate statement of representative status, "nobody is deceived into thinking that the person signing the check is meant to be liable." U.C.C § 3-402, Official Comment 3. The drafters intended § 3-402(c) to overrule cases decided under former Article 3 that imposed personal liability on agents who did not indicate their agency status.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency, and codifications._ This section is a consolidated treatment of points made in many separate sections of Restatement Second, Agency, including §§ 144, 146, 147, 149, 150, 151, 152, 155, 156, 157, 159, 163, 292, 293, 296, 320, 327, 328, 334, 363, and 364.

Codified treatments of agency contain general statements that are relevant to the points covered by this section. Under Cal. Civ. Code § 2330 (1985 & Supp. 2004), "\[a\]n agent represents his principal for all purposes within the scope of his actual or ostensible authority, and all the rights and liabilities which would accrue to the agent from transactions within such limit, if they had been entered into in his own account, accrue to the principal." Under § 2343, an agent is responsible to third parties with whom the agent deals: "1. When, with his consent, credit is given to him personally in a transaction; 2. When he enters into a written contract in the name of his principal, without believing, in good faith, that he has authority to do so; or 3. When his acts are wrongful in their nature."

Under Ga. Code § 10-6-51 (1996 & Supp. 2004), "\[t\]he principal shall be bound by the acts of his agent within the scope of his authority. . . ." Under § 10-6-62, "\[t\]he principal shall have advantage of his agent's contracts in the same manner as he shall be bound by them, so far as they come within the scope of his agency." Section 10-6-80 provides that "\[a\]ny act authorized or required to be done under this Code by any person in the prosecution of his legal remedies may be done by his agent. . . ." Although under § 10-6-82 an agent generally has no right of action on contracts made for a principal, an agent has a right of action as "(1) A factor contracting on his own credit; (2) Where promissory notes or other evidences of debt are made payable to an agent of a corporation; (3) In all cases where the contract is made with the agent in his individual name, though his agency be known; (4) Auctioneers may sue in their own names for goods sold by them; (5) In cases of agency coupled with an interest in the agent, known to the party contracting with him."

The Louisiana Civil Code provides in art. 3016 that "\[a\] mandatary who contracts in the name of the principal within the limits of his authority does not bind himself personally for the performance of the contract." La. Civ. Code Art. 3016 (2005). Under art. 3019, "\[a\] mandatary who exceeds his authority is personally bound to the third person with whom he contracts, unless that person knew at the time the contract was made that the mandatary had exceeded his authority or unless the principal ratifies the contract." Art. 3020 obliges a principal to perform "the contract that the mandatary, acting within the limits of his authority, makes with a third person." Art. 3022 binds the third party to perform a contract when "a mandatary contracts in the name of the principal, or in his own name as mandatary . . . ."

_b. Bases and consequences of contractual liability when agent acts on behalf of disclosed principal._ Illustration 1 is a variation on Illustration 3 to Restatement Second, Agency § 144. Illustration 2 is a variation on Illustration 10 to Restatement Second, Contracts § 90. Illustration 10 is based on [_Hoffman v. Red Owl Stores, Inc., 133 N.W.2d 267 (Wis. 1965)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-WY70-003G-3350-00000-00&context=1530671). In _Hoffman,_ as in Illustration 2, a franchisor dealt with a prospective franchisee through its original regional manager and his successor, with on-site visits from home-office representatives. The court's opinion upholds a jury determination that the prospective franchisee's actions in reliance were reasonable but does not focus on the agency dimensions of dealings between the franchisor and the prospective franchisee.

For illustrations of the basic proposition that an agent may bind a disclosed principal to a third party in a transaction, see, e.g., [_Commercial Union Ins. Co. v. Alitalia Airlines, S.p.A., 347 F.3d 448, 462-463 (2d Cir. 2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:49V7-62H0-0038-X54Y-00000-00&context=1530671) (by issuing waybill on behalf of airline, agent subjected airline to liability to shipper of pasta packaging machine damaged in transit); Delaware River Tow, LLC, v. Nelson, 2005 WL 331706, at \*5-\*6 (E.D.Pa. 2005) (agent of insurer who told managing partner of towing firm to "\[d\]o whatever is necessary to save the boat" and furnished claim number issued by insurer when yacht sank in its berth acted with apparent authority to bind insurer to oral contract with towing firm; alternatively, insurer's agent subjected it to liability under doctrine of promissory estoppel, having impliedly made a promise on which agent should have expected towing company to rely to its detriment).

An agent for a disclosed principal who is not personally liable on a contract is not a necessary party to breach-of-contract litigation. See [_Ryan v. Volpone Stamp Co., 107 F.Supp.2d 369, 387 (S.D.N.Y.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40WC-TJ50-0038-Y06F-00000-00&context=1530671).

For the point that an agent who enters into a contract on behalf of a disclosed principal does not become liable if the principal fails to perform obligations under the contract, see [_Salim Oleochemicals, Inc. v. M/V Shropshire, 169 F.Supp.2d 194, 200 n.3 (S.D.N.Y.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:42Y6-W1C0-0038-Y4M9-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_40 Fed. Appx. 626 (2d Cir.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4691-1H10-0038-X3XD-00000-00&context=1530671) (manager of ship and manager of shipper who acted solely as agents for disclosed principals are not proper defendants in action brought by consignee to recover for damage to shipment because they are not liable for breach of contract); see also _Callahan v. Harvest Bd. Int'l, Inc., 138 F.Supp.2d 147, 163 (D.Mass.2001)_ (corporation's Senior Vice President for Finance not personally liable for corporation's breach of contract; officer did not sign or guarantee promissory notes on which plaintiff sought recovery); see also [_Hirsovescu v. Shangri-La Corp., 831 P.2d 73, 74 (Or.App.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-Y5T0-003F-Y0RM-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporation's directors not subject to individual liability on employment contract entered into on corporation's behalf with plaintiff).

For history on the point that an agent's liability is based on an implied warranty of authority when the agent lacks power to bind the principal but purports to have authority, see J. Dennis Hynes & Mark J. Loewenstein, Agency, Partnership and the LLC: The Law of Unincorporated Business Enterprises 402-405 (6th ed. 2003). Although some early cases characterized the agent as a party to the contract, other courts recognized that "it was illogical to hold the agent on a promise he never made . . . ." [_Id. at 402_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40WC-TJ50-0038-Y06F-00000-00&context=1530671). Contemporary cases may occasionally state in passing that an agent who exceeds authority is subject to personal liability on contracts made by the agent. For a recent example, see [_Clark v. Robert W. Baird Co., 142 F.Supp.2d 1065, 1074 (N.D.Ill.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:432V-63J0-0038-Y0GW-00000-00&context=1530671). However, the line of authority cited by _Clark_ for this proposition includes a case making it clear that the basis for an agent's liability is breach of a warranty of authority. See [_Vulcan Corp. v. Cobden Mach. Works, 84 N.E.2d 173, 176-177 (Ill.App.1949)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-7VS0-003C-41XC-00000-00&context=1530671). Terms in a contract to which an agent commits a disclosed principal may nonetheless carry legal consequences for the agent. See [_Wildwood Imports v. M/V Zim Shanghai, 2005 WL 425490, at \*2 (S.D.N.Y.2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4FJN-RG80-TVW3-P3C0-00000-00&context=1530671) (forum-selection clause in contract agent entered into on behalf of principal binds agent for purposes of claim by principal against agent); [_Deputy v. Lehman Bros., Inc., 374 F.Supp.2d 695 (E.D.Wis.2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4GV9-JYJ0-TVXD-42WK-00000-00&context=1530671) (arbitration clause in contract with brokerage firm made by investor acting in custodial capacity for minor applicable to disputes arising from investor's separate individual account with firm because clause applied to investor's accounts regardless of capacity in which she held them).

On separate and single contracts, see Restatement Second, Contracts § 31, Comment _a_ ("\[a\]n offer may request several acts or promises as the indivisible exchange for the promise or promises in the offer, or it may request a series of contracts to be made from time to time. Whether several promises create several contracts or are all part of one contract is determined by principles of interpretation. . . .").

On the agency status of non-vessel-operating common carriers, see [_Orion Ins. Co. v. M/V Humacao, 851 F.Supp. 575, 577-578 (S.D.N.Y.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-KBG0-003B-V18S-00000-00&context=1530671). Under 46 App. U.S.C. § 1702(17)(B) (1999 & Supp. 2005), an NVOCC is characterized as a shipper in its relationship with an ocean common carrier. A shipper may or may not act as agent for another. How a particular shipper is characterized may turn on the documents issued. See Carrington Slipways Pty. Ltd. v. Patrick Operations Pty. Ltd., (1991) 24 N.S.W.L.R. 745. When a sea leg is a substantial part of transport arrangements that also involve land-based modes of transport, a Himalaya clause in a bill of lading between a cargo owner and intercontinental ocean shipping protects a land carrier that is not in privity with the cargo owner. See [_Norfolk So. Ry. Co. v. Kirby, 125 S.Ct. 385 (2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4DRV-15J0-004B-Y00X-00000-00&context=1530671). A "Himalaya" clause, which derives its name from Adler v. Dickson (The Himalaya), \[1955\] 1 Q.B. 1, limits the liability of downstream providers of services to the cargo owner. _Kirby,_ "a maritime case about a train wreck" in the Court's characterization, see [_125 S. Ct. at 390_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4DRV-15J0-004B-Y00X-00000-00&context=1530671), holds that federal common law governs liabilities stemming from land-based conduct in transit sequences in which transport by sea is a substantial element, given the importance of uniformity in the law applicable to a single bill of lading, [_id. at 395-396_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4DRV-15J0-004B-Y00X-00000-00&context=1530671). For discussion of the impact of _Kirby,_ see Joseph C. Sweeney, Crossing the Himalayas: Exculpatory Clauses in Global Transport, [_36 J. Mar. L. & Com. 155 (2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4H21-2B30-00CV-J0C8-00000-00&context=1530671).

_c. Contract made on behalf of a disclosed principal._ On the admissibility of parol evidence to establish the identity of parties to a contract, see [_Affordable Elegance Travel, Inc. v. Worldspan, L.P., 774 A.2d 320 (D.C.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:438S-JGC0-0039-44H7-00000-00&context=1530671) (trial court did not err in admitting testimony addressing identity of party to contract when identity of party to contract was at issue; witness testified that individual told her he was acting on behalf of particular corporation while that corporation maintained that individual signed contract on behalf of another corporation); [_Lunders v. Estate of Snyder, 963 P.2d 372 (Idaho 1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3T31-YF20-0039-41JM-00000-00&context=1530671) (trial court correctly denied real-estate vendors' motion for summary judgment given dispute over identity of parties to real-estate listing contract and permitted introduction of extrinsic evidence; individual agents as well as corporation could be parties to listing contract when both used same assumed name, to which listing agreement granted exclusive right to sell property).

Illustrations 4, 5, and 6 are variations on the facts of [_Stryker Farms Exch. Co. v. Mytczynskyj, 717 N.E.2d 819, 822 (Ohio App.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3TCW-K060-0039-44WF-00000-00&context=1530671) (father who owned farm land and leased it to son who operated farm not liable on revolving account with farm-supply store; father acted only in name of son's "business entity" and entity was disclosed to supplier). In _Stryker Farms,_ the court's opinion does not identify the type of "business entity" through which the son operated the farm or discuss whether the son simply operated his business using an assumed name. The supplier first sued the son, who then filed for bankruptcy; the supplier amended its complaint to add the father as a defendant. [_Id. at 820_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3TCW-K060-0039-44WF-00000-00&context=1530671).

On whether a person's signature satisfies the Statute of Frauds, see [_Sutton v. Culver, 204 F.Supp.2d 20, 33 (D.Me.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45WX-1RT0-0038-Y144-00000-00&context=1530671) (deposit of would-be-purchaser's earnest-money check by lawyer who represented co-owner of property did not serve as the equivalent of a signed writing on behalf of other co-owner of property, who never authorized lawyer to act on her behalf).

The manner of an agent's signature may also be determinative of the principal's rights. For example, under former U.C.C. § 9-402(1), a financing statement was effective only if "signed by the debtor. . . ." Cases applying this requirement held that the signature of an agent for a debtor by itself did not suffice as the debtor's signature unless it evidenced that the signer was an officer of a corporate debtor or it identified the source of the signer's authority. See [_In re Goolsby, 284 B.R. 638, 643 (M.D.Tenn.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:472S-HT50-0038-Y1G8-00000-00&context=1530671) (although jurisdictions are split on whether signature affixed in individual capacity suffices as signature of corporate debtor, no jurisdiction finds such a signature to be sufficient if it does not evidence either that signer is an officer of corporation or is duly authorized to sign on its behalf). In _Goolsby,_ the court held that a financing statement was not effective when signed by a creditor's employee "'_Authorized signature_ by: \[name\],'" the debtor having previously given the creditor a power of attorney to sign and file a financing statement. In contrast, current Article 9 does not require that a financing statement be signed, which facilitates paperless filing. See U.C.C. § 9-503. However, under § 9-510, a financing statement is not effective unless the person who files it is entitled to do so under § 9-509.

Illustration 7 is a variation on Illustration 1 to Restatement Second, Agency § 149.

For the point that it is a question of fact whether the fact of agency has been disclosed, see [_Jensen v. Alaska Valuation Serv., Inc., 688 P.2d 161, 164 (Alaska 1984)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-1B70-000H-80G3-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Affordable Elegance Travel, 774 A.2d at 326_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:438S-JGC0-0039-44H7-00000-00&context=1530671). _Jensen_ places on the agent the burden of proving that the third party had notice of the principal's existence and identity. See [_688 P.2d at 163_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-1B70-000H-80G3-00000-00&context=1530671). But see [_A.S. Abell Co. v. Skeen, 288 A.2d 596, 598 (Md.1972)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-6V30-003G-247B-00000-00&context=1530671) (burden on third party), abrogated on other grounds by [_Buck v. Cam's Broadloom Rugs, Inc., 612 A.2d 1294, 1296 (Md.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-3980-003G-2129-00000-00&context=1530671).

A third party's actual knowledge that an agent dealt on behalf of a disclosed principal and the identity of the principal defeats the third party's assertion of individual liability against the agent in [_Washington Nat'l Ins. Co. v. Hendricks, 855 F.Supp. 1542, 1559 (W.D.Wis.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-GPS0-003B-V3G8-00000-00&context=1530671) (third party aware that agent acted for individual principal, who would be responsible for payment). See also [_American Bank & Trust Co. v. Boggs & Thompson, 821 So. 2d 585, 589 (La. App.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:462D-RNB0-0039-43YH-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent not subject to personal liability on contract when agent proves facts establishing third party was on notice of agency relationship).

On circumstances that may not constitute disclosure of a principal, see Nomura Sec. Int'l, Inc. v. E\* [_Trade Sec., Inc., 280 F.Supp.2d 184, 196 (S.D.N.Y.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:49G0-K930-0038-Y4D4-00000-00&context=1530671) (smallness of fee allegedly due party not indicative of party's status as agent as opposed to principal in electronic lending transaction); _Crawford v. Washington, 872 S.W.2d 140, 142 (Mo.App. 1994)_ (sign bearing corporation's name over entrance to shop insufficient to provide notice of person's status as agent to landlord with whom he had oral month-to-month rental agreement); [_African Bio-Botanica, Inc. v. Leiner, 624 A.2d 1003, 1006 (N.J.Super.App.Div.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-WC60-003C-P4NK-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[i\]f the person with whom the agent is dealing does not know of the agency and has no reasonable way to know except by asking, the agent has the burden of disclosing his agency and the identity of his principal in order to avoid liability on contracts which he makes"; agent who dealt on behalf of corporation using only its trade name did not meet burden of establishing that she disclosed she acted solely as an agent on behalf of corporation); [_Harco Energy, Inc. v. Re-Entry People, Inc., 23 S.W.3d 389, 393 (Tex.App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YGM-W0J0-0039-447G-00000-00&context=1530671) (principal's identity and existence are not disclosed simply because a third party "could have conceivably discovered that the agent was working only in a representative capacity").

On the significance to be given to an agent's use of a trade or assumed name, see [_Odyssey Travel Ctr., Inc. v. RO Cruises, Inc., 262 F.Supp.2d 618, 625 (D.Md.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48NR-CV10-0038-Y0C7-00000-00&context=1530671) (trade name insufficient to identify principal); [_Perry v. Breland, 16 S.W.3d 182, 187 (Tex. App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:401X-YG30-0039-44KF-00000-00&context=1530671) (disclosure of trade name does not amount to disclosure of principal). If a trade name has been registered in compliance with a statute requiring registration of assumed names, the weight of authority does not treat registration as sufficient to give notice to a third party of the identity of an agent's principal, despite "constructive notice" language in the statute. See [_J & J Builders Supply v. Caffin, 56 Cal.Rptr. 365, 369 (Cal.App.1967)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-9VK0-003C-J4BH-00000-00&context=1530671) (constructive notice of contents of fictitious-name certificate not equivalent to actual notice of agent's capacity); [_Robinson & St. John Adver. & Pub. Relations, Inc. v. Lane, 557 So. 2d 908 (Fla.Dist.App. 1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-F000-003F-3097-00000-00&context=1530671) (treating question as long-settled, court holds that disclosure of fictitious trade name, registered under Fla. Stat. § 865.09, is insufficient as notice of identity of agent's corporate principal; statute does not state that it may be used as a shield against agent's common-law liability); [_A to Z Rental Ctr. v. Burris, 714 S.W.2d 433, 437 (Tex.App.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-3S10-003C-22S0-00000-00&context=1530671) (filing assumed-name certificate immaterial to whether agent discharged duty to disclose name of principal, not just its assumed or trade name). See also [_Waste, Water & Land, Inc. v. Lanham, 955 P.2d 997 (Colo.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S6V-36C0-0039-451R-00000-00&context=1530671) (notice provision in LLC statute, Colo. Rev. Stat. § 7-80-203, does not relieve agents of LLC of common-law duty to disclose existence and identity of principal to third parties; statute protects LLC members from liability as members but not from consequences of their acts as agents). The sole authority to the contrary appears to be [_Metro Bulletins Corp. v. Soboleski, 620 A.2d 1314 (Conn.App.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-3B70-003D-81XB-00000-00&context=1530671), certification granted in part, _625 A.2d 823 (Conn. 1993)_. The majority in _Metro Bulletins_ stresses the notice-giving purpose of the statute, stating that the third party's "theory would mean that a business being conducted under a trade name could not use that trade name without providing an affirmative disclosure of the entity behind the business in every transaction with the public." [_Id. at 1318_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-3B70-003D-81XB-00000-00&context=1530671). The dissent reads the statute more narrowly and points out that "\[i\]t does not seem to be too much to ask of an individual acting as an agent in a given transaction who wants to avoid personal liability on a particular contract specifically to disclose his principal to the other party." [_Id. at 1320_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-3B70-003D-81XB-00000-00&context=1530671). The statute in question, Conn. Gen. Stat. § 35-1 (1997 & Supp. 2005) does not address whether a filing under the statute should be treated as providing notice to third parties.

For the proposition that it is a question of fact whether an agent's use of corporate checks provides sufficient notice that the agent acts only as representative of a corporation, see [_Jensen v. Alaska Valuation Serv., Inc., 688 P.2d 161, 164 (Alaska 1984)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-1B70-000H-80G3-00000-00&context=1530671). It is relevant whether corporate checks are used consistently. Compare [_Aeroflot Russian Int'l Airlines v. O'Brien, 824 F.Supp. 4, 6 (D.D.C. 1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-MD80-001T-639G-00000-00&context=1530671) (airline not put on notice of corporate nature of travel service when only some checks received by airline used corporate name) with [_Ridgewells Caterer, Inc. v. Nelson, 688 F. Supp. 760, 762-763 (D.D.C. 1988)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-8NR0-003B-62H6-00000-00&context=1530671) (all checks, correspondence, and contracts between parties indicated corporate status of principal) and [_Masinton v. Dean, 659 P.2d 50 (Colo.App.1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-4GK0-003D-94CH-00000-00&context=1530671) (constant practice of paying for goods with checks bearing name of corporation).

Some cases take inconsistent and categorical positions on the significance to be accorded an agent's use of corporate checks. Compare [_Potter v. Chaney, 290 S.W.2d 44, 46 (Ky. 1956)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRN-0BS0-003F-K12M-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent's consistent use of corporate checks to pay for coal, when corporation existed prior to plaintiff's deliveries of coal, sufficient as a matter of law to place coal seller on notice that he dealt with a corporation) with [_Jahncke Serv., Inc. v. Heaslip, 76 So. 2d 463, 465 (La.App. 1954)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-7SX0-0044-C0SK-00000-00&context=1530671) (when defendant incorporated business during course of several years' dealings with plaintiff, court holds that payments through 5 or 6 corporate checks legally insufficient to give notice that defendant dealt only as agent of corporation).

Illustration 8 is based on [_Kimco Corp. v. Murdoch, Coll & Lillibridge, Inc., 730 N.E.2d 1143 (Ill.App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40B8-R9M0-0039-4113-00000-00&context=1530671). The same principle is formulated and applied in [_Howell v. Smith, 134 S.E.2d 381, 385 (N.C.1964)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-YHB0-000G-K0FB-00000-00&context=1530671) (if third party to contract involving undisclosed principal discovers principal's existence and identity "while a continuing, divisible contract for the furnishing of goods or supplies is still executory, he then has the option to deal either with the agent or the principal with respect to the future performance of the contract. Ordinarily, the agent who made the original purchase is not liable if the third party continues to deliver goods after acquiring knowledge of the principal's identity unless he has agreed to be personally liable.").

_d. Agreement that agent shall be a party to contract_

_(1). In general._ On loose usage of first-person pronouns, see [_Kay v. Freeman, 785 S.W.2d 90, 92 (Mo.App. 1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-8YT0-003F-C232-00000-00&context=1530671) (reversing judgment against president and sole shareholder of disclosed corporation, court declines to treat use of personal pronouns as substantial evidence of intention to be bound personally by corporate contract; "one would expect the use of those terms in informal speech or writing, and they are not indicative of a purpose to assume personal liability.").

For the point that, as in Illustration 10, an agent for a disclosed principal is not subject to liability for breach of warranty, see [_Johnson v. Scandia Assocs., Inc., 641 N.E.2d 51, 57 (Ind.App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-72K0-003F-X12G-00000-00&context=1530671), vacated on other grounds, [_717 N.E.2d 24 (Ind.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XJK-FG30-0039-401W-00000-00&context=1530671) (leasing agent for disclosed principal not subject to liability to tenant on implied warranty of habitability). An agent may incur individual liability for acts or omissions that breach duties the agent owes individually to the third party. See, e.g., [_Smith v. Union Nat. Life Ins. Co., 187 F.Supp.2d 635, 651 (S.D.Miss.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:454K-HW00-0038-Y559-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent may incur individual liability under Mississippi law when conduct constitutes gross negligence, malice, or reckless disregard for rights of plaintiff).

For the point that the third party has the burden of establishing an agent's agreement to become an individual party to an agreement, see [_Leutwyler v. Royal Hashemite Court of Jordan, 184 F.Supp.2d 303, 309 (S.D.N.Y.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:44FV-V010-0038-Y24S-00000-00&context=1530671). _Leutwyler_ applies New York law, which requires proof of such an agreement by "'clear and explicit evidence of the agents' \[sic\] intention to substitute or superadd his personal liability for, or to, that of his principal,'" citing [_Mencher v. Weiss, 114 N.E.2d 177, 179 (N.Y. 1953)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-XJH0-003C-C2CJ-00000-00&context=1530671). Other cases apply this requirement when the issue is whether an agent is personally subject to noncontractual liability. See [_Evergreen Fund, Ltd. v. Anderson, 701 N.Y.S.2d 812, 813-814 (Sup.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XVH-BFY0-0039-424B-00000-00&context=1530671) (liability for building-code violations).

On the liability of lawyers when the third party knows the client's identity, compare [_Urban Court Reporting, Inc. v. Davis, 551 N.Y.S.2d 235 (App. Div.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S2R-9M10-003V-B4VD-00000-00&context=1530671) (lawyer liable for services or goods obtained in connection with litigation unless liability disclaimed), Ruiz v. Gerber & Gerber, LLP, 223 N.Y.L.J., at 25, col. 6 (Sup. Ct. May 2, 2000) (cases subsequent to _Urban Reporting_ have recognized that "modern litigation practices warrant holding counsel responsible for the services of persons retained in the course of the law suit"; contrary rule would require plaintiff to pursue claims against a "whole host of individual taxi companies, a circumstance clearly not contemplated by the parties" when court reporter was retained by law firm that represented taxi-company clients), and [_Cahn v. Fisher, 805 P.2d 1040, 1043 (Ariz. App.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-XMX0-003F-T0Y1-00000-00&context=1530671) (uncontroverted evidence established custom and usage that lawyer would be responsible for paying court reporter) with [_Shank, Irwin, Conant & Williamson v. Durant, Mankoff, Davis, Wolens & Francis, 748 S.W.2d 494, 497 (Tex.App.1988)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-35N0-003C-21G4-00000-00&context=1530671) (as matter of law, law firm that retained services of another law firm to render opinion held not liable for fee because engagement letter established on its face that firm acted as agent for disclosed principal). A lawyer's request for less routine services may also bind the lawyer individually, although the third party is aware of the client's identity. See [_Kelly v. Levandoski, 825 N.E.2d 850, 859-860 (Ind.App.2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4G01-DWG0-0039-44Y1-00000-00&context=1530671) (when lawyer requested that vehicle-towing service store clients' vehicle for 4 years because vehicle was needed for clients' personal-injury lawsuit and did not disclaim individual liability, lawyer subject to liability to towing service for storage charges).

On the personal liability of freight forwarders, see F. H. Bertling Ltd. v. Tube Devs. Ltd., \[1999\] 2 Lloyd's L. Rep. 55, 57 (Sess. 1998) (recognizing established practice).

On the point that statutes may impose liability for principals' contractual obligations on agents, see Marine Insurance Act § 53(1) (1906), which provides that a broker who effects a policy of marine insurance on behalf of an insured is "directly responsible to the insurer for the premium" and that the insurer is "directly responsible to the assured for the amount which may be payable in respect of losses, or in respect of returnable premium." In Prentis Donegan & Partners Ltd. v. Leeds & Leeds Co., \[1998\] 2 Lloyd's Rep. 326, 328 (Q.B. 1998), the court characterized this provision as "reflecting the custom previously established in the marine insurance market in England. . . ." For a domestic illustration of agents' liability, see [_8 U.S.C. § 1321(a)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8S6M-T0S2-D6RV-H49P-00000-00&context=1530671), which imposes liability for failure to prevent the landing of alien crewmen in the United States on the "owners, masters, officers, and agents" of vessels, aircraft, and international bridges and toll roads. See also [_Leonard v. McMorris, 63 P.3d 323 (Colo.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:47VH-3C20-0039-44B2-00000-00&context=1530671) (officers and agents of corporation are not jointly and severally liable for payment of wages and compensation under Colorado Wage Claim Act, Colo. Rev. Stat. § 8-4-101 et seq. although statute defines "employer" to include "and any agent or officer thereof"; majority of court characterizes language of statute as ambiguous, meaning either that legislature intended to subject corporate principals to liability on basis of acts of officers and agents, or that officers and agents were subject to individual liability).

Illustration 10 is a variation on Illustration 1 to Restatement Second, Agency § 320.

Illustrations 11 and 12 are based on [_Ryan v. Volpone Stamp Co., 107 F.Supp.2d 369 (S.D.N.Y.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40WC-TJ50-0038-Y06F-00000-00&context=1530671). For the point that a third party's knowledge of an agent's representative status is deemed to continue in subsequent transactions of the same character and between the same parties, see [_Ryan at 388_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4DRV-15J0-004B-Y00X-00000-00&context=1530671). On the point that an agent, as in Illustration 12, may be expressly subject to a duty of performance under a contract, see [_Ryan at 390_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4DRV-15J0-004B-Y00X-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Hair Assocs., Inc. v. National Hair Replacement Servs., Inc., 987 F.Supp.569, 580 (W.D.Mich. 1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RFX-48V0-0038-Y0R0-00000-00&context=1530671) (although individual officer not subject to liability for corporation's failure to make payments due under franchise agreement, provision in franchise agreement bound officer individually to refrain from engaging in similar business following termination of franchise).

Illustration 13 is based on [_McCarthy v. Azure, 22 F.3d 351 (1st Cir. 1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-6FD0-003B-P1DM-00000-00&context=1530671). In accord with _McCarthy_ on comparable facts are [_Westmoreland v. Sadoux, 299 F.3d 462 (5th Cir. 2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:469V-XDF0-0038-X4CS-00000-00&context=1530671), cert. den., _537 U.S. 1232 (2003)_ and [_Britton v. Co-op Banking Group, 4 F.3d 742 (9th Cir.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-D4K0-003B-P509-00000-00&context=1530671). But see [_Arnold v. Arnold Corp.--Printed Commc'ns. for Bus., 920 F.2d 1269, 1282 (6th Cir.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-1270-003B-53YP-00000-00&context=1530671) (interpreting clause requiring arbitration of disputes arising out of purchase agreement to require arbitration of claims asserted against nonsignatory employee; "the language of the arbitration agreement indicates that the parties' basic intent was to provide a single arbitral forum to resolve all disputes arising under the stock purchase agreement"). The _McCarthy_ court emphasizes its reluctance to introduce an asymmetry that would permit an agent to compel another party to submit to arbitration when the agent could not be compelled to arbitrate. See [_22 F.3d at 361_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-6FD0-003B-P1DM-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[i\]n other words, an agent for a disclosed principal would enjoy the benefits of the principal's arbitral agreement, but would shoulder none of the corresponding burdens. He would have found a way, contrary to folklore, to run with the hare and hunt with the hounds."). For the general point that an agent who signs a contract on behalf of a disclosed principal does not become bound to arbitrate claims brought in the agent's individual capacity simply because the contract includes an arbitration clause, see [_Mionis v. Bank Julius Baer & Co., 749 N.Y.S.2d 497, 503 (App.Div.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:473N-R590-0039-44TV-00000-00&context=1530671).

_(2). Organizational executives and other agents._ For the basic point that an organizational officer does not become party to a contract made on behalf of an organization simply by making the contract on its behalf or signing it in the officer's representative capacity, see [_Ryan, 107 F.Supp.2d at 387-388_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40WC-TJ50-0038-Y06F-00000-00&context=1530671) (signature by corporate officer who signed agreement only as representative of corporation does not make officer individually liable); [_DeSalle v. Appelberg, 759 A.2d 537 (Conn.App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:41DB-JK50-0039-416B-00000-00&context=1530671) (president of corporation not personally liable on promissory note when note signed in capacity as president, note contained no indication of individual liability, and corporation satisfied its obligation to seller of business it purchased by producing note at closing); [_Duncan v. Minick, 737 N.Y.S.2d 440, 442 (App.Div.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:456J-1SG0-0039-44FP-00000-00&context=1530671) (president of construction company not individually liable on construction contract; only document signed by president without indicating corporate capacity was brief addendum to construction specifications, and specifications themselves were signed by president under corporate name); [_Orlob v. Wasatch Mgmt., 33 P.3d 1078, 1082 (Utah App.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:444R-4690-0039-42X0-00000-00&context=1530671) (sole shareholder and president of corporation would not have had an individual interest in agreement with customer had president not signed agreement as an individual in addition to signature as president of corporation and had provisions in agreement not imposed individual obligations on president).

An organizational officer who signs his name to a contract, without more, is subject to individual liability. See [_84 Lumber Co. v. Denni Constr. Co., 571 N.E.2d 231 (Ill.App. 1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-4390-003D-H4H1-00000-00&context=1530671) (contract unambiguous). But see [_A-1 Racing Specialties, Inc. v. K & S Imports, Inc., 576 So. 2d 421 (Fla.Dist.App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-C1G0-003F-32B4-00000-00&context=1530671) (although individual officer's signature may result in personal liability when officer signs contract without indicating representative capacity, parol evidence admissible to establish whether officer was acting in personal or representative capacity). A contract may be ambiguous when an officer is designated in an individual capacity but the officer signs the contract in an expressly representative capacity. See [_Dille v. Doerr Distrib. Co., 867 P.2d 997 (Idaho App.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2VD0-003D-3411-00000-00&context=1530671).

On proof to determine whether an officer is an individual party to an agreement, see [_Andersons, Inc. v. Horton Farms, Inc., 166 F.3d 308, 316-317 (6th Cir.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3VBR-WBT0-0038-X3K7-00000-00&context=1530671) (parol evidence admissible to determine whom parties intended to bind when corporate president signed grain-delivery contracts without designating his corporate capacity; clear that third party understood it dealt with corporation, and not president as an individual when its records placed corporation's name on account for each contract, and president made it clear in negotiating each agreement that he intended to make the contracts under corporation's account); [_LeBoeuf, Lamb, Greene & MacRae v. Worsham, 185 F.3d 61 (2d Cir.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3X0T-KTD0-0038-X1JS-00000-00&context=1530671) (no evidence that law firm performed services personally for individual who was chairman of and principal shareholder in corporations as opposed to performing services for corporations; law firm submitted no retainer agreement, bills were paid by corporate checks, and fact that individual was addressee of correspondence from law firm does not indicate capacity in which correspondence received; although as principal shareholder individual benefited from law firm's work, no evidence individual assumed corporation's obligation to law firm); [_Frost v. Drew, 586 A.2d 1242, 1243 (Me.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-3MP0-003F-N28V-00000-00&context=1530671) (jury would be justified in finding that defendant sold house as agent on behalf of corporation although agent never told purchasers that he acted as corporation's agent; defendant gave purchasers his corporate business card when he first met them, sales agreement recited that corporation was vendor, and defendant disclosed corporation's ownership to listing agent and gave deed to property to agent).

The contents of the body of an agreement are relevant to whether an officer has individual liability. See, e.g., Operating Eng'rs Local # [_49 Health & Welfare Fund v. Listul Erection Corp., 220 F.Supp.2d 1042, 1044 (D.Minn.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:46T8-K8F0-0038-Y2X6-00000-00&context=1530671) (participation agreement for union welfare-benefit plan provided that corporation was signator to agreement and that president of corporation signed agreement only as corporation's president; agreement also provided that, if signed for and on behalf of a corporation, an officer's signature "'not only binds the corporation but individually binds himself to the full and faithful performance of the Agreement stated herein'"; granting summary judgment for president, court holds latter language at most is "hortative and does not overcome the strong presumption that agents acting for a disclosed principal do not personally bind themselves to the performance of a contract"); [_Hair Assocs., Inc. v. National Hair Replacement Servs. Inc., 987 F.Supp. 569, 579-581 (W.D.Mich.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RFX-48V0-0038-Y0R0-00000-00&context=1530671) (franchise agreement unambiguous although officer of corporate franchisee signed agreement with word "individual" under signature line for his name; rights and duties stated in agreement applicable only to franchisor and corporate franchisee, with exception of duty to refrain from competition following termination of agreement, which agreement specifically made applicable to "'the Principals of the Franchisee'"; individual officer not subject to liability for payments due franchisor under agreement); [_Tattersall Club Corp. v. White, 501 S.E.2d 851, 853 (Ga.App.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3T7M-D5J0-0039-4563-00000-00&context=1530671) (employment agreement stated agreement was between corporation and employee; officer signed agreement "Vice President, Operations, Tattersall Club"); [_F & G Invs., L.L.C. v. 1313 Hickory, Ltd., 807 So. 2d 1004, 1006 (La.App.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:44Y4-2DF0-0039-41B1-00000-00&context=1530671) (relevant to whether individual officer, who signed lease, agreed to assume personal liability that last page of lease included paragraph for signature of surety, which was left unsigned).

Illustration 14 is based on [_Robert C. Malt & Co. v. Carpet World Distribs., Inc., 763 So. 2d 508, 510 (Fla. Dist.App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40S8-1NX0-0039-400J-00000-00&context=1530671). For similar facts, see [_American Mgmt. Corp. v. Dunlap, 784 F.Supp. 1245 (N.D.Miss.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-R250-008H-F01C-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporate officer signed agreement structured as personal guarantee but word "title" preceded signature line on which officer signed; summary judgment against officer appropriate because no issues of material fact present on officer's personal liability). See also [_Amwest Sur. Ins. Co. v. Vaughn, 100 F.Supp.2d 335, 339 (E.D.N.C. 2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YTS-1GY0-0038-Y20F-00000-00&context=1530671) (4 corporate officers signed indemnification agreement with surety under payment and performance bonds with their names and offices; court holds that agreement unambiguously creates individual liability for officers because signatures appeared in a section of agreement headed "Individuals," notary page made clear distinction between corporate and individual signatures, and "unless to obligate themselves personally, there is no reason for all officers of a corporation to sign such a contract"). The court held an agreement entitled "Personal Guarantee" to be ambiguous in [_780 L.L.C. v. DiPrima, 611 N.W.2d 637 (Neb.App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40F3-9NR0-0039-422N-00000-00&context=1530671) (title on document inconsistent with manner in which corporate president signed agreement). In contrast, in [_Saltzman Sign Co. v. Beck, 176 N.E.2d 74, 76 (N.Y.1961)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-WWS0-003C-C2JM-00000-00&context=1530671), the court held that the contract insufficiently manifested intent to be bound on the part of a corporation's president. Although the contract contained a provision stating that, if made with a corporation, its officers personally guaranteed payment, the president signed the contract only once, doing so explicitly in his capacity as president.

_e. Agent's position as party to contract._ On a third party's rights against an agent who has agreed to be a party to a contract, see [_Spalding Ford Lincoln-Mercury, Inc. v. Turner Broad. Sys., Inc., 415 S.E.2d 26, 28 (Ga.App.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX8-BV90-003G-P4VS-00000-00&context=1530671) (television station that ran advertising campaign brought suit against advertising agency and advertiser for amount due when contract language provided that advertiser and agency assumed joint and several responsibility for payment; uncollectible default judgment that station obtained against agency did not bar it from proceeding against advertiser).

An agent may become a party to a contract by providing an independent warranty to the third party. See, e.g., [_Kelly v. Tillotson-Pearson, Inc., 840 F. Supp. 935, 944 (D.R.I.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-T8T0-003B-V3WP-00000-00&context=1530671) (absent evidence of agent's intention to provide independent warranty, court will not presume that agent has agreed to be subject to personal liability). An agent may also manifest assent to also be bound by the entirety of the duties that the contract imposes on the principal. See, e.g., [_Dulik v. Amante, 570 N.Y.S.2d 590, 591 (App.Div. 1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S2R-8J50-003V-B14T-00000-00&context=1530671) (officer of corporate owner of yacht signed agreement with yacht salesman twice, once in corporate and once in individual capacity); [_Freidus v. Sardelli, 595 N.Y.S.2d 981, 983 (App.Div.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S2R-7HH0-003V-B3P6-00000-00&context=1530671) (defendant signed joint-venture agreement both in corporate and individual capacities).

An agent's status does not confer a right on the agent to enforce a term for the agent's own benefit. See [_Fericks v. Lucy Ann Soffe Trust, 100 P.3d 1200, 1206 (Utah 2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4DKM-NPW0-0039-412X-00000-00&context=1530671) (realestate agent may not enforce attorney-fee provision in contract made on behalf of seller against purchasers).

_f. Sealed and negotiable instruments._ U.C.C. § 2-203 provides that "\[t\]he affixing of a seal to a record evidencing a contract for sale or an offer to buy or sell goods does not constitute the record a sealed instrument. The law with respect to sealed instruments does not apply to such a contract or offer."

Examples of cases that the drafters of (Revised) U.C.C. § 3-402(c) intended to overrule are [_Griffin v.
