Ellinger, 538 S.W.2d 97 (Tex.1976)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRH-9Y80-003C-501X-00000-00&context=1530671) and [_Cooper v. Emery & Sons, 829 S.W.2d 642 (Mo.App.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-7HN0-003F-C238-00000-00&context=1530671) (signatory personally liable on check returned for insufficient funds although name of corporation was imprinted in check's upper left-hand corner because signatory did not indicate she signed check in representative capacity). But see Mountain States Commercial Collections, Inc. v. 99 cents [_Liquidators, Inc., 940 P.2d 934 (Colo.App.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-29M0-003D-92TC-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporate officer who signed checks returned for insufficient funds is individually liable under Colo. Rev. Stat. § 13-21-109(2), which provides that person who obtains anything of value by check that is not paid is liable to holder or assignee of check for 3 times face amount of check; dissent notes incompatibility with U.C.C. § 3-402(c), as does [_Kunz v. Cycles West, Inc., 969 P.2d 781, 784 (Colo. App.1998))_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3TW9-SMW0-0039-40FX-00000-00&context=1530671).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 6.02_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8W0-00YG-4002-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 6- Contracts and Other Transactions with Third Parties_ > _Topic 1- Parties to Contracts_**

**§ 6.02 Agent for Unidentified Principal**

**When an agent acting with actual or apparent authority makes a contract on behalf of an unidentified principal,**

**(1) the principal and the third party are parties to the contract; and**

**(2) the agent is a party to the contract unless the agent and the third party agree otherwise.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section states the basic principle that when an agent enters into a contract on behalf of an unidentified principal, the agent is a party to the contract, as are the principal and the third party. A principal is unidentified when the third person has notice that the agent acts on behalf of a principal but does not have notice of the principal's identity. See § 1.04(2)(c). An agent for an unidentified principal does not become a party to a contract if the agent and the third party so agree. Comment _b_ discusses the bases for these doctrines and contrasts the position of an agent who acts for an unidentified principal with the position of an agent who acts for a disclosed principal and an agent who acts for an undisclosed principal. Comment _c_ examines circumstances that determine who becomes a party to a contract made on behalf of an unidentified principal. Comment _d_ discusses when a third party has notice of an agent's status and a principal's identity. Comment _e_ discusses the position of an agent as a party to a contract made on behalf of an unidentified principal.

Section 1.04(2) defines the terms "disclosed principal," "unidentified principal," and "undisclosed principal." Sections 2.01 and 2.02 define actual authority. Section 3.01 states how a principal may create actual authority. Section 2.03 defines "apparent authority." Section 3.03 states how a principal may create apparent authority. Comment _f_ to § 2.03 discusses apparent authority in relation to unidentified principals.

_b. Bases of contractual liability when agent acts on behalf of unidentified principal._ An agent acts on behalf of an unidentified principal when the third party with whom the agent deals has notice that the agent acts on behalf of a principal but does not have notice of the principal's identity. See § 1.04(2)(c). Many cases, like Restatement Second, Agency § 4(2), refer to such a principal as a "partially disclosed principal." This Restatement instead uses the term "unidentified principal." See § 1.04, Comment _b._

An agent has power to make contracts on behalf of an unidentified principal when the agent acts with actual or apparent authority, just as an agent acting with actual or apparent authority has power to make contracts on behalf of a disclosed principal. See § 6.01(1). If an agent purports to act on behalf of an undisclosed principal but lacks actual or apparent authority to bind the principal, the agent is subject to liability on the agent's implied warranty of authority. See § 6.10.

Like the power of an agent who acts on behalf of a disclosed principal, the contracting power of an agent for an unidentified principal is consistent with the bargain principle in contemporary contract law. See § 6.01, Comment _b._ An agent makes a contract with a third party on behalf of an unidentified principal by manifesting assent to an exchange that constitutes valid consideration. The third party's manifestation of assent is made to the agent, who the third party has notice acts on behalf of a principal. An unidentified principal may also be subject to liability as a consequence of a promise made by an agent that is not supported by consideration but that should reasonably be expected to induce action or forbearance on the part of a third party. See id. In either situation, the third party has notice that the agent acts on behalf of another person. On the other hand, when an agent acts on behalf of an undisclosed principal, the third party lacks notice that it deals with an agent. See § 1.04(2)(b).

Unless the third party and the agent agree otherwise, an agent who makes a contract on behalf of a disclosed principal does not become a party to the contract. See § 6.01(2). In contrast, as stated in subsection (2) of this section, an agent who makes a contract on behalf of an unidentified principal becomes a party to the contract unless the third party and the agent agree otherwise. When a third party has notice that an agent deals on behalf of a principal but does not have notice of the principal's identity, it is not likely that the third party will rely solely on the principal's solvency or ability to perform obligations arising from the contract. Without notice of a principal's identity, a third party will be unable to assess the principal's reputation, assets, and other indicia of creditworthiness and ability to perform duties under the contract. If an agent provides reassurances about the principal's soundness only generally or describes the principal, the third party will be unable to verify such claims without notice of the principal's identity.

It is possible that a third party would view the fact that a principal is represented by a particular agent as a reliable proxy for the principal's reliability, if for example the agent is known to select among prospective principals carefully and to represent only those who warrant a high degree of confidence. Although this is possible, it does not typify the larger run of agency relationships.

It is also possible that, once on notice that an agent represents a principal, the third party may bargain with the agent for additional protection, such as the agent's guaranty of the principal's performance or the agent's primary liability on the contract. The third party's opportunity so to bargain does not justify a different default rule, which would make an agent for an unidentified principal subject to liability only if the agent and the third party so agree. Without notice of a principal's identity, a third party who has notice that an agent represents a principal does not have a basis on which to assess the value of the agent's liability compared to that of the unidentified principal. To make that relative assessment, which may affect other terms in the contract, the third party requires notice of the principal's identity. Moreover, if the third party must bargain for terms that explicitly make the agent subject to liability, a cautious third party will delay forming a contract with an unidentified principal until reliable information about the principal and its creditworthiness can be obtained and assessed. Thus, unless a third party and an agent agree otherwise, an agent who makes a contract on behalf of an unidentified principal becomes a party to the contract.

The principal and third party have the same rights, liabilities, and defenses against each other as if the principal had made the contract directly, subject to §§ 6.05-6.09.

An unidentified principal becomes a party to a contract made by an agent who acts with actual or apparent authority, unless the agent and the third party agree that the principal shall not become a party. Making such an agreement may breach the agent's duty to the principal as stated in § 8.09. Although a third party who contracts with an agent for an unidentified principal may rely on the agent's liability as a party to a contract, the principal becomes a party because the agent acts on the principal's behalf and because ordinarily a third party would wish to have the liability of the person on whose behalf the agent contracts.

Through ratification, a person may become a party to a contract purportedly made on that person's behalf by another who acted without actual or apparent authority. See § 4.02.

_c. Contract made on behalf of unidentified principal._ A principal is unidentified when the third party does not have notice of the principal's identity at the time the agent enters into a contract on the principal's behalf. Ordinarily it is the agent who provides notice of the principal's identity. However, the source of the third party's enlightenment is irrelevant to the legal relations among the parties. Regardless of the source of notice of the principal's identity, once the third party has such notice, the third party is in a position to assess the principal's reliability.

**Illustrations:**

1\. P, who deals in antiques, retains A to purchase antiques on P's behalf. P directs A not to disclose P's identity to persons with whom A deals. A complies. A contracts to buy an antique clock owned by T, telling T that A is purchasing the clock on behalf of A's principal. P is A's unidentified principal. P and T are parties to the contract for the sale of the clock. A is also a party to the contract unless A and T agree otherwise.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that, prior to contracting to sell the clock, T learns from a friend that A represents P. As to T, P is A's disclosed principal. T knew P's identity when making the contract with A. P and T are parties to the contract for the sale of the clock. A is not a party unless A and T agree otherwise. See § 6.01(2).

A person who wishes to act as an undisclosed or unidentified principal to a contract may provide instructions to an agent prohibiting disclosure of the principal's existence or identity in the agent's dealings with third parties on the principal's behalf. If the agent then reveals the principal's identity contrary to those instructions, or the third party otherwise learns the principal's identity, the principal nevertheless will be bound by the contract if the agent acted with actual or apparent authority in making it. See § 6.01, Comment c.

If an agent makes a contract in the agent's name despite instructions from the principal to contract only in the principal's name, the principal is a party to the contract unless by its terms the contract excludes the principal as a party. By acting contrary to the principal's instructions, the agent becomes subject to liability to the principal as stated in § 8.09.

**Illustration:**

3\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that P instructs A to make a contract in P's name to buy the clock owned by T. A instead makes the contract in A's name. P, T, and A are parties to the contract. P is an unidentified principal because T has notice only that A acts on behalf of another. P would be an undisclosed principal if T has no notice that A acts on behalf of another. See § 1.04(2)(b).

If an agent complies with a principal's instruction to misrepresent the principal's identity to induce a third party to deal, the principal may not enforce the resulting contract against the third party when the principal has notice the third party would not knowingly have dealt with the principal. See § 6.11(4). Likewise, when an agent has notice that a third party will not deal with the agent individually, the agent may not enforce a contract made with the third party by falsely purporting to represent an unidentified principal. For a general discussion of agents who purport to act on behalf of principals that do not exist, or that lack capacity to be party to a contract, see § 6.04.

**Illustrations:**

4\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that A, complying with an instruction from P, falsely tells T that A is contracting to buy the clock on behalf of R Museum. P so instructs A because P knows that T will sell the clock only to a museum. P may not enforce the contract against T. P had notice that T would deal only with purchasers who were unlike P.

5\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that A in fact represents no principal and wishes to buy T's antique clock, knowing that T will sell the clock only to a museum. A enters into a contract to buy the clock from T on behalf of "my principal, a museum," which A does not identify. A may not enforce the contract against T.

An agent who represents more than one principal may enter into a contract with a third party who does not have notice of the identity of the principal on whose behalf the agent has contracted. The identity of the principal on whose behalf the agent contracted is a question of fact. The burden of proof is on the party who seeks to establish that the agent acted on behalf of a particular principal. See § 1.02, Comment _d._ Many indicia of a relationship may be relevant in resolving this factual issue, including an agent's internal business records and communications between an agent and the principals whom the agent represents.

**Illustration:**

6\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that A is retained by an additional antique dealer, B, to purchase antiques on B's behalf. Like P, B directs A not to disclose B's identity to persons with whom A deals. It is a question of fact whether, in contracting to purchase the clock from T, A acted on behalf of P or B.

_d. Notice of principal's identity and agent's status._ A third party has notice of a principal's identity when, regardless of the source, the third party has notice of facts reasonably sufficient to identify the principal. It is a question of fact whether a third party has such notice. It is a presumption that notice of facts that form a basis for further sleuthing by a third party, such as searches in public records, do not constitute reasonably sufficient notice of a principal's identity. In particular, an agent's use of a trade name, which may be traced to its registered user through a search of public filings, is not sufficient to disclose a principal's identity. For extended discussion of this point, see § 6.01, Comment c. The presumption may be overcome by evidence of custom in a particular trade or industry. In some circumstances, custom and usage may make it reasonable to expect that third parties will ascertain principals' identities by checking records maintained by others. This may be so in industries where parties regularly deal though intermediaries and information about principals' identities is readily accessible to those who provide services.

**Illustration:**

7\. P Corporation owns a restaurant, named The Magic Moment, that A manages. A enters into a contract with T, an interior designer, to refurbish the entrance to the restaurant. A signs the contract "A, The Magic Moment." T, A, and P Corporation are parties to the contract. Depending on what a reasonable person in T's position would understand to be the meaning of "A, The Magic Moment" under the circumstances, P Corporation is either A's unidentified or A's undisclosed principal in A's dealings with T. Both A and P Corporation are subject to liability on the contract with T.

It is possible that in the same transaction with a third party, an agent may appear to act on behalf of a disclosed principal but also act on behalf of an unidentified or undisclosed principal.

**Illustration:**

8\. A and B are individuals who plan to construct a restaurant through a limited-liability company, which they form using the name "Preferred Income Investors, L.L.C." A invites T to submit an offer for engineering work. B instructs A to tell T to send the offer to B. A also gives T A's business card, which contains A's name and B's address. B's address is also the principal office and place of business stated in Preferred Income Investors, L.L.C.'s articles of formation filed with the state in which it was formed. Although the LLC's name is not on A's card, the card includes the initials "P.I.I." A does not mention the LLC to T. As A requests, T sends the offer to B at B's address. B instructs A to tell T to do the work. T receives a telephone call from A telling T that B says to go ahead and do the work. On this basis, T believes that A acts as an agent for B. T does the work but is not paid.

Preferred Income Investors, L.L.C., A, and B are subject to liability to T. In A's dealings with T although T believed that A acted as B's agent, A acted as an agent for an unidentified principal, Preferred Income Investors, LLC. Having directed A to tell T to submit an offer to B and to go ahead with the work, B is estopped to deny that A acted as B's agent. See § 2.05. Dealing with T through A, B acted as agent for Preferred Income Investors, L.L.C. The LLC was B's undisclosed principal because T did not have notice that B acted for any principal.

When an agent acts on behalf of an organization or a firm, as in Illustrations 7 and 8, the third party may be aware that the agent represents an organization or firm but lack notice of its salient characteristics. Chief among these is whether the firm is a limited-liability entity. In contrast, when a third party has notice of the name of a firm and the name indicates that the firm is a limited-liability entity, the third party has notice that it ordinarily may not look to individual assets of owners of the firm to satisfy obligations owed the third party by the firm.

A third party may have notice that an agent acts as an agent for an unidentified principal from writings executed by the agent, among other sources.

**Illustration:**

9\. T, who owns a painting by Edgar Degas, enters into a contract to sell it to A. Prior to making the contract with T, A agrees to acquire the painting on behalf of P. The contract made by A and T states that "it is expressly understood between Buyer and Seller that the ultimate buyer is an Undisclosed Principal unknown to the Seller. Consequently, Buyer warrants that said Undisclosed Principal guarantees this purchase and performance in every respect. Should Seller not receive timely payment, Buyer agrees to divulge to Seller the exact identity of the Undisclosed Principal." P is an unidentified principal. T had notice that A acted as an agent but did not have notice of P's identity. A, T, and P are parties to the contract. A's liability under the contract is not limited to revealing P's identity to T.

A consignment of goods creates an agency relationship between the consignor and the consignee. The consignee has power to sell the goods on behalf of the consignor, who retains title to them. If a third-party purchaser has notice of the consignor's identity, the consignee acts as an agent for a disclosed principal. If the third-party purchaser has notice that the goods have been consigned but no notice of the consignor's identity, the consignee acts as agent for an unidentified principal, the consignor. Revised U.C.C. Article 9 applies to many consignment transactions, subject to exceptions. It amends former U.C.C. § 2-326 to delete all references to consignments. When Revised Article 9 is applicable, it provides the sole route through which a consignor may protect its interest in the consigned goods against creditors of the consignee. In a majority of states, the U.C.C. does not apply to consignments of works of art from artists to art dealers if the consignor complies with requirements imposed by the statute, such as giving notice that the work is sold on a consignment basis.

_e. Agent's position as party to contract._ In an action by a third party against an agent who has made a contract on behalf of an unidentified principal, the agent may assert all defenses that arise from the transaction itself and defenses available to the agent personally.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency, and codifications._ This section is a consolidated treatment of points made in numerous sections of Restatement Second, Agency, that deal with disclosed as well as unidentified ("partially disclosed") principals. See § 6.01, Reporter's Note to Comment _a._ An exception is Restatement Second, Agency § 321, which states that, unless otherwise agreed, an agent for a partially disclosed principal is a party to a contract made on behalf of the principal.

Most codifications of agency law do not include provisions that recognize the possibility that a principal may be unidentified, as opposed to undisclosed. An exception is the Louisiana Civil Code, as amended in 1997. Art. 3023 of the Code provides that "\[a\] third person with whom a mandatary contracts without disclosing his status or the identity of the principal is bound to the principal for the performance of the contract unless the obligation is strictly personal or the right non-assignable. The third person may raise all defenses that may be asserted against the mandatary or the principal." La. Civ. Code, art. 3024 (2005). Under Art. 3018, "\[a\] mandatary who enters into a contract and discloses his status as a mandatary, though not his principal, binds himself personally for the performance of the contract. The mandatary ceases to be bound when the principal is disclosed."

_b. Bases of contractual liability when agent acts on behalf of unidentified principal._ For the point that an agent for an unidentified principal becomes a party to the contract, as do the principal and the third party, see [_Weil v. Murray, 161 F.Supp.2d 250, 255-256 (S.D.N.Y.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:42SX-H180-0038-Y32W-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Dimension Graphics, Inc. v. Liebowitz, 994 P.2d 658, 661 (Kan.App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3Y5N-BTN0-0039-420J-00000-00&context=1530671). For the point that an unidentified principal becomes a party to a contract, see Warren A. Seavey, Agency § 70E (1964).

Representative cases holding that an agent for an unidentified principal may be subject to liability on a contract made on behalf of the principal include [_UBS Sec., Inc. v. Tsoukanelis, 852 F.Supp. 244, 247 (S.D.N.Y.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-JFX0-003B-V0GT-00000-00&context=1530671) (president of corporation who signed put agreement failed to disclose that firm on whose behalf he signed was a corporation; purpose of rule is "to make sure that a party entering a contract knows precisely with whom it is dealing and protects a party from unknowingly being required to do business with an entity incapable of meeting its contractual obligations"); [_Turner & Boisseau, Chtd. v. Marshall Adjusting Corp., 775 F. Supp. 372, 382 (D.Kan.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-B620-001T-72M8-00000-00&context=1530671) (London reinsurers characterized as partially disclosed principals; agent for reinsurers, who agreed to cover losses of insurer that subsequently became insolvent, liable for fees due law firm that represented insureds covered by policy issued by insolvent insurer); [_Atlantic Salmon A/S v. Curran, 591 N.E.2d 206 (Mass.App. 1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-BJ80-003C-V0C1-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent who purchased fish for resale on behalf of incorporated fish dealer subject to liability to suppliers of fish when agent did not reveal name of incorporated dealer to suppliers, dealing instead in names of corporations that did not exist); [_Purbaugh v. Jurgensmeier, 483 N.W.2d 757, 763 (Neb.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-4MH0-003D-B1PV-00000-00&context=1530671) (personal representative of estate may be subject to liability for breach of real-estate sales contract when representative did not reveal capacity at time of making contract; initials "P.R." following representative's signature would not indicate what or whom was represented, and record insufficient to determine whether third parties otherwise had notice of capacity in which representative acted).

English and Canadian cases do not follow the rule stated in subsection (2). English cases differentiate between oral and written contracts. An agent for an unidentified principal is not a party to an oral contract unless the agent and the third party agree explicitly that the agent shall be a party. See N. & J. Vlassopullos Ltd. v. Ney Shipping Co. (The Santa Carina) \[1977\] 1 Lloyd's Rep. 478. The terms of a written contract may be read to impose liability on an agent although they do not do so expressly. See Tudor Marine Ltd. v. Tradax Exp. S.A. (The Virgo) \[1976\] 2 Lloyd's Rep. 135 (charterparty stated it was entered into between "\[named\] owners," and "\[named\] charterers"; clause in charterparty provided that "\[t\]his vessel chartered on behalf of \[named third party\]"; charterer liable). For further discussion, see Francis M.B. Reynolds, Bowstead & Reynolds on Agency 469-470, 485, 490-491 (17th ed. 2001). The Supreme Court of Canada rejected the rule stated in subsection (2) in Q.N.S. Paper Co. v. Chartwell Shipping Ltd., \[1989\] 2 S.C.R. 683.

An agent's ability to bind an unidentified principal is, of course, subject to other law. For example, under the Carriage of Goods by Sea Act, a bill of lading issued by a ship's master that contains a provision limiting a carrier's liability is ineffective if it contravenes 28 U.S.C. § 1303(8). See [_Epstein v. United States, 86 F. Supp. 740 (S.D.N.Y.1949)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-J7J0-003B-115C-00000-00&context=1530671).

_d. Notice of principal's identity and agent's status._ Illustration 7 is based on [_Van D. Costas, Inc. v. Rosenberg, 432 So. 2d 656, 659 (Fla. Dist.App.1983)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-8M00-003C-X3MW-00000-00&context=1530671) (noting that disclosure of a trade name is not generally viewed as sufficient to disclose principal's identity).

For the point that third parties are not required to check assumed-name filings to ascertain the identity of a principal, see _Lumer v. Marone, 569 N.Y.S.2d 321, 322 (Sup.App.Term 1990)_ (incorporated auto-repair business filed assumed-name certificate as "Corvette Station," and used assumed name on its stationery; liability of individuals who did business as "Corvette Station" not defeated by fact that repair customer could have ascertained name of corporation by checking records on file with secretary of state).

In the maritime context, a third party's ability to ascertain a principal's identity through public records may suffice to disclose the principal's identity. See _Port Ship Serv., Inc. v. Norton, Lilly & Co., 883 F.2d 23, 24 (5th Cir.1989)_; _Instituto Cubano v. The S.S. Theotokos, 155 F. Supp. 945 (S.D.N.Y.1957)_. If a principal is identified as a vessel's owner, the owner's identity can be determined through reliable industry publications. See Fireman's Fund McGee Marine v. M/V "Caroline," 2004 WL 287663, at \*3 (S.D.N.Y.2004).

Illustration 8 is based on [_Waste, Water & Land, Inc. v. Lanham, 955 P.2d 997 (Colo.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S6V-36C0-0039-451R-00000-00&context=1530671). The court held that the counterpart of B in the Illustration is subject to liability to the third party because the third party did not have notice that B acted on behalf of an LLC. The court's opinion does not consider whether A's counterpart is also subject to liability; a lower court dismissed A as a party from the lawsuit and it appears T's counterpart did not appeal. The court's opinion focuses on whether Colo. Rev. Stat. § 7-80-208 should be treated as charging third parties who deal with an agent of an LLC with notice of the LLC's identity and existence. Section 7-80-208 provides that "\[t\]he fact that the articles of organization are on file in the records of the Secretary of State is notice that the limited liability company is a limited liability company and is notice of all the other facts set forth therein which are required or expressly permitted to be set forth in the articles of organization . . . ." The court held that § 7-80-208 does not oust the applicability of common-law agency doctrines that impose individual liability on an agent who fails to disclose the existence and identity of the principal on whose behalf the agent enters into contracts. See [_955 P.2d at 1002_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S6V-36C0-0039-451R-00000-00&context=1530671). Instead, the court interpreted the effect of § 7-80-208 to insulate members and managers of LLCs from individual liability due simply to their status as such. [_Id. at 1004_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S6V-36C0-0039-451R-00000-00&context=1530671). Other LLC statutes contain language comparable to the Colorado statute. See, e.g., Del. Code Ann., tit. 6, § 18-207 (2001 & Supp. 2004) ("\[t\]he fact that a certificate of formation is on file in the office of the Secretary of State is notice that the entity formed in connection with the filing of the certificate of formation is a limited liability company formed under the laws of the State of Delaware and is notice of all other facts set forth therein . . . ."). The court in _Waste, Water & Land_ also saw no indication that the legislature, in enacting an LLC statute, intended to insulate agents from individual liability when the LLC's identity was not disclosed to the third party. [_955 P.2d at 1003_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S6V-36C0-0039-451R-00000-00&context=1530671). See also Robert B. Thompson, The Taming of Limited Liability Companies, [_66 U. Colo. L. Rev. 921, 939-940 (1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S3T-T9C0-00CV-N067-00000-00&context=1530671) (stating that it is "unlikely" that the insulation from liability afforded by LLCs will exceed that available through use of the corporate form).

For the point that notice that an agent acts on behalf of a firm does not constitute notice that the firm is a limited-liability entity, see [_Benjamin Plumbing, Inc. v. Barnes, 470 N.W.2d 888, 895 (Wis.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-VRW0-003G-32W8-00000-00&context=1530671) ("the fact that \[T\] was aware that \[A\] was acting on behalf of an entity called RHN reveals nothing of \[T's\] awareness of the type of business organization it was dealing with. All business entities are not corporations. . . . the fact that RHN was manifestly a charitable, nonprofit organization does not lead to the inference that it was a corporation . . . . RHN might just as well have been an unincorporated association."). However, the fact that a contract names a corporation as a party but omits to follow its name with an "Inc." or other indicator that it is a limited-liability entity does not categorically establish that the parties intended that the person who signed on behalf of the corporation would be individually liable on the contract. See [_Promotion Co. v. Sweeney, 782 N.E.2d 117, 123 (Ohio App.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:47DK-HNW0-0039-42M5-00000-00&context=1530671) (when plaintiff, who drafted contract, as well as individual defendant, signed above name of corporation without labeling himself its president, fact that name of corporation on whose behalf defendant signed was not followed by "Inc." at most created issue of material fact on parties' intention allowing factfinder to resolve question through use of parol evidence).

Illustration 9 is based on [_Weil v. Murray, 161 F.Supp.2d 250 (S.D.N.Y. 2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:42SX-H180-0038-Y32W-00000-00&context=1530671) (language in contract unambiguously made immediate purchaser of painting an agent for an unidentified principal, not an intermediary whose sole contractual obligation was to reveal principal's identity).

On the U.C.C. and consignment transactions, see G. Ray Warner, Consigned to Confusion: Consignments Under Revised Article 9, 20 Am. Bankr. Inst. J. 30 (Dec. 2001/Jan. 2002). Revised Article 9 does not apply to consignments if a merchant conducts its business in the same name as the consignor, is an auctioneer, or is "generally known by its creditors to be substantially engaged in selling the goods of others." U.C.C. § 9-102(a)(20)(A). Revised Article 9 is also inapplicable to low-value consignments, those for which, "with respect to each delivery, the aggregate value of the goods" is less than \$ 1000 at the time of delivery. Id. § 9-102(a)(20)(B). On statutes applicable to consignments of art, see Marc Marcone, The UCC and Consignment: Making the Code Safe for Artists and Other "Little Fellows," [_12 Cardozo Arts & Ent. L.J. 579 (1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S3T-WRX0-00CW-3154-00000-00&context=1530671). On the rights of consignors, compare [_Shuttie v. Festa Rest., Inc., 566 So. 2d 554 (Fla.Dist.App.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-CKH0-003F-354X-00000-00&context=1530671) (artist who consigned paintings to gallery but did not comply with notice provisions in Artists Consignment Act, Fla. Stat. 568.502, sought to replevy paintings after gallery gave them to restaurant as security for loan; court holds restaurant has superior possessory interest as an innocent third party that took security interest in paintings) with [_Manger v. Davis, 619 P.2d 687 (Utah 1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-5NK0-003G-F3RJ-00000-00&context=1530671) (owner of diamond ring consigned it for sale to gemologist, who pledged it to defendants as security for a personal loan, representing that he owned it; court holds that ring's owner may recover it because gemologist-consignee had possession of ring only for purpose of selling it and pledge transaction was not incidental to authorized sale transaction). In _Manger,_ in contrast with _Shuttie,_ the consignee did not regularly deal in goods of the kind. As a consequence, U.C.C. § 2-403 would have been inapplicable to a sale of the ring. It provides that "\[a\]ny entrusting of goods to a merchant that deals in goods of that kind gives the merchant power to transfer all of the entruster's rights to the goods and to transfer the goods free of any interest of the entruster to a buyer in ordinary course of business." Id. § 2-403(2).
