[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 6.03_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8W0-00YG-4003-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 6- Contracts and Other Transactions with Third Parties_ > _Topic 1- Parties to Contracts_**

**§ 6.03 Agent for Undisclosed Principal**

**When an agent acting with actual authority makes a contract on behalf of an undisclosed principal,**

**(1) unless excluded by the contract, the principal is a party to the contract;**

**(2) the agent and the third party are parties to the contract; and**

**(3) the principal, if a party to the contract, and the third party have the same rights, liabilities, and defenses against each other as if the principal made the contract personally, subject to §§ 6.05-6.09.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section states the basic principle that, when an agent enters into a contract on behalf of an undisclosed principal, the third party, the principal, and the agent become parties to the contract. Comment _b_ discusses the rationales for contractual liability when an agent acts on behalf of an undisclosed principal. Comment c discusses the circumstances that determine when an agent has made a contract on behalf of an undisclosed principal, as well as consequences for an undisclosed principal who becomes a party to a contract. Comment _d_ covers circumstances that may affect the rights and liabilities of an undisclosed principal, including contracts that explicitly or implicitly exclude an undisclosed principal. Comment _e_ discusses the position of an agent as a party to a contract made on behalf of an undisclosed principal. Comment _f_ examines the rights and liabilities of undisclosed principals as parties to sealed contracts and negotiable instruments.

"Undisclosed principal" is defined in § 1.04(2)(b). Sections 2.01 and 2.02 define actual authority.

_b. Rationales for contractual liability._ Contemporary contract doctrine requires a manifestation of mutual assent to an exchange and consideration for the formation of a contract. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 17(1). However, when an agent acts on behalf of an undisclosed principal, the third party does not manifest assent to an exchange with the principal and the principal does not make a manifestation of assent to the third party. The third party's manifestation of assent is made to the agent to whom the third party expects to render performance and from whom the third party expects to receive performance. In contrast, when an agent makes a contract on behalf of a disclosed principal, the third party has notice of the principal's existence and identity. See § 6.01, Comment _b._ When an agent makes a contract on behalf of an unidentified principal, the third party has notice that the agent acts on behalf of a principal but does not have notice of the principal's identity. See § 6.02, Comment _b._ In both cases, the third party manifests assent to an agent with notice that the agent represents another person who will be a party to the contract.

However, well-settled doctrine treats an undisclosed principal as a party to a contract that an agent makes on behalf of the principal, unless the contract excludes the principal as a party. If an agent acts with actual authority in making a contract on an undisclosed principal's behalf, the basis for treating the principal as a party to the contract is that the agent acted reasonably on the basis of the principal's manifestation of assent to the agent. See §§ 2.01 and 2.02. The principal's liability on the contract is thus consistent with the agent's reasonable understanding of the principal's wishes. The principal has rights under the contract because the agent acted on the principal's behalf in making the contract. A third party, however, may exclude an undisclosed principal as a party to a contract by explicitly so providing in a contract.

Moreover, even if comparable results could be achieved through assignment, it may be commercially more convenient to treat an undisclosed principal as a party to contracts made on the principal's behalf by an agent. It may be advantageous to an undisclosed principal to acquire contractual rights directly through an agent, rather than risk any delay or other slippage that may intervene if the principal may acquire rights only by assignment. It may also be advantageous to an agent to represent principals whose interest is not disclosed. If an agent invests in developing a distribution network, the agent may be concerned that third parties who know the identity of the agent's principals will deal directly with them, bypassing the agent.

In some circumstances, moreover, a third party's reasonable expectations will receive adequate protection only if an undisclosed principal is liable on a contract made on its behalf by an agent. For example, the undisclosed principal and not the agent may own assets against which a third-party creditor may reasonably expect to be able to execute a judgment.

**Illustration:**

1\. A, the sole proprietor of a construction business, deals over several years with T, who is in the building-supplies business. A maintains an account with T that requires A to pay for supplies 30 days following delivery. Without notice to T, A incorporates A's business as "A Construction Corp." and transfers to A Construction Corp. ownership of all assets used by A in operating the business. A continues to run the business as before and continues to purchase supplies on credit from T. Both A and A Construction Corp. are subject to liability for payment owed to T for supplies delivered by T to A's business after A incorporates it. A acted as the agent of A Construction Corp., A's undisclosed principal.

An agent who makes a contract on behalf of an undisclosed principal also becomes a party to the contract. The basis for treating the agent as a party to the contract is the expectation of the third party. The agent has dealt with the third party as if the agent were the sole party whose legal relations would be affected as a consequence of making the contract.

A notification given or received by an agent for an undisclosed principal is effective as a notification given or received by the principal when the agent acts with actual authority or the principal is estopped as stated in § 2.06. On notification generally, see § 5.02.

_c. Contract made on behalf of undisclosed principal._ If an agent acts without actual authority in making a contract on behalf of an undisclosed principal, the principal may be subject to liability as stated in § 2.06. A principal in such a case may acquire rights against the third party by ratifying the agent's conduct as stated in § 4.03. An agent does not act with apparent authority with regard to an undisclosed principal because the principal has made no manifestation requisite for apparent authority. See §§ 2.03 and 3.03.

Whether an agent acts on behalf of an undisclosed principal is a question of fact. Parol evidence is admissible to prove that an agent entered into a contract on behalf of an undisclosed principal, although the contract is evidenced by a writing or record that does not mention the principal. Parol evidence is not admissible to show that the agent is not subject to liability. The writing or record evidencing the contract between the agent and the third party would be contradicted by parol evidence admitted to show that it was intended that the agent would not be a party to the contract.

An undisclosed principal only becomes a party to a contract when an agent acts on the principal's behalf in making the contract. Thus, an undisclosed principal does not become a party to a contract when the agent does not intend to act for the principal. Factual circumstances at the time the agent acts are relevant to proving the agent's intention.

**Illustration:**

2\. P authorizes A to buy a particular computer from T in A's name for P and gives A the money necessary for the purchase. Instead, A purchases the computer from T on A's own credit, intending to abscond with both the computer and P's money. P is not liable to T on the contract for the computer. A did not intend to act on P's account in purchasing the computer.

A principal is undisclosed if, at the time a contract is made, the third party with whom the agent deals has no notice that the agent is acting on behalf of a principal. It is a question of fact whether the third party has received sufficient notice that the contract is made with an agent who represents a principal and sufficient notice of that principal's identity. The third party is not subject to a duty to discover the principal's existence or identity; the responsibility is the agent's if the agent wishes to avoid personal liability on the contract. However, a third party may have sufficient notice of the principal's existence or identity from sources apart from the agent.

**Illustrations:**

3\. A, the President of P College, hires T, an architect, to prepare plans for a new college building. T has reason to know that A contracts on behalf of P College.

4\. A is the President of P Corporation, which owns a large ranch. A hires T to grind stumps on ranch property. T knows that the ranch property is large and that the ranch uses a trade name, "Rocking D Ranch." T's knowledge of these facts does not give T reason to know that A makes the stump-grinding contract on behalf of P Corporation.

For further discussion of circumstances that may give notice to a third party that an agent acts on behalf of a principal, see § 6.01, Comment c.

As a party to a contract made by an agent, an undisclosed principal may assert defenses available to a party that would not be available to an assignee of the contract. Unless otherwise provided by statute, an undisclosed principal may assert personal defenses, such as usury.

_d. Circumstances that affect rights or liabilities of undisclosed principal; contract excluding undisclosed principal as party._ An undisclosed principal does not become a party to a contract if the contract excludes the principal. An explicit exclusion limits the third party's manifestation of assent to be bound. Such an explicit exclusion provides a simple device through which a third party may exclude the interests of persons other than those identified as parties to the contract. An undisclosed principal is not excluded from a contract by language stating that the contract is not assignable.

**Illustration:**

5\. A enters into a stock-purchase agreement with T Corporation. The agreement contains a representation made by A that A acts solely for A's account and that no other person will have any interest in the securities that A will acquire from T Corporation. The agreement excludes P, A's undisclosed principal, from rights or obligations under the agreement.

In contrast, an undisclosed principal and an agent may agree that the agent alone shall be liable on a contract. An agreement between an undisclosed principal and an agent cannot effectively exclude the principal as a party to the contract because the third party has not assented to such exclusion.

By dealing on behalf of an undisclosed principal, an agent does not implicitly represent that the agent is not acting for a principal. This is because it is usually not material to a third party whether the person with whom the third party deals, and who becomes subject to liability on contracts made with the third party, acts as an agent for an undisclosed principal. See § 6.11, Comment _d._ If an agent falsely represents that the agent does not act on behalf of a principal, the third party may avoid a contract made with the agent under the circumstances stated in § 6.11(4). A third party may avoid a contract made by an agent acting for an undisclosed principal if the agent or the principal knows or has reason to know that the third party would not have dealt with the principal as a party to the contract. See id. See also § 6.02, Comment c.

The nature of the performance that a contract requires determines whether performance by an undisclosed principal will be effective as performance under the contract and whether an undisclosed principal can require that the third party render performance to the principal. Performance by an undisclosed principal is not effective as performance under a contract if the third party has a substantial interest in receiving performance from the agent who made the contract. This limit corresponds to the limit on delegability of performance of a duty as stated in Restatement Second, Contracts § 318(2).

**Illustrations:**

6\. T enters into a contract with A in which A promises to manage T's investment portfolio. A does not disclose that A makes the contract on behalf of P. P offers to manage T's portfolio. T is free to accept or reject P's offer of performance. P's offer does not constitute an offer of performance of the contract made by A. T has a substantial interest in receiving investment-management services from A.

7\. T enters into a contract to purchase a quantity of coal of a specified kind from A. A does not disclose that A makes the contract on behalf of P. P tenders coal to T of the specified kind and quantity. P's tender has the effect of a tender by A because T has no substantial interest in receiving the coal from A.

The nature of the performance that a contract requires from a third party determines whether an undisclosed principal is entitled to receive that performance. An undisclosed principal may not require that a third party render performance to the principal if rendering performance to the principal would materially change the nature of the third party's duty, materially increase the burden or risk imposed on the third party, or materially impair the third party's chance of receiving return performance. These limits correspond to the limits imposed on assignment of a contractual right. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 317(2).

**Illustrations:**

8\. T agrees to work as a nanny for A. P, A's undisclosed principal, cannot require T to work as a nanny for P. The contract between T and A requires that T render personal services in an ongoing close association. Requiring T to render the services to P would materially change the nature of T's duties.

9\. T agrees to sell Blackacre in exchange for cash to A, who acts on behalf of P, A's undisclosed principal. P may require performance from T. The contract made by A requires only the payment of money in exchange for Blackacre.

_e. Position of agent as party to contract._ As a party to a contract made on behalf of an undisclosed principal, an agent may sue the third party in the agent's own name. The agent is subject to liability on the contract. If sued on the contract, the agent may assert all defenses arising from the transaction and defenses personal to the agent.

**Illustration:**

10\. P owns a farm which A manages. A makes a contract to sell a quantity of potatoes from P's farm to T, expressly warranting that they are seed potatoes. T does not have notice that A acts on behalf of P. After T takes delivery of the potatoes, pays for them, and resells them, T discovers that they are not seed potatoes. A and P are subject to liability to T for breach of warranty.

_f. Sealed and negotiable instruments._ Unless a statute provides otherwise, a principal has rights under and is subject to liability on a contract whether or not the contract is under seal. This is so whether the principal is disclosed, unidentified, or undisclosed. See § 6.01, Comment _f,_ for discussion of this point as it concerns disclosed principals. In contrast, under Restatement Second, Agency § 191, an undisclosed principal is not liable as a party to a sealed instrument in the absence of a statute providing otherwise. Under Restatement Second, Agency § 296, in the absence of statute, an obligor named in a sealed instrument given to an agent on behalf of a principal is not subject to liability to the principal in an action at law unless the principal appears in the instrument as a covenantee.

Any general distinction between sealed and unsealed contracts has been abolished by statute in many states. Additionally, U.C.C. § 2-203 makes law respecting sealed instruments inapplicable to contracts for the sale of goods or offers for such contracts. At the time of completion of Restatement Second, Contracts, the seal's "decay" had reached the point that it had come to seem "archaic" and its principal legal consequence was often only the application of a longer statute of limitations, as opposed to more substantive consequences. See Restatement Second, Contracts, Chapter 4, Topic 3, Introductory Note. Many cases from the 20th century that consider the question do not permit an undisclosed principal to avoid liability on a contract when the contract recites that it is made under seal. Some cases reach this conclusion in the absence of legislation abolishing distinctions between sealed and unsealed contracts.

In the absence of a controlling statute, ascribing significance to the presence or absence of a seal is inconsistent with the expectations of most contemporary parties. Many parties, if they are aware of the question, may well believe that sealing no longer carries operative significance because so many significant transactions may be undertaken without seals. This likelihood is enhanced by the fact that legislative and common-law changes in many states have eliminated many operative consequences that sealing carried in the past. Moreover, legislation concerning the effect of seals is far from uniform and, as noted above, some courts have concluded that the presence or absence of a seal on a contract should not affect the rights or liabilities of an undisclosed principal even in the absence of legislation abolishing the effect of sealing as a general matter.

Moreover, the functional argument in support of the rule stated in Restatement Second, Agency §§ 191 and 296 is not strong, at least in the contemporary environment described above. The common-law rule excluding the liability of an undisclosed principal when an instrument is sealed is not necessary to enable parties, if they wish, to exclude an undisclosed principal from a contract. This is because the parties may accomplish this objective by so providing in the contract. See Comment _d_ for discussion. A provision that explicitly excludes an undisclosed principal as a party from a contract, such as the provision in Illustration 5, is likely to be a more accurate manifestation of the parties' intention than the fact that the writing states that it is made "under seal," because most parties are unaware of the consequences of so stating.

The liabilities of an undisclosed principal on a negotiable instrument are governed by U.C.C. § 3-401, which provides that a person is not liable on an instrument unless the person signed it or "the person is represented by an agent or representative who signed the instrument and the signature is binding on the represented person under Section 3-402." Under § 3-402(a), a person is bound by a representative's signature "to the same extent the represented person would be bound if the signature were on a simple contract" and regardless of whether the representative signs in the representative's own name or that of the represented person. Section 3-301 defines the persons who are entitled to enforce an instrument.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency, codifications, and other jurisdictions._ This section is a consolidated treatment of matter covered in several separate sections of Restatement Second, Agency, including §§ 186, 189, 190, 191, 192, 197, 199, 302, 303, 309, 310, 322, 334, 363, 364, and 368.

Most codifications of agency law currently in effect in the United States recognize the possibility that an undisclosed principal may become a party to a contract made by an agent. Representative code sections include Ga. Code § 10-6-54 (when identity of principal is discovered, person who dealt with agent may "go directly upon the principal under the contract, unless the principal shall have previously accounted and settled with the agent"); and Cal. Civil Code § 2330 ("\[a\]n agent represents his principal for all purposes within the scope of his actual or ostensible authority, and all rights and liabilities which would accrue to the agent from transactions within such limit, if they had been entered into on his own account, accrue to the principal").

Louisiana's Civil Code, as amended in 1997, recognizes the possibility of an undisclosed mandate in art. 3017 ("\[a\] mandatary who contracts in his own name without disclosing his status as a mandatary binds himself personally for the performance of the contract."). Under art. 3020, an undisclosed principal is bound to a third party when a mandatary makes a contract within the scope of the mandatary's authority. Under art. 3023, "\[a\] third person with whom a mandatary contracts without disclosing his status or the identity of the principal is bound to the principal for the performance of the contract unless the obligation is strictly personal or the right non-assignable. The third person may raise all defenses that may be asserted against the mandatary or the principal." Prior to the 1997 amendments, Louisiana law recognized that an undisclosed principal may be subject to liability on a contract made on the principal's behalf by an agent. See [_Woodlawn Park Ltd. P'ship v. Doster Constr. Co., 623 So. 2d 645, 648 (La.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-B110-003G-N20X-00000-00&context=1530671).

In contrast, civilian systems of agency "tend to draw a bright line between acting in the name of another and acting in one's own name. . . . The requirement that the agent must conclude the contract 'in the name of his principal might lead one to infer _e contrario_ that the principal acquires no rights or liabilities if the agent, though acting within the scope of his authority, does _not_ act in the name of his principal, in other words does _not_ make it clear that the legal consequences are to attach to him. This is indeed the conclusion drawn by Continental systems: if a person concludes a contract in his own name, it is not a case of agency, and he alone acquires the rights and is subject to the duties arising therefrom, even if he was authorized by the principal to enter into the transaction, did so on the principal's behalf, was bound to account to him, and was promised a fee for his efforts." Konrad Zweigert & Hein Kotz, Introduction to Comparative Law 433 (3rd ed., Tony Weir transl. 1998) (emphasis in original). One scholar explained the intellectual and institutional origins of this difference as follows: "While … there is no evidence that historically the doctrine of the undisclosed principal was developed by the Court of Chancery, there can be no doubt that equity has mentally conditioned the English lawyer to accept with equanimity a situation in which a person, acting in his own name but in the interest of another, constitutes a direct bond between that other and the third party. The civil lawyer, whose legal philosophy does not include tri-dimensional thinking in terms of legal ownership, equitable ownership and obligation, can approach the concept of mercantile commission only by means of the two-contract construction." Clive M. Schmitthoff, Agency in International Trade: A Study in Comparative Law, in Clive M. Schmitthoff's Select Essays on International Trade Law 306, 327 (Chia-Jui Cheng ed., 1988). For discussion of solutions within civilian systems to the absence of direct ties between an undisclosed principal and the third party, see Jan H. Dalhuisen, Dalhuisen on International Commercial, Financial and Trade Law 286-289 (2000).

Under Puerto Rico's Civil Code, an undisclosed principal is not subject to liability when an agent contracts in the agent's own name. See [_Rodriguez v. Republic of Costa Rica, 139 F.Supp.2d 173, 186 (D.P.R.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:42W2-YBD0-0038-Y3YX-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_297 F.3d 1 (1st Cir.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4692-S0P0-0038-X3YS-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Laws of Puerto Rico Ann. tit. 31, § 4426, court holds that government is not liable on lease entered into by its consuls in their own name; under Puerto Rican law, government is responsible for torts committed by its agent if there is a reasonable relationship between the agent's acts and the principal's purposes or the agent acted for the principal's benefit), aff'd, [_297 F.3d 1 (1st Cir.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4692-S0P0-0038-X3YS-00000-00&context=1530671).

_b. Rationales for contractual liability._ On the practical importance of undisclosed agency, see J. Dennis Hynes & Mark J. Loewenstein, Agency, Partnership, and the LLC: The Law of Unincorporated Business Enterprises 351-352 (6th ed. 2003) (by dealing as undisclosed principal, person interested in assembling tract of land from multiple owners for largescale project can overcome problem of hold-out owners who, once buyer's interest becomes known, may exploit buyer's vulnerability by demanding prices well in excess of current market value). On the reasons why an agent may prefer that principals be undisclosed, see Tan Cheng-Han, Undisclosed Principals and Contract, 120 L.Q. Rev. 480, 482-484 (2004).

Early and influential authority for the proposition that an undisclosed principal is a party to a contract made on the principal's behalf by an agent and may thus assert rights under the contract is Scrimshire v. Alderton, 2 Strange 182, 93 Eng. Rep. 1114 (K.B.1743) (farmer whose oats were sold by agent had right to receive payment from buyer who had paid agent despite farmer's notice not to do so). The agent in _Scrimshire_ was a factor who, in exchange for a commission, took the risk that the buyer would default.

For the point that third-party reliance is the basis for the personal liability of the agent who deals on behalf of an undisclosed principal, see [_Kimco Corp. v. Murdoch, Coll & Lillibridge, Inc., 730 N.E.2d 1143, 1146-1147 (Ill.App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40B8-R9M0-0039-4113-00000-00&context=1530671) (third party "is obviously relying on the credit of the agent and not the principal"). See also [_Fisher Scientific Co. v. McCorkle, 295 S.E.2d 366 (Ga.App.1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-7MS0-003F-J362-00000-00&context=1530671) (third party who makes contract with agent for undisclosed principal may elect to sue agent; although employer permitted employee to order supplies from third party using its name, employee in fact acted as agent for his friend, characterized by court as his undisclosed principal).

For the point that an undisclosed principal's rights on a contract made by an agent may be traced to the relationship between the principal and the agent and the agent's duties to the principal, but also characterizing the relationship as one in which the agent "transfers" rights acquired to an undisclosed principal, see Randy E. Barnett, Squaring Undisclosed Agency Law with Contract Theory, [_75 Cal. L. Rev. 1969, 1981 (1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S41-2700-00CV-60W4-00000-00&context=1530671) (consent theory of agency relationship "contemplates that all agents who are empowered to enter into contracts with third parties on behalf of their principals also enter into a contract with their principals--a contract in which they explicitly or implicitly commit to transfer to their principals any rights they receive under the contracts they enter into in their own name but on the principal's behalf").

Illustration 1 is based on the facts of [_Grinder v. Bryans Road Bldg. & Supply Co., 432 A.2d 453 (Md.1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-4NN0-003G-23XM-00000-00&context=1530671). In _Grinder_ itself, the question is whether a supplier, having taken judgment against the principal, is barred from pursuing the agent. The court's answer is no. This issue is covered by § 6.09.

For the basic point that an undisclosed principal may sue or be sued on a contract made on the principal's behalf by an agent, see [_Piedmont Resolution, L.L.C. v. Johnston, Rivlin & Foley, 999 F. Supp. 34 (D.D.C. 1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SBS-WV70-0038-Y00M-00000-00&context=1530671) (undisclosed principal may sue bank when agent arranged electronicfunds transfer and contract did not exclude principal).

_c. Contract made on behalf of undisclosed principal._ The classic case on the admissibility of parol evidence is [_Ford v. Williams, 62 U.S. (21 How.) 287, 289 (1858)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-K1M0-003B-H356-00000-00&context=1530671). Not all jurisdictions follow the doctrine as stated, see [_Lee v. YES of Russellville, Inc., 784 So. 2d 1022, 1027-1028 (Ala.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:41PP-CH00-0039-44Y6-00000-00&context=1530671) (parol evidence not admissible to establish liability of undisclosed principal when agent signs writing using words that describe agent as an agent, trustee, or other representative; when agent signed contract identifying himself as officer of business, contract is ambiguous and parol evidence is admissible to show that agent acted as principal's agent).

Illustration 2 is based on Illustration 1 to Restatement Second, Agency § 199.

Illustration 3 is based on [_Johnson v. Armstrong, 18 S.W. 594, 595 (Tex. 1892)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S11-VHP0-003D-R2MK-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 4 is based on [_Burch v. Hancock, 56 S.W.3d 257 (Tex.App. 2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:447N-PMW0-0039-4508-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Leprino Foods Co. v. Gress Poultry, Inc., 379 F.Supp.2d 650 (M.D.Pa.2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4GSJ-JYX0-TVWB-J0N2-00000-00&context=1530671) (individuals who owned storage company not subject to liability to bailor due to damage to 8 million pounds of mozzarella stored in facility; although corporate and fictitious names used interchangeably in parties' dealings, bailor aware that corporate entity conducted business and bailor failed to present evidence that individuals actually operated business as a partnership); [_Interlode Constructors, Inc. v. Bryant, 974 P.2d 89, 93 (Idaho App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3W01-SBP0-0039-40BW-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporate manager personally liable when neither manager nor his agent made third party aware of existence of corporation).

For the point that an undisclosed principal may, absent a statute to the contrary, assert a usury defense to enforcement of a contract made by an agent, see [_Lenart v. Ragsdale, 385 N.W.2d 282, 284 (Mich.App.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-HBD0-003D-625D-00000-00&context=1530671). On the statute of limitations when an undisclosed principal brings suit on a contract made by an agent, see [_Reid v. Wells, 721 N.E.2d 163, 165-166 (Ill.App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XY8-4920-0039-443W-00000-00&context=1530671) (10-year limitations period applicable to written contracts available to undisclosed principal; suit not barred by 5-year limitations period applicable to unwritten contracts).

On the composition of Lloyd's syndicates, compare _Indiana Gas Co. v. Home Ins. Co., 141 F.3d 314, 318-319 (7th Cir.)_, cert. denied, _525 U.S. 931 (1998)_ (managing underwriter of syndicate acts as agent for syndicate's names, who are undisclosed principals; for purposes of determining whether diversity of citizenship is present, Lloyd's syndicate should be treated as a partnership, which makes syndicate a citizen of every state of which any name is a citizen) with [_Certain Interested Underwriters at Lloyd's v. Layne, 26 F.3d 39, 43 (6th Cir.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-5FV0-003B-P0V9-00000-00&context=1530671) (citizenship of names in syndicate is irrelevant because active underwriter can be characterized as agent for undisclosed principal). _Layne's_ reasoning is specifically rejected by Indiana Gas, see _141 F.3d at 319_ ("_Layne_ is mistaken. The proposition that an agent for an undisclosed principal _is_ liable does not imply that the undisclosed principal is _not_ bound by the contract; the full statement of the 'venerable rule' is that both principal and agent are bound.") and by [_E.R. Squibb & Sons, Inc. v. Accident & Cas. Ins. Co., 160 F.3d 925, 930-931 (2d Cir.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3V62-RKK0-0038-X208-00000-00&context=1530671). Names who are identified in the insurance policy are characterized as disclosed principals in [_Northland Ins. Co. v. Arthur Hill & Assocs., 126 F.Supp.2d 1066, 1071-1072 (E.D.Mich.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:424D-BCY0-0038-Y1DY-00000-00&context=1530671) (court must determine citizenship of "the Lloyd's collective" in case by reference to citizenship of each member of each participating syndicate). On the assignment of risk, see [_Annes v. Carolan-Graham-Hoffman, Inc., 168 N.E. 637, 639 (Ill.1929)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-4BM0-003F-1082-00000-00&context=1530671) (no principal at time contract written; unknown to agent who will be obligated, which will not be known until underwriting completed).

_d. Circumstances that affect rights or liabilities of undisclosed principal; contract excluding undisclosed principal as party._ Illustration 5 is based on John J. Slain, Charles A. Thompson & Freda F. Bein, Agency, Partnership and Employment: A Transactional Approach 4-87 (1980) (noting that such representations by buyers are customary and are intended to maintain availability of exemptions from registration under the Securities Act of 1933 that turn on purchaser's identity).

On the position of an undisclosed principal under a letter of credit, see [_Kools v. Citibank, N.A., 872 F.Supp. 67 (S.D.N.Y.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-W1Y0-001T-50SS-00000-00&context=1530671), question certified, [_73 F.3d 5 (2d Cir.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-9490-001T-D2H5-00000-00&context=1530671), certification withdrawn, 1996 WL 450776 (2d Cir. 1996) (holding that an undisclosed principal is excluded from a letter of credit, despite absence of language of exclusion in letter, because according rights to an undisclosed principal would as a functional matter violate principle of separation that protects issuer of letter from responsibility to police underlying transaction that the letter is intended to finance). To the contrary is [_Taub v. Colonial Coated Textile Corp., 387 N.Y.S.2d 869 (App. Div.1976)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-F3J0-003C-F51S-00000-00&context=1530671), which _Kools_ expressly declines to follow. The letter in _Kools_ incorporated the terms of the UCP (Uniform Customs and Practices for Documentary Credits), "an internationally accepted codification of banking practice whose provisions, when incorporated in a contract, displace Article 5 of the Uniform Commercial Code...." [_872 F.Supp. at 71_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-W1Y0-001T-50SS-00000-00&context=1530671). The UCP provides that a letter of credit creates a tripartite relationship among the bank that issues the letter, the customer who applies for the credit, and the beneficiary of the credit, with the sole exception arising when an issuing bank at the request of the applicant authorizes another bank to effect payment. Under the UCP, an issuing bank "is 'in no way concerned with or bound' by any contract outside its agreement with the applicant 'even if any reference whatsoever to such contract is included in the credit.'" Id. In contrast, former U.C.C. § 5-103(1)(g) provided that a customer of a letter of credit is a "buyer or other person who causes an issuer to issue a credit." This definition is less restrictive than the UCP and is more compatible with an argument that "the customer can be someone other than the person who actually enters into the agreement with the bank." Id.

Illustration 7 is based on Illustration 2 to Restatement Second, Contracts § 318.

Illustration 8 is a contemporary version of the point made in Restatement Second, Agency § 310, Comment _b,_ that "one agreeing to become a butler for the agent has no duty to render services as servant of the principal." On an undisclosed principal's right to tender performance, see [_Birmingham Matinee Club v. McCarty, 44 So. 642 (Ala.1907)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3Y9P-B6C0-0046-80B9-00000-00&context=1530671) (undisclosed principal may not, over objection of third party, tender its warranty deed when contract called for agent's warranty deed).

An undisclosed principal may be able to enforce rights under some contracts of insurance when the identity of the person whose name appears on the policy is immaterial to the risk insured against. See Siu Yin Kwan v. Eastern Ins. Co., \[1994\] 1 Lloyd's Rep. 616 (P.C.1993) (undisclosed principal of shipping agent who obtained policy of insurance against employees' claims could sue insurer under policy unless agent should have realized insurer was unwilling to contract with anyone other than it; trial judge found actual identity of employer to be immaterial to insurer's risk when insurer would have insured employer of crew of particular ship, whoever that might be). Accord, [_Sig M. Glukstad, Inc. v. Lineas Aereas Paraguayas, 619 F.2d 457 (5th Cir.1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-CJX0-0039-W4X9-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent for undisclosed principal, a freight forwarder, may obtain insurance in own name on behalf of undisclosed principal).

_e. Position of agent as party to contract._ For the point that an agent, as a party to a contract made on behalf of an undisclosed principal, may sue the third party in the agent's own name, see [_Perry v. Breland, 16 S.W.3d 182, 187 (Tex.App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:401X-YG30-0039-44KF-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent contracting for undisclosed principal "has the right to sue if it is apparent that the contract would be enforceable against him if the opposite party sued"; plaintiff sued seller of truck that plaintiff bought as agent for undisclosed principal).

On the availability of defenses to an agent when sued by the third party on a contract made on behalf of an undisclosed principal, see [_O'Connor v. Hancock, 604 A.2d 565, 566-567 (N.H. 1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-4P00-003G-B14W-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent has all defenses that arise out of transaction itself; agent may assert recoupment defense in action against agent and principal for breach of contract).

Illustration 10 is based on [_Maine Farmers Exch. v. McGillicuddy, 697 A.2d 1266 (Me.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RJN-3RM0-0039-4017-00000-00&context=1530671).

_f. Sealed and negotiable instruments._ Cases not following the "sealed contract" rule include [_Lake Shore Mgmt. Co. v. Blum, 235 N.E.2d 366, 368-369 (Ill.App.1968)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-6D30-003C-445N-00000-00&context=1530671) (seal no longer has independent legal validity as result of Ill. Rev. Stat. 1965, ch. 30, § 1566); _Efta v. Swanson, 132 N.W. 335 (Minn.1911)_ (distinction between sealed and unsealed contracts abrogated by statute in state); [_J.B. Streeter, Jr., Co. v. Janu, 96 N.W. 1128 (Minn.1903)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-5610-003G-S1C4-00000-00&context=1530671) (distinction discarded by statute). But see [_Donner v. Whitecotton, 212 S.W. 378 (Mo.App. 1919)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XCM-WSN0-00KR-F4XG-00000-00&context=1530671) (although statute abolished distinction between sealed and unsealed contracts, suit for breach of warranty in a deed may not be maintained against undisclosed principal because undisclosed principal does not appear on face of covenant as a party to it). Courts explicitly abolished the "sealed contract" rule in the absence of legislation abolishing any general distinction between sealed and unsealed contracts in [_Nalbandian v. Hanson Rest. & Lounge, Inc., 338 N.E.2d 335, 338 (Mass.1975)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-73D0-003C-T12H-00000-00&context=1530671) (to allow undisclosed principal to avoid liability because contract recites it is made under seal "is today illogical and in many cases produces a result which is inequitable and unjust") and [_Harris v. McKay, 122 S.E. 137 (Va.1924)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W1C0-003D-543M-00000-00&context=1530671) (reason for rule no longer exists).

Relatively recent cases following the rule are [_McMullen v. McMullen, 145 So. 2d 568, 569 (Fla.Dist.App. 1962)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-9CY0-003C-W037-00000-00&context=1530671) and [_Tooke v. Thom, 281 So. 2d 34 (Fla.Dist.App.1973)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-78K0-003C-W09H-00000-00&context=1530671) (contract executed under seal not enforceable against undisclosed principal in absence of allegations of fraud or other misrepresentations). Older authority includes _Hollingsworth v. Georgia Fruit Growers, Inc., 196 S.E. 766, 768 (Ga.1938)_ (undisclosed principal not liable on sealed note that did not name principal); [_Toll v. Pioneer Sample Book Co., 94 A.2d 764, 766 (Pa. 1953)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-VJR0-003C-M4XR-00000-00&context=1530671) (to ignore effect of seal "would in many instances negate the acts and intentions of the parties; and would require us to change or overrule many established principles and decisions with respect to sealed instruments which parties have relied on for a century"). See also [_Crowley v. Lewis, 146 N.E. 374, 374 (N.Y.1925)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-SSJ0-003F-634M-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[m\]any times the seal must have been used for the express purpose of relieving the undisclosed principal from personal liability"), but see N.Y. Gen. Constr. L. § 44-A (2003) (presence or absence of a seal on a written instrument executed after August 31, 1941, shall be without legal effect).

English authority retains deeds as formal documents and acknowledges "strict rules concerning deeds." Francis M.B. Reynolds, Bowstead & Reynolds on Agency 357-358 (17th ed. 2001). However, when an agent who enters into a deed in the agent's own name holds rights under the deed as trustee for the principal, the principal may enforce such rights in proceedings to which the agent is a plaintiff or a defendant. [_Id. at 357_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-73D0-003C-T12H-00000-00&context=1530671), citing Harmer v. Armstrong, \[1934\] Ch. 65.

The Canadian Supreme Court recently reaffirmed the "sealed contract" rule in Friedmann Equity Devs. Inc. v. Final Note Ltd., \[2000\] 188 D.L.R. (4th) 269 (hereinafter _"Final Note"_). One commentator on _Final Note_ observed that "\[i\]t will come as a surprise to many lawyers that there exists a common law rule known as the 'sealed contract rule'. Its existence is obscure and its rationale is even more so." Geoff R. Hall, Preserving the Clavicle in the Cat: Stunted Reform of Common Law Rules in the Supreme Court of Canada, 36 Can. Bus. L.J. 89, 89 (2001). The court's opinion in _Final Note_ acknowledges that "One might argue … that the practice of sealing documents, and hence the incidents thereof, are now out of step with the fabric of society . . . . Scarcely anyone in society today would agree that the affixation of a seal is evidence of the greater solemnity and force of a promise." 188 D.L.R. (4th) at 292. Change in a common-law rule would be warranted, in the court's view, "\[w\]hen the hardship which a rule causes becomes so acute and widespread that it outweighs any purpose that it may once have served. . . . there must be evidence of a change in commercial reality which makes such a change in the common law necessary." The court considered the contemporary utility of the "sealed contract" rule, noting that it "exists within a system of rules which relate to sealed instruments" and that to abolish the rule "would thus amount to a fundamental reform of the common law rather than an incremental change." [_Id. at 293-294_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-HBD0-003D-625D-00000-00&context=1530671). The function served by the rule, in the court's view, is to "provide a valid means for undisclosed principals to avoid personal liability...." [_Id. at 293_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-HBD0-003D-625D-00000-00&context=1530671). The court's analysis of the rule's function does not take into account the fact, noted elsewhere in the opinion, see [_id. at 279_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:401X-YG30-0039-44KF-00000-00&context=1530671), that an undisclosed principal may be excluded from a contract by explicitly so providing in the contract. See Anthony J. Duggan & Kent Roach, A Further Note on _Final Note:_ The Scope and Limits of Judicial Law Making, 36 Can. Bus. L.J. 115, 121 (2001). For a comprehensive discussion of _Final Note_ and contracts under seal, see G.H.L. Fridman, Some Words About Deeds, 81 Can. Bar Rev. 69 (2002).

In the United States, debate last touched on the sealed-contract doctrine in the late 1920s. In a series of lectures, Justice Cardozo used the then-contemporary debate over whether courts should abolish the doctrine as an example of the importance of stare decisis. See Benjamin N. Cardozo, Cardozo's Paradoxes of Legal Science (1928). Justice Cardozo argued that recent precedents applying the doctrine made departing from it "difficult if stare decisis was not to be abandoned altogether." Id. at 70. On that basis, the New York Court of Appeals reaffirmed the doctrine, despite the fact that "\[m\]uch of the law as to seals has small relation in society as now organized to present-day realities." Id. See [_Crowley v. Lewis, 146 N.E. 374 (N.Y.1925)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-SSJ0-003F-634M-00000-00&context=1530671). For further discussion of this component of Cardozo's analysis, see Jay W. Stein, The Hobgoblin Doctrine: Identifying "Foolish" Consistency in the Law, [_29 Tex. Tech. L. Rev. 1017, 1032-1033 (1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3TPC-V4X0-00CT-V1B9-00000-00&context=1530671). Cardozo's "Paradoxes" lectures overall emphasized "the importance, difficulty, and subtlety of the judicial job, which was to shape and reshape, as society changed over time, the common law principles that guided the daily lives of ordinary citizens." Andrew L. Kaufman, Cardozo 220 (1998).

On the substantive merits of the "sealed contract" rule, Justice Cardozo argued that, in addition to the implications for stare decisis, in Crowley v. Lewis "there were other and deeper grounds of policy. Men had taken title in the names of 'dummies,' and through them executed deeds and mortgages with the understanding, shared by the covenantees, that liability on the covenant would be confined to the apparent principal." Id. In addition to expectation-based reliance on a shared understanding, Cardozo argued that the "sealed contract" rule had a function, which was to provide "a device by which the liability of the apparent principal could be substituted without elaborate forms for the liability of another in back of him who was to reap the profits of the transaction." Id. Any retroactive change would thus be unjust. Id. at 70-71. Any change, if warranted, should be by statute. Id. at 71.

On statutes that abolish the effect of seals, see Eric Mills Holmes, Stature and Status of a Promise Under Seal as Legal Formality, 29 Willamette L. Rev. 617, 639 (1993). For an extensive analysis of statutory provisions that modify or abolish entirely the effect of seals, see Restatement Second, Contracts, Chapter 4, Topic 3, Statutory Note. Statutes may distinguish between corporate seals and those of individuals. Statutes that modify or abolish the effect of sealing include: Alaska Stat. § 09-25-130 (2002) ("\[P\]rivate seals and scrolls as a substitute for seals are abolished. They are not required to an instrument, but when used their effect remains unchanged"); Ariz. Rev. Stat. Ann. § 1-102 (2002 & Supp. 2004) (unless specifically required by law, addition of private or corporate seal not necessary to validity of instrument; neither addition nor omission of seal shall in any way affect instrument); Ark. Const. Sched. § 1 (2004) (abolishing distinction between sealed and unsealed instruments, except for provisions respecting statutes of limitations); Cal. Civil Code § 1629 (1985 & Supp. 2005) (distinction between sealed and unsealed contracts abolished); Idaho Code § 29-108 (2005) (all distinctions between sealed and unsealed contracts abolished); 815 Ill. Comp. Stat. 650/0.01 (1999 & Supp. 2005) (use of private seals abolished on written contracts, deeds, mortgages, or other written instruments or documents); Ind. Code § 34-37-1-1 (1999) (distinction between sealed and unsealed contracts abolished); Iowa Code § 16-106 (2005) (distinction between sealed and unsealed contracts abolished); Kan. Stat. Ann. § 16-106 (1995) (distinction between sealed and unsealed contracts abolished); Mich. Comp. Laws § 600.1401 (no bond, deed, conveyance, or other written contract, signed by any party, or party's agent or attorney, shall be invalid for want of a seal or scroll); Minn. Stat. § 358.01 (2004 & Supp. 2005) (private seals abolished); Miss. Code Ann. §§ 75-19-1 to 75-19-7 (1999 & Supp. 2004) (private seals abolished); Mo. Rev. Stat. § 431.010 (2003 & Supp. 2005) (addition of private seal to any instrument "shall not in any manner affect its force, validity, character, or in any way change the construction thereof"); Mont. Code Ann. § 1-4-204 to 1-4-206 (2003) (distinction abolished); Neb. Rev. Stat. § 76-212 (2003) (private seals abolished); N.M. Stat. § 47-1-6 (2003) (seal unnecessary); N.Y. Gen. Constr. Law § 44-A (2003) (except when statute provides otherwise, presence or absence of seal on written instrument without legal effect for instruments executed after Aug. 31, 1941); N.D. Cent. Code § 9-06-11 (1987 & Supp. 2003) (distinction abolished); Ohio Rev. Code Ann. § 5.11 (2002 & Supp. 2005) (seal abolished); Okla. Stat. Ann. tit. 15, § 139 (1993 & Supp. 2005) (distinction abolished); Or. Rev. Stat. § 42.115 (2003) (distinction abolished unless another statute provides otherwise); S.D. Codified Laws § 53-1-2(4) (2004 & Supp. 2005) (statute held to abolish distinction between sealed and unsealed instruments, except for statutes of limitations, in [_Heffleman v. Pennington County, 52 N.W. 851 (S.D.1892))_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YYM-65D0-00KR-C4CK-00000-00&context=1530671); Tenn. Code Ann. § 47-50-101 (2001 & Supp. 2005) (private and corporate seals abolished); Tex. Rev. Civ. Stat. Ann. art. 27 (1969 & Supp. 2004-2005) (private seals abolished); Utah Code Ann. § 104-48-4 (1933), repealed 1951 (whether seal has been legislatively abolished in Utah questioned in Comment, The Status of the Common-Law Seal Doctrine in Utah, 3 Utah L. Rev. 73 (1953)); Wash. Rev. Code § 64.04.100 (2005) (private seals abolished); Wyo. Stat. § 34-2-125 and 34-3-126 (2004 & Supp. 2005) (distinction abolished).

A substantial minority of states have statutes eliminating the need for seals on conveyances of property. See Ala. Code § 35-4-21 (1996 & Supp. 2004); Colo. Rev. Stat. § 38-10-125 (2000 & Supp. 2004); 765 Ill. Comp. Stat. 1070/1 (1999 & Supp. 2005); Ind. Code § 32-2-5-1 (2002 & Supp. 2004); Me. Rev. Stat. Ann. tit. 33, § 352 (1999 & Supp. 2004); Md. Code Ann., Real Prop. § 4-101 (2003 & Supp. 2004); Mich. Comp. Laws § 600.1401 (2001 & Supp. 2005); Mo. Rev. Stat. § 431.010 (1992 & Supp. 2005); Neb. Rev. Stat. § 52.315 (2004); N.H. Rev. Stat. Ann. § 477:1 (2003 & Supp. 2004); N.M. Stat. § 47-1-6 (2003); N.Y. Gen. Constr. Law § 44-A (2003); N.C. Gen. Stat. § 39-6.5 (2001 & Supp. 2004) (but inapplicable to requirement for affixing a seal of the officer taking an acknowledgment of the instrument); Or. Rev. Stat. § 93.101 (2003); 21 Pa. Stat. Ann. § 10 (2005); R.I. Gen. Laws § 34-11-2 (2004); Tex. Rev. Civ. Stat. Ann. art. 27 (2001 & Supp. 2004); W. Va. Code § 36-3-1 (2005); Wash. Rev. Code §§ 64.04.090 and 64.04.105 (2005); and Wyo. Stat. § 34-2-125 (2004 & Supp. 2005).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 6.04_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8W0-00YG-4004-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 6- Contracts and Other Transactions with Third Parties_ > _Topic 1- Parties to Contracts_**

**§ 6.04 Principal Does Not Exist or Lacks Capacity**

**Unless the third party agrees otherwise, a person who makes a contract with a third party purportedly as an agent on behalf of a principal becomes a party to the contract if the purported agent knows or has reason to know that the purported principal does not exist or lacks capacity to be a party to a contract.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section states the rule that, unless the third party agrees otherwise, a person who purports to act as an agent becomes a party to a contract made on behalf of the purported agent's purported principal when the purported agent knows or has reason to know that the purported principal does not exist at the time of the contract or lacks capacity to be a party to a contract.

On capacity to act as a principal, see § 3.04. On the effect of death, cessation of existence, and suspension of powers, see § 3.07. On loss of capacity, see § 3.08.

_b. Parties to contract when principal does not exist or lacks capacity._ The rule stated in this section is a consequence of the basic principle that to be a party to a contract requires capacity to incur at least voidable contractual duties. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 12(1). To have such capacity requires existence, as discussed further in Comment _c._ Additionally, an individual person who exists lacks such capacity when the person has been adjudicated to lack capacity or when the person is subject to mental or physical disability to a degree that prevents a manifestation of assent to the transaction. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 12, Comment _a._ A person who acts as agent on behalf of a disclosed principal does not become a party to a contract made with a third party unless the agent and the third party so agree. See § 6.01(2). An agent does not become a party to a contract made on behalf of a disclosed principal who has power to avoid contracts made on the principal's behalf, such as a minor.

However, if a person who purports to act as an agent knows or has reason to know that the person purportedly represented does not exist or lacks capacity to be a party to even a voidable contract, the person purporting to act as agent will become a party to the contract. This will occur if the purported agent and the third party manifest assent to make a contract that binds the third party. However, the purported agent and the third party may manifest agreement that the purported agent shall not become a party to a contract. For example, they may structure their agreement as an offer made by the third party that may be accepted only by the person whom the agent purports to represent if and when that person comes into existence. To constitute an option contract, the third party's offer must consist of a promise that meets the requirements for formation of a contract and that limits the third party's power to revoke the offer. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 25.

If a person purporting to act as an agent does not know or have reason to know that the purported principal does not exist or lacks capacity, either the purported agent or the third party may be able to avoid the contract by showing that both were mistaken about a basic assumption on which the contract was made that has a material effect on the exchange of performances. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 153.

It is important whether an agent has notice that an individual principal has died or has lost capacity to become a party to contracts. Under § 3.07(2), a principal's death is effective to terminate an agent's actual authority to act on behalf of the principal only when the agent has notice of the death. Under § 3.08(1), a principal's loss of capacity is effective to terminate an agent's actual authority to act on behalf of the principal only when the agent has notice that the principal's loss of capacity is permanent or that the principal has been adjudicated to lack capacity. Under § 3.08(2), a principal's loss of capacity does not have this effect when a written instrument makes an agent's actual authority effective upon a principal's loss of capacity or confers it irrevocably regardless of such loss.

**Illustration:**

1\. P gives A a power of attorney that authorizes A to sell P's computer. P dies. Knowing that P has died, A sells the computer to T, expressly warranting its reliability. The computer proves unreliable. A is subject to liability to T for breach of warranty. A knew of P's death when A contracted to sell P's computer to T.

_c. Contracts made on behalf of entities yet to be formed._ The rule stated in this section is applicable when a person purports to make a contract with a third party on behalf of an entity that does not exist. If that person and the third party manifest assent that the contract shall bind the third party, the person who purports to act on behalf of the entity is personally liable on the contract.

**Illustration:**

2\. A is the president and sole shareholder of "Marketing Designs, Inc." Wishing to do business as a wholesaler of seafood, A makes a contract to purchase salmon from T, a fish importer. A expressly makes the contract on behalf of "Boston International Seafood Exchange, Inc.," believing that the name more closely identifies A with the seafood industry. A knows that Boston International Seafood Exchange, Inc., does not exist. Payment for the salmon is not made as required by the contract. A is subject to liability on the contract with T. As A knew, the corporation on whose behalf A purportedly made the contract did not exist.

The classic instance of this situation arises when a person enters into a contract purportedly on behalf of an entity that has not yet been formed, such as a business or a not-for-profit corporation or a limited-liability company. Promoters of entities yet-to-be-formed may find it attractive and even imperative to obtain binding commitments from third parties before the formal process of organization has been completed. The statute applicable to forming the particular type of entity may address the circumstances under which a promoter will be subject to individual liability on a contract made on behalf of a not-yet-formed entity. When an entity comes into existence, it may adopt the contract made by the promoter. See § 4.04, Comment c. An entity's adoption of a contract made by a promoter does not by itself release the promoter from any individual liability that the promoter may have on the contract. See id.

If a promoter enters into a contract with a third party on behalf of an entity that has not yet commenced legal existence, the entity itself cannot be a party to the contract prior to its existence. This is so whether the contract purports to be made on behalf of an entity characterized as one yet to be formed or characterizes the entity as one presently in existence. If the contract is to bind the third party prior to the entity's existence, unless the relationship is structured as an option that may be exercised by the entity when formed, either the promoter or someone else must be liable on the contract. The parties' intention must often be determined on the basis of inferences to be drawn from the circumstances, which include whether the promoter and the other party are aware that the entity does not yet exist. A third party may agree in a contract made by a promoter to release the promoter's liability when and if the entity adopts the contract. In the absence of an express agreement by the third party to release the promoter, some cases permit the promoter to establish that the parties intended that the promoter would not be personally liable. An entity may adopt a contract made by a promoter by accepting its benefits with knowledge of its terms. For further discussion, see § 4.04, Comment c.

Similar questions arise when a person purports to take action on behalf of an entity when its powers have been suspended or forfeited, or when the entity has been dissolved. The organizational statute applicable to the entity may specify the circumstances under which such action will result in individual liability to third parties.

**Illustration:**

3\. Same facts as Illustration 2, except that Marketing Designs, Inc., has been dissolved by the time A makes a contract with T expressly on its behalf. A knows of the dissolution. Under applicable organizational law, the effect of the dissolution of Marketing Designs, Inc., is to terminate its existence. Unless applicable organizational law provides otherwise, A is subject to liability on the contract.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ This section is the counterpart to Restatement Second, Agency § 326. Section 326 states that "\[u\]nless otherwise agreed, a person who, in dealing with another, purports to act as agent for a principal whom both know to be nonexistent or wholly incompetent, becomes a party to such a contract."

_b. Parties to contract when principal does not exist or lacks capacity._ For the point made by Illustration 1, see _Mickam v. Joseph Louis Palace Trust, 849 F.Supp. 516, 525-526 (E.D.Mich.1993)_. In _Mickam,_ the agents were appointed trustees of a trust that was subsequently determined to be invalid. They sold property via a warranty deed following the death of the trust's grantor. In the court's analysis, "because the trust was invalid, \[the agents\] could not have been acting as cotrustees at the time they entered into the land contract and warranty deed. They entered into these contracts as ordinary agents of an undisclosed principal. As such, they rendered themselves personally liable on the contracts." Id.

_c. Contracts made on behalf of entities yet to be formed._ Illustrations 2 and 3 are based on [_Atlantic Salmon A/S v. Curran, 591 N.E.2d 206 (Mass. App.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-BJ80-003C-V0C1-00000-00&context=1530671).

On action taken on behalf of an entity that has been dissolved, see [_Bullington v. Palangio, 45 S.W.3d 834, 838 (Ark.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:43DD-2N10-0039-4428-00000-00&context=1530671) (shareholder who undertook performance of construction contract following dissolution of corporation is subject to individual liability for breach of express and implied warranties); [_Sims v. Ottenhoff, 879 P.2d 436, 437 (Colo.App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2J60-003D-93WX-00000-00&context=1530671) (liability not imposed on persons who lack any knowledge of dissolution; directors of dissolved not-for-profit corporation not personally liable for negligence of employee, despite corporation's dissolved status); [_Powell v. Jaysons Constr. & Interiors, 713 N.Y.S.2d 802, 805 (Dist.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:41FN-B780-0039-416B-00000-00&context=1530671) (individual who signed contract on behalf of corporation that had been dissolved is proper party defendant in action for breach of contract). On action taken when an entity's powers have been forfeited, see [_Cain v. State, 882 S.W.2d 515, 519 (Tex.App. 1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-0SG0-003C-20X1-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporate officer personally liable for debt incurred to plug oil wells; debt came into existence after forfeiture of corporation's powers due to its failure to file franchise-tax return, when state regulatory agency ordered plugging and officer was responsible for seeing that corporation complied with its duty to plug wells). For an example of statutory treatment of the consequences of a corporation's reinstatement, see Del. Code Ann., tit. 8, § 312(e) (upon renewal and revival, corporation "shall be as exclusively liable for all contracts, acts, matters and things made, done or performed in its name and on its behalf by its officers and agents prior to its reinstatement, as if its certificate of incorporation had at all times remained in full force and effect").
