[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 6.05_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8W0-00YG-4005-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 6- Contracts and Other Transactions with Third Parties_ > _Topic 2- Rights, Liabilities, and Defenses_ > _Title A- General_**

**§ 6.05 Contract That Is Unauthorized in Part or That Combines Orders of Several Principals**

**(1) If an agent makes a contract with a third party that differs from the contract that the agent had actual or apparent authority to make only in an amount or by the inclusion or exclusion of a separable part, the principal is subject to liability to the third party to the extent of the contract that the agent had actual or apparent authority to make if**

**(a) the third party seasonably makes a manifestation to the principal of willingness to be bound; and**

**(b) the principal has not changed position in reasonable reliance on the belief that no contract bound the principal and the third party.**

**(2) Two or more principals may authorize the same agent to make separate contracts for them. If the agent makes a single contract with a third party on the principals' behalves that combines the principals' separate orders or interests and calls for a single performance by the third party,**

**(a) if the agent purports to make the combined contract on behalf of disclosed principals, the agent is subject to liability to the third party for breach of the agent's warranty of authority as stated in § 6.10, unless the separate principals are bound by the combined contract;**

**(b) if the principals are unidentified or undisclosed, the third party and the agent are the only parties to the combined contract; and**

**(c) unless the agent acted with actual or apparent authority to bind each of the principals to the combined contract,**

**(i) subject to (1), none of the separate principals is subject to liability on the combined contract; and**

**(ii) the third party is not subject to liability on the combined contract to any of the separate principals.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ The rules stated in this section apply to two separate but potentially related situations. Subsection (1) covers situations in which an agent makes a contract with a third party that diverges from the contract that the agent had actual or apparent authority to make, but only in an amount or by including or excluding a separable part. Subsection (2) covers situations in which an agent represents multiple principals who have authorized the agent to make separate contracts on their behalf. Instead, the agent makes a single contract with a third party in an amount that combines the principals' separate orders or property.

Multiple principals who appoint the same agent to act for them in the same matter or transaction are covered by § 3.16.

_b. Contracts unauthorized in part._ Ordinarily, a principal is not a party to a contract when the agent who enters into the contract on the principal's behalf acts without actual or apparent authority. A principal may nonetheless be subject to liability on a contract when an agent enters into a contract that differs from the contract the agent had actual or apparent authority to make only in an amount or by the inclusion or exclusion of a severable part. In that event, if the third party seasonably manifests that it is willing to be bound and if the principal has not changed position in reasonable reliance on the absence of a binding contract, the principal is subject to liability to the extent of the contract that the agent had actual or apparent authority to make. On circumstances that constitute change of position, see Restatement Third, Restitution and Unjust Enrichment §    .

In effect, the third party has an option to bind the principal that is limited to the extent of the principal's prior manifestations of assent to the agent or to third parties. Although the agent exceeded the bounds of actual and apparent authority, it is fair to subject the principal to liability consistently with the principal's prior manifestations of assent to be bound, so long as the principal has not changed position. This option for the third party is comparable to the option-like power that ratification creates in a principal when an agent has acted without actual or apparent authority. See § 4.01, Comment _b._

**Illustrations:**

1\. P, a restaurateur, engages A to purchase wine from vineyards for P's restaurant, telling A that P wishes A to purchase 100 cases of wine for P. A enters into a contract with T, who owns a vineyard, for 200 cases of wine. A had neither actual nor apparent authority to make the contract and P does not ratify A's conduct. Acting seasonably and before P has changed position based on the belief that P is not bound to T, T informs P that T is willing to sell 100 cases of wine to P. P is subject to liability to T to the extent of 100 cases of wine.

2\. P retains A to sell Blackacre for P, authorizing A to sell Blackacre only without warranties. T, who knows of the limits on A's authority, agrees to buy Blackacre. The sales agreement, executed by A on P's behalf, provides that Blackacre will be sold by a deed with warranties. T seeks specific performance, offering to pay the contract price and to take a deed without warranties. T is entitled to specific performance if T seeks it seasonably and before P has changed position in reliance on the assumption that the contract made by A does not bind P.

The third party has the same option when the agent who added the unauthorized terms, or who otherwise acted inconsistently with the extent of the agent's actual or apparent authority, also intended to defraud the principal. If the third party to the contract has not colluded with the agent and does not otherwise have notice of the agent's fraudulent intention, the third party's position should be no worse as a consequence of the agent's intention. Similarly, under U.C.C. § 3-407(c) an instrument that has been fraudulently altered may be enforced according to its original terms by a payor bank or drawee paying the instrument, or by a person who takes the instrument for value, in good faith, and without notice of the alteration.

_c. Contract that combines orders or interests of several principals._ Several principals may engage the same agent to represent their interests through separate transactions. For example, principals who each own a comparable type of property may engage the same agent to sell or lease their separate property. Principals who wish to buy or sell the same type of goods may engage the same agent to deal in such goods on their separate accounts. Principals who use a common agent may authorize the agent to make a single contract with a third party that combines their individual orders or property. Unless a contrary intention is manifested, the combined contract represents a promise by each principal for the same entire performance. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 288(2). Each principal is bound for the whole performance due under the contract. See id. § 289(1). Other bodies of common-law rules and statutes determine whether the principals have only joint or joint and several duties. Id. § 289(2) and (3).

Unless an agent acts with actual or apparent authority to bind multiple principals to a single combined contract, if the agent combines or "lumps" the principals' orders or interests into a single contract with a third party, none of the principals is subject to liability on the contract. This is because none among the principals has manifested assent to the agent or the third party to be bound for the lumped or combined performance. The third party is not subject to liability to any single principal on the contract that lumps the principals' orders or interests. Moreover, the third party is not subject to liability to any one or more of the single principals for that principal's proportionate share of the performance due from the third party under the contract. The third party's manifestation of assent to be bound by the contract reflected a bargain for a given quantity or number. The third party may set the price at which it is willing or able to perform with reference to that quantity or number. However, under subsection (1), the third party may manifest its willingness to be bound to perform by, for example, tendering performance to a single principal of that principal's proportionate share of the lumped contract. See Comment _b_ for further discussion.

**Illustrations:**

3\. P and S are restaurateurs, each owning a separate restaurant. P and S separately engage A to purchase wine from vineyards for their restaurants. P tells A that P wishes A to purchase 500 cases of wine for P. S tells A that S wishes A to purchase 500 cases of wine for S. A enters into one contract with T, who owns a vineyard, for 1000 cases of wine. A does not tell T that A acts as an agent. A and T are the only parties to the contract. Neither P nor S is subject to liability on the contract. T is not subject to liability on the contract to P or S.

4\. Same facts as Illustration 3, except that T learns P's identity and arranges a mutually convenient date and time for T to deliver 500 cases of wine to P. Unless P has changed position following the contract made by A for 1000 cases of wine, P is subject to liability to T for 500 cases of wine.

If an agent acts without actual or apparent authority in making a contract that combines the separate orders of multiple principals, the principals may become parties to the contract by ratifying the agent's action.

**Illustration:**

5\. Same facts as Illustration 3, except that T refuses to deliver the 1000 cases of wine to A because the market price has risen by the time required for delivery under the contract. If P and S ratify A's action in making the combined contract, they may enforce the contract against T, as may A.

When principals who share a common agent are unidentified or undisclosed as in Illustration 3, the agent becomes a party to the entire contract that lumps the principals' separate orders or interests.

The agent holds the single contract for the benefit of the agent's multiple principals. The multiple principals are not parties to the single contract unless the agent acted with actual or apparent authority in making it on the multiple principals' behalf, or unless the multiple principals join forces and ratify the agent's action, as in Illustration 5.

**Illustration:**

6\. P is one of 35 owners of piggyback semitrailers suitable for use in intermodal transportation. All 35 owners retain A as their agent to lease their semitrailers to railroads. A enters into a single lease with T, a railroad, covering the semitrailers owned by all 35 owners. The lease provides that T will make payments for the use of each semitrailer to A. A's agreement with each owner requires A to remit each owner's proportionate share of T's payments to the owner after deducting A's commission. A stops making remittances to the owners, including P. P sues T to recover P's proportionate share of the payments that T is obliged by the lease to make to A. Regardless of whether P is a disclosed, unidentified, or undisclosed principal, T is not subject to liability to P. The terms of the lease T made with P do not require T to make proportionate payments to individual owners of semitrailers, such as P.

To permit an individual principal to sever an entire contract, as P attempts to do in Illustration 6, is inconsistent with the presumed bargained-for expectations of a third party like T in Illustration 6. As the lessee of the semitrailers, T bargained for lease terms that require T to make lump-sum payments to one recipient, the agent who acted on behalf of the semitrailers' owners. This simplifies performance for the lessee and reduces its costs of performance. Additionally, the semitrailers' owners may benefit from lease terms that, by reducing the lessee's cost of performance, increase the overall amount the lessee is willing to pay under the lease.

This doctrine does not leave individual principals without remedy in situations like Illustration 6. In addition to joining forces with the other principals, a single principal may seek to obtain control over the agent, such as by seeking an injunction requiring T to make subsequent payments to a court-appointed receiver. If the agent has unpaid obligations to a particular principal, the principal may enforce them against the agent.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ The rule stated in subsection (1) consolidates the treatment in Restatement Second, Agency §§ 164 and 198. Section 164 states that:

(1) Except as stated in subsection (2), an agent for a disclosed or partially disclosed principal who exceeds his powers in making an unauthorized contract with a third person does not bind the principal either by the contract as made or by the contract as it would have been made had he acted in accordance with his authority.

(2) Where the only difference between the contract as authorized and the contract as made is a difference as to amount, or the inclusion or exclusion of a separable part, the principal is liable upon the contract as it was authorized to be made, provided that the other party seasonably manifests his willingness to accept the contract as it was authorized.

Section 198 applies the same principle to contracts made by agents on behalf of undisclosed principals. Section 164 states a caveat that "\[t\]he rule stated … does not include the situation in which the agent in making the unauthorized terms also intends to act in fraud of the principal, as to which no statement is made." Section 198 contains a caveat to the same effect. The rule in subsection (2) consolidates the treatment in Restatement Second, Agency §§ 148, 187, 294, and 305. For example, § 148 states that "\[u\]nless otherwise agreed between the principal \[sic\] and the agent, no one of two or more disclosed or partially disclosed principals, each of whom independently authorizes the same agent to make a contract, is liable upon a single contract … which combines the orders of the principals and calls for a single performance."

The language in subsection (2)(c)(i) that subjects a principal or principals to liability to the third party if the third party makes a seasonable manifestation of willingness to be bound as stated in subsection (1) is not contained in Restatement Second, Agency. The possibility of so providing was discussed on the floor of the 1956 Annual Meeting. It appears that a majority of those who voted on the question supported the language as stated in subsection (2)(c)(i). See 33 A.L.I. Proc. 348-353 (1956) (vote by 30 or 31 in favor of, and 13 against, motion to make § 148 subject to § 164). Comment _c_ to § 164 declares that the rule stated therein is "not in conflict with that stated in Section 148 dealing with multiple principals. The contract which results when an agent 'lumps' the orders of several principals who independently authorize him, is entirely different from the separate ones by which alone he was authorized to bind them."

_b. Contracts unauthorized in part._ Illustration 2 is based on Illustration 1 to Restatement Second, Agency § 164. Sections 164 and 198 of Restatement Second, Agency, represented a change in position from §§ 164 and 198 of the first Restatement, which did not permit a third party to hold a principal to the extent of the principal's manifested assent. According to Comment _a_ to Restatement Second, Agency § 164, "\[t\]his was done in order to observe strictly the rules of contract without reference to the agency situation, although there were a few cases which reached the result now stated. It is believed that the earlier statement is too restrictive. In agency situations, formal contract rules have been departed from in several instances, notably in making the undisclosed principal a party to a contract made by an agent." The Reporter's Notes for Restatement Second, Agency § 164 observe that the cases are "not conclusive" but also note that U.C.C. § 3-407(3) permits a holder in due course "to enforce an improperly changed instrument according to its original tenor." This remains so under Revised U.C.C. Article 3; § 3-407(c) provides that "\[a\] payor bank or drawee paying a fraudulently altered instrument or a person taking it for value, in good faith and without notice of the alteration, may enforce rights with respect to the instrument (i) according to its original terms, or (ii) in the case of an incomplete instrument altered by unauthorized completion, according to its terms as completed."

For the proposition that an agent's intention to defraud a principal should not defeat the third party's right to hold the principal to the extent of the principal's manifested intention to be bound, see [_DeBoer Constr., Inc. v. Reliance Ins. Co., 540 F.2d 486, 491 (10th Cir.1976)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-1N60-0039-M2V1-00000-00&context=1530671) (principal liable when agent's fraud is within apparent scope of agent's authority; principal who appointed bonding agent not subject to liability on bond issued by agent in amount that exceeded stated amount in power of attorney held by agent).

_c. Contract that combines orders or interests of several principals._ Illustration 6 is based on [_Brunswick Leasing Corp. v. Wisconsin Cent., Ltd., 136 F.3d 521 (7th Cir.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S0V-3C60-0038-X2N7-00000-00&context=1530671). _Brunswick Leasing_ appears to be the sole case to apply these principles that is of sufficiently recent vintage to apply Restatement Second, Agency § 187. See [_136 F.3d at 527_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S0V-3C60-0038-X2N7-00000-00&context=1530671) (characterizing question of whether Illinois or any state would follow § 187 as one of first impression). Indeed, _Brunswick Leasing_ appears to be the sole case to apply any of the relevant sections in Restatement Second that correspond to subsection (2). Although the parties and the court in _Brunswick Leasing_ characterized the semitrailer owners as "undisclosed principals," it is possible that they were unidentified principals because the railroad that leased the semitrailers may have had notice that the lessor represented the interests of owners of semitrailers other than or in addition to itself.

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 6.06_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8W0-00YG-4006-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 6- Contracts and Other Transactions with Third Parties_ > _Topic 2- Rights, Liabilities, and Defenses_ > _Title A- General_**

**§ 6.06 Setoff**

**(1) When an agent makes a contract on behalf of a disclosed or unidentified principal, unless the principal and the third party agree otherwise,**

**(a) the third party may not set off any amount that the agent independently owes the third party against an amount the third party owes the principal under the contract; and**

**(b) the principal may not set off any amount that the third party independently owes the agent against an amount the principal owes the third party under the contract.**

**(2) When an agent makes a contract on behalf of an undisclosed principal,**

**(a) the third party may set off**

**(i) any amount that the agent independently owed the third party at the time the agent made the contract and**

**(ii) any amount that the agent thereafter independently comes to owe the third party until the third party has notice that the agent acts on behalf of a principal against an amount the third party owes the principal under the contract;**

**(b) after the third party has notice that the agent acts on behalf of a principal, the third party may not set off any amount that the agent thereafter independently comes to owe the third party against an amount the third party owes the principal under the contract unless the principal consents; and**

**(c) the principal may not set off any amount that the third party independently owes the agent against an amount that the principal owes the third party under the contract, unless the principal and the third party agree otherwise.**

**(3) Unless otherwise agreed, an agent who is a party to a contract may not set off any amount that the principal independently owes the agent against an amount that the agent owes the third party under the contract. However, with the principal's consent, the agent may set off any amount that the principal could set off against an amount that the principal owes the third party under the contract.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section concerns the circumstances in which an agent, a principal, or a third party may offset an amount independently owed to it against an amount owed by it pursuant to a contract made by the agent with the third party on behalf of the principal. Other bodies of law address more fundamental questions concerning setoff, including how a right to setoff may be asserted and whether a particular amount is of a type that may be set off against an amount due under a contract. This Restatement covers neither the relationship between common-law rights to setoff and bankruptcy law nor the application of rules governing setoff in litigation.

_b. Setoff--in general._ Setoff is a general legal principle and practice with origins in Roman law. Setoff enables an amount that one party owes another to be matched with an offsetting amount owed, thereby extinguishing or reducing it. Cross-demands for payment are thereby recognized and "netted out" to avoid circular payments and, in litigation contexts, multiple suits. Parties often arrange for setoff without judicial involvement. It is not unusual for merchants to set off account debts generated by cross dealings or for banks to use a customer's deposit account--which represents a debt the bank owes the customer--to set off against an overdue loan the customer owes the bank. General rules on setoff are prescribed by statutes in many states and by cases. Parties may expand by agreement the types of situations in which setoff may occur. Setoff differs from recoupment, in which the offsetting amounts must arise from the same transaction.

_c. Setoff rights and agency relationships._ Setoff raises special questions within agency because the party who wishes to exercise setoff may wish to set off a right to payment owed by a party other than the party to whom the first party owes payment. If, for example, the third party to a contract made by an agent owes a payment to the principal, the third party may demand setoff of rights to payment that the third party has against the agent.

Basic doctrines of common-law agency rationalize the specific rules stated in this section. An agent who enters into a contract on behalf of a principal acts as the principal's fiduciary, whether the principal is disclosed, unidentified, or undisclosed. If the third party with whom the agent deals has notice that the agent acts on behalf of a principal, the third party is on notice that, in matters pursuant to the agency relationship, the agent's duty is to act only for the principal's benefit unless the principal otherwise consents. In particular, the agent may not use the principal's money, property, or other assets for the agent's separate benefit without the principal's consent. See § 8.05. If a third party sets off an amount that the agent owes the third party against an amount that the third party owes the principal, the agent benefits because the setoff reduces or even extinguishes the agent's debt. The benefit to the agent results in a corresponding detriment to the principal because it reduces the amount that the third party owes to the principal.

When a third party has notice that the person with whom it deals acts as an agent, whether for a disclosed or unidentified principal, the third party is on notice that the principal's consent will be required to reduce the agent's debt to the third party through setoff. The principal's consent may be manifested in a specific agreement with the third party that permits the third party to set off debts owed by the agent or by custom in a particular industry. Additionally, a principal may consent after the fact of setoff through ratification. See § 4.01, which defines "ratification."

In contrast, when a third party does not have notice that it deals with an agent, the third party is not on notice that the person with whom it deals acts as a fiduciary on behalf of a principal. The third party thus would expect to have available all defenses otherwise available against the person with whom it deals, if sued on the contract, and would expect to be able to set off amounts owed it by the person with whom it deals, when general rules applicable to setoff make it available. The fact that an agent acts on behalf of an undisclosed principal should not disappoint these expectations, which follow from the third party's manifestation of assent to contract with a particular person, the agent. However, once a third party has notice that it has made a contract with an agent, the third party is on notice that the agent acts on behalf of a principal. Under subsection (2), once the third party has notice that the agent acts on behalf of a principal, the third party may set off, against amounts the third party owes the principal, outstanding amounts the agent owed the third party at the time of making the contract and amounts that the agent independently came to owe the third party after making the contract up until the time the third party had notice that the agent acts on behalf of a principal. Without the principal's consent, the third party may not set off amounts that the agent came to owe the third party after the third party had notice that the agent acts on behalf of a principal.

A third party's rights to setoff when the third party had no notice that it dealt with an agent do not vary under subsection (2)(a) depending on whether the agent acted with actual authority in not revealing the principal's existence or identity. In contrast, under Restatement Second, Agency § 306(2), a third party does not have setoff rights if an agent acted without actual authority in not disclosing the principal's existence or identity unless the principal entrusted the agent with "possession of chattels which he disposes of as directed or unless the principal has otherwise misled the third person into extending credit to the agent."

A third party's right to set off amounts owed it by an agent should not turn on whether it is established that the agent had actual authority to deal without revealing the principal's existence. Whether an agent acted with actual authority depends on interactions between principal and agent that a third party is not in a position to observe. Regardless of instructions the principal has given the agent, a principal may be tempted to claim that the agent acted without actual authority in not revealing the principal's identity if by so doing the principal may defeat the third party's right to set off amounts that the agent owes the third party. See also § 6.01, Comment c, which discusses similar questions that arise when a principal claims that an agent's disclosure of the principal's existence or identity defeated the principal's objectives. Moreover, rights that the principal has against the third party stem from a contract made by the agent. The principal's rights should be limited by reasonable expectations formed by the third party in dealing with the agent, including rights to set off amounts that the agent owes the third party. See also § 6.07(3)(a), governing the effect of payments that a third party makes to an agent who has made a contract on behalf of an undisclosed principal.

If the third party learns only that an agent represents a principal but not the principal's identity, the third party is still on notice that the person with whom it deals acts as a fiduciary. Thus, although an agent for an unidentified principal becomes a party to a contract made on behalf of a principal unless the agent and the third party agree otherwise, see § 6.02(2), for purposes of setoff an agent for an unidentified principal is treated identically to an agent for a disclosed principal.

A principal may not set off amounts that the third party owes the agent against amounts that the principal owes the third party on the contract, unless the third party so agrees. This limit reflects the basic point that the principal's defenses to enforcement of the contract are, with some exceptions, the defenses available to the principal had the principal made the contract directly. This is so whether the principal is disclosed, unidentified, or undisclosed. Similarly, the capacity to do a legally consequential act by means of an agent is coextensive with the principal's capacity to do the act in person. See § 3.04, Comment _b._ If a third party agrees to setoff by the principal of claims owed to the agent by the third party, the agent may have claims against the principal unless the agent has also agreed. For the principal's duties to the agent, see §§ 8.13-8.15. The agent may continue, as well, to have a claim against the third party, unless the agent agrees to setoff by the principal.

**Illustrations:**

1\. P, who owns a farm, engages A to sell cattle owned by P and tells A to contract in A's own name. A sells the cattle to T for \$ 10,000. At the time of the sale, A also independently owes \$ 5000 to T. P demands payment from T. T pays P \$ 5000 for the cattle, having deducted the amount A owes to T. At the time A sold the cattle to T, T had no notice that A acted as an agent. Under applicable law, if A sued T on the sales contract for the cattle, T could set off the \$ 5000 that A owes T. T is not subject to liability to P for \$ 5000. T was entitled to set off the amount that A owed T.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that T had notice at the time of the sale that A acted as an agent. Unless P agrees, T may not set off the amount that A owes T. Having already paid P \$ 5000, T is subject to liability to P for an additional \$ 5000.

Once a third party has notice that it has made a contract with an agent who acts on behalf of a principal, the third party may not set off amounts resulting from debt that the agent subsequently incurs against amounts that the third party owes the principal on the contract without the consent of the principal.

**Illustration:**

3\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that A owes nothing to T at the time of the sale. After T has notice that A acted as an agent, A incurs a debt of \$ 5000 to T. Unless P agrees, T may not set off this amount against the amount that T owes P.

Many different circumstances may give notice that a person acts as an agent. See § 6.01, Comment c; § 6.02, Comment c; and § 6.03, Comment c. References to a principal in writings that accompany the parties' dealings may afford such notice, as may the nature of a transaction.

**Illustration:**

4\. P, a wholesale distributor of automotive parts and accessories, overpays A, from whom P has purchased products. To resolve A's indebtedness to P, P and A agree that A will sell products for P on commission with part of the commission used to reduce A's debt to P. Separately, A is indebted to T for \$ 28,000. Without P's consent, A agrees to ship 720 cases of antifreeze to T in exchange for a release of A's indebtedness to T. T knows that A does not manufacture antifreeze or maintain an inventory of it. A contacts P's general manager and directs that 720 cases of antifreeze be shipped to T. P ships the antifreeze to T. T acknowledges delivery by scrutinizing and signing a memorandum that lists P as the shipper of the antifreeze. T credits A's account for the antifreeze shipment and refuses to pay P for the shipment. The memorandum, signed by T, gave T reason to know that A acted as an agent in selling the antifreeze. A acted without P's consent in withholding from T knowledge of P's existence. T owes payment to P for the price of the 720 cases of antifreeze.

Additionally, a person's own circumstances, if known, may provide notice to others that the person acts as an agent. If a third party deals with a person, knowing that the person is insolvent or otherwise financially incapable of performance under the contract, the third party may have reason to know that the person acts as an agent. This is because such circumstances may provide notice to a third party that it will not be feasible for the person with whom it deals to perform the duties of performance created by their contract. In contrast, if the person with whom the third party deals has an appearance of commercial and financial substance, the third party may have less reason to question whether the person acts only as an agent on behalf of another.

_d. Principal's agreement to setoff by third party of debt owed by agent._ When an agent makes a contract on behalf of a disclosed or unidentified principal, the third party may not set off amounts that the agent owes the third party against amounts the third party owes the principal on the contract unless the principal agrees. It has been said that a third party acts at its peril if it knowingly receives money or other assets that belong to a principal in payment of an agent's debt to the third party. A principal may agree otherwise or may ratify an agent's use of the principal's property to satisfy the agent's debts.

Similarly, a principal may agree that a third party may set off, against amounts the third party owes the principal, amounts that the agent owes the third party. Custom in a particular industry, if known to a principal, may be a basis for establishing the principal's assent to setoff. Absent prior consent to setoff, a principal may assent after the fact to the benefit that the setoff confers on the agent. This may constitute ratification by the principal, either of the agent's receipt of benefit through the setoff or of the agent's unauthorized agreement with the third party to permit setoff. See § 4.01. If effective as a ratification, the principal's assent releases the third party from liability to the principal for the amount of the setoff. However, a principal is not bound by a ratification made without knowledge of material facts. See § 4.06.

**Illustration:**

5\. P engages A, a horse trader, to sell several thoroughbred yearlings owned by P at an annual thoroughbred auction conducted by T. T accepts the five yearlings for sale, registers them in P's name, and sells them for \$ 125,900 net of T's charges. As of the time of the sale, A separately owed T \$ 117,300. T sets off the amounts that A owes T against the amount due from T to P and remits the difference to A to hold for P's account. Neither A nor T tells P about the setoff. Reasonably believing that A holds \$ 125,900 on P's account, P then agrees to make a loan to A in the same amount. P's agreement to lend to A the amount that T owes P does not constitute a ratification of T's setoff of A's debt to T. When P agreed to make the loan to A, P was unaware of the material fact that the amount A received from T reflected T's setoff of A's debt to T. T owes P the remainder of the amount received by T, net of T's charges for selling the yearlings.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ This section is a consolidated treatment of provisions concerning setoff in several separate sections of Restatement Second, Agency: §§ 179, 203, 299, 306, 333, and 371. Restatement Second, Agency § 306(1) differs from § 306(1) in the former Restatement in that a third party's setoff rights against an undisclosed principal, when the agent has acted with actual authority in concealing the principal's existence, encompass only the agent's debts to the third party "at the time of making the contract and until the existence of the principal becomes known to him. . . ." The formulation in subsection (2) is intended to clarify that a third party's setoff right does not encompass debt incurred by the agent to the third party after the third party has notice that the agent made the contract on behalf of a principal. The formulation in subsection (2) also differs from the earlier formulations in § 306 because a third party's rights to set off amounts that the agent owes the third party does not depend on whether the agent acted with actual authority in not disclosing the principal's existence. Under Restatement Second, Agency § 306(2), "\[i\]f the agent is authorized only to contract in the principal's name, the other party does not have set-off for a claim due him from the agent unless the agent has been entrusted with the possession of chattels which he disposes of as directed or unless the principal has otherwise misled the third person into extending credit to the agent." Otherwise, the formulations in this section are not intended to differ in substance from their counterparts in Restatement Second, Agency. However, the formulations in Restatement Second, Agency, are all structured around claims that one party may assert against another. The formulations in this Restatement refer instead to an amount that one party owes another to clarify that setoff rights may be exercised in the absence of litigation.

_b. Setoff--in general._ For a helpful discussion of setoff in general, see Stephen L. Sepinuck, The Problems with Setoff: A Proposed Legislative Solution, [_30 Wm. & Mary L. Rev. 51 (1988)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S3V-3PV0-00CW-G2J0-00000-00&context=1530671). On setoff in the context of assignment, see E. Allan Farnsworth, Contracts 711-712 (4th ed. 2004). On setoff when a debtor is bankrupt, see Russell J. Passamano, Setoff in Bankruptcy: An Overview of the Mechanics, 105 Banking L.J. 349 (1993); Samuel R. Maizel, Setoff and Recoupment in Bankruptcy, in 24th Ann. Current Dev. Bankr. & Reorganization (2002). On setoff in English and Commonwealth law, see Rory Derham, Set-off (2d ed. 1996).

For the distinction between setoff and recoupment, see [_National City Bank v. Columbian Mut. Ins. Co., 282 F.3d 407, 409 (6th Cir.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:457K-VRW0-0038-X4WK-00000-00&context=1530671).

On the evolution of setoff in the United States and other nations, see Jan Dalhuisen, Dalhuisen on International Commercial, Financial and Trade Law 322-325 (2000). Setoff met with a hostile response in the early common law. In [_Green v. Farmer, 98 E.R. 154 (K.B.1768)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=administrative-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3T4F-28F0-0040-8034-00000-00&context=1530671), Lord Mansfield held that in the absence of statute, each party must recover separately in separate actions. In the United States, Justice Story allowed setoff in a nonbankruptcy context only when the parties' claims were connected. See [_Green v. Darling, 10 Fed.Cas. 1141 (D.R.I.1828)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-4YC0-003B-J1GG-00000-00&context=1530671).

For examples of contractual provisions that may create or expand setoff rights, see F.W. Neate, Set Off, 9 Int'l Bus. Law. 247 (1981). Contractual setoff is often implied, it is said, "on very slight grounds." See Dalhuisen at 323.

_c. Setoff rights and agency relationships._ For the point that restrictions on setoff reflect the agent's fiduciary duties to the principal, see [_Fuller v. Fasig-Tipton Co., 587 F.2d 103, 107 (2d Cir.1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-X640-0039-M2DV-00000-00&context=1530671). For an early account, see Joseph Story, Commentaries on the Law of Agency § 225 (8th ed. 1874), quoted in part in [_Gerard v. McCormick, 29 N.E. 115, 116 (1891)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRK-M810-003D-N1YR-00000-00&context=1530671), upon which _Fuller_ relies: "Similar considerations will apply to cases of general agency, where, from the usage of trade or, otherwise, persons dealing with the agent have knowledge, that he is transcending the ordinary and accustomed modes of transacting the like business; for they are presumed to understand the restrictions and limitations, thus imposed on the general agency, and are bound by them. Thus a person dealing with a factor or broker is bound to know, that, by law, a factor or broker, although a general agent, is not clothed with authority to pledge, deposit, or transfer the property of his principal for his own debts; and, if he receives such a deposit or pledge, the title is invalid, and the property may be reclaimed by the principal."

On the question of whether a third party's right to set off amounts that the agent owes the third party should turn on whether the agent acted with actual authority in not disclosing the principal's existence, see Warren A. Seavey, Studies in Agency 324-325 (1949). Restatement of Agency § 306, which drew this distinction, was based on cases in which "the principal has brought an action against the third person. All cases but one permit the principal to bring an action, thus indicating that he is a party to the transaction. If, however, he has not given the agent possession of the property, the disposal of which he authorizes, the cases do not permit the other party to set off against him their claims against the agent or to get the benefit of payments on account of contracts made to the agent. The result ignores the fact that the only right of action which the principal has is upon the contract made by the agent, which should include all of its incidents as agreed to by the parties to it." Id.

Illustrations 1 and 2 are based on [_Branham v. Fullmer, 181 N.W.2d 36 (Mich.App.1970)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRR-2C20-003D-70JN-00000-00&context=1530671).

On a party's financial condition as a circumstance that may furnish reason to know that the person acts only as another person's agent, see [_Branham, 181 N.W.2d at 37_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRR-2C20-003D-70JN-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 4 is based on [_Oil Supply Co. v. Hires Parts Serv., Inc., 726 N.E.2d 246 (Ind.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YXY-CKC0-0039-417P-00000-00&context=1530671). In the court's analysis: "When \[T\] received the cases of antifreeze as payment of \[A's\] antecedent debt, it signed shipping documents that made no mention of \[A\] at all, but declared that the goods were from \[P\]. This declaration should have alerted \[T\] to question the provenance of the cases and the nature of the transaction." [_Id. at 249_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YXY-CKC0-0039-417P-00000-00&context=1530671). In contrast, the majority opinion from the Court of Appeals did not focus on whether the third party had notice that the agent acted on behalf of a principal. Instead, the majority upheld the trial court's award, which permitted the third party to set off the credit it gave to the agent's account. See [_670 N.E.2d 86 (Ind.App.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-5HM0-003F-X1TH-00000-00&context=1530671), rev'd by [_726 N.E.2d 246 (Ind. 2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YXY-CKC0-0039-417P-00000-00&context=1530671). The majority opinion reasoned that the principal could have prevented the agent's fraud by confirming the order with the third party prior to shipping the antifreeze. Additionally, "\[w\]here one of two innocent parties must suffer from the fraud of a third person, the loss should fall on the person whose act or negligence enabled the fraud to be committed." [_Id. at 91_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-5HM0-003F-X1TH-00000-00&context=1530671). Although the Indiana Supreme Court agreed that the principal had been neglectful in supervising its agent, and that it could have prevented the loss by contacting the third party to confirm the order, the court emphasized that the third party had the last opportunity to prevent the loss before the matter was completed and, as it happened, the agent absconded. [_Oil Supply, 726 N.E.2d at 249_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YXY-CKC0-0039-417P-00000-00&context=1530671).

_d. Principal's agreement to setoff by third party of debt owed by agent._ For the point that a third party acts at its peril in accepting a principal's property to satisfy a debt that the agent owes the third party, see [_Gerard v. McCormick, 29 N.E. 115, 116 (N.Y.1891)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRK-M810-003D-N1YR-00000-00&context=1530671) ("a person who knowingly receives the money or property of a principal from an agent in payment of the latter's debt, does so at his peril; and, if the agent acted without authority, the principal may, on proof of these facts, recover his money.").

Illustration 5 is based on [_Fuller v. Fasig-Tipton Co., 587 F.2d 103 (2d Cir.1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-X640-0039-M2DV-00000-00&context=1530671). The horse-trading agent in _Fuller_ affirmatively represented to the horses' sellers that the full amount had been received from the auctioneer. The court observes that, as a consequence, the sellers "were not under any duty to investigate further to determine whether \[T\] had violated its contractual obligation to them by setting off \[A's\] personal indebtedness against \[T's\] obligation to pay over the net proceeds for the benefit of \[sellers\]." [_Id. at 108_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-X640-0039-M2DV-00000-00&context=1530671). In contrast, in [_Brown v. Cowden Livestock Co., 187 F.2d 1015 (9th Cir. 1951)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-1590-003B-02WY-00000-00&context=1530671), the principal had full knowledge of all material facts, including the agent's unauthorized use of part of the sale proceeds received from a third party to pay his personal indebtedness to the third party. With such knowledge, the principal entered into a new agreement that looked to the agent and not the third party for payment of the sale proceeds, and thereby released the third party from liability to the principal. An alternative analysis of the facts in _Fuller_ and Illustration 5 is that the auctioneer acted as the subagent of the agent retained by the owners who wish to sell their horses. So analyzing the facts gives the auctioneer no greater right to set off the agent's debt to it against the debt it owes the sellers.

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 6.07_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8W0-00YG-4007-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 6- Contracts and Other Transactions with Third Parties_ > _Topic 2- Rights, Liabilities, and Defenses_ > _Title B- Subsequent Dealings between Third Party and Principal or Agent_**

**§ 6.07 Settlement with Agent by Principal or Third Party**

**(1) A principal's payment to or settlement of accounts with an agent discharges the principal's liability to a third party with whom the agent has made a contract on the principal's behalf only when the principal acts in reasonable reliance on a manifestation by the third party, not induced by misrepresentation by the agent, that the agent has settled the account with the third party.**

**(2) A third party's payment to or settlement of accounts with an agent discharges the third party's liability to the principal if the agent acts with actual or apparent authority in accepting the payment or settlement.**

**(3) When an agent has made a contract on behalf of an undisclosed principal,**

**(a) until the third party has notice of the principal's existence, the third party's payment to or settlement of accounts with the agent discharges the third party's liability to the principal;**

**(b) after the third party has notice of the principal's existence, the third party's payment to or settlement of accounts with the agent discharges the third party's liability to the principal if the agent acts with actual or apparent authority in accepting the payment or settlement; and**

**(c) after receiving notice of the principal's existence, the third party may demand reasonable proof of the principal's identity and relationship to the agent. Until such proof is received, the third party's payment to or settlement of accounts in good faith with the agent discharges the third party's liability to the principal.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section covers the consequences when a principal or a third party pays or otherwise settles accounts with an agent.

Rights to setoff are covered in § 6.06. Other subsequent dealings between agent and third party are covered in § 6.08.

_b. Settlement with agent--in general._ When an agent makes a contract with a third party on behalf of a principal, both principal and third party have duties of performance under the contract. Subsection (1) states when a principal discharges the principal's duty of performance to the third party by paying or otherwise settling with the agent. Subsections (2) and (3) state when a third party discharges the third party's duty of performance to the principal by paying or otherwise settling with the agent.

When an agent has made a contract on behalf of a principal, under subsection (1) the principal may discharge the duty of performance owed the third party by paying or otherwise settling with the agent only when the principal acts on the basis of a manifestation by the third party, not induced by misrepresentation of the agent, that the agent has settled the account with the third party. This is so because the contract creates a duty of performance that the principal must tender to the third party. Tendering performance to another party--the agent--does not discharge the duty of performance that the principal owes the third party. However, the third party may lead the principal reasonably to believe that the agent has performed the principal's duty by making a manifestation to that effect. If the third party makes such a manifestation, the principal discharges the duty the principal owes the third party by paying or otherwise settling with the agent. If deception by the agent induces the third party to make such a manifestation, it is not effective as a manifestation of the third party. See § 1.03, Comment _d._

**Illustration:**

1\. P, who owns a catalog company that sells decorative home furnishings, engages A to arrange shipping for merchandise that P sends to customers. A arranges shipping for an order with T, a common carrier. In error, T marks the invoice for the shipment "prepaid." By so marking the invoice "prepaid," T makes a manifestation that A has performed P's duty of payment to T of T's charges for shipping. If P acts in reliance on the invoice in paying A the amount of T's shipping charges, P's payment to A discharges P's duty to T.

It is possible, although less likely, that a third party may make such a manifestation and the principal may rely on it when the principal is undisclosed. A person may learn of a manifestation even when the manifestation has not been made directly to the person; manifestation is a broader concept than communication. See § 1.03, Comment _b._

**Illustration:**

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that T believes that A owns the catalog business and is the shipper of goods to its customers. A obtains the invoice erroneously marked "prepaid" by T and gives it to P. If P acts in reliance on the invoice in paying A the amount of T's shipping charges, P's payment to A discharges P's duty to T.

Whether a third party discharges its duty of performance to a principal by paying or otherwise settling accounts with an agent depends on whether the agent made the contract on behalf of a disclosed or unidentified principal or whether the agent acted on behalf of an undisclosed principal who remained undisclosed at the time of payment or other settlement by the third party. If the agent made the contract on behalf of a disclosed or unidentified principal, or an undisclosed principal of whose existence the third party had notice at the time of settling with the agent, the third party's settlement with the agent discharges the duty of performance that the third party owes the principal when the agent acts with actual or apparent authority in accepting the payment or other settlement.

**Illustrations:**

3\. T contracts with P Corporation, which operates a dairy, for daily delivery of milk to T's household. P Corporation bills its retail delivery customers on a weekly basis and instructs its route drivers to deliver the bills. P Corporation permits its route drivers to accept payments from customers. By paying A, the route delivery person for T's account, T discharges T's duty to pay P Corporation.

4\. Same facts as Illustration 3, except that P Corporation instructs its route drivers not to accept payment from retail delivery customers. On each bill sent to a customer, including T, P Corporation states: "Payment may be made in person at our business office or by mailing a check or money order to our business office. Do not send cash through the mail. Our drivers have been instructed not to accept payments." By paying A, T does not discharge T's duty to pay P Corporation unless P Corporation ratifies A's acceptance of payment from T. Section 4.01 defines ratification.

In contrast, when an agent makes a contract on behalf of an undisclosed principal, the third party does not have notice that the agent acts on behalf of another person to whom the third party owes a duty of performance. Under subsection (3)(a), by settling with the agent, the third party discharges the duty of performance owed a principal who remains undisclosed. Indeed, the third party lacks any basis on which to tender performance to anyone other than the agent. By remaining undisclosed, the principal bears the risk that payment made to the agent will not make its way to the principal.

Once a third party has notice that an agent acts on behalf of another person, the third party has notice that paying or otherwise settling with the agent may not suffice to discharge the third party's duty of performance under the contract. The now-disclosed principal may, for example, direct that the third party's payment be made directly to the principal. Such a direction may constitute a manifestation that would make it unreasonable for the third party to believe that payment should instead be made to the agent.

_c. Notice of existence of heretofore undisclosed principal._ A principal's revelation of its existence may place a third party in an uncomfortable quandary if the third party's duty of performance under the contract is then due and the third party doubts the legitimacy of the newly disclosed principal's claim to receive performance. Under Restatement Second, Agency § 307, Comment c, a third party who has dealt with an agent for an undisclosed principal is, upon knowledge of the principal's existence, in the same position as a third party who has dealt with an agent of a disclosed or unidentified principal, "except to the extent that he is privileged to act in the protection of interests acquired before then." This approach does not expressly permit the third party to delay rendering performance to the newly disclosed principal until the third party receives confirmation of the principal's identity and the legitimacy of the principal's claim.

It is possible to argue that whether a third party has "knowledge" or has received "notice" of a principal's existence encompasses questions going to the principal's identity and the legitimacy of its claim. The availability of this line of argument may not provide much practical comfort to a third party who doubts that a purported principal's claim is legitimate. Whether to pay the person who claims to be the principal requires a present decision by the third party to make an expenditure. Deciding not to pay the person who claims to be the principal carries the risk that, after the fact, that person will be able to establish in litigation that the third party had sufficient notice of the principal's existence and relationship to the contract. Deciding to pay that person carries the risk that, if doubts about the legitimacy of the person's claim are valid, the payment will not discharge the duty that the third party owes to the person with whom the third person contracted. Moreover, if the third party pays neither the newly disclosed principal nor the agent, and regardless of the legitimacy of the principal's claim, the third party may default on an obligation owed to someone.

A comparable problem arises when an account debtor receives a notification that the account has been assigned. Under U.C.C. § 9-406(c), an account debtor may request reasonable proof that an assignment has been made and, until such proof is received, may pay the assignor. Although the position of a third party who has made a contract with an agent for an undisclosed principal is not identical to that of an account debtor whose account has been assigned, the dilemma created by the revelation of the principal's existence places the third party in comparable uncertainty. A previously undisclosed principal may give initial notice of its existence to the third party in such form that it is not reasonable to require additional information. Unless the principal does so, common-law agency should protect a third party who pays or otherwise settles with an agent in good faith before receiving reasonable proof of the principal's identity and the principal's relationship with the agent, as stated in subsection (3)(c). Such proof may in appropriate circumstances consist of confirmation by the agent that the person claiming to be the principal is in fact the principal. This approach is preferable to permitting the third party only to suspend performance because its consequences are simpler. If the third party suspends performance prior to receiving reasonable proof from the principal, the third party risks the possibility that its suspension of performance will be characterized as a default that triggers consequences, in particular under other contracts containing cross-default provisions.

_d. Undisclosed principal who pays or otherwise settles with agent._ This section does not deal symmetrically with settlements made with agents by third parties and by principals who remain undisclosed at the time of the settlement. When an agent has made a contract on behalf of an undisclosed principal and while the principal's existence remains undisclosed, the rule stated in subsection (1) does not treat a payment that a principal makes in good faith to the agent as effective to discharge the principal's duty of performance to the third party unless the principal makes the payment on the basis of a manifestation from the third party that the agent has settled the account. In contrast, under subsection (3), a third party who lacks notice of the principal's existence may discharge the third party's duty to the principal by paying or otherwise settling with the agent.

This asymmetry is justified on theoretical as well as pragmatic grounds. When an agent makes a contract on behalf of an undisclosed principal, the principal becomes a party to the contract unless excluded from it, see § 6.03(1), and has the right to receive the third party's performance unless rendering performance to the principal would materially change the nature of the third party's performance. See § 6.03, Comment _d._ In exchange, an undisclosed principal has a duty of performance to the third party. That duty is not discharged by rendering performance to someone else, including the agent. Unless a duty created by a contract may be discharged on some other basis, only full performance is effective to discharge it. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 235, Comment _a._

A principal may protect its position by making its existence credibly known to the third party and by demanding that the third party pay only the principal. The principal chose to deal on an undisclosed basis through an agent whom the principal also chose and whom the principal had the right to control. See § 1.01, Comment _f(1)._ It is the principal's choice whether to remain undisclosed after the agent has made a contract on the principal's behalf. By revealing its identity after the agent has made the contract, the principal does not risk losing the contract that binds the third party. Thus, the rule as stated in subsection (1) does not require a principal to sacrifice the benefits the principal may anticipate receiving by dealing through an agent who may make contracts on the principal's behalf without disclosing the principal's existence.

The rule stated in subsection (1) is articulated in Restatement Second, Agency § 208. The contrary rule discharges an undisclosed principal's duty of performance to the third party when the principal pays the agent in good faith. This rule is followed by statutory codifications in five states and by a majority of U.S. cases as of the time of the drafting of Restatement Second. Subsequent cases are sparse; one controlled by a statutory codification applies the contrary rule, but those not so controlled apply the rule as stated in § 208 and subsection (1) of this Restatement.

Pragmatic grounds also justify the rule stated in subsection (1) and explain why it is preferable to the contrary rule. Under the contrary rule, the dispositive question is whether the principal paid or settled with the agent in good faith. The contrary rule places on the third party, who did not observe the interactions between agent and principal, the burden of establishing that the principal had knowledge or reason to know that the agent was insolvent or otherwise unreliable when the principal paid or otherwise settled with the agent. It may be impossible for the third party to establish, even when it is true, that the principal doubted the agent's solvency or other indicia of reliability when the principal settled with the agent. The contrary rule may also require a court to draw distinctions among fine shadings of culpability that depend on whether and why the principal may have doubted the agent's reliability. The rule stated in subsection (1) is simpler to apply than the fact-intensive and generally formulated contrary rule. Additionally, by placing the risk of an agent's dishonesty on the principal, the rule stated in subsection (1) encourages principals to exercise diligence in selecting and monitoring their agents.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency, and codifications._ The counterpart sections in Restatement Second, Agency, are §§ 181 (subsequent dealings between third party and agent for disclosed or partially disclosed principal), 183 (settlement with agent by disclosed or partially disclosed principal), 208 (settlement with agent by undisclosed principal), 206 (effect of performance rendered by third party to agent of undisclosed principal), and 207 (dealings between third party and agent postdisclosure of existence of principal).

Some codifications of agency deal with the effect of an undisclosed principal's settlement with an agent prior to disclosure of the principal's existence. See Ga. Code § 10-6-54 (2001 & Supp. 2005) (person who dealt with agent, when agent failed to disclose principal, may "go directly against the principal under the contract, unless the principal shall have previously accounted and settled with the agent"); Cal. Civil Code § 2335 (1985 & Supp. 2005) (if exclusive credit given agent, principal exonerated by payment to agent in good faith made before notice of creditor's election to hold principal); Mont. Code § 28-10-609 (2004), N.D. Cent. Code § 3-03-07 (1987 & Supp. 2003), and S.D. Codified Laws § 59-6-7 (2004 & Supp. 2005) (same).

_b. Settlement with agent--in general._ Illustration 1 is a contemporary variation on the facts of [_Southern Ry. Co. v. W. A. Simpkins Co., 100 S.E. 418 (N.C.1919)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XRN-NV90-000G-K02C-00000-00&context=1530671). The fairness of this outcome has been questioned. See Warren A. Seavey, Undisclosed Principal; Unsettled Problems, 1 How. L.J. 79, 84 (1955) ("As to these … cases, I am in some doubt, since the third person had no reason to suspect that the receipt given to one who appeared to be acting for himself would have a harmful effect upon anyone else.").

On a third party's payment to an agent, see [_Gordon v. Tobias, 817 A.2d 683 (Conn.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48DP-VWC0-0039-42GY-00000-00&context=1530671) (mortgage company had actual authority to receive payment of balance due on mortgage on behalf of mortgagee, discharging obligation under mortgage). On a principal's payment to an agent, see [_News Am. Mktg., Inc. v. Lepage Bakeries, Inc., 791 N.Y.S.2d 80, 82-83 (App.Div. 2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4FN8-M5D0-0039-42P9-00000-00&context=1530671) (advertiser's payment, as a disclosed principal, to its agent of amount due printer does not discharge principal's obligation to pay printer; proffered expert testimony on practice of "'sequential liability'" in advertising industry inapt when contract not ambiguous).

A person may choose to deal through an intermediary who is not an agent. See, e.g., [_Farrell Lines Inc. v. Titan Indus. Corp., 306 F.Supp. 1348 (S.D.N.Y.1969)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-MPY0-0054-83V2-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _419 F.2d 835 (2d Cir.1969)_ (freight forwarder was not carrier's agent for purposes of receiving payment from shipper, nor was it shipper's agent for purposes of transmitting payment to carrier; when carrier dealt with freight forwarder as an independent contractor, extending credit to freight forwarder in amount of freight due in form of outward freight bills and giving shipper a freight prepaid bill, carrier may not recover amount of unpaid freight charges from shipper following insolvency of freight forwarder). On allocation of the risk that a nonagent cargo consolidator will fail to forward freight payments to the carrier, see [_Hawkspere Shipping Co. v. Intamex, S.A., 330 F.3d 225, 236-237 (4th Cir.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48PF-HP90-0038-X4VT-00000-00&context=1530671) (noting split among circuits on whether risk is borne by shipper or carrier). See also [_Tarstar Shipping Co. v. Century Shipline, Ltd., 451 F. Supp. 317, 324-325 (S.D.N.Y.1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-M370-0054-74MD-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_597 F.2d 837 (2d Cir.1979)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-W5J0-0039-M2JM-00000-00&context=1530671) (once shipping company became aware of vessel owner's lien on freights and subfreights, shipping company as principal had duty to determine whether its payment agent still had possession of funds paid to agent and, if so, to inform agent not to release funds to charterer).

_c. Notice of existence of heretofore undisclosed principal._ U.C.C. § 9-406(c) is substantially the same as former U.C.C. § 9-318(3). Professor Farnsworth predicted that courts will "apply these rules by analogy, even when Article 9 does not literally apply." See E. Allan Farnsworth, Contracts 704 (4th ed. 2004). Some courts have been criticized for insufficient sympathy with an obligor's position. See id. (citing specifically [_Robert Parker's Truck & Trailer Repair, Inc. v. Speer, 722 S.W.2d 45 (Tex.App.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-3MK0-003C-216B-00000-00&context=1530671), in which court held obligor had notice of assignment when obligor's "agents possessed enough information to cause them to make a reasonable inquiry into the existence of an assignment").

The classic case in which a third party refused to pay a previously undisclosed principal when the principal demanded payment is Scrimshire v. Alderton, 2 Strange 1182, 93 Eng. Rep. 1114 (K.B.1743). Although the report of the case itself is unclear on the point, the judge ultimately required the third party to pay the principal, despite four refusals by separate juries to find on behalf of the principal in the face of direction by the court that the agent made a contract that bound the third party to the undisclosed principal. See J. Dennis Hynes & Mark J. Loewenstein, Agency, Partnership, and the LLC: The Law of Unincorporated Business Enterprises 353 (6th ed. 2003). In _Scrimshire,_ the agent acted as a factor who sold oats consigned by a farmer as the factor's undisclosed principal. The factor received an enhanced commission for taking "the risque of the debts." 93 Eng. Rep. at 1114. On evolution in the functions served by factors, see Roderick Munday, A Legal History of the Factor, 6 Anglo-Am. L. Rev. 221 (1977). Like the agent in _Scrimshire,_ factors were agents who sold consigned goods. Factors sold in their own names. Id. at 229. By taking the "risque" that the buyer would default, factors provided financing to consignors. In contrast, most contemporary factors buy debts owed to small- and medium-sized firms, in exchange for commission, and become responsible for collecting the debts and other credit-management functions. Id. at 222. In both forms, factoring provides financing to its customers.

_d. Undisclosed principal who pays or otherwise settles with agent._ For the point that an undisclosed principal's payment to an agent does not discharge the principal's duty of performance to the third party, see [_Poretta v. Superior Dowel Co., 137 A.2d 361 (Me.1957)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YPJ-4320-00KR-F55T-00000-00&context=1530671). See also Randy J. Barnett, Squaring Undisclosed Agency Law with Contract Theory, [_75 Cal. L. Rev. 1969, 1984-1985 (1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S41-2700-00CV-60W4-00000-00&context=1530671) (third party has not consented to satisfaction of debt by undisclosed principal's payment to agent). _Poretta_ also emphasizes the value of adopting a rule that is "clear cut and explicit." See [_137 A.2d at 373_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YPJ-4320-00KR-F55T-00000-00&context=1530671).

Cases applying the rule as formulated in this section and Restatement Second, Agency § 208 are [_Poretta, 137 A.2d 361_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YPJ-4320-00KR-F55T-00000-00&context=1530671); [_A. Gay Jenson Farms Co. v. Cargill, Inc., 309 N.W.2d 285 (Minn. 1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-2T70-003G-V2RF-00000-00&context=1530671). The case controlled by a statutory codification is [_Shasta Livestock Auction Yard, Inc. v. Bill Evans Cattle Mgmt. Corp., 375 F.Supp. 1027 (D. Idaho 1974)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-RNR0-0054-64RH-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Cal. Civ. Code § 2335). For the cases prior to Restatement Second, Agency, see Reporter's Note to § 208. [_Dicta in Senor v. Bangor Mills, 211 F.2d 685, 689 (3d Cir.1954)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-Y000-003B-04VG-00000-00&context=1530671), note that a majority of American cases hold that an undisclosed principal's good-faith settlement with an agent "gives the principal a complete defense even against the third person who, on tardily discovering the principal's existence, establishes that the agent acted within the scope of his authority and asserts that the principal should bear the burden of the agent's authorized contract from which the principal has benefited." The accuracy with which the _Senor_ court characterized the cases has been questioned. See Warren A. Seavey, Undisclosed Principal; Unsettled Problems, 1 How. L.J. 79, 84 (1955) ("\[i\]n some of them, as in \[_Senor_\], the principal furnished money to an agent with which to buy goods and the agent was not authorized to buy goods except on payment of the money furnished by the principal. . . . In other cases of this general sort the third person assisted the agent to deceive the principal by giving the agent a receipt, although he had not been paid.").

Some cases and secondary authorities may begin with different views of dealings between an undisclosed principal and a third party. Some appear to view undisclosed principals with a measure of skepticism. See Warren A. Seavey, Agency § 80, at 134 (1964) ("the agent's failure to reveal that he is not acting on his own account, although without sinister overtones, does involve deception."). In contrast, the _Senor_ court characterizes the situation as one in which the third party has only tardily "discovered" the principal's existence, as opposed to characterizing the scenario as one in which the agent has failed to disclose the principal's existence, thereby suggesting that the third party evidenced a lack of due inquisitiveness. [_211 F.2d at 689_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-Y000-003B-04VG-00000-00&context=1530671).
