[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 6.08_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8W0-00YG-4008-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 6- Contracts and Other Transactions with Third Parties_ > _Topic 2- Rights, Liabilities, and Defenses_ > _Title B- Subsequent Dealings between Third Party and Principal or Agent_**

**§ 6.08 Other Subsequent Dealings Between Third Party and Agent**

**(1) When an agent has made a contract with a third party on behalf of a disclosed or unidentified principal, subsequent dealings between the agent and the third party may increase or diminish the principal's rights or liabilities to the third party if the agent acts with actual or apparent authority or the principal ratifies the agent's action.**

**(2) When an agent has made a contract with a third party on behalf of an undisclosed principal,**

**(a) until the third party has notice of the principal's existence, subsequent dealings between the third party and the agent may increase or diminish the rights or liabilities of the principal to the third party if the agent acts with actual authority, or the principal ratifies the agent's action; and**

**(b) after the third party has notice of the principal's existence, subsequent dealings between the third party and the agent may increase or diminish the principal's rights or liabilities to the third party if the agent acts with actual or apparent authority or the principal ratifies the agent's action.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ On rights to setoff, see § 6.06. On payment to or settlement with an agent, see § 6.07.

_b. Subsequent dealings between agent and third party--in general._ Having made a contract on behalf of a principal, an agent may continue dealing with the third party in relation to the contract. If effective, such dealings may carry diverse legal consequences. For example, if effective the agent's dealings with the third party may modify terms of the original contract, waive compliance with requirements it establishes, or satisfy duties of performance it creates. In addition, the agent may give or receive notifications on behalf of the principal that carry legal consequences. For the definition of "notification" and the effect of notifications given or received by agents, see § 5.01(1) and (2) and § 5.02. Comment _e_ discusses when an agent's performance is effective to discharge a duty owed by the principal to the third party.

_c. Contract made by agent on behalf of disclosed or unidentified principal._ Subsequent dealings between a third party and an agent who has made a contract on behalf of a disclosed or unidentified principal bind the principal if the agent acts with actual or apparent authority or if the principal ratifies the agent's action. An agent may have actual or apparent authority to make a contract on a principal's behalf and lack actual or apparent authority to take subsequent actions with relation to the contract, such as binding the principal to modifications of its terms. The context in which a contract between two parties will be performed may also involve actions by agents of one party or the other, or both, who may lack actual or apparent authority to modify terms in the contract or waive compliance with requirements established by the contract.

**Illustration:**

1\. P Medical Center engages A Corporation as its construction manager for the construction of a 23-story building. T Corporation, an electrical contractor, enters into a written contract with P Medical Center to install electrical systems in the building. The contract provides that it may be modified only by a writing signed by an officer of P Medical Center and that A Corporation and its representatives have no authority either to agree to modifications of the contract or to waive compliance with its requirements for modifications. P Medical Center makes no manifestation to the contrary to A Corporation. After the project experiences delays, A Corporation's on-site manager, B, directs T Corporation to perform extra work beyond that covered by the contract, assuring T Corporation's on-site foreman that "it will be taken care of at the end of the job." A Corporation does not have actual or apparent authority to modify the terms of P Medical Center's contract with T Corporation, nor does A Corporation have actual or apparent authority to waive compliance with the writing requirement imposed by the contract. P Medical Center, however, may ratify A Corporation's actions. See § 4.01.

On facts comparable to those of Illustration 1, it is not likely that a third party will be able to establish that the agent with whom the third party dealt had apparent authority to modify terms in the contract because the contract on its face explicitly states that only designated representatives of the principal may bind it to modifications. Separately, the contract in Illustration 1 explicitly addresses whether persons other than the principal's designated representatives have authority to waive compliance with the contract's modification provisions. If, in contrast, the contract is silent on these questions, a third party may be more likely to establish that the agent with whom it dealt had actual or apparent authority to modify the contract or to waive compliance with its provisions.

**Illustrations:**

2\. T enters into a series of consecutive one-year leases with P for a residential seaside condominium. Throughout this period, A manages the property for P. A prepares the leases and negotiates their terms with prospective tenants, receives rental payments, and arranges for necessary repairs. All dealings between T and P are through A. During the final lease, T decides to move and asks A whether T may sublease the condominium or terminate the lease. A tells T that P does not want the condominium to be subleased and that T may vacate the premises and terminate the lease by so stating in writing. T does so. P sues T for rent due under the remainder of the lease. The trier of fact may find that A had apparent authority to release T from obligations under the lease, if the trier of fact finds that it was reasonable for T to believe (and that T did believe) that A had authority to release T from the remainder of the lease. The trier of fact may find that A had actual authority to release T from the remainder of the lease if A reasonably believed that P consented to A so doing on the basis of manifestations from P.

3\. G Corporation acts as general contractor in the construction of a building, subcontracting most of the work to P Corporation. P Corporation enters into a subcontract with T Corporation for specified masonry work on the building. The subcontract between P Corporation and T Corporation provides that any change orders must be in writing. The subcontract between P Corporation and T Corporation is negotiated by A, the manager designated by P Corporation for the project. A also signs the subcontract with T Corporation on P Corporation's behalf. Consistent with custom in the construction industry, T Corporation will be paid by P Corporation and not by G Corporation. At a meeting on the construction site, G Corporation's representative demands that T Corporation perform additional work at overtime rates. T Corporation's project manager, M, replies that the subcontract does not authorize overtime. A directs M to have the overtime work done, stating, "Don't worry about it, you'll be paid for it." Thus assured, M directs that the overtime work be done. When P Corporation does not pay T Corporation for the overtime work, T Corporation sues P Corporation. The trier of fact may find that A had apparent authority to waive compliance with the subcontract's requirement that change orders be approved in writing. On behalf of P Corporation, A negotiated and executed the subcontract with T Corporation and acted as P Corporation's on-site representative when T Corporation was directed to perform overtime work. The subcontract did not expressly limit authority to waive compliance with its procedures for change orders. T Corporation and M may have reasonably believed that A acted with actual authority.

_d. Contract made by agent on behalf of undisclosed principal._ When an agent makes a contract with a third party on behalf of an undisclosed principal, unless and until the third party has notice of the principal's existence, the third party will believe that the agent alone is subject to liability on the contract. In contrast to the situations discussed in Comment c, the third party lacks any basis to be aware of restrictions that the principal may have imposed on the agent's actual authority subsequently to deal under the contract. If an agent who has made a contract on behalf of an undisclosed principal subsequently agrees to modify its terms when the third party lacks notice of the principal's existence, the principal is bound by the contract as modified if the agent had actual authority to make a contract initially with such terms. If the agent lacks actual authority, the principal may be bound if the principal ratifies the contract as modified, as stated in § 4.03. Additionally, as stated in § 2.06, an undisclosed principal may be subject to liability if the principal is estopped to deny the extent of an agent's authority.

**Illustrations:**

4\. P, who owns a restaurant, engages A to buy truffles for P without specifying a quantity. P directs A to make the purchases in A's own name and also directs A to make the purchases for cash, which P furnishes to A. A agrees to buy a quantity of truffles from a truffle hunter, T, which T is to deliver to A in one week in exchange for cash payment. Two days later, A agrees to accept a smaller quantity of truffles from T in exchange for a proportionately smaller payment. P is subject to liability on the purchase contract as modified by T and A.

5\. Same facts as Illustration 4, except that A agrees to pay T on credit, not in cash, in exchange for the smaller quantity of truffles. Unless P ratifies the contract as modified, or is estopped as stated in § 2.06, P is not subject to liability on the contract as modified. A did not have actual authority to contract on credit.

Once a third party has notice of the principal's existence, the third party should realize that the agent with whom it has made a contract acts as representative for another person. If thereafter the third party agrees to adjustments under the contract that are uniquely favorable to the agent and not beneficial to the principal, the third party runs the risk that the principal may not be bound because of the agent's lack of apparent authority.

**Illustration:**

6\. T, who owns a farm, engages A to provide wheat-combining services in exchange for cash payment upon completion. Before A completes the combining work, A tells T that A made the combining contract on behalf of P. Thereafter, T agrees to reduce the amount of money to be paid upon A's completion of the combining and, in exchange, to cancel a debt that A owes T for potatoes that A previously purchased from T. A then completes the combining. After T pays P the lesser amount of money to which A agreed, P sues T for the amount of money that T originally agreed to pay. T is subject to liability to P unless T can establish either that A acted with actual authority in agreeing to the modified contract or that P ratified A's action in so agreeing. A did not act with apparent authority in agreeing to accept a lesser amount of money in exchange for T's agreement to cancel A's debt to T.

_e. Performance by agent of principal's duty under contract._ A duty under a contract is discharged by performance of the duty. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 235(1). Performance by an agent discharges the principal's duty under a contract unless the contract requires a performance that only the principal may give, performance by the agent is contrary to public policy, or the third party has a substantial interest in receiving performance from the principal. These limits are comparable to those on delegation of performance of a duty. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 318. Performance by the agent may give the agent a claim against the principal for indemnity, see § 8.14.

**Illustrations:**

7\. P retains A to purchase coal for P. T, who has notice that A acts as an agent, enters into a contract to sell a quantity of coal to A, in exchange for cash on delivery. T delivers the coal to A, who pays for it with A's own cash. A's payment to T discharges P's duty to pay T for the coal. A has a claim against P for indemnity as stated in § 8.14.

8\. P, a law professor, agrees to give a series of lectures in a program of continuing legal education operated by T. P appoints A as P's agent for purposes of delivering the lectures. A, who is as competent to give the lectures as is P, contacts T and offers to deliver the lectures. A's offer to T to deliver the lectures need not be accepted by T because T contracted for P's personal performance.

9\. Same facts as Illustration 8, except that, as T knows, it is customary for those in P's position to dispatch delegates who are believed competent to deliver the lectures in question. The custom, known to T, supplements the agreement between P and T. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 222(3). A's offer to T to deliver the lectures is effective as an offer by P.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 6.09_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8W0-00YG-4009-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 6- Contracts and Other Transactions with Third Parties_ > _Topic 2- Rights, Liabilities, and Defenses_ > _Title B- Subsequent Dealings between Third Party and Principal or Agent_**

**§ 6.09 Effect of Judgment Against Agent or Principal**

**When an agent has made a contract with a third party on behalf of a principal, unless the contract provides otherwise,**

**(1) the liability, if any, of the principal or the agent to the third party is not discharged if the third party obtains a judgment against the other; and**

**(2) the liability, if any, of the principal or the agent to the third party is discharged to the extent a judgment against the other is satisfied.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section states when a judgment obtained by a third party against an agent or a principal discharges the liability of the other on a contract made with a third party by the agent on the principal's behalf. Other law governs when and for what purposes an adjudication is treated as a judgment. See Restatement Second, Judgments, Chapter 6.

_b. Judgment against principal or agent--in general._ The question of the effect to be given to a judgment obtained against a principal or an agent arises when a third party has not received the performance due under a contract or when the duty of performance has been repudiated, in either case giving rise to a cause of action for breach of contract. A statement or an act constitutes a repudiation as stated in Restatement Second, Contracts § 250. If the third party then sues principal or agent, or both, and obtains a judgment, the question is whether and when the judgment has the effect of discharging the liability of the principal, if the third party has obtained judgment against the agent, or discharging the liability of the agent, if the third party has obtained judgment against the principal.

The terms of the contract itself may bear on this question. For example, the contract may explicitly make principal and agent joint or joint and several obligors. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 289. Statutes may determine whether duties are joint or joint and several. See id. § 289(3). Statutes may also prescribe a default position that is subject to agreement otherwise. For example, under § 306(a) of the Uniform Partnership Act, all partners are jointly and severally liable for partnership obligations unless otherwise agreed by the claimant or otherwise provided by law. Section 307(d) requires a judgment creditor to exhaust partnership assets before enforcing a judgment against a partner's individual assets.

_c. Judgment on contract made on behalf of undisclosed principal._ In determining the effect of a judgment against a principal or an agent, the common law of agency long distinguished between contracts made on behalf of disclosed or unidentified principals and contracts made on behalf of undisclosed principals. A judgment obtained by a third party against an agent for a disclosed or unidentified principal discharged the principal's liability only to the extent the judgment was satisfied. Thus, by obtaining judgment against an agent for an unidentified principal, the third party did not forfeit its right to pursue the principal if the agent's assets proved insufficient to satisfy the judgment. Likewise, by obtaining judgment against a disclosed or unidentified principal, the third party did not forfeit its right to pursue the agent if resort to the principal's assets did not satisfy the judgment.

However, when an agent made a contract on behalf of an undisclosed principal, it was believed that the third party contracted for only one cause of action and one judgment and must elect between principal or agent. As stated in Restatement Second, Agency § 337, if the third party obtained a judgment against the principal, the agent's liability was discharged. Under Restatement Second, Agency § 210, if the third party obtained a judgment against the agent after learning of the principal's identity, the principal's liability was discharged.

Although the election rule is supported by many cases, the better rule, stated in several recent and well-reasoned opinions and by statute in New York, is that only the satisfaction of a judgment discharges the liability of an undisclosed principal or an agent who made a contract on behalf of an undisclosed principal. The "satisfaction" rule is consistent with the contemporary view that a judgment against one person who is liable for a loss does not terminate the claim that the injured party may have against another party who may also be liable for the loss. See Restatement Second, Judgments § 49.

Whatever may have been the theoretical justification for the election rule, its practical consequences are not justifiable. The election rule operates adversely to third parties who, at the point of obtaining judgment, err in predicting whether principal or agent will have sufficient assets to satisfy the judgment at the later point when execution is attempted on the judgment. The rule also operates adversely when neither principal nor agent alone has sufficient assets to satisfy a judgment. Sections 210 and 337 in Restatement Second, Agency, attempted to ameliorate the third party's predicament by noting that it may be procedurally possible to join both principal and agent in the same action and to elect between them only once evidence has been taken. Some states permit judgment to be entered against both principal and agent when they do not force the third party to make an election. See Restatement Second, Agency § 210, Comment _b._ Moreover, if an agent has a right to indemnity or exoneration from the principal, the third party may be able to reach that right as an asset of the agent. Id.

Notwithstanding these ameliorative possibilities, Comment _a_ to Restatement Second, Agency § 210 characterized as "inconsistent with the basic reason underlying the liability of the undisclosed principal" the doctrine that a third party's claim under a contract is discharged when the third party obtains judgment against the agent with knowledge of the principal's identity. This basic reason is that an undisclosed principal is a party to the contract made on the principal's behalf by an agent as stated in § 6.03(1). As a party to the contract, the principal may require the third party to tender performance to the principal, unless the burdens imposed on the third party would be materially enhanced. See § 6.03, Comment _d._ However, the election rule operates asymmetrically. Once a third party has notice of the principal's existence, the third party's payment to or other settlement of accounts with the agent does not discharge the third party's liability to the principal unless the agent acts with actual or apparent authority. See § 6.08(2)(b). Thus, a third party may not elect between agent and principal in performing duties under the contract, and a third party may not elect whether to be sued by principal or agent.

Additionally, the election rule may be triggered by action by a third party that does not reflect a fully considered choice, as when the third party joins both principal and agent in an action on a contract and obtains a default judgment against one of them. Even when the third party joins principal and agent and, at the point of obtaining judgment, makes a considered choice between them, the third party's information about their respective abilities to satisfy a judgment may be limited.

Discharging the liability of principal and agent only upon satisfaction of a judgment is consistent with the parties' expectations. A contract creates a right to receive the agreed-to performance. Both principal and agent know that either may be called upon to satisfy the duty of performance owed to the third party. Both are in this position because they consented to a relationship of agency in which the principal's existence and identity were not revealed to third parties. As agent for an undisclosed principal, the agent is subject to the duty because the agent is the person whom the third party expected to owe a duty of performance. The principal is subject to the duty because the agent made the contract on the principal's behalf. Although the third party has a right to one full performance under the contract, principal and agent separately owe obligations to the third party. Moreover, no windfall is conferred on the third party because the contract gave the third party the right to receive the agreed-to performance.

Additionally, the election rule is not a basis on which parties plan transactions or evaluate their prospective liabilities. Its operation is not predictable because a creditor's election determines whether the judgment will run against principal or agent.

If a third party obtains a judgment against a principal or against the agent and the judgment is not fully satisfied, the third party's recovery against both parties may not exceed the amount of the judgment. See Restatement Second, Judgments § 49, Comment c.

**Illustrations:**

1\. A, on behalf of P, agrees to buy goods for \$ 500 from T but does not disclose that A is acting on P's behalf. Neither A nor P pays for the goods. T brings an action against A to recover payment, at which time A notifies T that A acted as P's agent. Judgment for T against A is \$ 500. T is not prevented from bringing an action against P to recover payment for the goods, but T's recovery from A and P is limited to \$ 500.

2\. A operates a construction firm as a sole proprietor using the assumed name "A Builds It." A buys supplies on an open-account basis from T. T maintains A's records of A's account under A's name. Without notice to T, A incorporates A's construction business as "A Builds It, Inc." A continues to operate the business as before and to buy supplies for the business from T. Payment is not made when due on purchases totalling \$ 5000 made from T that are used in the business of A Builds It, Inc. T files suit on the open account against A individually and trading as A Builds It, and at the same time moves for summary judgment in the amount of \$ 5000. A opposes the motion on the basis that A made the purchases as agent for A Builds It, Inc. T files an amended complaint adding A Builds It, Inc., as a defendant. T moves for summary judgment against A Builds It, Inc.; A does not oppose the motion. The judgment against A Builds It, Inc., does not discharge A's liability to T. A's liability to T is discharged if the judgment against A Builds It, Inc., is fully satisfied. T's recovery against A and A Builds It, Inc., is limited to \$ 5000.

_d. Manifestation by third party to look only to principal or agent._ A third party may discharge the liability of a principal or an agent through a contract, just as any party to a contract may discharge an existing duty owed to that person under an earlier contract by accepting a substituted contract in satisfaction. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 279. A substituted contract is not effective unless supported by consideration or a substitute for consideration. Id. § 273.

A manifestation by a third party that the third party will hold only a principal or an agent is effective to discharge liability when it induces a detrimental and justifiable change in position by the person whose liability would be lessened if the manifestation is given effect.

**Illustration:**

3\. A enters into a lease for office space owned by T but does not disclose that A makes the lease on behalf of P Corporation. Rental payments are not made as required under the lease. T contacts A, who informs T that A made the lease as agent for P Corporation. T tells A that T will look only to P Corporation to recover the rent. T's statement is not effective to discharge A's liability on the lease unless, in reliance on the statement, A detrimentally and justifiably changes position.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency; comparative references._ This section is a consolidated treatment of topics covered by Restatement Second, Agency §§ 184, 210, 300(b), and 307(2).

In English law, the counterpart doctrine is known as the merger doctrine. The doctrine bars a third party from obtaining judgment against a heretofore undisclosed principal even though the third party obtained judgment against the agent without knowing of the principal's existence. See Francis M.B. Reynolds, Bowstead & Reynolds on Agency 378 (17th ed. 2001) ("\[t\]he rule seems to be based on two arguments. The first is that there is only one obligation, which is merged in the judgment. The analogy is with the case of joint debtors: but the reasoning seems rather that the obligation is an alternative one. This is reinforced by a rather rough and ready argument, that the liability of the undisclosed principal involves an interpretation of the facts inconsistent with that involving the agent's liability, and the third party cannot have it both ways: the principal's liability is in a sense a windfall, and the third party cannot complain if the windfall turns out to be of limited value. But … this rule relating to joint debtors has been changed by statute. And what may be called the 'windfall' argument can be regarded as reneging on the doctrine of undisclosed principal itself.")

_b. Judgment against principal or agent--in general._ Under the original Uniform Partnership Act, partners are jointly liable for partnership debts and obligations that do not arise from a partner's wrongful act or omission or breach of trust. See Unif. Partnership Act § 15(b) (1914). Partners are jointly and severally liable "for everything chargeable to the partnership" as a consequence of a partner's wrongful act or omission or breach of trust. Id. § 15(a).

_c. Judgment on contract made on behalf of undisclosed principal._ The New York statute is N.Y.C.P.L.R. § 3002(b) (McKinney 1991 & Supp. 2005), overruling [_Georgi v. Texas Co., 122 N.E. 238 (N.Y.1919)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-TC30-003F-625W-00000-00&context=1530671).

The leading case adopting the satisfaction rule, and rejecting prior authority adopting the election rule, is [_Grinder v. Bryans Rd. Bldg. & Supply Co., 432 A.2d 453 (Md.1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-4NN0-003G-23XM-00000-00&context=1530671). To the same effect are [_Crown Controls, Inc. v. Smiley, 756 P.2d 717 (Wash. 1988)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W2K0-003F-W47Y-00000-00&context=1530671) and [_Engelstad v. Cargill, Inc., 336 N.W.2d 284 (Minn.1983)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-2K60-003G-V1M1-00000-00&context=1530671). _Grinder_ is cited with approval in [_Tower Cranes of America v. Public Serv. Co., 702 F.Supp. 371 (D.N.H.1988)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-72K0-003B-61TX-00000-00&context=1530671). [_Carter v. Forstrom, 722 P.2d 23 (Or. App.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-YNF0-003F-Y1HS-00000-00&context=1530671), states support for the satisfaction rule but resolves the case on other grounds. [_Redi-Floors, Inc. v. Sonenberg Co., 563 S.E.2d 505 (Ga. App.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45GN-WBT0-0039-42W9-00000-00&context=1530671), discusses _Grinder_ with approval but remands to allow election on the basis that overruling the election rule would require action by Georgia's legislature or Supreme Court. See also [_Dimension Graphics v. Liebowitz, 994 P.2d 658, 662 (Kan. App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3Y5N-BTN0-0039-420J-00000-00&context=1530671) (court determines principal was unidentified, not undisclosed but observes: "\[i\]t should be noted that the above inconsistency and treatment appears \[sic\] to have no real basis.").

For the most part, cases applying the election rule, when the issue is cleanly presented, are of older vintage. A relatively recent exception applying the election rule is [_Wilkerson v. Stevens, 397 P.2d 983_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-6RF0-003G-F2WK-00000-00&context=1530671), reh., [_403 P.2d 31 (Utah 1965)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-6R10-003G-F2TF-00000-00&context=1530671) (holding question of election moot). See also [_Wahyou v. Kiernan, 302 P.2d 638, 639 (Cal.App.1956)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRK-SWP0-003C-32YB-00000-00&context=1530671) (dictum) and [_Watson v. Sierra Contracting Corp., 485 S.E.2d 563, 569 (Ga.App.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RJN-3C00-0039-4249-00000-00&context=1530671) (dictum). Older cases did not uniformly support the election rule. See [_Joseph Melnick Bldg. & Loan Ass'n v. Melnick, 64 A.2d 773, 776 (Pa.1949)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-VST0-003C-M11H-00000-00&context=1530671) (in Pennsylvania, third party may proceed against agent or principal, or both, but is entitled to only one satisfaction). Cases may refer to the election rule in dictum but resolve the case on other grounds. These include finding that the third party had notice of the principal's existence and thus the principal was not undisclosed, see [_Lubbock Feed Lots, Inc. v. Iowa Beef Processors, Inc., 630 F.2d 250, 274 (5th Cir.1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-7X50-0039-W4YS-00000-00&context=1530671), or that the third party dealt with the principal on a fully disclosed basis, see [_Orrock v. Crouse Realtors, Inc., 823 S.W.2d 40, 42 (Mo.App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-7VV0-003F-C35Y-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Freeman v. Liu, 112 F.R.D. 35, 39 (N.D.Ill.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-98W0-0039-R011-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent not an indispensable party in suit against principal).

Cases holding that principal and agent waive the right to election if they do not force the third party to elect include [_Amortibank Inv. Co. v. Rampart Associated Mgmt., Inc., 627 P.2d 389, 394 (Kan.App.1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-4M60-003F-D12W-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Davis v. Childers, 381 So. 2d 200, 202 (Ala. Civ.App.1979)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-BBK0-003C-949D-00000-00&context=1530671).

For the point that principal and agent owe separate obligations to the third party arising out of the same contract, see Merton L. Ferson, Undisclosed Principals, 22 U. Cinn. L. Rev. 131, 142-144 (1953).

More generally, election itself has been characterized as "an increasingly maligned doctrine \[that\] has now fallen into disrepute. Even though a party has a power of election, that party's mere recourse to court to get relief in an adversary proceeding is not regarded as such an indication of intention as will amount to an election. The plaintiff that seeks damages for breach of contract can be seen as indicating an intention to affirm the contract only if damages are paid." E. Allan Farnsworth, Changing Your Mind: The Law of Regretted Decisions 188 (1998) (footnote omitted).

Illustration 1 is derived from Illustration 4 to Restatement Second, Judgments § 49.

Illustration 2 is based on [_Grinder v. Bryans Rd. Bldg. & Supply Co., 432 A.2d 453 (Md.1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-4NN0-003G-23XM-00000-00&context=1530671).

_d. Manifestation by third party to look only to principal or agent._ Illustration 3 is based on [_Newark Paraffine Paper Co. v. Dugan, 394 A.2d 114, 116 (N.J.Super.App.Div.1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRN-0P00-003C-N4MR-00000-00&context=1530671). On the necessity for consideration, see in contrast Mich. Comp. Laws, ch. 566, § 1 (agreement to "change or modify, or to discharge … any contract, obligation, or lease, or any mortgage or other security interest in real or personal property" not invalid due to lack of consideration if "in writing and signed by the party against whom it is sought to enforce the change, modification, or discharge").

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 6.10_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8W0-00YG-400B-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 6- Contracts and Other Transactions with Third Parties_ > _Topic 2- Rights, Liabilities, and Defenses_ > _Title C- Agent's Warranties and Representations_**

**§ 6.10 Agent's Implied Warranty of Authority**

**A person who purports to make a contract, representation, or conveyance to or with a third party on behalf of another person, lacking power to bind that person, gives an implied warranty of authority to the third party and is subject to liability to the third party for damages for loss caused by breach of that warranty, including loss of the benefit expected from performance by the principal, unless**

**(1) the principal or purported principal ratifies the act as stated in § 4.01; or**

**(2) the person who purports to make the contract, representation, or conveyance gives notice to the third party that no warranty of authority is given; or**

**(3) the third party knows that the person who purports to make the contract, representation, or conveyance acts without actual authority.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section states the circumstances under which an agent or an actor purporting to be an agent gives an implied warranty of authority. Comment _d_ explains that an agent may expressly agree to become subject to liability if the person on whose behalf the agent purports to act is not bound by the agent's actions.

Section 6.11 states when a principal is subject to liability as a consequence of representations made by an agent.

Section 4.01 defines ratification. Sections 4.02 to 4.08 cover other aspects of ratification.

An agent who falsely warrants authority may be subject to liability for fraud or negligent misrepresentation. Section 7.01 covers an agent's liability to third parties harmed by the agent's tortious conduct.

_b. Agent's implied warranty of authority--in general._ An agent's implied warranty of authority is a solution to a problem otherwise confronted by third parties who deal with persons whom they believe to act as agents with power to bind a principal. Believing that a principal is bound by a contract, representation, or conveyance purportedly made on the principal's behalf, the third party will often rely on receiving performance from the principal, forgoing other opportunities and incurring costs in anticipation of receiving performance from the principal. Unless an agent risks some liability when a third party believes the agent acts with authority to bind the principal, agents may be tempted to act beyond the bounds imposed by the principal's manifestation of assent to the agent, in the hope that, if the transaction turns out to be advantageous for the principal, the principal will ratify what the agent has done.

However, an agent does not become a party to a contract made on behalf of a disclosed principal unless the agent so agrees with the third party. See § 6.01(2). Thus, if the principal on whose behalf the agent purports to act is not bound by a contract because the agent acted without actual or apparent authority, the third party may not subject the agent to liability on the contract unless the agent agreed to become a party. See § 6.01, Comments _d(1)_ and _d(2)._ The third party may be able to establish that the person on whose behalf the agent purported to act has ratified the agent's action, thereby creating the effects of actual authority, see § 4.02. Alternatively, the third party may be able to establish that the person on whose behalf action was purportedly taken should be estopped to deny the existence of an agency relationship with the actor, see § 2.05. If the third party cannot establish ratification or estoppel and the agent has not become a party to the contract, the third party faces the prospect of loss that is unrecoverable from either the agent or the principal on whose behalf the agent purported to act.

By purporting to enter into a contract or to take some other legally significant action on behalf of another, a person impliedly represents that the action will carry legal consequences for the other person. Thus, a person who purports to make a contract on behalf of another person impliedly represents that the other person will become a party to the contract and will be bound by it. If the implied representation is false, the person who makes it is subject to liability whether or not that person knows that the representation is false. The person who makes the implied representation bears the risk of its falsity because that person is better able than is the third party to ascertain the truth at the time of making the representation. At the time the representation's falsity becomes known, the third party may lack access to circumstances that would establish whether, at the time of impliedly making the representation, the person who made it knew it to be false. If the representation is known by its maker to be false, or if it is made negligently, the person who makes the representation may become subject to tort liability as stated in § 7.01, or, under some circumstances, criminal liability.

Such a representation may become false even though the person who makes it does so without fault. For example, an agent may reasonably believe that the principal whom the agent purports to bind is alive and has capacity to contract, but unbeknownst to the agent the principal may have died or lost capacity. However, the potential consequences for the agent of the principal's death or loss of capacity are mitigated, as stated in §§ 3.07(2) and 3.08(1), because the agent's actual authority to do an act is not terminated by those events until the agent has notice of them. Under § 3.11(2), an agent's apparent authority ends only "when it is no longer reasonable for the third party with whom an agent deals to believe that the agent continues to act with actual authority." See also § 3.11, Comment _b._

A principal may be bound by an agent's action although the agent acted without actual authority if the agent acted with apparent authority. See § 2.03, which defines "apparent authority," and § 3.03, which states how a principal may create it. If a principal is bound to a third party on the basis of an agent's apparent authority, the agent is not subject to liability to the third party on the agent's implied warranty of authority, although the agent acted without actual authority. The agent may be subject to liability to the principal as stated in § 8.09, Comment _b._ A third party may incur out-of-pocket costs to establish that a principal is bound because an agent acted with apparent authority. Although the third party receives the benefit of the principal's performance, this consequence leaves the third party less well off than had the principal rendered the performance without contesting the agent's authority to bind the principal. Other law governs the circumstances under which a successful litigant may recover the costs of establishing liability.

**Illustrations:**

1\. A and B are co-owners of property. A executes a lease of the property to T, signing the lease on two separate lines, one below the other: "A; A as agent for B." A had no actual or apparent authority to sign on B's behalf. Unless B ratifies A's action, A is subject to liability to T for loss to T resulting from T's reliance on A's implied representation that A had power to bind B.

2\. T owns a seaside vacation home and, wishing to obtain insurance against damage to the property, including damage that a hurricane may cause, contacts A, an insurance broker. A tells T that coverage for hurricane loss to vacation homes is very expensive but that A has been able to arrange comprehensive coverage, including coverage for hurricane damage, with P Insurance Company. T pays A the amount that A tells T is due for the first month's premium. A tells T that P Insurance Company will issue a written policy in due course, which A will deliver to T. In fact, P Insurance Company has not actually or apparently authorized A to bind it to cover risks associated with hurricanes. A is subject to liability to T for loss to T resulting from T's reliance on A's implied representation that A had power to bind P Insurance Company to an insurance policy covering hurricane damage.

3\. A, a mortgage broker claiming to act as agent for P Corporation, makes an oral contract to sell mortgage-backed securities owned by P Corporation to T Bank. A acted with neither actual nor apparent authority. A is subject to liability to T Bank for loss to T Bank resulting from T Bank's reliance on A's implied representation that A had power to bind P Corporation.

In situations like Illustrations 1, 2, and 3, an agent's implied representation that the principal will be bound by the agent's actions is an element of the agent's implied warranty of authority. A warranty, whether express or implied, is a promise concerning a past or present fact that incorporates a commitment by the promisor to be responsible if the fact is not as represented. An agent's liability for breach of the implied warranty of authority may be analyzed as a consequence of a contract made between the agent and third party. The agent impliedly offers to warrant that the principal will be bound if the third party enters into the contract with the principal or otherwise acts as requested. By entering into the contract with the principal or otherwise acting as requested, the third party provides consideration for the warranty given by the agent.

The measure of recovery for breach of an implied warranty of authority should reflect the fact that the third party may have been deprived of a benefit that the third party would have realized, had the principal been bound as the agent purported to have authority to do. Thus, if an agent purports to bind a principal to a contract, the third party's measure of recovery should compensate the third party for loss suffered and should include the benefit of the bargain to the third party, had the principal been bound by the contract. Some courts, in contrast, limit the third party's recovery to the damage or loss the third party suffered and exclude the third party's expected gain from the contract. Some cases explicitly characterize the third party's claim for breach of warranty as equivalent to a tort claim based on a misrepresentation.

The better rule recognizes that the function of the agent's implied warranty is to safeguard the third party's expectation that the agent in fact has power to bind the principal to the legal consequences of the agent's actions. Although the agent's implied warranty of authority is grounded in an implied representation made by the agent, the representation is that the agent's actions will bind the principal whom the agent purports to represent. When an agent purports to bind a principal to a contract but lacks power to do so, the third party has been deprived of the anticipated benefit of a bargain. The third party's recovery against the agent should include the value of this anticipated benefit. As noted above, an agent's implied representation may, separately, subject the agent to tort liability.

If a contract with the principal would not have benefited the third party, the third party has suffered no expectation damages. Thus, if the principal is unable to perform under the contract at the time the agent purportedly binds the principal to it or if the principal would have had a defense to enforcement of the contract, the contract would not have been of benefit to the third party had the principal been bound. Analogously, an assignor impliedly warrants that the right to be assigned "actually exists and is subject to no limitations or defenses good against the assignor other than those stated or apparent at the time of the assignment. . . ." Restatement Second, Contracts § 333(1)(b). When the assignor breaches the warranty, the assignee may recover damages, "not only for harm caused but also for the amount by which he would have been benefited if the assigned right had been as warranted." Id., Comment _d._ The assignee has no damages if the assigned right "would have been worthless aside from the breach of warranty . . . ." Id.

A person may be subject to liability when a third party suffers a loss because the person purported to bind another by making a promise on another's behalf when the person should have reasonably expected the promise to induce action or forbearance on the part of the third party. Thus, a promise, not supported by consideration, purportedly made on behalf of another may be the basis of liability for breach of the maker's implied warranty of authority.

_c. When no warranty of authority is implied._ Whether a person makes an implied representation of authority is a question of fact to be determined by inferences to be drawn from the person's conduct. No implied warranty of authority is deemed to have been given to a third party by one purporting to act as an agent on behalf of another when the purported agent expressly states that no warranty is given. It is a question of fact whether the manner in which the purported agent made the statement was effective as a manifestation made to the third party. If the agent gives the third party indicia of authority and a reasonable opportunity to examine them, the third party may evaluate the indicia. If the third party then enters into a contract with the person purporting to act as agent, the third party does not rely on an implied representation of authority. However, by presenting indicia of authority to the third party, the purported agent impliedly represents that the agent believes that the indicia are genuine and that no material fact has been withheld.

**Illustrations:**

4\. P, who manufactures children's clothing, has often sold clothing to T, who owns a children's boutique and deals with P through A, a manufacturer's representative. A receives an e-mail that purports to come from P and that authorizes A to make a contract with T on P's behalf. A has no reason to believe that the e-mail is not authentic. A prints an accurate copy of the e-mail and gives it to T to examine. T decides to proceed. A then makes a contract on P's behalf with T that is consistent with the statement of authority in the e-mail. If the e-mail does not constitute a manifestation made by P, A is not subject to liability to T on an implied warranty of authority.

5\. Same facts as Illustration 4, except that, before giving a copy of the e-mail to T, A receives a telephone call from P informing A that the e-mail was only a prank sent by P's child without P's consent. A does not tell T about the telephone call. A is subject to liability to T for breach of A's implied warranty of authority. A also made a fraudulent misrepresentation to T. See Restatement Second, Torts § 529. By giving T a printed copy of the e-mail, A impliedly represented that A withheld no material facts from T.

When a third party knows that a purported agent does not act with actual authority, the third party does not rely on an implied representation of authority in entering into a contract made by the purported agent on behalf of a principal. The third party's knowledge also precludes an appearance of authority that the agent might otherwise have had. Apparent authority is not present when the third party does not believe that the agent acts with actual authority. See § 2.03, Comment _d._

It is a question of fact whether a third party knew that a person purporting to act as an agent lacked actual authority. The circumstances surrounding the transaction, including inferences that a reasonable person would draw from facts known to the third party, are relevant to establishing the third party's knowledge. Also relevant are the third party's experience and familiarity with relevant business practices. For example, in Illustration 3, if the purchaser of the mortgage-backed securities knows from other similar transactions that no agent in A's position would have authority to make an oral contract of sale, it may be established that the purchaser knew that A lacked actual authority. However, most cases do not require a third party to conduct an investigation into the authority that an agent purports to have.

**Illustration:**

6\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that A agrees to lease the property to T, once again signing B's name without having actual authority to do so. During the lease negotiations, A tells T that B does not want to lease the property and that T will need to deal with B directly. T does not do so. A is not subject to liability to T for breach of an implied warranty of authority. A may be subject to liability to T if A's written representation of authority to T was tortious and if T appropriately relied on A's written representation rather than on A's oral statement concerning B's desire not to lease the property.

An agent for an undisclosed principal does not purport to be acting on behalf of another person. Thus, when an agent represents an undisclosed principal, no warranty of authority is implied, even when the agent erroneously believes that a contract or representation has been made with actual authority.

_d. Agent's express warranty of authority._ An agent may expressly agree to be subject to liability to a third party if the person on whose behalf the agent purports to act is not bound by the agent's actions. Such an assurance from the agent may be necessary to induce the third party to deal with the agent, especially when it is not feasible or convenient for the third party to contact the principal to confirm the existence and extent of the agent's actual authority.

**Illustration:**

7\. Same facts as Illustration 4, except that T attempts to contact P to obtain a more reliable statement of A's authority. T is unsuccessful. A then assures T: "I share your doubts about the e-mail, but if you will agree to be bound, I will guarantee my authority." A is subject to liability to T.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency, and codifications._ This section consolidates coverage in Restatement Second, Agency §§ 329, 330, 331, and 338.

Under the Louisiana Civil Code, "\[a\] mandatary who exceeds his authority is personally bound to the third party with whom he contracts, unless that person knew at the time the contract was made that the mandatary had exceeded his authority or unless the principal ratifies the contract." La. Civ. Code art. 3019 (2005), based on Quebec Civ. Code art. 2158.

Common-law codifications also impose liability on agents. Under Ala. Code § 8-2-5, "\[o\]ne who assumes to act as an agent thereby warrants to all who deal with him in that capacity that he has the authority which he assumes." (2002 & Supp. 2004) Under Ga. Code § 10-6-85, "\[a\]ll agents, by an express undertaking to that effect, may render themselves individually liable. Every agent exceeding the scope of his authority shall be individually liable to the person with whom he deals. . . ." (1993 & Supp. 2005) Under Cal. Civ. Code § 2342, "\[o\]ne who assumes to act as an agent thereby warrants, to all who deal with him in that capacity, that he has the authority which he assumes." (1993 & Supp. 2005).

_b. Agent's implied warranty of authority--in general._ For facts comparable to Illustration 1, see [_Meeks v. Adams Louisiana Co., 49 F.Supp. 489 (S.D.Ga.)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-JF70-003B-W37H-00000-00&context=1530671), modified on other grounds, [_138 F.2d 458 (5th Cir.1943)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RHM-JD80-003B-T125-00000-00&context=1530671).

On an insurance agent's breach of the implied warranty of authority, see _Hampton Rds. Carriers, Inc. v. Boston Ins. Co., 150 F.Supp. 338 (D.Md. 1957)_.

Illustration 3 is based on [_First Nat'l Bank v. Jefferson Mtg. Co., 576 F.2d 479 (3d Cir.1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-YP30-0039-M2BX-00000-00&context=1530671).

The definition of warranty is from 1 Samuel Williston, A Treatise on the Law of Contracts § 1:2, at 10 (Richard A. Lord ed., 4th ed. 1990).

On the scope of an implied warranty of authority, see Francis M.B. Reynolds, Breach of Warranty of Authority: A Point Elucidated, \[1998\] J. Bus. L. 151-153. The leading English case is Penn v. Bristol & West Bldg. Soc., \[1997\] 1 W.L.R. 1356 (C.A.), in which the court held that a solicitor's implied warranty of authority to bind a principal to a sale of property was given, not just to the purchaser of the property, but to the financial institution that lent money on the security of the property. See also Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 30(3) ("\[a\] lawyer is subject to liability to a third person for damages for loss proximately caused by the lawyer's acting without authority from a client . . . if: . . . (b) the lawyer purports to make a contract, conveyance, or affirmation on behalf of the client, unless the lawyer . . . does not warrant that the lawyer is authorized to act or the other party knows that the lawyer is not authorized to act"). However, in explicating § 30(3)(b), Comment _c_ relies on Restatement Second, Agency § 329, which limits the scope of the warranty to the third person with whom the agent deals.

Ordinarily an agent does not warrant or guarantee that a principal lacks power to disaffirm a contract. For discussion of an unusual case involving stock purchases made on behalf of a minor principal, who disaffirmed the purchases, see Note, Agency--Agent's Liability to Third Person--Breach of Warranty in Rescission of Contract by Infant Principal, 48 Harv. L. Rev. 1016 (1935), discussing [_Goldfinger v. Doherty, 276 N.Y.S. 289 (Sup.1934)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-2W90-0044-F136-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _280 N.Y.S. 778 (App.Div.1935)_ (agent not subject to liability when complaint did not allege that agent had reason to know principal was a minor or that seller of stock did not know purchaser of stock was a minor).

Although most cases permit a plaintiff who sues on an implied warranty of authority to recover damages based on the benefit anticipated from the transaction, some cases limit the plaintiff to loss suffered. Cases supporting a benefit-of-the-bargain measure of damages include [_Cargo Ships El Yam, Ltd. v. Stearns & Foster Co., 149 F.Supp. 754 (S.D.N.Y.1955)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-RKD0-003B-215M-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Weinstein v. Rothberg, 73 S.E.2d 106 (Ga.App.1952)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-72M0-003V-C17P-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Wolfson v. Beris, 295 N.W.2d 562 (Minn.1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-2WH0-003G-V366-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Wisnieski v. Rolf, 1993 WL 439956 (Neb.App. 1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-8390-003D-B4RH-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Schaefer v. Fraser, 290 P.2d 190, 208 (Or.1955)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRN-0MH0-0046-72W2-00000-00&context=1530671). Cases that limit a plaintiff to damages suffered include _Griswold v. Haas, 210 S.W. 356 (Mo. 1919)_; [_Martha A. Gottfried, Inc. v. Amster, 511 So. 2d 595, 598 (Fla.Dist. App.1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRR-6JY0-003D-X0FV-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Farm Credit Bank v. FCB Ltd. P'ship, 825 F.Supp. 932 (D.Kan.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-T3N0-001T-63F1-00000-00&context=1530671) (claim sounds in tort for purposes of statute of limitations; court also notes that under Kansas law, action for breach of implied warranty of authority may sound in either tort or contract and plaintiff may proceed on either). Characterizing the action as a tort claim is defended in Tim Faulkner, Breach of an Agent's Warranty of Authority: An Altogether Anomalous Cause of Action, 74 Aust. L.J. 465 (2000).

For the point that circumstances, such as a purported principal's insolvency, may affect the measure of damages recoverable by the plaintiff, see 1 Floyd R. Mechem, A Treatise on the Law of Agency § 1400, at 1032 (2d ed. 1914) ("\[s\]o if the contract had actually been authorized, a number of things might then affect its value, as for example, the solvency of the principal."). See also Nelson v. Nelson, \[1997\] 1 W.L.R. 233 (C.A.) (solicitor who commences proceedings warrants that he has a client bearing the name of the party to the proceedings and that the client has authorized the proceedings; solicitor not subject to liability for costs when, unbeknownst to solicitor, client is insolvent because solicitor does not warrant that client is solvent).

For the point that a promise not supported by consideration may be the basis for a claim of breach of the promisor's warranty of authority, see [_Schafer v. Fraser, 290 P.2d 190, 206 (Or.1955)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRN-0MH0-0046-72W2-00000-00&context=1530671) (lawyer breached implied warranty of authority by promising clients that other clients would contribute pro rata to litigation expenses, which induced payment by clients to whom promise was made).

_c. When no warranty of authority is implied._ Illustration 4 is a contemporary variation on Illustration 1 to Restatement Second, Agency § 331, in which A receives a telegram that purports to come from P.

A third party was charged with knowledge of an agent's lack of authority in [_Duncan v. Peninger, 624 F.2d 486, 490 (4th Cir.1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-CGG0-0039-W4RB-00000-00&context=1530671).

On knowledge of falsity of a representation made by another, see Restatement Second, Torts § 540 ("\[t\]he recipient of a fraudulent misrepresentation of fact is justified in relying upon its truth, although he might have ascertained the falsity of the representation had he made an investigation") and § 541 ("\[t\]he recipient of a fraudulent misrepresentation is not justified in relying upon its truth if he knows that it is false or its falsity is obvious to him"). Comment a to § 541 observes that "\[a\]lthough the recipient of a fraudulent misrepresentation is not barred from recovery because he could have discovered its falsity if he had shown his distrust of the maker's honesty by investigating its truth, he is nonetheless required to use his senses, and cannot recover if he blindly relies upon a misrepresentation the falsity of which would be patent to him if he had utilized his opportunity to make a cursory examination or investigation." In the example that follows, a person is induced to buy a horse by a representation that the horse is sound, whereas slight inspection would reveal that the horse is one-eyed. However, "a defect that any experienced horseman would at once recognize at first glance may not be patent to a person who has had no experience with horses." Id.

Illustration 6 is based on [_Sutton v. Culver, 204 F.Supp.2d 20 (D.Maine 2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45WX-1RT0-0038-Y144-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Yoakum v. Tarver, 64 Cal.Rptr. 7 (Cal.App.1967)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-9PR0-003C-J345-00000-00&context=1530671) (co-owner of motel told agent that his authority to execute sale contracts was conditional on other owner's assent); [_Montgomery v. Levy, 177 A.2d 448 (Pa. 1962)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-V040-003C-M1FD-00000-00&context=1530671) (builder had notice that architect's assurances exceeded his actual authority because builder's contract with city provided that only city could assent to additional work); [_Ershig Sheet Metal, Inc. v. General Ins. Co., 383 P.2d 291 (Wash.1963)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-YRC0-003F-R0HR-00000-00&context=1530671) (insured put on notice by insurance company's adjuster that policy probably did not cover repairs to pipeline; insured could not rely on prior statement by insurance agent that insured should proceed with repairs to minimize losses).
