[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 6.11_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8X0-00YG-400C-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 6- Contracts and Other Transactions with Third Parties_ > _Topic 2- Rights, Liabilities, and Defenses_ > _Title C- Agent's Warranties and Representations_**

**§ 6.11 Agent's Representations**

**(1) When an agent for a disclosed or unidentified principal makes a false representation about the agent's authority to a third party, the principal is not subject to liability unless the agent acted with actual or apparent authority in making the representation and the third party does not have notice that the agent's representation is false.**

**(2) A representation by an agent made incident to a contract or conveyance is attributed to a disclosed or unidentified principal as if the principal made the representation directly when the agent had actual or apparent authority to make the contract or conveyance unless the third party knew or had reason to know that the representation was untrue or that the agent acted without actual authority in making it.**

**(3) A representation by an agent made incident to a contract or conveyance is attributed to an undisclosed principal as if the principal made the representation directly when**

**(a) the agent acted with actual authority in making the representation, or**

**(b) the agent acted without actual authority in making the representation but had actual authority to make true representations about the same matter.**

**The agent's representation is not attributed to the principal when the third party knew or had reason to know it was untrue.**

**(4) When an agent who makes a contract or conveyance on behalf of an undisclosed principal falsely represents to the third party that the agent does not act on behalf of a principal, the third party may avoid the contract or conveyance if the principal or agent had notice that the third party would not have dealt with the principal.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ Subsections (1), (2), and (3) cover the circumstances under which representations made by an agent affect a principal's legal position in actions brought to enforce or rescind a contract. For the effect of an agent's tortious misrepresentations on a principal's legal position, see §§ 7.04, 7.05, and 7.08. Subsection (1) applies to an agent's false representations about the agent's authority. Subsections (2) and (3) apply to representations made by an agent incident to making a contract or conveyance. Subsection (4) applies when an agent who makes a contract on behalf of an undisclosed principal falsely represents to a third party that the agent is not acting on behalf of a principal. If the agent makes no representation that the agent does not act on behalf of a principal, the third party may avoid the contract only by satisfying the requirements for relief on the basis of a unilateral mistake. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 153, discussed in Comment _d._

Section 6.10 states when an agent is subject to liability on an implied warranty of authority.

_b. Agent's representation concerning agent's own authority._ An agent's own statements about the nature or extent of the agent's authority to act on behalf of the principal do not create apparent authority by themselves. An agent acts with apparent authority only when a third party's belief that the agent acts with authority is reasonable and is traceable to a manifestation made by the principal. See §§ 2.03 and 3.03. An agent does not act with apparent authority when a third party has reason to know that the agent acts without actual authority, despite protests by the agent to the contrary.

**Illustrations:**

1\. P, who owns a tree nursery, tells T, who owns a garden center, that A is authorized to sell P's trees only at prices set by P and communicated by P to T. A tells T that A now has P's authority to set the prices for P's trees. A's statement to T does not by itself create apparent authority to bind P to sell trees to T at prices set by A.

2\. A is the President of P Corporation, which has permitted A to deposit corporate checks in A's personal account at T Bank. P Corporation notifies T Bank that A has been removed from office and is no longer authorized to deposit corporate checks in A's personal account. A tells M, the manager of the branch of T Bank where A maintains an account, that M should disregard the notification from P Corporation because A was improperly removed from office and will shortly be reinstated. Believing A, M permits A to deposit \$ 31,000 in corporate checks into A's personal account. M's belief was not reasonable under the circumstances. P Corporation is not bound by A's representation to M, which A made without actual or apparent authority.

However, a principal's manifestation may invite a third party to deal with the principal on a basis to be stated by the agent. If so, representations made by the agent may create apparent authority when the principal's manifestation incorporates them by reference. The principal may then be subject to liability to the third party, although the agent has falsely represented the agent's authority to the third party, unless the third party had notice that the agent's representation was false. A principal does not invite a third party to deal on a basis to be stated by the agent simply because the agent has access to information about an event that is relevant to whether the agent has actual authority to take action.

**Illustrations:**

3\. P, who deals in cattle, retains A to buy cattle for P. P tells T, another cattle dealer, that A has authority to buy cattle when the market price of cattle in Mexico reaches a certain point. T makes a contract to sell cattle to A on P's account, relying on A's untruthful statement that the price of cattle in Mexico has reached the point specified by P. P is not subject to liability on the contract. By stating that A's authority to buy cattle was contingent on a price in a particular market, P did not invite T to rely on A's statement whether that contingency had occurred.

4\. Same facts as Illustration 3, except that P tells T that A has authority to buy cattle "when the Mexican market hits the right price." T asks what that price might be. P replies: "A will tell you." P has invited T to deal on terms to be stated by A. P is subject to liability on a contract A makes with P even though the price stated by A to be the right price in the Mexican market is not the right price as P defines it.

5\. P, who owns an antique store, marks the sales price on items displayed for sale in a code. For the use of P's sales force, P maintains a register that matches the codes with prices stated in dollars. T, a customer in the store, asks A, a clerk in the store, the price of a clock that attracts T's interest. A looks up the price in the register but, misreading it, quotes a lower price to T. The price quoted to T is not so low that T has reason to know that A has erred. T agrees to buy the clock at the price quoted by A. P is subject to liability on the contract to sell the clock to T at the price quoted by A. By making sales prices available only to P's sales force but not to customers, P invited T to deal with P on the basis of prices made known to T by A.

6\. Same facts as Illustration 5, except that A accurately quotes the price in P's register. T asks A whether A knows when the clock was manufactured. A replied, "I'll be in trouble if P knows I told you because P does not permit sales clerks to answer those kinds of questions. But I know that the clock was manufactured in 1890." A is mistaken about the date. T buys the clock at the price A quoted and later wishes to return it when T discovers that the clock was manufactured more recently than 1890. T is bound by the contract with P. T has reason to know that A did not have authority to make a representation about the clock's age. On claims that T may have against A, see § 7.01.

_c. Representations incidental to contract or conveyance._ When an agent makes a contract or conveyance on behalf of a principal, the agent may also make representations related to the transaction. For example, an agent who sells goods or property may give warranties about the subject matter. When an agent makes a contract with a third party on behalf of a disclosed or unidentified principal, representations incident to the transaction are attributed to the principal as if the principal had made the representations directly if the agent acted with actual or apparent authority in making the contract. Such representations by the agent do not affect the principal's legal position as if the principal made them directly when the third party has notice that the agent acts without actual authority in making the representations or that the representations are false.

**Illustrations:**

7\. P, a financial institution, owns the assets of an insolvent steel mill, including a building constructed of 35 tons of steel plate. P authorizes A to negotiate and execute a contract to sell the building. A negotiates a sales contract with T, a dealer in scrap metal, who plans to demolish the building. The contract provides that it will be P's responsibility to pay off accrued property taxes so that the county in which the building is situated will release a tax lien. Prior to closing the sale and paying A, T inquires whether the tax lien has been released. A replies: "We've taken care of it." In fact, A is mistaken. T pays for the building and begins demolishing it but is stopped by the local sheriff. The building is sold by the county at an auction to S. T sues P for the market value of the 35 tons of plate steel, less the costs of demolition and removal. P is subject to liability to T. A had authority to make a contract to sell the building to T. A's representation that the tax lien had been discharged was incident to the contract. T did not know or have reason to know that A's representation was false.

8\. P Corporation directs that an employee, A, obtain for it a policy of "key man" insurance on A's life. A completes an insurance application for a "key man" policy to be issued by T Insurance Co. A gives false negative answers to questions on the application about A's medical history that are material to T Insurance Co.'s decision whether to issue the insurance policy. T Insurance Co. issues the policy to P Corporation. T Insurance Co. may rescind the policy. A's false representations are attributed to P Corporation.

When an agent makes a contract or conveyance with a third party on behalf of an undisclosed principal, representations made by the agent incident to the transaction may affect the principal's legal position if the agent acted with actual authority in making the representations or if the agent had actual authority to make true representations about the same subject matter. Without notice that the agent represents a principal, the third party with whom the agent deals lacks a basis to assess whether the agent's representations appear to be made with actual authority. However, the agent's representations do not affect the principal's legal position when the third party has notice that they are untrue or made without actual authority.

**Illustration:**

9\. P, who owns a racehorse, directs A to sell the horse in A's name and to make truthful representations about the horse's racing history. A falsely represents to T that the horse has never lost a race. Relying on this representation, T buys the horse. Upon discovering the truth, T wishes to rescind the purchase. T may do so if other requirements for rescission are satisfied. A's false representations are attributed to P. A had actual authority to make representations about the same matter, the horse's racing history.

_d. Agent for undisclosed principal misrepresents relationship to third party._ When an agent deals with a third party on behalf of a principal but does not disclose the principal's existence, the agent does not impliedly represent that the agent does not act on behalf of a principal. This is so because in most cases the principal's involvement is not material to the third party's decisionmaking. That is, most parties decide to commit themselves to transactions on the basis of the price and other substantive terms plus an assessment of the likelihood that the other party will perform duties that the contract creates. An undisclosed principal's involvement ordinarily should not affect the price and other substantive terms of the contract. Moreover, an agent who makes a contract with a third party on behalf of an undisclosed principal becomes a party to the contract. See § 6.03(2). This enables the third party to assess the likelihood of performance under the contract by the party with whom it believes the contract is made.

If a third party wishes certainty that the party with whom it deals will be the only party with rights and liabilities under the contract, the third party may insist that the contract so provide. See § 6.03, Comment _d._ The third party may also insist on a provision prohibiting assignment of the contract. On the effectiveness of contractual anti-assignment provisions, see Restatement Second, Contracts § 322. The third party may also ask the person with whom it deals whether that person acts as agent for an undisclosed principal. If the agent declines to answer the question, the agent has made no representation to the third party. If the agent answers the third party's question falsely, the agent has made a false representation to the third party. This is a basis on which the third party may avoid a contract made with the agent on behalf of the undisclosed principal if the agent had notice that the third party would not have dealt with the principal.

An undisclosed principal or the agent who acts on the principal's behalf, or both, may know or have reason to know that a third party would not deal with the principal, if the third party knew the principal's identity. Mere doubts or suspicions about the third party's willingness to deal with the principal do not constitute reason to know that the third party would not deal with the principal. It is necessary that the third party manifest its unwillingness to deal with the principal, including an unwillingness to deal with anyone except the agent, and that the principal or the agent have reason to know of the manifestation. If the third party has made such a manifestation and the principal or the agent knows or has reason to know of it, the third party may avoid the contract, as stated in subsection (4), because of the agent's misrepresentation.

So to ignore an agent's misrepresentation, unless the agent has notice that the third party would not have dealt with the principal, protects an undisclosed principal who purchases property or goods from a third party who regrets the transaction after learning the purchaser's identity. Such regret on the part of a seller is often based on a belief that, had the purchaser's identity been known, the seller would have demanded, and the buyer would have paid, a higher price. The regretful seller may suspect that, for the purchaser, the goods or property had a special and above-market value that would have enabled the seller to bargain for a higher price. Since such negotiations did not take place, it would be difficult to determine how significant a role the purchaser's identity would have assumed in them. Moreover, the seller has manifested assent to be bound at a price then satisfactory to the seller.

This standard also protects a prospective purchaser of property from a seller who may claim, after a sale transaction is concluded, that the seller would not have sold the property to the purchaser and that the purchaser should have suspected that the purchaser's particular characteristics were objectionable to the seller. Some bases on which a seller may claim an unwillingness to deal are illegal. See, e.g., [_42 U.S.C. § 1982_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SHT-0712-D6RV-H525-00000-00&context=1530671) (race) and [_42 U.S.C. § 3604_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SHT-0722-D6RV-H1F9-00000-00&context=1530671) (race, color, religion, sex, familial status, and national origin). Other bases for claimed unwillingness to deal may reflect a third party's personal idiosyncracies, prejudices, or matters of personal taste that, even if observable by an agent or a principal, may not appear to create barriers to any transaction with the principal, regardless of its terms.

If the agent makes no representation to the third party, the third party may avoid the contract only by establishing that the identity of the parties on the other side of the transaction constituted a basic assumption about which the third party was mistaken; and either that the effect of the mistake is such that enforcing the contract would be unconscionable, or that the mistake was known to the agent or principal, and the mistake had a material and adverse effect on the exchange of performances due to the third party. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 153.

**Illustrations:**

10\. P, a real-estate developer, engages A to purchase several adjacent lots owned by separate persons so that P may redevelop the property into one large amusement park. P has reason to believe that, were P's interest to become known, some lot owners would demand a higher price and that still higher prices would be demanded by the last lot owners to sell. All lot owners sell their lots to A, who acts on behalf of P but does not disclose P's identity. T, who owns a lot, asks A why A is interested in buying T's lot. "To build my dream house," replies A. T may not rescind the sale unless T can establish either a mistake in basic assumption or that P or A had notice that T would not have dealt with P.

11\. Same facts as Illustration 10, except that A makes no misrepresentation to T. Unless T establishes a mistake in basic assumption, T may not rescind the sale.

12\. Same facts as Illustration 11, except that T is P's arch-competitor in real-estate development. P suspects that T would be unwilling to sell the lot to P. T may not rescind the sale unless T establishes a mistake in basic assumption. P's suspicions do not give P notice that T would be unwilling to deal with P.

A person may have notice that a third party will not deal with the person. If the person overcomes that obstacle by falsely representing that the person acts as an agent on behalf of a disclosed or an unidentified principal, the person may not enforce the contract made with the third party. See § 6.02, Comment _c._

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency._ This section is a consolidated treatment of points made in Restatement Second, Agency §§ 145, 162, 168, 169, 170, 171, 188, 196, and 304.

_b. Agent's representation concerning agent's own authority._ For a representative statement that an agent's representations, by themselves, do not create apparent authority, see [_Jennings v. Pittsburgh Mercantile Co., 202 A.2d 51, 53 (Pa.1964)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-TSK0-003C-M0B8-00000-00&context=1530671). An agent's testimony is admissible to establish the fact and extent of the agent's actual authority. See, e.g., [_Unlimited Equip. Lines, Inc. v. Graphic Arts Centre, Inc., 889 S.W.2d 926, 936 (Mo.App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-4YC0-003F-C0FF-00000-00&context=1530671).

The leading English authority on an agent's representations concerning the agent's own authority is First Energy (U.K.) Ltd. v. Hungarian Int'l Bank Ltd. \[1993\] 2 Lloyd's Rep. 194 (Ct. App.).

For the point that apparent authority is not present when a third party has reason to know that the agent acts without actual authority, see [_Ziv Television Programs, Inc. v. Duchaine, 191 F.Supp. 27, 32 (D.Mass. 1961)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-K8Y0-003B-21T6-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 2 is based on [_D & G Equip. Co. v. First Nat'l Bank, 764 F.2d 950 (3d Cir.1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-H170-0039-P4DR-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 3 is a variation on Illustration 1 to Restatement Second, Agency § 168.

Illustration 4 is a variation on Illustration 2 to Restatement Second, Agency § 169.

Illustration 5 is a variation on Illustration 1 to Restatement Second, Agency § 169.

_c. Representations incidental to contract or conveyance._ For the point that an agent does not act with apparent authority when the third party knows or has reason to know that the agent lacks actual authority, see [_Branch v. High Rock Realty, Inc., 565 S.E.2d 248, 253 (N.C.App.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:467Y-5NJ0-0039-43NB-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Ex parte ERA Marie McConnell Realty, Inc., 774 So. 2d 588, 591 (Ala. 2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4072-8R50-0039-416X-00000-00&context=1530671) (purchaser of real estate, who had personal knowledge of location of large trees and other characteristics that limited ingress and egress, did not reasonably rely on statements made by listing agent about access to property from street).

On statements made by agents generally, see Robert Stevens, Why Do Agents "Drop Out"?, 2005 Lloyd's Mar. & Comm. L.Q. 101, 103-104.

Illustration 7 is based on [_Cleveland Wrecking Co. v. FDIC, 66 F.Supp. 921 (E.D.Pa.1946)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-K6X0-003B-11JY-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Wallo v. Rosenberg, 331 S.W.2d 8, 13 (Mo. App.1960)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRH-P390-003F-B39R-00000-00&context=1530671) (purchaser of hotel may rescind purchase; agent who negotiated sale of hotel for owner misrepresented hotel's occupancy rate and operating expenses); [_Mid-America Tire, Inc. v. PTZ Trading Ltd., 768 N.E.2d 619, 640 (Ohio 2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:460H-1V80-0039-436G-00000-00&context=1530671) (purchaser of tires may enjoin payment under letter of credit; seller's agent misrepresented that seller was sole distributor of surplus Michelin tires as well as necessity for purchasing one type of tire to obtain another).

Illustration 8 is based on [_Equitable Life Assurance Soc'y v. New Horizons, Inc., 146 A.2d 466 (N.J.1958)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-Y820-003C-N0N1-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_First Am. Title Ins. Co. v. Lawson, 827 A.2d 230 (N.J.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4939-4XN0-0039-405P-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurer seeks to rescind policy of professional-liability insurance issued to law firm organized as limited-liability partnership when partner made material misrepresentation on policy application; court holds that, as a matter of law, insurer may treat policy as void as to firm itself, partner who made misrepresentations on policy application, and partner whose misappropriation of client funds was closely intertwined with misrepresentations on policy application; equities do not warrant rescission of policy coverage of innocent partner).

Illustration 9 is a variation on Illustration 1 to Restatement Second, Agency § 188.

_d. Agent for undisclosed principal misrepresents relationship to third party._ The result stated for Illustration 10 is supported by [_Finley v. Dalton, 164 S.E.2d 763 (S.C.1968)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RVJ-FNB0-003G-C12X-00000-00&context=1530671).

On the significance of undisclosed agency relationships in real-estate development, see J. Dennis Hynes & Mark J. Loewenstein, Agency, Partnership, and the LLC: The Law of Unincorporated Business Enterprises 352 (6th ed. 2003). A rationale for an undisclosed principal's position is that a third party who contracts with an agent manifests willingness additionally to contract with the agent's principal, should the agent have one. See Tan Cheng-Han, Undisclosed Principals and Contract, 120 L.Q. Rev. 480, 502, 509 (2004).

For the conclusion stated for Illustration 12, see [_Kelly Asphalt Block Co. v. Barber Asphalt Paving Co., 105 N.E. 88, 90 (N.Y.1914)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-V420-003F-61PM-00000-00&context=1530671) (where undisclosed principal's officers suspect hostility toward them by third party, a competitor, but "none had been expressed," contract made by agent for asphalt blocks not vitiated by purported mistake by third party about identity of party with whom it contracted; principal's suspicion that third party might be unwilling to deal with it based simply on fact that they were business competitors). On the need for an objective manifestation of the third party's unwillingness to deal, the court observed: "\[w\]e are asked to hold that a contract complete in form becomes a nullity in fact because of a secret belief in the mind of the undisclosed principal that the disclosure of his name would be prejudicial to the completion of the bargain. We cannot go so far." Id.

It has been argued that the reason for a third party's refusal to deal with the principal is relevant to whether the third party may avoid the contract. See Note, Personal Prejudice and the Doctrine of the Undisclosed Principal, 44 Harv. L. Rev. 1271, 1274 (1931) ("\[w\]here the third party's aversion to the principal is purely personal, a stronger case for rescission may be presented than some cases where it is based on economic considerations."). This may be because a showing of "purely personal" aversion to the principal that precedes the contract offsets the possibility that a third party's claim of aversion is a pretext to mask subsequent regret at the contract price. In contrast, when the question is whether an undisclosed principal should be able to obtain specific enforcement of a contract, others argue that the only relevant question in these disputes is whether the third party has a legally recognizable right that would be abrogated by enforcing the contract. See Note, Non-Disclosure of Principal as a Defense to Specific Performance, 75 U. Pa. L. Rev. 761, 769-770 (1927) ("\[t\]he right to an attitude which may be the result of personal idiosyncracy or emotional reaction is not a legally or equitably protected right"; known hostility of third party to principal should not enable third party to defeat enforcement of contract on basis that agent or principal acted with unclean hands).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 7.01_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8X0-00YG-400D-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 7- Torts--Liability of Agent and Principal_ > _Topic 1- Agent's Liability_**

**§ 7.01 Agent's Liability to Third Party**

**An agent is subject to liability to a third party harmed by the agent's tortious conduct. Unless an applicable statute provides otherwise, an actor remains subject to liability although the actor acts as an agent or an employee, with actual or apparent authority, or within the scope of employment.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section states the general proposition that an agent's tortious conduct subjects the agent to liability. Comment _b_ discusses the rule and its general implications. Comment _c_ examines how the rule applies when an agent's conduct violates a statute, a regulation, or an ordinance. Comment _d_ discusses implications of the point that only an agent's own tortious conduct subjects the agent to liability. Comment _e_ discusses when conduct by an agent that is otherwise tortious is protected by a privilege held by the principal or the agent. Section 7.02 covers circumstances under which an agent's breach of a duty owed to the principal constitutes tortious conduct against a third party.

If an agent's tortious conduct breaches a duty that the agent owes to the principal, the agent is subject to liability to the principal for harm caused by the breach. See §§ 8.01-8.12 for duties owed by an agent to a principal.

As stated in Restatement Second, Torts § 6, the term "tortious conduct" denotes "the fact that conduct whether of act or omission is of such a character as to subject the actor to liability under the principles of the law of Torts." For the elements of specific torts and other aspects of tort liability, see Restatement Second, Torts, and Restatement Third, Torts.

_b. In general._ An agent whose conduct is tortious is subject to liability. This is so whether or not the agent acted with actual authority, with apparent authority, or within the scope of employment. Sections 2.01 and 2.02 define actual authority. Section 2.03 defines apparent authority. Section 7.07(2) states when an agent who is an employee acts within the scope of employment.

The justification for this basic rule is that a person is responsible for the legal consequences of torts committed by that person. A tort committed by an agent constitutes a wrong to the tort's victim independently of the capacity in which the agent committed the tort. The injury suffered by the victim of a tort is the same regardless of whether the tortfeasor acted independently or happened to be acting as an agent or employee of another person. However, statutes may create immunities applicable to specific types of agents in specific circumstances. For example, the Federal Tort Claims Act, [_28 U.S.C. §§ 2671_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SG9-5HW2-D6RV-H109-00000-00&context=1530671)\-[_2680_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SG9-5HW2-D6RV-H10M-00000-00&context=1530671), and comparable state statutes bar actions against governmental employees for acts within the scope of their employment or require that the government be substituted as the defendant.

It is consistent with encouraging responsible conduct by individuals to impose individual liability on an agent for the agent's torts although the agent's conduct may also subject the principal to liability. Moreover, an individual agent, when liable to a third party, may be available as a source of recovery when the principal on whose behalf the agent acted is not.

An agent's liability is based on the agent's conduct; the agent's status is, separately, a basis for imposing liability only when a statute, regulation, or ordinance so provides. It is ordinarily immaterial to an agent's liability that the agent's tortious conduct may, additionally, subject the principal to liability. However, a statute may make an agent immune from liability when the agent commits a tort while acting within the scope of employment or duty, for example by limiting a plaintiff injured by a tort committed by an agent of a public body to a claim against only the body.

An agent's individual tort liability extends to negligent acts and omissions as well as to intentional conduct.

**Illustrations:**

1\. A is the sole proprietor of a delivery firm doing business as "Ace Trucking." A owns a truck used in Ace Trucking's business. A drives the truck negligently, injuring T, in the course of making a delivery. A is subject to liability to T.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that A has incorporated A's firm as "Ace Trucking, Inc." and transferred ownership of the truck to it. A is the sole shareholder, officer, and director of Ace Trucking, Inc. A is subject to liability to T. Ace Trucking, Inc., is also subject to liability to T. See § 7.07.

3\. Same facts as Illustration 2, except that the truck is driven by E, an employee of Ace Trucking, Inc. E is subject to liability to T. Ace Trucking, Inc., is also subject to liability to T. See § 7.07.

4\. A, an engineer, is an employee of P Corporation, a structural-engineering firm. T, who is considering purchasing a house, retains P Corporation to evaluate its structural stability. P Corporation assigns A to perform the inspection. A, who performs the inspection negligently, reports to T that the house is structurally sound, which in fact it is not. Relying on A's report, T purchases the house and suffers loss. A is subject to liability to T. P Corporation is also subject to liability to T as stated in §§ 7.07 and 7.08.

It is also immaterial to an agent's tort liability to a third party whether the agent benefited personally from the tortious conduct.

**Illustration:**

5\. A is the Chief Financial Officer of P Corporation, engaged in the manufacture of bulk pharmaceuticals. To finance an acquisition, P Corporation enters into a loan agreement with T Bank that requires P Corporation to place all payments from its customers into a special "blocked" bank account to be held in trust for T Bank. Instead, A diverts payments received from P Corporation's customers into other bank accounts for P Corporation's general use. Under applicable law, A's diversion of the payments constitutes conversion of T Bank's property. A is subject to liability to T for the conversion, although A did not derive a direct personal benefit from the converted funds.

The basic rule of this section is also applicable when an agent does its work through other agents. When an agent is an organization, such as a corporation or other legal entity, it can perform work only by deploying individuals to take action on its behalf as subagents. Subagency is defined in § 3.15(1). Comments _h_ and _i_ to § 1.04 contrast relationships of subagency with relationships of coagency, including those between superior and subordinate coagents. An agent who appoints a subagent is subject to liability to third parties for torts committed by the subagent when the subagent's conduct is attributable to the appointing agent. A subagent is also subject to liability when the subagent's conduct is tortious.

**Illustrations:**

6\. P, who owns Blackacre, engages A, a real-estate broker, to sell Blackacre for P. A assigns the work to E, A's employee. To effect the sale of Blackacre, E makes fraudulent misrepresentations about Blackacre to T, on which T reasonably relies in purchasing Blackacre. E is subject to liability to T, having made fraudulent misrepresentations on which T reasonably relied. A is also subject to liability to T. The tortious conduct of E, A's employee, is attributable to A. See §§ 7.07 and 7.08. P is also subject to liability to T. See § 7.08.

7\. Same facts as Illustration 6, except that, en route to meet with T, E's negligent driving injures V. E is subject to liability to V. A is also subject to liability to V. E's driving was within the scope of E's employment by A. See § 7.07(2). P is not subject to liability to V. Neither A nor E was P's employee. See § 7.07(3).

The rule stated in this section differs from a basic rule applicable to agents' liability stemming from contracts. An agent who makes a contract on behalf of a disclosed principal does not become a party to the contract, and is not subject to liability on it, unless the agent and the third party so agree. See § 6.01. Section 1.04(2)(a) defines the term "disclosed principal." A third party who enters into a contract with a disclosed principal through an agent does so in anticipation of receiving performance from the principal and rendering performance to the principal. See § 6.01, Comment _b._

Contract law and tort law may have overlapping consequences. For example, if a person falsely claims to have power to bind another to a contract or other transaction, that person gives an implied warranty of authority and is subject to liability to one who suffers loss caused by the false claim. See § 6.10. Additionally, if the person who falsely claims to have power to bind another knows that the claim is untrue, the person has made a fraudulent misrepresentation and is subject to liability to those who, justifiably relying on the representation, suffer loss as a consequence. See Restatement Second, Torts § 525.

If a principal ratifies an agent's tortious conduct, the ratification does not release the agent from liability to the third party unless the agent's conduct constituted a tort only because the agent acted without the principal's consent. Ratification is defined in § 4.01. By ratifying, the principal consents after-the-fact to the agent's conduct. Ratification creates, after-the-fact, the legal consequences of an action taken by an agent with actual authority. This subjects the principal to liability. See § 7.04.

The existence of an agency relationship shields neither the agent nor the principal from liability to the other stemming from a tort committed against the other.

_c. Constitutions, statutes, regulations, and ordinances._ Common-law tort law is not the sole basis upon which a person may be subject to nonconsensual liability. For example, a statute may declare conduct unlawful, impose a public-law penalty on the person whose conduct violates the statute, and expressly or impliedly impose civil liability in damages to the victim of the violation. For further discussion, see Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 14, Comment _b,_ and § 38, Comment _c_ (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005). A statute may also impose consequences other than damages on a person whose conduct violates the statute, such as the loss of a license to engage in a particular activity.

When an agent's conduct violates a constitution, statute, regulation, or ordinance, the agent is subject to liability although the agent acted at the principal's direction or to further the principal's interests, unless the imposition of liability is inconsistent with the constitution, statute, regulation, or ordinance. Some statutes explicitly impose liability only on principals or employers. For example, discrimination by an employee that violates [_42 U.S.C. § 2000e-2(a)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SHT-0712-D6RV-H54J-00000-00&context=1530671) results in liability only for the employer. Other statutes explicitly address when an individual actor is subject to liability for conduct that violates the statute. For example, CERCLA specifies that a person who is an "operator" of a facility is subject to liability, as is the facility's "owner." See [_42 U.S.C. § 9601 et seq._](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SHT-0722-D6RV-H52W-00000-00&context=1530671)

An agent's liability is based on the agent's own conduct and not conduct of the principal or other agents, unless a statute, regulation, or ordinance provides otherwise.

**Illustration:**

8\. P Corporation is engaged in the business of managing and leasing residential properties. A is the President of P Corporation and its sole shareholder. In contravention of the federal Fair Housing Act, E, an employee of P Corporation, refuses to rent a property to T on the basis of T's race. A does not know of, authorize, or ratify E's violation of the Act. A is not negligent in hiring or supervising E. The Act does not impose liability on the basis of an actor's supervisory position in or ownership of a firm. E is subject to liability to T, as is E's employer, P Corporation. A is not subject to liability to T. A's conduct was not tortious, and the statute does not impose liability on a person in A's position.

Statutory definitions of prohibited conduct may diverge from common-law definitions of tortious conduct. For example, statutes may impose strict liability on violators, eliminating culpability as an element of the violation. Individuals are subject to liability for violations of such statutes when they bear a responsible relationship to the conduct that constitutes the violation.

_d. Agent's own tortious conduct._ Only an agent's own tortious conduct subjects the agent to liability under this rule. An agent is not subject to liability for torts committed by the agent's principal that do not implicate the agent's own conduct; there is no principle of "respondeat inferior." Likewise, an agent is not subject to liability for torts committed by the agent's predecessor or coagent.

The fact that an agent occupies a superior position within an organization is in itself immaterial to whether the agent is subject to tort liability. An agent is not subject to liability on the basis of the agent's status within an organization. Thus, in Illustration 8, the fact that A is the President of P Corporation does not in itself subject A to liability for E's violation of the Fair Housing Act. In contrast, if A directs or participates in an action that violates the Act, A is subject to liability. However, an agent whose assigned function within an organization includes the supervision of others may be subject to liability when a failure by the agent properly to supervise breaches a duty that the agent owes to a third party.

Holding a position as an officer or director of a corporation or other organization does not insulate a person from liability for the person's own tortious conduct. Thus, an organizational officer is subject to liability when the officer directly participates in conduct that constitutes a tort, as in Illustrations 2 and 5. An organizational officer may also be subject to tort liability when the officer directs another actor to engage in conduct that injures a third person.

**Illustration:**

9\. P Corporation, in the wholesale produce business, employs A to make deliveries. A's work is scheduled by S, Vice President for Operations of P Corporation, and is also subject to S's supervision. S directs A to deliver produce to C and to use a particular truck owned by P Corporation. Both A and S know that the truck's brakes do not work properly. En route to making the delivery to C, the truck's brakes fail when A attempts to stop at a stop sign. A's failure to stop causes the truck driven by A to collide with a vehicle driven by T, injuring T and damaging T's vehicle. Under applicable law, T is not at fault. Both A and S are subject to liability to T. P Corporation is also subject to liability to T. See § 7.07.

If an organizational officer participates in wrongful conduct, the officer is subject to liability as discussed above. In order to reach that result, it is not necessary to "pierce the corporate veil" or "disregard the corporation's existence as a separate legal entity," or find that a corporation or other legal entity was a "sham" or the "alter ego" of another individual or entity.

A particular tort may require that an actor take action with a particular intention or that the actor take action or fail to act while having knowledge of a fact or reason to know the fact. An agent's action or failure to take action does not constitute such a tort if the agent acts or fails to act without the requisite intention or knowledge of or reason to know a fact.

**Illustration:**

10\. Same facts as Illustration 9, except that A does not know that the truck's brakes do not work properly. A's duties do not include inspecting the truck's brakes. A is not subject to liability to T.

Similarly, an agent who makes an untrue statement to a third party based on information provided by the principal is not subject to liability to the third party simply because the principal knew the information to be untrue, provided that the agent does not have notice that the statement is untrue. In some situations, an agent may have a duty to inquire further when provided with information by the principal or by a coagent. The agent's duty of inquiry may stem from the position the agent occupies within an organization, from the agent's professional role or duties, or from circumstances that make it unreasonable to rely on the information provided without further inquiry. Unless an agent has a duty of inquiry, the agent may properly rely on the principal's statements to the same extent the agent may rely upon statements from any other source.

**Illustrations:**

11\. P, who deals in antiques, employs A as a salesperson. P tells A that a table is a 17th-century work. A, who reasonably believes P's statement to be true, repeats the statement to T. T, relying on A's statement, buys the table. In fact, as P knows, the table is a 19th-century reproduction. A is not subject to liability to T because A neither knew nor had reason to know that P's statement was false. P is subject to liability to T as stated in § 7.04.

12\. Same facts as Illustration 11, except that after A repeats P's statement to T but before consummation of T's purchase, A learns the truth about the table. A refrains from telling T, who buys the table. Both P and A are subject to liability to T.

13\. Same facts as Illustration 12, except that T's purchase of the table is consummated in A's presence by B, another salesperson employed by P. A remains silent. B does not know or have reason to know of either A's statement to T or the truth about the table. Both P and A are subject to liability to T. B is not subject to liability to T.

_e. Privileged conduct._ The term "privilege" denotes "the fact that conduct which, under ordinary circumstances, would subject the actor to liability, under particular circumstances does not subject him to such liability." Restatement Second, Torts § 10(1). A privilege may be based on another's consent. A privilege may also be created by law, regardless of consent, to protect the rights of individuals or the state. Finally, a privilege may also be based on "the fact that the actor is performing a function for the proper performance of which freedom of action is essential." Id. § 10(2)(c).

Most privileges held by a principal may be delegated to an agent. When a principal's privilege to act is based on the consent of a third party, the privilege is not delegable by the principal to an agent when the third party has consented only to action by the principal personally. To be protected by a privilege held by the principal, an agent must act for the purpose for which the privilege is given to the principal.

An act may be privileged because the actor reasonably believes that certain facts exist. Although an agent's conduct is not privileged simply because the agent acts on the basis of instructions received from the principal, the principal's instructions may give the agent reason to believe in the existence of facts on which a privilege is based, as may information that an agent receives from coagents.

**Illustration:**

14\. P, who owns a human-resources consulting firm, employs A as the manager of a branch office. Pursuant to instructions received from P, A destroys documents that belong to C, a client of the firm. A reasonably believes that the documents belong to P on the basis of information received from P. A is not subject to liability to C.

The functions that an agent performs may cloak with a privilege conduct by the agent that would otherwise be tortious because proper performance of the agent's functions requires that the agent have some freedom to take action without undue inhibition. An agent's responsibilities to a principal privilege advice and assistance given by the agent to the principal to protect the principal's interests. Thus, an agent who advises a principal not to deal with a third party is not subject to liability for interfering with the principal's contract or prospective contractual relations with the third party unless the agent acts to serve the agent's own interests or for another wrongful purpose. See Restatement Second, Torts § 770. This privilege is inapplicable when the agent's conduct consists of interference with contracts or prospective contractual relationships to which the agent's principal is not a party. For discussion of a lawyer's liability for advising or assisting a client who acts tortiously, see Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 56, Comment c.

Moreover, an agent's responsibilities to a principal often require that the agent report information to the principal. See § 8.11 for further discussion. In performing such a reporting function, an agent is conditionally privileged against liability for defamation. See Restatement Second, Torts § 595. The privilege is lost if the agent knows the report to be false or is recklessly indifferent to its truth or falsity, if the report is made for an improper purpose, or if the report includes defamatory matter not reasonably believed necessary to protect the principal's interests. See id., Comment a.

In contrast, the term "immunity" denotes that an actor "avoids liability in tort under all circumstances . . . because of the status or position of the favored defendant or his relationship with others." Restatement Second, Torts, Chapter 45A, Introductory Note. Specific immunities are treated in Restatement Second, Torts §§ 895A--895J.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency, and codifications._ This section consolidates treatment of points made by Restatement Second, Agency, in several sections, including §§ 217, 343, 344, 345, 346, 347, 348, 349, 350, 351, 358, and 360.

Most codified treatments of agency law cover an agent's individual liability for the agent's own torts. Cal. Civ. Code § 2343 (1993 & Supp. 2005) provides that "\[o\]ne who assumes to act as an agent is responsible to third persons as a principal for his acts in the course of his agency, in any of the following cases, and in no others: . . . 3. When his acts are wrongful in their nature." Mont. Code Ann. § 28-10-702(3) (2004); N.D. Cent. Code § 3-04-02(3) (1987 & Supp. 2003); S.D. Code § 59-5-2(3) (2004) (same). Under Ga. Code § 10-6-85 (2000 & Supp. 2005), "\[a\]ll agents, by an express undertaking to that effect, may render themselves individually liable. Every agent exceeding the scope of his authority shall be individually liable to the person with whom he deals; so, also, for his own tortious act, whether acting by command of his principal or not, he shall be responsible; for the negligence of his under-servant, employed by him on behalf of his principal, he shall not be responsible." Although Louisiana's Civil Code does not contain a precise counterpart, La. Civ. Code art. 2295 (1997 & Supp. 2005) provides that "\[t\]he manager must exercise the care of a prudent administrator and is answerable for any loss that results for his failure to do so."

_b. In general._ For the basic point that the capacity in which a tortfeasor acts is immaterial to liability, see, e.g., [_Oriental Trading Co. v. Firetti, 236 F.3d 938, 945 (8th Cir.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:423N-BKF0-0038-X40V-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporate officers who made fraudulent and negligent statements about payment of antidumping duties to purchaser of crayons to induce purchaser to make corresponding payments to corporation are subject to individual liability); [_Inter-Connect, Inc. v. Gross, 644 So. 2d 867, 869 (Ala.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-WVR0-003C-91WD-00000-00&context=1530671) (complaint alleged that president of seller of electronic equipment, in effecting its repossession, committed trespass and burglary of buyer's premises; reversing trial court's grant of summary judgment in favor of seller's president, court holds that the fact president took action "in an official corporate capacity" is immaterial to his individual tort liability); [_T.V. Spano Bldg. Corp. v. Dept. of Natural Res., 628 A.2d 53, 62 (Del.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRT-7080-003C-K27F-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporate officer may be subject to liability in connection with disposal of hazardous waste in appropriate circumstances; even though corporation owned property and defendant oversaw operations in capacity of corporate officer, officer not subject to individual liability when officer did not direct, control, oversee, or ratify decision to dispose of construction waste); [_Atram v. Star Tool & Die Corp., 581 N.E.2d 1110, 1113-1114 (Ohio App. 1989)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-NT10-008T-Y23N-00000-00&context=1530671) (president of corporation individually liable to plaintiff; president made fraudulent misrepresentations, upon which plaintiff relied, to induce plaintiff to resign current employment and come to work for corporation). For discussion of the basic point in the context of relationships between senior and junior lawyers, see Carol M. Rice, The Superior Orders Defense in Legal Ethics: Sending the Wrong Message to Young Lawyers, [_32 Wake Forest L. Rev. 887, 904-914 (1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S3T-W8B0-00CW-40H4-00000-00&context=1530671).

For The Federal Tort Claims Act, see [_28 U.S.C. §§ 2671_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SG9-5HW2-D6RV-H109-00000-00&context=1530671)\-[_2680_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SG9-5HW2-D6RV-H10M-00000-00&context=1530671) (2000). For an example of a state statute limiting causes of action stemming from torts committed by agents of public bodies, see [_Jensen v. Whitlow, 51 P.3d 599 (Or.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:46GB-9KJ0-0039-455S-00000-00&context=1530671) (Oregon Tort Claims Act, Or. Rev. Stat. § 30.265(1), eliminates any claim against an individual agent of a public body for torts committed while acting within scope of employment or duty and substitutes a single claim against public body itself, which is subject to damage-limitation caps imposed by statute; statute does not violate Oregon constitution).

Examples of an agent's individual liability based on the agent's negligent performance of duties include _Steel Coils, Inc. v. M/V Lake Marion, 2002 A.M.C. 1680 (E.D.La.2001)_, aff'd, _331 F.3d 422 (5th Cir.2003)_, cert. denied, _540 U.S. 949 (2003)_ (general maritime negligence claim against vessel's managing agent; managing agent's negligence in maintaining vessel's hatch covers foreseeably led to ingress of sea water and damage to plaintiffs cargo); [_Omaha Indem. Co. v. Royal Am. Managers, Inc., 777 F.Supp. 1488, 1492 (W.D.Mo.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-9WJ0-001T-7159-00000-00&context=1530671) (president of reinsurance management firm personally liable to reinsurer when management firm failed to exercise due care in performing its responsibilities to reinsurer; president participated in management firm's inadequate procedures in accepting underwriting risks and monitoring underwriting business on behalf of reinsurer); [_Cherry v. Ward, 420 S.E.2d 763 (Ga.App. 1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX8-BPC0-003G-P3W8-00000-00&context=1530671) (project foreman negligently supervised day-to-day construction activities); E.G. [_Rock, Inc. v. Danly, 633 A.2d 485, 494-495 (Md.App.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-8WX0-003G-24X1-00000-00&context=1530671) (rental management firm liable to tenant assaulted by intruder for its own acts of negligence in responding to reports of suspicious activity on premises of apartment building; irrelevant that management firm acted as agent on behalf of building's owner, its disclosed principal).

Illustration 4 is based on [_Guilbeau v. Anderson, 841 S.W.2d 517 (Tex. App.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-1H20-003C-23PD-00000-00&context=1530671).

Additional examples of claims of negligent misrepresentation against agents include [_Flowers v. ERA Unique Real Estate, Inc., 227 F.Supp.2d 998, 1000-1001 (N.D.Ill. 2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4741-C750-0038-Y255-00000-00&context=1530671) (purchaser of apartment building containing hazardous lead-based paint stated claim of negligent misrepresentation against seller's agent; under Illinois law, seller's agent is subject to liability to purchaser if agent who knows or should know of latent defect fails to disclose it); [_Lombardo v. Albu, 14 P.3d 288, 291 (Ariz. 2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:426R-G330-0039-405X-00000-00&context=1530671) (trial court erroneously granted purchaser's agent's motion for summary judgment; both purchaser and agent owed duty to prospective vendor of house, after agreeing to terms of purchase, to disclose subsequent event prior to agreed closing date that jeopardized purchaser's financial condition and ability to perform contract); [_Mahler v. Keenan Real Estate, Inc., 876 P.2d 609, 617 (Kan.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-3300-003F-D13M-00000-00&context=1530671) (recognizing applicability of tort of negligent misrepresentation to real-estate agent who induces buyer to purchase real estate); [_Gross v. Sussex Inc., 630 A.2d 1156, 1169-1170 (Md. 1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-35T0-003G-20TM-00000-00&context=1530671) (genuine issues of material fact concerning whether listing agent negligently misrepresented facts about building permits to purchasers of new residence; claim of misrepresentation must be assessed in light of Md. Code Ann., Bus. Occ. & Prof. § 16-322(a)(4), which subjects a broker's license to revocation whenever the broker intentionally or negligently fails to disclose a material fact relating to the property to a person with whom the broker deals when the broker knows or should know the fact; statute may provide a basis for duty of disclosure to purchaser); [_Skowronski v. Sachs, 818 N.E.2d 635, 639 (Mass.App.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4DYY-M5B0-0039-4565-00000-00&context=1530671), rev. denied, _823 N.E.2d 782 (Mass.2005)_ (misrepresentation made by jeweler to customer concerning grade of diamond; fact that jeweler acted as agent for employer does not create shield against liability for recklessly made misstatement); [_Williams v. DeVinney, 856 P.2d 546, 550 (Mont.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-3RN0-003G-81K4-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent for seller of mobile home subject to liability to home's purchaser; agent negligently assured purchaser that installing home on concrete piers would provide adequate foundation and that ground was sound); [_Oberlin Capital, L.P. v. Slavin, 554 S.E.2d 840, 846 (N.C.App.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:44DV-1FK0-0039-44PP-00000-00&context=1530671) (president of corporate borrower not subject to liability on lender's claims of negligence and negligent misrepresentation; although president personally participated in loan negotiations and signed loan agreement, lender had opportunity to investigate and could have discovered relevant facts upon reasonable inquiry); [_Kennett v. Marquis, 798 A.2d 416, 418 (R.I.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4627-SBG0-0039-43H9-00000-00&context=1530671) (real-estate agent not subject to liability to purchaser of property on basis of misrepresentation that property did not contain wetlands; agent did not know of presence of wetlands and did not fail to exercise reasonable care in transmitting groundwater report that, while stating property had no wetlands, was also partially illegible). See also J. Jarvis & Sons Ltd. v. Castle Wharf Devs. Ltd., 2001 WL 14907 at P 53 (Ct.App.2001) (recognizing "no reason in principle why the professional agent of the employer cannot become liable to a contractor for negligent misstatements made by the agent to a contractor to induce the contractor to tender, if the contractor relies on those misstatements"; depends on all the circumstances whether a duty of care arises in a particular situation).

Illustration 5 is based on [_Glenfed Fin. Corp. v. Penick Corp., 647 A.2d 852 (N.J.Super.App.Div.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W8W0-003C-P3V5-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Deere & Co. v. Miller-Godley Auction Co., 549 S.E.2d 762, 766 (Ga. App.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:436D-WFC0-0039-42MG-00000-00&context=1530671) (auctioneer who sold farm equipment for which U.C.C. 1 financing statement had been filed is subject to liability to secured creditor for conversion); [_DCA Architects, Inc. v. American Bldg. Consultants, Inc., 417 S.E.2d 386, 389 (Ga.App.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX8-BSC0-003G-P4DR-00000-00&context=1530671) (individual liability of corporate officer based on participation in or direction of particular tortious act, not receipt of personal profit; trial court erred in directing verdict for president of construction company on plaintiffs conversion claim when president, knowing that agreement required payment to plaintiff of percentage of funds received from clients, determined that no further payments to plaintiff would be made); [_Charles Bloom & Co. v. Echo Jewelers, 652 A.2d 1238, 1243 (N.J.Super.App.Div.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W890-003C-P3KM-00000-00&context=1530671) (action for conversion of gems by corporate bailee; corporate officers liable to persons injured by officers' own torts, "even though they were acting on behalf of the corporation and their intent was to benefit the corporation"); [_Polonetsky v. Better Homes Depot, Inc., 760 N.E.2d 1274, 1278-1279 (N.Y.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:44G9-S400-0039-43KG-00000-00&context=1530671) (individual officer of corporation subject to liability for fraud under consumer-protection statute although officer did not stand to gain personally; officer participated personally in transactions and personally made fraudulent promises to customers); [_Dixon v. State, 808 S.W.2d 721, 723 (Tex.App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-22X0-003C-24B6-00000-00&context=1530671) (individual officer of corporation subject to liability to state for amount of fuel taxes collected but not paid to state; officer is liable "for any tort committed by the corporation through him, regardless of whether the officer personally benefits from the tort committed"). On the applicability of the same principle in a criminal context, see [_State v. Lang, 417 S.E.2d 808, 809 (N.C.App.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-08H0-003G-03H0-00000-00&context=1530671) (upholding conviction of corporation's president on count of obtaining property by false premises, although president claimed he received no personal benefit; court states that "\[i\]t is well established that an agent obtaining anything of value for a corporation by false premises is subject to conviction along with the corporation.").

In Standard Chartered Bank v. Pakistan Nat'l Shipping Corp., \[2003\] 1 A.C. 959, the House of Lords held that an agent is subject to liability for the agent's fraud, although it is evident to the third party that the agent acts on behalf of a principal. "No one can escape liability for his fraud by saying: 'I wish to make it clear that I am committing this fraud on behalf of someone else and I am not personally liable.'" Id. at 968 (per Lord Hoffmann). _Standard Chartered_ distinguishes Williams v. Natural Life Health Foods Ltd. \[1998\] 1 W.L.R. 830, an action for damages for negligent misrepresentation, on the basis that in _Williams_ the agent who made the statement did not assume any personal responsibility for it. See \[2003\] 1 A.C. at 968. For discussion of both cases, see Stephen Todd, Assuming Responsibility for Torts, 119 L.Q. Rev. 119 (2003).

On the basic rationale for the doctrine of an agent's individual tort liability, see, e.g., Grynberg Prod. Corp. v. British Gas, p.l.c., [_817 F.Supp. 1338, 1350-1351 (E.D.Tex.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-TJ80-001T-64N6-00000-00&context=1530671) (discussing interest of Texas in rule that does not "enable Texas residents to hide behind the corporate veil and discourage responsible individual behavior," in contrast to doctrine claimed by defendants to be law of Kazakhstan, which exculpates individual employees from liability for torts committed within the scope of employment when employer is subject to liability; court queries doctrine's applicability to conduct that is fraudulent, not negligent). The basis for the claim about Kazakhi law in _Grynberg_ was Soviet law, see [_id. at 1351_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-TJ80-001T-64N6-00000-00&context=1530671), based on a negligence case holding that tort liability ran solely against an employer. Under German law, an employee of a public body is subject to liability only when the public body itself is not and only if the employee has the status of a civil servant. See 2 Basil S. Markesinis, Always on the Same Path: Essays on Foreign Law and Comparative Methodology 233 n.36 (2001). In English law, an agent's tortious conduct subjects the agent to individual liability unless the conduct is privileged. See Francis M.B. Reynolds, Bowstead & Reynolds on Agency 530 (17th ed. 2001).

On an individual agent's availability as a source for recovery when the principal is unavailable, see, e.g., [_Oklahoma Federated Gold & Numismatics, Inc. v. Blodgett, 24 F.3d 136, 141-142 (10th Cir.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-63N0-003B-P4HR-00000-00&context=1530671) (under Oklahoma law, corporate officer liable for torts committed personally; claims against officer not subject to automatic stay imposed by [_11 U.S.C. § 362(a)(1)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:6GH3-WRK3-RRVN-0158-00000-00&context=1530671) on plaintiff's claims against corporation when it filed bankruptcy petition because stay does not extend to solvent codefendants of corporate debtor when claims against codefendant are separate from and independent of claims against corporation); [_New Crawford Valley, Ltd. v. Benedict, 877 P.2d 1363, 1368 (Colo.App.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2MH0-003D-948R-00000-00&context=1530671) (officers and directors of insolvent corporation are subject to individual liability in action brought by creditor; creditor alleged, inter alia, that defendants participated in fraudulent schemes that diverted funds owed to insolvent corporation into another corporation they controlled; creditor's prior judgment against corporation does not preclude assertion of like claims against individual defendants).

Illustrations 6 and 7 are based on Illustrations 1 and 2 to Restatement Second, Agency § 362.

On relationships between contract and tort law, see [_Husky Spray Serv., Inc. v. Patzer, 471 N.W.2d 146, 154-155 (S.D.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S71-2FP0-003G-D0TH-00000-00&context=1530671) (president of corporate owner of airplane who represented to buyer that it was "ready to go" not subject to liability on purchaser's contract claim for breach of express warranty because sale contract did not impose individual liability on seller's president; recovery denied on tort claims against president on basis that purchaser did not establish that representation was made fraudulently).

An employee or other agent who commits a tort while acting within the scope of employment may not be covered by an insurance policy that provides coverage for the employer or principal if the policy excludes coverage of employees. See [_Continental Cas. Co. v. Godur, 476 So. 2d 242 (Fla.Dist.App.1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-5YF0-003C-X50T-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurer has cross-claim for indemnity against employee who injured plaintiff while driving within the scope of employment; insurer settled with plaintiff for full amount of policy issued to employer; although insurer may not maintain action for indemnity against its own insured, policy that covered employer's liability excluded coverage of employees for injuries arising out of automobile accidents).

_c. Constitution, statutes, regulations, and ordinances._ Cases discussing individual agents' liability in the context of statutes that create an express cause of action include [_United States v. Bestfoods, 524 U.S. 51 (1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SWJ-DTV0-004B-Y002-00000-00&context=1530671) (liability for site cleanup costs under CERCLA, [_42 U.S.C. § 9601 et seq._](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SHT-0722-D6RV-H52W-00000-00&context=1530671), as operator of facility); [_Leddy v. Standard Drywall, Inc., 875 F.2d 383, 388 (2d Cir.1989)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-BVT0-003B-545F-00000-00&context=1530671) (liability under ERISA § 502, [_29 U.S.C. § 1132_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:67F7-77M3-CGX8-037W-00000-00&context=1530671), for employer's contributions owed to employee fund; individual corporate officer who defrauds or conspires to defraud fund is subject to liability); [_Mone v. Dranow, 945 F.2d 306, 308 (9th Cir.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-88T0-008H-V4FK-00000-00&context=1530671) (liability to consumer for actual and punitive damages for obtaining credit report for purpose impermissible under Fair Credit Reporting Act, _15 U.S.C. § 1681b_; although defendant claimed he obtained credit report on behalf of corporation to determine whether corporation's former employee would be able to satisfy judgment in unfair-competition suit brought by former employer, defendant is subject to personal liability on principle that "both California corporate law and federal common law hold that corporate officers are personally liable for their torts even if the torts were committed on behalf of the corporation."); [_Caterpillar, Inc. v. Jerryco Footwear, Inc., 880 F.Supp. 578, 589 (C.D.Ill.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-75J0-003B-V3HG-00000-00&context=1530671) (conspiracy liability under RICO, [_18 U.S.C. § 1962(c)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SG9-5042-D6RV-H0PV-00000-00&context=1530671)\-(d); upholding grant of preliminary injunction against corporation and individual officers who participated in fraudulent scheme to divert licensing royalties owed to plaintiff). See also [_Rodgers v. Richmond Memory Gardens, Inc., 896 S.W.2d 64, 69 (Mo. App.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-4N00-003F-C4TB-00000-00&context=1530671) (president of cemetery corporation in charge of day-to-day management subject to individual liability when corporation failed to make payments into perpetual-care fund for cemetery as required by statute, Mo. Rev. Stat. §§ 214.270--214.410).

On agents' liability under federal copyright, patent, trademark, and unfair-competition statutes, see [_Donsco, Inc. v. Casper Corp., 587 F.2d 602, 606 (3d Cir.1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-X7H0-0039-M2RB-00000-00&context=1530671) (liability to competitor under Lanham Act, [_15 U.S.C. § 1125(a)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8S9D-W4W2-8T6X-7177-00000-00&context=1530671), for acts of unfair competition; corporate officer who authorized and approved acts of unfair competition is subject to individual liability); [_Hoover v. Custom Metalcraft, Inc., 84 F.3d 1408, 1412 (Fed.Cir.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-KT10-003N-44SF-00000-00&context=1530671) (to impose individual liability on corporate officer for patent infringement requires either showing sufficient to pierce corporate veil or that officer "act\[ed\] culpably in that officer must actively and knowingly assist with the corporation's infringement"); Dixon v. Atlantic Recording Corp., 1985 WL 3049 at \*2 (S.D.N.Y.1985) (collecting and licensing agent for owner and publisher of song "Whole Lotta Love" subject to individual liability in copyright-infringement action brought by copyright owner of song; apart from "intricacies of agency law," a person "who promotes or induces an infringement can be held liable as a 'vicarious infringer,' even though he has no actual knowledge that a copyright is being violated, if (1) he has the right and ability to control or supervise the infringing activity, and (2) he has a direct financial interest in the exploitation of the copyrighted materials").

On individual liability in the context of administrative penalties, see, e.g., [_State ex rel. Webster v. Missouri Res. Recovery, Inc., 825 S.W.2d 916, 926 (Mo.App.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-7N50-003F-C2H5-00000-00&context=1530671) (civil penalties for violations of state hazardous-waste-management act; president of corporation, who participated in "'hands-on'" style in all of corporation's activities, subject to individual liability); _In re Recycling & Salvage Corp., 586 A.2d 1300, 1316 (N.J.Super.App.Div.1991)_ (principals of corporations who ran waste-transfer station and made no effort to halt its illegal operations subject to individual liability for monetary penalties assessed by state board of public utilities); [_N.S. Sportswear, Inc. v. State, 819 S.W.2d 230, 232-233 (Tex.App. 1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-1X20-003C-22D0-00000-00&context=1530671) (statutory liability for amount of use taxes collected but not paid to state, and common-law liability for conversion; individual corporate officer's liability limited to amount he collected or converted).

On individual liability for violation of an ordinance, see [_Longenecker v. Commonwealth, 596 A.2d 1261, 1263 (Pa.Commw.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-1310-003C-S1B2-00000-00&context=1530671) (president and sole shareholder of bankrupt corporation cited for violations of municipal ordinance for failing to cut weeds and grass on corporation's property and for failing to secure a building; liability may not be imposed on a corporate officer on the basis of officer's managerial position, and no evidence officer intentionally neglected property or otherwise participated in wrongdoing).

For examples of individual liability in the context of statutes that impose strict liability on violators, see, e.g., [_Commissioner v. RLG, Inc., 755 N.E.2d 556 (Ind.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:442R-JBC0-0039-42C6-00000-00&context=1530671) (individual who was sole shareholder, director, and officer of corporation that operated landfill subject to individual liability for civil penalties imposed for landfill's violations of state environmental-management act; individual was directly involved in corporate activities, ordering landfill's manager to fill garbage outside permitted contours of site; corporation had no subordinate officer principally responsible for environmental compliance; individual listed his own name as "Responsible Party" in permit when statute required designation of such a party); [_In re Dougherty, 482 N.W.2d 485, 490 (Minn.App.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-7C00-003F-V1B1-00000-00&context=1530671) (administrative penalty order issued by commissioner of state pollution-control agency following ongoing violations of regulations applicable to handling of hazardous waste; corporation's president and principal shareholder, who did not personally handle hazardous materials, subject to liability as corporate officer with responsible relationship to violations because he was in a position of responsibility as corporation's president and its emergency coordinator, the violations were "clearly within his sphere of influence and involvement," and he "failed to prevent the violations and to take proper corrective action once the violations occurred").

On whether individual liability is consistent with a statute, see, e.g., [_Lenhardt v. Basic Inst. of Tech., Inc., 55 F.3d 377, 381 (8th Cir.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3D-8HY0-001T-D1T1-00000-00&context=1530671) (noting that other circuits do not characterize a supervisory employee as an "employer" for Title VII purposes, and predicting similar construction by Missouri Supreme Court of state statute); [_Otanez v. Blue Skies Mobile Home Park, 3 Cal.Rptr.2d 210, 212-213 (Cal.App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-H400-003D-J52K-00000-00&context=1530671) (managers of mobile-home park who caused termination of tenant's utility services not subject to individual liability under statute prohibiting willful termination by landlords; managers not subject to liability under Civ. Code § 2343 because their acts were not "wrongful in their nature"). See also [_Rehm v. Baltimore Storage Co., 300 F.Supp.2d 408, 417 (W.D.Va.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4BMH-X3K0-0038-Y31X-00000-00&context=1530671) ([_49 U.S.C. § 13907_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SDD-0S82-8T6X-711N-00000-00&context=1530671), by providing that motor carrier furnishing transportation for household goods "shall be responsible for all acts or omissions of any of its agents which relate to the performance of household goods transportation services" permits dismissal of agent allegedly responsible for damage to shipper's real property).

Illustration 8 is based on [_Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:47ST-7B70-004C-000P-00000-00&context=1530671). For developments on remand, see [_Holley v. Crank, 400 F.3d 667 (9th Cir.2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4FN6-TKF0-0038-X196-00000-00&context=1530671) (remanding to district court for further proceedings; as designated broker/officer of real-estate corporation, its sole shareholder had personal duty to supervise salesperson).
