For examples of statutory language making an agent's status as such a basis for liability, or otherwise varying the common-law rule, see [_46 U.S.C. § 12507(d)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SDD-0M72-D6RV-H3D3-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[i\]f a person, not an individual, is involved in a violation \[relating to a vessel identification system\], the president or chief executive of the person is also subject to any penalty provided in this section"); [_15 U.S.C. § 24_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8S9D-W4V2-8T6X-74YF-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[w\]henever a corporation shall violate any of the . . . antitrust laws, such violation shall be deemed to be also that of the individual directors, officers, or agents of such corporation who shall have authorized, ordered, or done any of the acts constituting in whole or in part such violation"; predecessor statute discussed in [_United States v. Wise, 370 U.S. 405, 411-414 (1962))_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-H880-003B-S01V-00000-00&context=1530671).

Related questions may arise in the context of criminal liability. See, e.g., [_United States v. Doig, 950 F.2d 411, 415 (7th Cir.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-72X0-008H-V396-00000-00&context=1530671) (employee not subject to liability as aider and abettor of employer's criminal violation of OSHA, [_29 U.S.C. § 666(e)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SG9-5TK2-8T6X-7079-00000-00&context=1530671); court holds it is "logically inconsistent to hold corporation criminally liable because of the acts of its agent, and, at the same time to hold the agent liable for aiding and abetting the corporation"); [_Sea Horse Ranch, Inc. v. Superior Court, 30 Cal.Rptr.2d 681 (Cal. App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-G520-003D-J13X-00000-00&context=1530671) (individual officers of corporation that owned ranch charged with involuntary manslaughter when horses escaped from enclosure and caused fatal collision on nearby highway; corporate officer subject to individual criminal liability for negligent homicide when officer had personal knowledge and awareness of risks created by ranch's horses, including prior escapes and defective fence); [_Kuriansky v. Azam, 573 N.Y.S.2d 369, 373 (Sup.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S2R-8HC0-003V-B077-00000-00&context=1530671) (individual corporate officer subject to liability for criminal contempt when corporation fails to comply with grand-jury subpoena only when officer personally participates in corporation's wilful disobedience; "in New York . . . courts are not free to create criminal liability of an individual for the failure of a corporation to comply with an order unless specifically provided by statute. Absent statutory authority the court cannot create personal liability of an individual for the acts or conduct or omission of a corporation.").

On statutes that define violations in terms that differ from related common-law torts, see, e.g., [_People v. Apple Health & Sports Clubs, Ltd., 613 N.Y.S.2d 868, 869 (App.Div.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S2R-6SG0-003V-B28X-00000-00&context=1530671) (special proceeding against health club and its shareholders and officers under Executive Law § 63(12); statute authorizes proceeding against any person or business engaging in repeated fraudulent or illegal conduct and defines fraud "so as to include acts characterized as dishonest or misleading and eliminating the necessity of proof of an intent to defraud"; corporate officer who participated in commission of fraud or had actual knowledge of misrepresentation subject to liability under statute for any money owed to consumers and for civil fines owed to State); see also [_United States v. Jorgensen, 144 F.3d 550, 559 (8th Cir.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SMY-M510-0038-X1N2-00000-00&context=1530671), abrogated on other grounds by [_Neder v. United States, 527 U.S. 1 (1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WNV-2M50-004C-2006-00000-00&context=1530671) (criminal convictions for misbranding meat under Federal Meat Inspection Act, [_21 U.S.C. § 601(c)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SDD-0C82-8T6X-73DT-00000-00&context=1530671), which prohibits any "person, firm or corporation" from distributing meat in interstate commerce that is misbranded "in any particular"; corporate officers subject to conviction if officer had an intent to defraud, and either personally participated in misbranding activity or had a "responsible relationship" within the company concerning it).

_d. Agent's own tortious conduct._ For the statement that respondeat inferior is not a recognized principle, see [_Speer v. Taira Lynn Marine, Inc., 116 F.Supp.2d 826, 830 (S.D.Tex. 2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:41HR-7RC0-0038-Y1PB-00000-00&context=1530671) (plaintiff, who attended training course on barge, may not assert claim of unseaworthiness against agent of plaintiffs employer); see also [_United Magazines Co. v. Murdoch Magazines Distribution, Inc., 353 F.Supp.2d 433, 441 (S.D.N.Y.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4F5K-GPK0-TVW3-P29T-00000-00&context=1530671) (rule that even if principal is undisclosed, agent not subject to liability for principal's torts applicable to claim under Robinson-Patman Act, [_15 U.S.C. § 13(a)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8S9D-W4V2-8T6X-74XJ-00000-00&context=1530671)); [_Loeb v. United States, 793 F.Supp. 431, 437 (E.D.N.Y.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-KN30-008H-F19B-00000-00&context=1530671) (under New York law, independent travel or booking agent "cannot be held responsible for the negligence of its principal" when agent only makes reservation or packages a tour).

For the point that an agent is not subject to liability for torts committed by the agent's predecessor in a position, see [_Mallia v. PaineWebber, Inc., 889 F. Supp. 277, 280 (S.D.Tex. 1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-HPM0-001T-544V-00000-00&context=1530671).

For formulations of the general point, see, e.g., [_McCaskey v. Continental Airlines, Inc., 159 F.Supp.2d 562, 577 (S.D.Tex.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:43VC-7FB0-0038-Y4DS-00000-00&context=1530671) (granting summary judgment in favor of president of airline on which plaintiff's deceased spouse suffered stroke during trip; although president was also passenger on allegedly overheated plane on which decedent suffered stroke, president would be liable only if plaintiff establishes he owed decedent "an independent duty of reasonable care," but no credible evidence tending to show president had any role in actions of plane's crew); [_Camacho v. 1440 Rhode Island Ave. Corp., 620 A.2d 242, 247-248 (D.C.App.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRT-8V10-003G-11M3-00000-00&context=1530671) (officer of corporate landlord may be subject to individual liability for wrongful eviction of tenant; such liability "is based on wrongful acts by the individual being held accountable. . . . \[t\]he relevant inquiry is whether the individual committed the tort, or at least participated to such an extent that it can be said that he is responsible for a significant share of the commission of the tort").

On individual liability when an agent directs conduct by another, see, e.g., [_Schwartz v. Pillsbury, Inc., 969 F.2d 840, 843-844 (9th Cir.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-2840-008H-V40H-00000-00&context=1530671) (genuine issues of material fact concerning whether former president of franchisor subject to individual liability for fraud in franchisees' action; former president swore to truth of franchise offering statements, and a reasonable jury could infer she tacitly consented to providing franchisees with false information when former president knew that vice president made representations concerning franchise's potential earnings and knew representations to be unsupported by data); [_People ex rel. Hartigan v. E & E Hauling, Inc., 607 N.E.2d 165, 179 (Ill.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-1TT0-003D-H30H-00000-00&context=1530671) (fraud action brought by state's attorney general based on public contractors' alleged misrepresentations in affidavits of compliance with minority-enterprise requirements; contractor's vice-president may be subject to liability when complaint alleges he assisted in preparation of affidavit and "'knew or was reckless in not knowing that the information was incorrect'" and he "'instituted the policies complained of in this case and has been and continues to be responsible for establishing, supervising, directing, and controlling \[corporations'\] business activities and practices'"); [_Tedrow v. Deskin, 290 A.2d 799, 802-803 (Md.1972)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-6SX0-003G-2431-00000-00&context=1530671) (reversing grant of summary judgment for corporation's officers and employees who sold used car to plaintiff allegedly knowing that odometer had been altered; officer subject to liability if officer specifically directed that particular act be done or participated or cooperated in it); see also [_Piper Jaffray Cos. v. National Union Fire Ins. Co., 967 F.Supp. 1148, 1158 (D.Minn.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RR5-XC00-00B1-F1DB-00000-00&context=1530671) (under Minnesota law, corporate officer is responsible for torts of employees in which officer participates, or which officer directs, or negligently fails to prevent; complaint, read broadly, does not preclude possibility that officers may have negligently failed to prevent torts committed by other officers or employees).

For the point that an officer's individual liability is distinct from piercing the corporate veil, see, e.g., [_Lambert v. Kazinetz, 250 F.Supp.2d 908, 913 (S.D.Ohio 2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:484N-P980-0038-Y3RB-00000-00&context=1530671); [_State ex rel. Stephan v. Commemorative Servs. Corp., 823 P.2d 831, 840 (Kan.App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-4360-003F-D1XS-00000-00&context=1530671); [_In re Dougherty, 482 N.W.2d 485, 491-492 (Minn.App.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-7C00-003F-V1B1-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Jackson's Marina Inc. v. Jorling, 597 N.Y.S.2d 749, 752 (App.Div.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S2R-7G80-003V-B1SD-00000-00&context=1530671). For a discussion of this point in the broader context of corporate liability, see Robert B. Thompson, Unpacking Limited Liability: Direct and Vicarious Liability of Corporate Participants for Torts of the Enterprise, [_47 Vand. L. Rev. 1, 8 (1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S0M-D7N0-00CW-709C-00000-00&context=1530671). Professor Thompson notes that "as to direct participation, the corporate shield really only works for contracts, a narrowness of protection that may surprise many entrepreneurs who intend to use corporations to avoid liability." The surprise "probably arises because piercing the corporate veil dominates the legal discussion of the limited liability issue. . . . In close corporations in which the same participants serve as both managers and shareholders, a court's decision to pierce the veil usually results in liability being directly imposed on managers, although the language of the decision may suggest liability as shareholders. In that sense, the failure of piercing cases to unpack the passive and active participants means that piercing cases arising in tort may place liability on managers while appearing to place liability on shareholders." Id. at 7, n.29. A corporation's direct liability is also distinct from whether the corporate veil should be pierced. See [_United States v. Bestfoods, 524 U.S. 51, 65 (1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SWJ-DTV0-004B-Y002-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporation may be subject to liability under CERCLA as operator of polluting facility; "whereas the rules of veil piercing limit derivative liability for the actions of another corporation, CERCLA's 'operator' provision is concerned primarily with direct liability for one's own actions.").

On the individual liability of corporate officers, see, e.g., [_Itofca, Inc. v. Hellhake, 8 F.3d 1202, 1205 (7th Cir. 1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-BN60-003B-P2G8-00000-00&context=1530671) (tortious interference with lease; president of corporation was sole person involved in negotiations that constituted interference with plaintiff's lease); [_Manville Sales Corp. v. Paramount Sys., Inc., 917 F.2d 544, 553 (Fed.Cir.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-JCF0-0039-V0BD-00000-00&context=1530671) (standard for liability for patent infringement under [_35 U.S.C. § 271(b)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SCB-BHS2-8T6X-734N-00000-00&context=1530671) requires that alleged infringer knowingly induce infringement; defendant officers not aware of patent until after suit filed); [_Braintree Labs., Inc. v. Nephro--Tech, Inc., 81 F.Supp.2d 1122, 1132 (D.Kan.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YHF-01S0-0038-Y40K-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_15 Fed. Appx. 799 (Fed.Cir.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:43KH-C5N0-003B-92Y8-00000-00&context=1530671) (patent infringement; as sole person in charge of corporation's day-to-day operations, defendant was aware of existence of plaintiff's patent and continued to promote sale of infringing product despite knowledge of plaintiff's belief that sales infringed patent); [_Clarke v. Max Advisors, LLC, 235 F.Supp.2d 130, 150 (N.D.N.Y.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:47HH-T5C0-0038-Y20C-00000-00&context=1530671) (fraud; officers of corporation who allegedly made material misrepresentations to plaintiffs are subject to liability, as is corporation on whose behalf they acted); [_PMC, Inc. v. Kadisha, 93 Cal.Rptr.2d 663, 678 (Cal.App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YT5-RWV0-0039-41C1-00000-00&context=1530671) (liability for misappropriation of trade secrets; officers and directors who did not personally use trade secrets subject to liability when they "must have known or had reason to know of the misappropriation and then unreasonably participated in the unlawful conduct"; liability may also be based on acquisition of corporation "whose sole assets were purportedly corruptly acquired resources" when defendants knew or had reason to know of tortious misappropriation); [_Kerns v. G.A.C., Inc., 875 P.2d 949, 958 (Kan.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-3350-003F-D14J-00000-00&context=1530671) (negligence in maintaining swimming pool on premises of mobile-home park; officers of corporate owner of park either had or assumed responsibility to maintain pool properly); [_Beri, Inc. v. Salishan Props., Inc., 580 P.2d 173 (Or.1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-X9J0-003F-Y3HM-00000-00&context=1530671) (negligence in development of oceanside property; officers of development corporation participated actively in development activities underlying plaintiffs' claim of negligence, including acquisition, planning, development, promotion, and leasing of lots). For evaluation of the merits of imposing vicarious liability on a corporation's chief officer, see Timothy P. Glynn, Beyond "Unlimiting" Shareholder Liability: Vicarious Tort Liability for Corporate Officers, [_57 Vand. L. Rev. 329, 396-433 (2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4CW4-6W60-00CW-705M-00000-00&context=1530671).

On liability for failing to take action, see, e.g., [_Gray v. Beverly Enters.-Mississippi, Inc., 390 F.3d 400, 410 (5th Cir.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4DS3-2PF0-0038-X54V-00000-00&context=1530671) (under Mississippi law, managers of nursing home may be subject to liability as direct participants in tortious conduct "by merely authorizing or negligently failing to remedy misconduct by one's subordinates"); Bienaime v. Kitzman, 2000 WL 381932, at \*3 (E.D.La.2000) (visitor to apartment complex killed in elevator shaft; motion to add manager of complex as defendant granted, court noting that standard for liability is whether defendant owed duty of care to third person, delegated to defendant by principal or employer, and defendant breached duty through personal fault by failing to discharge duty "whether such failure be due to malfeasance, misfeasance, or nonfeasance, including when the failure results from not acting upon actual knowledge of the risk to others as well as from a lack of ordinary care in discovering and avoiding such risk of harm which has resulted from the breach of the duty"); Skelton v. Chemical Leaman Tank Lines, Inc., 1996 WL 278343, at \*9 (Conn.Super.1996) (liability of corporation's employees following explosion on its premises; "the plaintiffs allege that they had responsibility over certain equipment, substances, and personnel who worked with these substances and equipment and their failure to take certain steps regarding the supervisory role resulted in the discharge of contaminants which caused injury to the plaintiffs").

Illustration 9 is based on [_Schaefer v. D & J Produce, Inc., 403 N.E.2d 1015 (Ohio App.1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-TF70-0054-C404-00000-00&context=1530671). For similar facts, see [_Peters v. Frey, 429 S.W.2d 847, 849 (Ky.App.1968)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX8-CFR0-003F-P3K4-00000-00&context=1530671) (defendant in charge of corporation's truck fleet failed to determine whether truck that injured plaintiff was in good working order).

Illustrations 11, 12, and 13 are based on Illustrations 3 and 4 to Restatement Second, Agency § 348. On the point made by Illustration 13, see [_Lee Hawkins Realty, Inc. v. Moss, 724 So. 2d 1116, 1118 (Miss.App.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3VCC-T5Y0-0039-43V7-00000-00&context=1530671) (although agent who represented vendors of house was entitled to rely on vendor's statement that house was covered by a warranty, agent would be subject to liability to purchasers if agent knew house was not covered by warranty but "assists in the deceit"; agent left closing to investigate when questions arose about warranty, then later nodded her head when paralegal with law firm representing vendors stated to purchasers that existence of warranty had been verified).

_e. Privileged conduct._ For the general points on the delegability of a principal's privilege to an agent, see [_Carvin v. Arkansas Power & Light Co., 1991 WL 540481 (W.D.Ark.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:594N-SN71-F04C-R07C-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent of utility entitled to protection of flowage easements held by utility).

In many cases involving an agent's liability for tortious interference with the principal's contract or prospective contractual relation with a third party, the plaintiff who alleges interference is a former employee of a common principal or employer. Courts vary in how they formulate the elements of privilege in these cases. Compare [_Pegram v. Hebding, 667 So. 2d 696, 701 (Ala.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-WNF0-003C-90FC-00000-00&context=1530671) (officer or employee not subject to liability "unless that person was acting outside the scope of employment and with actual malice at the time of the interference"); [_Nordling v. Northern States Power Co., 478 N.W.2d 498, 506 (Minn.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-49V0-003F-V0T1-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporate officer or agent subject to liability only if action is outside scope of duties to corporation; actions of officer or agent who terminates an employee's employment or causes it to be terminated are actions of corporation, and subjecting officer or agent to individual liability would chill "company personnel in the performance of their duties."); and [_Nelson v. WEB Water Dev. Ass'n, Inc., 507 N.W.2d 691, 700 (S.D.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S71-2CP0-003G-D0B7-00000-00&context=1530671) (same) with [_Winkler v. V.G. Reed & Sons, Inc., 638 N.E.2d 1228, 1236 (Ind.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-1T40-003F-X0X0-00000-00&context=1530671) (officers of corporation that purchased assets of plaintiff's employer not subject to liability for interfering with plaintiff's 15-year employment contract when purchasing corporation did not adopt it; defendants did not engage in conduct that was otherwise wrongful and defendants' conduct was "fair and reasonable under the circumstances" as required by Restatement Second, Torts § 767, Comment _j_); [_Ettenson v. Burke, 17 P.3d 440, 449 (N.M.App. 2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:426B-H8G0-0039-44TS-00000-00&context=1530671) (CEO subject to liability only if plaintiff establishes that, in summarily terminating plaintiff's employment and failing to provide severance pay, CEO did not act "in good faith in furtherance of corporate goals" but "use\[d\] corporate power to serve . . . own, personal ends"); and [_Forrester v. Stockstill, 869 S.W.2d 328, 334-335 (Tenn.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-3JM0-003F-92W2-00000-00&context=1530671) (when corporate officer, director, or employee "acts within the general range of his authority, and his actions are substantially motivated by an intent to further the interest of the corporation," officer, director, or employee's action "is considered to be the action of the corporation and is entitled to the same immunity from liability").

On tortious-interference claims apart from employment disputes, see [_Geolar, Inc. v. Gilbert/Commonwealth Inc., 874 P.2d 937, 948 (Alaska 1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-W5W0-003C-G0KM-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent has qualified privilege to interfere with contract between third party and principal "so long as he is predominantly motivated by a desire to protect his principal's economic interest," a question to be resolved by trier of fact); [_Citylink Group, Ltd. v. Hyatt Corp., 729 N.E.2d 869, 877 (Ill. App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:408B-Y8G0-0039-40K7-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporate officers, directors, and shareholders are "normally" privileged against tortious-interference claims as to third party's relationship with principal; privilege may be overcome by showing defendant acted "in its own interests and contrary to the interests of the principal, or engage\[d\] in conduct totally unrelated to or antagonistic to the interest giving rise to the privilege"); 9 to [_5 Fashions, Inc. v. Spurney, 538 So. 2d 228, 231 (La.1989)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-4720-008T-X09G-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporate officer privileged to induce corporation to breach contract or to make its performance more burdensome "provided that the officer does not exceed the scope of his authority or knowingly commit acts that are adverse to the interests of his corporation"); [_Bossuyt v. Osage Farmers Nat'l Bank, 360 N.W.2d 769, 778 (Iowa 1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2JW0-003G-509G-00000-00&context=1530671) (director or stockholder privileged to interfere with corporation's contract so long as improper means are not used and action is in best interests of corporation); [_Wilson v. McClenny, 136 S.E.2d 569, 578 (N.C.1964)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-YF20-000G-K068-00000-00&context=1530671) (same); [_Wiekhorst Bros. Excavating & Equip. Co. v. Ludewig, 529 N.W.2d 33, 40 (Neb. 1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-4C20-003D-B0RC-00000-00&context=1530671) (design engineers employed by corporation recommended that plaintiff's contract be terminated by public contracting authority and that their firm withdraw from second project unless contract with plaintiff was terminated; court holds that absent a showing of malice or bad faith, design professionals are protected from claims of tortious interference); [_Welch v. Bancorp Mgmt. Advisors, Inc., 679 P.2d 866 (Or.1984)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-X470-003F-Y36C-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent who serves as financial adviser holds qualified privilege comparable to that of corporate officer or employee and is privileged, within scope of employment and with intent to further principal's best interests, to interfere with or induce breach of principal's contracts or business relations; trial court properly granted summary judgment for adviser when no evidence adviser acted outside scope of authority in advising real-estate investment trust and no genuine issue of material fact that advice was given with intent of benefiting trust); [_Holloway v. Skinner, 898 S.W.2d 793, 794-796 (Tex.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W560-003C-20B4-00000-00&context=1530671) (president and shareholder of corporation subject to individual liability by forcing corporation to default on its contractual obligations to plaintiff; evidence supported verdict for plaintiff on basis that defendant did not act in good faith in best interest of corporation; fact that defendant increased his own salary at time of default suggests funds were available to pay plaintiff; burden on plaintiff to establish that agent acted in manner "so contrary to the corporation's best interests that his actions could only have been motivated by personal interests").

Civil-conspiracy claims against a corporation and its agents also meet the obstacle that acts of a corporate agent acting within the scope of employment are acts of the corporation. See [_Black v. Bank of Am. N.T. & S.A., 35 Cal.Rptr.2d 725, 728 (Cal. App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-FXM0-003D-J06D-00000-00&context=1530671) (debtors alleged civil conspiracy among bank and its officers when bank failed to renew loans or grant new loans; when acts of corporate employee are within course of employment and on behalf of corporation, "there is no entity apart from the employee with whom the employee can conspire"). The scope of intracorporate immunity is subject to limitations. See e [_Plus Tech., Inc. v. Aboud, 313 F.3d 166, 179-180 (4th Cir.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:47C4-MKF0-0038-X0BH-00000-00&context=1530671) (intracorporate immunity not applicable when corporate agent has an "independent personal stake" in illegal conduct independent of relationship to corporation; individual who owned and controlled corporation used in scheme to defraud suppliers of computer equipment had personal stake in scheme, having organized corporation with objective of sending it into bankruptcy).

For the point that an agent's interference with contracts or prospective contractual relations between third parties is not privileged, see [_American Airlines v. Christensen, 967 F.2d 410, 417 (10th Cir.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-34Y0-008H-V2V7-00000-00&context=1530671) (officers of brokerage company that dealt in airline frequent-flyer awards contrary to award terms are subject to individual liability for tortiously interfering with airline's contracts with awardees; brokerage business was established by individual defendants to interfere with known contracts between airlines and frequent flyers and to misappropriate airline's services by purchasing and reselling award certificates that by their terms prohibited sale).

Some courts use "immunity" language interchangeably with "privilege" in the context of an agent's individual liability for tortious interference. See, e.g., [_Sun Drilling Prods. Corp. v. Rayborn, 798 So. 2d 1141, 1155 (La.App.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:44BD-H020-0039-41X1-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[t\]here are strong reasons that corporate officers should enjoy immunity from liability for intentional interference committed within the scope of corporate authority for the corporation's benefit. . . . However, the officer's privilege should not be absolute, it should be limited by the purpose for which it is granted. . . .").

On an agent's conditional privilege against liability for publishing a defamatory statement, see [_Hern v. Bankers Life Cas. Co., 133 F.Supp.2d 1130, 1137-1138 (D.Minn.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:42JJ-4C40-0038-Y0H5-00000-00&context=1530671) (employment reference given by former employee's supervisor; conditional privilege may be overcome by showing common-law malice consisting of actual ill will or intent to injure plaintiff wantonly or without cause).

On the availability of a principal's immunity to an agent, see [_Butters v. Vance Int'l, Inc., 225 F.3d 462, 466 (4th Cir.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:415Y-HKS0-0038-X0JX-00000-00&context=1530671) (court notes "well-settled" proposition that "contractors and common-law agents acting within the scope of their employment for the United States have derivative sovereign immunity"; court extends protection to private agents of foreign sovereigns on basis that "\[a\]ll sovereigns need flexibility to hire private agents to aid them in conducting their governmental functions," and on foreign sovereigns' "lack of human resources" within the United States); [_PYCA Indus., Inc. v. Harrison County Waste Water Mgmt. Dist., 177 F.3d 351, 378-379 (5th Cir.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WY3-0Y60-0038-X55M-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent should not be liable for committing act when principal would not be liable if act committed directly by principal; general contractor for wastewater-management district enjoys same sovereign immunity as district for alleged wrongs committed pursuant to district's instructions or in accord with public representative's directions to perform essential part of contract).

On the relevance of statutory construction, see [_Envirologix Corp. v. City of Waukesha, 531 N.W.2d 357, 365 (Wis.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-XDD0-003G-329W-00000-00&context=1530671) (statute confers sovereign immunity on volunteer fire departments and municipalities for intentional torts committed by their officers, officials, and agents; concluding that statute extends protection to agents only for "acts done in the exercise of legislative, quasi-legislative, or judicial functions," court finds insufficient evidence to warrant designating independent contractor as an "agent" for this purpose when contractor performed its duties negligently); [_Proctor v. North Shore Cmty. Arts Found., 713 N.E.2d 969, 974 (Mass. App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3X1V-X3K0-0039-42W3-00000-00&context=1530671) (charitable immunity available to arts foundation would not protect foundation's wholly owned for-profit subsidiary from claim that it negligently placed and maintained umbrella in outdoor picnic area it operated, even if subsidiary characterized as agent of foundation; state statute abolishes defense of charitable immunity for torts committed in the course of primarily commercial activities even when purpose is to obtain revenue to be used for charitable purpose, see Mass. Gen. L. Ann., ch. 231, § 85K). See also _Thames Shipyard & Repair Co. v. United States, 350 F.3d 247 (1st Cir.2003)_, cert. denied, _542 U.S. 905 (2004)_ (implied discretionary-function exception to consent to suit under 46 U.S.C. §§ 742 and 781 protects Coast Guard's forcible evacuation of master from ship, given powers of Coast Guard under 14 U.S.C. § 88 to "perform any and all acts necessary to rescue and aid persons"; discretionary-function exception does not extend to Coast Guard's interference with efforts of private salvor).

An agent's immunity to civil liability does not shield the principal from liability based on respondeat superior for an act by an agent committed in the course of employment. See [_Napier v. Town of Windham, 187 F.3d 177, 191 (1st Cir.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3X4S-D2W0-0038-X3J5-00000-00&context=1530671).

If an agent is not subject to liability to a plaintiff in a civil action because a workers'-compensation statute creates an exclusive remedy against the agent, the agent's immunity to civil suit releases the agent's principal as well. See [_Plock v. Crossroads Joint Venture, 475 N.W.2d 105, 113 (Neb.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-4P70-003D-B200-00000-00&context=1530671), overruled on other grounds, [_558 N.W.2d 35 (Neb.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-4780-003D-B06F-00000-00&context=1530671); see generally Arthur Larson & Lex B. Larson, Larson's Worker's Compensation Law (1997).

On vicarious immunity in English law, see Francis M.B. Reynolds, Bowstead & Reynolds on Agency 533-534 (17th ed. 2001) (in general, English law does not recognize rule making principal's immunity created by contract applicable to agent).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 7.02_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8X0-00YG-400F-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 7- Torts--Liability of Agent and Principal_ > _Topic 1- Agent's Liability_**

**§ 7.02 Duty to Principal; Duty to Third Party**

**An agent's breach of a duty owed to the principal is not an independent basis for the agent's tort liability to a third party. An agent is subject to tort liability to a third party harmed by the agent's conduct only when the agent's conduct breaches a duty that the agent owes to the third party.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section states when conduct by an agent that breaches a duty owed to the agent's principal also constitutes a tort against a third party. Comment _b_ provides a general overview. Comment _c_ discusses an agent's liability when the agent's conduct causes physical harm to a third person or to tangible property of a third person. Comment _d_ discusses an agent's liability when the agent's conduct causes only economic loss to a third person.

The duties that an agent owes a principal are stated in Chapter 8, §§ 8.01-8.12. For the elements of specific torts and other aspects of tort liability, see Restatement Second, Torts, and Restatement Third, Torts. On the liability of a lawyer to a nonclient, see Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers §§ 51 (duty of care), 56 (liability under general law), and 57 (defenses and exceptions to liability).

_b. In general._ Conduct by an agent that breaches a duty the agent owes the principal may subject the agent to tort liability to a third party who is harmed as a consequence of the agent's conduct. If an agent acts contrary to the principal's instructions or otherwise acts without actual authority, and the principal suffers loss as a result, the agent has breached the agent's duty to the principal and is subject to liability to the principal. See § 8.09, Comment _b._ An agent also owes the principal a duty of care and is subject to liability to the principal for harm caused by conduct that breaches the duty. See § 8.08, Comment _b._

However, conduct that breaches an agent's duties to the principal does not always, additionally, subject the agent to liability to a third party although the agent's conduct also harms the third party. An agent is subject to liability to a third party only when the agent's conduct breaches a duty that the agent owes the third party. The duty may be derived from tort law, from a contract between the third party and the principal when the agent is a party to the contract, from a promise made by the agent to the principal for which the third party is an intended third-party beneficiary, or from the agent's assumption of duties toward the third person that are independent of the duties the agent owes the principal. In appropriate circumstances, a court may determine that a duty to a principal imposed on an agent by a statute creates a cause of action on behalf of a third party injured by the agent's breach of duty when the statute is designed to protect a class inclusive of the third party. See Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 14, Comment _g_ (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005).

Tortious conduct may consist of an omission or a failure to take action as well as an action. See Restatement Second, Torts § 6. For further discussion of this point in the context of an agent's individual liability, see § 7.01, Comment _d._

_c. Physical harm._ "Physical harm" means "the physical impairment of the human body or of real property or tangible personal property." Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 4 (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005). An actor who intentionally or negligently causes such harm is subject to liability for it. Id. §§ 5-6. An agent has a duty to exercise reasonable care in rendering services to a third person when the agent undertakes to do so to perform a duty owed by the principal to the third party. See id. § 43(b).

**Illustration:**

1\. P Corporation owns an apartment building and employs A as its manager. T is a resident in the building. A knows that the building's lack of outdoor lighting is hazardous to its residents and their invitees because lack of lighting facilitates criminal activity. Instances of such criminal activity are reported to A. T complains to A about the lack of lighting. A assures T that A will correct the problem. A does nothing to remedy the lack of lighting. T is injured in a nighttime assault inside the building. Under applicable law, the lack of outdoor lighting is a cause of T's injuries. A is subject to liability to T. P Corporation is also subject to liability to T. See § 7.07.

An agent's tortious conduct may cause harm to property. Harm to real or tangible personal property of a third party may occur independently of any bodily injury. Specific funds of a third party constitute property for this purpose.

**Illustration:**

2\. T, who wishes to finance the acquisition of business property, retains P Corporation, a loan broker, to locate a source of financing. P Corporation assigns T's matter to A, an employee of P Corporation. A tells T that K, who is in Europe at the time, will arrange loans from unidentified European sources if T obtains a contract to purchase the property and if T provides \$ 50,000 to be held by K as an escrow deposit. T complies, and A transmits T's \$ 50,000 to K. A has never met K personally, nor have any of A's colleagues at P Corporation. Neither A nor any of A's colleagues at P Corporation has any information about K's financial position apart from statements made by K. K does not obtain the promised loans for T and does not return T's \$ 50,000. Under applicable law, A was negligent in handling T's money. A is subject to liability to T. P Corporation is also subject to liability to T.

An agent's conduct may also cause the principal to breach a duty of performance that it owes a third party under a contract between the principal and the third party. A contractual obligation may create a duty of reasonable care that, if breached, also constitutes a tort. See Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 43 (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005).

If an agent has only limited control over a third party's property, the agent is subject to liability only to the extent of such control, unless the agent's conduct increases the risk of harm posed by that over which the agent has only limited control.

**Illustration:**

3\. P, who owns an office building, engages A to conduct security checks of persons seeking to enter P's building. P furnishes A with a metal detector to use for this purpose. T, who has business with a tenant in P's building, is injured when T trips over the protruding base of the metal detector while reaching over to retrieve jewelry that T removed prior to stepping through the detector. Under applicable law, T was not at fault. A is not subject to liability to T. A did not control the configuration of the metal detector furnished by P.

An agent may also be subject to liability to a third party when the agent gratuitously assumes a duty to render service to the third party. If the agent's failure to exercise reasonable care in fulfilling the duty causes physical harm to the third party or the third party's property, the agent is subject to liability when the agent's failure increases the risk of harm or the person to whom the services are rendered or another relies on the agent to exercise reasonable care. See Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 43, Comment _e_ (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005).

_d. Economic loss._ Conduct by an agent that breaches a duty owed by the agent to the principal does not subject the agent to liability to a third party who suffers pure economic loss as a result unless the agent's conduct also breaches a duty owed by the agent to the third party. Most cases hold that an agent does not owe a duty to a third party when the agent's negligent conduct causes only pure economic loss to a third party.

**Illustrations:**

4\. S, owner of Blackacre, lists it for sale with A. A is unaware that Blackacre is subject to a lien held by S's ex-spouse, X. T purchases Blackacre prior to obtaining title insurance, A having given T a title commitment prepared for a prior prospective purchaser that does not reflect X's lien. X sues T, leading to expenditures by T to defend against X's action. Under applicable law, a vendor's agent has a duty to the vendor to procure title insurance for the vendor. A is not subject to liability to T. A's failure to obtain title insurance did not breach any duty owed by A to T.

5\. Same facts as Illustration 4, except that T asks A whether Blackacre is subject to any lien. A knows of X's lien. A replies "no" to T. A is subject to liability to T. A's statement constitutes a fraudulent misrepresentation.

6\. P, a professional basketball player, retains A to arrange paid personal appearances to be made by P. A enters into a contract calling for a personal appearance by P at a basketball camp owned by T. Carelessly, A neglects to inform T in a timely fashion that P will not appear at the camp on the scheduled date. T incurs expenses in arranging a personal appearance by another professional basketball player, having promoted such an appearance in marketing the camp to prospective campers and their parents. A is not subject to liability to T. A undertook no duty to T. P is subject to liability to T for breach of contract.

7\. Same facts as Illustration 6, except that A also carelessly neglects to tell P about the appearance at T's camp to which A has committed P. A is not subject to liability to T. Although A's failure to tell P about the scheduled appearance breached A's duty to P, see § 8.08, A neither had nor assumed any duty to T.

An agent's misstatements may subject the agent to liability to a third party although the third party suffers only economic loss as a consequence. In Illustration 5, A's knowing misstatement to T subjects A to liability to T because A is subject to a duty, imposed by tort law, that A breaches by misstating a material fact to T when A knows the statement to be false. See § 7.01, Comment _d._ Misstatements by an agent that are negligent, likewise, may, under certain circumstances, subject the agent to liability to third parties who justifiably rely on the agent's statement. See id. and Restatement Second, Torts § 552.

These general points are applicable when a principal is an organization, such as a corporation. An agent's breach of a duty that the agent owes to an organizational principal does not create a claim that may be asserted by a third party although the agent's breach of duty causes economic loss to the third party, unless the agent's conduct also breaches a duty that the agent owes to the third party.

**Illustration:**

8\. T Corporation, an insurer, enters into a general agency contract with P Corporation that authorizes P Corporation to solicit and underwrite proposals for insurance in 11 states. A, the president of P Corporation, is negligent in negotiating the terms of an insurance policy issued to L. T Corporation incurs losses under the policy issued to L that it would not have incurred had A not been negligent. P Corporation is subject to liability to T Corporation. A is subject to liability to P Corporation. A is not subject to liability to T Corporation. A did not breach a duty owed to T Corporation.

An agent's breach of duty to the principal may subject the agent to liability to a third party for economic loss that the breach causes the third party when the third party is a third-party beneficiary of a promise of performance made by the agent. A third party may enforce a right to performance created by a promise when the third party is an intended beneficiary of the promise and not merely an incidental beneficiary.

**Illustration:**

9\. T contracts to buy Blackacre from P. P contracts with A Bank to accept payments made by T on P's behalf; to remit a portion of each payment to M, from whom P purchased Blackacre and who retains legal title to Blackacre pending full payment of the purchase price; and to deposit the remainder of each payment into P's personal savings account at A Bank. Thereafter, and without notice to T, P directs A Bank to credit the entire amount of each payment made by T to P's savings account. A Bank complies with P's instructions. After T makes all payments required under T's contract with P, T learns that M has terminated P's contract and will retain title to Blackacre. A Bank is subject to liability to T. A Bank's promise to remit a portion of T's payments to M was intended to benefit T. T justifiably relied on A Bank's promise. Without T's consent, A Bank's duty to perform its promise could not be discharged or modified by agreement between P and A Bank.

To assert a claim as an intended third-party beneficiary of a promise of performance made by an agent to the principal, a third party must establish that the agent made a promise of performance. A promise is "a manifestation of intention to act or refrain from acting in a specified way, so made as to justify a promisee in understanding that a commitment has been made." Restatement Second, Contracts § 2(1). A promisor manifests an intention if the promisor "believes or has reason to believe that the promisee will infer that intention from \[the promisor's\] words or conduct." Id. § 2, Comment _b._ If a principal instructs an agent to do an act that will benefit a third party, the agent has not made a promise of performance unless the agent makes a manifestation to the principal that the agent intends so to act. The manifestation made by the agent may be implicit or may be expressly stated. For example, in Illustration 9, the contract between P and A Bank contains an explicit manifestation by A Bank that it will make remittances to M, who retained title to Blackacre. On manifestations generally, see § 1.03.

A lawful instruction from the principal that evokes no such manifestation from the agent does not yield a promise on the basis of which a third party may assert a claim as a third-party beneficiary. Although an agent's failure to act as instructed by the principal breaches the agent's duty to the principal and subjects the agent to liability to the principal, see § 8.09, the agent's liability does not presuppose that the agent has expressly or impliedly made a promise to the principal that a specific instruction will be implemented.

**Illustration:**

10\. P, a real-estate developer, transfers a 30 percent interest in a condominium-conversion project to T. T and P later enter into a contract providing that P will create an escrow account. P engages A as project manager for the conversion and instructs A to set up the escrow account required by P's contract with T. A receives the instructions, but does not set up the escrow account. A is not subject to liability to T. Although A's negligent failure to set up the escrow account breached A's duty to P, A is not subject to liability to T for economic loss that A's breach of duty causes T because A made no promise to P of which T is an intended third-party beneficiary.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ This section consolidates treatment of material covered by Restatement Second, Agency §§ 352, 353, 354, 355, 356, and 357.

_b. In general._ For discussion of the relationship between a statutorily imposed duty and liability to a third party who suffers economic loss, see [_Peter v. Schumacher Enters., Inc., 22 P.3d 481, 490 (Alaska 2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4320-J9C0-0039-41DJ-00000-00&context=1530671) (statute required insurer to offer uninsured and underinsured motorist coverage; although insured has private cause of action against insurer whose agent failed to offer such coverage, and although agent's failure to offer coverage may have breached term of agent's agreement with insurer requiring compliance with state laws, insured does not have claim against agent simply because agent may have breached its contractual duties to principal). Accord, [_Macabio v. TIG Ins. Co., 955 P.2d 100, 111-112 (Hawaii 1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SVD-VYY0-0039-44MX-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Robinson v. Charles A. Flynn Ins. Agency, Inc., 653 N.E.2d 207, 208 (Mass.App.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-94D0-003C-V10B-00000-00&context=1530671). _Peter_ acknowledges that "sometimes a statute may form the basis for a common-law remedy which extends beyond the scope of the statute. We do not believe that this is an appropriate case for such a remedy. The legislative objective of assuring that high limit coverage is made available to all Alaskans can likely be achieved with reasonable comprehensiveness even if the duty to offer such coverage is restricted to insurance companies. The legislature could well have believed that there was no reason to impose a redundant duty on insurance agents." [_22 P.3d at 490_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4320-J9C0-0039-41DJ-00000-00&context=1530671).

_c. Physical harm._ Illustration 1 is based on E.G. [_Rock, Inc. v. Danly, 633 A.2d 485 (Md.App.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-8WX0-003G-24X1-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Frances T. v. Village Green Owners Ass'n, 723 P.2d 573, 585 (Cal.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-CTT0-003D-J15T-00000-00&context=1530671) (directors of condominium association subject to liability to unit owner injured by assault in unit when directors had notice of criminal activity and of inadequate outdoor lighting; directors and officers "have frequently been held liable for negligent nonfeasance where they know that a condition or instrumentality under their control posed an unreasonable risk of injury to the plaintiff but failed to take action to prevent it. . . . when the only persons in a position to remedy a hazardous condition are made specifically aware of the danger to third parties, then their unreasonable failure to avoid the harm may result in personal liability"); accord, Restatement Third, Property (Servitudes) § 6.13(1)(a) and Illustration 1. Many jurisdictions impose a limited duty on landlords to protect tenants against third-party criminal acts. See [_Frances T., 723 P.2d at 593_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-CTT0-003D-J15T-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Kline v. 1500 Massachusetts Ave. Apartment Corp., 439 F.2d 477 (D.C.Cir.1970)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-KB20-0039-X22N-00000-00&context=1530671); Prosser & Keeton, The Law of Torts § 56 (5th ed. 1984).

For other instances of agents' liability in the context of physical harm to third parties, see Lailhengue v. Mobil Oil Corp., 1992 WL 125488 (E.D.La.1992) (allegation that employees of petroleum company operated and controlled refinery unit negligently, causing explosion of unit and consequent injury to plaintiff, stated possible cause of action of negligence against employees); [_Dieter v. Hand, 333 N.W.2d 772 (Neb.1983)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-61K0-003D-B17B-00000-00&context=1530671) (individual engaged by electric utility to maintain power lines notices sagging line over public alleyway but neither repairs nor replaces line and does not post notice of peril; both individual actor and utility subject to liability to truck driver injured when truck door comes into contact with line).

An agent's negligent performance of duties in the construction context may subject the agent to liability. See [_Michaelis v. Benavides, 71 Cal. Rptr.2d 776 (Cal.App.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3H-PT50-0039-42J9-00000-00&context=1530671) (employee of construction firm whose negligent construction of patio and driveway caused damage to property subject to liability to property owner). This outcome is potentially at odds with the position taken by Restatement Second, Agency § 352, Illustration 2 ("P, who has agreed to build a house for T, employs A to build it. A is careless in the construction of the house, so that the house does not conform to the contract. A is not thereby liable to T for the failure to construct the house in accordance with the contract."). Illustration 2 does not state whether A's carelessness in constructing the house led to actionable defects in construction, in addition to nonconformity to the contract.

A plaintiff may be limited to a contract-law claim against a principal when a contract defines the scope of the parties' obligations and no duty that arose independently was owed to the plaintiff by an agent. See _Saltiel v. GSI Consultants, Inc., 788 A.2d 268 (N.J.2002)_ (individual officers of turf-grass corporation did not owe duty to architect, independent of contract between architect and corporation, to design and prepare turfgrass specifications for athletic fields; damages alleged by architect, including cost of preparing new specifications, arose from corporation's breach of contract, not from any duty imposed by law).

Illustration 2 is based on [_Miller v. Quarles, 410 S.E.2d 639, 642 (Va. 1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-VRV0-003D-50VC-00000-00&context=1530671) (both principal and agent subject to liability "for the agent's negligent performance of his common-law duty of reasonable care under the circumstances"). In a later case, [_Gerald M. Moore & Son, Inc. v. Drewry, 467 S.E.2d 811, 812 (Va.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-VNS0-003D-502M-00000-00&context=1530671), the court stated that _Miller_ "does not alter the privity requirement for recovery of purely economic loss damages in negligence actions. . . . In accepting the escrow deposit, \[agent and principal\] assumed a common law duty of reasonable care in safeguarding" the third party's property, which the agent breached "when he gave the \[third party's\] funds" to the European who absconded with them. "The damages sought and recovered against \[agent\], although stated in terms of dollars, reflected the direct loss of specific property, the escrow deposit, not an economic loss suffered by \[third party\]. _Miller_ did not involve recovery of economic loss damages. . . ." In _Moore & Son,_ the plaintiff, the president of an engineering firm, performed engineering work to produce a furnace under a contract between the firm and the purchaser of the furnace. Design and engineering defects caused the furnace to malfunction. The purchaser sued both the engineering firm and its president, alleging negligence, and sued the engineering firm alleging breach of contract. The court held that, in the absence of privity, "a person cannot be held liable for economic loss damages caused by his negligent performance of a contract." Id. See also [_Crawford v. Deutsche Bank AG, 244 F.Supp.2d 615, 616 (E.D.Va.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:47YJ-CSB0-0038-Y1DV-00000-00&context=1530671) (citing _Moore_ for proposition that under Virginia law, in absence of privity, an agent's negligence is not basis for recovery of economic loss; no allegation in complaint that plaintiffs, who contracted with bank for package of financial services, were in privity with bank's agent). But see [_Krawczyk v. Bank of Sun Prairie, 553 N.W.2d 299, 302 (Wis.App.)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-X4J0-003G-34R1-00000-00&context=1530671), rev. granted, _560 N.W.2d 273 (Wis. 1996)_ (bank officer not subject to liability to persons other than bank for negligent transfer of trust-account funds). _Krawczyk_ relies on [_Greenberg v. Stewart Title Guar. Co., 492 N.W.2d 147 (Wis.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-VP00-003G-31H4-00000-00&context=1530671), in which the court held that a title insurer is not subject to liability to a purchaser of property for a defect in title when the insurer did not assume a duty to conduct a reasonable title search in addition to contracting to insure title. _Krawczyk_ does not discuss whether a bank officer's negligent transfer of trust-account funds might be characterized as loss or injury to specific property, as in _Miller._

Illustration 3 is based on [_Klopp v. Wackenhut Corp., 824 P.2d 293 (N.M. 1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W340-003D-D2SP-00000-00&context=1530671). Restatement Second, Agency § 355, Comment _b,_ illustrates the basic point as follows: "So, a person appointed to guard the patrons of a circus from an escaping lion is not liable because the pike with which he is supplied as a preventive means is obviously insufficient. If, however, such agent aids in creating a situation in which the thing over which he has such partial control causes harm, he is subject to liability for such harm. . . . Thus, if the agent were to lead a lion down the street holding him by a small rope, this being the means supplied by his employer, he would be guilty of active negligence and therefore would be liable to a person harmed by the lion after escaping."

_d. Economic loss._ Illustration 4 is based on [_Wilson v. Haimbaugh, 482 N.E.2d 486 (Ind.App.1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-FTG0-003F-X31K-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Andrie v. Chrystal-Anderson & Assocs. Realtors, Inc., 466 N.W.2d 393, 396 (Mich.App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-BRM0-003D-6394-00000-00&context=1530671) (listing agent for homeowner owes no duty to would-be purchaser to convey offer accurately to owner, although agent's inaccurate submission of offer constitutes breach of fiduciary duty to owner that enables seller to rescind transaction; dissent notes that agent was also, in a separate representation, attempting to sell would-be purchaser's residence and that agent should be subject to a duty of care to would-be purchaser in carrying out "ministerial task of submitting an offer"); [_Tower Fin. Servs., Inc. v. Mapp, 402 S.E.2d 286 (Ga.App. 1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX8-C2G0-003G-P1HC-00000-00&context=1530671) (lender's allegedly negligent title search, performed solely to protect its own interests, does not create basis for liability to cotenants of property who did not rely to their detriment on results of search). On the difficulty in formulating the rule, see [_Greg Allen Constr. Co. v. Estelle, 798 N.E.2d 171, 174-175 (Ind.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:49Y5-54M0-0039-417Y-00000-00&context=1530671) (noting that phrasing rule as one of nonliability for economic loss "overstates the point somewhat," given instances of liability for economic loss in statutory settings and common-law fields, while phrasing rule as "whether the duty arises solely from contract" may be "largely tautological").

On an agent's liability for fraud, see, e.g., [_In re Humphrey's Pest Control Co., 80 B.R. 687, 695 (Bankr. E.D.Pa.1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-M8Y0-0039-K042-00000-00&context=1530671) (listing agent who knew of inspection report revealing active termite infestation did not disclose its existence to purchaser of residential property to avoid foreseeable wrath of seller's daughter, to whom seller delegated broad discretion to arrange sale; agent and daughter, who also knew of termite report, subject to liability to purchaser; seller subject to liability despite lack of knowledge); [_Lee Hawkins Realty, Inc. v. Moss, 724 So. 2d 1116 (Miss.App.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3VCC-T5Y0-0039-43V7-00000-00&context=1530671) (listing agent subject to liability if agent knew buyer was relying on prior material representations made by seller that agent learns are untrue).

Illustration 6 is based on [_Jones v. Archibald, 360 N.Y.S.2d 119 (App. Div.1974)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-Y0S0-003C-C0PB-00000-00&context=1530671).

New York cases decline to recognize a third party's reliance on an agent's promise or other undertaking as a basis for liability in the absence of a contract. See [_Anderson v. Radio Corp. of Am., 216 N.Y.S.2d 182 (Sup. 1961)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-CKF0-003C-D503-00000-00&context=1530671) (explicitly rejecting position taken in Restatement Second, Agency § 354). An early articulation of this rejection is [_H.R. Moch Co. v. Rensselaer Water Co., 159 N.E. 896 (N.Y. 1928)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-SFT0-003F-610K-00000-00&context=1530671). However, if an agent assumes complete control and management of its principal's affairs, the agent is subject to liability to a third party for damages resulting from the agent's breach of duty to the third person arising from the agent's assumption of control. See [_Flexi-Van Leasing, Inc. v. Pharos Lines, S.A., 808 F.Supp. 237, 254 (S.D.N.Y.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-K2K0-008H-F4FM-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying New York law; ocean-liner service delegated complete management authority to general agent; agent subject to delegated duty to equipment lessor to maintain and safeguard equipment and to effect timely payment of rental payments to lessor; agent subject to liability to lessor for damages arising out of breach of duty).

If an actor is not an agent of another party, the actor may be subject to liability to a third party when an agent would not be. See [_Carter Lincoln-Mercury, Inc. v. EMAR Group, Inc., 638 A.2d 1288 (N.J.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-VHJ0-003C-P4PM-00000-00&context=1530671) (when insurance broker who obtained insurance policy for lessee of truck selected insurer whom it had reason to know to be insolvent, broker subject to liability to truck's owner for negligence in selecting insolvent insurer).

On gratuitous assumption of a duty to a third party, see, e.g., [_Land Title Co. v. Anchorage Printing, Inc., 783 P.2d 767, 770 (Alaska 1989)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-W920-003C-G1BT-00000-00&context=1530671) (escrow company not subject to negligence liability when escrow agent who handled sale of land did not gratuitously assume duty to purchaser of land to obtain resolution from directors of corporate purchaser authorizing purchase by corporation's secretary-treasurer; additionally, no showing that seller relied on commitment by escrow agent to obtain resolution when seller's agent failed to object when secretary-treasurer signed purchase documents, knowing he did so without a resolution and believing one to be necessary).

Illustration 8 is based on [_United States Liab. Ins. Co. v. Haidinger-Hayes, Inc., 463 P.2d 770 (Cal.1970)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRK-JTK0-003C-H134-00000-00&context=1530671). In Illustration 8, as in _United States Liability,_ the firm for which the agent works does not undertake a relationship with the third-party insurer that creates a duty of care owed to the third-party insurer by employees and agents of the other firm. Such a duty is created if, for example, the other firm contracts to manage a segment of the third-party insurer's business. See [_Omaha Indem. Co. v. Royal Am. Managers, Inc., 777 F.Supp. 1488, 1492 (W.D.Mo.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-9WJ0-001T-7159-00000-00&context=1530671) (president of firm that contracted to manage an insurer's reinsurance business subject to liability to insurer when president and firm failed to exercise reasonable care in managing insurer's reinsurance business; under Missouri law, "\[a\] corporate officer may be liable to a third party for damages caused by his wrongful actions if he had actual or constructive knowledge of the actionable wrong and participated therein"; insurer relied on president to exercise knowledge and skill that president represented he and firm possessed).

For the general point that a corporation's officers and other agents are not subject to liability to third parties simply on the basis of breach of their duties to the corporation, see [_Newman v. Forward Lands, Inc., 418 F.Supp. 134, 136 (E.D.Pa.1976)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-JD50-0054-632M-00000-00&context=1530671) (directors of not-for-profit corporation who negligently allowed corporation's executive director to divert corporation's funds and real estate to personal use not subject to liability in action brought by party to land-acquisition contract with corporation; defendants' duty of care in managing affairs of corporation owed only to corporation); [_Speer v. Dighton Grain, Inc., 624 P.2d 952, 961 (Kan.1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-3P70-003F-D4PY-00000-00&context=1530671) (unsecured creditor of grain-elevator corporation did not allege any tortious act "which resulted in identifiable loss peculiar to the plaintiff and his property"; corporation's creditor may not maintain personal action on the basis of negligent mismanagement of corporation's affairs by its directors and officers); [_McGivern v. Amasa Lumber Co., 252 N.W.2d 371, 378 (Wis.1977)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-WBP0-003G-336K-00000-00&context=1530671) (although president of lumber company who mortgaged corporate assets to secure a personal obligation may have breached fiduciary obligation owed to corporation, creditor of corporation has no claim against president for loans made to corporation; corporation was solvent at time corporate assets were mortgaged and, "\[e\]ven where it could be said that in some sense a fiduciary obligation existed to creditors generally, the issue remains as to who has the right to sue for losses due to the breach of duty. Our prior cases treat the cause of action as an asset of the corporation, or the receiver or trustee thereof. An action may be maintained only in the right of such corporation for the benefit of persons entitled to participate in the distribution of assets.").

Illustration 9 is based on [_King v. First Nat'l Bank, 647 P.2d 596, 600 (Alaska 1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-1FJ0-000H-80X9-00000-00&context=1530671) (documents submitted by bank on its motion for summary judgment "support the proposition that \[purchasers\] might be third party beneficiaries of the contract between the Bank and the \[vendors\]. . . . The original instructions were drafted by the \[purchasers'\] attorney with particular sections intended to protect the \[purchasers'\] interest. . . . It is possible for the lower court to find that the \[purchasers\] justifiably relied on these instructions as written assurance of receiving the executed deed and request for reconveyance after they had finished paying the note"). In _King_ the purchasers also sued the attorney who prepared the purchase documents, including the collection agreement with the bank. [_Id. at 598_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-1FJ0-000H-80X9-00000-00&context=1530671).

In some circumstances, provisions in contracts that disavow the intention to benefit third parties may be ineffectual. See [_Hossain v. Rauscher Pierce Refsnes, Inc., 46 F.Supp.2d 1164, 1168 (D.Kan.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WF7-0B60-0038-Y3DN-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_15 Fed. Appx. 745 (10th Cir.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:43P9-09T0-0038-X3JY-00000-00&context=1530671) (provision in clearing agreement between clearing and introducing broker stated that parties did not intend to create any rights in nonparties; court characterizes provision as "nearly nonsensical" because "\[a\]n investor must be a beneficiary of a clearing agreement . . . without the intent to benefit the investor, there would be no need for the clearing agreement.").

The treatment of third-party beneficiary claims against agents in Restatement Second, Agency, is not entirely free of doubt on two scores: 1. whether it is consistent with contemporary contract-law principles; and 2. whether it is internally consistent. Section 342(2) provides that "\[a\]n agent whose promise to pay is primarily for the benefit of a third person may be liable in an action of contract to the third person for his failure to perform his promise." Section 342 is cited by [_Atlantic & Gulf Stevedores, Inc. v. Revelle Shipping Agency, Inc., 750 F.2d 457, 459 (5th Cir.1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-JS10-0039-P0BT-00000-00&context=1530671) (local agent's "violation of its principal's instructions gives no right of recovery to \[stevedore\], a stranger to that relationship"; stevedore made "no serious argument that the implied promise of \[local agent\]--to disburse the funds as requested by \[principal\] was for the primary benefit of \[stevedore\] so as to support a third party beneficiary claim by \[stevedore\] against local agent."). However, Illustration 2 to § 342 states that: "Having purchased goods from T through A, P sends A \$ 1,000 upon A's agreement to pay T and not to use the money to satisfy a claim which A has against P. It may be found that this is a contract for the benefit of T and that A is liable to T for failure to make the payment."

In contrast, Restatement Second, Contracts § 302(1)(a) does not make a promisor's intention determinative when performance of the promise will satisfy "an obligation of the promisee to pay money to the beneficiary," and § 302(1)(b) does not explicitly require that benefiting the beneficiary be the promisee's "primary" intention. Restatement Second, Contracts, also makes explicit that it is the promisee's intention that is relevant. See E. Allan Farnsworth, Contracts 662 (4th ed. 2004) (suggesting that Restatement formulation "might be paraphrased to require an indication 'that the promisee would have been willing to pay the fair value for the promisor's undertaking a duty to the beneficiary' "). See also Restatement Second, Contracts § 304, Comment _e_ ("\[i\]n cases of doubt, the question whether such an intention is to be attributed to the promisee may be influenced by the likelihood that recognition of the right will further the legitimate expectations of the promisee, make available a simple and convenient procedure for enforcement, or protect the beneficiary in his reasonable reliance on the promise.").

Restatement Second, Agency § 352, Illustration 1, is arguably or potentially at odds with Illustration 2 to § 342, quoted above. Illustration 1 to § 352 states that: "A is given money by P and directed to pay it to T. A fails to pay the money to T. A is not thereby liable to T." To reconcile the two Illustrations, it is necessary to assume that A in Illustration 1 just quoted would not be understood to have agreed or promised to make the payment to T, or that P does not have an obligation to pay money to T or does not intend that T receive the benefit of A's promise.

Illustration 10 is based on [_Coker v. Dollar, 846 F.2d 1302 (11th Cir.1988)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-0ND0-001B-K2JT-00000-00&context=1530671). For statutory treatment of the rights of third-party beneficiaries, see, e.g., Mich. Comp. Laws § 600.1405. See also [_State ex rel. William Ranni Assocs., Inc. v. Hartenbach, 742 S.W.2d 134 (Mo.1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-B4H0-003F-C264-00000-00&context=1530671) (beneficiaries of life-insurance policy are not third-party beneficiaries of contract between insurer and agent; agent's allegedly negligent handling of claim does not subject agent to liability to beneficiaries); [_Quigley v. Bay State Graphics, Inc., 693 N.E.2d 1368, 1372 (Mass. 1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SR9-8DG0-0039-40VX-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurance agent of tenant who failed to procure insurance not subject to liability to landlord; no evidence agent promised to procure insurance, that landlord relied on promise, or that agent owed duty to landlord). On the issue in the context of escrow holders, see [_Bescor, Inc. v. Chicago Title & Trust Co., 446 N.E.2d 1209, 1212-1214 (Ill.App.1983)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-6700-0054-H0VF-00000-00&context=1530671) (escrowee of proceeds of construction loan appointed by owner not subject to liability to subcontractor when escrowee disbursed proceeds to subcontractor's employee, who converted money to his own use; as agent of owner, escrowee not subject to liability to person not party to escrow agreement to whom no separate duty was owed; subcontractor an incidental, not an intended beneficiary of escrow agreement, which unambiguously provided that it excluded subcontractors as third-party beneficiaries of the trust created by the agreement and the balance of the agreement manifested that its "foremost purpose was to protect the security interests of the lender against possible mechanics' lien claims."); _Summit Fin. Holdings, Ltd. v. Continental Lawyers Title Co., 41 P.3d 548 (Cal.2002)_ (escrow agent not subject to liability to assignee of promissory note when agent who followed instructions of lender and borrower negligently paid assignor). On insurance brokers, see [_Highlands Ins. Co. v. Hobbs Group, LLC, 373 F.3d 347, 351-352 (3d Cir.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4CPG-3XJ0-0038-X45N-00000-00&context=1530671) (predicting New Jersey law, court holds that broker's duty of care extends to obligee of surety bond, analogously with duty of insurance broker to loss payees, other than insured, that are within broker's zone of harm).

Most cases treat an adjuster retained by an insurer as an agent of the insurer who is not liable to third parties for economic loss. Compare [_Hill v. Giuffrida, 608 F. Supp. 648, 649 (S.D.Miss.1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-FK70-0039-R32N-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Sanchez v. Lindsey Morden Claims Servs., Inc., 84 Cal.Rptr.2d 799 (Cal.App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WH6-DSN0-0039-427G-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Velastequi v. Exchange Ins. Co., 505 N.Y.S.2d 779, 782 (Civ.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-2S00-003D-G4DR-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Charleston Dry Cleaners & Laundry, Inc. v. Zurich Am. Ins. Co., 586 S.E.2d 586 (S.C.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:49J9-BXW0-0039-42B2-00000-00&context=1530671); and [_Dear v. Scottsdale Ins. Co., 947 S.W.2d 908, 917 (Tex. App.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-Y8R0-003C-217G-00000-00&context=1530671), overruled on other grounds by [_Apex Towing Co. v. Tolin, 41 S.W.3d 118 (Tex.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:42G6-HV70-0039-41C8-00000-00&context=1530671) (no liability) with [_Continental Ins. Co. v. Bayless & Roberts, Inc., 608 P.2d 281 (Alaska 1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-1KW0-000H-81HY-00000-00&context=1530671) (claims adjuster subject to liability).
