[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 7.03_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8X0-00YG-400G-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 7- Torts--Liability of Agent and Principal_ > _Topic 2- Principal's Liability_**

**§ 7.03 Principal's Liability--In General**

**(1) A principal is subject to direct liability to a third party harmed by an agent's conduct when**

**(a) as stated in § 7.04, the agent acts with actual authority or the principal ratifies the agent's conduct and**

**(i) the agent's conduct is tortious, or**

**(ii) the agent's conduct, if that of the principal, would subject the principal to tort liability; or**

**(b) as stated in § 7.05, the principal is negligent in selecting, supervising, or otherwise controlling the agent; or**

**(c) as stated in § 7.06, the principal delegates performance of a duty to use care to protect other persons or their property to an agent who fails to perform the duty.**

**(2) A principal is subject to vicarious liability to a third party harmed by an agent's conduct when**

**(a) as stated in § 7.07, the agent is an employee who commits a tort while acting within the scope of employment; or**

**(b) as stated in § 7.08, the agent commits a tort when acting with apparent authority in dealing with a third party on or purportedly on behalf of the principal.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section provides an overview of the bases on which a principal may be subject to liability for the conduct of an agent or other actor. The Comments also cover points of general significance throughout this Topic. Comment _b_ discusses general differences between a principal's liability based on the principal's own conduct and a principal's vicarious liability for torts committed by an agent. Comment _c_ discusses attribution of conduct when a principal is an organization. Comment _d_ discusses agents who have more than one principal, including individuals who are officers of interrelated corporations or other entities. Comment _e_ discusses a principal's liability for punitive damages when the principal is vicariously liable for a tort committed by an agent.

_b. Liability on basis of principal's own conduct; vicarious liability._ A principal's own fault may subject the principal to liability to a third party harmed by an agent's conduct. Often termed "direct liability," such liability stems either from the principal's relationship with an agent whose conduct harms a third party or from the agent's failure to perform a duty owed by the principal to the third party. A principal is subject to liability under § 7.04 when an agent acts with actual authority in committing a tort, that is, when the agent reasonably believes, based on a manifestation of the principal, that the principal wishes the agent so to act. A principal is subject to liability under § 7.05 on the basis of the principal's negligence in selecting, supervising, or otherwise controlling or failing to control the agent. A principal is subject to liability under § 7.06 when the principal owes a duty to protect a third party and an agent to whom the principal has delegated performance of the duty fails to fulfill it.

An agent's tort may, separately, subject a principal to vicarious liability. Under § 7.07, a principal is subject to liability when an agent who is an employee as defined in § 7.07(3) commits a tort while acting within the scope of employment. Under § 7.08, a principal is subject to liability when actions taken by an agent acting with apparent authority constitute a tort or enable the agent to conceal its commission. A principal who is vicariously liable may, additionally, be subject to liability on the basis of the principal's own conduct.

Significant consequences may follow from the distinction between direct and vicarious liability. In particular, a principal's vicarious liability turns on whether the agent is liable. In most cases, direct liability requires fault on the part of the principal whereas vicarious liability does not require that the principal be at fault. The distinction may also be relevant to whether a loss is insurable.

Principals, not agents, are subject to vicarious liability. However, an agent who appoints a subagent is subject to vicarious liability for the subagent's torts on the same bases that subject a principal to vicarious liability for an agent's torts. Comment _d(1)_ discusses this aspect of subagency.

_c. Organizational principals._ A principal that is not an individual can take action only through its agents, who typically are individuals. Even when an agent is not an individual, its ability to act derives from its own agents. An organization's tortious conduct consists of conduct by agents of the organization that is attributable to it. An organization may breach a duty of care that it owes to a third party even though the breach cannot be attributed to any single agent of the organization. Thus, an organization's conduct may be tortious when it fails to fulfill a duty that the organization owes to a third party. For example, a custodian owes a duty of reasonable care to third parties with respect to risks created by those in its custody. See Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 41(b)(2) (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005). A custodian that is an organization would breach its duty of reasonable care through the action or inaction of its employees and other agents, including the prescription and enforcement by managerial agents of directives and guidelines to be followed by other agents.

_d. Agents with multiple principals._ An agent who commits a tort may have more than one principal for at least some purposes. On agents with multiple principals in general, see Chapter 3, Topic 5. In the context of a principal's liability on the basis of an agent's tort, it is helpful to distinguish among three different types of relationships: (1) subagency; (2) employees who are "borrowed" by one employer from another employer; and (3) officers of interrelated entities.

_(1). Subagency._ A subagent is a person appointed by an agent to perform functions that the agent has consented to perform on behalf of the principal. See § 3.15(1). For general discussion of the consequences of subagency, see § 3.15, Comment _d._ A subagent may be an employee of the appointing agent. When a subagent is an employee, as defined in § 7.07(3), of the appointing agent, the appointing agent is subject to vicarious liability for torts committed by the subagent within the scope of employment. See § 7.07. Both the appointing agent and the principal are subject to vicarious liability when a subagent acts with apparent authority in committing a tort, as stated in § 7.08. An appointing agent may be subject to liability for torts of a subagent who is not an employee. For example, when a law firm assigns work on behalf of a client to a "temporary" lawyer who is neither a member of the firm nor its employee, the firm creates a relationship of subagency with the "temporary" lawyer that subjects it to liability to its client for acts and omissions of the "temporary" lawyer. See Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 58, Comment _e._

A relationship of subagency does not result when an agent appoints a coagent. Coagents share a common principal, although they may occupy dominant and subordinate positions in an organizational hierarchy. See § 3.15, Comment _b._ An agent who has authority to hire a coagent is not subject to vicarious liability for the coagent's tortious conduct.

_(2). "Lent employees," or "borrowed servants."_ When work requires specialized skills or equipment or requires that an actor perform a task on less than a full-time basis, it is not unusual that the actor who performs the work is employed by a firm that contracts to provide the actor's services to another firm. The types of actors who work through such arrangements vary greatly, as does the nature of the work they do. These arrangements span the range of highly skilled professionals, including nurses, lawyers, and other members of licensed professions; office workers; skilled construction workers; and unskilled manual laborers. The risks of injury to third parties posed by such work vary, as do the settings in which work is performed. Moreover, the specifics of such arrangements vary in several respects. These include the duration of an actor's placement in a particular workplace and the provision of supervision, training, and tools needed to perform work. An actor's employer may provide requisite training, furnish the actor with specialized equipment needed for work, and reassign the actor with frequency. Alternatively, the actor may work on a long-term basis within a particular firm using tools and receiving training provided by that firm.

When an actor negligently injures a third party while performing work for the firm that has contracted for the actor's services, the question is whether that firm (often termed the "special employer") or the initial employer (often termed the "general employer"), or both, should be subject to liability to the third party. Liability should be allocated to the employer in the better position to take measures to prevent the injury suffered by the third party. An employer is in that position if the employer has the right to control an employee's conduct. When both a general and special employer have the right to control an employee's conduct, the practical history of direction may establish that one employer in fact ceded its right of control to the other, whether through its failure to exercise the right or otherwise.

It is a question of fact whether a general or a special employer, or both, have the right to control an employee's conduct. Factors that a court may consider in making this determination include the extent of control that an employer may exercise over the details of an employee's work and the timing of the work; the relationship between the employee's work and the nature of the special employer's business; the nature of the employee's work, the skills required to perform it, and the degree of supervision customarily associated with the work; the duration of the employee's work in the special employer's firm; the identity of the employer who furnishes equipment or other instrumentalities requisite to performing the work; and the method of payment for the work.

Many cases allocate liability in this context on the basis that a general employer has an exclusive right of control over employees assigned to work for clients of the general employer. This presupposes that ties between a general employer and an assigned employee will remain strong despite the employee's emplacement in the special employer's workplace where the employee's performance will often be subject to some degree of direction and monitoring by members of the special employer's management. It may also reflect assumptions about links between the general employer and the risk that third parties will be injured when work is done in a special employer's workplace. A general employer is in a position to screen prospective employees to determine their general aptitude and fitness; a general employer may also provide training to those it selects for employment. If a general employer assigns an employee and furnishes the employee with equipment to use in performing assigned work, the general employer is also in a position to impose requirements for the proper usage and maintenance of the equipment. A general employer may also be in a better position to provide insurance coverage for an employee's actions. Any presumption that a general employer has the right to control an employee may be rebutted by proving factual indicia that the right has been assumed by a special employer. Even within the same jurisdiction, it may be difficult to predict whether a given set of indicia will demonstrate that a special employer has assumed the right of control.

However, a significant number of cases allocate liability to a special employer on the basis of its right and ability to direct a borrowed employee's specific actions in its workplace. A justification for this approach is that a borrowed employee may retain only formal ties to a general employer, depending on the duration and nature of the borrowed employee's relationship with the special employer, which weakens the likelihood that the general employer retains any practical capacity to control the borrowed employee's conduct. This approach also reflects the possibility that a special employer may in fact be in the better position to exercise control in a manner that reduces the risk of injury to third parties. This possibility may be especially likely when the nature of a borrowed employee's work requires coordinated effort as part of a skilled team and close direction or supervision by the team's leader. Some cases allocate liability to both general and special employer on the basis that both exercised control over the employee and both benefited to some degree from the employee's work.

A related question, arising when the third party injured by an actor's negligence is an employee of the special employer, is the applicability of workers'-compensation insurance law to the employee's claims. Although these questions may often be related as a practical matter, workers'-compensation questions are beyond the scope of this Restatement.

_(3). Officers of interrelated entities._ The fact that a corporation or other entity owns a majority of the voting equity in another entity does not create a relationship of agency between them or between each entity and the other's agents. Likewise, common ownership of multiple entities does not create relationships of agency among them. See § 1.01, Comment _f(2)._ An entity becomes the agent of another entity, and an individual becomes an entity's agent, only when they are linked by the elements of an agency relationship as stated in § 1.01.

Within a related group of corporations or other entities the same individuals may serve as officers or directors of more than one entity. An overlapping cast in multiple organizational roles does not in itself create relationships of agency that are not otherwise present.

When the same individuals serve multiple entities as their officers, directors, or employees, it may become necessary to determine the entity to which an individual's conduct should be attributed. There is a general presumption that contracts and other transactions entered into by a shared officer are attributed to the entity for which the officer purports to be acting. A rationale for this presumption is that the identity of the party who will be bound by a contract or transaction is often material to the other party to the contract or transaction. See § 6.01, Comment _b._ This rationale does not support applying the same presumption when a shared officer's conduct is tortious, especially when the party injured by the conduct has not chosen to engage in a transaction conducted by the shared officer. Moreover, that an officer purports to act on behalf of a particular entity does not create a basis for attribution that corresponds to policies and objectives that underlie contemporary tort law. When harm is caused negligently, these objectives include remedying an injustice that has been inflicted on a plaintiff by a defendant and providing a defendant with appropriate safety incentives. See Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 6, Comment _d_ (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005). On harm that is caused intentionally, see id. § 5, Comment _a._ Likewise, the contract-based presumption is inapt when a shared officer's conduct violates a statute that proscribes or regulates conduct, particularly conduct outside a transactional context.

_e. Punitive damages._ When a principal is subject to vicarious liability as a consequence of a tort committed by an employee or other agent, the principal is subject to liability to the plaintiff for the entire share of comparative responsibility assigned to the agent. See Restatement Third, Torts: Apportionment of Liability § 13. Thus, the principal is liable for that share of the compensatory damages awarded to the plaintiff. On the principal's claim for indemnity against the agent, see id. § 22.

Punitive damages may also be awarded against a principal when the principal is subject to vicarious liability for the agent's tortious conduct if punitive damages could be awarded against the agent under applicable law in the relevant jurisdiction. The trier of fact assesses the amount of such damages separately for agent and principal. Jurisdictions vary, however, on the circumstances under which it is proper to award punitive damages against a principal. A slight majority of states hold that punitive damages may be awarded against a principal that is vicariously liable on the basis that an employee-agent acted within the scope of employment when committing a tort, without requiring any additional showing of culpability on the part of the employer. A substantial minority of states follow the approach outlined in Restatement Second, Torts § 909, which requires a showing that the tortious conduct was that of a managerial agent or that the principal was otherwise implicated in the conduct. The approach outlined in § 909 is preferable because it requires consideration of circumstances relevant to a principal's culpability.

In the absence of some basis for awarding punitive damages against a principal, a principal that is an organization would be invulnerable to punitive damages because it can take action only through individual actors whose actions are attributable to it. This would be so even when the principal is itself directly liable for a tort under § 7.04 because managerial agents within the principal directed that the tort be committed or ratified its commission, or, more generally, because the agent who committed the tort acted with actual authority, that is, reasonably believed that the principal so wished the agent to act. See § 2.01, which defines actual authority.

Under Restatement Second, Torts § 909, punitive damages may be awarded against a principal on the basis of an agent's act only when (a) the principal or a managerial agent authorized "the doing and the manner" of the tortious act; (b) the agent was "unfit and the principal or a managerial agent was reckless in employing or retaining" the agent; (c) the agent was "employed in a managerial capacity and was acting in the scope of employment"; or (d) the principal or a managerial agent "ratified or approved" the agent's tortious act. What justifies this approach is that it "results from the reasons for awarding punitive damages, which make it improper ordinarily to award punitive damages against one who himself is personally innocent and therefore liable only vicariously." Id., Comment _b._ Thus, a tortious act committed by a nonmanagerial agent subjects a principal to liability for punitive damages only when the principal is itself implicated in the act, either by approving it before or after its commission, or by recklessly selecting or retaining the tortfeasor as an agent. A person acts recklessly when the person knows of a risk of harm created by conduct or knows facts that make that risk obvious to another in the person's situation and the precaution required to eliminate or reduce the risk is slight relative to the magnitude of the risk. See Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 2 (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005). The rationale for § 909's treatment of managerial agents is somewhat different. It is that "\[a\]lthough there has been no fault on the part of a corporation or other employer, if a person acting in a managerial capacity either does an outrageous act or approves of the act by a subordinate, the imposition of punitive damages upon the employer serves as a deterrent to the employment of unfit persons for important positions." Restatement Second, Torts § 909, Comment _b._

The difference between Restatement Second, Torts § 909 and simple vicarious liability in the context of punitive damages is less than it might initially appear to be. This stems, first, from the treatment under § 909 of principals that are organizations. Under § 909(c), a principal is subject to vicarious liability when a tortious act is committed by a managerial agent acting within the scope of employment. Subsections (a), (b), and (d) of § 909 hold an organizational principal itself accountable for punitive damages when a managerial agent authorizes the commission of a tort, recklessly selects or retains an agent, or ratifies or approves of an agent's tortious conduct. Moreover, regardless of the nature of the principal, a principal is not subject to vicarious liability even for compensatory damages unless the agent acted with apparent authority in committing the tort, as stated in § 7.08, or the agent is an employee whose tortious conduct was within the employee's scope of employment, as stated in § 7.07. Thus, the requisites for any vicarious liability tend to narrow the circumstances in which vicarious liability for punitive damages will be at issue.

The practical consequences of the differences are nonetheless significant. Under Restatement Second, Torts § 909, unless a tortfeasor is a managerial agent, punitive damages may be awarded against an organizational principal only when the culpability of a managerial agent can be shown. This limitation is likely to be especially significant when a principal deploys employees and other agents to perform tasks that involve entering into transactions on behalf of the principal or otherwise dealing with customers and other third parties with little on-site supervision. The formulations in § 909 also have the consequence of focusing attention on whether particular individuals within an organization should be characterized as its "managerial agents." Moreover, the limitations imposed by § 909 are significant when a principal is not an organization. An individual principal is subject to vicarious liability only when the culpability of the principal himself or herself can be shown.

These differences aside, it is justifiable to have some basis on which punitive damages may be, in appropriate circumstances, awarded against a principal, whether an individual or an organization. To limit the availability of punitive damages to an individual employee or agent disregards the possibility that a principal, especially an organization, may systematically influence the actions of its employees or other agents but do so in ways that do not amount to instances of legal fault that a plaintiff could prove. The availability of punitive damages provides a mechanism to focus attention at an organizational level on how best to exercise control over employees and other agents to reduce the risk of harm that their activities pose to third parties.

In contrast to a rule of simple vicarious liability, the more nuanced approach of Restatement Second, Torts § 909 makes it possible for a trier of fact to assess the degree to which a principal's mechanisms of control have fallen short and the degree to which an agent's tortious conduct fairly implicates the organization itself. It also enables the trier of fact to consider other relevant circumstances. For example, a principal who retains a licensed professional as an agent may lack any realistic ability to monitor the agent, given the agent's superior expertise, and retaining the agent's services may require delegating substantial control to the agent over how to deal with matters that may arise. On the other hand, if a principal chooses a particular agent due to the agent's reputation for achieving results through tortious conduct, or chooses the agent heedless of that reputation, the principal's choice implicates the principal in subsequent conduct by the agent.

**Illustration:**

1\. P, a landlord, employs A Ejectment Co. to dispossess a tenant. P knows that A Ejectment Co. has a reputation for using undue force in dealing with tenants. E, an employee of A Ejectment Co., in accordance with its usual methods, commits an unprovoked battery on T, a tenant, in order to induce T to leave. In an action by T against P, punitive damages can be awarded.

Although there is no rigid test to determine whether an agent is a "managerial agent" for purposes of Restatement Second, Torts § 909, the determination should focus on the agent's discretion to make decisions that would have prevented the injury to the plaintiff or that determine policies of the organization relevant to the risk that resulted in the injury. The title that an agent holds is not dispositive, nor is the fact that the agent is not among the highest in an organization's hierarchy. If an agent in fact manages a business or enterprise, the agent is a "managerial agent" of the principal on whose behalf it manages, although the agent is external to the principal's own organizational structure.

A statute may authorize the award of punitive damages or may provide for a penalty, such as treble damages, that may be awarded to a plaintiff injured by a defendant's violation of the statute. When a defendant is not itself the actor who committed the conduct that violates the statute, the court must then determine whether the actor's conduct should be attributed to the defendant. Unless the language of the statute itself resolves the question, the determination should reflect the purpose of the statute.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_b. Liability on basis of principal's own conduct; vicarious liability._ For general discussion of vicarious liability in various contexts, see P.S. Atiyah, Vicarious Liability in the Law of Torts (1967); Dan B. Dobbs, The Law of Torts 905-906 (2000). For an economic analysis of a principal's vicarious liability, see A. Mitchell Polinsky, An Introduction to Law and Economics, ch. 15 (3d ed. 2003) (concluding that, from standpoint of inducing optimum precautions against risk of harm, imposing liability on principal and not the agent will often be superior in practice if the agent is judgment proof or the court cannot easily determine whether the agent was responsible for causing harm). On rationales for vicarious liability, see Gary T. Schwartz, The Hidden and Fundamental Issue of Employer Vicarious Liability, [_69 S. Cal. L. Rev. 1739 (1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S41-5XD0-00CV-5200-00000-00&context=1530671); Gary T. Schwartz, Mixed Theories of Tort Law: Affirming Both Deterrence and Corrective Justice, [_75 Tex. L. Rev. 1801, 1821-1822 (1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S41-26R0-00CV-60V9-00000-00&context=1530671).

For an example of a principal subject to direct as well as vicarious liability, see [_Alfa Mut. Ins. Co. v. Roush, 723 So. 2d 1250, 1256 (Ala. 1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3TN1-SHS0-0039-44JC-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent converted premiums paid by insureds; insurer "heedless" in encouraging its agents to sell more workers'-compensation coverage on pooled basis without establishing any mechanism for agents' accountability for premiums collected, but insurer did not act "wantonly" in failing to supervise agent).

On the insurability of punitive damages based on a principal's vicarious liability, see _Morgan Int'l Realty, Inc. v. Dade Underwriters Ins. Agency, Inc., 617 So. 2d 455, 459 (Fla.Dist. App.1993)_ (under Florida law, liability insurance may not cover punitive damages assessed on basis of a person's own wrongful conduct but may cover awards when person is only vicariously liable for another person's wrong; insurance policy at issue could not have covered award of punitive damages awarded against corporation due to conduct of its president and sole shareholder when her acts and those of corporation were "indistinguishable"); [_Westchester Fire Ins. Co. v. Admiral Ins. Co., 152 S.W.3d 172 (Tex.App.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4DY3-RBN0-0039-434P-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurer's coverage for punitive damages not void as against public policy when excess insurer contributed amount to settlement that included payment for punitive damages against insured nursing home; legislation that prohibited for-profit nursing homes from obtaining coverage for punitive damages under primary insurance policy also permitted specific endorsement for such coverage). See generally Alan I. Widiss, Liability Insurance Coverage for Punitive Damages? Discerning Answers to the Conundrum Created by Disputes Involving Conflicting Public Policies, Pragmatic Considerations, and Political Actions, [_39 Vill. L. Rev. 455 (1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S3V-79V0-00CT-V0J8-00000-00&context=1530671).

On the impact of an agent's exoneration on a principal's liability, see [_Crawford v. Signet Bank, 179 F.3d 926 (D.C.Cir.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WTW-F2R0-0038-X3F3-00000-00&context=1530671) (summary judgment in favor of agent on underlying negligence claim eliminated vicarious-liability claim against principal); _Molina v. Watkins, 824 So. 2d 959, 963-964 (Fla.Dist.App.2002)_ (ordering entry of judgment on behalf of corporation on counts on which jury found no liability on part of president); [_Gangl v. Ford Motor Credit Co., 641 N.E.2d 709, 711 (Mass.App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-9H90-003C-V26T-00000-00&context=1530671) (finding that agent was not negligent compels similar finding on behalf of employer or principal); [_Howard v. Youngman, 81 S.W.3d 101, 117-118 (Mo.App. 2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45M8-D950-0039-42XG-00000-00&context=1530671) (trial court did not err in dismissing interference and negligent-representation claims of vicarious liability made by purchaser of real estate against vendor when court entered summary judgment in favor of vendor's agent); [_Morton v. Chesapeake & Ohio Ry. Co., 399 S.E.2d 464 (W.Va.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXR-1G80-003G-H2BX-00000-00&context=1530671) (sole basis for plaintiff's malicious-prosecution claim against railroad was alleged wrongful acts of its special agent; trial court, having directed verdict in favor of agent, should also have directed verdict in favor of railroad). A release of an agent's liability releases the principal's vicarious liability in [_Plock v. Crossroads Joint Venture, 475 N.W.2d 105, 113 (Neb.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-4P70-003D-B200-00000-00&context=1530671), overruled on other grounds, [_558 N.W.2d 35 (Neb.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-4780-003D-B06F-00000-00&context=1530671) (any liability of principal derived solely from agent's liability).

For the point that a principal's tortious conduct does not subject an agent to vicarious liability, see [_Dorkin v. American Express Co., 345 N.Y.S.2d 891 (Sup.1973)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-9PV0-003C-D288-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_351 N.Y.S.2d 190 (App.Div.1974)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-Y6B0-003C-C3C1-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent for hotels and transportation companies that supplied services not subject to liability for companies' tortious conduct). See also [_Moorer v. Hartz Seed Co., 120 F.Supp.2d 1283, 1288 (M.D.Ala.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:41SX-MWN0-0038-Y3MM-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent has no standing to bring tort action in own name against third party whose conduct constitutes actionable wrong against principal).

_d. Agents with multiple principals_

_(1). Subagency._ On an appointing agent's liability for misconduct of its employees, see [_George F. Hillenbrand, Inc. v. Insurance Co. of N. Am., 128 Cal.Rptr.2d 586, 614-616 (Cal.App.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:47GS-8PY0-0039-40GX-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporate agent subject to liability for torts committed by employees; agent acted as claims adjuster on behalf of insurer, and jury found that its employees maliciously prosecuted actions against insured). _Hillenbrand_ dismisses the applicability of Cal. Civ. Code § 2351, which states that "\[a\] sub-agent, lawfully appointed, represents the principal in like manner with the original agent; and the original agent is not responsible to third parties for the acts of the sub-agent," on the basis that the statute should not absolve an employer from liability for its employees' torts.

This Restatement does not use the terms "subemployee" and "subservant." As defined by Restatement Second, Agency § 5(2), a "subservant" is "a person appointed by a servant empowered to do so, to perform functions undertaken by the servant for the master and subject to the control as to his physical conduct both by the master and by the servant, but for whose conduct the servant agrees with the principal to be primarily responsible." For recent usage of "subemployee" terminology, see [_Harper ex rel. Daley v. Toler, 884 So. 2d 1124 (Fla.Dist.App.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4DM1-S7M0-0039-42YR-00000-00&context=1530671) (newspaper did not conclusively establish that substitute newspaper carrier was not its subemployee; proper focus for analysis is relationship between newspaper and carrier who engaged substitute).

_(2). "Lent employees," or "borrowed servants."_ Restatement Second, Agency, does not provide a consistent answer to the question of allocation of liability between general and special employers. Although the black letter of § 227 is consistent with allocating liability to a special employer for specific conduct over which it exercises control, Comment _b_ states an inference that a borrowed employee remains in the general employer's service although "the general employer has permitted a division of control. . . ."

One court observed of this body of doctrine, "\[t\]he hallmark of the borrowed servant rule has been the inconsistent results which it has generated." [_Kastner v. Toombs, 611 P.2d 62, 64 (Alaska 1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-1KG0-000H-81G7-00000-00&context=1530671). Kastner's preferred solution is to jettison the assumption that an exclusive allocation of responsibility is necessary and, instead, allocate responsibility between general and special employer "in accordance with principles of contribution and indemnity," noting that "\[t\]hese principles may not be easy to apply in every case, but they are at least directly responsive to the problem, while the various approaches taken under the borrowed servant rule are not." [_Id. at 65_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-1KG0-000H-81G7-00000-00&context=1530671). See also _Dazo v. Globe Airport Sec. Servs., 295 F.3d 934, 939-940 (9th Cir.2002)_ (recognizing that borrowed employee may act on behalf of both general and special employer, screening service acted on behalf of 3 airlines, not solely the airline that provided carriage to plaintiff); [_Mullis v. Mechanics & Farmers Bank, 994 F.Supp. 680, 685 (M.D.N.C.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RWV-GDK0-0038-Y09N-00000-00&context=1530671) (temporary employee; special employer controlled both means and manner of work); [_Johnson v. Motors Dispatch, Inc., 360 N.E.2d 224, 228-229 (Ind.App.1977)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-HDG0-003F-X099-00000-00&context=1530671) (recognizing that both general and special employer may be subject to liability). However, dual liability has been characterized as a source of "a new area of uncertainty in the law and might force a change in business practices of borrowing and loaning employers, the purchase of additional insurance, and the negotiation of indemnity agreements." See [_DePratt v. Sergio, 306 N.W.2d 62, 65 (Wis.1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W4G0-003G-306R-00000-00&context=1530671). Moreover, dual liability may produce unfair results when the liability of one employer of an injured party is limited to workers'-compensation benefits, and although that employer is substantially more at fault, the other employer cannot require contribution from it. Id. Put differently, the separation between common-law respondeat superior and workers'-compensation law impedes greater overall coherence and clarity in the law when the plaintiff is a fellow employee.

For a comprehensive discussion of cases, see J. Dennis Hynes, Chaos and the Law of Borrowed Servant: An Argument for Consistency, 14 J. L. & Comm. 1 (1994). See also Note, Borrowed Servants and the Theory of Enterprise Liability, 76 Yale L.J. 807 (1967); John J. Slain et al., Agency, Partnership & Employment 2-64 to 2-72 (1980).

Recent examples drawn from medical-malpractice cases illustrate the fact-specificity of borrowed-servant cases, regardless of the doctrinal formulation a court applies. If liability may shift to a special employer on the basis that it had the right to control specific activities of a borrowed employee, it may be open to question what elements of control or criteria for determining control are relevant. For a recent example, see [_St. Joseph Hosp. v. Wolff, 94 S.W.3d 513 (Tex. 2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:475B-HDJ0-0039-446T-00000-00&context=1530671) (majority of court determines that surgical resident is employee of foundation to which resident was assigned by teaching hospital, emphasizing immediate responsibility of supervising physician who was plaintiffs attending physician; dissent emphasizes contract between foundation and teaching hospital that, inter alia, made all of foundation's assignments of work to residents subject to approval of hospital's chief of surgery, as well as requirement imposed by accrediting organization for hospital's surgical residency program that hospital assume final responsibility for residents' supervision and training). See also [_Brown v. StarMed Staffing, L.P., 490 S.E.2d 503, 506 (Ga.App.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S12-W040-0039-4487-00000-00&context=1530671) (nurse was "borrowed servant" of hospital with "complete supervisory control over nurse" in provision of care to patient and power to discharge nurse although staffing firm paid nurse's wages, provided health and workers'-compensation insurance, and required that nurse abide by its rules). However, similar questions arise in jurisdictions that require relinquishment by a general employer of its right of control to shift liability to a special employer. For a recent example, see [_Estate of Hegarty v. Beauchaine, 638 N.W.2d 355 (Wis. App.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:44B7-BS40-0039-414T-00000-00&context=1530671) (majority of court holds that issues of material fact preclude summary judgment for hospital, which assigned medical resident through rotation program to another hospital, where resident was supervised by an attending physician and a senior resident but resident's day-to-day assignments, supervision, and review were supervised by assigning hospital through a faculty member; dissent argues that record shows that assigning hospital was a clearing house with rights to set policies to be enforced by others and to terminate resident's participation in program based on information reported by supervisors). Some cases bridge the doctrinal difference by focusing on whether a "temporary" master-servant relationship came into existence for purposes of the conduct in question. See [_Harris v. Miller, 438 S.E.2d 731 (N.C.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-XF80-003G-02VH-00000-00&context=1530671) (majority holds that surgeon may be held vicariously liable for negligence of nurse anesthetist during surgical procedure, although nurse anesthetist was employed by hospital in which surgery took place and in which surgeon had staff privileges; terms of surgeon's staff privileges assigned direct responsibility to surgeon for selection of anesthetic agents and their proper administration).

When the issue is not the allocation of liability on respondeat superior grounds but the applicability of workers'-compensation law, additional questions become relevant. A borrowed employee is not an employee of a special employer for purposes of determining whether the special employer is liable for workers'-compensation benefits unless the employee has made an express or implied contract of employment with the special employer, the work being done is essentially the special employer's, and the special employer has the right to control details of the work. See 3 Arthur Larson & Lex K. Larson, Larson's Workers' Compensation Law § 67.01 (2003). For authority that an employee may have two employers for workers'-compensation purposes although only one employer may be subject to liability to third parties under the borrowed-servant doctrine, see [_Wingfoot Enters. v. Alvarado, 111 S.W.3d 134, 146 (Tex. 2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:490B-MSF0-0039-43F5-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[n\]or is the concept that there can be two employers for workers' compensation purposes foreclosed by the right to control principles that we have articulated in the tort context in analyzing respondeat-superior and borrowed-servant principles. We have said that a general employee of one employer may become the borrowed servant of another with respect to _some_ activities.") (emphasis in original).

Still further questions may become relevant when questions about the status of borrowed employees arise in a context requiring statutory construction. See, e.g., [_Yeary v. United States, 921 F.Supp. 549 (S.D.Ind. 1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-SBF0-006F-P0DK-00000-00&context=1530671) (court bars United States from using borrowed-employee doctrine to characterize National Guard technicians as borrowed employees of state, although Indiana law allocates liability for employee negligence to special employer when special employer exercises spot control over employee conduct and also recognizes possibility of dual liability between general and special employer; application of doctrine would be inconsistent with National Guard Technicians Act of 1968, [_32 U.S.C. § 709(e)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SCB-BFY2-D6RV-H3N9-00000-00&context=1530671), which makes technicians federal employees for purposes of personal immunity under the Federal Tort Claims Act, [_28 U.S.C. § 2679(b)(1)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SG9-5HW2-D6RV-H10K-00000-00&context=1530671)). Employees of one governmental unit are frequently detailed to another. See, e.g., [_Kirkland v. District of Columbia, 70 F.3d 629 (D.C.Cir.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-9K70-001T-D53D-00000-00&context=1530671) (suspect killed by FBI agent who was acting as part of a Joint Fugitive Apprehension Team of metropolitan and federal officers).

For the basic point that an employee or other agent may serve more than one principal without abandoning service to any one principal, see, e.g., [_NLRB v. Town & Country Elec., Inc., 516 U.S. 85, 94-97 (1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRC-25M0-003B-R162-00000-00&context=1530671) (paid union organizer may also be employee of company); _Dazo v. Globe Airport Sec. Servs., 295 F.3d 934, 942 (9th Cir.2002)_ (O'Scannlain, J., concurring and dissenting in part) (by serving "multiple masters," airline-security screening firm's service "to one airline in no way involved an abandonment to the others"); [_White v. Revco Discount Ctrs., Inc., 33 S.W.3d 713, 724-725 (Tenn.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:41RP-JYJ0-0039-44T0-00000-00&context=1530671) (simultaneous service to 2 masters permissible "so long as the objectives of one master are not contrary to the objectives of the other"; both private employer of off-duty police officer as well as municipality may be subject to vicarious liability for torts committed by officer under specified circumstances governed by common-law agency doctrines).

_(3). Officers of interrelated entities._ In United States v. Bestfoods, the Court adopted a presumption attributing the wrongful conduct of a dual officer to a subsidiary corporation, not its parent. See [_524 U.S. 51, 69-70 (1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SWJ-DTV0-004B-Y002-00000-00&context=1530671) (for purposes of determining liability as an operator of a hazardous-waste facility under CERCLA, [_42 U.S.C. § 9601 et seq._](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SHT-0722-D6RV-H52W-00000-00&context=1530671), dual officer of subsidiary and of parent corporation presumed to act as officer of subsidiary in operating facility that illegally dumped hazardous substances). The cases cited in the Court's opinion do not state that such a presumption applies in a noncontractual context, nor does the secondary authority cited by the Court. The cited cases involve whether a subsidiary corporation was itself the "alter ego" of its parent, see [_Lusk v. Foxmeyer Health Corp., 129 F.3d 773, 779 (5th Cir.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RHM-PV00-0038-X3RD-00000-00&context=1530671) (for purposes of ADEA liability, dual officers of parent and subsidiary do not make both corporations a single employer); [_United States v. Jon-T Chems., Inc., 768 F.2d 686 (5th Cir.1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-FHR0-0039-P189-00000-00&context=1530671) (subsidiary corporation was "mere appendage" and alter ego of parent; dual officers made fraudulent misrepresentations to obtain agricultural subsidies); or deal with a question arising under a contract. See _Fisser v. International Bank, 282 F.2d 231 (2d Cir.1960)_ (arbitration clause in maritime contract executed by officer of subsidiary; dual officers do not by themselves establish that subsidiary was parent's alter ego). See also Phillip I. Blumberg, Law of Corporate Groups: Procedural Problems in the Law of Parent and Subsidiary Corporations § 1.02.1, at 12 (1983) (in context of discussion of veil-piercing, notes that "court respect form, even though the 'separate' decisions of the subsidiary are being made by individuals who are officers and directors of the parent, as well as officers and directors of the subsidiary. Courts accepting the formalist view are quite ready to accept the decisions of such common officers and directors as the 'separate' decisions of the subsidiary on the theory that the directors are wearing their 'subsidiary hats' and not their 'parent hats' when acting for the subsidiary"). The presumption is criticized in [_Robert B. Thompson, Agency Law and Asset Partitioning, 71 U. Cinn. L. Rev. 1321 (2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4B36-HHB0-00CW-50KF-00000-00&context=1530671), as insensitive to the policy objectives that should shape attribution of conduct when what is at stake is noncontractual liability.

An entity that undertakes managerial responsibility may be subject to liability when its individual agent participates in tortious conduct, despite language in an organizational statute insulating against liability simply by reason of managerial status. See [_Estate of Countryman v. Farmers Co-op Ass'n, 679 N.W.2d 598 (Iowa 2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4CC9-C7K0-0039-43T9-00000-00&context=1530671) (limited-liability company (LLC) that managed cooperative subject to liability on basis of negligence of LLC's full-time employee in distribution of propane gas; LLC statute, Iowa Code Ann. § 490A.603, not intended to insulate manager from liability for participation in tortious conduct when conduct was within scope of managerial role).

_e. Punitive damages._ On vicarious liability for punitive damages generally, see Gerald W. Boston, Punitive Damages in Tort Law ch. 7 (1993 & Supp. 1998) (summarizing position in each state); Douglas Laycock, Modern American Remedies 719-734 (3d ed. 2002).

The leading academic critique of vicarious liability for punitive damages is Dorsey D. Ellis, Jr., Fairness and Efficiency in the Law of Punitive Damages, [_56 S. Cal. L. Rev. 1, 63-71 (1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S41-6H80-00CV-52RK-00000-00&context=1530671) (arguing that efficient levels of deterrence would be achieved by imposing punitive damages only on employees). The most influential defense is Clarence Morris, Punitive Damages in Tort Cases, 44 Harv. L. Rev. 1173, 1202-1203 (1931) (arguing that awards of punitive damages against employers encourage extralegal sanctions against employees who engage in wrongdoing).

The rationale for separate determinations of punitive damages against an agent and a principal is that, if their financial circumstances differ, awards in differing amounts may be needed to achieve deterrence. See [_Bradshaw v. Deming, 837 S.W.2d 592, 594 (Mo.App.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-75T0-003F-C10Y-00000-00&context=1530671).

In Pacific Mut. Life Ins. Co. v. Haslip, the Court held that the Constitution's Due Process Clause is not violated by a punitive-damages award based solely on respondeat superior, with no fault on the part of the principal. See [_499 U.S. 1, 14-15 (1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S65-KVN0-003B-R37K-00000-00&context=1530671).

For a recent statement that punitive damages may be awarded on the basis of vicarious liability, see, e.g., [_Shiner v. Moriarty, 706 A.2d 1228, 1240 (Pa.Super.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S13-MNN0-0060-F075-00000-00&context=1530671) (no requirement that agent commit act at principal's direction or that principal ratify act; spouse did not act as other spouse's agent in pursuing litigation because he "engaged in the objectionable portions of this litigation unilaterally, through attorneys of his employ, and in the exercise of his own independent judgment" and no evidence demonstrated other spouse's ability to direct details of litigation). For recognition that an organization's culpability may be based on the cumulative conduct of its employees, see [_Clay v. Ferrellgas, Inc., 881 P.2d 11, 15-16 (N.M.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W1S0-003D-D1JG-00000-00&context=1530671) (culpable mental state of corporation "may be inferred from the very fact that one employee could be ignorant of the acts or omissions of other employees with potentially disastrous consequences").

For the point that a trier of fact assesses the amount of punitive damages separately for agent and principal, see [_Weeks v. Baker & McKenzie, 74 Cal.Rptr.2d 510, 526-527 (Cal.App. 1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SM9-SDM0-0039-454F-00000-00&context=1530671). The quantum of punitive damages awarded against an agent does not limit the amount that may be awarded against a principal, although the jurisdiction follows the rule that a principal is subject to vicarious liability for punitive damages without an additional showing of culpability on the part of the principal. See [_Watson v. Dixon, 532 S.E.2d 175, 178 (N.C. 2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40SW-5B90-0039-41NN-00000-00&context=1530671).

South Dakota applies a "foreseeability" standard for vicarious liability for punitive damages. See [_Olson v. Tri-County State Bank, 456 N.W.2d 132, 134 (S.D.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S71-2GH0-003G-D124-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[l\]iability will be imposed on the principal when the nexus is sufficient to make the resulting harm foreseeable"; bank vicariously liable for fraud of vice-president bank manager). _Olson_ slightly preceded the Eighth Circuit's opinion in [_Davis v. Merrill Lynch, Pierce, Fenner & Smith, Inc., 906 F.2d 1206 (8th Cir.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-4N90-003B-52NS-00000-00&context=1530671), in which the court upheld a jury instruction using a respondeat superior standard for punitive damages. In _Davis,_ an account executive employed by a brokerage firm churned the plaintiff's account. _Davis_ applied _Olson_ as follows: "it is clear that \[firm\] is vicariously liable in punitive damages for the acts of \[account executive\]. Securities brokers are responsible for managing their clients' money, and thus hold positions of trust. Like the bank branch manager in _Olson,_ it 'is far from unusual or startling that \[a securities broker\] would use his position to misappropriate money.' . . . It is certainly not unfair to include the loss caused by \[account executive\] among \[firm's\] costs of doing business." [_906 F.2d at 1222 n.19_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-4N90-003B-52NS-00000-00&context=1530671).

States occasionally shift from one position to another. See, e.g., [_Stroud v. Denny's Rest., Inc., 532 P.2d 790 (Or.1975)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-XDM0-003F-Y09G-00000-00&context=1530671) (abandoning rule stated in Restatement Second, Torts § 909; employer liable for punitive damages when nonmanagerial employee's tortious conduct is within scope of employment; plaintiff succeeded in action for malicious prosecution against restaurant's cook, following dispute over meal's price). See also [_Smith's Food & Drug Ctrs., Inc. v. Bellegarde, 958 P.2d 1208, 1214 (Nev.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SVG-VBS0-0039-44WR-00000-00&context=1530671) (clarifying its position, court adopts formulation of Restatement Second, Torts § 909 as exclusive rule governing an employer's liability for punitive damages for its agents' acts or omissions).

A more stringent standard, articulated in [_Lake Shore & M.S. Ry. Co. v. Prentice, 147 U.S. 101 (1893)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-FHY0-003B-H432-00000-00&context=1530671), requires that a principal have authorized or ratified the tortious conduct, even when the conduct is that of a managerial agent. The _Lake Shore_ standard has been characterized as one of "strict complicity." See _Muratore v. M/S Scotia Prince, 845 F.2d 347, 355 (1st Cir.1988)_ (explaining distinction between _Lake Shore_ standard and Restatement Second, Torts § 909; declining to determine whether First Circuit should follow other circuits and adopt expanded standard of § 909 when tortfeasors did not occupy managerial positions and no evidence of authorization or ratification by principal, the bareboat charterer of a cruise vessel). See also _CEH, Inc. v. F/V Seafarer, 70 F.3d 694, 704-705 (1st Cir.1995)_ (adopting modified version of Restatement Second, Torts § 909, imposing liability for act of managerial agent acting in scope of employment). The _Lake Shore_ standard originated in [_The Amiable Nancy, 16 U.S. (3 Wheat.) 546 (1818)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-KS70-003B-H0HW-00000-00&context=1530671), in which crew members of the Scourge, an American privateer commissioned to act as a private armed vessel during the War of 1812, stole goods from the Amiable Nancy, a neutral Haitian vessel, when the Scourge's captain sent his crew aboard only to check the Amiable Nancy's papers. The Court, although recognizing that just compensation should be made to "unoffending neutrals," held that the Scourge's owners were not liable for punitive damages because "\[t\]hey are innocent of the demerit of this transaction, having neither directed it, nor countenanced it, nor participated in it in the slightest degree." [_Id. at 559-560_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-KS70-003B-H0HW-00000-00&context=1530671). Although the explicit basis for the Court's analysis in _The Amiable Nancy_ was the desire not to frustrate the government's policy of commissioning privateers to assist in the War of 1812, subsequent cases interpreted the case's holding more broadly. See [_In re Exxon Valdez, 270 F.3d 1215, 1233-1234 (9th Cir.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:44CX-9VD0-0038-X0YK-00000-00&context=1530671). Admiralty courts may apply a more restrictive standard for punitive damages than do their state-court counterparts. See _Jones v. Compagnie Generale Mar., 882 F.Supp. 1079, 1086 & n.5 (S.D.Ga. 1995)_ (contrasting _In re P & E Boat Rentals, Inc., 872 F.2d 642, 652 (5th Cir.1989)_ ("punitive damages may not be imposed against a corporation when one or more of its employees decides on his own to engage in malicious or outrageous conduct," relying on _Lake Shore_) with Georgia common-law rule that "principals are liable for punitive damages if their agents acted in the course of or in connection with their employment and the agents' behavior itself warranted a punitive award").

When an agent commits a tort in the course of performing a nondelegable duty of the principal, punitive damages may be awarded against the principal on the basis that the agent's act is in effect the act of the principal itself. See _Apache Corp. v. Moore, 891 S.W.2d 671, 683 (Tex.App.1994)_, punitive award reduced on remand, [_960 S.W.2d 746 (Tex.App.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RJN-3TN0-0039-41GS-00000-00&context=1530671) (punitive damages properly awarded against well operator when agent was grossly negligent in maintaining wells, leading to blowout and damage to adjacent property); [_Missouri Pac. R. Co. v. Lemon, 861 S.W.2d 501, 521 (Tex.App.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-15R0-003C-2020-00000-00&context=1530671) (compliance with requirement that grade crossing not be obstructed was a nondelegable duty of railroad; crew of railroad would be characterized as railroad's vice-principals as matter of law due to nature of duty, and award of punitive damages against railroad sustained even in absence of evidence of authorization or ratification by vice-principal). Under Texas law, the term "vice principal" encompasses a corporate officer; personnel "with authority to employ, direct, and discharge" employees; personnel engaged in performing a principal's nondelegable duty; and personnel to whom an employer "has confided the management of the whole or a department or a division of the business." [_Mobil Oil Corp. v. Ellender, 968 S.W.2d 917, 922 (Tex.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SN6-M9S0-0039-43FJ-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 1 comes from Restatement Second, Torts § 909, Illustration 1. The point at which a principal is sufficiently associated with the consequences of its conduct may be open to dispute. See [_Denver & Rio Grande Ry. Co. v. Harris, 122 U.S. 597 (1887)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-H610-003B-H1P7-00000-00&context=1530671) (affirming award of punitive damages against railroad that used large armed force to dispossess employees of another railroad from road; plaintiff-employee of other railroad injured in assault); [_Brueckner v. Norwich Univ., 730 A.2d 1086, 1096-1097 (Vt. 1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3VS3-9H70-0039-40HX-00000-00&context=1530671) (reversing award of punitive damages against military college when upperclass leaders of freshman orientation acted within scope of employment in hazing freshmen, college was aware of prior incidents of hazing, and college had rules prohibiting hazing and investigated reported incidents; even if college was "'indifferent'" to health and safety of freshmen, "indifference attributable to negligence is not malice," and punitive damages may not be imposed on basis of conduct that heedlessly disregards consequences).

On the position of a principal who engages an expert agent, see [_Wyatt v. Wehmueller, 806 P.2d 870 (Ariz. 1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-WF30-003F-T0TB-00000-00&context=1530671) (purchasers of real estate not subject to statutory liability for treble damages when their lawyer wrongfully filed a notice of lis pendens; improper to subject client to award of treble damages when client did not at least acquiesce in lawyer's wrongdoing and statute requires scienter on part of wrongdoer). The court also noted that awarding treble damages against the client would not further the statutory purpose of "deterring individuals from knowingly filing groundless lis pendens claims. If the underlying rationale is deterrence rather than compensation of actual loss, the punitive nature of § 33-420 militates against permitting the scienter requirement to be satisfied by imputed knowledge. If the lawyer is guilty and the client innocent, the lawyer may be punished, and deterred, by an award of attorney's fees to be paid by him personally . . . . There is, however, no deterrent value in a rule that punishes an unknowing, innocent client." [_Id. at 875-876_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-WF30-003F-T0TB-00000-00&context=1530671).

On the definition of "managerial agent," see [_Egan v. Mutual of Omaha Ins. Co., 620 P.2d 141, 148 (Cal.1979)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S11-S160-003C-R0RC-00000-00&context=1530671) (question is whether employee has discretion to make "decisions that will ultimately determine corporate policy"; employee may act in a managerial capacity, despite the employee's title: "'\[d\]efendant should not be allowed to insulate itself from liability by giving an employee a nonmanagerial title and relegating to him crucial policy decisions,'" quoting [_Merlo v. Standard Life & Accident Ins. Co., 130 Cal.Rptr. 416, 429 (Cal.App.1976)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S11-V040-003C-R512-00000-00&context=1530671) (Tamura, J., dissenting in part)); [_Ginda v. Exel Logistics, Inc., 42 F.Supp.2d 1019, 1022 (E.D.Cal.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3W95-HGK0-0038-Y1P3-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying _Egan;_ employer's general manager, who had "virtually unlimited discretion" in handling employee complaints of harassment, was a managerial agent); [_Brashear v. Packers, 883 P.2d 1278, 1280 (N.M.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W1M0-003D-D1FR-00000-00&context=1530671) ("'\[m\]anaging agent' is a recognized term of art and is defined as one who has sufficient 'discretionary or policymaking authority' such that the agent may speak and act independently of higher corporate authority," quoting [_Albuquerque Concrete Coring Co. v. Pan Am World Servs., Inc., 879 P.2d 772, 778 (N.M.1994))_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W1S0-003D-D1JP-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Hammerly Oaks, Inc. v. Edwards, 958 S.W.2d 387, 392 (Tex.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RHR-WD80-0039-43P1-00000-00&context=1530671) (leasing agent for apartment complex, whose duties included showing apartments to prospective tenants and preparing paperwork, was not a "vice principal," i.e., a managerial agent). An influential formulation is whether an agent, although not the highest officer in a hierarchy, is "a person of such responsibility as to arouse the 'institutional conscience.'" _Doralee Estates, Inc. v. Cities Serv. Oil Co., 569 F.2d 716, 722 (2d Cir.1977)_. See also [_Lightning Lube, Inc. v. Witco Corp., 4 F.3d 1153, 1192-1193 (3d Cir.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-CY80-003B-P3VW-00000-00&context=1530671) (upholding district-court finding that no member of defendant's top management participated in or knew of acts of tortious interference perpetrated by defendant's salespersons against plaintiff). Focusing on an actor's organizational status may warrant imposing punitive damages when a managerial agent commits a tort while performing a nonmanagerial task. See [_Ramos v. Frito-Lay, Inc., 784 S.W.2d 667 (Tex.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W9W0-003C-21D7-00000-00&context=1530671) (sales manager, driving delivery truck to replace vacationing driver, assaulted owner of convenience store during altercation over ownership of display rack).

If a principal delegates management responsibility to another organization, the managing organization is a "managerial agent" for purposes of Restatement Second, Torts § 909. See _CEH, Inc. v. F/V Seafarer, 880 F.Supp. 940, 956 (D.R.I.1995)_, aff'd, _70 F.3d 694, 705 (1st Cir.1995)_ (owner of trawling vessel liable for punitive damages to owner of lobster vessel when captain of trawling vessel acted with malice in destroying lobster traps and other equipment; "\[o\]wner knew that he had hired his captains to work in an atmosphere characterized in part by the tension that raged between lobstermen and draggers. \[Owner's\] delegation of nearly absolute managerial authority to a captain in this atmosphere does not free him from liability. In fact, it engenders it.").

Statutes may articulate standards for the imposition of punitive damages against a principal. In [_Smith v. Printup, 866 P.2d 985 (Kan.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-33N0-003F-D178-00000-00&context=1530671), the court interpreted Kan. Stat. Ann. § 60-3701(d)(1), which provides that "\[i\]n no case shall exemplary or punitive damages be assessed pursuant to this section against: (1) \[a\] principal or employer for the acts of an agent or employee unless the questioned conduct was authorized or ratified by a person expressly empowered to do so on behalf of the principal or employer. . . ." The court held that authorization for this purpose "may be either express or implied and generally is accomplished before or during the employee's questioned conduct. It may be based on an express grant of authority or on a course of conduct indicating that the employee was empowered or given the right or authority to engage in the questioned conduct. Ratification . . . may be either express or implied and may be accomplished before, during, or after the employee's questioned conduct. It may be based on an express ratification or based on a course of conduct indicating the approval, sanctioning, or confirmation of the questioned conduct." [_Id. at 1003_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-33N0-003F-D178-00000-00&context=1530671). The court's summary of the statute's import is that "knowledge of an employee's wrongful conduct, coupled with failure to discipline the employee, amounts to implied ratification or authorization." [_Id. at 1002_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-33N0-003F-D178-00000-00&context=1530671). For interpretations of other statutes, see [_Khalid Bin Talal v. E.F. Hutton & Co., 720 F.Supp. 671, 683 (N.D.Ill.1989)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-BGX0-0054-41CK-00000-00&context=1530671) (company's awareness of employee's illegal trading activity constitutes authorization and ratification); [_Hartman v. Shell Oil Co., 137 Cal.Rptr. 244 (Cal.App.1977)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S11-TS00-003C-R3XC-00000-00&context=1530671) (managers aware of employee's fraud but do not discharge or discipline employee); [_Matthiessen v. Vanech, 836 A.2d 394, 406-407 (Conn.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4B7N-XC60-0039-44TC-00000-00&context=1530671) (nothing in language of Conn. Gen. Stat. § 52-183 suggests that nonoperator owner of motor vehicle may be subject to punitive damages; statute simply relieves plaintiff of burden of going forward with evidence of agency relationship between vehicle's owner and its operator); [_Wirig v. Kinney Shoe Corp., 448 N.W.2d 526, 534 (Minn.App.1989)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-8HB0-003F-V4WV-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd in part, rev'd in part on other grounds, [_461 N.W.2d 374 (Minn.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-4G30-003F-V18K-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporation implicitly ratified employee's harassment of coworker). See also [_Berrier v. Bizer, 57 S.W.3d 271 (Ky.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4449-4JY0-0039-41X7-00000-00&context=1530671) (interpreting Ky. Rev. Stat. § 411.184(3), which provides that "\[i\]n no case shall punitive damages be assessed against a principal or an employer for the act of an employee or agent unless such principal authorized or ratified or should have anticipated the conduct in question"; in contrast with statutes in other states, court notes breadth of limitation imposed by Kentucky statute).

Under Cal. Civ. Code § 3294(b) (1997 & Supp. 2005), an employer is liable for punitive damages "based on the acts of an employee" if the employer "had advance knowledge of the unfitness of the employee and employed him or her with a conscious disregard of the rights or safety of others or authorized or ratified the wrongful conduct for which the damages are awarded or was personally guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice." If the employer is a corporation, the conduct "must be on the part of an officer, director, or managing agent. . . ." See [_White v. Ultramar, Inc., 981 P.2d 944, 947 (Cal.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3X7M-NNR0-0039-413B-00000-00&context=1530671) (statutory term "managing agent" means "only those corporate employees who exercise substantial independent authority and judgment in their corporate decisionmaking so that their decisions ultimately determine corporate policy"; scope of an employee's discretion and authority is a question of fact). Additionally, an employer is not subject to liability for punitive damages for a tort committed by a managing agent unless the tort is committed within the scope of employment. See [_College Hosp., Inc. v. Superior Court, 882 P.2d 894, 906 (Cal.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-1NJ0-003D-J2CK-00000-00&context=1530671) (hospital not subject to liability when cardiopulmonary supervisor engaged in consensual, on-premises sexual affair with adult patient not under his care; whatever the meaning of "managing agent" in statute, "the concept assumes that such individual was _acting in a corporate or employment capacity_ when the conduct giving rise to the punitive damages claim against the employer occurred.") (emphasis in original).

On statutory purpose in the construction of statutes providing for punitive or treble damages, see [_Kolstad v. American Dental Ass'n, 527 U.S. 526, 544 (1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WSC-DW90-004B-Y01S-00000-00&context=1530671) (punitive damages may not be awarded against an employer for intentional employment discrimination under [_42 U.S.C. § 1981a_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SHT-0712-D6RV-H524-00000-00&context=1530671), on basis of vicarious liability for decisions of managerial employees, when managerial employees' decisions are contrary to employer's "good-faith efforts to comply with Title VII"; punitive damages based on vicarious liability would reduce incentives for employers to implement antidiscrimination programs by leading courts to believe that employer was aware of Title VII's requirements) and [_American Soc'y of Mech. Eng'rs v. Hydrolevel Corp., 456 U.S. 556, 572-573 (1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-5J60-003B-S04K-00000-00&context=1530671) (treble damages may be awarded under _15 U.S.C. § 15_ against principal when its agent-officers violated federal antitrust laws, acting with apparent authority; if principal is subject to liability, in addition to agents' liability, "it is much more likely that similar antitrust violations will not occur in the future. . . . Only \[principal\] can take systematic steps to make improper conduct on the part of all its agents unlikely, and the possibility of civil liability will inevitably be a powerful incentive for \[principal\] to take those steps. Thus, a rule that imposes liability on the \[principal\] . . . is most faithful to the congressional intent that the private right of action deter antitrust violations."). _Kolstad_ does not cite _Hydrolevel. Kolstad_ has been criticized for failing to give sufficient weight to the "broader deterrent purpose of Title VII and the 1991 \[Civil Rights\] Act and to Congress's decision that 'the best way to induce Title VII compliance is by providing a negative incentive--punishment--to those who engage in intentional discrimination.'" See The Supreme Court, 1998 Term--Leading Cases, [_113 Harv. L. Rev. 359, 365, 367 (1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3XYF-F0H0-00CV-50P7-00000-00&context=1530671). On the possible significance of Kolstad's failure to cite _Hydrolevel,_ see [_United States v. Southern Maryland Home Health Servs., Inc., 95 F.Supp.2d 465, 472 (D.Md.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:408S-2B90-0038-Y223-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporation not vicariously liable for a civil penalty under False Claims Act, [_31 U.S.C. § 3729(a)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SCB-BFD2-8T6X-70GG-00000-00&context=1530671), for act of low-level employee acting within the scope of her employment in absence of proof that employer authorized or ratified act or acted with knowledge of or reckless indifference; although statute does not refer to "punitive damages" the reasoning of _Kolstad_ is nonetheless applicable, especially statements questioning vicarious liability, which call into question _Hydrolevel). Southern Maryland_ acknowledges that _Kolstad_ and _Hydrolevel_ involve different statutes, id., and that its holding is inconsistent "with the majority of cases that have addressed this specific issue," [_id. at 473_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:408S-2B90-0038-Y223-00000-00&context=1530671).
