[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 7.04_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8X0-00YG-400H-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 7- Torts--Liability of Agent and Principal_ > _Topic 2- Principal's Liability_**

**§ 7.04 Agent Acts with Actual Authority**

**A principal is subject to liability to a third party harmed by an agent's conduct when the agent's conduct is within the scope of the agent's actual authority or ratified by the principal; and**

**(1) the agent's conduct is tortious, or**

**(2) the agent's conduct, if that of the principal, would subject the principal to tort liability.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section covers the liability of a principal when an agent's actions harm a third party and are within the scope of the agent's actual authority or are ratified by the principal. Comment _b_ discusses the applicability of this general principle, including its applicability to relationships that are not ones of agency. Comment _c_ discusses a principal's tort-law liability when an agent is not personally subject to liability.

Section 2.01 defines actual authority; § 2.02 states how the scope of an agent's actual authority is determined.

_b. In general._ When an agent acts with actual authority, the agent reasonably believes, in accordance with manifestations of the principal, that the principal wishes the agent so to act. See § 2.01. If a principal ratifies an agent's conduct, the principal assents to be affected by the legal consequences of the action. See § 4.01(2). The legal consequences created by ratification are those of actual authority. See § 4.01, Comment _b._ If an agent's action within the scope of the agent's actual authority harms a third party, the principal is subject to liability if the agent's conduct is tortious. If the agent's conduct is not tortious, the principal is subject to liability if the same conduct on the part of the principal would have subjected the principal to tort liability. For example, an agent's action may not be tortious because the agent lacks information known to the principal. In either situation, the agent is the instrumentality through which the principal achieves the principal's objective. If the agent uses means other than those intended by the principal, the principal is subject to liability if the agent's choice of means is within the scope of the agent's actual authority. See § 2.02.

The rule stated in this section is applicable even when the relationship between two persons is not a relationship of agency as defined in § 1.01. Under general tort-law principles, a person who orders or induces an actor's tortious conduct is subject to liability for harm resulting to a third person when the person "knows or should know of circumstances that would make the conduct tortious if it were his own. . . ." See Restatement Second, Torts § 877. When a person is subject to liability as a consequence of this rule, it is immaterial whether the relationship between the person and the actor is a relationship of agency as defined in § 1.01.

**Illustrations:**

1\. P, who publishes a newspaper, engages D to deliver copies of the newspaper to a neighboring community. P directs D to deliver the newspapers in the midst of an ice storm when both P and D know that driving conditions are hazardous, that the vehicle D will use is not equipped for such conditions, and that D lacks experience in driving under such conditions. In the course of making deliveries, D skids on an icy road, injuring T. P is subject to liability to T. P's instruction to D directed D to act in a negligent manner. D is also subject to liability to T. See § 7.01.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that P contracts with A Corporation to provide delivery services. D is an employee of A Corporation. D's supervisor, also an employee of A Corporation, relays P's instruction to D. P is subject to liability to T, even if P's contract with A Corporation does not establish a relationship of agency as defined in § 1.01. P's instruction to D, relayed through A Corporation, directed D to act in a negligent manner. D is also subject to liability to T. See § 7.01. A Corporation is also subject to liability to T. D acted within the scope of D's employment. See § 7.07.

3\. P, who owns Blackacre Towers, leases an apartment in it to T. P then wishes instead to lease T's apartment to S. P tells S to enter T's apartment and remove and destroy T's personal property. S does so. Entering T's apartment and removing and destroying T's personal property is tortious. P is subject to liability to T. S is also subject to liability to T whether or not S acted as P's agent.

If a result follows from a directive to engage in tortious conduct and the person who gives the directive intends that result, it is immaterial to the person's liability that the person did not contemplate the particular tortious means through which the result was accomplished. See Restatement Second, Torts § 877, Comment _a._

**Illustration:**

4\. Same facts as Illustration 3, except that S pays F to enter T's apartment and remove and destroy T's personal property. F, P, and S are subject to liability to T. F committed a tort at S's direction. P intended the result that followed from F's conduct, although P did not contemplate that F as opposed to S enter T's apartment.

It may seem problematic to characterize an agent's tortious conduct as falling within the scope of the agent's actual authority because in ordinary usage characterizing an action as "authorized" connotes that it is done rightfully, which is not the case from the perspective of a third party who is harmed by a tort committed by an agent. However, an agent acts with actual authority when the agent reasonably believes, on the basis of a manifestation of the principal, that the principal wishes the agent so to act. Tortious conduct by an agent thus may be consistent with the agent's actual authority. That does not, of course, immunize either the agent or the principal from liability to a third party harmed by the agent's conduct. On an agent's liability, see § 7.01.

_c. When actor is not personally subject to liability._ A person may be subject to tort liability because of an actor's conduct although the actor is not subject to liability. For example, a person who directs conduct may have notice of facts that the actor lacks.

**Illustration:**

5\. P, in the produce business, directs A to make a delivery to a customer using a particular truck. P knows that the truck's brakes do not work properly. A does not know this and has no reason or duty to know it. T, a pedestrian, is injured when the truck's brakes fail while A is driving the truck to make the delivery and attempts to stop at a stop sign. P is subject to liability to T. A is not subject to liability to T.

If a principal is subject to liability under the rule stated in this section, the principal's liability is independent of any liability to which the agent may be subject.

**Illustration:**

6\. P, who deals in antiques, employs A as a salesperson. P tells A that a table is a 17th-century work. A, who reasonably believes P's statement to be true, repeats the statement to T. T, relying on A's statement, buys the table. In fact, as P knows, the table is a 19th-century reproduction. P is subject to liability to T. A is not subject to liability to T.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency, and codifications._ This section corresponds to Restatement Second, Agency § 212. Section 212, entitled "Principal Intends Conduct or Consequences," states that "\[a\] person is subject to liability for the consequences of another's conduct which results from his directions as he would be for his own personal conduct if, with knowledge of the conditions, he intends the conduct, or if he intends its consequences, unless the one directing or the one acting has a privilege or immunity not available to the other." Although the Comments to § 212 frequently refer to a principal "directing" an agent's conduct, overall the Comments are consistent with the apparently broader formulation in this section. Indeed, Comment _e,_ captioned _"Where intent is not expressed,"_ discusses the relationship between principal and agent in terms that are almost synonymous with the scope of actual authority as defined in § 2.02 ("\[a\] master may direct a servant to do an act although his commands literally interpreted are that the servant shall not do it. In determining what the direction to the servant is, the entire conduct of the master and his relation to the servant must be considered. One cannot shield himself by a form of words. Likewise, a master may intend that the servant shall do an act although he forbids the servant to do it; it may be evident that the master intends what he professes not to desire.").

Although codified treatments of agency do not contain a precise counterpart to this section, they recognize the possibility that a principal may be subject to liability because the principal has authorized or directed an agent's tortious conduct. Cal. Civ. Code § 2338 (1985 & Supp. 2005) provides that "\[a\] principal is responsible to third persons for the negligence of his agent in the transaction of the business of the agency, including wrongful acts committed by such agent in and as a part of the transaction of such business, and for his wilful omission to fulfill the obligations of the principal." Under § 2339, "\[a\] principal is responsible for no other wrongs committed by his agent than those mentioned in the last section, unless he has authorized or ratified them, even though they are committed while the agent is engaged in his service." Under Ga. Code § 10-6-61 (2000 & Supp. 2005), "\[t\]he principal shall not be liable for the willful trespass of his agent unless done by his command or assented to by him."

_b. In general._ For examples of ratification in this context, see, e.g., [_Lee v. United States, 171 F.Supp.2d 566, 577 (M.D.N.C.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4586-F3Y0-0038-Y1T9-00000-00&context=1530671) (employer ratified managerial employee's assault through affidavit of plant manager establishing that plant manager believed employee was acting within scope of employment at the time of assault); LaBozzo v. Brooks Bros., Inc., 2002 WL 1275155 (N.Y.Sup. 2002) (factfinder might conclude that upper-echelon executives ratified the tortiously abusive conduct of a vice president when executive vice president witnessed incident but failed to repudiate it on corporation's behalf).

For the basic point made by Illustrations 1 and 2, see, e.g., [_Paulson v. Madison Newspapers, Inc., 80 N.W.2d 421 (Wis.1957)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRN-0TV0-003V-H29R-00000-00&context=1530671) (newspaper publisher directed bulk delivery of sacks of papers in careless manner that resulted in injury to plaintiff, a pedestrian).

If an agent's tortious conduct is within the scope of the agent's actual authority, the agent has no duty to indemnify the principal against loss arising from the agent's conduct. See [_Putnam v. DeRosa, 963 F.2d 480, 485 (1st Cir.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-4000-008H-V2MV-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Massachusetts law).

For the general point that a principal is subject to liability for conduct of its agent when the agent takes action with actual authority, see [_Alexander v. Local 496, Laborer's Int'l Union of N. Am., 177 F.3d 394, 409-410 (6th Cir.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WC4-1JV0-0038-X0T4-00000-00&context=1530671) (international union both directly and vicariously liable for discrimination practiced by local union; international union's direct liability based on its own Uniform Local Constitution, which required membership policy for local union that had disparate impact on African Americans and on international union's affirmative duty to oppose local's discriminatory practices when local is an agent of international; "\[w\]e are baffled and amazed as to how \[international union\] can contend that it did not instigate, support, ratify, or encourage a policy that it created"); [_Chubb & Son Inc. v. Consoli, 726 N.Y.S.2d 398, 400 (App.Div.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4347-61T0-0039-42NT-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurance adjuster acted within scope of actual or apparent authority in preparing and submitting claim for insureds). In this context, "primary" is a synonym for "direct" liability. See Robert Stevens, Why Do Agents "Drop Out"?, \[2005\] Lloyd's Mar. & Com. L.Q. 101, 104 ("If I authorize a statement knowing it to be untrue, I will be liable in deceit, regardless of whether the person who actually speaks is wholly innocent. My liability is primary, not vicarious.").

For the basic point that direction of conduct is a basis for liability in the absence of a formal relationship of agency or employment between the person who provides directions and the person who executes them, see [_Givens v. Mullikin, 75 S.W.3d 383, 395 (Tenn.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45G1-0GJ0-0039-40RP-00000-00&context=1530671) (although insurer lacked right to control a lawyer retained to defend an insured, insurer who exercises actual control over lawyer is subject to liability to insured for lawyer's tortious acts or omissions that are "directed, commanded, or knowingly authorized" by the insurer).

The rule stated in this section does not subject a person to liability when the person is not shown to have directed the actor's tortious conduct. See [_National Bank of Commerce v. HCA Health Servs. of Midwest, Inc., 800 S.W.2d 694, 697 (Ark.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-XFD0-003C-14V1-00000-00&context=1530671) (summary judgment properly granted in favor of parent corporation in action for injuries sustained by patient in nursery of hospital owned and operated by subsidiary corporation; although liability theory in case was that proximate cause of patient's injuries was understaffed condition of nursery, and that hospital's manager failed to provide more nursing staff despite complaints, no evidence indicated that parent corporation was responsible for staffing level in nursery; issue moot when jury found alleged agent not liable).

For the point that liability is not dependent on whether a relationship of agency connects a person who directs a tortious act and the actor, see, e.g., [_Campbell v. Preston, 379 S.W.2d 557, 561-562 (Mo.1964)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRH-NHF0-003F-B0DY-00000-00&context=1530671) (physician who directed intern and nurses to administer drug to hospitalized patient subject to liability when drug administered contrary to label advising administration by only a qualified anesthesiologist; "\[w\]here a person actively participates as by expressly ordering or directing the act which proves to be negligent or wrongful, such person is liable to a third person damaged thereby even though the relation of employer and employee does not exist between the one who directs the act and the one who performs it.").

Illustration 3 is based on [_Levin v. Lynn, 708 A.2d 454 (N.J.Super.App.Div.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SJV-PHH0-0039-41WB-00000-00&context=1530671). The court does not discuss explicitly whether the prospective tenant acted as the landlord's agent, but characterizes the landlord's liability as a consequence of "a basic principle of agency law . . . \[c\]ertainly if \[landlord\] had, herself, entered the apartment and physically removed and destroyed plaintiff's property, she would be liable for the damages prescribed in the unlawful entry and detainer statutes." [_Id. at 458-459_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SJV-PHH0-0039-41WB-00000-00&context=1530671).

In Restatement Second, Agency, the counterpart to Illustration 4 is Illustration 2 to § 212, which states: "P directs A to bribe B to destroy T's machinery. Not being able to find B, A personally destroys the machinery. P is subject to liability to T."

_c. When actor is not personally subject to liability._ Illustration 5 is derived from Illustration 5 to Restatement Second, Agency § 212.

Although an agent acts without actual or apparent authority, the principal may be subject to liability to a third party harmed by the agent's conduct when the principal acted as a joint tortfeasor. See [_Heffernan v. Marine Midland Bank, N.A., 701 N.Y.S.2d 4, 6 (App.Div.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3Y46-93R0-0039-43VY-00000-00&context=1530671) (if bank had actual notice that employee perpetrated Ponzi scheme against plaintiffs and cooperated in scheme to misappropriate plaintiffs' trust funds on deposit with it, bank would be subject to liability as joint tortfeasor; rate of return promised by employee so extraordinary that plaintiffs acted unreasonably in failing to inquire into the scope of employee's actual authority).

Releasing an agent from liability may not always release the principal as well. See [_Zimprich v. North Dakota Harvestore Sys., Inc., 419 N.W.2d 912, 914 (N.D.1988)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-5B30-003G-92T0-00000-00&context=1530671) (plaintiff's release of claims against retailer does not release claims against finance company that directed retailer to repossess storage system; "\[f\]or directing another to commit wrongful conduct \[finance company\] can be held directly liable apart from any theory of vicarious liability which may apply"). See also [_York v. InTrust Bank, N.A., 962 P.2d 405, 417-418 (Kan.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3T6Y-DYK0-0039-419V-00000-00&context=1530671) (covenant not to sue that plaintiff executed in settlement with real-estate developer and marketer does not release liability of financial institution alleged to have independently and willfully violated statute prohibiting fraud in consumer transactions).
