[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 7.05_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8Y0-00YG-400J-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 7- Torts--Liability of Agent and Principal_ > _Topic 2- Principal's Liability_**

**§ 7.05 Principal's Negligence in Conducting Activity Through Agent; Principal's Special Relationship with Another Person**

**(1) A principal who conducts an activity through an agent is subject to liability for harm to a third party caused by the agent's conduct if the harm was caused by the principal's negligence in selecting, training, retaining, supervising, or otherwise controlling the agent.**

**(2) When a principal has a special relationship with another person, the principal owes that person a duty of reasonable care with regard to risks arising out of the relationship, including the risk that agents of the principal will harm the person with whom the principal has such a special relationship.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ The rules stated in this section are specific instances of general tort-law principles. An actor in a special relationship with another owes a duty of reasonable care to third persons with regard to risks posed by the other that arise out of the relationship. Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 41 (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005). An actor in a special relationship with another also owes that person a duty of reasonable care with regard to risks that arise within the scope of the relationship. Id. § 40. Although the rules stated in this section apply to principals who deploy employees or other agents to act on their behalf, the general principles are applicable regardless of whether a person conducts an activity through an employee or other agent. Comment _b_ discusses the rationale for the rule stated in subsection (1). Comment _c_ examines issues of causation and duty. Comment _d_ examines when a risk is foreseeable and when it is reasonable to take precaution against a risk. Comment _e_ discusses a principal's liability under the rule stated in subsection (2) when a person to whom the principal owes a duty of reasonable care is harmed by an agent or employee of the principal.

_b. In general; relationship to other bases for liability._ The rules stated in this section stem from general doctrines of tort law not limited in their applicability to relationships of agency as defined in § 1.01. A person owes a duty of reasonable care to third persons when the person has a special relationship with another, with regard to risks arising within the scope of the relationship. Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 41 (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005). A person's conduct is negligent "if the person does not exercise reasonable care under all the circumstances." Id. § 3. The most important circumstances are the foreseeable likelihood that conduct will result in harm, the foreseeable severity of that harm, and the burdens imposed by precautions to eliminate or reduce the possibility of harm. Id.

A foreseeable risk of harm may be created when one person conducts an activity through another person. For example, the actor chosen for a task may lack competence to perform it without endangering others. A task may require using an instrumentality that is dangerous to others unless the user has appropriate skill or supervision. Some tasks require performance in settings that pose a foreseeable risk of criminal or other intentional misconduct against third parties or their property unless the actor is chosen with due care in reference to that risk.

**Illustrations:**

1\. P, who owns an apartment building, employs A as its on-site manager. P knows that A is impatient and has a violent temper. T, one of P's tenants, complains to A about the lack of heat in T's apartment. Enraged, A assaults T. P is subject to liability to T. P hired A knowing that A's temperament was not suited to the foreseeable demands of on-site residential management. P's liability to T under this section is independent of whether P is subject to liability to T under § 7.07 on the basis that A's conduct was within the scope of A's employment by P.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that P instructs A to make repairs to the building's exterior. P knows that A is incompetent as a builder but is inexpensive. A is negligent in repairing the exterior of P's building, and, due to A's negligence, a portion of it falls on and injures T, a pedestrian on an adjacent sidewalk. P is subject to liability to T. It was reasonably foreseeable to P that A would perform repairs in a manner that would create risks to persons such as T. P is also subject to liability to T as stated in § 7.07.

3\. P, who owns a furniture store, employs A to deliver furniture to retail customers. A's duties include entering customers' homes to situate items they have purchased. Having entered T's home to deliver a sofa, A assaults T. Prior to employing A, P conducted no check of A's background. Had P done so, P would have discovered criminal convictions for assault. Had P known of A's criminal history, P would not have employed A to make deliveries. P is subject to liability to T.

Ordinarily, a person has a duty to exercise reasonable care when the actor's conduct poses a risk of physical harm to another. Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 7(a) (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005). Liability for purely economic harm, in contrast, is often governed by contract-law rules or tort-law rules applicable to misrepresentation. As a consequence of this basic limitation on negligence-based liability, cases imposing liability on the basis of the rule stated in this section typically involve physical harm to a third person or a third person's property, or harm stemming from misrepresentations made by the actor through whom the defendant conducted activities.

It is not a defense to liability under this rule that the actor whose conduct harms a third party does not have a relationship of agency as defined in § 1.01 with the person who conducted an activity through the actor.

**Illustration:**

4\. Same facts as Illustration 2, except that A is the employee of Contract Building Services, Inc. P knows of A's incompetence based on prior dealings with A. A is neither P's agent nor P's employee. P is subject to liability to T. P's liability to T for P's own negligence in selecting A does not turn on whether T can establish that P and A had a relationship of agency or employment.

The basis for liability under this rule is distinct from other bases for liability. In particular, an employer may be subject to liability under this rule for injury caused by tortious conduct of an employee acting outside the scope of employment. Such conduct does not subject an employer to vicarious liability under the rule stated in § 7.07(1).

_c. Causation and duty._ Liability under this rule is limited by basic principles of tort law, including requirements of causation and duty.

**Illustration:**

5\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that P reasonably believes that A's temperament is suited to the duties of on-site apartment management on the basis of P's inquiries to A's prior employers. However, P does not check with law-enforcement authorities. Had P so checked, P would have discovered that A has been convicted of income-tax evasion. P would not have viewed that conviction as material to A's duties as an on-site manager, which do not involve tax-compliance tasks, and P would have employed A had P known of A's tax-evasion conviction. P is not subject to liability to T as a consequence of A's assault on T. Even if P acted negligently in failing to check A's record with law-enforcement authorities, P's failure to detect A's tax-evasion conviction was not the factual cause of harm to T and did not increase the risk of an assault by A against T.

Liability under this rule also requires some nexus or causal connection between the principal's negligence in selecting or controlling an actor, the actor's employment or work, and the harm suffered by the third party. If the actor's tort is causally unrelated to the actor's employment by the defendant, this rule does not subject the defendant to liability to a third party injured by the actor's tort although the defendant was negligent in employing or retaining the actor. When the actor's tort follows termination of the actor's employment, the tort and the employment may not be causally related for purposes of this rule. Likewise, when the actor's tort occurs in the course of an extramural activity unrelated to the actor's employment, the tort may lack a sufficient causal relationship to the actor's employment.

**Illustrations:**

6\. A is a sales associate of P & Associates, a real-estate firm. A tells T, who has purchased a property listed for sale with P & Associates, that A has a valuable investment opportunity for T. A tells T that P & Associates has nothing to do with A's investment business and that investments should be made by check payable to A and sent to A's residence. All of A's contacts with T take place off the premises of P & Associates. T gives A money for the investment. A does not make the investment, instead using T's funds for A's own purposes. A is subject to liability to T. Even if T can establish that P & Associates was negligent in employing A, P & Associates is not subject to liability to T.

7\. Same facts as Illustration 6, except that A's conversations with T take place on P and Associates' premises, where A is permitted to conduct A's investment business. When T inquires about the success of A's investment strategies, A refers T to S, A's immediate supervisor at P & Associates, who confirms A's investment successes. Under applicable law, P & Associates was negligent in supervising A. P & Associates is subject to liability to T. A's employment is causally connected to A's tortious conduct toward T, who had a business relationship with P & Associates.

A person is not subject to liability to a third party harmed by an actor's tortious conduct unless the person owes a duty of care to the third party. If a defendant owed no duty of care to a plaintiff, the defendant's negligence in hiring or retaining the actor does not subject the defendant to liability to the plaintiff.

_d. Foreseeable risk and reasonable precaution._ Conduct that results in harm to a third person is not negligent or reckless unless there is a foreseeable likelihood that harm will result from the conduct. A determination that a person acted negligently also requires consideration of the foreseeable severity of harm to a third person. Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 3 (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005). When a principal conducts an activity through another person, the nature of the task to be performed and the conduct required for performance are relevant to whether the principal acted negligently, either in selecting the actor or in instructing, supervising, or otherwise controlling the actor.

**Illustration:**

8\. T Corporation, which manufactures gold-framed sunglasses, maintains an inventory of gold required for its manufacturing processes. T Corporation contracts with P Corporation to provide security for its manufacturing facility. The contract requires P Corporation to provide security officers on a continuous basis for T Corporation's facility. A, a part-time employee of P Corporation, is assigned to guard duty at T Corporation's facility on weekends. Prior to employing A, P Corporation determined that A had no prior criminal record and sent reference forms to the high school from which A graduated and to A's prior employer, a hospital where A worked as an emergency-room technician for one month. Neither form sought comments pertaining to A's honesty and trustworthiness. P Corporation did not contact the character references provided by A and did not seek any affirmative confirmation of A's honesty. On guard duty at T Corporation's facility, A admits confederates, who steal large quantities of gold from T Corporation's inventory. P Corporation is subject to liability to T Corporation. P Corporation's conduct in hiring A was not reasonable under the circumstances. The circumstances of A's task placed A in charge of valuable and easily transportable property of T Corporation.

A principal may also act negligently in how it deals with an actor whom it has selected for a task. For example, in Illustration 8, in which the allure of easy but dishonest wealth is associated with A's task, A's employer may fail to take reasonable care unless it trains and supervises A in a manner sensitive to the risks inherent in guarding valuable property that is readily moveable. A principal's duty to exercise reasonable care extends to the principal's supervision of the actors who carry out activities on its behalf. The type of supervision required by the principal's duty of reasonable care, like its relative degree of intensity, are functions of the risk posed to third parties and the burdens imposed by precautions that the principal may take in reference to the risk.

**Illustration:**

9\. T, who owns a liquor store, contracts with P Corporation for a burglar-alarm service. The contract provides that P Corporation will dispatch employees to T's store when an alarm signal is received at P Corporation's central monitoring station and that P Corporation's employees will gain entry to T's store using keys that T will provide to P Corporation for this purpose. P Corporation supervises its employees only by conducting occasional spot checks. A and B, employees of P Corporation who have access to the keys to T's store, repeatedly steal large quantities of liquor on the pretext of responding to real or bogus alarms from the store. P Corporation is subject to liability to T. P Corporation did not exercise reasonable care in supervising its employees, given the ease with which they may steal from its customers.

A principal's duty to use reasonable care encompasses the instructions it provides to its agents and others through whom it conducts activities because proper instructions may be necessary to prevent an undue risk of harm to third parties. A principal's duty to use reasonable care in furnishing instructions reflects the complexity of the principal's organization and encompasses instructions given to superior agents concerning their dealings with subordinate agents. A principal's duty of care also encompasses reasonable mechanisms to assure compliance with instructions.

**Illustration:**

10\. P Bank employs B as a branch manager and A as the branch's assistant manager. T, an elderly customer of the branch, has been diagnosed with dementia and visibly appears to be less than fully competent. A and B, who befriend T, help T open a trust account at the branch. A and B hand-write a customer-account agreement for T. Opening the account requires the approval of P Bank's President, who approves the account without independently investigating and verifying T's intentions. The account names A and B as the account's sole beneficiaries upon T's death. P Bank provides no training to its employees on interactions with elderly or mentally impaired customers. P Bank's internal compliance mechanisms do not detect the irregularities accompanying the opening of T's account. Upon T's death, A and B remove substantial amounts from T's account. P Bank is subject to liability.

_e. Principal's special relationship to person subject to risk._ General principles of tort law impose a duty of reasonable care on an actor in a special relationship with another with regard to risks that arise within the scope of the relationship. Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 40 (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005). Such relationships include a common carrier with its passengers, an innkeeper with its guests, a school with its students, and a custodian with those in its custody when the custodian is required by law or voluntarily takes custody of a person in circumstances in which the custodian has a superior ability to protect the other. Whether such a relationship exists is a question of law. The duty of reasonable care to which an actor in such a relationship is subject reflects the circumstances of the relationship. Id., Comment _d._ The scope of the actor's duty extends to dangers arising within the geographic and temporal confines of the relationship and does not extend to other risks. Id., Comment _f._

When an employee or other agent harms a person with whom the principal has a special relationship, the principal may be subject to liability although the employee or other agent acted without actual or apparent authority. See §§ 7.04 and 7.08. An employer may be subject to liability although in harming the person an employee acted outside the scope of employment as stated in § 7.07. The fact that a principal has delegated performance of its duty, whether or not the delegate is an agent, does not relieve the principal of liability. See § 7.06.

The basis for imposing liability on a principal or employer under this rule is its failure to discharge its duty of reasonable care to the person with whom it has a special relationship. A principal may fail to discharge its duty although it used reasonable care in choosing its employees and other agents. For example, in some settings and relationships, a person's vulnerability to harm may affect the measures that a principal may reasonably be required to take to safeguard against risks, including those posed by employees and other agents. In particular, relationships that expose young children to the risk of sexual abuse are ones in which a high degree of vulnerability may reasonably require measures of protection not necessary for persons who are older and better able to safeguard themselves. Such measures may include prohibiting unsupervised contact between a child and an employee. Likewise, persons of any age taken into custody by law-enforcement officers are vulnerable to risks of harm against which they may lack the ability to safeguard themselves.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Comparison with Restatement Second, Agency._ This section is the counterpart of Restatement Second, Agency § 213, entitled "Principal Negligent or Reckless." Section 213 states that:

A person conducting an activity through servants or other agents is subject to liability for harm resulting from his conduct if he is negligent or reckless:

(a) in giving improper or ambiguous orders of \[sic\] in failing to make proper regulations; or

(b) in the employment of improper persons or instrumentalities in work involving risk of harm to others: \[sic\]

(c) in the supervision of the activity; or

(d) in permitting, or failing to prevent, negligent or other tortious conduct by persons, whether or not his servants or agents, upon premises or with instrumentalities under his control.

The black-letter formulation in this section does not delineate specific conduct that constitutes negligence or recklessness. The formulation in Restatement Second, Agency § 213, likewise was "not intended to exhaust the ways in which a master or other principal may be negligent in the conduct of his business." Id., Comment _a._ The formulation in this section, as in § 213, is not limited to situations in which an actor is characterized as the agent or employee of the person who conducts an activity through the actor. The formulation is broader because the narrower formulation may imply, contrary to virtually all cases, that a person who engages the services of actors who are not agents is not subject to liability for negligent or reckless conduct in selecting or otherwise dealing with the actor. Restatement Second, Agency § 213 does not appear to have intended this consequence because Comment _a_ emphasizes that "\[t\]he rule stated in this Section is not based upon any rule of the law of principal and agent or of master and servant. It is a special application of the general rules stated in the Restatement of Torts. . . ."

_b. In general; relationship to other bases for liability._ This rule has been characterized as one that "enjoys a secure position in the mainstream of American common law." See [_Midwest Knitting Mills, Inc. v. United States, 950 F.2d 1295, 1298 (7th Cir.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-6WF0-008H-V229-00000-00&context=1530671) (tort of negligent supervision of employees).

Illustration 1 is taken from Restatement Second, Torts § 307, Illustration 1.

For the point that the rule does not create liability when an actor's conduct results in pure economic loss, see [_Midwest Knitting Mills, 950 F.2d at 1300_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-6WF0-008H-V229-00000-00&context=1530671) (affirming dismissal of claim for compensatory damages when plaintiff alleged that Small Business Administration was negligent in supervision of employee who had responsibility for processing advance payments by SBA to plaintiff; applying Wisconsin law, court determines that in all circumstances negligence suit to recover only economic loss will fail, even when, in contrast to facts of case, parties had no contractual relationship); [_Piper Jaffray Cos. v. National Union Fire Ins. Co., 967 F.Supp. 1148, 1157 (D.Minn.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RR5-XC00-00B1-F1DB-00000-00&context=1530671) (Minnesota courts "have made clear that this tort may not be predicated upon economic loss."). See also Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 7, Comment _d_ (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005); Dan B. Dobbs, The Law of Torts 1115 (2000) ("\[b\]roadly speaking, the plaintiff who claims a stand-alone commercial or economic tort must usually (but not invariably) prove intent rather than negligence"); W. Page Keeton et al., Prosser & Keeton on the Law of Torts § 92, at 657 (5th ed. 1984) ("\[g\]enerally speaking, there is no general duty to exercise reasonable care to avoid intangible economic loss or losses to others that do not arise from tangible physical harm to persons or tangible things.").

Illustration 3 is based on a simplified version of the facts of [_Tallahassee Furniture Co. v. Harrison, 583 So. 2d 744 (Fla.Dist.App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-BD60-003F-316N-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Weiss v. Furniture-in-the-Raw, 306 N.Y.S.2d 253 (Civ.1969)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-B770-003C-D1W0-00000-00&context=1530671) (owner of furniture store permits delivery person to hire additional help off street when needed but provides no instructions about how to select or control helpers; store owner subject to liability to customer when person hired by delivery person steals customer's wallet from customer's coat once inside customer's residence).

Illustration 4 is based on Illustration 2 to Restatement Second, Torts § 307. Many cases recognize that it is not a defense, to liability for one's own negligence in connection with an actor whose conduct injured a third party, that the actor was not an agent or an employee. See, e.g., [_Jurgens v. Poling Transp. Corp., 113 F.Supp.2d 388, 403 (E.D.N.Y.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:418X-77R0-0038-Y4G8-00000-00&context=1530671); [_McDonnell v. Music Stand, Inc., 886 P.2d 895, 900 (Kan.App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-4YG0-003F-D3M1-00000-00&context=1530671) (firm retained to effect repossessions; plaintiff's claim fails due to failure to plead or prove any physical harm); [_Jones v. Beker, 632 N.E.2d 273, 277 (Ill.App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2P60-003D-H289-00000-00&context=1530671) (employee of independent contractor retained to repossess automobile); [_A.E. Inv. Corp. v. Link Builders, Inc., 214 N.W.2d 764 (Wis. 1974)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-WHS0-003G-34WK-00000-00&context=1530671) (architect's alleged failure properly to supervise construction of building); [_Laesch v. L & H Indus., Inc., 469 N.W.2d 655 (Wis.App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-Y4H0-003G-34J8-00000-00&context=1530671) (railroad's failure properly to supervise contractor engaged to remove rails from right-of-way). A rare case holding to the contrary is [_Acme Delivery Serv., Inc. v. United States, 817 F.Supp. 889, 892 (D.Colo.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-T4X0-001T-63K1-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _38 F.3d 1220 (10th Cir.1994)_ (granting summary judgment for government against subcontractor of carriers that contracted to transport belongings of military personnel, when government admitted it negligently failed to discontinue using carriers when it learned of their adverse financial condition that culminated in their insolvency, leading to loss to subcontractor when it was not paid by carriers; court holds that liability under Restatement Second, Agency § 213 requires establishing a principal/agent relationship between defendant and actor because, although Comments to § 213 provide that the rule is an application of tort law and is not based on agency or master-servant law, "\[t\]he plain language of section 213 clearly indicates that such a relationship is required for application of the section: 'A person conducting an activity _through servants or other agents._ . . .'") (emphasis added by court). However, as the subcontractor's damages stemmed from negligence resulting in pure economic loss as opposed to physical contact, the result reached by the court in _Acme Delivery_ may be supportable on other grounds. See generally Douglas Laycock, Modern American Remedies: Cases and Materials 116-119 (3d ed. 2000) (characterizing economic-harm rule as, inter alia, "a way of saying that _A_ can contract with _B_ without incurring a duty to others who have contracted with _B._").

In at least some jurisdictions, if an employer stipulates that an employee acted within the scope of employment in committing a tort, the employer is not subject to liability under the rule stated in this section. See [_Tindall v. Enderle, 320 N.E.2d 764, 767-768 (Ind.App.1974)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-HNR0-003F-X1GP-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Cook v. Greyhound Lines, Inc., 847 F.Supp. 725, 732 (D.Minn.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-S140-003B-V1F3-00000-00&context=1530671) (theories of negligent hiring and negligent retention are premised on employee conduct that falls outside scope of employment, while theory of negligent supervision "derives from the _respondeat superior_ doctrine"; to establish liability for negligent supervision, claimant must establish that "the employee who caused an injury did so within the scope of his or her employment.") Other jurisdictions recognize the possibility that liability is not mutually exclusive. See, e.g., [_Pruitt v. Pavelin, 685 P.2d 1347, 1359-1360 (Ariz.App.1984)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-Y8N0-003F-T3WH-00000-00&context=1530671) (noting that award of punitive damages may be appropriate under one theory of liability but not the other).

Statutory provisions may impose different standards for liability. For example, under § 20(a) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, [_15 U.S.C. § 78t(a)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8S9D-W4W2-8T6X-701Y-00000-00&context=1530671), a person who directly or indirectly controls any person who is liable for selling securities in violation of the Act is liable to the same extent as the seller, unless the controlling person acted in good faith and did not directly or indirectly induce the seller's act. If a plaintiff shows that a person, such as an employer, had the ability to discipline an employee for the conduct in question, it is then the employer's burden to show that it acted in good faith and failed to induce the employee's act. See [_Martin v. Shearson Lehman Hutton, Inc., 986 F.2d 242, 244 (8th Cir.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-HPD0-003B-P2MN-00000-00&context=1530671); see also [_Harrison v. Dean Witter Reynolds, Inc., 974 F.2d 873, 881 (7th Cir.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-1950-008H-V3KH-00000-00&context=1530671) (relevant standard is whether defendant "actually participated in, that is, exercised control over, the operations of the person in general and, then, . . . whether the alleged control-person possessed the power or ability to control the specific transaction or activity upon which the primary violation was predicated, whether or not that power was exercised"). For a broader definition of "controlling person," see [_Hollinger v. Titan Capital Corp., 914 F.2d 1564, 1574 (9th Cir. 1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-27W0-003B-54KP-00000-00&context=1530671) (expressly rejecting standard of culpable participation applied by district court; plaintiff "need show only that . . . \[the actor\] was not himself a registered broker-dealer but was a representative employed by or associated with a registered broker-dealer").

_c. Causation and duty._ For the point made in Illustration 5, see [_Argonne Apartment House Co. v. Garrison, 42 F.2d 605 (D.C.Cir.1930)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-Y750-003B-K39V-00000-00&context=1530671) (employer's knowledge of employee's conviction for intoxication offense did not put employer on notice employee might be a thief); [_Doe v. Boys Clubs, Inc., 868 S.W.2d 942, 952 (Tex.App. 1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-1290-003C-23WS-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_907 S.W.2d 472 (Tex. 1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W520-003C-2094-00000-00&context=1530671) (fact that volunteer, working for club under court-ordered sentence of community service, had been convicted of driving while intoxicated would not have given club notice that volunteer might be a pedophile).

On the necessity for a causal link between a principal's alleged negligence in dealing with agents and the agent's tortious conduct, see [_Focus Inv. Assocs. Inc. v. American Title Ins. Co., 797 F.Supp. 109, 115 (D.R.I. 1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-K7X0-008H-F52N-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd in part and rev'd in part on other grounds, [_992 F.2d 1231 (1st Cir.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-GDV0-003B-P2MD-00000-00&context=1530671) (title insurer's alleged negligence in failing to provide training to policy-issuing attorney not cause of harm to plaintiff when attorney chose to rely on representations of another attorney without personally verifying record). See also [_Pruitt v. Pavelin, 685 P.2d 1347, 1355 (Ariz.App.1984)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-Y8N0-003F-T3WH-00000-00&context=1530671) (realty company subject to liability on negligent-hiring theory when salesperson defrauded client by forging signatures on sales documents and security documents in transaction not involving realty company; despite knowing that salesperson had previously been convicted of passing bad checks and had forged a signature on a document, principals of realty company enabled salesperson to obtain a state license by manifesting confidence in her honesty to state department; real-estate licensing process imparted a "patina of respectability" to salesperson that created a sufficient nexus between realty firm and salesperson's fraud).

On post-termination tortious conduct, see [_Coleman & Co. Sec., Inc. v. Giaquinto Family Trust, 236 F.Supp.2d 288, 303-304 (S.D.N.Y. 2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:46WX-F6G0-0038-Y45V-00000-00&context=1530671) (broker-dealer's liability for failure to supervise investment adviser properly ended when adviser left firm to join another; fact that adviser remained a board member and a part owner of broker-dealer irrelevant to broker-dealer's liability for failure to supervise when plaintiff alleged no injury tied to adviser's board membership or part ownership); [_Ledbetter v. United Am. Ins. Co., 624 So. 2d 1371, 1374 (Ala.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-X070-003C-92WK-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurers not subject to liability when, following termination of agency relationships, former agents induced insureds to whom they had sold policies to entrust them with money for investment in their own separate companies).

Illustration 6 is based on a revised version of the facts of _Semrad v. Edina Realty, Inc., 493 N.W.2d 528 (Minn.1992)_. In _Semrad,_ A's counterpart conducted his investment activities on the real-estate firm's premises.

Illustration 7 is based on [_Heller v. Patwil Homes, 713 A.2d 105 (Pa.Super.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3STK-07F0-0039-40VY-00000-00&context=1530671).

For the point that a defendant is not subject to liability in the absence of a duty owed to the plaintiff, see [_Harrison v. Dean Witter Reynolds, Inc., 974 F.2d 873 (7th Cir.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-1950-008H-V3KH-00000-00&context=1530671) (no duty-creating relationship present between brokerage firm and plaintiff, who invested in bogus scheme executed by firm's employees; plaintiff had no account at firm, and had never bought or sold securities through firm); [_Fraioli v. Lemcke, 328 F.Supp.2d 250, 266 (D.R.I.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4D1J-JD90-0038-Y30W-00000-00&context=1530671) (same; financial firm had no relationship with plaintiff that would create duty to plaintiff); [_Janssen v. American Hawaii Cruises, Inc., 731 P.2d 163, 166-167 (Hawaii 1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-1830-003F-G10R-00000-00&context=1530671) (former common employer of waiter and chef, and union that screened chef's application for employment, not subject to liability when chef sexually assaulted waiter during time they shared living accommodations on shore following completion of work on ship; although chef may have posed a risk of harm to anyone with whom he came into contact, that risk "was in no way magnified by the fact of his employment on the ship," employer did not require its employees to share accommodations or otherwise associate with each other on shore, and neither union nor common employer owed a duty of care to plaintiff in connection with offshore conduct); [_Belmar v. Dixie Bldg. Maint., Inc., 226 So. 2d 280, 281 (Fla.Dist.App.1969)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-85M0-003C-W478-00000-00&context=1530671) (employer of cleaning personnel not subject to liability to victim of theft committed by personnel when employer did not owe duty to theft victim; personnel's employer contracted to clean business premises of another party and personnel obtained access to victim's premises by climbing over partition wall and into victim's premises).

_d. Foreseeable risk and reasonable precaution._ Illustration 8 is based on [_Welsh Mfg., Div. of Textron, Inc. v. Pinkerton's, Inc., 474 A.2d 436 (R.I.1984)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-Y5W0-003D-F3Y0-00000-00&context=1530671). The court also held that the evidence presented at trial supported the jury's verdict for the plaintiff on its claim of negligent training and supervision. The guard's employer did not have a training program of "a formal and inculcative nature," the guard had no comparable prior work experience, and the employer did not assign work in "ascending levels of sensitivity." The employer did conduct surprise checks of its guards but rarely on weekends when the guard in question was on duty. [_Id. at 443_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-Y5W0-003D-F3Y0-00000-00&context=1530671).

On the foreseeability of risk to a third party when an actor is not suitably matched with the demands of a particular task, see [_Watsontown Brick Co. v. Hercules Powder Co., 265 F.Supp. 268 (M.D.Pa.1967)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-RJX0-0054-83D1-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_387 F.2d 99 (3d Cir.1967)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-WK50-0039-Y2DN-00000-00&context=1530671) (although employee of blasting company held license and had worked as a blaster for defendant for 13 years without mishap, he did not know how to set off particular type of blast that damaged plaintiff's quarry; defendant did not rely on employee's reputation for competence because employee had no established reputation for the type of blast he was directed to conduct).

On reasonable care in connection with determining an actor's suitability, see [_Brown v. Youth Servs. Int'l, Inc., 89 F.Supp.2d 1095 (D.S.D.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YWW-HXS0-0038-Y33R-00000-00&context=1530671) (sufficient evidence to withstand defendant's motion for summary judgment that employer of counselor in residential treatment facility for juveniles acted negligently in hiring employee; employer conducted background check that did not reveal any propensity for child abuse but, knowing that prospective employee lacked any experience counseling adolescents, employer had duty of reasonable care, consistent with its own rules, to conduct further screening of some sort); [_Easley v. Apollo Detective Agency, Inc., 387 N.E.2d 1241 (Ill.App.1979)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S11-XD60-003C-B37W-00000-00&context=1530671) (employer of security guard subject to liability to tenant assaulted by guard; employer conducted almost no investigation into guard's suitability for work and failed to discover guard's arrest record; when work gave guard access to keys to residential apartments, employer's conduct exhibited reckless disregard for tenants' safety).

A principal is not subject to liability when the risk of harm to a third party through an actor's conduct is not reasonably foreseeable. See, e.g., [_Olson v. Staggs-Bilt Homes, Inc., 534 P.2d 1073, 1075-1076 (Ariz.App.1975)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRH-GYG0-003F-H40D-00000-00&context=1530671) (homebuilder not subject to liability to service-station attendant wounded by accidental discharge of gun owned by guard employed by homebuilder; although guard drew gun while on trip to fill up truck with gas before beginning patrol of subdivision, gun was not drawn "for any conceivable purpose pertinent to" employment, employer had no notice guard was "known to be vicious or careless," and guard's job was a relatively simple one that required no special training); [_Monty v. Orlandi, 169 Cal. App.2d 620, 625 (Cal.App.1959)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRK-N670-003C-349B-00000-00&context=1530671) (owner of bar not subject to liability when bartender assaulted patron; bar owner was not negligent, having observed bartender over period of time and "there was nothing in his demeanor to indicate that he was pugnacious"; trial court correctly instructed jury that to establish bar owner's vicarious liability plaintiff had burden of establishing that assault occurred within scope of bartender's employment, which required finding that bartender's duties included maintaining order in bar); [_Van Horne v. Muller, 705 N.E.2d 898, 905-906 (Ill.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3V7S-B080-0039-44G4-00000-00&context=1530671) (radio station that employed disc jockey with reputation for outrageous and offensive conduct not subject to liability when disc jockey made false and defamatory on-air statements about plaintiff; disc jockey's prior conduct did not involve defamation and "\[t\]he mere fact that \[he\] had previously engaged in offensive or outrageous conduct during his radio programs does not establish that he had a propensity to make false, defamatory statements"); [_Staten v. Ohio Exterminating Co., 704 N.E.2d 621 (Ohio App.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RJN-59P0-0039-453P-00000-00&context=1530671) (exterminating company not subject to liability when service technician procured murder of customer allegedly to prevent her testimony against him, after customer accused technician of stealing diamond ring from her home following extermination performed by technician and filing of felony charges against technician; although employer did not investigate whether technician had a criminal record, employee's criminal record--consisting of conviction for aggravated menacing, guilty plea to carrying concealed weapon, and charges of aggravated burglary, theft, assault, and domestic violence--did not make it foreseeable that employee would have customer murdered 6 months after employment ceased and 4 months after criminal charges were filed against employee).

On the significance of whether an actor is licensed to engage in a particular activity, see [_Watsontown Brick, 265 F.Supp. at 270_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-RJX0-0054-83D1-00000-00&context=1530671) (presumption of competence); [_Noble v. Sears, Roebuck & Co., 109 Cal.Rptr. 269, 275 (Cal.App.1973)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-8ND0-003C-J18F-00000-00&context=1530671) (fact that detective agency chosen by store to investigate plaintiff's claim of personal injury on store premises was licensed "is one fact to be considered in determining" whether store and its lawyers were negligent in selecting agency to do investigation; plaintiff alleged that investigator gained admittance to plaintiffs hospital room and deceived plaintiff into revealing address of elusive witness); [_Jones v. Beker, 632 N.E.2d 273 (Ill.App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2P60-003D-H289-00000-00&context=1530671) (no showing by plaintiff that repossessor's lack of repossessor's license was causally related to injury suffered by plaintiff, a pedestrian struck by repossessor while driving repossessed automobile).

Illustration 9 is based on [_International Distrib. Corp. v. American Dist. Tel. Co., 569 F.2d 136 (D.C.Cir. 1977)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-YY00-0039-M48Y-00000-00&context=1530671) (holding a jury might conclude on facts presented that employer's supervision was inadequate). On negligence in supervision, see also [_Sheltry v. Unum Ins. Co., 247 F.Supp.2d 169, 179-180 (D.Conn.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:481M-W1N0-0038-Y258-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurer has duty to supervise agent); [_Brueckner v. Norwich Univ., 730 A.2d 1086, 1093, 1096 (Vt.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3VS3-9H70-0039-40HX-00000-00&context=1530671) (military college that engaged upperclassmen for orientation of freshmen, charging upperclassmen with "indoctrinating and orienting freshmen," owed duty of care to freshman plaintiff who was assaulted by orientation leader when college was negligent in supervision of orientation leaders; although college instructed orientation leaders not to mistreat freshmen, college knew that serious hazing incidents had occurred in past and left orientation leaders in situation of virtually unsupervised control over freshmen).

A principal's duty to use reasonable care in providing instructions may encompass situations in which actors are not necessarily agents as defined in § 1.01. See [_Shafer v. Bull, 194 A.2d 788 (Md.1963)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S12-2740-003D-Y0N5-00000-00&context=1530671) (operator of grain elevator subject to liability for injuries suffered by patron in truck while patron's grain was unloaded at elevator; owner permitted other patrons to speed unloading of others' trucks by operating hoist that raised front end of loaded truck but placed no one in charge of hoist and provided no instructions on how to operate it safely).

Illustration 10 is based on Estate of DiCesare, 2003 WL 22053336 (Pa. Comm.Pl.2003).

_e. Principal's special relationship to person subject to risk._ Courts apply a variety of doctrinal formulations to analyze intentional sexual misconduct by employees. A basic line of demarcation is whether the misconduct is characterized as falling within the scope of employment.

For recent determinations that intentional employee sexual misconduct falls outside the scope of employment, see _Girden v. Sandals, Int'l, Ltd., 206 F.Supp.2d 605 (S.D.N.Y.2002)_, aff'd, [_67 Fed. Appx. 27 (2d Cir.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48R2-HSB0-0038-X092-00000-00&context=1530671) (under Connecticut law, sexual assault against hotel guest not within scope of employment of hotel employee because, as a matter of law, employee not actuated at least in part to serve employer's business); [_Osborne v. Payne, 31 S.W.3d 911, 915 (Ky.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:41SP-P3T0-0039-414D-00000-00&context=1530671) (priest who, acting as marriage counselor, had adulterous affair with coun-selee, was not acting within scope of employment by diocese; priest was not advancing any cause of the diocese or engaging in behavior appropriate to the normal scope of his employment); [_Judith M. v. Sisters of Charity Hosp., 715 N.E.2d 95, 96 (N.Y.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WN7-W5J0-0039-44R0-00000-00&context=1530671) (allegation of sexual abuse by hospital employee against inpatient; court affirms summary judgment for hospital: "\[a\]ssuming plaintiff's allegations of sexual abuse are true, it is clear that the employee here departed from his duties for solely personal motives unrelated to the furtherance of the Hospital's business."); [_Kuhn v. Youlten, 692 N.E.2d 226, 233 (Ohio App.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RJN-2F40-0039-44S2-00000-00&context=1530671) (skating instructor's sexual molestation of minor student not within scope of employment; no showing molestation "facilitated or promoted" employer's business); [_Doe v. Boys Clubs of Greater Dallas, Inc., 868 S.W.2d 942, 950 (Tex.App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-1290-003C-23WS-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_907 S.W.2d 472 (Tex.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W520-003C-2094-00000-00&context=1530671) (club volunteer worker, characterized as employee for purposes of respondeat superior, acted outside scope of employment in molestation of minor); see also [_Niece v. Elmview Group Home, 929 P.2d 420 (Wash.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-VT10-003F-W0BT-00000-00&context=1530671) (employer not subject to liability on theory of nondelegable duty when employee raped resident of home for developmentally disabled persons).

California cases apply a broader definition of scope of employment. They focus on whether a tortious act arose out of employment duties or tasks or was "engendered by events or conditions relating to" them. Risks "engendered by events unrelated to the employment, so that the mere fact that an employee has an opportunity to abuse facilities or authority necessary to the performance of his or her duties" do not render the employer vicariously liable. See [_Farmers Ins. Group v. County of Santa Clara, 906 P.2d 440, 450 (Cal.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-0KC0-003D-J3F6-00000-00&context=1530671) (employer not subject to vicarious liability on basis of supervisory employee's sexual harassment; supervisory employee not entitled to indemnity from employer for costs of defense of federal sexual-harassment case under statute requiring indemnification by public-entity employer of costs arising out of civil lawsuit when act or omission giving rise to an injury occurred "in the scope of his or her employment as an employee of the public entity," Cal. Gov. Code §§ 825(a), 825.2(b), and 995); see also [_Lisa M. v. Henry Mayo Newhall Mem. Hosp., 907 P.2d 358, 364 (Cal.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-0HS0-003D-J2Y2-00000-00&context=1530671) (hospital not subject to liability when ultrasound technician, after completion of exam, molested patient in examination room; molestation was not "generated by or an outgrowth of workplace responsibilities, conditions or events."). The first California case to depart from the motivation-to-serve rule was _Carr v. Wm. C. Crowell Co., 171 P.2d 5, 7 (Cal.1946)_ (question is whether employee "inflicts an injury out of personal malice, not engendered by the employment"). Despite the tests articulated in _Farmers Group_ and _Lisa M.,_ California cases continue to impose vicarious liability broadly when an employee is an on-duty police officer who commits an act of sexual misconduct against a member of the public. See [_Mary M. v. City of Los Angeles, 814 P.2d 1341 (Cal.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-82R0-003D-J1GR-00000-00&context=1530671) (rape of motorist by on-duty police officer). The employer's vicarious liability stems from the "great power and control" that police officers have over criminal suspects, [_id. at 1349_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-82R0-003D-J1GR-00000-00&context=1530671), and "creates a strong incentive for vigilance by those in a position to guard substantially against the evil to be prevented," [_id. at 1347_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-82R0-003D-J1GR-00000-00&context=1530671). The employer's liability also serves to compensate the victim, [_id. at 1348-1349_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-82R0-003D-J1GR-00000-00&context=1530671), and to ensure that the victim's loss will be equitably borne by the victim in relation to those who benefit from the enterprise giving rise to the injury: "\[t\]he cost resulting from misuse of \[police\] power should be borne by the community, because of the substantial benefits that the community derives from the lawful exercise of police power," [_id. at 1349_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-82R0-003D-J1GR-00000-00&context=1530671).

The formulation in [_Doe v. Samaritan Counseling Center, 791 P.2d 344, 349 (Alaska 1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-W8R0-003C-G18K-00000-00&context=1530671) focuses on whether an employee's tort is "sufficiently connected with the enterprise \[to be\] in effect . . . deeds of the enterprise itself." _Doe_ reversed a summary judgment for the defendant employer; the plaintiff alleged that a counselor's sexual misconduct resulted in negligent infliction of emotional injury. To the same effect is [_Marston v. Minneapolis Clinic of Psychiatry & Neurology, Ltd., 329 N.W.2d 306, 311 (Minn.1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-2MW0-003G-V1XD-00000-00&context=1530671) (allegation that, by engaging in sexual relationship with patient, psychiatrist committed intentional tort and that emotional control over patient vitiated consent; clinic may be subject to vicarious liability because such sexual relations between psychiatrist and patient are "a well-known hazard and thus, to a degree, foreseeable and a risk of employment. . . . the instant situation would not have occurred but for \[the psychologist's\] employment; it was only through his relation to \[the patient\] as a therapist that \[the psychologist\] was able to commit the acts in question"). See also [_Brown v. Youth Servs. Int'l, Inc., 89 F.Supp.2d 1095, 1105-1106 (D.S.D.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YWW-HXS0-0038-Y33R-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying South Dakota law, court holds that counselor at residential juvenile treatment facility acted within scope of employment in committing sexual assaults against residents; test is whether a nexus existed between employment and "the activity which actually caused the injury sufficient to make the harm foreseeable"; a harm is "foreseeable if the employee's conduct 'is not so unusual or startling that it would be unfair to include the loss caused by the injury among the costs of the employer's business,'" quoting [_Leafgreen v. American Family Mut. Ins. Co., 393 N.W.2d 275, 280-281 (S.D.1986))_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S71-2KM0-003G-D1X8-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Red Elk v. United States, 62 F.3d 1102, 1107 (8th Cir.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-CH80-001T-D2F4-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying South Dakota law; under Federal Tort Claims Act tribal police officer's rape of minor picked up for curfew violation within scope of employment; "foreseeable that a male officer with authority to pick up a teenage girl out alone at night in violation of the curfew might be tempted to violate his trust"); [_St. John v. United States, 240 F.3d 671, 677 (8th Cir.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:429P-8YH0-0038-X1XS-00000-00&context=1530671) (if tribal police officer threatened plaintiff with rearrest to compel her to enter his truck, trier of fact could find that officer's conduct culminating in sexual assault within scope of employment). _Leafgreen_ itself involves a theft by an insurance agent of the insured's property, not a physical tort; the court held that theft of the insured's property by a criminal confederate of the agent, effected after the agent conducted an inventory of the property, was insufficiently connected with the agent's employment to subject the employer to liability. [_393 N.W.2d at 279_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S71-2KM0-003G-D1X8-00000-00&context=1530671).

A few recent cases analyze employee sexual misconduct using a standard based on Restatement Second, Agency § 219(2)(d), which states that a master is subject to liability "for the torts of his servants acting outside the scope of their employment" when "the servant purported to act or to speak on behalf of the principal and there was reliance upon apparent authority, or he was aided in accomplishing the tort by the existence of the agency relation." In [_Costos v. Coconut Island Corp., 137 F.3d 46 (1st Cir.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S53-HH50-0038-X2V9-00000-00&context=1530671), applying Maine law, the court held that the owner and corporate manager of an inn were subject to liability to a guest who was raped by the inn's manager; the manager's position gave him access to room keys and knowledge of the room occupied by the plaintiff. "\[D\]efendants are well within the scope of § 219(2)(d) liability. By virtue of his agency relationship with the defendants, as manager of the inn, \[manager\] was entrusted with the keys to the rooms, including \[plaintiffs\] room. . . . Because he was the manager of the inn, \[manager\] knew exactly where to find \[plaintiff\]. The jury could have found that \[manager\] had responsibilities to be at the inn or to have others there late at night." [_Id. at 50_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S53-HH50-0038-X2V9-00000-00&context=1530671). The court characterizes the employee's tort as one "accomplished by an instrumentality, or through conduct associated with the agency status." [_Id. at 49_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S53-HH50-0038-X2V9-00000-00&context=1530671). More recently, the Maine Supreme Court has noted that "\[t\]he _Costos_ decision has been the subject of some criticism." [_Mahar v. StoneWood Transport, 823 A.2d 540, 546 (Me.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48HP-FHG0-0039-41G0-00000-00&context=1530671). _Mahar_ characterizes the holding in _Costos_ as dependent on the employee's use of deceit: "the employee acted deceitfully by using his position as an employee to learn the room number of the female guest, and by misusing a duplicate key to enter her room." Id. The court footnotes a law-review article making the point that _Costos_ does not "delineate the scope of the instrumentality." See id. n.6, citing Daniel M. Coombs, Note, [_73 U. Colo. L. Rev. 1099, 1112 (2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:465C-W8C0-00CV-N008-00000-00&context=1530671). Following _Costos,_ a majority of the court adopted § 219(2)(d) as "applicable in assessing whether an employer has liability for the tortious conduct of an employee when that conduct falls outside the scope of his or her employment" in [_Doe v. Forrest, 853 A.2d 48, 56 (Vt.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4CB7-H6T0-0039-4132-00000-00&context=1530671). In _Forrest,_ the plaintiff was allegedly coerced into sexual conduct by an on-duty deputy sheriff. The court noted the uniqueness of the situation in light of "the extraordinary power that a law enforcement officer has over a citizen." [_Id. at 61_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4CB7-H6T0-0039-4132-00000-00&context=1530671). The _Forrest_ majority does not disagree with the outcome reached in _Mahar_ and, while "more comfortable with the analysis in _Costos,_" see [_id. at 65_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4CB7-H6T0-0039-4132-00000-00&context=1530671), characterizes a "sexual assault on a citizen by a law enforcement officer as probably the strongest application of the core principles behind § 219(2)(d) as explained by" the [_Supreme Court in Farragher v. City of Boca Raton, 524 U.S. 775 (1998), id. at 67_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3TGV-N290-002K-600F-00000-00&context=1530671). In [_Primeaux v. United States, 181 F.3d 876 (8th Cir. 1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WRJ-JYC0-0038-X2DT-00000-00&context=1530671), applying South Dakota law, the court emphasized the distinctiveness of apparent authority as a basis for vicarious liability, in contrast with conduct by an employee that is within the scope of employment. [_Id. at 880-881_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WRJ-JYC0-0038-X2DT-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[t\]he Supreme Court of South Dakota has long recognized that vicarious liability for conduct within the scope of employment is based on the employment relationship, whereas vicarious liability for conduct within an agent's apparent authority is based on estoppel"). As a consequence, apparent authority was not the correct test of whether a tribal police officer acted within the scope of his employment in committing a rape for purposes of liability under the Federal Tort Claims Act.

Courts differ on whether it is sufficient to focus on the general job activity that gave rise to the tortious act, as opposed to the precise act or omission. In [_Fearing v. Bucher, 977 P.2d 1163 (Or.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3W7F-P7S0-0039-40FR-00000-00&context=1530671) and [_Lourim v. Swensen, 977 P.2d 1157 (Or.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3W7G-H180-0039-40G7-00000-00&context=1530671), both cases involving allegations of sexual assaults against minors, the Oregon Supreme Court held that it was a question for the trier of fact whether the employee's torts resulted from acts within the scope of employment: "a jury could infer that the sexual assaults were the culmination of a progressive series of actions that began with and continued to involve \[priest's\] performance of the ordinary and authorized duties of a priest. . . ." [_977 P.2d at 1166-1167_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3W7F-P7S0-0039-40FR-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Plummer v. Center Psychiatrists, Ltd., 476 S.E.2d 172, 174 (Va.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RPY-3B00-003D-503N-00000-00&context=1530671) (question for trier of fact whether psychologist acted within scope of employment in sexual encounter with patient).

Jurisdictions outside the United States, likewise, are not in accord on the appropriate standard for vicarious liability when an employee commits an intentional tort involving sexual misconduct in connection with the employee's work. French law defines the scope-of-employment concept broadly to encompass a tort committed by an employee (a _fait illicite_) during the time and at the place where the employee exercised job functions. See [_Arno v. Club Med, Inc., 22 F.3d 1464, 1469 (9th Cir.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-69V0-003B-P0SP-00000-00&context=1530671) (interpreting French Civ. Code art. 1384: "\[a\] person is responsible not only for the injury which he causes by his own act, but also for that which is caused by the act of persons for whom he is responsible, or things which he has in his care (including) . . . \[m\]asters and employers, for the injury caused by their servants and employees in performing the duties for which they are employed"; reversing summary judgment for defendant; resort could be subject to liability when supervisor raped employee during business hours and on resort premises and then reminded employee he would transmit her job evaluation to home office the following week).

Recent cases from Canada and England subject an employer to liability when a "trusting relationship develops as a necessity of the employer's objectives." Bazley v. Curry, \[1999\] 174 D.L.R. (4th) 45, 81. The claim in _Bazley_ was sexual misconduct by a house parent employed by a residential facility for emotionally troubled children. The court held that its requisites for vicarious liability were satisfied. In Lister v. Hesley Hall Ltd. \[2002\] A.C. 215, involving similar facts, the House of Lords applied the formulation in _Bazley:_ "\[t\]he question is whether the warden's torts were so closely connected with his employment that it would be fair and just to hold the employers vicariously liable. On the facts of the case the answer is yes. After all, the sexual abuse was inextricably interwoven with the carrying out by the warden of his duties. . . . Matters of degree arise. But the present cases clearly fall on the side of vicarious liability." [_Id. at 229_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RPY-3B00-003D-503N-00000-00&context=1530671). See also Allen M. Linden, Canadian Tort Law 516 (7th ed. 2001) (discussing _Bazley_ and _Lister_). In contrast, a majority of the High Court of Australia declined to adopt the formulation in _Bazley,_ remanding for retrial a case involving allegations of sexual misconduct in nonresidential school contexts. See New South Wales v. Lepore \[2003\] 212 C.L.R. 558. An opinion joined by two justices noted that imposing vicarious liability in such a case might lack deterrent value: "\[i\]f the criminal law will not deter the wrongdoer there seems little deterrent value in holding the employer of the offender liable in damages for the assault committed." Id. at 587 (Hayne & Gummow, JJ.). Moreover, _Bazley_ imposes liability for risks not confined to "those risks which attend the _furtherance_ of the \[employer's\] venture but include the risks of conduct that is directly antithetical to those aims." Id. And "\[a\]nalysis by reference to risk may also obscure the importance of considering questions of that kind when deciding whether the school authority, or other employer, has itself breached a duty of care owed to the injured person. If the school authority should reasonably have taken steps to prevent the abuse of a pupil . . . orthodox principles of negligence would be engaged. . . . To transfer considerations of risk in the sense just identified to the field of vicarious liability does not assist the proper development of that field." Id. at 589.

It has been argued that only a regime of strict liability through respondeat superior will suffice to create proper incentives when an employee is a male police officer empowered to meet in private with female juveniles. See [_West v. Waymire, 114 F.3d 646, 649 (7th Cir. 1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-GP30-00B1-D1SJ-00000-00&context=1530671) (dictum) ("when the employee is a male police officer whose employer has invested him with intimidating authority to deal in private with troubled teenage girls, his taking advantage of the opportunity that authority and proximity and privacy give him to extract sexual favors from these girls should be sufficiently within the orbit of his employer-conferred powers to bring the doctrine of respondeat superior into play . . . even though he is not acting to further the employer's own goals but instead is on a frolic of his own. We want the police department to supervise its officers in this domain with especial care. . . .").

On the duty of care owed by those in charge of vulnerable persons, see Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 40 (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005); [_Frugis v. Bracigliano, 827 A.2d 1040 (N.J.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:495M-TTS0-0039-40JV-00000-00&context=1530671) (reasonable trier of fact could only have found that school board breached duty of care in connection with sexual abuse of elementary-school pupils by school's principal; board has duty to use reasonable care to assure that teachers and administrators do not endanger or exploit students). See also [_Hardwicke v. American Boychoir School, 845 A.2d 619 (N.J.Super.App.Div.)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4C18-RG10-0039-42YV-00000-00&context=1530671), leave to appeal granted, [_845 A.2d 186 (N.J.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4C41-CBM0-0039-420W-00000-00&context=1530671) (statutory grant of charitable immunity inapplicable to nonprofit boarding school as consequence of articulation of fundamental principles of tort law in _Frugis_).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 7.06_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8Y0-00YG-400K-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 7- Torts--Liability of Agent and Principal_ > _Topic 2- Principal's Liability_**

**§ 7.06 Failure in Performance of Principal's Duty of Protection**

**A principal required by contract or otherwise by law to protect another cannot avoid liability by delegating performance of the duty, whether or not the delegate is an agent.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section states the basic tort-law principle that a principal may be subject to liability to a third person when the principal is under a duty to protect that person. The principle is often termed one of "nondelegable" duty because a principal is not relieved of liability on the basis that the principal has delegated performance of the duty to another person, whether or not that person is an agent of the principal. It is beyond the scope of this Restatement whether such a duty is present in particular circumstances, as is the applicability or inapplicability of this duty to governmental actors.

Courts have found nondelegable duties to use reasonable care to be present in the situations identified by Restatement Second, Torts §§ 416 to 429. These include the performance of work that is dangerous in the absence of specific precautions, see id. § 416, and the performance of work that has inherent dangers, see id. § 427. Additionally, in some circumstances tort law imposes liability without regard to a defendant's negligence or intent to cause harm. Such liability may not be avoided by delegating performance of the activity that occasions the tort. Courts have imposed strict liability in the circumstances identified by Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm §§ 20 to 23 (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005). These include carrying on an abnormally dangerous activity, see id. § 20, and owning or possessing a wild animal, see id. § 22.

_b. In general._ Delegating performance of a duty does not in itself discharge the duty unless the person to whom the duty is owed so agrees. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 318(3). This basic principle has the effect of expanding the range of circumstances in which an actor's tortious conduct subjects a principal to liability to a third party because it is not limited by whether the actor has a relationship of agency with the principal, whether the principal has chosen the actor with reasonable care, or, if the actor is an employee, by whether the employee's tortious conduct occurs within the scope of employment under § 7.07(2).

The circumstances under which a principal is subject to liability for the negligence of a person who is not an agent and who has been chosen by the principal to perform work are stated in detail in Restatement Second, Torts §§ 416 to 429. For example, if the work to be done poses a "peculiar risk of physical harm to others unless special precautions are taken," a principal is subject to liability to a third party who is injured when the actor whom the principal engages to do the work fails to take reasonable care to take such precautions. Id. § 416. The underlying principle is that "the employer remains liable for injuries resulting from dangers which he should contemplate at the time that he enters into the contract, and cannot shift to the contractor the responsibility for such dangers, or for taking precautions against them." Id., Comment _a._ Likewise, a principal remains liable to third parties for harm caused when the principal knows or has reason to know that the work involves "an abnormally dangerous activity," to the same extent had the principal undertaken the work itself. Id § 427A. On the definition of abnormally dangerous activities, see Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 20 (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005).

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency._ This section corresponds to Restatement Second, Agency § 214.

_b. In general._ On governmental actors, see, e.g., [_Logue v. United States, 412 U.S. 521 (1973)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-CSY0-003B-S2D4-00000-00&context=1530671) (for purposes of Federal Tort Claims Act, personnel in charge of county jail that housed federal prisoner were neither federal employees nor a federal agency).

On common carriers' duties, see generally W. Page Keeton et al., Prosser & Keeton on The Law of Torts § 34 (5th ed. 1984). Compare [_Nazareth v. Herndon Ambulance Serv., Inc., 467 So. 2d 1076, 1078 (Fla.Dist.App.1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-6CY0-003C-X2FV-00000-00&context=1530671) (implied contract between victim-passenger and carrier subjects carrier to "extraordinary duty" that "does not terminate until the journey is complete"; duty would subject ambulance service to liability to passenger allegedly raped by attendant during trip in ambulance) with [_Adams v. New York City Transit Auth., 666 N.E.2d 216 (N.Y.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S2R-5PP0-003V-B145-00000-00&context=1530671) (employer not subject to liability on basis of assault against passenger by clerk in subway-token booth because attack was outside scope of employment), overruling [_Stewart v. Brooklyn & Crosstown R.R. Co., 90 N.Y. 588 (N.Y.1882)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3STM-YMR0-0039-42KM-00000-00&context=1530671).

In admiralty cases, it was long assumed that a ship owner was subject to liability for its employees' misconduct against passengers. See [_New Jersey Steamboat Co. v. Brockett, 121 U.S. 637 (1887)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-H6G0-003B-H1S2-00000-00&context=1530671) (ship owner liable when employee forcibly removed passenger from area of ship); [_New Orleans & N.E.R.R. Co. v. Jopes, 142 U.S. 18 (1891)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-FWW0-003B-H4NF-00000-00&context=1530671) (extending owner's strict liability under _Brockett_ to circumstances in which employee acted outside scope of employment, as by shooting passenger). In [_Kermarec v. Compagnie Generale Transatlantique, 358 U.S. 625, 632 (1959)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-J140-003B-S3XJ-00000-00&context=1530671), the Court held that "the owner of a ship in navigable waters owes to all who are on board for purposes not inimical to his legitimate interests the duty of exercising reasonable care under the circumstances of each case." _Kermarec_ did not overrule _Brockett_ or _Jopes,_ however, and involves not an employee's tort against a passenger but a passenger's fall down a defectively constructed staircase. District courts within the Second Circuit interpret _Kermarec_ to eliminate vicarious liability for employees' intentional torts against passengers. See, e.g., [_York v. Commodore Cruise Line, Inc., 863 F.Supp. 159, 162 (S.D.N.Y. 1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-BND0-003B-V2NW-00000-00&context=1530671). Courts in other circuits, however, continue to hold ship owners vicariously liable for crew members' willful misconduct against passengers. See, e.g., [_Doe v. Celebrity Cruises, Inc., 394 F.3d 891 (11th Cir. 2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4F43-P8S0-0038-X2B8-00000-00&context=1530671), cert. denied, _126 S. Ct. 548 (2005)_; [_Morton v. De Oliveira, 984 F.2d 289 (9th Cir.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-J300-003B-P12C-00000-00&context=1530671); _Muratore v. M/S Scotia Prince, 845 F.2d 347 (1st Cir.1988)_; _Jackson Marine Corp. v. M/V Blue Fox, 845 F.2d 1307 (5th Cir.1988)_.
