[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 7.07_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8Y0-00YG-400M-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 7- Torts--Liability of Agent and Principal_ > _Topic 2- Principal's Liability_**

**§ 7.07 Employee Acting Within Scope of Employment**

**(1) An employer is subject to vicarious liability for a tort committed by its employee acting within the scope of employment.**

**(2) An employee acts within the scope of employment when performing work assigned by the employer or engaging in a course of conduct subject to the employer's control. An employee's act is not within the scope of employment when it occurs within an independent course of conduct not intended by the employee to serve any purpose of the employer.**

**(3) For purposes of this section,**

**(a) an employee is an agent whose principal controls or has the right to control the manner and means of the agent's performance of work, and**

**(b) the fact that work is performed gratuitously does not relieve a principal of liability.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ Subsection (1) repeats the basic doctrine of respondeat superior stated in § 2.04 as a basis on which legal consequences of one person's actions may be attributed to another person. Subsection (2) states when an employee's tortious conduct occurs within the scope of employment for purposes of subjecting the employer to liability. Comment _b_ discusses the rationale for the formulation in subsection (2), contrasting it with its counterparts in Restatement Second, Agency § 228 and in cases. Comment _c_ discusses employee conduct that constitutes performance of work and is within the scope of employment. Comment _d_ discusses other employee conduct that is subject to an employer's control. Comment _e_ examines employees' peregrinations, that is, their travel necessitated by or otherwise in connection with their work. Comment _f_ discusses the definition of employee in subsection (3).

For general discussion of respondeat superior, see § 2.04, Comment _b._

Section 1.04(3) defines gratuitous agent.

This section is inapplicable to an employer's liability for one employee's tortious conduct toward a fellow employee, a topic being considered by Restatement Third, Employment Law, in preparation as Restatement Third, Agency, was completed. Section 7.08 covers the vicarious liability of an employer or other principal for torts committed by agents in dealing or communicating with third parties on or purportedly on behalf of the principal when actions taken by the agent with apparent authority either constitute the tort or enable the agent to conceal its commission.

_b. When tortious conduct is within the scope of employment--in general._ An employee's conduct, although tortious, may be within the scope of employment as defined in subsection (2). If an employee commits a tort while performing work assigned by the employer or while acting within a course of conduct subject to the employer's control, the employee's conduct is within the scope of employment unless the employee was engaged in an independent course of conduct not intended to further any purpose of the employer. The formulation in subsection (2) reflects the definition of scope of employment applied in most cases and in most jurisdictions.

In many cases in which an employee's tortious conduct is outside the scope of employment as stated in subsection (2), the employee's action lies beyond the employer's effective control. If an employee's tortious conduct is unrelated either to work assigned by the employer or to a course of conduct that is subject to the employer's control, the conduct is outside the scope of employment. If an employee undertakes a course of work-related conduct for the sole purpose of furthering the employee's interests or those of a third party, the employee's conduct will often lie beyond the employer's effective control. When an agent is not an employee, the principal lacks the right to control the manner and means of the agent's physical conduct in how work is performed. However, although an employer's ability to exercise control is an important element in justifying respondeat superior, the range of an employer's effective control is not the limit that respondeat superior imposes on the circumstances under which an employer is subject to liability.

An employer who is negligent or reckless in how it selects or otherwise deals with an employee is subject to direct liability as stated in § 7.05 to third parties for harm caused by the employee's tortious conduct. Thus, when tortious conduct by employees is reasonably foreseeable by an employer, the basic negligence principle stated in § 7.05 is applicable. Moreover, if an employer has a special relationship with a person, the employer owes the person a duty of reasonable care with respect to risks arising within the scope of the relationship. If the employer's failure to fulfill its duty enables an employee to injure the person to whom the employer owes such a duty, the employer is subject to liability. See § 7.05, Comment _e._

In contrast, respondeat superior subjects an employer to vicarious liability for employee torts committed within the scope of employment, distinct from whether the employer is subject to direct liability. An employer's ability to exercise control over its employees' work-related conduct enables the employer to take measures to reduce the incidence of tortious conduct. It may be difficult, after the fact of an employee's tortious conduct, to identify an instance of negligence on the part of the employer. This may be so even when, before the fact of the employee's tortious conduct, steps were available to the employer that, if taken, would have prevented the tort. In contrast, when an employee's tortious conduct is outside the range of activity that an employer may control, subjecting the employer to liability would not provide incentives for the employer to take measures to reduce the incidence of such tortious conduct. Moreover, for an employer to insure against a risk of liability, whether from third-party sources or its own assets, the risk must be at least to some degree ascertainable and quantifiable.

In assessing the scope of employment limitation on respondeat superior, it is helpful to recognize that the range of tortious conduct by employees to which the doctrine is relevant is not all-encompassing. Respondeat superior is not the sole basis for liability when an employer itself is at fault, see §§ 7.04, 7.05, and 7.06, nor is it the sole basis for vicarious liability when an employee's apparent authority enables the employee to commit a tort, see § 7.08.

Respondeat superior is a basis for vicarious liability when an identifiable employee-actor commits a tort in an interaction with a third party. An important doctrine in contemporary tort law--strict liability for defectively manufactured products--allocates residual risk to manufacturers and other sellers of products without regard to whether the defect was caused by an employee acting within the scope of employment. See Restatement Third, Torts: Products Liability. A manufacturer may not defeat this allocation of residual risk by demonstrating that a defect occurred in a particular unit of a product because of action by an employee that could be characterized as a departure from the scope of employment, such as deliberate sabotage. Moreover, it is not necessary for a plaintiff in a manufacturing-defect case to establish the converse, that is, that tortious conduct on the part of an identifiable employee or employees acting within the scope of employment produced the defect. Such a showing is not required in cases in which a product is alleged to be defective on the basis of its design or a manufacturer's failure to provide appropriate warnings to consumers of the product. The functional consequence is that respondeat superior is not relevant to outcomes in products-liability cases. Disaggregating the complex of human calculation, misjudgment, and foible that may have contributed to a defective product is not relevant to products-liability cases. A manufacturer or other seller is subject to liability in such cases without regard to whether its employees' contributions to the defective product stemmed from conduct within the scope of their employment.

In situations to which respondeat superior is relevant, tortious conduct by an employee takes place in the context of an identifiable employee-actor's interaction with a third party. This fact alone is insufficient to justify subjecting the employer to vicarious liability. An employer does not assume the role of insurer against all harm suffered by third parties with whom its employees may interact. Nor is it sufficient that an employer might have taken additional measures to control the employee, unless the employer was negligent in failing to take such measures. See § 7.05.

Under subsection (2), an employee's tortious conduct is outside the scope of employment when the employee is engaged in an independent course of conduct not intended to further any purpose of the employer. An independent course of conduct represents a departure from, not an escalation of, conduct involved in performing assigned work or other conduct that an employer permits or controls. When an employee commits a tort with the sole intention of furthering the employee's own purposes, and not any purpose of the employer, it is neither fair nor true-to-life to characterize the employee's action as that of a representative of the employer. The employee's intention severs the basis for treating the employee's act as that of the employer in the employee's interaction with the third party.

The formulation of the scope-of-employment doctrine in subsection (2) differs from its counterparts in Restatement Second, Agency §§ 228 and 229 because it is phrased in more general terms. Under Restatement Second, Agency § 228(1)(b), conduct falls within the scope of employment when it "occurs substantially within the authorized time and space limits." This formulation does not naturally encompass the working circumstances of many managerial and professional employees and others whose work is not so readily cabined by temporal or spatial limitations. Many employees in contemporary workforces interact on an employer's behalf with third parties although the employee is neither situated on the employer's premises nor continuously or exclusively engaged in performing assigned work. Moreover, under § 228(1)(c), conduct is not within the scope of employment unless "it is actuated, at least in part, by a purpose to serve" the employer. Under § 228(2), conduct is not within the scope of employment if it is "too little actuated by a purpose to serve" the employer. Under § 235, conduct is not within the scope of employment "if it is done with no intention" to perform an authorized service or an incidental act. These formulations are not entirely consistent; an act motivated by _some_ purpose to serve the employer could still be _"too little actuated"_ to be within the scope of employment.

In contrast, under subsection (2) of this section, an employee's conduct is outside the scope of employment when it occurs within an independent course of conduct intended to serve no purpose of the employer. Most cases apply the standard stated by this section.

Alternative formulations avoid the use of motive or intention to determine whether an employee's tortious conduct falls within the scope of employment. These tests vary somewhat in how they articulate the requisite tie between the tortfeasor's employment and the tort. In general, such a tie is present only when the tort is a generally foreseeable consequence of the enterprise undertaken by the employer or is incident to it. In this context, "foreseeability" means that, in the context of the employer's particular enterprise, the employee's conduct is not so unusual or startling that it seems unfair to include the loss resulting from it in the employer's business costs. This is a separate inquiry from whether an employer acted negligently. One test directs the trier of fact to determine whether an employee's tort was "engendered by the employment," or an "outgrowth" of it, or whether the tort arose out of a personal dispute or other causative factor unrelated to employment. An alternative looks to whether the employment furnished the specific impetus for a tort or increased the general risk that the tort would occur. These tests leave to the finder of fact the challenge of determining whether a tortfeasor's employment did more than create a happenstance opportunity to commit the tort.

Although formulations that focus on an employee's intention may be difficult to apply in some cases, formulations based on assessments of "foreseeability" are potentially confusing and may generate outcomes that are less predictable than intent-based formulations. "Foreseeability" has a well-developed meaning in connection with negligence and to use it, additionally, to define a different boundary for respondeat superior risks confusion. Moreover, references to "foreseeability" in the respondeat superior context tend to conflate the foreseeable likelihood, from an employer's standpoint, that mishaps and slippage will occur in connection with the performance of assigned work, with the possibility that the work may lead to or somehow provide the occasion for intentional misconduct that is distinct from an employee's actions in performing assigned work. To be sure, the latter possibility is indeed always "foreseeable," given human frailty, but its occurrence is not a risk that an employer can effectively control and its occurrence may be related causally to employment no more than to other relationships and circumstances in an errant employee's life more generally.

Moreover, a "foreseeability" formulation for imposing vicarious liability may penalize an employer who has taken reasonable precautions against employee misconduct to the extent it enables a plaintiff to demonstrate that the employer did indeed foresee the risk of misconduct. Attaching such a penalty to the taking of reasonable precaution against the risk of harm to third parties to whom an employer owes a duty of care is inconsistent with a central objective of contemporary negligence law, which is to create incentives toward efficient measures to reduce the occurrence of torts. See generally Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 3 (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005) (defining negligence as a person's failure to exercise "reasonable care under all the circumstances," including the foreseeable likelihood that conduct will result in harm, the foreseeable severity of that harm, and the burden on the person and others of taking precautions to eliminate or reduce the possibility of harm). Nonetheless, formulations focused on "foreseeability" may be appropriate in some circumstances, such as an alignment in interest between employer and employee conducive to employee testimony that no intention to serve the employer actuated the employee's tortious conduct.

Whether an employee acted within the scope of employment in committing a tort is also relevant in statutory contexts. The common-law test of scope of employment may be relevant but not dispositive in determining whether an employee's literary, artistic, or other creative work constitutes a work for hire for purposes of federal copyright legislation. Coverage determinations under workers'-compensation statutes require assessments with elements that may overlap with but are distinct from the definition of scope of employment.

_c. Conduct in the performance of work and scope of employment._ An employee's conduct is within the scope of employment when it constitutes performance of work assigned to the employee by the employer. The fact that the employee performs the work carelessly does not take the employee's conduct outside the scope of employment, nor does the fact that the employee otherwise makes a mistake in performing the work. Likewise, conduct is not outside the scope of employment merely because an employee disregards the employer's instructions.

**Illustrations:**

1\. P, who writes bail bonds, employs A as a bond "runner." A's assigned work is to locate persons for whom P has written bonds who jump bail and return them to custody. P directs A to search for J. A mistakenly identifies T as J, breaks down the door of T's home, and holds T at gunpoint. Under applicable law, A's conduct toward T is tortious. P is subject to liability to T. A's actions constituted performance of work P assigned to A.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that P instructs its runners to contact Ps office prior to attempting a forcible entry into a residence. A neglects to do this before breaking down the door of T's home. Same result.

In Illustration 2, A's conduct is within the scope of employment, despite A's disregard for Ps instruction, because A is engaged in doing work assigned by P. Likewise, had A contravened an instruction not to exceed the speed limit while pursuing a bail jumper and caused an accident that injured another driver or a pedestrian, A would have been acting within the scope of employment. Thus, the fact that an employee's action violates a generally applicable law, such as a speeding limit, does not by itself place the employee's conduct outside the scope of employment. These results are not surprising. An employee may believe that the employer wishes the employee to disregard an inconvenient constraint when the employee fears that compliance would jeopardize completing the employee's assigned mission at all or completing it on or ahead of schedule. Although the employee's belief may be mistaken, it is compatible with acting in an assigned role to do an assigned task. However, the character, extreme nature, or other circumstances accompanying an employee's actions may demonstrate that the employee's course of conduct is independent of performing work assigned by the employer and intended solely to further the employee's own purposes. See Illustrations 7, 8, and 9 below.

An employee's failure to take action may also be conduct within the scope of employment. For example, an employee's failure to do work assigned by the employer, when harm to a third party results, under some circumstances may constitute negligence. A negligent action "frequently involves a failure to take a reasonable precaution . . . . \[which\] can be described as an omission, and it hence can be said that the omission is itself negligent." Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 3, Comment _c_ (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005).

**Illustrations:**

3\. O owns Blackacre, a large property with many trees, several of which overhang a municipal parking lot. 0 engages P, a landscaper, to maintain the trees on Blackacre. P assigns responsibility for maintaining Blackacre's trees to A, an employee. Negligently, A fails to inspect Blackacre's trees and thus fails to notice a major crack in a large ailanthus with branches overhanging the municipal parking lot. T, a patron of the lot, is injured when T is struck by a branch from the ailanthus. P is subject to liability to T. P is vicariously liable for A's negligent failure properly to maintain the tree, which constituted nonperformance of A's assigned work.

4\. Same facts as Illustration 3, except that 0 is a not-for-profit foundation that owns Blackacre, a site of historic interest, and P gratuitously undertakes responsibilities for tree maintenance. Same result.

An employee's assigned work may include tasks that contemplate the necessity of using physical force to complete the assigned work, as in Illustrations 1 and 2. An employee's assigned duties may also place the employee in situations in which physical consequences may follow in an uninterrupted sequence from verbal exchanges with third parties. An escalation in the pitch of an employee's conduct does not by itself transform the conduct into an independent course of conduct that represents a departure not within the scope of employment. It is a question of fact what motivated an employee's conduct as verbal exchanges escalate or when an employee's use of physical force becomes more pronounced.

**Illustrations:**

5\. P, who owns a fast-food restaurant, employs A as a junior manager. A's assigned work includes attempting to resolve complaints made by customers. T, a customer, complains to C, a cashier, when T is not served promptly. C asks for A's assistance. A discusses T's complaint with T. T makes profane statements to A in a loud voice. While announcing that A will listen to T no longer, A slams A's fist into a nearby stack of plastic trays, causing the trays to scatter. T is injured by one of the trays. Under applicable law, A's conduct toward T is tortious. P is subject to liability to T. A's action in slamming the trays was incidental to performing work that P assigned to A.

6\. Same facts as Illustration 5, except that T's statements to A include an accusation of misbehavior unrelated to A's work in the restaurant. Same result. A's conduct toward T was incidental to performing work that P assigned to T. T's introduction of an extramural grievance does not take A's conduct outside the scope of A's employment.

7\. Same facts as Illustration 6, except that A follows T into the restaurant's parking lot and assaults T, stating that A cannot allow T's accusation to go unanswered. P is not subject to liability to T. In A's pursuit of and assault on T, A departed from As work and engaged in an independent course of conduct not intended to further Ps interests.

8\. P, who employs A as a truck driver, assigns A the task of hauling a load of cargo at night on a road that runs directly from the loading to the delivery point through an isolated area with no visible sites of ongoing human activity. Soon after beginning the journey, A becomes enraged when T, driving a car immediately behind As truck, flashes the car's high beams. A stops the truck, blocking the road, and approaches T's car, holding a length of pipe in a threatening manner. A desists when the driver behind T begins to yell at A. Under applicable law, As conduct toward T is tortious. P is not subject to liability to T. In leaving the truck to threaten T, A embarked on a course of conduct independent of performing work assigned to A by P and motivated by As own interests.

9\. Same facts as Illustration 8. A next reenters the truck and straightens it so that it no longer blocks the highway. T then passes A. For the next 50 miles, A follows T very closely and in a manner that T reasonably perceives as threatening. Under applicable law, As conduct toward T is tortious. P is not subject to liability to T. Although As physical actions take the cargo closer to its destination, A, additionally, acted to threaten T, with no intention to further Ps interests.

In determining whether an employee's tortious conduct is within the scope of employment, the nature of the tort is relevant, as is whether the conduct also constitutes a criminal act. An employee's intentionally criminal conduct may indicate a departure from conduct within the scope of employment, not a simple escalation. The nature and magnitude of the conduct are relevant to determining the employee's intention at the time. In Illustration 9, moreover, it is relevant that the occasion for the truck driver's close pursuit of the car ahead is a previous altercation between the truck driver and the car's driver, and not, for example, a desire on the truck driver's part to cause a slowly moving vehicle to move aside so that the truck's cargo can be delivered more rapidly. Matters of degree are also relevant to this determination. For example, in Illustration 5, A's act is within the course of employment when A slams into the stack of trays at the culmination of A's attempt to resolve T's complaint about service in the restaurant. If, instead, A draws a gun and shoots T, the nature of A's action--which constitutes a serious crime--and its extreme quality may indicate that A has launched upon an independent course of action. In Illustration 9, A's resumption of driving the truck also constitutes a vehicle for A's independent course of action, which is harassing T.

The determinative question is whether the course of conduct in which the tort occurred is within the scope of employment. Intentional torts and other intentional wrongdoing may be within the scope of employment. For example, if an employee's job duties include determining the prices at which the employer's output will be sold to customers, the employee's agreement with a competitor to fix prices is within the scope of employment unless circumstances establish a departure from the scope of employment. Likewise, when an employee's job duties include making statements to prospective customers to induce them to buy from the employer, intentional misrepresentations made by the employee are within the scope of employment unless circumstances establish that the employee has departed from it.

An employee may engage in conduct, part of which is within the scope of employment and part of which is not. For example, if the irate driver in Illustration 9, while continuing driving down the highway, leans out of the truck cab and shoots the driver of a car who has enraged the truck's driver, the shooting is not within the scope of employment although driving the truck is within the scope of employment. Alternatively, suppose the truck's driver chats with a friend on a cell phone, becomes distracted, and negligently causes an accident. Talking on the cell phone is a non-work-related independent course of action outside the scope of employment. However, the negligent driving that results is within the scope of employment because driving is part of the driver's assigned task. In Illustration 9, the irate truck driver has converted performance of an assigned task into a vehicle with which to pursue an independent and tortious course of conduct, intended to serve only the driver's own purposes.

When an employee's tortious conduct is outside the scope of employment, alternate theories of liability may be available against the employer. For example, in Illustrations 7 and 9, the irate employee's employer would be subject to liability under the rule stated in § 7.05 if the harm to the third party was caused by the employer's failure to exercise reasonable care in selecting, training, supervising, or otherwise controlling the employee.

_d. Other conduct within employer's control._ Purely personal acts that an employee undertakes during the work period--such as personal hygiene, smoking, and eating--may be within the scope of employment because they are incidental to the employee's performance of assigned work. However, a tortious act committed in connection with purely personal conduct is not within the scope of employment if the employee is motivated solely by purposes other than the employer's. Moreover, if an employer exercises control over an employee's personal acts while the employee is off the employer's premises and not otherwise engaged in work, the employee's conduct remains within the scope of employment because it is subject to the employer's control. However, an employee's conduct is not within the scope of employment, although the employee is physically present on the employer's premises, when the employee's actions are far removed in purpose from work assigned by the employer. In some circumstances, even multitasking at an employee's assigned work station may remove the employee's conduct from the scope of employment because it may provide an employee with an occasion to commit a tort motivated solely by the employee's own purposes.

**Illustrations:**

10\. P Insurance Co. furnishes telephones to its office staff with the direction that they may be used only when necessary to an employee's work. A, a claims processor, uses the telephone P Insurance Co. provides to make statements defamatory of T, a personal enemy of A's. The recipients of A's defamatory statements are unrelated to P Insurance Co.'s business. P Insurance Co. is not subject to liability to T. A's conduct in defaming T was not within the scope of A's employment. A acted only for A's own purposes.

11\. Same facts as Illustration 10, except that P Insurance Co. permits its employees to make personal use of the telephones that it furnishes. Same result. By permitting employees to make personal use of its telephones, P Insurance Co. did not expand A's scope of employment to encompass defamation of A's personal enemies.

_e. Peregrinations._ In general, travel required to perform work, such as travel from an employer's office to a job site or from one job site to another, is within the scope of an employee's employment while traveling to and from work is not. However, an employer may place an employee's travel to and from work within the scope of employment by providing the employee with a vehicle and asserting control over how the employee uses the vehicle so that the employee may more readily respond to the needs of the employer's enterprise. An employee's travel to and from work may also be within the scope of employment if the employee does more than simply travel to and from work, for example by stopping for the employer's benefit to accomplish a task assigned by the employer.

An employee's travel during the work day that is not within the scope of employment has long been termed a "frolic" of the employee's own. De minimis departures from assigned routes are not "frolics." A "frolic" may also consist of activity on an employer's premises and within working hours, as in Illustrations 10 and 11. The conventional meaning of the term "detour" is a deviation from travel on an assigned route that is still within the scope of employment.

**Illustrations:**

12\. P, who owns a tree-maintenance service, employs A as foreman for a crew that provides tree-trimming services for utility companies and owners of commercial and residential property. P furnishes A with a pickup truck and authorizes A to use the truck to commute between A's residence and the day's job site. P also directs A to use the truck to make sales calls to prospective customers. En route from home to a utility job site, A stops to visit C, a prospective residential customer. As A turns to pull into C's driveway, A injures T, a pedestrian whom A negligently failed to notice. P is subject to liability to T. Visiting C was part of A's assigned work for P.

13\. Same facts as Illustration 12, except that en route to visit C, A departs slightly from the most direct route to visit A's favorite take-out restaurant. A's departure is within the scope of employment.

14\. P, who owns a nursery, employs A. A's assigned duties include caring for the nursery's lawn and keeping the nursery's lawn mower filled with gas. En route to the nursery from home, A stops at a drugstore to pick up medicine for A's spouse, then stops at a gas station across the street to buy a can of gas for the nursery's lawn mower. A next returns home to drop off the medicine, then stops at another gas station to buy gas for A's truck. Leaving this second gas station, A drives negligently and collides with T. P is subject to liability to T. A was transporting gas to fill the nursery's lawnmower, necessary for A to perform A's assigned work. A's stop for gas was an incidental deviation from A's performance of assigned work.

Frolics end when an employee reenters employment, that is, when the employee is once again performing assigned work and taking actions incidental to it. In Illustrations 10 and 11, for example, A's telephonic frolic ends when A returns to A's tasks as a claims-processor or to activity incidental to performing those tasks. When a frolic consists of a physical journey away from the workplace or a material departure from an assigned route of travel, an employee reenters employment when the employee has taken action consistent with once again resuming work.

Even when travel by an employee is outside the scope of employment, an employer may be subject to liability for harm to third persons caused by the employee en route if the employer has breached its duty of reasonable care with regard to risks to third parties posed by the employee that the employer knows or should know. See § 7.05; Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 37, Comment _d,_ and § 41, Comment _e_ (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005). For example, if an employer knows or should know that an employee's condition makes it unsafe for the employee to drive home from work, the employer's duty of reasonable care to third parties may require that the employer take reasonable means to reduce the risk, such as providing alternative transportation. It is especially likely that an employer will know or should know that an employee is unfit to drive if it is the employer itself that is responsible for the employee's condition, for example by requiring the employee to work past the point of exhaustion. Likewise, an employer owes a duty of reasonable care to third persons who may be harmed by an intoxicated employee with regard to risks posed by the employee that the employer knows or should know. Such risks are especially likely to be foreseeable when an employer itself furnishes alcohol to its employees or permits consumption of alcohol within work time or requires employee attendance at an occasion at which the employer knows alcohol will be served.

_f. Definition of employee._ For purposes of respondeat superior, an agent is an employee only when the principal controls or has the right to control the manner and means through which the agent performs work. The definition has the consequence of distinguishing between employees and agents who are not employees because they retain the right to control how they perform their work. If a person has no right to control an actor and exercises no control over the actor, the actor is not an agent. See § 1.01, Comment _f(1)._

The fact that an agent performs work gratuitously does not relieve a principal of vicarious liability when the principal controls or has the right to control the manner and means of the agent's performance of work.

A person who causes a third party to believe that an actor is the person's employee may be subject to liability to the third party for harm caused by the actor when the third party justifiably relies on the actor's skill or care and the actor's conduct, if that of an employee, would be within the scope of employment. For the general principle of estoppel, see § 2.05.

Numerous factual indicia are relevant to whether an agent is an employee. These include: the extent of control that the agent and the principal have agreed the principal may exercise over details of the work; whether the agent is engaged in a distinct occupation or business; whether the type of work done by the agent is customarily done under a principal's direction or without supervision; the skill required in the agent's occupation; whether the agent or the principal supplies the tools and other instrumentalities required for the work and the place in which to perform it; the length of time during which the agent is engaged by a principal; whether the agent is paid by the job or by the time worked; whether the agent's work is part of the principal's regular business; whether the principal and the agent believe that they are creating an employment relationship; and whether the principal is or is not in business. Also relevant is the extent of control that the principal has exercised in practice over the details of the agent's work.

In some employment relationships, an employer's right of control may be attenuated. For example, senior corporate officers, like captains of ships, may exercise great discretion in operating the enterprises entrusted to them, just as skilled professionals exercise discretion in performing their work. Nonetheless, all employers retain a right of control, however infrequently exercised.

**Illustration:**

15\. A, the CEO of P Corporation, exercises general managerial authority over its operations. P Corporation's directors, concerned that A's impaired vision makes it unsafe for A to drive, direct A to use a driver and car to be furnished by P Corporation when A travels by car on business. P Corporation's directors have the right so to direct A. A is an employee of P Corporation for this purpose.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency._ This section is a consolidated treatment of topics covered in several separate sections of Restatement Second, Agency, including §§ 219, 220, 228, 229, 230, 231, 232, 233, 234, 235, 236, 237, and 267.

_b. When tortious conduct is within the scope of employment--in general._ For the tie between respondeat superior and the difficulty of establishing negligent conduct by an employer, see Gary T. Schwartz, The Hidden and Fundamental Issue of Employer Vicarious Liability, [_69 S. Cal. L. Rev. 1739, 1760 (1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S41-5XD0-00CV-5200-00000-00&context=1530671). On the relationships among respondeat superior, an employer's capacity to control employees' activities, and insurability, see James A. Henderson, Jr., Why Negligence Dominates Tort, [_50 UCLA L. Rev. 377, 402 (2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:47ST-TXV0-00CV-6089-00000-00&context=1530671).

An employer's right to control employee action emerges as a central justification and limiting principle for vicarious liability in accounts within philosophy as well as economics. See Tony Honore, The Morality of Tort Law--Questions and Answers, in Philosophical Foundations of Tort Law 73, 85 (David G. Owen ed., 1995) ("\[t\]he conventional reasons given for holding that the employer ought to bear the risk of loss within certain limits for the employee's harmful conduct in the course of his work are that the employer (i) has control over the business, including the work of employees, and (ii) stands to profit from the employee's services. . . . the person who, in a situation of uncertainty, has a degree of control over how it will turn out, and who stands to gain if it goes in his favor, must bear the risk that it will turn out to harm another."); William M. Landes & Richard A. Posner, The Economic Structure of Tort Law 208-209 (1987) ("\[i\]n the case of an intentional tort the court is likely to insist in addition that the employee in committing the tort have been trying, however mis-guidedly, to advance the employer's goals, as where the employee assaults a debtor of the employer in order to collect the debt. If the employee is actuated by purely personal motives, the employer's practical ability to prevent the tort will be slight. The employer should be able to ensure that the employee not only use due care but also avoid overzealous pursuit of the employer's goals, but it is much harder for the employer to screen and monitor employees for purely personal attitudes.").

Professor Seavey, too, characterized the justification for respondeat superior in terms that highlight the significance of an employer's control over its employees: "it appears safe to assume that an employer can and does bring a pressure to bear upon his employees which has a greater effect upon them than would the chance of their being made defendants in suits, which, if action were permitted only against them, ordinarily would not be brought. The pressure upon the employee is thus twofold." Warren A. Seavey, Speculations as to Respondeat Superior, in Studies in Agency 129, 148 (1949). Alternatively, "\[i\]f there is not this reasonably intimate connection between the employment and the injury to the third person, the master is not liable, a result which, using the language of causation, may be explained on the theory that the employment has so little connection with the final result that it may be disregarded." Id. at 133.

More generally, whether an individual may be characterized as part of a collective endeavor is characterized as a function of the individual's "participatory intention, or an intention to act as part of a group." Christopher Kutz, Complicity: Ethics and Law for a Collective Age 67 (2000). It follows that an individual whose action is not so intended is not engaged in "collective action . . . the product of individuals who orient themselves around a joint project." Id. Acting with no purpose of serving an employer disassociates the employee's action from the employer's project.

On the treatment of manufacturing defects in contemporary products-liability law, see generally Dan B. Dobbs, The Law of Torts § 372 (2000). Professor Dobbs characterizes manufacturing-defect cases as "now the core case for strict liability, because if a product's risk is unknowable, the design and warning claims now generally fail." Id. at 1033. Some statutes make "state-of the-art" defenses applicable to manufacturing-defect claims as well as design and labeling claims. A "state of the art" statute holds a manufacturer responsible for a defect if it should reasonably have known of an avoidable risk but forecloses liability otherwise. See id. at 1032, 1033 nn.12-13 (noting Iowa Code § 668.12 and Ky. Rev. Stat. § 411.310). Other statutes appear to contemplate strict liability in manufacturing-defect cases (e.g., Miss. Code Ann. § 11-1-63 (1999 & Supp. 2004); Mo. Stat. § 537.764(2) (2000 & Supp. 2005); and Neb. Rev. Stat. § 25-21,182) (1995 & Supp. 2003). In any event, in Professor Dobbs's assessment, "knowability of risks is logically no part of the manufacturing defect case; product flaws are happenstantial, so the fact that the flaw was not known or reasonably known seems to be irrelevant as a common law matter." Id. at 1033. The presence of a manufacturing defect may often be indicative of negligence on the part of the manufacturer and its employees: "\[i\]n many cases manufacturing defects are in fact caused by manufacturer negligence but plaintiffs have difficulty proving it. Strict liability therefore performs a function similar to the concept of res ipsa loquitur, allowing deserving plaintiffs to succeed notwithstanding what would otherwise be difficult or insuperable problems of proof." Restatement Third, Torts: Products Liability § 2, Comment _a._ Negligence on the part of a manufacturer may, of course, reflect negligence on the part of its employees in carrying out their assigned tasks.

For an example of the point that a contemporary employee may engage in work simultaneously with nonwork activity and while physically located away from the employer's premises, see [_Clo White Co. v. Lattimore, 590 S.E.2d 381 (Ga.App.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:49WH-3JN0-0039-407M-00000-00&context=1530671) (trial court properly denied summary judgment for employer when employee driving on way to office injured plaintiff; employee may have been acting within scope of employment at time of accident because cell-phone records indicate three calls to employer around time of accident, inter alia to inform supervisor of estimated arrival time and to obtain information to be used in fulfilling duties upon arrival). _Clo White_ notes that the employer had its employee's cell-phone number "and could also access him 24 hours a day by paging him on a pager that the company provided. \[Employee\] would often call his office in response to several pages that he received outside of his regular working hours." [_Id. at 382_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:49WH-3JN0-0039-407M-00000-00&context=1530671).

A well-known instance of an expansive treatment of employee motivation is [_Nelson v. American-West African Line, 86 F.2d 730, 731 (2d Cir. 1936)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-TMK0-003B-K494-00000-00&context=1530671) (trial court erred in granting defendant's motion for summary judgment; drunken boatswain who routed plaintiff out of his bunk with a blow, saying "\[g\]et up, you big son of a bitch, and turn to" and then continued to fight might have thought he acted in interests of ship). _Nelson_ is cited in _Ira S. Bushey & Sons v. United States, 398 F.2d 167, 170 (2d Cir. 1968)_ as an instance of the artificiality invited by the motivation test. See also [_Thompson v. United States, 504 F. Supp. 1087 (D.S.D.1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-V6P0-0039-S2MG-00000-00&context=1530671) (police trainee, who shot fellow officer in station while practicing quick-draw technique, was motivated to benefit employer by improving firearms handling). Professor Kleinberger characterizes the motivation test as "always pliable." See Daniel S. Kleinberger, Agency, Partnerships, and LLCs 92 (2d ed. 2002).

The standard articulated in _Bushey_ is whether an employee's tortious conduct is "not so 'unforeseeable' to charge the \[defendant\] with responsibility." The opinion quotes 2 Harper & James, The Law of Torts 1377-1378 (1956): "'what is reasonably foreseeable in this context \[of _respondeat superior_\] . . . . is quite a different thing from the foreseeably unreasonable risk of harm that spells negligence . . . .The proper test here bears far more resemblance to that which limits liability for workmen's compensation than to the test for negligence. The employer should be held to expect risks, to the public also, which arise 'out of and in the course' of his employment of labor.'" _398 F.2d at 171-172_. However, the court's analysis in _Bushey_ does treat the errant employee's motive as a limiting factor. In _Bushey,_ an inebriated seaman, returning from shore leave to his ship in dry dock, turned wheels on the drydock wall, which opened valves that led to flooding and then to the ship's fall against the wall, plus partial sinkage of the drydock and the ship. The court held that "\[t\]he risk that seamen going and coming from the \[ship\] might cause damage to the drydock is enough to make it fair that the enterprise bear the loss," having previously noted that "the proclivity of seamen to find solace for solitude by copious resort to the bottle while ashore has been noted in opinions too numerous to require citation." _Id. at 172_. But the court also observes that "while \[the seaman's\] act is not readily explicable, at least it was not shown to be due entirely to facets of his personal life." Id. For further discussion of _Bushey_ and its bearing on vicarious liability more generally, see Gregory C. Keating, The Idea of Fairness in the Law of Enterprise Liability, [_95 Mich. L. Rev. 1266, 1277-1282, 1287-1295 (1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S41-2T30-00CV-52W6-00000-00&context=1530671).

California cases have long defined scope of employment for respondeat superior purposes by equating it to scope for purposes of workers'-com-pensation statutes, placing an employee's conduct within the scope whenever it "arose out of or in the course of employment. See, e.g., [_Rodgers v. Kemper Construction Co., 124 Cal.Rptr. 143 (Cal.App.1975)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S11-V5N0-003C-R0RD-00000-00&context=1530671) (subcontractor vicariously liable for assault committed by employees who drank for several hours in rest area on job site, although assault committed after work hours; after-hours conduct had become a customary incident of employment relationship).

Spatial proximity between an employee's tort and either the workplace or a rest facility furnished by the employer is a significant limiting factor in _Bushey_ and _Rodgers._ See [_Ta-ber v. Maine, 67 F.3d 1029, 1037 n.4 (2d Cir.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-HCN0-001T-D0JP-00000-00&context=1530671) (tort committed off-base that resulted from off-base drinking would not be within scope of employment; applying California law, court holds that tort committed by off-duty seaman was within scope of employment when it stemmed from on-base drinking). _Taber_ characterizes the "pervasive control that the military exercises over its personnel while they are on base" as a basis for allocating "the costs of base operations to the government. . . . quite apart from whether or not the military benefits from the boost in morale achieved through fairly lenient on-base drinking policies." [_Id. at 1037_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-HCN0-001T-D0JP-00000-00&context=1530671).

On statutory contexts requiring determinations that overlap to some extent with scope-of-employment determinations, see Arthur Larson & Lex K. Larson, 2 Larson's Worker's Compensation Law chs. 20-46 (2003). On scope-of-employment determinations and copyright law, see [_Avtec Sys. v. Peiffer, 21 F.3d 568, 571-572 (4th Cir. 1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RTX-FFX0-003B-P049-00000-00&context=1530671) (determination of whether employer owned valid copyright in computer program as work made for hire under [_17 U.S.C. §§ 101(1)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8S7X-DF02-8T6X-73XY-00000-00&context=1530671) and [_201(b)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8S7X-DF02-8T6X-73YW-00000-00&context=1530671) requires determination of whether employee created computer program within the scope of employment, citing [_Community for Creative Non-Violence v. Reid, 490 U.S. 730, 737-738 (1989))_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RV9-SB40-003B-400K-00000-00&context=1530671).

_c. Conduct in the performance of work and scope of employment._ Illustration 1 is based on [_Coastal Bail Bonds, Inc. v. Cope, 697 So. 2d 48 (Ala.Civ.App.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S59-MRH0-0039-452X-00000-00&context=1530671).

In Illustrations 3 and 4, Blackacre's owner may be an additional defendant. See [_McDermott v. Calvary Baptist Church, 819 A.2d 795 (Conn.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48GD-PKN0-0039-428J-00000-00&context=1530671) (not-for-profit foundation subject to liability when volunteer neglects to maintain trees properly).

Illustrations 5 and 6 are based on [_Reynolds v. L & L Mgmt., Inc., 492 S.E.2d 347, 350 (Ga.App.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RJ8-M3N0-0039-4094-00000-00&context=1530671) (characterizing situation as "the uninterrupted escalation of an argument between a customer and an employee performing one of the duties for which he is employed"). See also [_Lee v. United States, 171 F.Supp.2d 566, 575-577 (M.D.N.C.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4586-F3Y0-0038-Y1T9-00000-00&context=1530671) (manager of postal facility acting within scope of employment when manager assaulted plaintiff; assaults resulted from escalation of work-related disputes and discussions, not unrelated personal motives); [_Patterson v. Blair, 172 S.W.3d 361 (Ky.2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4H75-1BW0-TVV9-0230-00000-00&context=1530671) (tortious acts of employee of car dealer toward truculent customer who failed to pay balance due on vehicle within scope of employment because either employee was acting to further interests of employer or conduct was incidental to actions authorized by employer; at stoplight, having set out to locate customer, employee exited his car and fired 4 shots into tires of vehicle after customer refused to exit vehicle). _Patterson_ characterizes as an "aberration" an earlier case, [_Citizen's Fin. Co. v. Blair, 239 S.W.2d 77 (Ky.1951)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-YKV0-000F-S2SW-00000-00&context=1530671), in which the court held that an employee of a finance company, dispatched to collect a debt from the plaintiff, acted outside the scope of employment when he struck and twisted the plaintiffs hand. _Blair_ held that the employee's act was not foreseeable because the employee's duty "did not carry with it as a reasonably contemplated act, the authority to assault \[plaintiff\]." _Patterson_ notes _Blair's_ consistency with an example in Restatement Second, Agency § 245, Comment _a:_ "Thus, one who is sent to collect a bill does not normally make the employer responsible if he seizes money from the debtor, since this would be an unusual and unexpectable proceeding of a bill collector. On the other hand, one who is sent to recapture property is likely to come into contact with a possessor unwilling to surrender it, and persons sent to recapture goods are frequently \[of\] the kind who would be not unlikely to attempt force.'" [_172 S.W.3d at 370_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4H75-1BW0-TVV9-0230-00000-00&context=1530671).

On Illustration 7, see [_Monty v. Or-landi, 337 P.2d 861, 865-866 (Cal.App. 1959)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRK-N670-003C-349B-00000-00&context=1530671) (bar owner not liable when bartender assaults patron outside bar in course of personal dispute with bartender's common-law wife, whom bartender directed to leave bar; even if bartender's duties involved keeping peace in bar, burden on plaintiff to establish "that at the time he suffered the injuries the relationship of master and servant existed and that the wrongful act was within the scope of the servant's employment. . . . we can only conclude that the jury determined that the assault upon \[plaintiff\] was a personal altercation"). On escalations in an employee's use of physical force, see [_Brueckner v. Norwich Univ., 730 A.2d 1086, 1091-1092 (Vt. 1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3VS3-9H70-0039-40HX-00000-00&context=1530671) (upperclassmen used by military college to indoctrinate freshmen, although directed to refrain from mistreatment, acted within scope of employment in hazing activities directed toward plaintiff, including physical assaults; "the actions involved in hazing . . . may fairly be seen as qualitatively similar to the indoctrination and orientation with which the \[upperclassmen\] were charged.").

Illustrations 8 and 9 are based on [_Mahar v. StoneWood Transp., 823 A.2d 540 (Me.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48HP-FHG0-0039-41G0-00000-00&context=1530671). For a similar case in which the physical actions that constitute an employee's performance of work overlap with tortious conduct outside the scope of employment, see Power v. Central S.M.T. Co., Ltd., \[1949\] S.C. 376, 380 (First Division). In _Power,_ the employee was a bus conductor who, taking vengeance against a passenger with whom the conductor had previously had an altercation, rang the bell to start the bus before the passenger had fully alighted from the bus. The court held that the employer was not liable because the conductor acted "while engaged at work, for the gratification of the \[conductor's\] personal spite. . . ." The court's reasoning in _Power_ is criticized in P. S. Atiyah, Vicarious Liability in the Law of Torts 199 (1967), as "completely un-supportable, for the servant was plainly acting in the course of her employment. She was authorised to ring the bell with due regard for the safety of the passengers, and whether she rang it negligently, or with the deliberate intention of causing injury to the plaintiff, the fact remains that it was merely an improper mode of performing an authorised act." The dissenting opinion in _Mahar_ reasons similarly: "there can be no dispute that \[trucker's\] conduct, driving \[employer's\] truck, was exactly the conduct for which he was employed. Further his conduct occurred within the time and on the route where \[employer\] assigned \[trucker\] to drive the truck." [_823 A.2d at 549_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48HP-FHG0-0039-41G0-00000-00&context=1530671). On vicarious liability stemming from employee assaults on third parties, it has been observed that "the line separating a master's _respondeat superior_ liability from a servant's individual responsibility for the latter's on-duty assault is to be drawn somewhere between a servant's venting purely personal spleen and the point at which the servant continues to press the master's interest with mistaken vigor and in an inappropriately aggressive manner." See [_Baker v. St. Francis Hosp., 126 P.3d 602, 609 n.3 (Okla.2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4HVM-DM00-0039-401D-00000-00&context=1530671) (Opala, J., concurring and dissenting in part).

On other conduct outside the scope of employment, see [_Los Ranchitos v. Tierra Grande, Inc., 861 P.2d 263, 268 (N.M.App.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-X2G0-003D-D3M0-00000-00&context=1530671) (even if clerical worker characterized as employee of defendant, employee's embezzlement of funds of partnership where clerical worker performed work was outside scope of employment because "activated by her personal motives").

For representative recent cases involving intentional torts within the scope of employment, see [_Quick v. Peoples Bank, 993 F.2d 793, 798 (11th Cir.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-FNT0-003B-P3CH-00000-00&context=1530671) (doctrine of respondeat superior applicable in civil RICO suit; bank subject to liability when officer acted in furtherance of bank's business in making fraudulent loans); [_Tompkins v. Cyr, 995 F. Supp. 664, 684 (N.D.Tex.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RT8-W1W0-0038-Y362-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_202 F.3d 770 (5th Cir.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YFF-K110-0038-X43K-00000-00&context=1530671) (officer of organizations dedicated to elimination of abortion acted within scope of employment in directing conduct against plaintiff that constituted intentional infliction of emotional distress); [_Smith v. Goodyear Tire & Rubber Co., 856 F.Supp. 1347 (W.D.Mo.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-H960-003B-V4N0-00000-00&context=1530671) (employee of franchisor who allegedly acted intentionally to inflict harm on franchisee by failing to submit tire invoices to franchisor's office may have acted within scope of employment because employee could have acted to benefit employer; summary judgment inappropriate due to dispute over material fact of employee's motivation); [_Adams v. Hyannis Harborview, Inc., 838 F.Supp. 676, 692 (D.Mass.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-C980-001T-63S3-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_73 F.3d 1164 (1st Cir.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-5DY0-006F-M1BM-00000-00&context=1530671) (bank director acted within scope of employment in selling unregistered securities to plaintiffs; director intended to produce some benefit to bank, which made loans to purchasers); [_Glanz v. Vernick, 756 F.Supp. 632, 636-637 (D.Mass.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-FVN0-001T-72NS-00000-00&context=1530671) (clinic subject to liability if doctor employed by clinic violated Rehabilitation Act, [_29 U.S.C. § 504_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SG9-5TK2-8T6X-704R-00000-00&context=1530671), by denying treatment to HIV-positive patient); [_Larsen Chelsey Realty Co. v. Larsen, 656 A.2d 1009, 1021-1022 (Conn.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-YXW0-003D-81XC-00000-00&context=1530671) (action under Connecticut Unfair Trade Practices Act; employee's acts that constituted unfair competition and interference with plaintiff's contractual relations within scope of employment when employee acted in furtherance of employer's business); [_Youngblood v. Wall, 815 S.W.2d 512 (Tenn.App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-4R30-003F-92T3-00000-00&context=1530671) (real-estate agent's misrepresentation within scope of employment); [_GTE Southwest, Inc. v. Bruce, 998 S.W.2d 605, 617-618 (Tex. 1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WVC-5K10-0039-40JM-00000-00&context=1530671) (employer subject to liability when supervisory employee's actions constituted intentional infliction of emotional distress on plaintiffs; employee's intentional tort within scope of employment when "committed in the accomplishment of a duty entrusted to the employee, rather than because of personal animosity"; employer submitted no evidence supervisor's actions were "motivated by personal animosity rather than a misguided attempt to carry out his job duties."). See also [_Inter Mountain Mtge., Inc. v. Sulimen, 93 Cal.Rptr.2d 790, 795 (Cal.App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YTS-5ND0-0039-43DS-00000-00&context=1530671) (employee of mortgage company acting within scope of employment when employee fraudulently induced mortgage lender to make loan; nexus existed between tort and employment as a "loan representative" because employee placed in position that enabled submission of fraudulent loan applications); [_Larsen v. Chinwuba, 832 A.2d 193 (Md.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:49H2-M980-0039-43KP-00000-00&context=1530671) (for purposes of immunity under Maryland Tort Claims Act, insurance commissioner's statements about subject of investigation to press were within the scope of his public duties, although violative of a statute, because they were made incidentally to performing his duties as commissioner and were not made for his own benefit).

On an employer's vicarious liability when an employee's misconduct constitutes a crime, see, e.g., _Moecker v. Honeywell Int'l, Inc., 144 F.Supp.2d 1291, 1321 (M.D.Fla.2001)_ (although criminal, an employee's tortious act may subject employer to liability "where the tort is committed at least partly to serve the employer or is committed within the scope of his employment"; genuine issues of material fact whether employees' criminal violations of antitrust laws were within the scope of employment).

For the general point that an employee's actions on the employee's own behalf do not subject an employer to respondeat superior liability, see, e.g., [_Pruitt v. Pavelin, 685 P.2d 1347, 1357 (Ariz.App.1984)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-Y8N0-003F-T3WH-00000-00&context=1530671) (real-estate salesperson was acting on own behalf, and not on behalf of realestate firm, in property transaction in which salesperson represented only her own interests and firm had no other association with transaction); [_Peddinghaus v. Peddinghaus, 733 N.E.2d 797, 800 (Ill.App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40NF-GNT0-0039-43P0-00000-00&context=1530671) (neither plaintiff nor defendant thought defendant acted other than on defendant's own behalf in transaction in shares in family trust); [_Lomonaco v. Lomonaco's Landscaping, Inc., 732 N.Y.S.2d 434, 435 (App.Div.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:44D6-2C90-0039-42SK-00000-00&context=1530671) (plaintiffs injuries sustained when president and sole shareholder of landscaping firm was performing work at plaintiff's house, "as a son doing a favor for his father . . . performing a private errand, resulting from his personal relationship with plaintiffs, with no relation to the corporate business").

_d. Other conduct within employer's control._ On purely personal acts, compare [_Edgewater Motels, Inc. v. Gatzke, 277 N.W.2d 11 (Minn.1979)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-3280-003G-V42V-00000-00&context=1530671) (smoking during work within scope of employment), [_Iandioirio v. Kriss & Senko Enters., Inc., 517 A.2d 530 (Pa. 1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-XWN0-003C-S52N-00000-00&context=1530671) (same; employer dictated where employees could take smoke breaks) and [_Levine v. Peoples Broad. Corp., 140 S.E.2d 438 (W.Va.1965)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXR-42N0-003G-H273-00000-00&context=1530671) (use of toilet during work hours within scope of employment) with [_Haybeck v. Prodigy Servs. Co., 944 F.Supp. 326 (S.D.N.Y.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RVJ-5KN0-006F-P0ND-00000-00&context=1530671) (employer not subject to vicarious liability when computer-service employee became acquainted with customer via sex chat room and ultimately allegedly had unprotected sexual contact with customer, communicating HIV).

_e. Peregrinations._ The "frolic or detour" usage originates with Joel v. Morrison, 172 Eng. Rep. 1338, 1338-1339 (1834) ("\[i\]f the servants, being on their master's business, took a detour to call upon a friend, the master will be responsible. . . . The master is only liable when the servant is acting within the course of his employment. If he was going out of his way, against his master's implied commands, when driving on his master's business, he will make his master liable; but if he was going on a frolic of his own, without being at all on his master's business, the master will not be liable.").

Illustration 12 is based on [_Warren v. Estate of Kirk, 954 S.W.2d 722 (Tenn.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RJW-TPY0-0039-403P-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 14 is based on _Standley v. Johnson, 276 So. 2d 77 (Fla.Dist. App.1973)_. See also [_Courtless v. Jol-liffe, 507 S.E.2d 136, 139 (W.Va.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3T63-JFW0-0039-454S-00000-00&context=1530671) (reversing grant of summary judgment for employer; accident occurred when employee was en route to work from home but had stopped to buy shock absorbers for truck; although employee owned truck, employer reimbursed employee for monthly payments, furnished maintenance services and gasoline without charge, and reimbursed employee for cost of shock absorbers).

Whether an employee has reentered employment from a frolic requires a fact-specific inquiry. For the classic formulation, see [_Fiocco v. Carver, 137 N.E. 309, 310-311 (N.Y.1922)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-T0J0-003F-64PK-00000-00&context=1530671) (viewing facts most favorably to plaintiff, "\[a\]ll that we can find . . . is a suggestion that after a temporary excursion in streets remote from the homeward journey, the servant had at last made up his mind to put an end to his wanderings and return to the garage. He was still far away from the point at which he had first strayed from the path of duty, but his thoughts were homeward bound. Is this enough, in view of all the circumstances, to terminate the temporary abandonment and put him back in the sphere of service? . . . Location in time and space are circumstances that may guide the judgment, but will not be suffered to control it, divorced from other circumstances that may characterize the intent of the transaction. The dominant purpose must be proved to be the performance of the master's business. Till then there can be no resumption of a relation which has been broken and suspended.").

On an employer's duty to third parties when an employee is unfit to drive safely, see [_Robertson v. LeMaster, 301 S.E.2d 563, 567 (W.Va.1983)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXR-2C60-003G-H4KP-00000-00&context=1530671) (employer who required employee to work for 27 hours straight and then "set\[\] him loose upon the highway without providing alternative transportation or rest facilities to its exhausted employee created an unreasonable risk of harm to others that was foreseeable"; trial court erred in ruling that as matter of law employer owed no duty to motorist injured in collision with employee); see also [_Bussard v. Minimed, Inc., 129 Cal. Rptr.2d 675 (Cal.App.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:47S1-74T0-0039-43GS-00000-00&context=1530671) (accident caused by employee while driving home was within scope of employment because foreseeable risk was created within the scope of employment; superior sent employee home after employee became dizzy due to overexposure to pesticide at work). But see [_Pilgrim v. Fortune Drilling Co., 653 F.2d 982 (5th Cir.1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-05M0-0039-W4P1-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Texas law) (employer owed no duty to third parties to prevent employees from driving home when exhausted; employees outside scope of employment in returning home from place of work).

On an employer's liability in connection with intoxicated employees, see generally [_David M. Holliday, Intoxicating Liquors: Employer's Liability for Furnishing or Permitting Liquor on Social Occasion, 51 A.L.R. 4th 1048_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:5S4C-JX10-00CR-103X-00000-00&context=1530671); see also [_Chastain v. Litton Sys., Inc., 694 F.2d 957 (4th Cir.1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-19T0-003B-G0S8-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying North Carolina law; employee became intoxicated at all-day Christmas party sponsored by employer on work day and on its premises; facts would support jury determination that employee's attendance at party was within scope of employment, making it foreseeable that he would attempt to drive home while still intoxicated); [_Wadley v. Aspillaga, 163 F.Supp.2d 1, 8 (D.D.C.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:441F-PM80-0038-Y1JX-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_82 Fed. Appx. 227 (D.C.Cir. 2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4BYH-5FN0-0038-X207-00000-00&context=1530671) (same; if employee was within scope of employment in attending holiday party, employer "should have been able to control the amount of alcohol that he consumed"); [_Harris v. Trojan Fireworks Co., 174 Cal.Rptr. 452 (Cal.App.1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S11-SGT0-003C-R2XD-00000-00&context=1530671) (employer subject to liability for injuries from collision caused by inebriated employee driving home from company Christmas party; collision occurred within course of employment when party held during work hours and employee was paid to attend it); [_Otis Eng'g Corp. v. Clark, 668 S.W.2d 307 (Tex. 1983)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-WF10-003C-22V8-00000-00&context=1530671) (summary judgment improperly granted for employer when employee's supervisor knew that employee was inebriated, consistent with his known history of drinking on the job; supervisor suggested that employee go home and accepted employee's assurance that he could make it home driving himself); but see [_Overbaugh v. McCutcheon, 396 S.E.2d 153 (W.Va.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXR-1H50-003G-H2FS-00000-00&context=1530671) (employer who furnished alcohol to employee at employer-sponsored party not subject to liability to estate of motorist killed in collision with employee; although employer knew that employee had overindulged, employer instructed employee to stay put until employer or employer's son could drive him home). Cases applying a more restrictive standard for employer liability in connection with off-premises drinking by employees include [_Healthdyne, Inc. v. Odom, 325 S.E.2d 847, 849 (Ga.App.1984)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-6T90-003F-J3PF-00000-00&context=1530671) (employer not subject to liability for injuries from collision caused by employee driving home in inebriated state from meeting with fellow employee and prospective vendor because employee not within scope of employment at time of collision); [_Sayles v. Piccadilly Cafeterias, Inc., 410 S.E.2d 632, 634 (Va.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-VRV0-003D-50VF-00000-00&context=1530671) (inebriated employee not within scope of employment when driving from employer's Christmas party to another party; attendance at employer's party voluntary).

_f. Definition of employee._ Statutes may explicitly adopt the common-law test articulated in this section to determine whether a person should be characterized as an employee for purposes of the statute. See, e.g., [_Internal Revenue Code § 3121(d)(2)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:67F7-7313-CGX8-02K0-00000-00&context=1530671) (defining "employee" as "any individual who, under the usual common law rules applicable in determining the employer-employee relationship, has the status of an employee").

Many cases apply the specific criteria mentioned in the Comment, which are drawn from Restatement Second, Agency § 228. See generally 27 Am. Jur. 2d Employment Relationship § 2 (1996). Courts may draw on these criteria to determine whether a person is an employee for purposes of a statute that itself does not define the term or refer explicitly to the common-law definition. See, e.g., [_Nationwide Mut. Ins. Co. v. Darden, 503 U.S. 318, 323 (1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S65-KJ30-003B-R4CB-00000-00&context=1530671) (for purposes of ERISA, "we adopt a common-law test for determining who qualifies as an 'employee'").

For the point that a principal's exercise of control over the details of work may create vicarious liability, see [_Royal Ins. Co. v. Schubert Marine Sales & Servs., Inc., 2003 WL 21665006 (E.D.La.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4949-NP10-0038-Y0BW-00000-00&context=1530671) (genuine issue of material fact whether security guard was employee of shipyard operator when trier of fact could find operator had exercised control over details of guard's job); [_Fisher v. Townsends, Inc., 695 A.2d 53, 61 (Del. 1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRT-6NR0-003C-K029-00000-00&context=1530671) (genuine issue of material fact present whether weigh master of chicken-catching crew was servant and/or agent of chicken-processing business or an independent contractor; "\[i\]f the owner's or contractee's control or direction dominates the manner or means of the work performed, the non-agent status of the independent contractor can be destroyed and the independent contractor becomes an agent capable of rendering the principal vicariously liable for the acts of the independent contractor").

On respondeat superior and volunteers, see [_Celaya v. Hall, 85 P.3d 239 (N.M.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4BW0-9TN0-0039-402S-00000-00&context=1530671) (genuine issue of material fact whether volunteer chaplain for sheriff's department was acting within scope of his duties at time of accident in Wal-Mart parking lot; chaplain, on call 24 hours per day, was driving car furnished by department and was running personal errand at the time but was unable to remember activity immediately prior to errand); [_Doe v. Boys Clubs of Greater Dallas, Inc., 868 S.W.2d 942 (Tex.App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-1290-003C-23WS-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_907 S.W.2d 472 (Tex.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W520-003C-2094-00000-00&context=1530671) (volunteer acted outside scope of employment in sexually molesting children when volunteer worked at club due to community-service requirement of court-imposed sentence for driving under the influence; volunteer has status of an employee for purposes of respondeat superior when employer has right to direct volunteer's duties, has an interest in the work to be accomplished, accepts direct or indirect benefit from the work, and has right to fire or replace volunteer; fact that volunteer compelled to serve does not change result). See also [_Wilson v. St. Louis Area Council, Boy Scouts of America, 845 S.W.2d 568 (Mo.App.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-7380-003F-C0SK-00000-00&context=1530671) (upholding summary judgment for defendant; scouting organization not subject to liability on respondeat superior or apparent-authority theories for negligence of volunteer scout master on camping trip when organization did not control, have right to control, or purport to control scout master's activities); [_Mayfield v. Boy Scouts of Am., 643 N.E.2d 565 (Ohio App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-YD70-003C-82RM-00000-00&context=1530671) (reversing grant of summary judgment for defendant; issue of material fact whether scouting organization retained degree of discretion and control over activities of volunteer scout masters).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 7.08_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8Y0-00YG-400N-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 7- Torts--Liability of Agent and Principal_ > _Topic 2- Principal's Liability_**

**§ 7.08 Agent Acts with Apparent Authority**

**A principal is subject to vicarious liability for a tort committed by an agent in dealing or communicating with a third party on or purportedly on behalf of the principal when actions taken by the agent with apparent authority constitute the tort or enable the agent to conceal its commission.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ The rule stated in this section applies to (1) agents, whether or not they are employees as defined in § 7.07(3); and (2) agents who are employees as defined in § 7.07(3) whose tortious conduct is not within the scope of employment under § 7.07(2). The torts to which this section applies are those in which an agent appears to deal or communicate on behalf of a principal and the agent's appearance of authority enables the agent to commit a tort or conceal its commission. Such torts include fraudulent and negligent misrepresentations, defamation, tortious institution of legal proceedings, and conversion of property obtained by an agent purportedly at the principal's direction.
