Apparent authority stems from a manifestation made by the principal. See § 2.03. Comment _b_ discusses the necessary connection between an agent's tortious conduct and apparent authority. Comment _b_ also contrasts the operation of this rule with that stated in § 7.07, which subjects a principal to liability for torts committed by its employees acting within the scope of employment, and with §§ 7.04 and 7.05, which state when a principal is subject to tort liability as a consequence of conduct of an agent or other actor. Comment _c_ examines several specifics of this rule's operation in the context of tortious misrepresentations made by an agent. Comment _d_ discusses the application of this rule to defamatory statements published by an agent.

Apparent authority is defined in § 2.03. Section 3.03 states how a principal may create apparent authority.

If an agent commits a tort while acting within the scope of actual authority, the principal is subject to direct liability. See § 7.04.

This section is inapplicable to an employer's vicarious liability for an employee's tortious conduct against a fellow employee, a topic being considered by Restatement Third, Employment Law, in preparation as Restatement Third, Agency, was completed.

_b. Connection between apparent authority and agent's tortious conduct_ An agent acts with apparent authority with regard to a third party when the third party reasonably believes that the agent or other actor has authority to act on behalf of the principal and that belief is traceable to manifestations made by the principal. See § 2.03. Manifestation is defined in § 1.03. When an agent acts with apparent authority, the agent's motivation is immaterial to the legal consequences that the agent's action carries for the principal. Likewise, the fact that an agent's conduct is not in fact beneficial to the principal does not shield the principal from legal consequences. This is because apparent authority holds a principal accountable for the results of third-party beliefs about an actor's authority to act as an agent when the belief is reasonable and is traceable to a manifestation made by the principal. See § 2.03, Comment _c._ So to charge a principal is fair because it is the principal's manifestation that clothes the agent with the appearance of authority to act on the principal's behalf and that induces the third party reasonably to believe that the agent acts with actual authority. See id.

Apparent-authority doctrine thus focuses on the reasonable expectations of third parties with whom an agent deals. This focus is inapposite to many instances of tort liability. However, apparent-authority doctrine is operative in explaining a principal's vicarious liability when a third party's reasonable belief in an agent's authority to speak or deal on behalf of a principal stems from a manifestation made by the principal and it is through statements or dealings that the agent acts tortiously.

A principal's liability under the rule stated in this section does not depend on whether the principal benefits from the agent's tortious conduct.

**Illustrations:**

1\. P Numismatics Company urges its customers to seek investment advice from its retail salespeople, including A. T, who wishes to invest in gold coins, seeks A's advice at an office of P Numismatics Company. A encourages T to purchase a particular set of gold coins, falsely representing material facts relevant to their value. T, reasonably relying on A's representations, purchases the set of coins. P is subject to liability to T. A is also subject to liability to T. See § 7.01.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that A persuades T to pay cash for the coins and to leave the coins with A so that they may be safely stored by P Numismatics Company. A then absconds with both the coins and the cash paid by T. Same results.

A principal is also subject to liability under the rule stated in this section when an agent's tortious conduct leads to physical injury.

**Illustration:**

3\. O, who owns an office building, retains P, a construction firm, to renovate the building. T, a prospective tenant, visits the building. T asks P's site manager, A, whether it will be safe for T to inspect a particular group of offices. A tells T to ask G, a security guard in the building, saying "G's our point person for safety." G tells T that the offices in question are safe to visit although G does not know whether this is so. Unbeknownst to T, G is an employee of Guards, Inc., not P. P and Guards, Inc., have instructed A and G never to direct prospective tenants within the building, but T neither knows nor has reason to know this. T reasonably believes that G, to whom A directs T, has authority to answer questions from prospective tenants. T is injured when T falls through the weakened flooring in one of the offices. P is subject to liability to T. G is also subject to liability. See § 7.01. Guards, Inc., is subject to liability as stated in § 7.07.

A principal is not subject to liability under the rule stated in this section unless there is a close link between an agent's tortious conduct and the agent's apparent authority. Thus, a principal is not subject to liability when actions that an agent takes with apparent authority, although connected in some way to the agent's tortious conduct, do not themselves constitute the tort or enable the agent to mask its commission.

**Illustrations:**

4\. P Insurance Company appoints A as an agent with authority to sell policies and collect premiums on its behalf. A approaches T, a prospective customer and, after T agrees to buy insurance policies through A, furnishes T with policy applications provided by P Insurance Company. Complying with a direction in the policy applications, T gives A checks in the amount of premiums due on policies that A sells to T. A does not forward the premiums to P Insurance Company, instead using the funds for A's own purposes. As a consequence, P Insurance Company does not issue the policies to T. T sustains a loss covered by policy language. P Insurance Company is subject to liability to T. A acted with apparent authority in making statements about insurance policies to be issued by P Insurance Company and in collecting premiums. Separately, A's conduct in accepting T's premium payments is attributed to P Insurance Co.

5\. P Insurance Company appoints A as an agent with authority to sell policies and collect premiums on its behalf. A writes an insurance policy on the life of T without T's knowledge or consent, forging T's signature on the application and naming B as the policy's beneficiary. A then arranges to have T murdered by B, who obtains and cashes the check issued by P Insurance Company when it receives notice of T's death. P Insurance Company is not subject to liability to T's estate. Neither A nor B acted with apparent authority in committing their wrongful acts against T.

6\. A, an agent of P Insurance Company, visits T's premises to inventory T's property for purposes of renewing a policy of property insurance that P Insurance Company has issued to T. Before entering T's premises, A notices a handsome sports car parked outside. Upon inquiry from A, T tells A that the car belongs to T and that it is insured by S Insurance Company. After completing the inventory, A returns to T's premises and steals T's sports car. P Insurance Company is not subject to liability to T. A acted with actual and apparent authority only in inventorying T's property insured by P Insurance Company. A does not act with apparent authority in stealing T's car. Alternatively, P Insurance Company may be subject to liability to T as stated in § 7.05 if it failed to use reasonable care in hiring, supervising, or otherwise controlling A.

In Illustrations 5 and 6, it is true that the agent's activities as an agent bear some relationship to the agent's wrongful conduct against the third party. In Illustration 5, the vehicle through which A and A's confederate wrongfully sought to benefit was a forged insurance policy on T's life, which would, moreover, be void in some jurisdictions because A had no insurable interest in Ts life. A's position as an insurance agent no doubt familiarized A with the insurance-application process. In Illustration 6, A learned about the property subsequently stolen by A in the course of performing authorized work for A's principal. In neither Illustration, however, does the wrongful act itself take place under the cloak of the manifestation that the principal has made to the third party concerning the agent's authority. In contrast, in Illustration 4, it is the principal's manifestation that A has authority to collect premiums on its behalf, made in policy applications drafted by the principal, that induces T to give premium checks to A. Additionally, in Illustrations 5 and 6, A was not uniquely situated to commit the tort against the third party. The insurance agent in Illustration 5, that is, did not occupy a position making the third-party victim uniquely vulnerable to the agent as a person who might forge a signature on an application for a life-insurance policy. In Illustration 6, A's customer, T, was not uniquely vulnerable to A as a potential thief of T's sports car. In Illustration 4, in contrast, the principal's manifestation to A's customer, T, makes T especially vulnerable to A. In theory, another person could have stolen premium checks written by T, for example by taking them from the mail if T sent them directly to the insurer. However, A's principal directed T to give premium checks to A, thereby manifesting its willingness to be bound by A's actions in connection with the checks once T entrusts them to A.

If a third party knows or has reason to know that an agent lacks authority to take a particular action, the agent does not act with apparent authority. See § 2.03, Comment _d,_ for further discussion. If an agent proposes a transaction to a third party that the third party knows or has reason to know is irregular or otherwise not within the ordinary course of business that the agent conducts for the principal, it may not be reasonable for the third party to believe that the agent is acting within the scope of the agent's actual authority, in the absence of some manifestation from the principal that encompasses such a transaction. See § 3.03, Comment _b._

**Illustrations:**

7\. A sells an annuity issued by P Insurance Company to T. Later, A suggests that T surrender this annuity and authorize A to invest the proceeds on T's behalf in an annuity to be issued by S Insurance Company, a competitor of P Insurance Company. T agrees and surrenders the annuity to P Insurance Company, directing P Insurance Company to make the check for the proceeds payable to A. P Insurance Company does so. A appropriates the funds to A's own use. P Insurance Company is not subject to liability to T. P Insurance Company made no manifestation to T on the basis of which T could reasonably believe P Insurance Company authorized A, as its agent, to reinvest T's money with a competitor of P Insurance Company. A is subject to liability to T. A converted Ts money. Also, T, by authorizing A at A's request to take control of Ts money and to invest it on Ts behalf, created an agency relationship with A. A breached A's duties to T as stated in §§ 8.01, 8.05(1), 8.09(1), and 8.12.

8\. A is an account executive with P Securities, Inc. A tells T, who is not a customer of P Securities, Inc., that if T sends money to A personally, A will place it in A's personal employee account at P Securities, Inc., and will then invest it in a special municipal-bond fund. A tells T that this investment strategy will yield a significantly increased return for T because the bonds in the fund will be purchased at P Securities, Inc.'s cost and because P Securities applies a lower commission charge to its employees' own transactions. P Securities, Inc., prohibits the use of employee accounts in this manner. T sends money to A at A's home address, as A directs. After depositing T's money in A's employee account, A then invests T's money in risky options, not in a municipal-bond fund. Most of T's money is lost when A's options-trading strategy is unsuccessful. P Securities, Inc., is not subject to liability to T under the rule stated in this section. It was not reasonable for T to believe that P Securities, Inc., authorized A to use A's employee account for T's benefit. Moreover, A's transactions with T were not in the ordinary course of P Securities, Inc.'s business.

A principal's liability may, of course, be based on rules other than or in addition to the rule stated in this section. In Illustration 8, P Securities, Inc., has no relationship with T that would subject it to liability to T under the rule stated in § 7.05. In contrast, had T been a customer of P Securities, Inc., it would owe T a duty of care in connection with its conduct in selecting and otherwise dealing with its employees, agents, and other actors through whom it conducts its activities. Additionally, statutory bases of liability may be available under some circumstances. For example, P Securities, Inc., may be subject to liability to T if P Securities is characterized as a controlling person over A's operations on the basis that P Securities possessed power or ability to control A's transactions. See Securities Exchange Act § 20(a), [_15 U.S.C. § 78t_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8S9D-W4W2-8T6X-701Y-00000-00&context=1530671).

A principal is not subject to liability on the basis that an agent or other actor took action with apparent authority unless the third party reasonably believed that the agent acted with actual authority and that belief is traceable to a manifestation made by the principal. See § 2.01, which defines actual authority. Thus, the rule stated in this section is inapplicable when a third party does not reasonably believe that an agent's action has been authorized by the principal.

When an agent's conduct constitutes a tort such as assault or arson, only in unusual circumstances would a third party harmed by the tort believe that the agent acted with actual authority in committing it. It is also unlikely that such tortious conduct would occur while an agent acts with actual authority. Indeed, if an agent acts with actual authority in committing a tort, the principal is subject to direct liability as stated in § 7.04. If a tort is committed by an employee acting within the scope of employment, the employer is subject to liability as stated in § 7.07. The determination of whether a tort is committed within an employee's scope of employment does not turn on whether the victim reasonably believed that the employer wished the employee so to act. See § 7.07(2), which states when an employee's tortious conduct is within the scope of employment.

An actor may continue to possess apparent authority although the principal has terminated the actor's actual authority or the agency relationship between them. This is so because a third party may reasonably believe that the actor continues to act as an agent and within the scope of actual authority on the basis of manifestations previously made by the principal. Such a manifestation, once made, remains operative until the third party has notice of circumstances that make it unreasonable to believe that the actor continues to have actual authority. See § 3.11, Comment _c._

Many cases treat the rule stated in this section as a foundational rule of common-law agency that is relevant to resolving questions that arise under statutes. Many statutes, such as the Sherman Act, [_15 U.S.C. §§ 1_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8S9D-W4V2-8T6X-74X3-00000-00&context=1530671)\-[_12_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8S9D-W4V2-8T6X-74XH-00000-00&context=1530671), define the elements requisite for civil liability under the statute but do not explicitly address the bases upon which one person's conduct may create liability for another person. Some statutes explicitly use language that invites reference to common-law doctrines of apparent authority. For example, under Uniform Partnership Act § 14(a) (1914), a partnership must make good the loss "\[w\]here one partner, acting within the scope of his apparent authority receives money or property of a third person and misapplies it. . . ."

This rule differs from the counterpart material in Restatement Second, Agency, in two respects. First, much of the treatment in Restatement Second, Agency, is structured on a tort-specific basis, see Restatement Second, Agency §§ 247-249 ("Specific Torts of Servants") and §§ 253-264 ("Agents' Torts--Liability Not Dependent Upon Relation of Master and Servant"). Second, Restatement Second, Agency § 219(2)(d) states that an employer is subject to liability for an employee's tort when the employee acted outside the scope of employment when "the servant purported to act or to speak on behalf of the principal and there was reliance upon apparent authority. . . ." Section 219(2)(d) concludes with a further general basis for an employer's vicarious liability, which is whether an employee "was aided in accomplishing the tort by the existence of the agency relation." This Restatement does not include "aided in accomplishing" as a distinct basis for an employer's (or principal's) vicarious liability. The purposes likely intended to be met by the "aided in accomplishing" basis are satisfied by a more fully elaborated treatment of apparent authority and by the duty of reasonable care that a principal owes to third parties with whom it interacts through employees and other agents. See § 7.05.

_c. Tortious misrepresentations_

_(1). In general._ An agent's misrepresentation is tortious if it is made fraudulently or negligently. An agent's misrepresentation subjects neither agent nor principal to liability unless the requisite elements of liability are present.

_(2). Fraudulent misrepresentations._ A misrepresentation about a matter is made fraudulently when its maker knows or believes that the matter is not as the maker represents it to be, lacks confidence in the accuracy of the express or implied representation, or knows that the representation lacks the basis that the maker states or implies. Restatement Second, Torts § 526. A person who fraudulently makes a misrepresentation of fact, opinion, intention or law to induce another person to act or refrain from acting in reliance upon the misrepresentation is subject to liability for pecuniary loss caused to the other person by that person's justifiable reliance on the misrepresentation. Id. § 525. To be actionable, a misrepresentation of fact must concern a fact that is then important to the course of conduct to be chosen by the person to whom the misrepresentation is made. Id. § 525, Comment _e._ An omission may make a statement misleading and subject the maker to liability. See id. § 551(2)(b) and (c).

An agent acts with apparent authority in making a fraudulent misrepresentation to a third party when the third party reasonably believes that the agent has authority to make the particular representation on behalf of the principal. An agent may make a misrepresentation by an affirmative statement, which may be made expressly or impliedly, or by conduct that amounts to an assertion that is untruthful.

**Illustrations:**

9\. A, the chief executive officer of P Corporation, has actual authority to raise capital for P Corporation from banks and other lenders without specific approval from P Corporation's board of directors for amounts up to \$ 500,000. Over several years, A persuades T to give A \$ 200,000 for investment in P Corporation. A provides T with promissory notes of P Corporation in exchange for each payment that T makes to A. T meets with A on P Corporation's premises, and communications concerning T's investment are made on stationery bearing P Corporation's letterhead. A, however, uses T's money for A's own purposes. P Corporation is subject to liability to T. A acted with apparent authority in dealing with T, including A's misrepresentation that T's payments would be invested in P Corporation.

10\. P Pre-owned Automobiles, Inc., employs A as a salesperson. Wishing to effect a sale of a car to T, A turns back its odometer from 60,000 miles to 18,000 miles. T asks A whether the odometer reading is correct, given the car's model year. A confirms the correctness of the reading, stressing that the car's prior owner drove it infrequently. P Pre-owned Automobiles, Inc., is subject to liability to T. A had apparent authority to answer T's questions about cars in inventory. A knew that A's answer to T's inquiry was false. A answered falsely to induce T to buy the car. Additionally, by altering the car's odometer reading, A made a misrepresentation to T. A is also subject to liability to T. See § 7.01.

11\. Same facts as Illustration 10, except that T does not ask A about the odometer's accuracy. Same results. By altering the car's odometer, A misrepresented its mileage to T.

An agent may also make a misrepresentation through concealment to defeat a third party's discovery of the truth and, in some circumstances, through silence or a failure to disclose information.

**Illustrations:**

12\. P Pre-owned Automobiles, Inc., employs A as a salesperson. A wishes to effect a sale to T of a car that has substantial rust on its body. A applies paint over the rust to conceal it. P Pre-owned Automobiles, Inc., is subject to liability to T. By painting over the rust, A took affirmative steps to conceal it. A is also subject to liability to T. See § 7.01.

13\. P, who owns a large commercial building, retains A as agent to sell it. A has the building inspected by S, an environmental expert. S reports to A that the building contains substantial amounts of asbestos but that it is evident only upon inspection by an expert such as S. A contracts on P's behalf to sell the building to T. A does not provide S's report to T nor does A tell T about the asbestos. Under applicable law, P has an obligation to inform a purchaser of the existence of a nonobvious defect such as the presence of the asbestos. P is subject to liability to T. A's knowledge of the defect and of the contents of S's report is imputed to P. See § 5.03. A is also subject to liability to T. See § 7.01.

A principal's knowledge, when coupled with action taken by an agent, may subject the principal to liability to a third party although the agent is innocent of fault. For example, in Illustration 13, if A does not know or have reason to know about the asbestos in the building, but P knows that asbestos is present, P is subject to liability to T. Applicable law requires P to disclose the presence of the asbestos to T, and the fact that P deals with T through an agent who is uninformed about its presence does not furnish a defense to P. Likewise, in Illustration 12, if the principal is an individual who paints over the car's rust, the principal is subject to liability to T although it is A who deals directly with T. In Illustration 10, if the principal is an individual who turns back the car's odometer, the principal is subject to liability to T even though it is A who assures T of the accuracy of its reading. This is so even if applicable law does not affirmatively require disclosure that a car's odometer has been reset. The principal has placed A in a position in which it is foreseeable to the principal that A will represent that the odometer is accurate to a prospective purchaser like T. The principal is subject to liability even if A is negligent in making such a representation to T if it is foreseeable to P that A will make the representation.

A principal is not subject to liability when an agent defrauds a third party unless the agent acts with actual or apparent authority to engage in the conduct that perpetrates or conceals the fraud. The principal's liability under this rule requires a causal connection more substantial than a peripheral or happenstance connection between the fraud and the agent's use of apparent authority.

An agent does not act with apparent authority in connection with a misrepresentation made to a third party when the third party knows or has reason to know that the agent does not have authority to make the representation on behalf of the principal. If a third party knows or has reason to know that the person who makes a representation to the third party is not an agent of the person on whose behalf the representation is purportedly made, the person who makes the representation does not act with apparent authority. Likewise, an agent does not act with apparent authority when the person with whom the agent deals knows that the agent is dealing solely on the agent's own behalf and not on the principal's behalf.

When a principal is undisclosed, an agent does not act with apparent authority. See § 2.03, Comment _f._ However, an undisclosed principal may be subject to liability for an agent's fraud when the agent acts in the principal's affairs or deals with the principal's goods or property, and by so doing is enabled to defraud a third party.

**Illustrations:**

14\. P, who owns a horse, Silver Blaze, authorizes A to sell the horse in A's name because P has a poor reputation in equine trading circles. A intentionally misrepresents Silver Blaze's lineage to T, who purchases the horse in reasonable reliance on A's misrepresentation. P does not direct A to make this misrepresentation and does not have notice of it. P is subject to liability to T, as is A. For A's liability, see § 7.01.

15\. Same facts as Illustration 14, except that A absconds with both Silver Blaze and the sale proceeds. Same results. A acted on P's account in selling Silver Blaze and in making representations about the horse.

_(3). Negligent misrepresentations._ An agent who fails to exercise reasonable care in making a representation that the agent has apparent authority to make on behalf of the principal subjects the principal to liability to a third party only when the principal has a pecuniary interest in supplying such information and the principal owes the third party a duty of care. See Restatement Second, Torts § 552. It is beyond the scope of the law of agency whether, under particular circumstances, a principal owes a duty of care to a third party.

Liability for negligence in making a representation is narrower than liability when a misrepresentation is made fraudulently. The difference stems from the difference between "the obligations of honesty and of care, and . . . the significance of this difference to the reasonable expectations of the users of information that is supplied in connection with commercial transactions." Id., Comment a. Honesty requires less of a person who makes a representation than does a duty to be careful, but honesty is also a standard that is unequivocal and reasonably understood to be applicable regardless of the nature of a transaction or the situation of the party who relies on the representation. In contrast, a duty to use reasonable care "implies an undertaking to observe a relative standard, which may be defined only in terms of the use to which . . . information will be put, weighed against the magnitude and probability of loss that might attend that use if the information proves to be incorrect." Id. It is not reasonable for a user of information to assume that any person who makes a representation has assumed a duty to use care, in particular that such a duty has been assumed without knowledge of the use to be made of information. But it is reasonable to assume that a person who makes a representation has undertaken to speak honestly.

_(4). Exculpatory clauses._ The effect of contractual language on a principal's liability for tortious misrepresentations made by an agent is determined by contract-law principles, as well as agency-law doctrines. A contractual term that purports to exempt or exculpate a party to the contract from tort liability for harm that the party intentionally or recklessly causes is unenforceable on grounds of public policy. Restatement Second, Contracts § 195(1). Ordinarily, a party may, through a contractual term, provide for an exemption from liability for harm caused by negligence. Id., Comment a. Such a provision is unenforceable if it purports to exempt an employer from liability to an employee for an injury in the course of employment; if it purports to exempt a party charged with a duty of public service, such as a public utility, from liability to compensate a person to whom the duty is owed for breach of the duty; or if the plaintiff is a member of a class protected against the class to which the defendant belongs. Id. § 195(2).

When a principal deals with third parties through agents, the principal may be aware that its ability to select and monitor its agents will never furnish flawless protection against the risk that, acting with apparent authority, an agent may subject the principal to liability to a third party through a tortious misrepresentation. A principal may protect itself against this risk by requiring its agents to use documentation for transactions, prepared by the principal, that provides effective warning to third parties with whom the agent deals of limitations on the agent's authority to bind the principal through representations. Such a provision, if effective, precludes a third party's ability to subject the principal to liability on the basis that the agent acted with apparent authority in making a misrepresentation. In the third party's dealings with the agent, it is not reasonable for the third party to believe that the agent acted with actual authority in making such representations. It is a question of fact whether a provision in a contract is effective to provide notice to a third party of limitations on an agent's authority to make a particular type of representation. The efficacy of the provision may also be a matter of contract interpretation.

However, a contractual provision is not operative to protect a principal from liability when an agent tortiously makes misrepresentations to a third party with whom the agent deals when the principal knows or has reason to know that the agent is likely to make such a misrepresentation. The principal may know or have reason to know of the likelihood that the agent will make misrepresentations because the principal knows the agent has made them in the past. Additionally, a contractual provision may not be operative to protect a principal from liability to a third party to whom an agent has tortiously made misrepresentations when the principal ratifies the agent's conduct. See § 4.01(2), which states how a person ratifies another's act. If a principal knows of the agent's misrepresentation and accepts the benefits of the transaction with the third party that the agent has induced through a misrepresentation, the principal has ratified the agent's misrepresentation and is subject to liability to the third party.

A tortious misrepresentation does not subject its maker to liability when it does not cause the person to whom it was made to engage in a transaction. Restatement Second, Torts § 547(1). Thus, a principal is not subject to liability to a third party on the basis of tortious misrepresentations made to the third party by an agent when the third party does not rely on the agent's misrepresentations. If, instead, the third party relies on the third party's own independent investigation in deciding whether to engage in a transaction, an agent's tortious misrepresentation subjects neither agent nor principal to liability to the third party. However, a third party's investigation is not operative to relieve the principal of liability if the principal prevents the investigation from being effective, or if the agent does so acting with actual or apparent authority, whether by making false statements to the third party or otherwise.

_d. Defamation._ An agent who acts with apparent authority in making a defamatory statement may subject the principal to liability. A statement is defamatory "if it tends so to harm the reputation of another as to lower him in the estimation of the community or to deter third persons from associating or dealing with him." Restatement Second, Torts § 559. Liability for defamation requires: (1) a false statement concerning another person; (2) unprivileged publication of the statement to a third party; (3) at least negligent fault on the part of the person who publishes the statement; and (4) the existence of either special harm caused by the publication or a publication that is actionable regardless of whether it results in special harm. Id. § 558.

When an agent acts without actual authority in making a defamatory statement, or an employee makes a defamatory statement outside the scope of employment, the principal is subject to liability if the agent or employee acted with apparent authority in making the statement. The effectiveness of the defamation--its credibility to those to whom it is published and its propensity to harm the person defamed--is often tied to the reputation and thus the personality of its publisher. Thus, if an agent speaks with apparent authority on behalf of the principal and defames a third party, the principal is subject to liability.

**Illustrations:**

16\. P Department Store, Inc., employs A as a security guard in a store. A accuses T of shoplifting in the presence of law-enforcement officials. A's accusation is false. A made the accusation without using reasonable care under the circumstances. Under applicable law, A was negligent in making the accusation and A was not privileged to make it. P Department Store is subject to liability. A's statement to law-enforcement officials is a false statement that accuses T of committing a crime.

17\. Same facts as Illustration 16, except that A makes the accusation because A has a private feud with T. Same result. A acted with apparent authority in making the accusation.

The relevant standard of fault is higher when the subject of a defamation is a public official or public figure. In [_New York Times Co. v. Sullivan, 376 U.S. 254 (1964)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-GWF0-003B-S50C-00000-00&context=1530671), the United States Supreme Court placed constitutional limits on state law. The Court held that the First Amendment does not permit a public official defamed in connection with the official's conduct, fitness, or role to maintain an action for defamation unless the official proves that the defendant acted with "actual malice," that is that the defendant either knew the defamatory statement to be false or acted in reckless disregard of its truth or falsity. Subsequent cases extend the actual-malice requirement to candidates for public office and to public figures. The Court's opinions do not explicitly address how the actual-malice requirement applies to a defendant when an agent of the defendant published the defamatory statement. In most subsequent cases, a defendant is subject to liability on the basis that the defamation was published by an employee who met the requisite standard of fault and who acted within the scope of employment.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency, and codifications._ This section corresponds to the coverage of several sections in Restatement Second, Agency, including §§ 219(2)(d), 251(b), 253, 254, 257, 258, 259, 260, 261, 262, and 263.

Under Ala. Code § 8-2-6 (2002 & Supp. 2004), "\[a\] principal is bound by acts of his agent under a merely ostensible authority to those persons only who have in good faith and without want of ordinary care incurred a liability or parted with value on the faith thereof." Accord, Cal. Civ. Code § 2334 (1993 & Supp. 2005); Mont. Code Ann. § 28-10-606 (2004); N.D. Cent. Code § 3-03-03 (1987 & Supp. 2003); S.D. Codified Laws § 59-6-3 (2004 & Supp. 2005). Under Ala. Code § 8-2-7, a principal is "responsible to third persons for the negligence of his agent in the transaction of the business of the agency, including wrongful acts committed by such agent in and as a part of the transaction of such business, and for his willful omission to fulfill the obligations of the principal." Accord, Cal. Civ. Code § 2338; Mont. Code Ann. § 28-10-602(1); S.D. Codified Laws § 59-6-9. Under N.D. Cent. Code § 3-03-09(2), a principal is not responsible for "injuries or death to passengers and other persons or damage to property resulting from . . . operation or use of a motor vehicle, not owned, leased or contracted for by the principal in a ridesharing arrangement," defined in § 8-02-07. Ga. Code § 10-6-56 (2000 & Supp. 2005) provides that a "principal shall be bound by all representations made by his agent in the business of the agency and also by his wilful concealment of material facts, although they are unknown to the principal and known only by the agent." Under § 10-6-60, "\[t\]he principal shall be bound for the care, diligence, and fidelity of his agent in his business, and hence he shall be bound for the neglect and fraud of his agent in the transaction of such business."

_b. Connection between apparent authority and agent's tortious conduct._ On the connection between a principal's contractual liability and tort-law liability when an agent has acted with apparent authority, Professor Mechem wrote that "if the principal causes or permits another to appear to be his agent or to appear to be authorized to make a certain contract or to perform a certain act, when he is not, responsibility for misrepresentations and deceit as part of the contract or act will attach on the same grounds as responsibility for the contract or act itself." 2 Floyd R. Mechem, A Treatise on the Law of Agency § 1989, at 1556 (2d ed. 1914).

On the connection between a principal's vicarious liability and the principal's right of control, see, e.g., [_Bat-zel v. Smith, 333 F.3d 1018, 1035 (9th Cir.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48XC-TBP0-0038-X2MM-00000-00&context=1530671), cert. denied, _541 U.S. 1085 (2004)_ (sponsor of website did not have agency relationship with operator of site and related listserv, who distributed message defamatory of plaintiff; "\[i\]n order for \[sponsor\] to be held vicariously liable for the torts of \[website operator\] on a theory of agency, \[sponsor\] must have had the ability to control \[website operator's\] activities"; sponsorship agreement gave sponsor no right to control what site manager published and did not constitute assent to control by site manager but disclaimed it).

Illustration 3 is derived from Daniel S. Kleinberger, Agency, Partnerships, and LLCs 94 (2d ed. 2002).

For the point that liability under the rule stated in this section is distinct from an employer's liability for employee torts committed within the scope of employment, see, e.g., [_Kan-sallis Fin. Co. v. Fern, 659 N.E.2d 731, 735 (Mass.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2X60-003C-V0YW-00000-00&context=1530671) ("they are not equivalent concepts. A servant or agent may sometimes act within the scope of his master's employment and yet lack apparent authority. The clearest instances are in accident cases where the victim neither knew nor cared what the wrongdoer's relation to his employer might be. . . . The scope of employment test asks the question: is this the kind of thing that in a general way employees of this kind do in employment of this _kind._ It does not ask a different question: whether a reasonable person in the victim's circumstances _in the particular case_ would have taken the agent to be acting with the principal's authority."); [_Parlato v. Equitable Life Assurance Soc., 749 N.Y.S.2d 216, 221 (App.Div.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4713-4CR0-0039-42G7-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[i\]n considering a principal's potential liability for the tortious acts of its agent, it is important to distinguish the doctrine of apparent authority . . . from the distinct doctrine of respondeat superior. To succeed on a respondeat superior theory, a plaintiff need not show either apparent authority or reasonable reliance thereon, but must show that the agent was acting 'in furtherance of the \[principal's\] business and within the scope of employment'. . . . By contrast, where, as here, the asserted basis for the principal's liability is apparent authority, there is no requirement that the tortious act be committed in furtherance of the principal's business. The plaintiff suing on the basis of apparent authority is required, however, to prove that the principal created an appearance of authority on which the plaintiff reasonably relied, thereby enabling the agent to successfully perpetrate the tort. . . .") (citations omitted). See also [_Ackerman v. Northwestern Mut. Life Ins. Co., 172 F.3d 467, 471 (7th Cir.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3W4G-X4H0-0038-X2H8-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[w\]hen an agent who is authorized to make a contract on his principal's behalf (and the principal needn't be the agent's employer and was not here) uses fraud to induce the contract, the principal is liable even if the agent is acting solely to feather his own nest"; if agents act "at least in part to enrich the company as well as themselves. . . . \[t\]hat would make it a standard respondeat superior case.").

For the point that it is not a defense to liability that a principal derived no benefit from an agent's actions and that the agent acted entirely for the agent's own purposes, see, e.g., _Moecker v. Honeywell Int'l, Inc., 144 F.Supp.2d 1291, 1312 (M.D.Fla.2001)_ (not a defense to principal's vicarious liability for agents' criminal violations of antitrust law that agents may have acted solely for own purposes).

Illustration 4 is based on [_E.A. Prince & Son, Inc. v. Selective Ins. Co., 818 F.Supp. 910 (D.S.C.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-SWS0-001T-62VJ-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Sheltry v. Unum Life Ins. Co., 247 F.Supp.2d 169, 176-177 (D.Conn.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:481M-W1N0-0038-Y258-00000-00&context=1530671) (denying summary judgment to defendants; insurer's agent stole premium moneys submitted to him by insurance-policy applicants; broker's agreement between agent and insurer authorized agent to solicit and procure applications for insurance policies and "'to collect the initial premium or prepayment of expenses thereon'"). On a principal's vicarious liability for conversion by agents, see [_Richards v. Attorneys' Title Guar. Fund, Inc., 866 F.2d 1570 (10th Cir.1989)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-D4G0-003B-555F-00000-00&context=1530671) (title insurer liable for closing agent's embezzlement of funds representing proceeds from sale of property; agent instructs bank, in which funds were deposited into trust account, to wire funds to account in another bank, from which agent received funds in cash and absconded). See also [_Olson v. Tri-County State Bank, 456 N.W.2d 132, 135 (S.D.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S71-2GH0-003G-D124-00000-00&context=1530671) (physical custody of customers' property required by nature of employer's business; employment placed agent in such position that theft not so unusual or startling that it is unfair to include loss in costs of employer's business). For a further application of the same basic point, see [_Groob v. KeyBank, 801 N.E.2d 919, 924-927 (Ohio App.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4B8B-MX10-0039-4297-00000-00&context=1530671), appeal allowed, _808 N.E.2d 397 (Ohio 2004)_ (bank subject to liability when loan officers used information provided in confidence by loan applicant to acquire business opportunity identified in application, while also rejecting application; "the agent's position facilitates the consummation of the fraud").

Construction of an insurance policy may require a court to characterize employees' diversion of funds entrusted to an employer. In [_Stop & Shop Cos. v. Federal Ins. Co., 136 F.3d 71 (1st Cir.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S1P-SS70-0038-X3SV-00000-00&context=1530671), the plaintiff had a tax-service agreement with a third party under which it deposited funds with the third party to be used by it to make timely payments to taxing authorities on behalf of the plaintiff. The agreement allowed commingling of the plaintiffs funds with funds received from other clients and also allowed the third party to invest the plaintiff's funds and to use them for its own expenses so long as tax payments were made when due. The agreement prohibited assignment to anyone not an employee of the third party. Senior officers of the third party diverted about \$ 55 million of the plaintiffs funds; the plaintiff sought indemnification for its losses under a policy of crime insurance issued by the defendant. The policy provided coverage for "'direct losses caused by the . . . disappearance, wrongful abstraction or Computer Theft of Money and Securities. . . .'" It excluded coverage for loss due to "'theft or any other fraudulent, dishonest or criminal act . . . by any \[e\]mployee, director, trustee or authorized representative of the Insured whether acting alone or in collusion with others.'" [_Id. at 72_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S1P-SS70-0038-X3SV-00000-00&context=1530671). The court held that an "authorized representative" could be "either a person or company empowered to act on an entity's behalf," [_id. at 74_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S1P-SS70-0038-X3SV-00000-00&context=1530671) and that, within the context of the insurance policy, the term meant "one of a series of capacities in which an individual who commits theft may have been given access to funds by the insured," [_id. at 76_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S1P-SS70-0038-X3SV-00000-00&context=1530671). The exclusion applied to the theft because officers of the tax-service firm were given access to clients' funds and power to divert them. Id.

A related question concerns allocation of the risk that an employee with access to checks payable to the employer will forge an endorsement and convert the funds or otherwise use access to an employer's checking facility as a mechanism to perpetrate fraud. As between the employer and the bank that takes the check or pays it, U.C.C. § 3-405 allocates the risk of an employee's fraudulent endorsement to the employer, based on "the belief that the employer is in a far better position to avoid the loss by care in choosing employees, in supervising them, and in adopting other measures to prevent forged indorsements on instruments payable to the employer or fraud in the issuance of instruments in the name of the employer." Official Comment 1. Under § 3-405, the employer may shift loss to the bank if the bank failed to exercise ordinary care to the extent the bank's failure contributed to the loss. Under § 3-406, a person whose failure to exercise ordinary care substantially contributes to an alteration of an instrument or the forgery of a signature on an instrument is precluded from asserting the forgery or alteration against a person who, in good faith, either pays the instrument or takes it for collection. See [_Menichini v. Grant, 995 F.2d 1224, 1235 (3d Cir.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-FXP0-003B-P4X5-00000-00&context=1530671) (employer was negligent in entrusting single employee with responsibility for recording and sending invoices, plus receiving and recording incoming payments, "without supervision or any control mechanism," which enabled employee to steal checks in amounts in excess of firm's annual profits and employee's annual compensation).

Illustration 5 is based on [_Vereen v. Liberty Life Ins. Co., 412 S.E.2d 425 (S.C.App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RVJ-CPS0-003G-C34P-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 6 is a variation on the facts of [_Leafgreen v. American Family Mut. Ins. Co., 393 N.W.2d 275 (S.D.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S71-2KM0-003G-D1X8-00000-00&context=1530671). In _Leafgreen_ itself, the court characterizes the facts as "indeed ambiguous," [_id. at 278_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S71-2KM0-003G-D1X8-00000-00&context=1530671), but affirms the trial court's grant of summary judgment to the insurance company. The insurance agent in _Leafgreen_ shared information about a customer's property and the location of the customer's lockbox with two professional burglars who burglarized the customer's home five weeks after the agent's inspection. The agent, a social friend of the customer's, learned when the customer would be away from home and shared that information as well with his confederates. The _Leafgreen_ court acknowledges that "a principal may be held liable for fraud and deceit committed by an agent within his apparent authority, even though the agent acts solely to benefit himself." [_Id. at 277_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S71-2KM0-003G-D1X8-00000-00&context=1530671). The court assessed the situation as ambiguous because the agent obtained the information "by purporting to act within the scope of his powers as the insurer's agent," and did so without immediate harm to his customers. But "the transaction . . . was used to facilitate the burglary which occurred approximately five weeks later." [_Id. at 278_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S71-2KM0-003G-D1X8-00000-00&context=1530671). The court quoted at length from the Reporter's Note to Restatement Second, Agency § 261, which states that "\[i\]n some cases the situation is ambiguous: the agent performs his primary function as an agent of the bank acting within the scope of his powers as such agent without loss to the other from the transaction itself, but the transaction is used as a means by which the agent may defraud such other. . . . If . . . the principal is not benefited, and the transaction which actually causes the loss is not one in which the agent purports to represent the principal, liability should not follow." The _Leafgreen_ court does not focus on whether the agent acted with apparent authority in "the transaction which actually causes the loss" (i.e., the burglary). Instead, the test applied by the court was whether "a nexus sufficient to make the harm foreseeable exists between the agent's employment and the activity which actually caused the injury; foreseeable is used in the sense that the employee's conduct must not be so unusual or startling that it would be unfair to include the loss caused by the injury among the costs of the employer's business." [_Id. at 280-281_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S71-2KM0-003G-D1X8-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 7 is based on [_Woepke v. Federal Kemper Life Assurance Co., 39 F.Supp.2d 1186 (E.D.Mo. 1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3W0V-MSH0-0038-Y39R-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _208 F.3d 219 (8th Cir. 2000)_.

Illustration 8 is based on [_Harrison v. Dean Witter Reynolds, Inc., 974 F.2d 873 (7th Cir.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-1950-008H-V3KH-00000-00&context=1530671). In _Harrison,_ the court reversed the district court's grant of summary judgment to the defendant on the plaintiff's controlling-person claim, holding that the record raised triable issues of fact about whether the defendant had the power or ability to control its employee's trading and whether the defendant, if it had such power or ability, nevertheless had an affirmative defense under § 20(a) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, [_15 U.S.C. § 78t(a)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8S9D-W4W2-8T6X-701Y-00000-00&context=1530671). The statute creates an affirmative defense if "the controlling person acted in good faith and did not directly or indirectly induce the act or acts constituting the violation or cause of action." The court noted that the defendant "had rules in place to prevent various types of fraud," including a requirement that supervisors monitor employees' investments and "ensure an employee's investments were commensurate with his or her resources." [_Id. at 881-882_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-1950-008H-V3KH-00000-00&context=1530671). Two employees were involved in the actual scheme in _Harrison;_ they lost \$ 600,000 trading in their employee accounts during the first year of the scheme and \$ 2,000,000 over the next two years. On more than one occasion, the head of the defendant's compliance department asked the local branch manager to investigate the heavy trading volume in the employees' accounts; the branch manager accepted without further investigation the employees' explanations that they traded only with their own money.

Apparent authority rarely serves as a basis for liability when an employee or agent commits an intentional physical tort. One formal possibility is suggested by Restatement Second, Agency § 219(2)(d), which provides that "\[a\] master is not subject to liability for the torts of his servants acting outside the scope of their employment, unless: . . . the servant purported to act or to speak on behalf of the principal and there was reliance upon apparent authority, or he was aided in accomplishing the tort by the existence of the agency relation." Comment _e_ to § 219 observes: "\[c\]lause (d) includes primarily situations in which the principal's liability is based upon conduct which is within the apparent authority of a servant, as where one purports to speak for his employer in defaming another or interfering with another's business. . . . Apparent authority may also be the basis of an action of deceit . . . and even physical harm." Interpreting "aided in accomplishing the tort by the existence of the agency relation" beyond situations in which an employee purports to speak or transact on behalf of the employer has the potential to expand an employer's vicarious liability well beyond the established boundaries of the scope-of-employment test. See Paula J. Dalley, All in a Day's Work: Employers' Vicarious Liability for Sexual Harassment, [_104 W. Va. L. Rev. 517, 547-548 (2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:482X-SY60-00CV-W054-00000-00&context=1530671). Recent cases applying Restatement Second, Agency § 219(2)(d) beyond conventional uses of apparent authority involve two distinct situations: (1) employee torts against third parties; and (2) sexual harassment by supervisory employees. In [_Costos v. Coconut Island Corp., 137 F.3d 46 (1st Cir.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S53-HH50-0038-X2V9-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Maine law), the court held the owner of an inn vicariously liable when its manager raped a guest of the inn, having used his access to room keys to gain access to her locked room. The court stated: "defendants are well within the scope of § 219(2)(d) liability. By virtue of his agency relationship with the defendants, \[manager\] was entrusted with the keys to the rooms. . . . Because he was the manager of the inn, \[manager\] knew exactly where to find \[plaintiff\]. The jury could find that \[manager\] had responsibilities to be at the inn or to have others there late at night." [_Id. at 50_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S53-HH50-0038-X2V9-00000-00&context=1530671). The court does not examine whether an innkeeper owes a heightened duty of care to guests. For an assessment of the _Costos_ opinion and the history of § 219(2)(d), see Daniel M. Combs, Costos v. Coconut Island Corp.: Creating a Vicarious Liability Catchall Under the Aided-By-Agency-Relation Theory, [_73 U. Colo. L. Rev. 1099 (2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:465C-W8C0-00CV-N008-00000-00&context=1530671). The Maine Supreme Judicial Court has not endorsed _Costos,_ noting that it has produced controversy. See [_Mahar v. StoneWood Transp., 823 A.2d 540, 546 (Me.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48HP-FHG0-0039-41G0-00000-00&context=1530671). But the Vermont Supreme Court adopted [_Costos in Doe v. Forrest, 853 A.2d 48 (Vt.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4CB7-H6T0-0039-4132-00000-00&context=1530671) (plaintiff coerced into sexual encounter by on-duty deputy sheriff).

In Faragher v. City of Boca Raton, the Court's analysis of principles on which an employer may be liable under Title VII on the basis of sexual harassment by supervisory employees begins with § 219(2)(d). See [_524 U.S. 775, 801-803 (1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3TGV-N290-002K-600F-00000-00&context=1530671). The Court reads § 219(2)(d) to extend beyond an abuse of apparent authority; to do otherwise "would seem to ask us to shut our eyes to the potential effects of supervisory authority, even when not explicitly invoked." [_Id. at 802_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3TGV-N290-002K-600F-00000-00&context=1530671). Thus, "the aided-by-agency-relation principle embodied in § 219(2)(d) . . . provides an appropriate starting point for the kind of harassment presented here." Id. The Court's opinion, however, modulates an employer's liability when a supervisory employee's harassment has not resulted in a tangible employment action (which would automatically subject the employer to liability under [_Meritor Sav. Bank, FSB v. Vinson, 477 U.S. 57 (1986))_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-6N20-0039-N3CX-00000-00&context=1530671). When no tangible employment action is taken, an employer has an affirmative defense if it can establish that it exercised reasonable care to prevent and correct sexually harassing behavior, and that the plaintiff "unreasonably failed to take advantage of any preventive or corrective opportunities provided by the employer or to avoid harm otherwise." In short, when a plaintiff establishes that sexual harassment created a hostile environment but did not result in a "tangible employment action" adverse to the plaintiff, an employer's liability is not automatic.

On the relevance of common-law principles of apparent authority as a basis for vicarious liability in the construction of statutes, see, e.g., [_American Soc. of Mech. Eng'rs, Inc. v. Hy-drolevel Corp., 456 U.S. 556, 565-566 (1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-5J60-003B-S04K-00000-00&context=1530671) (antitrust violations); [_Jones v. Federated Fin. Reserve Corp., 144 F.3d 961, 965-966 (6th Cir.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SS0-5N40-0038-X01W-00000-00&context=1530671) (violations of Fair Credit Reporting Act; application of apparent-authority doctrine "advances FCRA's goals and produces no inconsistencies with other FCRA provisions"; deterrence goal of FCRA "would be subverted if a corporation could escape liability for a violation that could only occur because the corporation cloaked its agent with the apparent authority to request credit reports."); [_Ditty v. CheckRite, Ltd., 973 F.Supp. 1320, 1335 (D.Utah 1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S0D-X850-00B1-F313-00000-00&context=1530671) (liability premised on apparent authority for FCRA violation applicable to conduct of agents who are not employees); [_Margan v. Niles, 250 F.Supp.2d 63, 74-75 (N.D.N.Y. 2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:485R-J270-0038-Y46F-00000-00&context=1530671) (violations of Driver's Privacy Protection Act, which protects against the "unauthorized use and disclosure of personal information"); _Pippenger v. McQuik's Oilube, Inc., 854 F. Supp. 1411, 1421 (S.D.Ind.1994)_ (fraud in violation of § 10(b) of Securities Exchange Act); [_In re Atl. Fin. Mgmt., Inc., 784 F.2d 29 (1st Cir.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-9500-0039-P37V-00000-00&context=1530671) (misrepresentation in violation of § 10(b)); [_Thomas v. Ross & Hardies, 9 F.Supp.2d 547, 557-558 (D.Md.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SYG-MR50-0038-Y1J4-00000-00&context=1530671) (vicarious liability under RICO of partnership when partnership and racketeering enterprise are not identical); [_Larsen Chelsey Realty Co. v. Larsen, 656 A.2d 1009 (Conn.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-YXW0-003D-81XC-00000-00&context=1530671) (violation of Connecticut Unfair Trade Practices Act); [_Kansallis Fin. Ltd. v. Fern, 659 N.E.2d 731, 735 (Mass.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2X60-003C-V0YW-00000-00&context=1530671) (liability of partnership for partner's fraud under Uniform Partnership Act). For further discussion of the applicability of apparent-authority doctrine and respondeat superior generally in the context of the federal securities laws, see James D. Cox et al., Securities Regulation 1031-1032 (2d ed. 1997).

_c. Tortious misrepresentations_

_(2). Fraudulent misrepresentations._ Illustration 9 is based on [_Grease Monkey Int'l, Inc. v. Montoya, 904 P.2d 468 (Colo.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-0040-003D-924F-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Blackburn v. Witter, 19 Cal.Rptr. 842 (Cal.App.1962)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-BJH0-003C-J0F6-00000-00&context=1530671) (securities salesman, who was customer's investment advisor, induced her to sell stock through firm to purchase notes issued by nonexistent corporation).

Numerous cases subject a principal to liability on the basis that an agent acted with apparent authority in defrauding a third party. Representative examples include [_A.I. Credit Corp. v. Legion Ins. Co., 265 F.3d 630 (7th Cir.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:441P-GF80-0038-X22H-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent of insurers defrauded premium finance company by misrepresenting status of collateral and intended use of loan proceeds); [_Universal Premium Acceptance Corp. v. Preferred Nat'l Ins. Co., 157 F.Supp.2d 1222 (D.Kan.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:43YB-1K80-0038-Y0K8-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurer's agent, having actual authority to make truthful representations in premium finance agreements, also had apparent authority to make false representations in agreements; insurer liable to premium finance company for damage sustained due to agent's false representations); [_Fidelity Funding, Inc. v. Reinhold, 79 F.Supp.2d 110, 118-119 (E.D.N.Y.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YJ3-PGY0-0038-Y480-00000-00&context=1530671) (business entities subject to liability when agents, acting with apparent authority, made misrepresentations to asset-based lender by sending checks to pay for fraudulent invoices with money received from other participant in fraud); [_Miller v. Mason-McDuffie Co., 739 P.2d 806 (Ariz.1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-WKJ0-003F-T1R4-00000-00&context=1530671) (jury question whether manager of branch office of mortgage bank had apparent authority to represent to property developer that bank's loan commitment was legitimate; developer, in reliance on manager's assurance, paid loan-commitment fee, which manager used for own purposes); _Travelers Cas. & Sur. Co. v. Castegnaro, 772 A.2d 456 (Pa.2001)_ (insurance agent who was authorized to bind coverage for insurance agency with insurer fraudulently submitted cancellation notices on policies or failed to process renewals, knowingly misrepresented to insureds that their policies had been renewed, and fraudulently cashed insureds' checks remitted to him; agency vicariously liable, having placed agent in position within scope of which he acted in defrauding policy holders). See also [_NationsBank, N.A. v. Dilling, 922 S.W.2d 950 (Tex.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W4C0-003C-204H-00000-00&context=1530671) (bank not subject to liability when bank teller issued cashier's check as component of scheme to defraud investor in sham business; bank took no action that would lead reasonable person to believe that teller had authority to make representations about investment's soundness). An isolated recent case to the contrary is [_Millan v. Dean Witter Reynolds, Inc., 90 S.W.3d 760 (Tex.App.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:469F-SXJ0-0039-42WV-00000-00&context=1530671), rev. den., Dec. 19, 2003. In _Millan,_ a broker embezzled \$ 287,000 from his mother, an investor in municipal bonds. He effected the embezzlements by opening an additional account with his employer in her name, forging her signature on the application, then using check-writing and credit-card privileges associated with the second account. He also created false account statements that purported to come from his employer. The majority reasoned that the broker had "general authority" to open brokerage accounts for customers, receive deposits for accounts, and purchase and sell securities as directed by clients. However, the broker "greatly exceeded the scope of his authority when, through a litany of deceitful acts, he stole money from his mother." [_Id. at 768_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:469F-SXJ0-0039-42WV-00000-00&context=1530671). The dissenting opinion notes that the majority's approach suggests that "a firm could actually rely on its agents to embezzle from accounts, then claim ignorance." [_Id. at 769_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:469F-SXJ0-0039-42WV-00000-00&context=1530671). The dissent also notes that the majority's conclusion that the broker's acts were outside his general authority is unsupported by authority and, indeed, ignores the foreseeability of fraudulent acts associated with family accounts. [_Id. at 770_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:469F-SXJ0-0039-42WV-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Patsos v. First Albany Corp., 741 N.E.2d 841, 852 (Mass.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4293-CVR0-0039-41PF-00000-00&context=1530671) (recognizing general principle that broker-dealer that assumes broad fiduciary obligations to client can be subject to liability as a result of broker's embezzlement of client's funds by writing checks on client's account; client's receipt of monthly statements from broker-dealer did not bar claim when client unaware of embezzlement, statements did not reveal payees of checks, and broker told client not to worry when client said he did not understand statements).

For discussion of apparent authority and respondeat superior as contrasting bases for vicarious liability when an employee defrauds a third party, see Charles Davant IV, Employer Liability for Employee Fraud: Apparent Authority or Respondeat Superior?, [_47 S.D. L. Rev. 554 (2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:47CR-0MB0-00CT-W023-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 11 is based on Illustration 1 to Restatement Second, Torts § 525. Comment _b_ of § 525, which discusses the meaning of "misrepresentation," observes that "words or conduct asserting the existence of a fact constitute a misrepresentation if the fact does not exist."

Illustration 13 is based on [_Heider v. Leewards Creative Crafts, Inc., 613 N.E.2d 805 (Ill.App.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-3580-003D-H4FF-00000-00&context=1530671) (if defendant's agents know of existence of report and of asbestos, principal liable for agents' misrepresentations and concealment). On a principal's liability when an agent who sells property to a third party does not know of a nonobvious defect known to the principal, the court states: "if the defendant's agents did not know of the existence of the report and the asbestos, then their principal, defendant, is still liable for the misrepresentation. Where a principal is selling a property with a hidden defect . . . the principal has an obligation to inform a selling agent of the defect. In such cases, the failure to inform the agent may be the basis of liability." [_Id. at 812_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-3580-003D-H4FF-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_In re Humphrey's Pest Control, 80 B.R. 687, 695 (Bankr. E.D.Pa.1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-M8Y0-0039-K042-00000-00&context=1530671) (broker, vendor, and vendor's daughter who acted as her agent subject to liability for fraud; broker and agent, who handled sale of property, knew of report detailing extent of termite infestation and damage but did not provide it although purchaser asked about status of any termite reports or certifications).

For the point that a happenstance connection between a fraud and an agent's use of apparent authority does not create authority under the rule adopted in this section, see [_Security Union Title Ins. Co. v. Citibank (Florida) N.A., 715 So. 2d 973 (Fla. Dist.App.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SX4-MGF0-0039-4031-00000-00&context=1530671) (representations concerning property value not made by agent in capacity as agent for title insurer or with actual or apparent authority of title insurer; when agent was lawyer retained by real-estate developer in transaction with bank who additionally closed transaction on behalf of title insurer, and both lawyer and developer knew that valuation of property was substantially overstated, title insurer not subject to liability to bank because agent's actual and apparent authority with regard to title insurer was limited to title insurance and closing the loan and did not extend to valuation of property). Compare _Sears Mtge. Corp. v. Rose, 634 A.2d 74 (N.J.1993)_ (title insurer, having retained lawyer to serve as closing agent, subject to liability for lawyer's theft of closing funds entrusted to him by purchaser). See also Credit Lyonnais Nederland N.V. v. Export Credit Guarantee Dept., \[2000\] 1 A.C. 486, 495 (H.L.) (credit guarantor not subject to liability to bank for loss caused by scheme in which employee participated by authorizing the issue of credit guarantees used by employee's friend to trick bank into making loans to finance imaginary contracts for sales of goods, which were supported by fraudulently drawn bills of exchange containing forged acceptances in names of imaginary buyers; employer subject to vicarious liability only when employee's conduct occurs within the course of employment but when tort is committed jointly, "then it is conduct which is within the course of employment sufficient to constitute the tort, irrespective of which tortfeasor performed the acts, which is necessary. . . . before there can be vicarious liability, all the features of the wrong which are necessary to make the employee liable have to have occurred in the course of employment.").

For the point that a person is not subject to liability when the person to whom a representation is made knows that it is made by one who is not an agent of the person on whose behalf the representation purportedly is made, see [_Wells Fargo Fin. Leasing, Inc. v. LMT-Fette, Inc., 382 F.3d 852 (8th Cir.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4D9R-RV10-0038-X10M-00000-00&context=1530671) (seller of copier did not act with apparent authority when seller presented purchaser with plaintiff's preprinted credit application and lease agreement with understanding that seller would enter necessary terms; plaintiff did nothing to create impression that seller acted as an agent or subagent on its behalf). Indeed, the second page of the lease stated, directly above the line on which the purchaser signed, "YOU UNDERSTAND THAT WE ARE A SEPARATE AND INDEPENDENT COMPANY FROM ANY VENDOR OR MANUFACTURER AND THAT NEITHER THE VENDOR NOR ANY OTHER PERSON IS OUR AGENT. YOU AGREE THAT NO REPRESENTATION, GUARANTEE OR WARRANTY BY THE VENDOR OR OTHER PERSON IS BINDING ON US, AND NO BREACH BY THE VENDOR OR OTHER PERSON WILL EXCUSE YOUR OBLIGATIONS TO US." See [_250 F.Supp.2d 1120, 1128 (S.D.Iowa 2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:485Y-H940-0038-Y49T-00000-00&context=1530671). Similarly, if a third person knows that an agent acts in a transaction solely on the agent's own behalf, the principal is not subject to liability on the basis that the agent acted with apparent authority. See [_Pruitt v. Pavelin, 685 P.2d 1347 (Ariz.App. 1984)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-Y8N0-003F-T3WH-00000-00&context=1530671) (seller of property defrauded by real-estate salesperson, who forged signatures on contract and security documents, knew salesperson acted solely on her own behalf and not on behalf of or in concert with her employer in connection with transaction; although employer not subject to vicarious liability for salesperson's fraud, employer independently subject to liability on negligent-hiring claim). A person is not held to be aware that an agent is acting on the agent's own behalf simply because the person is aware of contractual defaults. See, e.g., Citibank, N.A. v. Nyland (CF8) [_Ltd., 878 F.2d 620 (2d Cir.1989)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-B9J0-003B-50Y9-00000-00&context=1530671) (mortgagee's awareness of defaults under mortgage does not constitute awareness that property's managing agent was engaged in self-dealing).

Additionally, a principal is not subject to liability on the basis that an agent acted with apparent authority when it is not reasonable for a third party to believe that the agent is authorized. See § 2.03, Comment _d._ In the partnership context, a partner's power to impose liability on the partnership through a wrongful act or omission is limited to acts or omissions of a partner "acting in the ordinary course of business of the partnership or with authority of the partnership." Rev. Unif. Partnership Act § 305(a). However, a partnership is liable for the loss of money or property misapplied by a partner if the partner "in the course of the partnership's business or while acting with authority of the partnership" receives or causes the partnership to receive that money or property of a person not a partner. Id. § 305(b). On the limitation under the English Partnership Act § 10 comparable to the "ordinary course of business" limitation under § 305(a), see J.J. Cough-Ian Ltd. v. Ruparelia, 2003 WL 21554610 P 18 (App.2003) (partner in firm of solicitors not acting within ordinary course of a solicitor's business in defrauding client of \$ 500,000 through a bogus investment scheme that promised 6000% risk-free return on an annualized basis because the type of transaction, characterized as "abnormal and incredible" by trial court, was not of a type ordinarily done by solicitors).

On the point made by Illustrations 14 and 15, Restatement Second, Agency, observes in § 262, Comment _b:_ "\[t\]hus, if the manager and apparent owner of defendant's store misrepresents the qualities of goods given him to sell and embezzles the proceeds, the defendant is liable."

_(3). Negligent misrepresentations._ For the point that an agent's negligent representation may subject a principal to liability, see [_John v. Robbins, 764 F. Supp. 379 (M.D.N.C. 1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-DGT0-001T-721T-00000-00&context=1530671) (vendor of property subject to liability for negligent misrepresentations made by agent); [_Gibb v. Citicorp Mortgage, Inc., 518 N.W.2d 910, 920-922 (Neb.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-4DK0-003D-B0XS-00000-00&context=1530671) (same); [_Silva v. Stevens, 589 A.2d 852 (Vt.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXR-12P0-003G-G1HJ-00000-00&context=1530671) (same). See also [_VRT, Inc. v. Dutton-Lainson Co., 530 N.W.2d 619 (Neb. 1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-4BR0-003D-B0P1-00000-00&context=1530671) (lawyer for company selling assets related to business of manufacturing patient-care equipment negligently misrepresented status of patent application, resulting in seller's failure to deliver and assign filed application pursuant to contract; as consequence, court precluded from finding that seller had substantially performed contract, which precluded seller's claim against buyer for unpaid royalties due under contract). See also [_Wilson v. Century 21 Great W. Realty, 18 Cal.Rptr. 2d 779 (Cal. App.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-GHY0-003D-J2M1-00000-00&context=1530671) (negligent misrepresentation under Cal. Civ. Code §§ 1572(2) and 1710(2) requires an "assertion" or a "positive assertion" of a fact; real-estate broker's failure to disclose defect in property's foundation does not constitute a positive assertion).

When a defendant does not owe a duty of reasonable care to a plaintiff, the plaintiff lacks a basis for a negligent-misrepresentation claim against the defendant. See [_Focus Inv. Assocs., Inc. v. American Title Ins. Co., 797 F.Supp. 109, 114 (D.R.I.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-K7X0-008H-F52N-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd in part and vacated in part on other grounds, [_992 F.2d 1231 (1st Cir.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-GDV0-003B-P2MD-00000-00&context=1530671) (title insurer, through its policy-issuing attorney, did not owe duty to mortgagee to search title or to search for and disclose existing mortgages prior to issuing title policy; title insurer does not generally guarantee state of title but agrees to indemnify insured against loss).
