[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 8.02_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8Y0-00YG-400R-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 8- Duties of Agent and Principal to Each Other_ > _Topic 1- Agent's Duties to Principal_ > _Title B- Duties of Loyalty_**

**§ 8.02 Material Benefit Arising Out of Position**

**An agent has a duty not to acquire a material benefit from a third party in connection with transactions conducted or other actions taken on behalf of the principal or otherwise through the agent's use of the agent's position.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section states the rule that an agent has a duty not to acquire material benefits in connection with transactions or other actions undertaken on the principal's behalf or through the agent's use of position. Comment _b_ discusses the rationales for the rule and its relationship to the rules stated in §§ 8.03 to 8.05. Comment _c_ examines the nature of a material benefit and its connection to the agent's relationship to the principal. Comment _d_ discusses an agent's usurpation of a business opportunity from the principal. Comment _e_ discusses remedies available when an agent breaches this duty and the liability of any third party who induces or otherwise assists the agent's breach.

A principal may consent to conduct by an agent that would otherwise constitute a breach of the duty stated in this section. See § 8.06. Section 8.05 covers an agent's use, without the principal's consent, of confidential information or other assets of the principal for the agent's own purposes or those of a third party.

A similar rule is applicable to directors and senior executives of corporations. See Principles of Corporate Governance: Analysis and Recommendations § 5.04. A lawyer who uses confidential client information for the lawyer's pecuniary gain other than in the practice of law is subject to liability to the client for any profits made. See Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 60(2) and Comment _j._

_b. Rationale._ This rule stems from the ordinary expectation that a person who acts as an agent does so to further the interests of the principal and that it is the principal who should benefit from turns of good fortune that may occur in connection with transactions that the agent undertakes on the principal's behalf. This expectation may stem from the fact when an agent acts with actual or apparent authority, the principal risks being bound by transactions that may turn out to be disadvantageous to the principal in some respect.

An additional rationale for this rule stems from risks to a principal's interests that may arise when an agent pursues material benefits from third parties in connection with actions taken on behalf of the principal. For example, an agent's interest in acquiring a benefit from a third party may supersede the agent's commitment to obtain terms from the third party that are best from the standpoint of the principal. Although the agent may believe that no harm will befall the principal, the agent is not in a position disinterestedly to assess whether harm may occur or whether the principal's interests would be better served if the agent did not pursue or acquire the benefit from the third party. Only the principal can assess the potential impact on the principal's interests of an agent's anticipated receipt of a material benefit to be furnished by a third party. By providing a material benefit to a person known to act as an agent, a third party may become subject to liability to the principal. A third party who provides substantial assistance or encouragement to an agent in breaching the agent's duty to the principal is also subject to liability to the principal. See § 8.01, Comment _b;_ Restatement Second, Torts § 876(b).

**Illustrations:**

1\. P, who owns a racehorse, Grace, engages A, a jockey, to ride Grace in an upcoming race. P agrees to pay A a fee of \$ 500. T, who has made a large bet that Grace will win the race, promises to pay A \$ 5000 if Grace wins the race. T asks A not to tell P about T's promise. Neither A nor T tells P about T's promise. Grace, ridden by A, wins the race. T pays A \$ 5000. A and T are subject to liability to P. A's receipt of \$ 5000 from T breached A's duty to P. T knowingly provided substantial assistance and encouragement to A in A's breach of duty to P. For discussion of remedies available to P, see Comment _e._

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that T asks A to tell P about T's promise. Before the race, although A does not tell P as T requested, A assures T that A has told P. If it is reasonable for T to believe A, T is not subject to liability to P. A is subject to liability to P.

The purpose of this rule is prophylactic. To establish that the agent is subject to liability, it is not necessary that the principal show that the agent's acquisition of a material benefit harmed the principal. The benefit realized by the agent can often be calculated more readily than any harm suffered by the principal. However, when the principal can establish that the agent's conduct resulted in harm to the principal, the principal may recover compensatory damages from the agent.

Moreover, an agent's acquisition of a material benefit may breach this rule even though the agent's ability to acquire the benefit depends on achieving an outcome that may appear consistent with the principal's interests. In Illustration 1, P presumably wishes that Grace will win the race, as do T and A. However, T's promise to pay A if Grace wins the race undermines P's ability to exercise control over A and may thwart P's objectives as P understands them. T's promise may induce A to spur Grace on to assure that Grace wins the race and A receives an additional \$ 5000. However, P, unbeknownst to A, may plan to enter Grace in another race that P views as more important and thus may wish that Grace's energies not be overtaxed. Thus, the point of the rule is to focus the agent's efforts on furthering the principal's interests as the agent reasonably understands them, taking into account manifestations made by the principal. Permitting an agent's focus to encompass additional incentives offered by a third party is inconsistent with the singleness of focus due the principal.

A principal may consent to conduct by an agent that would otherwise breach the agent's duty as stated in this section. See § 8.06. An agent's duty under the rule stated in this section is also limited by the scope of the agency relationship, including its duration.

In many cases to which this rule applies, and in contrast with Illustration 1, it is more likely that the material benefit acquired by the agent comes at a relatively direct cost to the principal because the benefit is conferred on the agent by a third party with whom the agent transacts on the principal's behalf. In some manner, the terms of the transaction between the principal and the third party are likely to reflect the third party's cost of providing the benefit to the agent. As discussed more fully in Comment _e,_ the principal's remedies against the agent include recovery of the benefit or its value, plus damages to compensate the principal for harm caused by the agent's conduct. Although many typical patterns of conduct that violate this rule may also create criminal liability, it is beyond the scope of this Restatement whether an agent's or a third party's conduct constitutes a crime.

**Illustrations:**

3\. P, who owns a used-car lot, employs A as its general manager. A's duties include contracting with suppliers of used cars to replenish P's inventory. One supplier, T, pays A \$ 500 for each car that A purchases for sale on P's lot. A is subject to liability to P. The payments A received from T are material benefits that A acquired in connection with transactions A conducted on P's behalf.

4\. Same facts as Illustration 3, except that T does not pay A for cars that A purchases on behalf of P. Instead, T gives A a three-year-old BMW, stating, "This is a gift from me to you in gratitude for our good relationship." Same result. A is subject to liability to P.

An agent may breach this rule by acquiring a material benefit through the agent's use of the agent's position although the benefit is not acquired from a third party with whom the agent conducts a transaction on behalf of the principal. An agent's attempted acquisition of a benefit from a third party may also subject the agent to liability to the principal.

**Illustrations:**

5\. Same facts as Illustration 3, except that A tells S, another supplier of used cars, that A will not consider purchasing cars from S unless S pays A \$ 1000 in advance of any transaction. S pays A \$ 1000. A purchases no cars from S on behalf of P. A is subject to liability to P.

6\. Same facts as Illustration 5, except that S refuses to pay A. A is subject to liability to P. On the remedies available to P, see Comment _e._

The rule stated in this section is closely related to other rules in this Topic and in application may overlap with them in some cases.

The operation of the rule, like other common-law rules, may be circumscribed by legislation or administrative regulation.

_c. Material benefit._ Many material benefits are explicit, direct, and pecuniary in nature. However, an agent may breach the agent's duty to the principal by acquiring a material benefit more indirectly if the benefit is acquired through the agent's use of position or in connection with a transaction conducted on behalf of the principal.

**Illustrations:**

7\. P, who contemplates building a new house, acquires a lot in Blackacre Estates. P enters into a contract with A, a residential contractor, in which A, in exchange for \$ 65,000, promises to provide P with preconstruction services, including furnishing "architectural and site plan/landscape design." A then enters into a contract on P's behalf with E, an architect. The contract provides that E will prepare architectural plans for P's house. The contract also provides that E's plans cannot be used unless A is also hired to build the house. P refuses to hire A to build the house, and A and E refuse to furnish A with E's plans. By entering into a contract on P's behalf with E that restricted P's right to use E's plans unless P also agreed to use A's services to build the house, A breached A's duty to P. The restrictive term in the contract with E confers a material benefit on A. A breaches the duty stated in this section by withholding consent to P's use of E's plans.

8\. A is the CEO of P, Inc. On behalf of P, Inc., A retains investment-banking firms, including T. To demonstrate gratitude to A and to enhance T's chances of obtaining future investment-banking work for P, Inc., T allocates to A the opportunity to purchase shares in initial public offerings ("IPOs") underwritten by T. T does so at a time when activity in the IPO market leads to rapid increases in the secondary-market price of shares over the IPO price. A purchases shares through IPO allocations made available by T, then sells the shares soon thereafter for a profit. A is subject to liability to P Inc. for the profit realized by A. T allocated IPO shares to A in connection with A's work on behalf of P, Inc.

An agent's attempted acquisition of a material benefit may breach the agent's duty to the principal, as in Illustration 6. However, if the attempt is unsuccessful, the agent has not acquired a benefit; any recovery by the principal against the agent would require a different basis, such as harm caused the principal by the agent's conduct or forfeiture of compensation otherwise due the agent. See § 8.01, Comments _d(1)_ and _d(2),_ for discussion of remedies for breach of fiduciary duty.

An agent may also acquire a material benefit although by its terms the benefit, its value, or its amount is contingent. In Illustration 3, for example, the amount of payment that A receives from T depends on how many cars A purchases from T for P's used-car lot. The benefit A realizes in Illustration 8 is contingent on the secondary market for the shares allocated to A.

_d. Business opportunities._ An agent's position may enable the agent to learn of business opportunities. If the agent's work for the principal involves identifying, assessing, and pursuing such opportunities on the principal's behalf, the agent's duties of performance require the agent to exercise reasonable care and diligence in connection with the opportunity. See § 8.08. Moreover, all agents, even those whose assigned work does not involve the assessment or pursuit of business opportunities, have a fiduciary duty to the principal not to take personal advantage of an opportunity and not to give the opportunity to a third person, when either the nature of the opportunity or the circumstances under which the agent learned of it require that the agent offer the opportunity to the principal. In making this determination, courts appropriately apply the test applicable to determining whether a director or a senior officer of a corporation may take personal advantage of a corporate opportunity. For a definition of "corporate opportunity," see Principles of Corporate Governance: Analysis and Recommendations § 5.05(b).

Regardless of whether an opportunity is characterized as one that an agent should offer to the principal, the agent has a duty stated in § 8.05 not to use property or confidential information of the principal without the principal's consent. Contemporary partnership legislation provides that a partner's duty of loyalty to the partnership and the other partners requires that the partner "account to the partnership and hold as trustee for it any property, profit, or benefit … derived from a use by the partner of partnership property, including the appropriation of a partnership opportunity . . . ." Rev. Unif. Partnership Act § 404(b)(1).

_e. Remedies._ When an agent breaches the duty stated in this section, the principal may recover monetary relief from the agent and, in appropriate circumstances, from any third party who participated in the agent's breach. A principal may avoid a contract entered into by the agent with a third party who participated in the agent's breach of duty. The principal may recover any material benefit received by the agent through the agent's breach, the value of the benefit, or proceeds of the benefit retained by the agent. The principal may also recover damages for any harm caused by the agent's breach. If an agent's breach of duty involves a wrongful disposal of assets of the principal, the principal cannot recover both the value of the asset and what the agent received in exchange. If a principal recovers damages from a third party as a consequence of an agent's breach of fiduciary duty, the principal remains entitled to recover from the agent any benefit that the agent improperly received from the transaction.

**Illustrations:**

9\. Same facts as Illustration 3, except that A buys Blackacre with the \$ 10,000 received from T. At P's election, P may recover Blackacre from A.

10\. Same facts as Illustration 3, except that A enters into a contract with T on P's behalf for 100 used cars. P may rescind the contract. P may also recover from A and T the amount of any benefit that A acquired from T in connection with the contract. P may also recover any losses suffered by P, including costs related to litigation with T.

If a principal seeks to recover a material benefit received by an agent, the value of the benefit, or its proceeds, the principal's recovery is not subject to a deduction for expenses incurred by the agent to induce a third party to confer the benefit on the agent.

**Illustration:**

11\. Same facts as Illustration 3, except that A gives T an all-expense-paid vacation in Hawaii to induce T to agree to make the payments to A. P's recovery against A is not reduced by the cost of the vacation to A.

However, if an agent breaches the agent's fiduciary duty by taking personal advantage of a business opportunity as discussed in Comment _d,_ the principal may recover property that the agent acquired through the breach only if the principal reimburses the agent. The amount of reimbursement is either the amount paid by the agent for the property or the amount for which the principal could have obtained the property, whichever is less. See Restatement of Restitution §§ 194, 195; Principles of Corporate Governance: Analysis and Recommendations, Comment to § 5.05(e) and Illustration 13.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency, and codifications._ This section is the counterpart of Restatement Second, Agency § 388.

Section 388 states that "\[u\]nless otherwise agreed, an agent who makes a profit in connection with transactions conducted by him on behalf of the principal is under a duty to give such profit to the principal." This section omits the prefatory "\[u\]nless otherwise agreed" in § 388 because § 8.06 comprehensively covers a principal's consent to an agent's conduct that would otherwise constitute a breach of a duty of loyalty. This section is also formulated more generally than § 388 to make it explicit that a principal's remedies are not limited to recovery of profit from the agent.

Under La. Civ. Code art. 3004 (2005), "\[t\]he mandatary is bound to deliver to the principal everything he received by virtue of the mandate, including things he received unduly."

_b. Rationale._ For statements of the basic point in varied contexts, see [_Zucker v. Occidental Petroleum Corp., 192 F.3d 1323 (9th Cir.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XNX-BGG0-0038-X1V2-00000-00&context=1530671) (class member may have standing to challenge settlement of class action that did not provide for cash payments to class members; member has an interest in award of attorney's fees, as would individual client whose lawyer accepts payment without client's consent from adversary in lawsuit); In re Seizure of [_\$ 82,000, 119 F.Supp.2d 1013, 1020 (W.D.Mo.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:41P6-MY60-0038-Y324-00000-00&context=1530671) (currency found in gas tank of vehicle purchased from government after prior owner forfeited vehicle should be treated as abandoned property found by mechanic to whom new owner took vehicle after noticing its fuel problem; abandoned property found by agent is property of principal if agent acting within scope of authority at the time). For an unusual qualification of the basic principle, see [_D.A.B. v. Brown, 570 N.W.2d 168 (Minn.App.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S42-H650-0039-440H-00000-00&context=1530671) (claim by patients based on physician's receipt of kickback payments from manufacturer and distributor of human growth hormone; any cause of action sounds in medical malpractice, not breach of fiduciary duty unless patients can establish injury, with consequence that applicable limitations period is 2-year period applicable to medical-malpractice claims); [_Neade v. Portes, 739 N.E.2d 496 (Ill. 2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:41HC-4330-0039-40BM-00000-00&context=1530671) (breach of fiduciary duty based on physician's failure to disclose interest in medical-incentive fund to patient duplicative of medical-malpractice claim).

For the point that only the principal can assess the significance to the principal's interests of an agent's receipt of a benefit from a third party, see [_O'Malley v. Boris, 742 A.2d 845, 850-851 (Del.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3Y5D-9MK0-0039-4158-00000-00&context=1530671) (reversing lower court's dismissal of complaint; customers of broker who switched their money-market "sweep" account to a new fund without fully disclosing that broker had received 20% equity stake in joint venture with fund in exchange for facilitating switch would have claim against broker for breach of fiduciary obligation; customers "might consider it important to know the basis for \[broker's\] choice of sweep account funds. The disclosed fact that \[broker\] will participate in \[fund's\] profits gives part, but not all, of the answer. The \[plaintiffs\] also allege that it would have been significant also to know that \[broker\] used the transfer of its clients' money to buy its share of the venture. We cannot say that a reasonable investor would not share the \[plaintiffs'\] view. The financial impact on each investor's sweep account may be minimal, but the fact that the broker used the investor's money to advance its own interests may be important not only to the choice of sweep accounts, but also to the investor's choice of brokers."). See also [_O'Malley v. Boris, 2002 WL 453928 (Del.Ch.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45K0-K6B0-0039-401B-00000-00&context=1530671) (prospectus sent to clients omitted material information that transfer of clients' money was a quid pro quo for broker's stake in joint venture with fund; partial disclosure not consistent with broker's fiduciary duty to clients).

On the irrelevance of the intention with which an agent breaches this rule, see _Jerlyn Yacht Sales, Inc. v. Wayne R. Roman Yacht Brokerage, 950 F.2d 60, 67 (1st Cir.1991)_ ("even in the absence of a corrupt intent on the part of the agent, where the agent secures a profit unbeknownst to the principal in connection with a transaction undertaken on the principal's behalf, the agent has an affirmative duty to account and pay over that profit to the principal, absent an agreement between the agent and the principal to the contrary"; yacht broker would violate duty to parties by failing to disclose to purchaser that broker's commission had been enhanced when broker failed to tell purchaser that seller of yacht may have accepted prior offer made by purchaser at lower price).

For the point that a third party who lends substantial assistance to an agent's breach of fiduciary duty is subject to liability to the principal, see [_Independent Order of Foresters v. Donaldson, Lufkin & Jenrette, Inc., 157 F.3d 933, 943-944 (2d Cir.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3TTK-YCF0-0038-X309-00000-00&context=1530671) (summary judgment for defendant brokerage firm improper when firm made deposits into account of plaintiffs investment manager in close temporal proximity to manager's purchase of collateralized mortgage-derivative securities from defendant); [_Twenty First Century L.P.I. v. LaBianca, 19 F.Supp.2d 35, 41-42 (E.D.N.Y.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3TK0-1PS0-0038-Y0S6-00000-00&context=1530671) (third parties who submitted inflated invoices to agents in exchange for kickback payments made to agents liable for aiding and abetting agents' breach of fiduciary duty). See also [_BBS Norwalk One, Inc. v. Raccolta, Inc., 60 F.Supp.2d 123 (S.D.N.Y.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WTG-F6R0-0038-Y241-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _205 F.3d 1321 (2d Cir.2000)_ (granting summary judgment for cousin of corporate officer who allegedly aided and abetted officer's appropriation of corporate opportunity; no showing that cousin acted with requisite intent or had reason to suspect officer's actions were not authorized by corporation).

Illustration 1 is based on the facts of [_McDevitt v. Stokes, 192 S.W. 681 (Ky.1917)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3Y2W-8SR0-00KR-D3X7-00000-00&context=1530671) (defendant's promise to pay jockey if horse wins race not enforceable).

On the outcome stated for Illustration 2, see [_O'Malley v. Boris, 2002 WL 453928, at \*8 (Del.Ch.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45K0-K6B0-0039-401B-00000-00&context=1530671) (fund that provided equity stake in joint venture to broker in exchange for broker's agreement to facilitate switch of money in clients' sweep accounts to fund may not be subject to liability for aiding and abetting broker's breach of fiduciary duty to clients; "there is not a per se prohibition on self-dealing. Our law only prohibits self-dealing by agents in the absence of fully informed consent by their principals. Thus, even if the other defendants knew that they were helping \[broker\] enter into a self-dealing transaction it may be the case that they expected \[broker\] to disclose all elements of the transaction to its clients, thereby eliminating any potential breach of the duty of loyalty.").

Illustration 3 is based on [_Condon Auto Sales & Serv., Inc. v. Crick, 604 N.W.2d 587 (Iowa 1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3Y5N-BTF0-0039-4205-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Hadden v. Consolidated Edison Co., 382 N.E.2d 1136, 1138 (N.Y.1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-B150-003C-F340-00000-00&context=1530671) (receipt by corporation's vice-president of bribes and secret cash gifts from construction firms with which employer did business and in contravention of employer's rules "constituted such grave misconduct as to justify the servant's discharge").

The best-known case in which an agent makes use of the agent's position to acquire a benefit from a third party, apart from engaging in a transaction with a third party on behalf of the principal, is Reading v. Attorney-General \[1951\] A.C. 507 (H.L.). An Army sergeant, stationed in Cairo, received payment for accompanying civilian trucks containing illegal shipments of alcohol through police check-points while wearing his uniform, which assured that the trucks would pass through the check-points without inspection. After military police seized the money received by the sergeant, he sued unsuccessfully to recover it. The court held that he received the money by virtue of his employment but in dereliction of his duty; the fact that the Crown could not have legally acquired the money in the same way did not entitle the sergeant to its return. Moreover, "\[i\]t was also argued that because a master is not liable for damages caused by a servant's use of the master's property when not engaged in the master's business it follows that the master cannot claim the profits. This argument appears to me to confuse the obligations of a servant or agent to his master with the principal's or master's obligations to third parties." [_Id. at 518_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:41HC-4330-0039-40BM-00000-00&context=1530671). (per Lord Oaksey).

On an agent's attempt to collect a personal benefit, as in Illustration 6, see [_Queens Structure Corp. v. Jay Lawrence Assocs., Inc., 758 N.Y.S.2d 664 (App.Div.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48FC-KGH0-0039-44SP-00000-00&context=1530671) (vendors of property stated claim for breach of fiduciary duty against their brokers who attempted to collect a "'consulting fee'" of 2% from purchaser--which purchaser refused to pay--without vendors' consent).

_c. Material benefit._ The best-known recognition that breach of fiduciary duty turns on whether an agent receives or anticipates the receipt of a benefit, and that such benefit may be other than a monetary benefit, is the Court's statement in [_Dirks v. SEC, 463 U.S. 646, 663-664 (1983)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-4GS0-003B-S3KX-00000-00&context=1530671), that whether a corporate insider's tip of corporate information constitutes a breach of fiduciary duty turns on "whether the insider receives a direct or indirect personal benefit from the disclosure, such as a pecuniary gain or a reputational benefit that will translate into future earnings . . . . there may be a relationship between the insider and the recipient that suggests a _quid pro quo_ from the latter, or an intention to benefit the particular recipient. The elements of fiduciary duty … also exist when an insider makes a gift of confidential information to a trading relative or friend."

Illustration 7 is based on [_Sokoloff v. Harriman Estates Dev. Corp., 754 N.E.2d 184 (N.Y.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:43G4-TC50-0039-41J8-00000-00&context=1530671).

See also _Jerlyn Yacht Sales, Inc. v. Wayne R. Roman Yacht Brokerage, 950 F.2d 60 (1st Cir.1991)_ (yacht broker concealed respective positions of yacht's owner and prospective purchaser from each other, with consequence that broker retained the difference between amount purchaser paid and maximum amount that owner thought purchaser was willing to pay). In _Jerlyn Yacht,_ the yacht's owner and her buyer joined forces to sue the yacht broker. The court held that the trial court committed reversible error when it declined to instruct the jury that the broker had a fiduciary duty not to make secret profits or to conceal material information from his principal: "\[t\]he court's charge as given only informed the jury that a broker has a general duty to disclose possible 'conflicts of interest' that _might_ make an agent act in his own interest at the expense of his principal. That was an understatement. A fiduciary's duty goes much farther. Absent an agreement to the contrary, a broker must put his principal's interest first and refrain from competing with the principal as to the subject matter of the agency. . . . He must be 'up front' with his principal at all times" (emphasis in original). _Id. at 69_.

Illustration 8 is based on In re e Bay, Inc. S'holders. Litig., 2004 WL 253521, at \*5 (Del.Ch.2004) ("the defendant directors were … not free to accept this consideration from a company … that was doing significant business with eBay and which arguably intended the consideration as an inducement to maintaining the business relationship in the future."). The court also denied the defendants' motion to dismiss the plaintiffs' alternate claim that the IPO allocations should be characterized as corporate opportunities, see id. at \*4, as well as the defendant investment bank's motion to dismiss the aiding and abetting claims against it, see id. at \*5. For discussion of the legal issues raised by preferential IPO allocations to officers and employees of present or prospective clients, see Therese H. Maynard, Spinning in a Hot IPO--Breach of Fiduciary Duty or Business as Usual?, [_43 Wm. & Mary L. Rev. 2023 (2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4626-S6V0-00CW-G17C-00000-00&context=1530671).

On regulation that may circumscribe the operation of this rule, see [_Dahl v. Charles Schwab & Co., 545 N.W.2d 918 (Minn.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-34C0-003F-V1F6-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Guice v. Charles Schwab & Co., 674 N.E.2d 282 (N.Y.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S53-3XH0-003V-B2XK-00000-00&context=1530671); and [_Shulick v. Paine-Webber, Inc., 722 A.2d 148 (Pa.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3VF2-RMT0-0039-44B9-00000-00&context=1530671) (SEC's Rule 10b-10 preempts common-law liability based on agent's breach of fiduciary obligation when retail stock broker receives payments for directing customer's order flow without disclosing amount of payment to customer prior to transaction). See also Jonathan R. Macey & Maureen O'Hara, The Law and Economics of Best Execution, J. Fin. Intermediation, Dec. 1977 (evaluating feasibility and consequences of regulatory strategies).

_d. Business opportunities._ Outside the partnership context, in most cases in which it is alleged that a defendant breached a fiduciary duty by personally acquiring a business opportunity, the defendant is a director or a senior officer of a corporation. For exceptions, see, e.g., [_Prodromos v. Everen Sec., Inc., 793 N.E.2d 151 (Ill.App.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48Y1-RWK0-0039-44D4-00000-00&context=1530671) (reversing trial court's award of summary judgment for defendant investment banker; genuine issues of material fact whether relationship between investment banker and client constituted an agency relationship and, if so, whether investment banker breached its fiduciary obligation to client by usurping client's deal to purchase savings bank); [_Berenson v. Nirenstein, 93 N.E.2d 610 (Mass.1950)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-9T30-003C-T55X-00000-00&context=1530671) (broker retained to purchase all shares of stock issued by corporation breached duty by purchasing all shares for himself); [_Gomez v. Bicknell, 756 N.Y.S.2d 209 (App.Div.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:47J1-37K0-0039-435B-00000-00&context=1530671) (employee of firm that provided merger and acquisition advising services breached fiduciary duty to employer by keeping to himself the name of a prospective merger client, then securing contract on behalf of prospect after resigning from firm); [_IBE Trade Corp. v. Litvinenko, 748 N.Y.S.2d 741 (App.Div.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4730-15N0-0039-43KG-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent retained by principal to pursue business opportunity usurped it through abuse of power of attorney granted by principal); [_Hill v. Southeastern Floor Covering Co., 596 So. 2d 874 (Miss.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-4M70-003G-74J0-00000-00&context=1530671) (general manager appropriated opportunity to subcontract asbestos encapsulation job that employer could not legally perform directly although it often profited by subcontracting such work to others).

_e. Remedies._ On the remedies available to a principal when an agent receives a secret commission from a third party, see [_Twenty First Century L.P.I. v. LaBianca, 19 F.Supp.2d 35, 41 (E.D.N.Y.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3TK0-1PS0-0038-Y0S6-00000-00&context=1530671) (fast-food franchisee's director of construction and his immediate supervisor colluded with construction firms that submitted inflated invoices for work in exchange for kickback payments; both liable for "the direct consequences of their illegal actions," including loss to franchisee, which paid inflated invoices); [_Tarnowski v. Resop, 51 N.W.2d 801 (Minn.1952)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-4S00-003G-V2SJ-00000-00&context=1530671) (principal rescinds underlying contract with third party, recovering down payment; principal also recovers commission paid to agent by third party, plus attorney's fees, expenses, and amount for loss of time devoted to matter). On remedies available when an agent diverts a business opportunity of the principal, see [_Gomez, 756 N.Y.S.2d 209_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:47J1-37K0-0039-435B-00000-00&context=1530671) (claim for usurpation of business opportunity does not require plaintiff to show damages, in contrast to claim that agent breached noncompete clause; sufficient for plaintiff to show gross fee received by agent through usurpation of opportunity from principal's mergers-and-acquisitions advisory business; burden then shifts to agent to show direct costs incurred to generate fee; plaintiff then has opportunity to challenge reasonableness of deductions from gross fee proposed by agent).

On a third party's liability to the principal, see generally Restatement Second, Torts § 874, Comment _c_ (third party "responsible only for harm caused or profits that he himself has made from the transaction"; third party "not necessarily liable" for profits made by fiduciary); [_Independent Order of Foresters v. Donaldson, Lufkin & Jenrette, Inc., 157 F.3d 933, 944 n.11 (2d Cir.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3TTK-YCF0-0038-X309-00000-00&context=1530671) (against brokerage firm that allegedly made payments into account for benefit of investment manager in exchange for manager's purchase of derivative securities, "some measure of restitutionary relief might be recoverable whether or not compensatory relief was," citing Restatement of Restitution § 138(2)). See also [_Donemar, Inc. v. Molloy, 169 N.E. 610 (N.Y. 1930)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-S7F0-003F-64KV-00000-00&context=1530671) and _Kinzbach Tool Co. v. Corbett-Wallace Co., 160 S.W.2d 509 (Tex. 1942)_ (imposing liability on third party to pay principal amount of payment made to agent). The harm that a principal suffers may be measured by the amount wrongfully paid by the third party to the agent. See [_Franklin Med. Assocs. v. Newark Pub. Schs., 828 A.2d 966, 975 (N.J.Super.App.Div.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4980-HDY0-0039-41T1-00000-00&context=1530671). However, the principal may not recover the amount of a bribe twice, once from the agent who received it and once from the payor as a measure of damages. [_Id. at 976_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4980-HDY0-0039-41T1-00000-00&context=1530671). A third party may be able to show that a portion of its profit did not result from the agent's disloyalty and represented "ordinary" profits that the third party would have realized in any event when it is highly probable that the principal would in any event have entered into a transaction with the third party. See Fyffes Group Ltd. v. Templeman, \[2000\] 2 Lloyd's L. Rep. 643, 672 (Comm. Ct.) (third party who paid bribe to agent liable for damages to extent agent's disloyalty led to more favorable terms of contract with principal; third party not subject to liability for all profit realized through transaction when a portion of its profit represented an ordinary element that "would have been agreed to by an honest and prudent negotiator on \[principal's\] behalf"). For discussion of _Fyffes_ and other recent English cases, see Charles Mitchell, Civil Liability for Bribery, 117 L.Q. Rev. 207 (2001).

For available remedies when a defendant is a director or a senior executive of a corporation, see Principles of Corporate Governance: Analysis and Recommendations § 5.04(c) (director or senior executive liable "only to the extent of any improper benefit received and retained, except to the extent that any foreseeable harm caused by the conduct of the director or senior executive exceeds the value of the benefit received, and multiple liability based on receipt of the same benefit is not to be imposed."). The reference to "multiple liability" means that a corporation and a shareholder in a direct action may not both recover the same amount of benefit nor may either recover for the harm done to the other. Id., Comment _c(5)._

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 8.03_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8Y0-00YG-400S-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 8- Duties of Agent and Principal to Each Other_ > _Topic 1- Agent's Duties to Principal_ > _Title B- Duties of Loyalty_**

**§ 8.03 Acting as or on Behalf of an Adverse Party**

**An agent has a duty not to deal with the principal as or on behalf of an adverse party in a transaction connected with the agency relationship.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section states rules applicable when an agent acts as or on behalf of an adverse party in a transaction with a principal. Comment _b_ discusses the rationale for these rules and the breadth of their formulation. Comment c discusses when an agent acts as or for an adverse party for purposes of these rules. Comment _d_ discusses remedies available to the principal when an agent's conduct breaches the rules stated in this section.

A principal may consent to action by an agent that would otherwise constitute a breach of the agent's duty as stated in this section. See § 8.06. On the consent of multiple principals when an agent acts for more than one principal in a transaction between or among the principals, see § 8.06(2).

Section 8.04 is applicable when an agent competes with the principal or acts on behalf of a competitor of the principal.

Section 5.04 covers imputation to the principal of notice of a fact known by an agent when the agent acts adversely to the principal. For discussion of the meaning of "adversely" in that context in contrast to the rule stated in this section, see § 5.04, Comment _c._

_b. Rationale._ As a fiduciary, an agent has a duty to the principal to act loyally in the principal's interest in all matters in connection with the agency relationship. See § 8.01. The rule stated in this section is a specific application of this general principle. When an agent deals with the principal on the agent's own account, the agent's own interests are irreconcilably in tension with the principal's interests because the interest of each is furthered by action--negotiating a higher or a lower price, for example--that is incompatible with the interests of the other. If an agent acts on behalf of the principal in a transaction with the agent, the agent's duty to act loyally in the principal's interest conflicts with the agent's self-interest. Even if the agent's divided loyalty does not result in demonstrable harm to the principal, the agent has breached the agent's duty of undivided loyalty. Likewise, an agent who acts on behalf of more than one principal in a transaction between or among the principals has breached the agent's duty of loyalty to each principal through undertaking service to multiple principals that divides the agent's loyalty.

A principal may consent to conduct by an agent that would otherwise constitute a breach of the agent's duty. See § 8.06. On consent by multiple principals when an agent conducts a transaction between or among the principals, see § 8.06(2).

The duty stated in this section is formulated broadly. So long as the transaction in which an agent acts as or on behalf of an adverse party is connected with the agency relationship, the agent is subject to the duty although the agent does not have direct or indirect responsibility for conducting the transaction on behalf of the principal. The breadth of this formulation requires that an agent disclose adverse interests to the principal so that the principal may evaluate, as only the principal is situated to do, how best to protect its interests in light of the agent's interest. See § 8.06. The breadth of the formulation also makes it unnecessary for a principal to prescribe its agents' duties and prohibit outside interests that its agents may have or acquire with great specificity in formulating the initial terms of its relationship with its agents.

**Illustrations:**

1\. P Corporation, which sells fabric-forming systems used for purposes such as lining ditches, uses polypropylene fabric as a principal component in constructing systems. P Corporation hires A to make sales and perform marketing functions. A's duties do not involve negotiating the terms of P Corporation's purchase of polypropylene fabric. Unbeknownst to P Corporation, A owns one-half of the equity of T Corporation, the principal supplier of polypropylene to P Corporation. A has breached A's duty to P Corporation. A's ownership interest in T Corporation makes A an adverse party in P Corporation's dealings with T Corporation.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that A tells C, P Corporation's President, that A owns an interest in T Corporation. P Corporation, through C, has knowledge that in dealing with T Corporation, it deals with A as an adverse party.

A principal's knowledge that an agent deals as or on behalf of an adverse party does not relieve the agent of duties to the principal in connection with that transaction. Under the rule stated in § 8.06, the agent has a duty to deal fairly with the principal and to disclose to the principal all facts of which the agent has notice that are reasonably relevant to the principal's exercise of judgment, unless the principal has manifested that the principal already knows them or does not wish to know them. Thus, a principal's knowledge that its agent acts as or on behalf of an adverse party does not convert the relationship between principal and agent into an arm's-length relationship. Moreover, as stated in § 8.11, an agent has a duty to use reasonable effort to furnish information to the principal although the agent does not deal as or on behalf of an adverse party.

**Illustration:**

3\. Same facts as Illustration 2, except that A knows that T Corporation is well along in the process of developing a new line of polypropylene fabric that could be superior for P Corporation's purposes. A has a duty to disclose this fact to P Corporation. That a superior product is in the offing is a fact that a user of the current product would reasonably take into account in determining how much of the current product to purchase.

It is, of course, possible that an agent may assume an adverse position in which the agent may not legally discharge the duties of disclosure that the agent owes to the principal because the agent owes a duty to another person not to disclose a fact that §§ 8.06 and 8.11 require be disclosed to the principal. In Illustration 3, for example, A's duties to T Corporation may prohibit A's disclosure of new product developments to T Corporation's customer, P Corporation. Unless it is possible for T Corporation to shield A from access to facts that A will have a duty to disclose to P Corporation, A's position is not tenable, and consequently A must withdraw as P Corporation's agent.

However, an agent whose acts on behalf of a party consist solely of ministerial acts that require no exercise of discretion, judgment, or skill does not act on behalf of that party for purposes of determining whether the agent acts adversely to another principal. Thus, one principal's agent who performs only ministerial acts for another does not become a dual agent.

A custom in an industry that permits self-dealing by agents, if unknown to the principal, does not relieve the agent of the duty to refrain from self-dealing. If an agreement between principal and agent grants the agent discretion to take such adverse action, the agent is subject to a contract-law duty of good faith and fair dealing in exercising the discretion. See § 8.01, Comment _b._

Many business-organization statutes contain provisions that address self-dealing conduct on the part of specified organizational actors. Under contemporary partnership legislation, a partner has a duty to "refrain from dealing with the partnership in the conduct or winding up of the partnership business as or on behalf of a party having an interest adverse to the partnership. . . ." Rev. Unif. Partnership Act § 404(b)(2). Similarly, a manager of a limited-liability company (LLC) has a comparable duty, as does a member of a member-managed LLC. See Unif. Limited Liability Company Act § 409(b)(2). Many corporation statutes have provisions applicable to transactions between a corporation and a director, or between a corporation and an officer, in which mechanisms are specified through which the director or officer may fulfill duties owed to the corporation in connection with the transaction. See Principles of Corporate Governance: Analysis and Recommendations § 5.02.

_c. Agent acts as or for an adverse party._ An agent is deemed to act as or for an adverse party in a transaction when the agent has a substantial economic interest in the party with whom the principal deals. An agent's interest may also be adverse to the principal's interest when a transaction is between the principal and an associate of the agent's, such as a close friend or relative. For a definition of "associate," see Principles of Corporate Governance: Analysis and Recommendations § 1.03.

**Illustration:**

4\. P, who owns Blackacre, lists it for sale with A, telling A that P would consider selling Blackacre in exchange for installment payments over 15 years, with a down payment of at least \$ 7500. P agrees to pay A a five percent commission. A presents P with an offer from T for \$ 210,000 but does not tell P that A has agreed to lend T the \$ 7500 required for the down payment. P accepts T's offer. A has breached A's duty to P. A has a substantial economic interest in T. A has also breached A's duty, stated in § 8.11, to disclose material facts to P.

The rule stated in this section does not apply to a transaction that occurs prior to or after termination of an agency relationship, unless the prior or former agent otherwise has a confidential relationship with the principal. For example, in Illustration 4, if A agrees to make a loan to T after A has completed A's work on behalf of P, A would not have had an interest adverse to P's at the prior time a contract was formed between P and T. Similarly, an agent may be free to purchase or sell to the principal following termination of the agency relationship.

**Illustration:**

5\. Same facts as Illustration 4, except that P rejects T's offer to purchase Blackacre. P's listing agreement with A terminates. A then offers to buy Blackacre. P accepts. A then resells Blackacre to T. A is not subject to liability to P.

_d. Remedies._ In many cases, an agent's contravention of the rules stated in this section leads to forfeiture of commissions otherwise due the agent by the principal or principals on whose behalf the agent acted. See § 8.01, Comment _d(2)._ An agent's breach of duty also subjects the agent to liability for loss caused the principal and to liability for benefits acquired by the agent. See § 8.01, Comment _d(1)._ An agent's undisclosed self-dealing is a basis on which the principal may avoid the transaction with the agent. If an agent represents multiple principals in a transaction between or among them, any principal who does not know of the agent's divided loyalties may rescind the transaction.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency, and codifications._ This section is the counterpart to Restatement Second, Agency §§ 389 and 391. Both § 389 (applicable to an agent's action as an adverse party without the principal's consent) and § 391 (applicable to an agent's action on behalf of an adverse party without the principal's consent) are prefaced by "\[u\]nless otherwise agreed. . . ." This Restatement, unlike Restatement Second, addresses on a comprehensive basis how a principal may consent to conduct by an agent that would otherwise contravene the agent's duties of loyalty. See § 8.06.

Under Ga. Code § 10-6-24 (2000 & Supp. 2005), "\[w\]ithout the express consent of the principal after a full knowledge of all the facts, an agent employed to sell may not himself be the purchaser; and an agent to buy may not himself be the seller." Under § 10-6-31, "\[a\]n agent who shall have discharged his duty shall be entitled to his commission and all necessary expenses incurred about the business of his principal. If he shall have violated his engagement, he shall be entitled to no commission."

La. Civ. Code art. 2998 (2005) states that "\[a\] mandatary who represents the principal as the other contracting party may not contract with himself unless he is authorized by the principal, or, in making the contract, he is merely fulfilling a duty to the principal." Under art. 3000, "\[a\] person may be the mandatary of two or more parties, such as a buyer and a seller, for the purpose of transacting one or more affairs involving all of them. In such a case, the mandatary must disclose to each party that he also represents the other."

_b. Rationale._ Illustrations 1 and 2 are based on [_Construction Techniques, Inc. v. Dominske, 928 F.2d 632, 638 (4th Cir.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-FCY0-008H-V4K6-00000-00&context=1530671) (as a matter of law, "the situation in which \[employee\] placed himself when, as part owner of \[supplier\], he became an employee of \[supplier's\] sole customer is a situation in which the presence of adverse interests threatens the fiduciary relationship between principal and agent"; employee adequately disclosed interest in supplier to employer's president although employee did not reveal extent of interest in supplier because "\[employee\] stood to profit from transactions with \[supplier\] regardless of the percentage of his interest."). See also [_Geman v. SEC, 334 F.3d 1183 (10th Cir.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:491C-D1R0-0038-X0T8-00000-00&context=1530671) (broker-dealer breached fiduciary duty to customers by failing to disclose that it profited by engaging in trades with them as a principal); [_Magnum Corp. v. Lehman Bros. Kuhn Loeb, Inc., 794 F.2d 198, 201 (5th Cir.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-3FR0-0039-P4NB-00000-00&context=1530671) (stockbroker breached obligation to clients who placed order to buy stock by not disclosing that, due to volume of buy orders, broker was becoming a market-maker, buying for its own account and selling to its clients, which would delay execution of order; clients "were entitled to be told about this development so that they could elect to buy no … stock or to go elsewhere to place their orders."); [_McLendon v. Georgia Kaolin Co., 837 F.Supp. 1231, 1240 (M.D.Ga.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-C2J0-001T-63DH-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent for purchaser of mineral interests, if also agent for sellers, would have duty of disclosure to sellers); [_Auction Effertz, Ltd. v. Schecher, 611 N.W.2d 173, 178 (N.D.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40BW-R430-0039-42XH-00000-00&context=1530671) (company in which auctioneer had ownership interest purchased cattle after auctioneer agreed with cattle owner to find a buyer and negotiate a private sale; auctioneer disclosed identity of buyer to seller, seller was aware of auctioneer's interest in buyer, and disclosure was made when seller had time and opportunity to reject the sale and pursue other alternatives, such as not selling at all or selling by auction); [_Howard v. State, 557 N.Y.S.2d 631, 632 (App.Div.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S2R-9BK0-003V-B20H-00000-00&context=1530671) (real-estate broker who bid on property on behalf of firm in which broker had interest fully complied with statutory disclosure requirement and is entitled to brokerage commission); [_Ritchie v. Weston, Inc., 757 N.E.2d 835, 838 (Ohio App.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:42YV-6HB0-0039-4306-00000-00&context=1530671) (if real-estate agent's entitlement to receive commission through assignment of right by broker created dual-agency relationship, agent satisfied disclosure requirement).

On the antagonism between an agent's interest and that of the principal when the agent acts as an adverse party, see [_Hall v. Paine, 112 N.E. 153, 158 (Mass.1916)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-F2P0-003C-T0J0-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[a\] broker's obligation to his principal requires him to secure the highest price obtainable, while his self-interest prompts him to buy at the lowest practicable price. The law does not trust human nature to be exposed to the temptations likely to arise out of such antagonistic duty and influence.").

For the point that an agent's liability does not require a showing of harm by the principal, see [_Green v. H & R Block, Inc., 735 A.2d 1039, 1056 (Md.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3X83-K140-0039-42JB-00000-00&context=1530671) (genuine issues of material fact made summary judgment for defendant improper; issue of fact whether defendant had agency relationship with clients for whom it prepared and filed income-tax returns when defendant also received commissions from lenders for arranging income-tax refund loans for tax-preparation clients).

On creation of a relationship of dual agency, see Sears v. Polan's 5 cents to [_\$ 1.00 Store, Inc., 243 A.2d 602 (Md.1968)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-7SG0-003G-2224-00000-00&context=1530671) (granting specific performance of contract for purchaser of property; agent's relationship with vendors of property terminated when sale contract executed, while prior to consummation of sale agent engaged in activity on behalf of buyer and eventually became buyer's assignee of sale contract; at time of execution of sale contract, consummation of sale was contingent on rezoning of property, which imposed no additional duties on agent); [_Coldwell Banker Residential Real Estate v. Berner, 609 N.Y.S.2d 948, 951 (App.Div.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S2R-7080-003V-B2CP-00000-00&context=1530671) (triable issues of fact whether broker entitled to recover commission from landowner who retained broker to negotiate lease and sales contract although broker also acted on behalf of potential lessee in identifying properties and did not disclose to landowner that particular lessee had concern about land's configuration; when broker first approached landowner, broker told landowner that "he had a financial institution that might be interested in leasing space in the proposed building"; if this "information was sufficient to impart knowledge" that broker also acted on behalf of prospective lessee and landowner consented to dual agency, broker's acts may not have breached fiduciary duty to landowner); [_Allison v. Cook, 744 N.E.2d 254 (Ohio App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:41G1-G8J0-0039-420S-00000-00&context=1530671) (if real-estate agent represented both vendor and purchaser, agent breached duty to purchaser by failing to disclose nonconfidential facts regarding property to purchaser).

On ministerial acts, see [_Hidden Brook Air, Inc. v. Thabet Aviation Int'l Inc., 241 F.Supp.2d 246, 266 (S.D.N.Y.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:47F6-4320-0038-Y127-00000-00&context=1530671) (whatever the definition of dual agency under New York law, "it is well-established that the alleged dual agent's acts on behalf of the parties must not be 'merely ministerial,'" citing [_Bernstein v. Centaur Ins. Co., 644 F.Supp. 1361, 1370 (S.D.N.Y.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-8G70-0039-R1CV-00000-00&context=1530671); "\[m\]inisterial acts are those that 'involve\[\] obedience to instructions or laws instead of discretion, judgment, or skill,'" citing Black's Law Dictionary 1011 (7th ed. 1999)). The court's opinion earlier suggests that "dual agent" might be defined in two different ways: a person might be a "dual agent" only when the person undertakes to act on behalf of two principals, subject to the control of each, see [_241 F.Supp.2d at 267_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:47F6-4320-0038-Y127-00000-00&context=1530671); alternatively, an agent might become a "dual agent" by agreeing to "serve" another party in a manner inconsistent with the agent's duties to the principal, see id. See also [_Olsen v. Vail Assocs. Real Estate, Inc., 935 P.2d 975, 980-981 (Colo.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-YSJ0-003D-90HN-00000-00&context=1530671) (dual-agency relationship not present in sale of property when vendor's agent performed only "auxiliary or ministerial" tasks of a limited nature for purchaser of adjoining property, which included contacting owner, introducing him to purchaser, and assembling "'discussion draft contract'" because agent knew purchaser would not buy principal's property without simultaneously purchasing adjacent property; agent received no commission on sale of adjacent property).

Provisions in corporation statutes applicable to self-dealing transactions are often termed "safe-harbor" statutes because they specify a route through which a transaction may be insulated from successful challenges. See Principles of Corporate Governance: Analysis and Recommendations § 5.02, Comment _a._ Although safe-harbor provisions in some statutes extend to transactions between corporations and their "directors or officers," see Del. Code Ann., tit. 8, § 144, others are limited to transactions between corporations and their directors, see Rev. Model Bus. Corp. Act, subchapter F, Introductory Comment (applicable to directors only).

_c. Agent acts as or for an adverse party._ On transactions between a principal and an agent's close family members, see [_Arst v. Stifel, Nicolaus & Co., 954 F.Supp. 1483, 1494 (D.Kan.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-JGV0-00B1-F35R-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent who undertook to find private buyers for block of stock owned by plaintiff purchased a large number of shares for himself and his family but did not disclose identity of purchasers to plaintiff despite demand for disclosure, in contravention of SEC's rule 10b-10(a)(7)(i) and agent's fiduciary duty; "an agent violating the fundamental duty of loyalty by undisclosed self-dealing will not be permitted to evade that duty by the expedient of arranging the sale of the principal's property to a close family member. The rule precluding undisclosed self-dealing by an agent includes all indirect transactions in which the agent benefits from the transfer," including transfers to agent's spouse, sibling, child, or any close relative).

Illustration 4 is based on [_Miller v. Berkoski, 297 N.W.2d 334 (Iowa 1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2X70-003G-5294-00000-00&context=1530671). In _Miller,_ after the installment purchaser defaulted, the agents arranged a resale at a profit, which damaged the original seller through additional taxes and mortgage prepayment penalties. The court held that the agents' breach of duty terminated their right to receive a commission from the seller. See also _Hardy v. Davis, 164 A.2d 281, 282 (Md.1960)_ (agent's undisclosed loan to purchaser did not breach agent's duty to vendor; agent would breach duty by making loan or agreement to lend before vendor signs contract, which would conclude agency relationship); [_Sing v. John L. Scott, Inc., 948 P.2d 816 (Wash.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S65-MJM0-0039-4138-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent with whose firm property had been listed for sale, who purchased property herself by accepting counteroffer made by vendor, did not breach duty to vendor; agent also did not breach any duty to plaintiff, who made prior offer to purchase property). _Miller_ and the outcome stated for Illustration 4 are arguably at odds with a literal reading of one statement in Restatement Second, Agency § 391, Comment _b:_ "An agent can properly deal with the other party to a transaction if such dealing is not inconsistent with his duties to the principal. Thus, an agent employed to sell can properly lend money to the buyer to complete the purchase. . . ." In the installment-sale structure in _Miller_ and Illustration 4, it is material to the vendor's decision whether a prospective buyer is likely to default on the installment-payment obligations. The agent's interest, however, is to obtain the vendor's assent to a transaction and earn the promised commission and not to jeopardize the commission by revealing information about the buyer that may dissuade the vendor from assenting.

On an agent's adverse position more generally, see [_Merrill Lynch, Pierce, Fenner & Smith, Inc. v. Cheng, 901 F.2d 1124, 1129-1130 (D.C.Cir.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-5NX0-003B-517P-00000-00&context=1530671) (broker who made unauthorized trades in client's nondis-cretionary account "developed an interest inconsistent with that of the principal"; broker had duty fully to disclose all material facts concerning unauthorized transactions, including in particular all reasonably available courses of action open to principal); [_Roberts v. Lomanto, 5 Cal.Rptr. 3d 866 (Cal.App.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:49XJ-YDX0-0039-42TJ-00000-00&context=1530671) (real-estate agent breached fiduciary duty to property owner, from whom she purchased property, by failing to disclose that prior to purchase when property was still listed for sale with agent, agent agreed to assign contract to purchase property in exchange for \$ 1.2 million fee); [_American Motorists Ins. Co. v. American Ins. Co., 438 N.E.2d 368, 370-371 (Mass.App. 1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-G170-003C-V468-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent who represented multiple insurers breached fiduciary duty; insurer that had committed to protect building against loss from fire requested that agent reduce or replace its commitment, which agent did by reinsuring, dividing coverage among 5 other insurers he represented without disclosing his representation of first insurer or obtaining consent of reinsurers); [_In re Estate of Friedman, 407 N.Y.S.2d 999, 1008 (App.Div.1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-D0V0-003C-F3M5-00000-00&context=1530671) (art dealer to whom artist's widow consigned paintings had interests potentially adverse to widow's if agreement permitted dealer to hold out for a price far in excess of paintings' fair market value and deny widow any payment while retaining title to paintings; if treated as a sale of paintings, agreement unconscionable).

On Illustration 5 and an agent's transaction with a former principal, see, e.g., [_Dubbs v. Stribling & Assocs., 752 N.E.2d 850, 852 (N.Y.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:438C-G060-0039-435P-00000-00&context=1530671) (real-estate broker purchased apartment listed with broker, waiving any commission; broker did not breach fiduciary duty to vendors by also purchasing adjacent apartment without telling vendor that adjacent apartment had now become available when broker knew vendors had been interested in acquiring adjacent apartment; broker had a duty "to inform \[vendors\] of her intent to purchase their apartment and to disclose any information that could reasonably bear on \[vendors'\] consideration of her offer," and broker fulfilled duty "for there is no evidence in the record that \[broker\] withheld any relevant information that was in the record" when broker entered into purchase contract for apartment; thereafter, broker no longer characterized as vendors' agent, as vendors themselves recognized in purchase contract itself).

_d. Remedies._ On forfeiture as a remedy when an agent acts as a dual agent without the consent of both principals, see [_Darby v. Furman Co., 513 S.E.2d 848 (S.C.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3W1Y-7V10-0039-40C9-00000-00&context=1530671). On forfeiture as a remedy for an agent's self-dealing, see [_Zakibe v. Ahrens & McCarron, Inc., 28 S.W.3d 373, 386 (Mo.App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4101-6S40-0039-452M-00000-00&context=1530671) (forfeiture of rights to all compensation after commencement of wrongdoing; trial court did not err in treating breach of fiduciary duty as defense to claim for bonuses and severance pay due under contract).

On disgorgement of profits as a remedy for undisclosed self-dealing, see [_Arst, 954 F.Supp. at 1494_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-JGV0-00B1-F35R-00000-00&context=1530671) (duty to mitigate relevant only to claim for damages, not to disgorgement of profits).

On a principal's right to avoid a contract, see [_Sphere Drake Ins. Ltd. v. All Am. Life Ins. Co., 221 F.Supp.2d 874, 879-880 (N.D.Ill.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:453R-N0P0-0038-Y4XT-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent's self-dealing without knowledge or consent of principal makes transaction voidable at principal's option); [_American Motorists Ins. Co., 438 N.E.2d at 371_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-G170-003C-V468-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurance companies not bound by commitments to reinsure fire risk made on their behalf by agent acting at request of insurer on whose behalf agent had issued binder to protect against fire loss when agent did not disclose representation of insurer or obtain consent of reinsurers).

On the availability of monetary relief when a principal avoids a transaction, see [_Gosai v. Abeers Realty & Dev. Mktg., Inc., 605 S.E.2d 5, 9 (N.C.App.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4DKT-N9N0-0039-422H-00000-00&context=1530671) (rescission of note and deed of trust, plus award of monetary relief to principal equal to amount paid in interest on note and profits made by defendants in transaction; unbeknownst to principal who purchased property through real-estate broker acting as purchaser's agent, broker had previously purchased property and placed it in third party's name).
