[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 8.04_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8Y0-00YG-400T-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 8- Duties of Agent and Principal to Each Other_ > _Topic 1- Agent's Duties to Principal_ > _Title B- Duties of Loyalty_**

**§ 8.04 Competition**

**Throughout the duration of an agency relationship, an agent has a duty to refrain from competing with the principal and from taking action on behalf of or otherwise assisting the principal's competitors. During that time, an agent may take action, not otherwise wrongful, to prepare for competition following termination of the agency relationship.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section states the rule that an agent's fiduciary duty of loyalty requires that the agent refrain from competition with the principal during the agency relationship. The duty encompasses competitive action taken directly by the agent with the principal, actions taken by the agent on behalf of third-party competitors of the principal, and other assistance furnished by the agent to the principal's competitors. Comment _b_ discusses the general operation of the rule and the compromise between rival policies that it represents. Comment _c_ discusses an agent's right to compete following termination of an agency relationship, as well as preparations an agent may make to compete once an agency relationship has terminated.

A principal may consent to conduct by an agent that would otherwise breach this rule. See § 8.06, Comment c. An agent may agree to limitations on the agent's right to compete following termination of the agency relationship.

Section 8.05 applies to an agent's use of confidential information or property of the principal, whether for purposes of competition or otherwise. Section 8.03 is applicable when an agent acts as or on behalf of an adverse party in a transaction with the principal.

On the standard governing competition with a corporation by its directors and senior executives, see Principles of Corporate Governance: Analysis and Recommendations § 5.06.

_b. In general._ Action by an agent that competes with the principal's business or assists a competitor of the principal, when connected to the agency relationship, contravenes the general fiduciary principle stated in § 8.01 because it is contrary to the principal's interests, although the agent does not use the principal's property or confidential information. See Comment _c_ for discussion of the connection between an agency relationship and competitive action by an agent. Moreover, to the extent an agent has confidential information about the principal's present business or the principal's plans for the future, and to the extent an agent's duties enable the agent to make decisions that affect the principal's business, an agent who engages in competition does so from a position superior to third-party competitors of the principal. This is a risk that a principal may reduce but not eliminate through mechanisms that limit access to sensitive information. Such mechanisms may fail and, more generally, a degree of vulnerability to competitive betrayal is inherent in any relationship of agency, especially when a principal is an organization.

For a variety of reasons, an agent's competition with a principal may not, in fact, injure the principal. However, an agent's assessment of the risks that competition by the agent poses for the principal may often be shaded by the benefits that the agent anticipates through competition, as well as limited by the fact that the agent's knowledge of the principal's business plans and other interests will often be only partial. Thus, consistent with the basic fiduciary principle stated in § 8.01, the principal's consent is necessary for an agent to be free to compete during an agency relationship in matters connected with it. See § 8.06(1).

The prohibition against competing with the principal contemporaneously with an agency relationship is a widely recognized implication of an agent's fiduciary duty to the principal. Contemporary partnership legislation requires that a partner "refrain from competing with the partnership in the conduct of the partnership business before the dissolution of the partnership." Rev. Unif. Partnership Act § 404(b)(3). A partnership agreement may provide otherwise by identifying "specific types or categories of activities that do not violate the duty of loyalty, if not manifestly unreasonable" although the agreement may not eliminate a partner's duty of loyalty. Id. § 103(b)(3).

Competition as governed by this section is not limited to conduct subject to the law governing unfair competition or antitrust law. In determining whether an agent's conduct contravenes the rules stated in this section, the relevant objective to be served is facilitating compliance with and enforcement of the agent's fiduciary duty to the principal, as opposed to objectives that may be relevant only in other contexts.

Following termination of an agency relationship, the agent is free to compete with the principal, except to the extent that the agent has entered into an enforceable agreement not to compete with the principal. As a general matter, actors in a free-enterprise economy are free to compete with one another, subject to liability when specific regulatory rules are contravened. See Restatement Third, Unfair Competition § 1, Comment a. Moreover, competition by former employees and other agents furthers the general social interest in the benefits of a competitive economy because many new business enterprises are creations of people formerly employed by or otherwise associated with older enterprises.

However, the tactics that an agent may use in competing or preparing to compete are subject to legal limits. Without the principal's consent, an agent may not make use of property of the principal, see § 8.05(1), nor may the agent use confidential information contrary to the rule stated in § 8.05(2). An agent's preparations for competition during the agency relationship are also subject to limits. See Comment c. Moreover, a former agent, or an agent who contemplates post-termination competition, is not free to engage in behavior that is tortious or otherwise wrongful. The actions of individual and soon-to-be-former agents may become wrongful when they constitute concerted action designed with the purpose of leaving the principal in the lurch. See id.

An agent may agree to limit the agent's right to compete following termination of an agency relationship. An agent's promise to refrain from competition is unenforceable if it is unreasonably in restraint of trade. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 186(1). An agent's promise to refrain from competition may be enforceable as an ancillary restraint on competition. Id. § 188(2)(b) and Comment _g._ However, some states impose statutory restrictions in the context of employment relationships on the enforceability of post-termination restraints on competition. See Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code §§ 16600-16602; Colo. Rev. Stat. § 8-2-113(2).

As discussed in Comment c, the scope of an agency relationship limits the range of conduct to which an agent's duties are applicable. However, the rules stated in this section are applicable regardless of the nature of an agent's duties or position or the relative status that an agent or other employee occupies within an organization.

**Illustrations:**

1\. P Corporation, a food-products business, employs A as a traffic manager to arrange for the transportation of P Corporation's products, principally canned tuna and ham and fresh poultry. A's duties include making arrangements with common carriers, coordinating shipping schedules, negotiating shipping rates, and supervising warehouse employees who load trucks. A becomes familiar with the identity of P Corporation's suppliers, customers, and common carriers, as well as delivery routes and rates. Without telling anyone else at P Corporation, A organizes a business that A operates out of A's home, through which A sells fresh poultry. A has breached A's duty to P Corporation. A is competing with P Corporation in one of its lines of business.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that all business operations of A's home-based business are handled by E, the sole employee of A's business. Same result. A owns a business that is a direct competitor of P Corporation. In operating the business, E acts as A's agent.

An agent's competitive activity may consist, as in Illustrations 1 and 2, of actions that directly engage in a business activity that rivals the principal's activity. An agent's conduct also breaches the duty stated in this section when the agent acts on behalf of or furnishes assistance to a competitor of the principal.

**Illustration:**

3\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that A's home-based business consists of arranging for the transportation of food products to retailers in exchange for commissions. Some of A's customers are food distributors who are competitors of P Corporation. A has breached A's duty to P Corporation. Without P Corporation's knowledge or consent, A owns and operates a business that provides assistance to direct competitors of P Corporation.

It is not a defense to an agent that the agent honestly believes that competitive activity will not injure the principal. In Illustration 3, for example, A may honestly believe that any risk posed to P Corporation by A's side business is remote at best. However, P Corporation may reasonably believe that A's side business may jeopardize its interests because it increases the likelihood that competitors of P Corporation will learn the identity of its customers or of its shipping practices or of routes that have proven especially efficient. Thus, when an employee or other agent plans to embark upon a side business, contemporaneously with the agency relationship, that might conflict with the principal's interests, the agent should inform the principal of this fact so that the principal may assess the substantiality of the risks posed to its interests. See § 8.06.

A principal's consent to action by an agent that would otherwise breach the duty stated in this section may be explicit. A principal may also consent through implied understandings and known customs, including those normally associated with a type of agency relationship in which an agent may act on behalf of multiple customers or clients. For further discussion, see § 8.06, Comment _e._

_c. Post-termination competition; preparations to compete._ Once a relationship of agency has terminated, unless the agent has agreed otherwise the agent has the right to compete with the now-former principal. A former agent may use skills and more general knowledge, although learned in the course of work done for the former principal, in such competition. However, a former agent's right to compete with the principal is not absolute and does not privilege conduct that would be tortious if committed by a third party. A former agent remains subject to duties concerning confidential information and property of the principal. See § 8.05. If a former agent continues to have a confidential relationship with the principal, for example by furnishing advice to the principal on which the principal relies, the former agent may owe fiduciary duties to the principal derived from their confidential relationship that prohibit competition on that ground.

Many employees and other agents who compete with their former principals do not terminate the agency relationship before commencing activity requisite to their eventual competition. Different reasons, some benign and some troubling, may explain the frequency with which competitive preparations overlap with continued service. Some agents need the income provided by ongoing employment and do not wish it to end until the last possible moment, especially when their new venture is untested or its economic promise lies well into the future. Some agents may also wish a degree of certainty that their new competitive venture will be ready to operate, or that their association with an established competitor of the principal is secured, before cutting the tie to their current principal. Some agents also may derive an eventual competitive advantage from continued association with their present principal, for example from ongoing knowledge of the strengths and weaknesses of the principal's operations and business plans and ongoing association with key employees, customers, and suppliers of the principal.

In retrospect it may prove difficult to assess the propriety of a former agent's conduct because many actions may be proper or improper, depending on the intention with which the agent acted and the surrounding circumstances. For that reason it may be difficult to draw a clean distinction between actions prior to termination of an agency relationship that constitute mere preparation for competition, which do not contravene an employee's or other agent's duty to the principal, and actions that constitute competition.

In assessing events that are often ambiguous, it is possible to identify a few unambiguous starting points of analysis. An agent who plans to compete is free to make extramural arrangements for setting up a new business, such as incorporating a new firm and arranging for space and equipment. On the other hand, an agent or employee is not free, while still employed, to commence doing business as a competitor or to solicit customers away from the principal. Although these prohibitions apply to all agents regardless of their relative status within an organization, as a practical matter higher-level employees may occupy positions that enable them to inflict greater damage on a soon-to-be-former principal.

In general, an employee or other agent who plans to compete with the principal does not have a duty to disclose this fact to the principal. To be sure, the fact that an agent has such a plan is information that a principal would find useful, but the agent's fiduciary duty to the principal does not oblige the agent to make such disclosure. Nor does an agent's duty to provide facts to the principal as stated in § 8.11 require disclosure to the principal of an agent's competitive plans. In this respect, the social benefits of furthering competition outweigh the principal's interest in full disclosure by its agents.

However, an agent has a duty not to mislead the principal about the agent's intentions. An agent's silence may mislead the principal when, for example, the agent knows that the principal is about to embark on an expansion in the principal's business in which the agent will play a crucial role that will not easily be replicated once the agent departs. More generally, conduct that would constitute a tort if committed by a third party against the principal also is tortious if committed by an agent. Thus, neither a third party nor an agent may use a principal's property to compete with the principal without the principal's consent. See § 8.05.

Some actions that an agent may take that further the agent's competitive plan are not equally available to third-party competitors as a practical matter. For example, an agent's work may require the agent to develop and maintain good relationships with the principal's customers and suppliers; the agent knows their identity and has ongoing and specific associations with them that a third party external to the principal's organization would likely not have developed. An employee, especially one in a managerial position or one who works with a wide range of colleagues, also has an opportunity to assess their skills and motivation and potential contributions to a new or existing competitive venture from a vantage point that is inaccessible outside the employer's organization. Employees may be able to time their departures so that maximum competitive injury is inflicted on the employer because their knowledge of the employer's vulnerabilities at particular times is especially acute, enabling employees who make a concerted plan to depart to time their departure en masse so as to enhance the injury inflicted on the principal.

Apart from conduct that is clearly wrongful or clearly permitted, many actions of employees and other agents who continue their association with the principal while planning to compete are ambiguous, not because the underlying legal principles are unclear, but because their application requires careful assessment of all relevant factual circumstances. For example, when an agent's work requires that the agent cultivate ties with customers of the principal, it is not a breach of fiduciary duty for the agent to continue performing the assigned work. However, interactions between an agent and the customer through which the agent solicits the customer's patronage for the agent's new competitive association breach the agent's fiduciary duty to the principal. An agent who, without the principal's knowledge, plans to depart but also wishes to develop a relatively secure base of customers for the agent's new competitive association inevitably risks liability. This is because when elements of the agent's conduct are dissected in detail in litigation following the agent's departure, the dissection is performed with after-the-fact knowledge that the agent became a competitor of the principal, a fact that inevitably shades how the agent's predeparture conduct is interpreted.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency._ This section is the counterpart of Restatement Second, Agency §§ 393, 394, and 396(a). Although § 394 is formulated more broadly, requiring that an agent refrain from acting or agreeing to act "during the period of his agency for persons whose interests conflict with those of the principal in matters in which the agent is employed," the specific situations discussed in the section involve action taken on behalf of a principal's competitors. The formulation in this Restatement does not encompass an agent's agreement to act on behalf of or to assist a competitor. This is because such a formulation appears to contravene the well-settled right of employees and other agents to make preparations to compete. See Restatement Second, Agency § 393, Comment _e,_ which recognizes that "\[e\]ven before the termination of the agency," the agent "is entitled to make arrangements to compete, except that he cannot properly use confidential information peculiar to his employer's business and acquired therein." Comment _e_ continues, "\[t\]hus, before the end of his employment, he can properly purchase a rival business and upon termination of employment immediately compete." This Restatement does not endorse Comment e's categorical assertion that an agent's ownership of a competing business would not breach the agent's duties to the principal.

Sections 393, 394, and 396(a) begin with the prefatory language, "\[u\]nless otherwise agreed . . . ." In this Restatement, § 8.06 addresses on a comprehensive basis how a principal may consent to conduct by an agent that would otherwise constitute a breach of the agent's duties of loyalty.

_b. In general._ For the point that many new businesses are created by people who work for older businesses, see John J. Slain et al., Agency, Partnership and Employment 3-179 (1980) (observing that "the common pattern is that an employee of an existing business decides that he can do as well or better than the employer. The employee makes plans and arrangements, leaves and starts to compete.").

For the basic point that an employee or other agent may not compete with the principal during the agency relationship, see [_Chem-Trend Inc. v. McCarthy, 780 F.Supp. 458, 460 (E.D.Mich.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-8XV0-001T-71XB-00000-00&context=1530671) (employee who developed own competitive product while employed did more than prepare for competition; employee "had customers evaluate \[product\] for him so that he could determine whether to leave \[employer\] and enter into direct competition"; court denies employee's motion for summary judgment, predicting that employee will "have a tough time factually establishing the distinction between customers testing \[employee's\] product and \[employee\] promoting the product"); [_T.A. Pelsue Co. v. Grand Enters., Inc., 782 F.Supp. 1476, 1486-1487 (D.Colo.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-8N00-001T-70H7-00000-00&context=1530671) (while employee, defendant who founded competing company designed, manufactured, and sold products that competed with employer's products); [_Veco Corp. v. Babcock, 611 N.E.2d 1054, 1059 (Ill. App.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-38N0-003D-H53H-00000-00&context=1530671) (former employees may compete with former employer "so long as there was no demonstrable business activity by the former employee before the termination . . . . In general, employees may plan, form, and outfit a competing corporation while still working for the employer but may not commence competition."). See also [_Material Supply Int'l, Inc. v. Sunmatch Indus. Co., 146 F.3d 983, 993 (D.C.Cir.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3T4H-YXF0-0038-X1XN-00000-00&context=1530671) (sales agent used principal's trademark to solicit sales for its own private label, diverting sales that would otherwise have gone to principal).

For the point that no general duty to refrain from competition exists absent an agency relationship, see [_Gann v. Williams Bros. Realty, Inc., 283 Cal.Rptr. 128, 133 (Cal.App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-H8P0-003D-J0J4-00000-00&context=1530671) (real-estate agent did not breach duty to developer by acquiring property for development itself when no agency existed; relationship contingent on developer taking title to property). If an enforceable covenant in an agreement bars competition by an agent with a former principal, the former agent's duty survives for the covenant's duration. See, e.g., [_Labor Ready, Inc. v. Williams Staffing, LLC, 149 F.Supp.2d 398, 414-415 (N.D.Ill.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:438B-M9C0-0038-Y25F-00000-00&context=1530671).

On the applicability of the basic fiduciary duty regardless of an agent's status within an organization, see [_Foodcomm Int'l v. Barry, 328 F.3d 300, 302 (7th Cir.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48H4-8420-0038-X2CF-00000-00&context=1530671) (sales employees breached duty by forming competing business with employer's chief customer); [_Eckard Brandes, Inc. v. Riley, 338 F.3d 1082, 1085-1086 (9th Cir.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:498N-9RH0-0038-X42H-00000-00&context=1530671), cert. denied, _541 U.S. 1009 (2004)_ (employees of pipe maintenance and repair firm breached fiduciary duty by setting up competing firm and soliciting customers by bidding against employer for contract with county); [_FryeTech, Inc. v. Harris, 46 F.Supp.2d 1144, 1152 (D.Kan.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3W6D-XFG0-0038-Y0BP-00000-00&context=1530671) (action to replevy equipment used by former employees in competing business; "\[w\]hile most of the cases which have addressed the fiduciary responsibilities of agents under Kansas law have involved corporate officers and directors, there is no basis for concluding that these are the only types of agents subject to fiduciary duties . . . . the cases speak of the duties of agents without respect to their exact status."); [_E.J. McKernan Co. v. Gregory, 623 N.E.2d 981, 993 (Ill.App.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2XD0-003D-H369-00000-00&context=1530671) (although employees may take preparatory steps toward competition, "an employee is held accountable for breaching his fiduciary duty to his employer when he goes beyond such preliminary competitive activities and commences business as a rival concern while still employed . . . . An employee need not be an officer or a director to be accountable since an agent must act solely for the principal in all matters related to the agency and refrain from competing with the principal").

On the outcome stated for Illustration 2, see Slain et al. at 3-180 to 3-181 ("\[i\]t would seem that the ownership of a significant interest in a business which is competitive with one's employer while one is still employed would not be preparation to compete after employment but competition during employment, conduct impermissible on any analysis. Employment contracts commonly define the ownership interest of a 5% interest in a competing business as current competition."). In contrast, Restatement Second, Agency § 393, Comment _e,_ in discussing a soon-to-be-former agent's preparations for competition, states that "before the end of his employment, he can properly purchase a rival business and upon termination of employment immediately compete." This statement, which does not appear to be supported by any authority, disregards the inconsistency between the general prohibition on competition with the principal during the tenure of an agency relationship in § 393 and ownership of a currently competitive "rival" of the principal.

A related line of cases involve competition by a parent corporation against a firm with an agency relationship with the parent's subsidiary. See [_Gossard v. Adia Servs., Inc., 723 So. 2d 182 (Fla.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3TWD-X080-0039-40PH-00000-00&context=1530671) (parent corporation that acquired firm that had granted plaintiff exclusive franchise-rights agreement within geographic territory responsible for plaintiffs competitive damages when a subsidiary of parent began to compete with plaintiff); [_King's Daughters & Sons Circle No. Two v. Delta Reg'l. Med. Ctr., 856 So. 2d 600 (Miss.App.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48W5-05C0-0039-43VR-00000-00&context=1530671) (board of hospital managed by subsidiary acted in good faith in opposing acquisition of competing hospital by parent corporation).

Illustration 3 is based on [_Cameco, Inc. v. Gedicke, 724 A.2d 783 (N.J. 1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3VV0-4YS0-0039-41W3-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Foodcomm, 328 F.3d at 302-303_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48H4-8420-0038-X2CF-00000-00&context=1530671) (sales employees became aware that principal's chief customer would no longer do business with it and formed competing business with now-former customer; court upholds district court's grant of injunction prohibiting former employees from providing services to new firm); [_Martin Pincus Mktg. v. Sawyer of Napa, Inc., 774 F.Supp. 171 (S.D.N.Y.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-B680-001T-72NG-00000-00&context=1530671) (manufacturer's exclusive sales representative breached duty to manufacturer by undertaking representation without disclosing its intention to represent a competing manufacturer; failure to make disclosure constituted fraud in inducement of contract with manufacturer, relieving manufacturer of duty to pay commissions to representative). However, an agent's duty may be less than absolute because the agent's position may implicate interests of third parties that outweigh those of the principal. For example, in [_Johnson v. Brewer & Pritchard, P.C., 73 S.W.3d 193, 202-203 (Tex. 2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45D9-75F0-0039-452D-00000-00&context=1530671), the court held that a law-firm associate did not breach the associate's fiduciary duty to the firm by gratuitously referring a prospective client to another law firm when the associate's employer had little experience in handling cases comparable to the prospective client's.

Of course, a principal may consent to actions that would otherwise breach an agent's duty. See, e.g., [_Warrantech Corp. v. Computer Adapters Servs., Inc., 134 S.W.3d 516 (Tex.App.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4C42-BVD0-0039-429F-00000-00&context=1530671) (computer-repair company had no policy proscribing moonlighting by its employees, either on their own or for repair firms that did business with company; president of corporate affiliate directed subordinate to refrain from scheduling employees' work in manner that would interfere with their moonlighting).

For an assessment of the possible impact of differences in the enforceability of covenants not to compete on technological innovation and economic growth, see Ronald J. Gilson, The Legal Infrastructure of High Technology Industrial Districts: Silicon Valley, Route 128, and Covenants Not to Compete, [_74 N.Y.U. L. Rev. 575 (1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3WTN-XGM0-00CW-707S-00000-00&context=1530671).

In some cases it is argued that the court should enjoin a former employee or other agent who is not bound by a covenant not to compete from working for a competitor of the principal on the basis that it is inevitable that the agent will inevitably disclose confidential information of the principal. See, e.g., [_EarthWeb, Inc. v. Schlack, 71 F.Supp.2d 299 (S.D.N.Y.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XSH-TM70-0038-Y29H-00000-00&context=1530671), remanded for more specific findings, _205 F.3d 1322 (2d Cir.2000)_ (stating factors relevant to whether injunction should be granted). Many cases applying the doctrine involve the prospect of disclosure of trade secrets by employees with "expertise in highly technical industries." [_International Paper Co. v. Suwyn, 966 F.Supp. 246, 258 (S.D.N.Y.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-8JD0-00B1-F36Y-00000-00&context=1530671). For further discussion, see David Linicum, Inevitable Conflict?: California's Policy of Worker Mobility and the Doctrine of "Inevitable Disclosure," [_75 S. Cal. L. Rev. 1257 (2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:46MT-THV0-00CV-502T-00000-00&context=1530671); Jennifer L. Saulino, Locating Inevitable Disclosure's Place in Trade Secrets Analysis, [_100 Mich. L. Rev. 1184 (2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:46G5-0XK0-00CV-502J-00000-00&context=1530671).

_c. Post-termination competition; preparations to compete._ For the point that an employee's failure to disclose plans to compete does not, by itself, constitute a breach of fiduciary duty, see [_Allied Supply Co. v. Brown, 585 So. 2d 33, 35 (Ala.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-XCY0-003C-918D-00000-00&context=1530671) (recognizing that employee has no fiduciary duty to disclose plans to resign, when resignation followed by competition with former employer; "\[i\]n addition, it would be unjust to require an employee-at-will to give his employer advance notice of his resignation without imposing a reciprocal duty on the employer to give notice of its intent to terminate employment."); [_Maryland Metals, Inc. v. Metzner, 382 A.2d 564, 573 (Md.1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-52C0-003G-24RJ-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Abetter Trucking Co. v. Arizpe, 113 S.W.3d 503, 509 (Tex.App.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:490B-MSM0-0039-43GC-00000-00&context=1530671).

For the point that setting up a business in anticipation of competing does not constitute a breach of fiduciary duty, see [_Mercer Mgmt. Consulting, Inc. v. Wilde, 920 F.Supp. 219, 235 (D.D.C.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-R570-006F-P4TD-00000-00&context=1530671) (stealth in setting up competing firm does not constitute breach of fiduciary duty); [_Instrument Repair Serv., Inc. v. Gunby, 518 S.E.2d 161, 164 (Ga.App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3X34-GW30-0039-40VT-00000-00&context=1530671) (no unlawful means used in setting up lawfully competing business). Kentucky may be an exception when the prospective competitor is a director or an officer. See [_Steelvest, Inc. v. Scansteel Serv. Ctr., 807 S.W.2d 476, 483 (Ky.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX8-C710-003F-P18B-00000-00&context=1530671) ("directors and officers of a corporation may not set up, or attempt to set up, an enterprise which is competitive with the business in which the corporation is engaged while still serving as directors and officers . . . . they should terminate their position/status as directors or officers when they first make arrangements or begin preparations to compete directly with the employer corporation."). But see Principles of Corporate Governance: Analysis and Recommendations § 5.06, Comment _e_ ("The mere fact that the director or senior executive makes preparations to compete before resigning from office is … not a breach of duty. It is the nature of the preparations and the extent to which they substantially hinder the business of the corporation that are significant.").

Although an agent has no general duty to disclose the agent's competitive intentions, some authority suggests that an agent has a duty to make disclosure in "situations in which silence on the part of the agent places the principal at a disadvantage in its dealings with its own agent." [_CTC Commc'ns., Inc. v. Bell Atl. Corp., 14 F.Supp.2d 133, 143 (D.Me. 1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3TCN-KVJ0-0038-Y433-00000-00&context=1530671) (sales agent who sold services provided by telecommunications company disclosed the possibility that it might become a reseller of services in negotiating term of agency agreement dealing with post-termination competition).

On predeparture solicitation of customers, see, e.g., [_American Express Fin. Advisors, Inc. v. Topel, 38 F.Supp.2d 1233, 1247 (D.Colo.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3W18-JD00-0038-Y3F4-00000-00&context=1530671) (financial planner solicited customers for new venture while still employed by plaintiff, in some cases ignoring customers' requests to keep investments with plaintiff and in one instance on plaintiff's letterhead; court grants summary judgment for plaintiff on claim for breach of fiduciary duty); [_T.A. Pelsue Co. v. Grand Enters., Inc., 782 F.Supp. 1476, 1486-1487 (D.Colo.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-8N00-001T-70H7-00000-00&context=1530671) (plaintiffs former executive vice-president solicited plaintiff's customers both before and after employment with plaintiff ended).

On predeparture solicitation of employees, see, e.g., [_Riad v. 520 S. Michigan Ave. Assocs. Ltd., 78 F.Supp.2d 748, 763 (N.D.Ill.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XHX-S8D0-0038-Y10D-00000-00&context=1530671) (employee would breach fiduciary duty under some circumstances if employee "solicits his employer's customers or entices co-workers away from his employer"; court grants former employee's motion to strike employer's counterclaim when employer did not allege that any employees actually quit, noting that poor conditions of employer's business suggested former employee's discussion of other employment options with former colleagues might simply have been "long-term career counseling to his charges"); [_GAB Bus. Servs., Inc. v. Lindsey & Newsom Claim Servs., Inc., 99 Cal.Rptr.2d 665, 675 (Cal. App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4130-V1N0-0039-42K1-00000-00&context=1530671) (former officer while employed "used his insider's knowledge of employee skills and salaries to recruit valued employees away from the corporation he owed a fiduciary duty to, and into jobs with the corporation's competitor"; although jury might have believed that defendant did not truly solicit employees, all in the process of leaving on their own or being terminated, a jury properly instructed on defendant's fiduciary duty would have found a breach of duty); [_Reading Radio, Inc. v. Fink, 833 A.2d 199, 211 (Pa.Super.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:49K0-7T60-0039-40D1-00000-00&context=1530671), appeal denied, _847 A.2d 1287 (Pa. 2004)_ (former station manager of radio station "actively diverted" fellow employees to competing station and, while still employed by plaintiff, refused to enforce covenants not to compete to which former fellow employees were bound).

Some cases discuss a "higher duty" applicable to corporate officers and directors, but in a context in which the position held has enabled the defendant to inflict substantial competitive damage. See, e.g., [_Veco Corp. v. Babcock, 611 N.E.2d 1054, 1059 (Ill. App.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-38N0-003D-H53H-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporate officers "owe a fiduciary duty of loyalty to their employer not to (1) actively exploit their positions within the corporation for their own personal benefit, or (2) hinder the ability of a corporation to continue the business for which it was developed"; defendant officer successfully solicited, for competing venture, all employees requisite to present employer's ability to service major account); [_E.J. McKernan Co. v. Gregory, 623 N.E.2d 981, 994-995 (Ill.App. 1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2XD0-003D-H369-00000-00&context=1530671) (directors held positions that "rendered them especially susceptible to breaches of fiduciary duty"; jury could have found that defendants purposely allowed company's lease to lapse into month-to-month tenancy in order to negotiate yearly leasehold for their own company following resignation); [_Shepherd of the Valley Lutheran Church v. Hope Valley Church, 626 N.W.2d 436 (Minn.App. 2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:430X-2GW0-0039-42FG-00000-00&context=1530671) (vice-president of church organized faction of church members to form new directly competitive church, following unsuccessful attempt to remove minister; vice-president changed locks on church building, necessitating conduct of church services elsewhere by members not allied with vice-president's faction).

On the use of deception to advance anticipated competition, see [_Jennette v. National Cmty. Dev. Servs., Inc., 520 S.E.2d 231, 234 (Ga. App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XM3-7490-0039-431X-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent for fundraiser diverted business to himself by creating impression his new firm was an affiliate of employer; he did not merely compete with a former employer but "deliberately used deception and misrepresentation to obtain for himself business and funds intended for the alleged principal.").

On the prospect that agents who plan to depart for a competitive venture will wish more rather than less security in business arrangements, see Slain et al. at 3-180: "It seems unlikely in the extreme that anyone would invest in, lend to, or quit another job to work for, the new agency, unless there were some basis for believing that it had a reasonable chance of success. If, as would often be the case, the new business will necessarily compete for the prior employer's customers, some kind of communication with them while plans are still inchoate would seem unavoidable." John J. Slain et al, Agency, Partnership & Employment at 3-180.

On arguable overlaps between an agent's continuation of work and impermissible competitive activity during employment, compare [_Mercer Mgmt. Consulting, Inc. v. Wilde, 920 F.Supp. 219, 234 (D.D.C.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-R570-006F-P4TD-00000-00&context=1530671) (following bench trial, court enters judgment for former employee on breach-of-fiduciary-duty claim, given absence of overt solicitations made to employer's clients or other improper actions; although former employer argued employee continued work for clients in an aim to "solidify \[\] relationships to inure to the benefit of" employee's new competitive firm, court "is not persuaded that an employee breaches his fiduciary duty by performing work for his employer at a time when he is planning to leave his employment") with [_Furash & Co. v. McClave, 130 F.Supp.2d 48, 53-54 (D.D.C.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4280-SWK0-0038-Y2MG-00000-00&context=1530671) (denying summary judgment for defendants; former CEO of consulting firm allegedly job-hunted while conducting merger talks on behalf of former employer and solicited work for new employer from clients of present employer "by inducing client loyalty to her personally rather than to the company"; CEO also allegedly timed departure to inflict maximum harm on former employer, just before a pending merger, which failed, as did former employer). See also [_Western Med. Consultants, Inc. v. Johnson, 835 F.Supp. 554, 559 (D.Or.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-H810-001T-64R3-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_80 F.3d 1331 (9th Cir.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-2NN0-006F-M12B-00000-00&context=1530671) (following bench trial, court finds that former employee of firm that provided independent medical examinations did not breach fiduciary duty although employee made business trips to visit with present and prospective customers in Alaska, the geographic focus of new firm created by former employee; no evidence that while in Alaska, employee "did not market \[employer's\] services exclusively … \[or\] ever indicated to any existing or potential customers that she would be starting a business in competition with \[employer\], nor did she in any way indicate that these entities should look to any entity other than \[employer\]" for examination services).

Although permissible predeparture activity conventionally is said to include arranging financing for a new firm, for a possible qualification see [_Steelvest, Inc. v. Scansteel Serv. Ctr., Inc., 807 S.W.2d 476, 484 (Ky.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX8-C710-003F-P18B-00000-00&context=1530671) (court holds summary judgment for defendants improperly granted, noting, inter alia, that former corporate officer who formed competitive venture "sought legal and accounting advice, made activities to acquire bank financing, and recruited investors, two of whom, coincidentally, were chief executive officers of major customers of \[employer\].").

For relatively extreme examples of actions taken by former agents that proved injurious to former employers, see [_Furash & Co., 130 F.Supp.2d at 53-54_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4280-SWK0-0038-Y2MG-00000-00&context=1530671) (firm's former CEO allegedly used merger negotiations as job-hunting occasion and timed announcement of departure to doom both merger negotiations and firm, which soon became insolvent); [_Dowd & Dowd, Ltd. v. Gleason, 816 N.E.2d 754 (Ill.App. 2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4D9X-SK60-0039-408W-00000-00&context=1530671) (partners with undisclosed plan to leave law firm for newly established firm caused old firm to make payments on its line of credit on which partners were personal guarantors in order to make new firm more attractive to lender; partners additionally depleted old firm's cash reserves through large cash bonuses for themselves and their allies); [_Comedy Cottage, Inc. v. Berk, 495 N.E.2d 1006 (Ill.App.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-4P40-0054-H030-00000-00&context=1530671) (manager of comedy club, negotiating for new lease for premises, entered into new month-to-month lease without permission of club's owner that listed himself as lessee; when lease terminated, manager resigned, negotiated new lease with landlord, and set up competing comedy club in same premises); [_Duane Jones & Co. v. Burke, 117 N.E.2d 237 (N.Y.1954)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-XJF0-003C-C2C4-00000-00&context=1530671) (advertising firm's officers and employees planned departure en masse without any warning to firm's majority stockholder and chairman, having invited clients to follow); [_Feddeman & Co., C.P.A., P.C. v. Langan Assocs., P.C., 530 S.E.2d 668, 673 (Va.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40GB-5CY0-0039-402P-00000-00&context=1530671) (directors and employees of accounting firm planned mass walkout if firm's major shareholder rejected their buyout offer, knowing that walkout would "'be devastating to'" firm, having secured agreement of 50% of firm's clients to transfer business to new competing firm).

On misleading conduct by an agent who plans to join a competitor of the principal, see [_Bancroft-Whitney Co. v. Glen, 411 P.2d 921 (Cal.1966)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRK-M570-003C-H1XD-00000-00&context=1530671) (officer of corporation misled employer into believing that larger competitor would not raid employer's personnel; officer suggested that colleagues' salaries be raised in two-step plan without disclosing plan to offer employment to present colleagues if his own negotiations with competitor succeeded).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 8.05_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8Y0-00YG-400V-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 8- Duties of Agent and Principal to Each Other_ > _Topic 1- Agent's Duties to Principal_ > _Title B- Duties of Loyalty_**

**§ 8.05 Use of Principal's Property; Use of Confidential Information**

**An agent has a duty**

**(1) not to use property of the principal for the agent's own purposes or those of a third party; and**

**(2) not to use or communicate confidential information of the principal for the agent's own purposes or those of a third party.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section states rules applicable to an agent's use of the principal's property and use or communication of the principal's confidential information. Comment _b_ discusses an agent's duty to refrain from using property of the principal for purposes of the agent or a third party without the principal's consent. Comment _c_ covers an agent's duties concerning confidential information, including trade secrets of the principal.

A principal may consent to action by an agent that would otherwise contravene the agent's duties as stated in this section. See § 8.06.

An agent is also subject to duties of management regarding the principal's property as stated in § 8.12.

Section 8.04 states an agent's duty to refrain from competition with the principal during an agency relationship.

For the standard applicable to directors and senior executives of a corporation, see Principles of Corporate Governance: Analysis and Recommendations § 5.04.

_b. Use of principal's property._ An agent who has possession of property of the principal has a duty to use it only on the principal's behalf, unless the principal consents to such use. See § 8.06. This rule is a specific application of an agent's basic fiduciary duty stated in § 8.01. See § 8.01, Comment _b,_ for discussion. The rule is also a corollary of a principal's right, as an owner of property, to exclude usage by others. An agent is subject to this duty whether or not the agent uses property of the principal to compete with the principal or causes harm to the principal through the use. An agent may breach this duty even when the agent's use is beneficial in some sense to the property or to the principal. An agent is subject to liability to the principal for any profit made by the agent while using the principal's property when the use facilitates making the profit, or otherwise for the value of the use.

**Illustrations:**

1\. P, who owns a stable of horses, employs A to take care of them. While P is absent for a month, and without P's consent, A rents the horses to persons who ride them. Although being ridden is beneficial to the horses, A is subject to liability to P for the amount A receives for the rentals.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that A permits A's friends to ride P's horses for free during P's absence. A is subject to liability to P for the value of the use made of the horses.

An agent's breach of the rules stated in subsections (1) and (2) often accompanies a breach of another fiduciary duty that the agent owes the principal, although no such breach is present in Illustrations 1 and 2. In particular, an agent may use a principal's property without the principal's consent in the course of breaching the agent's duty by competing with the principal, see § 8.04, or developing a business opportunity that should not have been taken personally by the agent, see § 8.02, Comment _d._ If an agent's use of a principal's property causes harm to the principal or the property, the agent is subject to liability to the principal. See § 8.01, Comment _d(1)._

Termination of an agency relationship does not end an agent's duties regarding property of the principal. A former agent who continues to possess property of a principal has a duty to return it and to comply with the management and record-keeping rules stated in § 8.12.

**Illustrations:**

3\. P Corporation, which manufactures carbon paper, employs A as production manager of a plant. A's supervisor, M, the plant's general manager, directs A to dismantle and scrap manufacturing equipment. After dismantling the equipment, A stores the pieces in a friend's warehouse instead of scrapping them. Later, after A determines to form a business to compete with P Corporation, A retrieves the pieces of equipment, reassembles them, resigns from P Corporation, and begins to manufacture and sell carbon paper in competition with P Corporation. A has breached A's duty to P Corporation. By not scrapping the dismantled equipment as M directed, A made use of it without P Corporation's consent. P Corporation's remedies against A include replevying the equipment.

4\. Same facts as Illustration 3, except that A resigns from P Corporation prior to retrieving and reassembling the equipment. Same result.

An agent who wrongfully obtains or continues to possess property of a principal is not entitled to recover compensation for improvements the agent makes to the property. Thus, in Illustrations 3 and 4, the fact that A has improved the equipment does not bar P's right to recover possession of it, nor does it entitle A to compensation for the cost or value of the improvements.

For purposes of this rule, "property" of a principal is defined broadly and includes intangible as well as tangible assets.

**Illustration:**

5\. P Corporation employs A as a manager. Disgruntled by the circumstances of A's employment at P Corporation, A, an alert reader of legal notices, discovers that the state in which P Corporation was incorporated has dissolved it because it neglected to file its annual report with the state. A files a document with the state reserving the name "P Corporation," for the purpose of increasing A's leverage in negotiations over the terms of A's employment. A knows that it will not be possible for P Corporation to reincorporate under its original name and anticipates that it will be expensive and commercially inconvenient for P Corporation to resume doing business using another name. A has breached A's duty to P Corporation. A misappropriated its name.

_c. Confidential information._ Many employees and other agents are given access by the principal to information that the principal would not wish to be revealed or used, except as the principal directs. Such information may pertain to the principal's business plans, personnel, nonpublic financial results, and operational practices, among a range of possibilities. The value of some types of confidential information is recognized by trade-secret law, which protects "any information that can be used in the operation of a business or other enterprise and that is sufficiently valuable and secret to afford an actual or potential economic advantage over others." Restatement Third, Unfair Competition § 39.

An agent may, additionally, acquire confidential information about a principal or otherwise in the course of an agency relationship that does not have competitive or other economic value. For example, in the context of a lawyer-client relationship, confidential client information encompasses all information, not generally known, that relates to representation of the client. See Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 59. An agent's relationship with a principal may result in the agent learning information about the principal's health, life history, and personal preferences that the agent should reasonably understand the principal expects the agent to keep confidential. An agent's duty of confidentiality extends to all such information concerning a principal even when it is not otherwise connected with the subject matter of the agency relationship.

An agent's duty of confidentiality is not absolute. An agent may reveal otherwise privileged information to protect a superior interest of the agent or a third party. Thus, an agent may reveal to law-enforcement authorities that the principal is committing or is about to commit a crime. An agent's privilege to reveal such information also protects the agent's revelation to a private party who is being or will be harmed by the principal's illegal conduct. Many statutes provide protection against termination to employees who engage in "whistle-blowing."

An agent's duties concerning confidential information do not end when the agency relationship terminates. An agent is not free to use or disclose a principal's trade secrets or other confidential information whether the agent retains a physical record of them or retains them in the agent's memory. If information is otherwise a trade secret or confidential, the means by which an agent appropriates it for later use or disclosure should be irrelevant. Feats of human memory, however commendable and intriguing in many respects, should not be privileged as instruments of disloyal conduct.

**Illustrations:**

6\. P, who owns a commercial cleaning service, maintains a list of customers and prospective customers, noting particulars about each. P's list would be of competitive use to others. P maintains the list on a computer in P's office and restricts access to high-level employees within P's organization. A, P's general manager, who wishes to establish a competing cleaning service, retains a hard copy of the list that P gave to A to use in A's work. A resigns, taking the list and planning to use it to solicit business for A's new competing firm. A has breached A's duty to P.

7\. Same facts as Illustration 6, except that A commits the list to memory, memorizing a portion each day and then typing that portion into A's home computer each evening. Same result.

An agent's use of the principal's confidential information for the agent's own purposes breaches the agent's duty as stated in subsection (2) although the agent's use of the information does not necessitate revealing it. Thus, it is a breach of an agent's duty to use confidential information of the principal for the purpose of effecting trades in securities although the agent does not reveal the information in the course of trading.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency, and codifications._ This section is the counterpart to Restatement Second, Agency §§ 395, 396, 398, and 404.

Under Ga. Code § 10-6-25 (2000 & Supp. 2005), "\[t\]he agent shall not make a personal profit from his principal's property; for all such he is bound to account." Section 10-6-27 provides that "\[a\] principal may follow his money deposited by an agent in the latter's name and recover the same wherever found, unless the rights of innocent third parties have intervened."

La. Civ. Code art. 3004 (2005) states that "\[t\]he mandatary is bound to deliver to the principal everything he received by virtue of the mandate, including things he received unduly. The mandatary may retain in his possession sufficient property of the principal to pay the mandatary's expenses and remuneration." Under art. 3005, "\[t\]he mandatary owes interest, from the date used, on sums of money of the principal that the mandatary applies to his own use."

_b. Use of principal's property._ Illustration 1 is derived from Restatement Second, Agency § 404, Illustration 1. For the basic point that unless the principal consents, an agent may not use property of the principal for the agent's own purposes or those of a third party, see, e.g., [_Cwikla v. Sheir, 801 N.E.2d 1103, 1111-1112 (Ill.App.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4B6M-W640-0039-42S9-00000-00&context=1530671) (corporation's treasurer breached fiduciary duty by diverting corporate funds to his mother-in-law). An agent's role and functions may shape how a court characterizes the agent's duty in handling property of the principal. Compare [_Bridge-stone/Firestone, Inc. v. Recovery Credit Servs., Inc., 98 F.3d 13, 20 (2d Cir.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-09X0-006F-M2MF-00000-00&context=1530671) (collection agent for credit-card issuer who failed to remit monies collected on issuer's account owed no obligations beyond those fixed by contract; agent exercised no discretion and was not relied upon for advice) with [_Moses v. Martin, 360 F.Supp.2d 533, 543-544 (S.D.N.Y. 2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4F02-0PW0-TVW3-P2ST-00000-00&context=1530671) (managing agency breached fiduciary duty to its principal, a celebrity wardrobe stylist, by retaining funds collected from stylist's clients and misrepresenting whether monies were owed to stylist; in contrast to collection agent in _Bridgestone/Firestone,_ managing agency had discretion to negotiate terms with stylist's clients on basis of its superior knowledge of industry).

For discussion of the difficulties in defining "property," see Laura S. Underkuffler, The Idea of Property: Its Meaning and Power (2003).

Illustrations 3 and 4 are based on a simplified version of [_FryeTech, Inc. v. Harris, 46 F.Supp.2d 1144, 1157 (D.Kan.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3W6D-XFG0-0038-Y0BP-00000-00&context=1530671) (former employees violated duty to employer by surreptitiously procuring property of employer; employer legally entitled to possession of equipment, unlawfully detained by defendants; improvements made to equipment by former employees neither bar replevin nor entitle former employees to compensation, citing Restatement of Restitution § 158, Comment _d_). On an agent's duty to return property held on the principal's behalf, see also [_Annesley v. Tricentrol Oil Trading, Inc., 841 S.W.2d 908 (Tex.App.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-1H30-003C-23PJ-00000-00&context=1530671), abrogated on other grounds, [_35 S.W.3d 61 (2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:414W-WYS0-0039-40FS-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent who held seats on commodities exchange on principal's behalf had duty to transfer seats to principal's designate). See also [_Foodcomm Int'l v. Barry, 328 F.3d 300, 302 (7th Cir.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48H4-8420-0038-X2CF-00000-00&context=1530671) (sales employees used computers and PDAs provided by employer to prepare business plan to submit to employer's chief customer, whom employees approached with inquiry about its interest in employing them to set up business to compete with employer).

Illustration 5 is based on a variation of the facts in [_Rexford Rand Corp. v. Ancel, 58 F.3d 1215 (7th Cir.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-DCB0-001T-D31H-00000-00&context=1530671). In _Rexford Rand,_ the corporation's name was reserved, following its administrative dissolution, by a minority shareholder who had been fired from his positions as an officer. The court observed: "\[shareholder\] may have been the victim of oppressive activity, and he may have believed that reserving the \[corporation's\] name for his own use would induce \[other shareholders\] to buy out his stock at a fair price. \[Shareholder's\] desire to obtain a fair buyout is not itself objectionable. . . . \[t\]he method by which he sought to induce a settlement, however, is troubling. By appropriating the corporate name, \[shareholder\] threatened to cause serious damage to the well-being of the corporation . . . . as a shareholder in a close corporation, \[shareholder\] should have placed the interests of the corporation above his personal interests." [_Id. at 1220_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-DCB0-001T-D31H-00000-00&context=1530671). For another application of the same point, see [_Mid-List Press v. Nora, 374 F.3d 690, 693 (8th Cir.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4CTM-P1J0-0038-X0HR-00000-00&context=1530671) (president of not-for-profit publishing company, wishing to market self-published book of poetry through Internet merchant, affixes publisher's ISBN number and name to book without approval of any other officer or director of publisher and despite editors' refusal to publish book unless president-author submitted it for consideration under company's program for first-time poets; president's unauthorized use of publisher's name and ISBN number violated Lanham Act and constituted a self-dealing use of corporate property, court noting that "we can see how publication of the corporate president's own book without following normal procedures could seriously tarnish \[publisher's\] reputation as a nonprofit publisher, and thus run counter to the corporation's interests.").

_c. Confidential information._ For the basic point that an agent has a duty not to use confidential information acquired in the agent's employment with the principal to compete with the principal, either during their relationship or following its termination, see [_ABKCO, Inc. v. Harrisongs Music, Inc., 722 F.2d 988, 994 (2d Cir.1983)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-Y750-003B-G2RF-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Western Med. Consultants, Inc. v. Johnson, 835 F.Supp. 554, 558 (D.Or.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-H810-001T-64R3-00000-00&context=1530671); [_David Welch Co. v. Erskine & Tully, 250 Cal.Rptr. 339 (Cal.App.1988)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-JC00-003D-J55G-00000-00&context=1530671). On the use of confidential information outside the context of competition with the principal, see, e.g., [_Hollinger Int'l, Inc. v. Black, 844 A.2d 1022, 1061 (Del.Ch. 2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4BVK-CY60-0039-42VY-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_872 A.2d 559 (Del.2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4G0V-9S90-0039-42H1-00000-00&context=1530671) (controlling shareholder breached fiduciary duty of loyalty, inter alia, by "improperly using confidential information belonging to \[corporation\] to advance his own personal interests and not those of \[corporation\], without authorization from his fellow directors"; controlling shareholder used confidential advice and nonpublic financial information to negotiate sale to third party of key corporate assets without knowledge of fellow directors on corporation's board, with whom controlling shareholder had previously entered into restructuring agreement that contemplated transaction for the equal and ratable benefit of all shareholders).

Not all information about present or prospective customers is protected as a trade secret. See, e.g., [_Western Med. Consultants, 835 F.Supp. at 559_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-H810-001T-64R3-00000-00&context=1530671) (former employee did not have "record of 'customer contacts'" as alleged by former employer; former employee found pool of potential customers for competing firm through yellow pages in phone book and listing maintained by state insurance division, both publicly accessible resources).

The result stated for Illustration 7 is not consistent with the position taken in Restatement Second, Agency. Restatement Third, Unfair Competition § 42, Comment _f,_ states that general rules governing trade secrets apply to customer lists. Likewise, under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, a customer list does not lose protection because it is taken through memorization and not in memorialized form. See [_Ed Nowogroski Ins., Inc. v. Rucker, 971 P.2d 936 (Wash.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3VWN-28B0-0039-44S1-00000-00&context=1530671). In contrast, Restatement Second, Agency § 396, Comment _b,_ stated a "memory rule": a former agent "is normally privileged to use, in competition with the principal, the names of customers retained in his memory as the result of his work for the principal and also methods of doing business and processes which are but skillful variations of general processes known to the particular trade," although the former employer has kept the information secret from competitors. Restatement Third, Unfair Competition § 42, Comments _d_ and _f,_ characterize intentional memorization by an employee as evidence that information is a trade secret. Most recent cases do not follow the "memory rule," see [_Ed Nowogroski Ins., 971 P.2d at 946-948_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3VWN-28B0-0039-44S1-00000-00&context=1530671). When an employee remembers information only through casual memory, as opposed to a deliberate exercise in memorization, that fact may weigh in a court's analysis of a trade-secret claim against a former employee. See [_Leo Silfen, Inc. v. Cream, 278 N.E.2d 636, 639 (N.Y. 1972)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-VR30-003C-C2SX-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_North Atl. Instruments, Inc. v. Haber, 188 F.3d 38, 46-47 & n.7 (2d Cir.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3X52-6CH0-0038-X3MF-00000-00&context=1530671) (suggesting that reference to "casual memory" in _Leo Silfen_ does not amount to categorical treatment of trade secrets remembered casually and that "such a rule would almost surely prove unworkable in situations where, as here, there is evidence that although the employee may have committed some information to memory, he also physically took other information. The task of crafting an injunction permitting use of casually remembered information, while prohibiting the use of a list that the employee physically pilfered, would be virtually impossible as a practical matter.").

These developments post-Restatement Second, Agency, may reflect, among other things, that proficiency in memorization has declined as a trait enjoying cultural and social acclaim. In earlier eras, matters were viewed differently. See, e.g., Jonathan D. Spence, The Memory Palace of Matteo Ricci (1984) (wide-ranging narrative account of life of Jesuit who, in late 16th century, taught Chinese a set of mental constructs that could be used to remember vast amounts of information).

For the point that an agent breaches the agent's fiduciary duty by effecting trades in securities on the basis of confidential information of the principal, see [_United States v. O'Hagan, 521 U.S. 642 (1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S65-HXT0-003B-R16B-00000-00&context=1530671) (lawyer purchases options to buy stock of takeover target identified by partner in law firm who represents bidder); [_Diamond v. Oreamuno, 248 N.E.2d 910 (N.Y.1969)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-W1Y0-003C-C024-00000-00&context=1530671) (sales by corporate insiders on basis of nonpublic information constitute breaches of fiduciary duty). Not all states so characterize insider trading. See [_Freeman v. Decio, 584 F.2d 186 (7th Cir.1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-XFS0-0039-M3SG-00000-00&context=1530671) (predicting that Indiana would not follow _Diamond)._

Whistleblowing issues typically arise when an employee alleges that an employer's termination of the employee was wrongful because it constituted retaliation for the employee's whistleblowing. Many cases focus on employee claims of retaliatory discharge when the employee makes internal reports of suspected wrongdoing. In jurisdictions that recognize a public-policy exception to the employment-at-will doctrine, terminating an employee in retaliation for making such reports is wrongful if the subject matter of the employee's complaint or report concerns a matter of public policy. For representative examples, see [_Green v. Ralee Eng'g Co., 78 Cal.Rptr.2d 16 (Cal.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3THM-7JM0-0039-43NM-00000-00&context=1530671) (termination followed employee's internal reports concerning employer's alleged failure to comply with inspection practices mandated by regulations implementing Federal Aviation Act); _Rocky Mountain Hosp. & Med. Serv. v. Mariani, 916 P.2d 519 (Colo. 1996)_ (CPA who managed human-resources department terminated in retaliation for complaints to supervisors concerning questionable accounting practices; state accountancy-board rules may constitute articulations of public policy for purposes of wrongful-discharge claim); [_Sheets v. Teddy's Frosted Foods, Inc., 427 A.2d 385 (Conn.1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-1C60-003D-83B6-00000-00&context=1530671) (employer allegedly terminated quality-control director following director's internal reports of deviations from state labeling requirements for food products). A termination contravenes public policy, likewise, when it retaliates against an employee's threat to make a report to regulatory or law-enforcement authorities. See, e.g., [_Sherman v. Kraft Gen. Foods, Inc., 651 N.E.2d 708 (Ill. App.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S47-KVW0-003D-H2NB-00000-00&context=1530671) (employee allegedly terminated following reports of asbestos hazards in workplace to supervisor and stated intention to report hazard to OSHA); [_McQuary v. Bel Air Convalescent Home, Inc., 684 P.2d 21 (Or.App.1984)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-YVJ0-003F-Y22C-00000-00&context=1530671) (nursing-home employee allegedly terminated following threat to report claims of abuse of patient to state nursing-home regulator). A termination contravenes public policy when it retaliates against a report to law-enforcement or regulatory authorities external to the employer's organization. See [_Prince v. Rescorp Realty, 940 F.2d 1104 (7th Cir.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-96J0-008H-V2TY-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Illinois law; chief engineer of high-rise apartment building stated claim of retaliatory discharge; believing that timer installed by employer's management on fire-safety system constituted a fire hazard, engineer stated concern to building manager and, receiving no response, to municipal officials; engineer terminated 2 years later). A claim of wrongful termination does not require that an employee establish an actual violation of law by the employer, only that the employee suspected illegal activity and had a reasonable basis for those suspicions. See [_Green, 78 Cal.Rptr. at 29_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-9BN0-003C-J0MC-00000-00&context=1530671). Some states recognize that public-policy claims may be based on nonlegislative sources, such as professional ethical codes in some circumstances. See _Rocky Mountain Hosp. & Med. Serv., 916 P.2d at 524-526_ ("any public policy must serve the public interest and be sufficiently concrete to notify employers and employees of the behavior it requires"; rule directing an accountant to refrain from knowingly misrepresenting facts and not to subordinate judgment to others "represents a clear mandate of public policy for purposes of establishing a claim for wrongful discharge in violation of public policy").

Separately, in many whistleblowing cases the dispositive question is whether an employee's conduct is covered by a statute that explicitly protects whistleblowing activity. For representative cases holding the employee's conduct to be protected, see, e.g., [_Marques v. Fitzgerald, 99 F.3d 1 (1st Cir.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-04C0-006F-M18J-00000-00&context=1530671) (Rhode Island Whistle-blower's Act; municipal employee terminated following report to supervisor that employee would not continue to perform work assignment on boat that lacked life preserver because employee, who could not swim, feared boat would capsize); [_Passaic Valley Sewerage Comm'rs v. Department of Labor, 992 F.2d 474 (3d Cir.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-GWW0-003B-P08V-00000-00&context=1530671) (Clean Water Act; employee reported allegations of inadequate sampling system within organization and then threatened report to EPA); [_Bechtel Constr. Co. v. Secretary of Labor, 50 F.3d 926 (11th Cir.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-FK60-001T-D1XB-00000-00&context=1530671) (Energy Reorganization Act; carpenter laid off following questions to superiors about proper handling of tools contaminated with radiation); [_Deneau v. Manor Care, Inc., 219 F.Supp.2d 855 (E.D.Mich.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:46P8-YWC0-0038-Y1D7-00000-00&context=1530671) (Michigan Whistle-blowers' Protection Act; nursing-home employee terminated after referring patients with significant weight loss to physicians contrary to management instructions and entering fact of referrals into data base submitted to state, as required by employee's job responsibilities); [_Norris v. Hawaiian Airlines, Inc., 842 P.2d 634 (Haw.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-1500-003F-G0GK-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_512 U.S. 246 (1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RJ6-FD60-003B-R0N6-00000-00&context=1530671) (Whistleblowers' Protection Act; airline mechanic contacted FAA to report problem on plane he serviced when he refused to sign off on plane contrary to supervisor's direction); _Abraham v. County of Hennepin, 639 N.W.2d 342 (Minn.2002)_ (Whistleblower Act and Minnesota OSHA; employees alleged termination in retaliation for complaints to supervisor and to state OSHA that chemical fumes in workplace were making them ill); [_Appeal of Bio Energy Corp., 607 A.2d 606 (N.H.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-4NK0-003G-B133-00000-00&context=1530671) (Whistleblowers' Protection Act; employee discharged after bringing to supervisor's attention that employer's practice of deducting sick days from wages paid violated state law). For a comprehensive presentation and analysis of state statutes governing whistleblowers, see Victoria L. Donati & William J. Tarnow, Whistleblowers and Other Retaliation Claims, 34 Inst. on Emp. L. 1095 (2005).

The federal Sarbanes-Oxley Act prohibits any public company from discriminating against any employee who lawfully provides information or otherwise assists in an investigation of conduct that the employee "reasonably believes" constitutes a violation of the federal securities laws. See Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002, § 806(a), codified at [_18 U.S.C. § 1514A(a)(1)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SG9-5042-D6RV-H0G2-00000-00&context=1530671). For discussion, see Leonard M. Baynes, Just Pucker and Blow?: An Analysis of Corporate Whistleblowers, the Duty of Care, the Duty of Loyalty, and the Sarbanes-Oxley Act, [_76 St. John's L. Rev. 875 (2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4805-7CP0-00CW-F1BH-00000-00&context=1530671).

On disclosure of confidential information to a third party with legitimate reason to receive it, see [_Hale Trucks, LLC v. Volvo Trucks N. Am., Inc., 224 F.Supp.2d 1010, 1025 (D.Md. 2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:46WV-6W60-0038-Y416-00000-00&context=1530671) (president of truck dealership did not breach fiduciary duty by informing customers that dealership was out-of-trust in handling financing monies because out-of-trust condition jeopardized availability of floor-plan financing, which would complicate delivery of new trucks to customers).
