[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 8.06_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8Y0-00YG-400W-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 8- Duties of Agent and Principal to Each Other_ > _Topic 1- Agent's Duties to Principal_ > _Title B- Duties of Loyalty_**

**§ 8.06 Principal's Consent**

**(1) Conduct by an agent that would otherwise constitute a breach of duty as stated in §§ 8.01, 8.02, 8.03, 8.04, and 8.05 does not constitute a breach of duty if the principal consents to the conduct, provided that**

**(a) in obtaining the principal's consent, the agent**

**(i) acts in good faith,**

**(ii) discloses all material facts that the agent knows, has reason to know, or should know would reasonably affect the principal's judgment unless the principal has manifested that such facts are already known by the principal or that the principal does not wish to know them, and**

**(iii) otherwise deals fairly with the principal; and**

**(b) the principal's consent concerns either a specific act or transaction, or acts or transactions of a specified type that could reasonably be expected to occur in the ordinary course of the agency relationship.**

**(2) An agent who acts for more than one principal in a transaction between or among them has a duty**

**(a) to deal in good faith with each principal,**

**(b) to disclose to each principal**

**(i) the fact that the agent acts for the other principal or principals, and**

**(ii) all other facts that the agent knows, has reason to know, or should know would reasonably affect the principal's judgment unless the principal has manifested that such facts are already known by the principal or that the principal does not wish to know them, and**

**(c) otherwise to deal fairly with each principal.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section states the requirements for valid consent by a principal to conduct by an agent that would otherwise breach the agent's fiduciary duty. The validity of the principal's consent turns in many respects on the agent's conduct in obtaining it. Comment _b_ discusses the rationale for these rules. Comment _c_ discusses their application when an agent's conduct consists of the acquisition of a material benefit in connection with actions taken on behalf of the principal as stated in § 8.02. Comment _d(1)_ discusses the rules' application when an agent acts as or on behalf of an adverse party in a transaction with the principal as stated in § 8.03. Comment _d(1)_ examines circumstances in which an agent acts as or on behalf of an adverse party in dealing with a single principal or group of principals. Comment _d(2)_ examines circumstances to which subsection (2) applies, in which an agent acts on behalf of multiple principals in a transaction between or among the principals. Comment _e_ discusses consent by a principal to conduct by an agent that would otherwise breach the agent's duty under § 8.04 to refrain from competition with the principal and from assisting competitors of the principal.

The terms "know," "reason to know," and "should know" are defined in § 1.04(4).

More specialized rules may apply in this context to particular types of agents. On client consent to conduct by a lawyer that would otherwise breach the lawyer's duties of loyalty to the client, see, e.g., Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 122 (client consent to conflict of interest) and § 126 (business transaction between lawyer and client).

_b. In general._ This section defines the circumstances under which conduct of a principal is effective as consent to conduct by an agent that would otherwise constitute a breach of the agent's duties of loyalty.

Common-law agency does not accord effect to all manifestations of assent by a principal that purport to eliminate or otherwise affect the fiduciary duties owed by an agent. This is so for two distinct reasons: (1) the law, and not the parties, determines whether a particular relationship is one of agency as defined in § 1.01; and (2) the law imposes restrictions on the efficacy of a principal's manifestations of assent in the interest of safeguarding the principal's intention in creating a relationship of common-law agency.

A relationship between two parties in which it is agreed that one party shall have the right to take action that affects the legal relations of the other party without regard for whether the action is for that party's benefit is not a relationship of agency as defined in § 1.01. For example, a power given as security enables the holder of the power to take action that affects the legal relations of the creator of the power and is held for the benefit of the holder or a third party. See § 3.12(1). Such a power creates neither a relationship of agency nor actual authority as defined in § 2.01. See § 3.12, Comment _b._ Moreover, to the extent that an agent is privileged to take action to protect the agent's own interests, for example by creating a lien against property of the principal when the agent has a right to do so, the agent's action is not that of an agent. See § 8.01, Comment c, for further discussion.

Moreover, although a person may empower another to take action without regard to the interests of the person who grants the power, the law applicable to relationships of agency as defined in § 1.01 imposes mandatory limits on the circumstances under which an agent may be empowered to take disloyal action. These limits serve protective and cautionary purposes. Thus, an agreement that contains general or broad language purporting to release an agent in advance from the agent's general fiduciary obligation to the principal is not likely to be enforceable. This is because a broadly sweeping release of an agent's fiduciary duty may not reflect an adequately informed judgment on the part of the principal; if effective, the release would expose the principal to the risk that the agent will exploit the agent's position in ways not foreseeable by the principal at the time the principal agreed to the release.

In contrast, when a principal consents to specific transactions or to specified types of conduct by the agent, the principal has a focused opportunity to assess risks that are more readily identifiable. Likewise, when a principal consents after-the-fact to action taken by an agent that would otherwise breach the agent's fiduciary duty to the principal, the principal has the opportunity to assess what the agent has done with a degree of specificity not available before the agent takes action. Other bodies of law, and law focused on particular types of agency relationships, may impose additional limitations on the efficacy of a principal's consent after-the-fact to conduct by an agent. See Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 54(3).

An agent bears the burden of establishing that the requirements stated in this section have been fulfilled.

_c. Agent acquires material benefit arising out of position._ A principal may consent to conduct by an agent that would otherwise breach the agent's duty as stated in § 8.02. A principal may consent to an agent's receipt of a material benefit in connection with a transaction conducted or other actions taken on behalf of the principal or otherwise through the agent's use of position.

In assessing whether an agent has obtained valid consent from the principal to the agent's acquisition of a material benefit, courts often state that it is necessary for the agent to make "full and fair" disclosure to the principal. In an organizational setting, such disclosure, to be effective, must be made to a disinterested decisionmaker who has authority within the organization to determine whether the agent's conduct is consistent with the organization's interests. Many organizations, aware of the manifold risks implicit when their agents make gifts to or receive gifts from parties with whom they deal on behalf of the organization, have explicit codes of conduct applicable to such practices.

An agent has the burden of establishing that the principal consented to the agent's acquisition of a material benefit. The rule entitles the principal to assume that the agent will make the disclosures requisite to effective consent by the principal.

The terms of an agreement between a principal and an agent may implicitly include customs known to both through which agents customarily receive gratuities from third parties, including parties with whom they deal on the principal's behalf. A principal may assent to an agent's receipt of a material benefit from a third party by observing such a practice and failing to object when it is reasonable for the agent to interpret the principal's failure to object as indicative of consent.

**Illustrations:**

1\. P, who owns a used-car lot, employs A as its manager. A's duties include contracting with suppliers of used cars to replenish P's inventory. One regular supplier, T, sends A a case of wine several times a year. This is not a customary practice in the used-car industry. P observes this and does not object. P has consented to A's conduct.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that A knows that P lacks awareness of the market value of the wines T sends to A because A knows P to lack any familiarity with wine values. P's failure to object to A's receipt of the wine does not constitute consent by P.

_d. Agent acts as or on behalf of an adverse party or on behalf of multiple principals in transactions between or among principals with knowledge of principals_

_(1). Agent acts as or on behalf of an adverse party--single principal or group of principals._ When an agent acts as or on behalf of an adverse party in a transaction with the principal, the agent breaches the duty stated in § 8.03. The agent may resolve the conflict between the agent's position and the agent's duty to the principal by making disclosure to the principal of the agent's position or interest in the transaction to the principal, as well as other facts material to the principal's judgment as stated in subsection (1)(a)(ii). If the agent wishes to make such disclosure prior to concluding the transaction, the agent's duty of good faith to the principal requires the agent to make the disclosure in sufficient time to enable the principal to determine whether to proceed with the transaction, having had the opportunity to evaluate the significance of the agent's interest and to make any other arrangements warranted in the principal's judgment.

When the principal is an organization, such disclosure by an agent--whether employed within the organization or situated externally--should be made to an agent designated by the organization who is disinterested in the transaction. The agent's disclosure should comply with any applicable rules prescribed by the organization itself.

_(2). Agent acts on behalf of multiple principals in transactions between or among principals with knowledge of principals._ If an agent acts on behalf of more than one principal in a transaction between or among them and the principals know of the agent's dual or multiple representation, the agent owes duties of good faith, disclosure, and fair dealing to all of the principals as stated in subsection (2). If an agent's duties of confidentiality to one principal prevent the agent from fulfilling the duties of disclosure that the agent owes to any other principal, the agent breaches the agent's duties by continuing to act on behalf of the principal or principals to whom the agent may not make the required disclosure. See also § 8.03, Comment _b._

_e. Competition._ The extent of an agent's duty under § 8.04 to refrain from competition with the principal may be defined by agreement between them. Although the terms of such agreement may be explicit, implied understandings and customs normally associated with a type of agency relationship may also define the extent to which an agent may compete with the principal or assist the principal's competitors. For example, it is reasonable to expect that a customer of a securities broker knows that the broker's other customers may be equally enthusiastic to purchase or sell the same security within the same period of time. Although regulation may shape how a broker deals with an excess of buy- or sell-side orders, the common law of agency does not limit the broker's right to accept competing orders from its customers unless otherwise agreed.

**Illustrations:**

3\. P, who owns an apartment, lists it for sale with A, a real-estate broker. A shows the apartment to T, who expresses interest in buying it. T also requests that A show T another apartment in the same building, owned by C and also listed for sale with A. A does so. With A's assistance, T purchases C's apartment, not P's. A has not breached A's duty to P. An agent in A's position is not customarily expected to decline a request from a prospective purchaser to see another property listed with that agent. Unless A and P agree otherwise, A's duties to P do not require A to represent P exclusively.

4\. P, a professional photographer who specializes in images of babies, retains A as exclusive agent for licensing images submitted by P and accepted by A. As P is aware, A also accepts images of babies from other photographers. By accepting and licensing images of babies submitted by other photographers, A does not breach A's duty to P.

Moreover, the default rule applicable to agency relationships like those in Illustrations 3 and 4 can be justified on the basis that an alternate rule mandating exclusive loyalty would, in many instances, be a rule that principals and agents would agree should not apply to their relationship. An obligation to represent only one prospective seller of a type of property, or only one producer or other provider within a genre of artistic work, limits an agent's clientele and is likely to require a corresponding upward adjustment in the agent's prospective compensation from each client, a cost that many prospective principals may be unwilling to bear. With fewer clients, moreover, an agent has a smaller base over which to spread the risk that representing any one client will not turn out to be remunerative, whether the agent is to be compensated by a fixed or an outcome-contingent fee. Many third parties benefit as well through the default rule of nonexclusive commitment in situations like Illustrations 3 and 4. For example, potential buyers of apartments often wish to be able to see as many as possible while working with the same real-estate broker. Licensees of photographic images benefit from the ready availability of a larger menu of images from which to choose through the same stock agency.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency._ This section does not have a precise counterpart in Restatement Second, Agency, which does not treat a principal's consent to conduct that would otherwise breach an agent's fiduciary duties in a comprehensive manner. However, its sections articulating an agent's duties of loyalty are prefaced by "\[u\]nless otherwise agreed. . . ." See Restatement Second, Agency §§ 387, 388, 389, 391, 393, 394, 395, and 396. Sections 390 and 392 are not prefaced "\[u\]nless otherwise agreed . . . ." Section 390 applies to an agent who acts as an adverse party with the knowledge of the principal. It obliges the agent "to deal fairly with the principal and to disclose to him all facts which the agent knows or should know would reasonably affect the principal's judgment, unless the principal has manifested that he knows such facts or that he does not care to know them." Section 392, applicable to an agent who acts with the knowledge of two principals in a transaction between them, subjects the agent to duties to each principal comparable to the duties stated in § 390.

_b. In general._ For analysis of related doctrines in trust law, see John H. Langbein, Mandatory Rules in the Law of Trusts, [_98 Nw. U. L. Rev. 1105 (2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4CK7-GVB0-00CW-004H-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_McNeil v. McNeil, 798 A.2d 503, 509 (Del.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45WP-NG50-0039-451P-00000-00&context=1530671) ("court … flatly refuse to enforce provisions relieving a trustee of all liability . . . . A trust in which there is no legally binding obligation on a trustee is a trust in name only and more in the nature of an absolute estate or fee simple grant of property.").

For similar limits in the corporate context, see Principles of Corporate Governance: Analysis and Recommendations § 5.09.

On the scope of agreements through which a principal may consent to conduct that otherwise constitutes a breach of the agent's fiduciary duty, see [_O'Malley v. Boris, 2002 WL 453928, at \*3 (Del.Ch.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45K0-K6B0-0039-401B-00000-00&context=1530671) (mere existence of contract conferring discretion on agent does not relieve agent of duty to exercise discretion in conformity with fiduciary duty); Melvin Aron Eisenberg, The Limits of Cognition and the Limits of Contract, [_47 Stan. L. Rev. 211, 249-250 (1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S3V-3040-00CW-82F6-00000-00&context=1530671) (noting that although "\[t\]he core duty-of-loyalty rules governing corporate fiduciaries cannot be waived … courts may give effect to highly specific agreements that do not present the dangers of systematic unforeseeability and potential for exploitation."). In _O'Malley,_ a broker changed the provider of sweep-account services for ongoing clients without disclosing that it received 20 percent of the stock in a new joint venture in exchange. In contrast, in [_Weil v. Morgan Stanley DW Inc., 877 A.2d 1024 (Del.Ch.2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4GRJ-BW50-TVSY-W29R-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, 2005 WL 3526441 (Del.2005), a broker sold its online brokerage accounts to another firm that provided money-market sweep accounts. The selling broker's agreement with its customers reserved the right to assign their accounts. Customers were free to close their sweep accounts post-assignment and, more generally, to terminate their relationships with the brokerage firm at any time. The court held that the sale and assignment did not constitute a breach of fiduciary duty although the price the broker received may have reflected assumptions about customer loyalty, declining to "permit\[\] the plaintiff to rework a voluntary contractual relationship and capture a windfall gain while depriving \[defendant\] of its reasonable expectations." [_Id. at 1026_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4GRJ-BW50-TVSY-W29R-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Neubauer v. Goldfarb, 133 Cal.Rptr.2d 218, 223-225 (Cal.App. 2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48FD-JRY0-0039-40KP-00000-00&context=1530671) (provision in agreement creating option to purchase majority of shares in corporation stated that neither corporate purchaser nor its officers or controlling shareholders "'have any fiduciary duty to seller or \[issuer\] in connection with the execution of this agreement or a sale including, but not limited to, the fairness of the overall consideration or the allocation thereof'"; court holds that agreement did not cancel purchasers' fiduciary duties as directors and majority shareholders because waiver of fiduciary duty to minority shareholders in close corporation "is against public policy and a contract provision in a buy-sell agreement purporting to effect such a waiver is void"). Moreover, were the duty waivable, according to _Neubauer,_ summary judgment for the defendants was precluded because it was a triable issue of fact whether the seller knowingly and intelligently relinquished the rights created by the purchasers' fiduciary duty. Although the purchase agreement stated the seller "'had requested and received such information as he believes to be necessary to make an informed decision to enter into this agreement,'" the seller alleged that the purchasers withheld information material to the value of his stock and that the seller "had to rely on the \[purchaser's\] good faith representation they had provided him with all the information necessary" to enter into the agreement, including its waiver provision. [_Id. at 225_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48FD-JRY0-0039-40KP-00000-00&context=1530671).

For discussion of the same point in the trusts context, see Charles Har-pum, Fiduciary Obligations and Fiduciary Powers--Where Are We Going?, in Privacy and Loyalty 145, 153 (Peter Birks ed., 1997) (criticizing view that trusteeship has an irreducible core as unsupported by English law; preferable to "examine the intended effect of the document without regard to the name which the 'settlor' has chosen to give to the arrangement. If it lacks the essentials of a valid trust, the court should decide that it is not a trust at all but something else," such as "an outright gift to the trustees, or … a mere power to make dispositions of trust property, with a resulting trust back to the settlor in default.").

The ineffectiveness of general provisions eliminating fiduciary duties also serves as a common-law backdrop against which courts assess the impact of statutory language permitting variations in fiduciary obligation. In [_Gotham Partners, L.P. v. Hall-wood Realty Partners, L.P., 817 A.2d 160 (Del.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:491Y-NBN0-TVT4-92W2-00000-00&context=1530671), the court discussed in dicta the import of language in Del. Code Ann. tit. 6, § 17-1101(d)(2) stating that, in a limited partnership, "the partner's or other person's duties and liabilities may be expanded or restricted by provisions in the partnership agreement." The court disapproved of language in the lower court's opinion stating that a limited-partnership agreement may eliminate the fiduciary duties or liabilities of a general partner, characterizing it as a "dubious dictum" that warrants "a note of concern and caution." [_Id. at 168_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:491Y-NBN0-TVT4-92W2-00000-00&context=1530671). The court instead emphasized the "general principle in our jurisprudence that scrupulous adherence to fiduciary duties is normally expected," [_id. at 167_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:491Y-NBN0-TVT4-92W2-00000-00&context=1530671), as well as "the historic cautionary approach of the courts of Delaware that efforts by a fiduciary to escape a fiduciary duty, whether by a corporate director or officer or other type of trustee, should be scrutinized searchingly," [_id. at 168_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:491Y-NBN0-TVT4-92W2-00000-00&context=1530671). As subsequently amended, § 17-1101(d) explicitly states that a partnership agreement may limit or eliminate fiduciary duties, subject to the proviso that a "partnership agreement may not eliminate the implied contractual covenant of good faith and fair dealing." Under subsection (f), "a partnership agreement may not limit or eliminate liability for any act or omission that constitutes a bad faith violation of the implied contractual covenant of good faith and fair dealing." See also [_Harbison v. Strickland, 900 So. 2d 385, 390-391 (Ala.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4DM6-SJF0-0039-438R-00000-00&context=1530671) (Alabama LLC Act does not permit provision in operating agreement that eliminates a manager's duty of loyalty; trial court erred in failing to look beyond four corners of operating agreement to determine whether manager breached fiduciary duty to LLC and its members by transferring property belonging to LLC to her son allegedly for less than its fair market value); [_BT-I v. Equitable Life Assurance Soc., 89 Cal.Rptr.2d 811, 817-818 (Cal.App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XSJ-MSC0-0039-408H-00000-00&context=1530671) (general partner's purchase and acquisition of partnership debt breached fiduciary duty; partnership agreement ineffective to permit general partner's conduct, and in any event partnership agreement "cannot relieve a general partner of its fiduciary duties to a limited partner and the partnership when the purchase and foreclosure of partnership debt is involved" because duty is fundamental).

Language in corporation statutes that authorizes the adoption of exculpatory provisions in corporations' organizational documents limits the extent to which a corporation's directors may be exculpated against monetary liability stemming from breaches of fiduciary duty. See, e.g., Del. Code Ann., tit. 8, § 102(b)(7) (exculpatory provision may not eliminate or limit director's liability stemming from breach of duty of loyalty, illegal distribution to shareholders, act of intentional wrongdoing or act not in good faith, or breach from which director received an improper personal benefit); Rev. Model Business Corp. Act § 2.02(4) (exculpatory provision may not eliminate or limit director's liability for amount of financial benefit received by director to which director was not entitled, illegal distribution to shareholders, intentional infliction of harm on corporation or shareholders, or intentional violation of criminal law). For analysis of the policy implications of such provisions, see Principles of Corporate Governance: Analysis and Recommendations § 7.19, Comment _c._

On laboratory experiments assessing the impact of disclosure by advisors of their conflicts of interest, see Daylian M. Cain et al., The Dirt on Coming Clean: Perverse Effects of Disclosing Conflicts of Interest, [_34 J. Legal Stud. 1 (2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4FJN-1N60-00CV-S0DJ-00000-00&context=1530671).

_c. Agent acquires material benefit arising out of position._ On the inefficacy of disclosure of a breach of fiduciary duty in an offhanded manner, see [_Ehlen v. Lewis, 984 F.Supp. 5, 9 (D.D.C.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S9J-YSD0-00B1-F1GN-00000-00&context=1530671) (egregious nature of breach of fiduciary duty such that offhand disclosure to client would not have been adequate; "\[w\]here a fiduciary acts in his own interest in dereliction of his beneficiaries' interest, more than some 'by the way' notice is required.").

On claims that an agent's receipt of pecuniary benefit reflected a custom, see [_Steamship Mut. Underwriting Assoc. Ltd. v. Cove Shipping, Inc., 36 F.Supp.2d 940, 946 (S.D.Ala.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3T2G-8B00-0038-Y06G-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _180 F.3d 274 (11th Cir.1999)_ (retrospective commission paid by marine-insurance club to insured's agent constituted a civil bribe under English law; although payment of commission from club to an agent is not extraordinary in London market, retrospective nature of payment was not customary and set payment "outside the customary commission payment so that plaintiff does not fall within the … exception to the prohibition against paying a secret commission to another's agent"); International Merch. Group Americas, Inc. v. Americo Group, Inc., 220 N.Y.L.J., Oct. 22, 1998 (Sup. Ct.) (buying agent for importer and seller of discount apparel, compensated by commissions from buyer, also demanded and received 5% commissions from manufacturers; agent did not present sufficient evidence to show either that its dual-commission practices reflected industry practice or that principal knew of or acquiesced in arrangement).

_d. Agent acts as or on behalf of an adverse party or on behalf of multiple principals in transactions between or among principals with knowledge of principals._ On an agent's duties of disclosure and fair dealing once a principal knows the agent is dealing as or on behalf of an adverse party, see [_Arthur D. Little Int'l, Inc. v. Dooyang Corp., 928 F.Supp. 1189, 1208 (D.Mass.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-KYT0-006F-P1D4-00000-00&context=1530671) (consulting firm's disclosure of conflicting interest to client on whose behalf it acted as agent in Venezuelan investment possibilities did "not absolve \[firm\] of all other fiduciary duties as an agent. . . . \[firm\] had the duty to inform \[client\] of all facts which it knew would reasonably affect \[client's\] judgment in permitting \[firm\] to represent both \[firm\] and \[government-affiliated Venezuelan company\]" including priority status given to project and problems with Venezuelan infrastructure); [_Kirkruff v. Wisegarver, 697 N.E.2d 406, 411-412 (Ill.App.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3T26-2PS0-0039-4024-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent retained by seller of property, who ultimately purchased and developed land individually, failed to disclose he had already hired developer; when fiduciary relationship exists at time of transaction, agent has burden to show full disclosure of all relevant information to principal before entering into transaction, payment of adequate consideration, and provision of "competent and independent advice to the principal"); [_Certified Sec. Sys., Inc. v. Yuspeh, 713 So. 2d 558, 564 (La.App. 1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3SS3-6040-0039-40WF-00000-00&context=1530671) (president of company that installed and serviced security alarms breached fiduciary duty when president was not forthcoming with directors concerning transaction in which he became involved in purchasing and selling security-system accounts and kept information about status of company's accounts payable from board); [_Lane v. Oustalet, 873 So. 2d 92, 99 (Miss.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4CCP-B0Y0-0039-4016-00000-00&context=1530671) (dual agent has "separate burdens" for each principal); [_National Plan Admin'rs, Inc. v. National Health Ins. Co., 150 S.W.3d 718, 733 (Tex.App.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4D92-WSH0-0039-437H-00000-00&context=1530671) (administrator of health-insurance programs breached fiduciary duty to health-insurance underwriter when it transferred policies to another insurer; administrator failed to meet its burden of showing "proof of good faith and that the transaction was fair, honest, and equitable" when administrator was able to continue as policy administrator without compensating original underwriter for value of transferred business).

On disclosure of an agent's interest within an organizational principal, see Principles of Corporate Governance: Analysis and Recommendations § 5.02, Comment _d_ ("\[g\]enerally speaking, approval by a disinterested superior of a transaction between \[a lower-level\] officer or employee and the corporation should have the same effect as approval by disinterested directors" of a transaction between a corporation and a director or senior executive).

_e. Competition._ Illustration 3 is based on [_Sonnenschein v. Douglas Elliman-Gibbons & Ives, Inc., 753 N.E.2d 857 (N.Y.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:43DF-8680-0039-448F-00000-00&context=1530671). If the apartment's owner and the prospective purchaser have formed a mutually binding contract for purchase, the agent may tortiously interfere with the contract by facilitating the sale of a substitute apartment to the same purchaser. In _Sonnenschein,_ the plaintiff was unable to establish that a binding contract had been reached with the purchaser. [_Id. at 861_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:43DF-8680-0039-448F-00000-00&context=1530671). On tortious interference with a third party's performance of a contract, see Restatement Second, Torts § 766. Claims of tortious interference may focus on a principal's contracts with customers or employees. See, e.g., [_Fred Siegel Co., L.P.A. v. Arter & Hadden, 707 N.E.2d 853 (Ohio 1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3W6M-XR30-0039-43GS-00000-00&context=1530671) (reversing grant of summary judgment on behalf of former associate of law firm and new firm; tortious-inter-ference claim based on alleged use of wrongful methods to solicit clients, including use of misappropriated client list and other information acquired through allegedly wrongful means); [_Hilb, Rogal & Hamilton Co. v. DePew, 440 S.E.2d 918 (Va.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-VPS0-003D-50DV-00000-00&context=1530671) (sufficient evidence that former employee wrongfully interfered with insurance-sales firm's at-will contracts with customers).

On common practice in real-estate transactions, see [_Winston Fin. Group, Inc. v. Fults Mgmt., Inc., 872 P.2d 1356 (Colo.App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2JD0-003D-93Y0-00000-00&context=1530671) (common practice for leasing brokers to show more than one rental property to prospects); Kelly v. Cooper \[1993\] A.C. 205, 214 (P.C.) ("\[i\]n a case where a principal instructs as selling agent for his goods or property a person who to his knowledge acts and intends to act for other principals selling property or goods of the same description, the terms to be implied into such agency contract must differ from those to be implied where an agent is not carrying on such general agency business. In the case of estate agents, it is their business to act for numerous principals"; estate agent did not breach duty to owner of property listed with agent by failing to tell owner that purchaser had also agreed to purchase adjacent property, also listed for sale with agent). In contrast, in [_Walker v. Woodworth, 981 P.2d 1282 (Or.App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WJ7-9YD0-0039-4493-00000-00&context=1530671), the defendant, a buyer's broker, bought a building that his principal had also been attempting to buy. Although the brokerage agreement had a provision terminating it prior to the broker's purchase, the court noted that the broker and principal continued to work together thereafter and held that summary judgment for the broker was improper.

For an extreme instance of competitive conduct by a manufacturer's sales representative, see Hospital Prods. Ltd. v. United States Surgical Corp., \[1984\] 156 C.L.R. 41 (representative of manufacturer of surgical staples, inter alia, repackaged used demonstration units to sell in representative's competing venture).

For extensive examination of issues in the context of law firms, see Robert W. Hillman, Hillman on Lawyer Mobility: The Law and Ethics of Partner Withdrawals and Law Firm Breakups (1998). On conflicts among a fiduciary's clients, see Arthur B. Laby, Resolving Conflicts of Duty in Fiduciary Relationships, [_54 Am. U. L. Rev. 75 (2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4FKR-63M0-00CW-G08P-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 4 is based on [_Gentieu v. Tony Stone Images/Chicago, Inc., 255 F.Supp.2d 838 (N.D.Ill.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:487T-1380-0038-Y01H-00000-00&context=1530671). In _Gentieu,_ the court additionally held that the photographic agency did not breach its duty to the plaintiff by actively seeking to create and commissioning images from other photographers that replaced images that the plaintiff had provided or would have provided. [_Id. at 865_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:487T-1380-0038-Y01H-00000-00&context=1530671). The court distinguished [_Van Valkenburgh, Nooger & Neville, Inc. v. Hayden Publ'g Co., 281 N.E.2d 142, 145 (N.Y.1972)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-VPS0-003C-C2NT-00000-00&context=1530671), in which the court held that although a publisher had the right to produce competing works that lessened an author's royalties, "there may be a point where that activity is so manifestly harmful to the author, and must have been seen by the publisher so to be harmful, as to justify the court in saying that there was a breach of the covenant to promote the author's work." _Gentieu_ notes that "the very nature of the stock photography business requires that the stock agency solicit images _on the very same subject_ so that it can offer clients a menu of images to choose from." [_Id. at 865_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:487T-1380-0038-Y01H-00000-00&context=1530671) (emphasis in original). Moreover, while in _Van Valkenburgh_ the publisher concealed its commissioning of competing works from the author, in _Gentieu_ the plaintiff was well aware of how the stock agency operated.

For an example of an agreement permitting competition in the context of a limited-liability-company agreement, see [_McConnell v. Hunt Sports Enters., 725 N.E.2d 1193, 1206 (Ohio App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3X9C-D290-0039-44H4-00000-00&context=1530671) (provision in agreement that permitted LLC members to engage or own "an interest in any other business venture of any nature, including any business venture which might be competitive with the business of the Company" permitted member to submit competing application for professional hockey franchise).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 8.07_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8Y0-00YG-400X-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 8- Duties of Agent and Principal to Each Other_ > _Topic 1- Agent's Duties to Principal_ > _Title C- Duties of Performance_**

**§ 8.07 Duty Created by Contract**

**An agent has a duty to act in accordance with the express and implied terms of any contract between the agent and the principal.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section makes the basic point that an agent's duties of performance to the principal are subject to the terms of any contract between them. Comment _b_ discusses aspects of contractually created duties of agents. Comment _c_ examines the legal consequences of a promise made by an agent or other actor that induces reliance by the promisee.

On the standard of care applicable to an agent's performance of a service, see § 8.08.

_b. Duties created by contract._ Although a contract is not necessary to create a relationship of agency, many agents and principals enter into agreements. Contract-law principles of general applicability govern whether such agreements are enforceable and how they are to be interpreted, among other questions. See Restatement Second, Contracts.

A contract may create duties of performance on the part of an agent through its express and implied terms. The terms of an agreement between a principal and an agent may incorporate, either expressly or impliedly, the custom or usage of a particular trade. For example, agreements between securities brokers and their customers may impliedly incorporate the rules and customs of stock exchanges on which brokers execute trades on behalf of their customers. Other terms may be implied as well, for example a requirement that an agent exercise best efforts.

**Illustrations:**

1\. P, a fashion designer, engages A to market P's designs to clothing manufacturers. P and A agree that each will receive half the revenue from sales of P's designs made by A. A promises P that A will make sales calls on five large clothing manufacturers. A has a duty to do so.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 2, except that A does not promise P that A will undertake any specific marketing efforts. P and A agree that A will be P's exclusive marketing representative. A is subject to an implied duty to use best efforts in marketing P's designs.

In many relationships between agents and principals, a principal makes an offer that invites acceptance by the agent by rendering a performance, as opposed to acceptance through a return promise. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 45. The contract that results when the offeree renders performance is often termed a "unilateral" contract. However, although only the offeror makes a promise, both offeror and offeree may be subject to duties to each other. Contemporary contract law restricts an offeror's right to revoke such an offer "when the offeree tenders or begins the invited performance or tenders a beginning of it." Id. § 45(1).

**Illustrations:**

3\. P, who owns Blackacre, appoints A as agent to sell Blackacre. P promises to pay A \$ 10,000 if A is successful in closing a cash sale of Blackacre for at least \$ 100,000. A does not succeed. P does not have a duty to pay A \$ 10,000.

4\. Same facts as Illustration 3, except that P promises A that, if A incurs the expense of employing an expert to appraise Blackacre, P's offer will be irrevocable for 30 days. A pays an expert to appraise Blackacre. P is subject to a duty not to revoke P's offer for 30 days. P invited a preliminary act of performance, which A rendered.

Likewise, although an offeree is under no duty to perform, having made no promise to do so, conduct by the offeree in the course of performance may subject the offeree to liability when the offeree breaches a duty owed to the offeror.

**Illustration:**

5\. Same facts as Illustration 3, except that, acting with apparent authority but without actual authority, A persuades T to buy Blackacre for \$ 100,000 by making a fraudulent misrepresentation about Blackacre. P is subject to liability to T for loss suffered by T. See § 7.08. A is subject to liability to P. A's misrepresentation to T breached A's duty to P to act only with actual authority. See § 8.09. A's misrepresentation to T also subjects A to liability to T. See § 7.01.

A contract between an agent and a principal may create a duty on the part of the agent that survives termination of the agency relationship itself. For example, the contract may require the agent to repay money advanced by the principal, regardless of whether the agency relationship continues. Unless otherwise agreed, an agent has duties to the principal in handling money and other property of the principal as stated in §§ 8.05 and 8.12.

Statutes and administrative regulations may prescribe or otherwise regulate the terms of contracts between particular types of principals and agents. For example, federal regulation applicable to customs brokers prohibits contractual limits on their liability when engaging in the customs business.

_c. Promise that induces reliance._ A promise to perform a service may have serious consequences for the promisee if it causes the promisee to refrain from taking other measures to achieve the same end. For example, if the marketing agent in Illustrations 1 and 2 promises the designer that the agent will collect overdue accounts from manufacturers, the designer may fail to make other arrangements to effect collections. When a promisor should reasonably expect that making such a promise will induce the promisee to take or forbear from taking action, by making the promise the promisor becomes accountable for the consequences of the promisee's reliance. The promisor assumes a duty either to perform the promised service with reasonable care or to inform the promisee that the promisor will not perform the service before it is too late for the promisee to make alternative arrangements. A promisor's liability does not depend on whether a relationship of agency otherwise exists between promisor and promisee; indeed it will always be arguable that by making a promise to undertake legally consequential service on behalf of another person, the promisor assents to becoming the promisee's agent and, if the promisee manifests assent to the performance, a relationship of agency as defined in § 1.01 results.

Cases applying this doctrine treat it as a tort-law doctrine that imposes liability on a promisor who fails to perform the promised service with due care. Elements of the doctrine overlap with doctrines in other areas of law that attach operative significance to a manifestation that elicits reasonably foreseeable reliance on the part of another person. See Restatement Second, Contracts § 90; Restatement Second, Torts §§ 872 and 894.

**Illustrations:**

6\. P retains A to locate a house for P. A locates a house, owned by O, that is acceptable to P. P agrees to buy the house from O for \$ 18,500. O has listed the house for sale with B. P tells A that it is important to P that the property be free and clear of all encumbrances. Based on B's representations, A tells P that encumbrances will be paid off by B at the time of closing and that A will see that this happens and will take care of everything else connected with the closing for P. At the closing, P accepts delivery of a warranty deed purporting to convey the house free and clear of all liens and encumbrances. A settlement sheet prepared by B shows an outstanding deed of trust on the property on behalf of L, a mortgage lender, securing an obligation of \$ 11,000. P endorses in blank a cashier's check for \$ 18,500 and hands it to B. After the closing, B does not pay L, instead converting \$ 11,000 to B's own uses. A makes no inquiry of B, or otherwise, to determine whether L's deed of trust has been released. B and A are subject to liability to P. B's conversion of \$ 11,000 is tortious conduct against P. A made a promise to P that A should reasonably have anticipated would induce P to rely on A instead of taking alternate action to protect P's interests, such as retaining a lawyer. A did not use reasonable care in protecting P's interests against the risk, foreseeable to one in A's position, that B might engage in conversion or embezzlement.

7\. Same facts, except that P does not retain A as P's agent. Same result. A's promise to P induced P to rely on A instead of taking alternate action to protect P's interests. It was reasonably foreseeable to A that P would rely on A's promise.

A person is not subject to liability unless the person makes a promise that the person should realize will induce action or forbearance by another. An expectation that a service will be performed, not prompted by a promise to perform the service, does not suffice as a basis for liability.

**Illustrations:**

8\. A is a long-term ticket holder for three seats at professional football games in A's community. Due to high demand, season tickets are otherwise available for sale to members of the public for only a short time at the start of the season. A promises to transfer one ticket apiece to P and S, friends of A, in exchange for reimbursement for the cost of the tickets. Although P and S reimburse A as A requested, A keeps all three tickets and returns their funds to P and S. The time at which P and S could have purchased season tickets has passed. A is subject to liability to P and S. A's promise induced P and S to rely on A, and A did not inform P and S that A would not transfer season tickets to them before it was too late for them to acquire season tickets otherwise.

9\. Same facts as Illustration 8, except that A transfers the tickets as promised. The next year, A does not repeat the promise but P and S refrain from purchasing season tickets when they are on sale in the expectation that A will, once again, transfer two tickets to them. A is not subject to liability to P and S. A did not make a promise on which P and S relied. A's earlier promise did not give A reason to expect that P and S will rely on A for tickets for a subsequent season.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency, and codifications._ The material covered in this section corresponds to the subject matter of Restatement Second, Agency §§ 376, 377, and 378. Restatement Second, Agency, characterizes the duties included in this Title as "Duties of Service and Obedience." The terminology used in this Title, "Duties of Performance," comes from Francis M.B. Reynolds, Bowstead & Reynolds on Agency 150 (17th ed. 2001). Restatement Second, Agency § 378 contained a caveat that "\[w\]hen the gratuitous agent has not entered upon performance, it is not clear that liability will be imposed." In contrast, this Restatement does not limit a promisor's liability to situations in which the promisor has begun performing. Likewise, Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 42, Comment _e_ (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005) does not impose such a limit.

Under La. Civ. Code art. 3001 (2005), a "mandatary is bound to fulfill with prudence and diligence the mandate he has accepted. He is responsible to the principal for the loss that the principal sustains as a result of the mandatary's failure to perform." Art. 2989 defines a mandate as "a contract by which a person, the principal, confers authority on another person, the mandatary, to transact one or more affairs for the principal."

_b. Duties created by contract._ On the incorporation of rules of stock exchanges into agreements between stock brokers and their customers, see [_Thomson McKinnon Sec., Inc. v. Clark, 901 F.2d 1568, 1570-1571 (11th Cir.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-56B0-003B-54FW-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Brumm v. McDonald & Co. Sec., Inc., 603 N.E.2d 1141, 1147 (Ohio App.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3M-1G70-003C-83BF-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 2 is based on [_Wood v. Lucy, Lady Duff-Gordon, 118 N.E. 214 (N.Y.1917)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-TJ10-003F-63DN-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent's implied obligation to use best efforts justified on basis that principal's prospect of receiving payment depends solely on agent's efforts; principal subject to liability for breach of contract of exclusive representation when principal allowed others to market designs). An agent's implied duty to use best efforts furnishes consideration for the principal's commitment to the agent of exclusive rights of representation. See E. Allan Farnsworth, Contracts 496 (4th ed. 2004). Such a duty is not implied when the agent's rights are not exclusive because the principal is not disabled from marketing directly or from appointing additional representatives. See id.; [_Zeyher v. S.S. & S. Mfg. Co., 319 F.2d 606 (7th Cir. 1963)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-2J40-0039-Y1GH-00000-00&context=1530671).

A contract between a principal and an agent may, of course, include an express provision requiring the agent to use best efforts. An express "best efforts" clause may not add substantive content to an agent's implied obligation if it does not articulate a level of performance that is required. See [_Gentieu v. Tony Stone Images/Chicago, Inc., 255 F.Supp.2d 838, 867-868 (N.D.Ill.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:487T-1380-0038-Y01H-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Illinois law, court concludes that contract did not provide standard by which agent's performance could be evaluated when contract that required agent to "'use its best efforts to license the Images and to maximize the overall earnings received by \[photographer\] for same'"; noting that contract designated agent as photographer's exclusive licensing representative, court analyzes agent's performance under standard of implied best-efforts obligation, "to use good faith, reasonable efforts to promote \[photographer's\] images").

Express terms in contract between a principal and an agent may create other duties to which the agent is subject; in some instances, the express term articulates a duty to which an agent would be subject in any event in a particular agency relationship. See, e.g., [_G.D. Searle & Co. v. Medicore Commc'ns, Inc., 843 F.Supp. 895, 907 (S.D.N.Y.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-SMR0-003B-V270-00000-00&context=1530671) (term in contract between pharmaceutical manufacturer and provider of medical-symposium services required service provider to pay third-party vendors once funds for that purpose were received from manufacturer; express provision "unnecessary to impose upon an agent so basic an obligation as the duty to pay the cost of third party contracts when the express purpose of the parties in creating the agency is to vest the agent with the authority to 'contract with third parties on behalf of' the principal and the principal remits funds to the agent to cover the cost of such contracts").

A principal may also have rights as the third-party beneficiary of contracts entered into by the agent. See [_Flickinger v. Harold C. Brown & Co., 947 F.2d 595, 600 (2d Cir.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-7VX0-008H-V2MD-00000-00&context=1530671) (customer of broker, as third-party beneficiary of contract between broker and clearing broker, was entitled to assert claim against clearing broker because customer was an intended beneficiary of contract that required clearing broker to render performance directly to customer).

Restatement Second, Contracts, does not use the terminology of "unilateral" as opposed to "bilateral" contract "because of doubt as to the utility of the distinction, often treated as fundamental, between the two types." See Restatement Second, Contracts § 1, Reporter's Note to Comment _f._

Illustration 4 is a variation on Restatement Second, Contracts § 45, Illustration 7.

For an example of an agent's repayment obligation that survived termination of the agency relationship, see [_Life Mktg., Inc. v. A.I.G. Life Ins. Co., 588 So. 2d 663, 665 (Fla.Dist. App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-B380-003F-32CY-00000-00&context=1530671) (general agency agreement obliged agent to repay commission advances to insurer on demand; obligation binding on agent although account in which advancements were held was treated as marital property in dissolution action and awarded to agent's ex-spouse).

On the regulatory prohibition of contractual provisions limiting the liability of customs brokers, see _Pacific Tall Ships Co. v. Kuehne & Nagel, Inc., 94 F.Supp.2d 928, 934 (N.D.Ill. 2000)_ (prohibition imposed by 19 C.F.R. § 111.44 inapplicable to contract between parties; plaintiff not acting as customs broker when plaintiff advised defendant to fumigate its cargoes).

_c. Promise that induces reliance._ Restatement Second, Torts § 872, "Tort Liability Based on Estoppel," subjects to liability a person who "makes to another person a definite misrepresentation of fact concerning the ownership of property or its disposition, knowing that the other intends to act in reliance on it, and subsequently does an act or makes a refusal that would be tortious if the statement were true," if the second person "in reasonable reliance upon the statement has so changed his position that it would be inequitable to deny an action for the act or refusal." This rule "is dependent upon the same principle" that underlies § 894. Restatement Second, Torts § 872, Comment _a._ Restatement Second, Torts § 894, "Equitable Estoppel as a Defense," is applicable to a person who "makes a definite misrepresentation of fact to another person having reason to believe that the other will rely upon it and the other in reasonable reliance upon it does an act that would not constitute a tort if the misrepresentation were true . . . ." Id. § 894(1). Under § 894(2), a person who "realizes that another because of his mistaken belief of fact is about to do an act that would not be tortious if the facts were as the other believes them to be … is not entitled to maintain an action of tort for the act if he could easily inform the other of his mistake but makes no effort to do so." On whether a misrepresentation is "definite," Comment a to § 872 of Restatement Second, Torts, observes that "\[g\]eneral statements of ownership by one having no special means of information are not a sufficient basis for estoppel; nor are casual statements, that purport to be based only on a general impression of facts sufficiently trustworthy to be a basis for reliance. On the other hand, definite statements of fact by one who is or purports to be a bailee of goods or a trustee, as to the existence or ownership of the subject matter or the extent of known liens against it, ordinarily can properly be relied upon."

Illustration 6 is based on [_Lester v. Marshall, 352 P.2d 786 (Colo.1960)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-X280-0040-01MM-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Runde v. Vigus Realty, Inc., 617 N.E.2d 572 (Ind.App.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-87C0-003F-X3XV-00000-00&context=1530671) (real-estate broker gratuitously assumed duty to purchasers of house to communicate defects to sellers but neglected to do so).

What a duty of care requires in particular circumstances is a question of fact. See [_Franklin Inv. Co. v. Huffman, 393 A.2d 119, 122-123 (D.C.App. 1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRT-9WJ0-003G-119S-00000-00&context=1530671) (if assignee of installment contract for purchase of automobile gratuitously promised to provide insurance for purchaser, question of fact for jury what assignee's duty of care required it to do).

Illustrations 8 and 9 are based on [_Davey v. King, 595 A.2d 999 (D.C.App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRT-8YX0-003G-126W-00000-00&context=1530671) and [_Tauber v. Jacobson, 293 A.2d 861 (D.C.App.1972)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRT-B610-003G-136D-00000-00&context=1530671), both involving relationships extending over several seasons of the Washington Redskins. In _Tauber_ the court notes that "we would have no hesitation in holding that in any year in which \[defendant\] called his friends and requested them to forward their share of the deposit if they wished to attend the games with him during the coming season, he thereby made himself their agent and was bound when the tickets were ultimately delivered to turn them over." [_293 A.2d at 865_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRT-B610-003G-136D-00000-00&context=1530671). The court acknowledges that a promise may be implied from an ongoing course of conduct: "\[defendant's\] method of dealing with his friends … is plainly not consistent with the notion that he had agreed or promised to act as their agent for an indefinite period. . . . his practice was to telephone each plaintiff, tell him the current amount of the deposit, and ascertain whether he was interested in renewing the arrangements of the previous year. Had there been an understanding binding him to make such arrangements in their behalf indefinitely, he would not have needed to check annually on their wishes in the matter." [_Id. at 866_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRT-B610-003G-136D-00000-00&context=1530671).

A promisor is not subject to liability when the promisor has no reason to know that the other party will rely on the promisor. See [_Moore v. Harris, 372 S.E.2d 654, 655 (Ga.App.1988)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX8-CKX0-003G-P15R-00000-00&context=1530671) (lawyer for sellers of paving company, who added addendum to sale agreement addressing possible change in structure of transaction to resolve tax questions, did not owe duty of care to purchasers: "the record shows that \[lawyer\] had no reason to believe that the \[purchasers\] were relying upon him for tax advice or that anything he had done or said regarding the transaction had caused the \[purchasers\] to refrain from seeking tax advice"; lawyer made it clear he represented only sellers, and purchasers told lawyer they had their own counsel). See generally Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 14(1)(b) (client-lawyer relationship arises when person manifests to a lawyer "the person's intent that the lawyer provide legal services for the person; and … the lawyer fails to manifest lack of consent to do so, and the lawyer knows or reasonably should know that the person reasonably relies on the lawyer to provide the services"). Liability under this rule also requires that the plaintiff be aware of the defendant's undertaking. See [_In re U.S. Office Prods. Co. Sec. Litig., 251 F.Supp.2d 77, 99 (D.D.C.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4847-RTM0-0038-Y3FC-00000-00&context=1530671) (plaintiff-shareholders could not establish that corporation breached duty to notify them of termination of restrictions on stock sale on basis that corporation notified other shareholders that restrictions had been lifted when plaintiffs were unaware that such notice had been given and unable to show any reason to rely on receiving notice).

On the bases on which liability may be imposed, see [_Great Plains Trust Co. v. Morgan Stanley Dean Witter & Co., 313 F.3d 305, 317-318 (5th Cir.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:47DD-FNN0-0038-X0R2-00000-00&context=1530671) (under Texas law, investment bank that performed due diligence and issued fairness opinion did not owe duty of care to holders of convertible debentures when bank did not undertake such duty; holders' negligent-representation claim correctly dismissed on bases that holders were not persons for whose benefit investment bank performed work and that bank did not know issuer would disregard contractual restrictions on dissemination of fairness opinion); [_Holtz v. J.J.B. Hilliard W.L. Lyons, Inc., 185 F.3d 732, 744 (7th Cir.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WYF-4V70-0038-X082-00000-00&context=1530671) (under Indiana law defendant may assume a duty through a promise or other conduct, acting with the knowledge that another party will rely on the promise; broker not subject to liability when broker failed to ask customer about customer's intentions in changing designation of beneficiary for customer's IRA when no evidence demonstrated broker assumed such duty or that customer relied upon broker to perform it; would-be beneficiary of account not characterized as third-party beneficiary of contract between broker and customer); [_Lowery v. Guaranty Bank & Trust Co., 592 So. 2d 79, 83-84 (Miss.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-4PW0-003G-74TB-00000-00&context=1530671) (circumstances may create at least a "quasi-fiduciary relationship" between a lender and a customer that obliges the lender to notify the customer that credit life insurance has lapsed).

There is some disagreement whether the basis for liability is the promise made by the promisor or the promisee's reliance. Compare Restatement Second, Agency § 354, Reporter's Note (stating that under § 378, "the element of reliance is obviously the basis of liability") with Edward Yorio & Steve Thel, The Promissory Basis of Section 90, [_101 Yale L.J. 111, 165_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S0M-8C90-00CW-718K-00000-00&context=1530671) & 166 n.359 (1991) (stating that "court enforce promises under Section 90 when they view the promises as serious and deserving of enforcement qua promise; they do not enforce them out of solicitude for promisees," and citing _Tauber_ as an instance of an absence of a promise and thus a case in which "the outcome turns largely on a problem with the promise"). See also [_In re Infocure Sec. Litig., 210 F.Supp.2d 1331, 1370-1371 (N.D.Ga.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:46DP-7VN0-0038-Y32P-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Georgia law; recovery under theory in legal-malpractice case requires promise that represents a specific undertaking to perform service on behalf of nonclient and induces reliance by nonclient).

A contract between a principal and an agent may have the effect of making reliance by the principal on the agent unjustifiable in some respect or another. See [_Bank of the West v. Valley Nat'l Bank, 41 F.3d 471 (9th Cir.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-0XH0-003B-P529-00000-00&context=1530671) (terms of participation agreement between lead bank and participating bank explicitly provided that each bank would investigate borrower independently and would exercise independent judgment; participating bank's reliance on lead bank not justifiable for purposes of fraud claim against lead bank, which did not immediately share adverse news about borrower with participating bank).
