[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 8.08_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8Y0-00YG-400Y-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 8- Duties of Agent and Principal to Each Other_ > _Topic 1- Agent's Duties to Principal_ > _Title C- Duties of Performance_**

**§ 8.08 Duties of Care, Competence, and Diligence**

**Subject to any agreement with the principal, an agent has a duty to the principal to act with the care, competence, and diligence normally exercised by agents in similar circumstances. Special skills or knowledge possessed by an agent are circumstances to be taken into account in determining whether the agent acted with due care and diligence. If an agent claims to possess special skills or knowledge, the agent has a duty to the principal to act with the care, competence, and diligence normally exercised by agents with such skills or knowledge.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section states the duties of care, competence, and diligence that an agent owes to a principal. Comment _b_ discusses relationships between the duties stated in this section, any contract between principal and agent, and statutory and regulatory provisions. Comment _c_ discusses an agent's duty of competence. Comment _d_ discusses an agent's duty of diligence. Comment _e_ discusses duties of agents who act gratuitously.

Section 1.04(3) states when an agent acts gratuitously.

_b. Common-law and statutory duties of care; regulatory duties._ A principal and an agent may establish benchmarks or other measures for the effort and skill to be expected from the agent. For example, a contract between principal and agent may specify measures for the effort that the agent has a duty to expend in pursuing the principal's objectives. An agent may also guarantee by contract that the agent's work will be successful in achieving an objective or that the agent's work will be satisfactory to the principal. On conditions that an obligor be satisfied with an obligee's performance, see Restatement Second, Contracts § 228. A contract may also, in appropriate circumstances, raise or lower the standard of performance to be expected of an agent or specify the remedies or mechanisms of dispute resolution available to the principal. Regardless of their content, contractually shaped or contractually created duties are grounded in the mutual assent of agent and principal.

In contrast to § 8.06, the rule stated in this section articulates a broader role for general agreements that a principal and an agent may make in advance. If an agreement between principal and agent is otherwise enforceable, it may define the standard of performance applicable to the agent across the board in general terms and without the specificity required under § 8.06(1)(b) for consent by a principal to conduct by an agent that would otherwise breach a duty of loyalty owed to the principal under §§ 8.01, 8.02, 8.03, 8.04, or 8.05. Moreover, a principal's consent under § 8.06 is ineffective unless in obtaining the consent the agent acted consistently with the requirements stated in § 8.06(1)(a) or (2).

The duties stated in this section will often overlap with an agent's duties of performance that are express or implied terms of a contract between principal and agent. However, the duties stated in this section are tort-law duties because they "denote the fact that the actor is required to conduct himself in a particular manner at the risk that if he does not do so he becomes subject to liability to another to whom the duty is owed for any injury sustained by such other, of which that actor's conduct is a legal cause." Restatement Second, Torts § 4. Tort law imposes duties of care on an agent because the agent undertakes to act on behalf of the principal, because the principal's reliance on that undertaking is foreseeable by the agent, and because it is often socially useful that an agent fulfill the agent's undertaking to the principal. See Restatement Second, Torts § 323; Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 42 (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005).

The overlap between duties derived from tort law and from an agent's contract with the principal will often provide the principal with alternative remedies when a breach of duty subjects the agent to liability. In particular, an agent is subject to liability to the principal for all harm, whether past, present, or prospective, caused the principal by the agent's breach of the duties stated in this section. See Restatement Second, Torts § 910. The agent's liability includes an obligation to indemnify the principal when a wrongful act by the agent subjects the principal to vicarious liability to a third person. See §§ 7.07 and 7.08. One agent's breach of the duties stated in this section does not bar an innocent principal from recovery against another agent who is also subject to liability.

Statutory provisions may also be relevant in determining the duties of care owed by an agent. For example, some organizational statutes establish standards of conduct for officers and others who act on behalf of an organization. Under § 8.42(a)(2) of the Model Business Corporation Act, an officer must act, in performing the officer's duties, "with the care that a person in like position would reasonably exercise under similar circumstances . . . ." The Revised Uniform Partnership Act states that a partner owes a duty of care to the partnership and to other partners "in the conduct and winding up of the partnership business" that is limited to "refraining from engaging in grossly negligent or reckless conduct, intentional misconduct, or a knowing violation of law." Rev. Unif. Partnership Act § 404(c). The Uniform Limited Liability Company Act imposes a comparable standard on an LLC's managers and members who are also managers. Unif. Limited Liability Co. Act § 409(c) and (h)(2).

An agent's conduct may be subject to regulation by statutes, administrative rules, or rules of a particular profession. An agent's violation of such a statute or rule does not in itself establish that the agent breached the agent's duty to the principal stated in this section. If the statute or rule is designed to protect persons in the principal's position, the trier of fact may consider the agent's violation of the statute in defining and applying the standard stated in this section.

**Illustration:**

1\. P maintains a brokerage account with A Brokerage Co., on cash terms that require P to pay for any purchases made in P's account at P's direction on the "settlement date," three days following the date of purchase. Through the account, P purchases many shares of T Corporation and pays promptly. P then directs the purchase of additional shares, creating a debt that P owes to A Brokerage Co. P does not pay as required on the settlement date. Regulation T, [_12 C.F.R. § 220.8_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=administrative-codes&id=urn:contentItem:603F-5591-DYB7-W2X8-00000-00&context=1530671), requires prompt liquidation of customer accounts when payment is not made in a timely fashion. A Brokerage Co. does not liquidate P's account promptly, and the securities in that account, which under Regulation T should have been sold to liquidate P's debt, thereafter decline in value. Following its eventual liquidation of P's account, A Brokerage Co. demands that P pay the outstanding debit balance. P refuses. A Brokerage Co.'s violation of Regulation T does not provide P with an affirmative defense to A Brokerage Co.'s claim against P for breach of contract or with a claim for losses in P's securities, which P suffered when P's account was not timely liquidated. Regulation T protects the general economy from an unwarranted infusion of credit, not persons in P's position. P should not be able to benefit from P's broker's violation of Regulation T, which ensued from P's breach of P's payment obligations.

_c. Duty of competence._ The specific skills that an agent must possess to be competent depend on the nature of the service that the agent undertakes to provide and the circumstances under which it will be provided, such as the magnitude and complexity of transactions that the agent will conduct on the principal's account. For example, an agent may be competent to lease apartments in a residential building but lack the competence required to negotiate a complex lease of commercial space.

If an agent undertakes to perform services as a practitioner of a trade or profession, the agent "is required to exercise the skill and knowledge normally possessed by members of that profession or trade in good standing in similar communities" unless the agent represents that the agent possesses greater or lesser skill. Restatement Second, Torts § 299A. An agent may reasonably be expected to know at least the basic rules and practices under which the agent's industry or profession operates. For the standard of care applicable to lawyers, see Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 52(1).

An agent's level of skill or knowledge may exceed the norm for similarly situated agents. Alternatively, an agent may falsely represent that this is so. An agent's performance should be evaluated consistently with the agent's claimed level of skill or knowledge unless the agent establishes that the principal knew the agent's claim to be false. The agent's professed level of skill or knowledge becomes the standard against which the agent's performance should be assessed. For a comparable rule applicable to trustees, see Restatement Third, Trusts § 77(3) (Tentative Draft No. 4, 2005). When an agent does not claim to possess special skills or knowledge but in fact has a level of skill or knowledge that exceeds the norm, the trier of fact may consider the agent's actual knowledge and skills in determining whether the agent acted with due care under the circumstances. See Restatement Third, Torts: Liability for Physical Harm § 12, Comment _a_ (Proposed Final Draft No. 1, 2005). An actor's actual state of knowledge will always be relevant to determining whether the actor behaved with due care, regardless of the source of that knowledge. Id., Comment a. An actor's knowledge and skills are combined to a degree that makes it difficult to disaggregate them. Id.

**Illustrations:**

2\. P, a successful pop singer, retains A, a talent agent, to represent P in negotiations with recording companies. A has skills as an investment manager that are unusual among otherwise similarly situated talent agents. At P's request, A undertakes to manage P's investment portfolio. However, A does so ineptly, resulting in losses for P. Under applicable law, A's management of P's investment portfolio is not consistent with the exercise of competence to be expected of an investment manager. A is subject to liability to P.

3\. Same facts as Illustration 2, except that A in fact lacks skills as an investment manager but represents to P that A has such skills. P does not know that A's claim is false. Same result.

4\. Same facts as Illustration 3, except that A makes no representation to P that A has skills as an investment manager. A is subject to liability if A failed to act with the care, competence, or diligence normally exercised by agents who undertake to manage property of a principal.

_d. Duty of diligence._ An agent's duty of diligence requires the agent to bring the agent's competence to bear on matters undertaken on behalf of the principal. Ordinarily, the scope of an agent's duty to be diligent is limited by the scope of the services the agent undertakes to perform for the principal. The scope of an agent's duty may be expanded by contract or by the existence of a special relationship of trust and confidence between agent and principal. For example, a securities broker's duty of diligence to a client who directs trading in the client's own account (a "nondiscretionary" account) is limited to executing the client's orders to purchase and sell securities in the account and does not extend to advising the client or issuing risk warnings on an ongoing basis. In contrast, a securities broker's duty may include the provision of advice and warnings when the broker's relationship with the client is one in which the client's trust and confidence are invited by the broker and given by the client.

Although an agent has a duty of diligence, that duty is to make reasonable efforts to achieve a result and not a duty to achieve the result regardless of the effort, risk, and cost involved. If an agent makes a reasonable effort, the agent is not subject to liability to the principal if the effort fails to accomplish the end desired by the principal.

_e. Gratuitous agents._ A person who gratuitously assents to acting as an agent acts as a fiduciary within the scope of the agency relationship. See §§ 1.01 and 8.01. A gratuitous agent is subject to the general fiduciary principle stated in § 8.01 and to the specific duties of loyalty stated in §§ 8.02 to 8.05. A gratuitous agent also owes duties of performance to the principal. See §§ 8.07 to 8.12. In particular, a gratuitous agent must furnish relevant information to the principal, see § 8.11; act only within the scope of actual authority, see § 8.09(1); and comply with the principal's lawful instructions, see § 8.09(2). Moreover, providing a service gratuitously may subject an agent to duties of competence and diligence that are no different than those owed to a principal who agrees to compensate the agent. See Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 38, Comment _c_ (lawyer may agree to represent client without payment) and § 50 (lawyer's duty of care to client). Statutes may limit the liability of actors who furnish services as volunteers under stated circumstances.

In general, the standard of care applicable to a gratuitous agent should reflect what it is reasonable to expect under the circumstances. Relevant circumstances include the skill, experience, and professional status that the agent has or purports to have. Thus, providing a service gratuitously may subject an agent to duties of competence and diligence to the principal that do not differ from the duties owed a principal who agrees to compensate the agent. See Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 16 (lawyer's duties to client); § 38, Comment _c_ (lawyer may agree to represent client without payment); and § 50 (lawyer's duty of care to client). It is also relevant whether the principal is aware of any limitations or shortcomings in the agent's skills.

**Illustrations:**

5\. P, who wishes to buy a used car, gives an amount of cash to P's friend, A, and asks A to select and purchase a suitable car for P. A is not employed as an auto mechanic but has substantial avocational experience in buying and selling cars and working on them. A selects and purchases a used car for P from T. A does not inspect the car or have an inspection conducted. Thus, A does not discover, nor does P, that the car has been in an accident that rendered it unsafe and without substantial value. Conducting a routine inspection, which A was competent to do, would have revealed the car's condition. A is subject to liability to P. A's failure to inspect the car or have it inspected prior to purchasing it for P was inconsistent with the exercise of competence and diligence reasonably to be expected of a person in A's position. It was reasonable for P to expect that A would exercise A's skill.

6\. Same facts as Illustration 5, except that the used car selected by A has a nonobvious defect in its internal computer system. To discern the presence of such a defect requires specialized skills apart from those ordinarily associated with auto mechanics. A is not subject to liability to P. A's skills do not extend to computer technology.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency, and codifications._ This section is the counterpart of Restatement Second, Agency § 379. Subsection (1) states that "\[u\]nless otherwise agreed, a paid agent is subject to a duty to the principal to act with standard care and with the skill which is standard in the locality for the kind of work which he is employed to perform and, in addition, to exercise any special skill that he has." Subsection (2) states that "\[u\]nless otherwise agreed, a gratuitous agent is under a duty to the principal to act with the care and skill which is required of persons not agents performing similar gratuitous undertakings for others."

Ga. Code § 10-6-22 (2000 & Supp. 2005) provides that "\[a\]n agent for hire is bound to exercise, about the business of his principal, that ordinary care, skill, and diligence required of a bailee for hire. A voluntary agent, without hire or reward, shall be liable only for gross neglect."

Under La. Civ. Code art. 3001 (2005), a "mandatary is bound to fulfill with prudence and diligence the mandate that he has accepted. He is responsible to the principal for loss that the principal sustains as a result of the mandatary's failure to perform." Under art. 3002, "\[w\]hen the mandate is gratuitous, the court may reduce the amount of loss for which the mandatary is liable."

_b. Common-law and statutory duties of care; regulatory duties._ The duties stated in this section are duties of performance, not duties of loyalty. Some courts label duties of care as "fiduciary," see, e.g., _Oxford Shipping Co. v. New Hampshire Trading Corp., 697 F.2d 1, 6 (1st Cir.1982)_ ("\[a\]s Oxford's agent Tager owed Oxford a 'fiduciary' duty of care"), as do some statutes, see, e.g., Rev. Uniform Partnership Act § 404(a) ("\[t\]he only fiduciary duties a partner owes the partnership and the other partners are the duty of loyalty and the duty of care set forth in subsections (b) and (c).").

On whether the economic-harm rule bars a principal's recovery against an agent for breach of duty, compare [_Lawyers Title Ins. Corp. v. Rex Title Corp., 282 F.3d 292, 294 (4th Cir.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:457K-3G80-0038-X4VK-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Maryland law; economic-harm rule does not bar insurer's negligence claim against its agent because insurance agent owes duty to principal that is independent of contract between them); [_Hilliard v. Black, 125 F.Supp.2d 1071, 1080 (N.D.Fla.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:42TT-4GV0-0038-Y3GV-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Florida law; clients' claims against sports agent for breach of fiduciary duty not barred by economic-harm rule because agent's wrongful acts were independent of acts that may have breached oral or written contracts with clients); and [_Hydro Investors, Inc. v. Trafalgar Power, Inc., 227 F.3d 8, 17 (2d Cir.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4176-DB10-0038-X167-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying New York law; economic-harm rule inapplicable to claims of professional malpractice) with [_Tuchman v. Pell Rudman Trust Co., 245 F.Supp.2d 1156 (D.Colo.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:480V-6TY0-0038-Y1R3-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Colorado law; economic-harm rule bars client's claims against broker when contract between parties set forth broker's duties, including duties of care and diligence). On developments in Florida post-Hilliard, see [_Rowell v. Holt, 850 So. 2d 474, 478 n.1 (Fla. 2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48XV-RRH0-0039-43GM-00000-00&context=1530671) (economic-harm rule does not bar recovery of damages for emotional distress stemming from "recognized intentional torts"; after criminal defendant presented his lawyer with a document that wholly exonerated client, lawyer neglected to set date for hearing, lost document, and allowed client to remain in jail for 13 days before obtaining client's release with a copy of document); [_Gracey v. Eaker, 837 So. 2d 348 (Fla.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:47GJ-7M80-0039-44XY-00000-00&context=1530671) (economic-harm rule does not bar tort action against psychotherapist; rule inapplicable when fiduciary relationship has been created). On the economic-harm rule generally, see Douglas Laycock, Modern American Remedies 116-119 (3d ed. 2002).

On provisions in agreements that relieve agents of liability, see [_Chemical Bank v. Security Pac. Nat'l Bank, 20 F.3d 375, 377 (9th Cir.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-72F0-003B-P4NS-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent acted negligently in failing to file financing statement, resulting in loss to principal in excess of \$ 1 million; under credit agreement agent subject to liability only for its own gross negligence or willful misconduct, neither of which principal established); [_Pavlovich v. National City Bank, 435 F.3d 560 (6th Cir.2006)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4J5R-D840-TVRV-B2BN-00000-00&context=1530671) (custody agreement that authorized custodian to rely on written directions provided by principal or by persons authorized in writing by principal and relieved custodian of any duty to review investments was not breached because custodian executed transactions as directed by principal's investment advisor, designated by principal as having trading authority); [_Carleton v. Tortosa, 17 Cal.Rptr.2d 734 (Cal.App. 1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-GK20-003D-J2SX-00000-00&context=1530671) (claim that real-estate broker failed to advise client that transaction could have adverse tax consequences defeated by provisions in agreement stating that broker made no representation or recommendation regarding tax consequences and directing client to seek tax advice elsewhere if desired). Some exculpatory provisions are unenforceable. See, e.g., Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 54(2) (agreement prospectively limiting lawyer's liability to client for malpractice).

Many cases hold that one agent's negligence does not bar a principal from recovery against another negligent agent. See _Oxford Shipping Co. v. New Hampshire Trading Corp., 697 F.2d 1, 6 (1st Cir.1982)_ (negligence of ship's captain and first officer does not bar ship owner's recovery against negligent agent retained to issue bills of lading and perform other tasks associated with loading and shipping of cargo of scrap metal); accord, [_Buhl v. Viera, 102 N.E.2d 774 (Mass.1952)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRJ-9PN0-003C-T4S8-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Brown v. Portizky, 283 N.E.2d 751 (N.Y.1972)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-VP30-003C-C2FB-00000-00&context=1530671), overruled on other grounds by [_Lusenskas v. Axelrod, 614 N.E.2d 729 (N.Y.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S2R-7G10-003V-B1DN-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Zulkee v. Wing, 20 Wis. 408 (1866)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XXD-0XM0-00KR-C4PG-00000-00&context=1530671). To the contrary are _Insurance Co. of N. Am. v. Anderson, 438 P.2d 265 (Idaho 1968)_ (principal charged with contributory negligence of one agent, barring claim against other negligent agent); [_Capitola v. Minneapolis, St. P. & S.S.M. R.R. Co., 103 N.W.2d 867 (Minn.1960)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-4BW0-003G-V0VT-00000-00&context=1530671). The persuasive analysis of the question in _Oxford Shipping_ notes that courts that use the contributory negligence of one agent to bar a principal's recovery against another agent "have simply reasoned by analogy from another situation without recognizing the difference. The different situation is that in which a principal seeks to recover from a third party, as when Firm A seeks to recover from a driver of a car who negligently collides with Firm A's truck. In such situations, courts have often held that the contributory negligence of Firm A's driver, if sufficient to bar the driver's own recovery, is sufficient to bar recovery by Firm A as well." _697 F.2d at 6_. In contrast, there is no reason to treat the negligence of two agents of the same principal as a basis to bar recovery against either of them.

On an agent's liability to indemnify the principal against loss resulting from the agent's breach of duty, see, e.g., _Oxford Shipping, 697 F.2d at 7_ (agent subject to liability to principal for principal's damages stemming from seizure of vessel caused by agent's breach of duties to principal; district court should defer entry of damage award based on principal's anticipated liability to third party stemming from same breach until liability is finally, if ever, established); [_GAB Bus. Servs., Inc. v. Syndicate 627, 809 F.2d 755 (11th Cir.1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-CJ80-001B-K0SX-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurance adjuster subject to liability, in either tort or contract, to owner of racehorse for harm resulting from its negligent conduct as an agent in investigating injury to horse, which led to death of horse); [_Kramer v. Nowak, 908 F.Supp. 1281, 1292 (E.D.Pa.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-6K20-001T-520J-00000-00&context=1530671) (junior lawyer whose negligence in representing client led to liability for employing lawyer subject to liability to employer; court can find "no reason why lawyer employees--and no others--should be exempt from the possibility of suit brought by their employers"); [_Central Nat'l Ins. Co. v. Devonshire Coverage Corp., 426 F.Supp. 7, 23 (D.Neb.1976)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-JFC0-0054-638S-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent liable to insurer for damages caused by agent's failure to act with degree of skill and care customarily exercised by insurance agents); [_Securities Investor Prot. Corp. v. R.D. Kushnir & Co., 267 B.R. 819, 824 (Bankr.N.D.Ill. 2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:444H-1ST0-0039-03B7-00000-00&context=1530671) (securities broker subject to liability to former employer for losses he caused it by causing it to become liable to customers); [_United States Liab. Ins. Co. v. Haidinger-Hayes, Inc., 463 P.2d 770, 774 (Cal.1970)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRK-JTK0-003C-H134-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurance agent who negligently induces insurer to assume coverage on which insurer suffers loss is subject to liability to insurer for amount of loss); [_Insurance Co. of N. Am. v. Miller, 765 A.2d 587, 599-601 (Md.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4245-0PJ0-0039-40JC-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurance agent subject to liability to insurer due to participation in double-billing scheme; insurer suffered injury because it did not receive premiums that should have been forwarded from agency's trust account). See also Lister v. Romford Ice & Cold Storage Co. \[1957\] A.C. 555 (claim by employer's insurer against negligent employee).

The gross negligence standard of Rev. Unif. Partnership Act § 404(c) is consistent with the common-law standard stated in, e.g., [_Rosenthal v. Rosenthal, 543 A.2d 348, 352 (Me.1988)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-43F0-003F-N3DF-00000-00&context=1530671). The Uniform Partnership Act (1914) does not state a duty of care.

Illustration 1 is based on [_Shearson Lehman Bros., Inc. v. M & L Invs., 10 F.3d 1510 (10th Cir.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-BF30-003B-P107-00000-00&context=1530671), with an adjustment to reflect current practice concerning settlement dates. In accord are [_Bache Halsey Stuart, Inc. v. Killop, 509 F.Supp. 256 (E.D.Mich. 1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-87S0-0054-718V-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Gregory-Massari, Inc. v. Purkitt, 82 Cal.Rptr. 210 (Cal.App.1969)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-9840-003C-J002-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Shearson Hayden Stone, Inc. v. Feldman, 439 N.Y.S.2d 975 (Civ.1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-G7S0-003C-F0PJ-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Styner v. England, 699 P.2d 234 (Wash.App.1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-XHM0-003F-W2W0-00000-00&context=1530671); see also [_Thomson McKinnon Sec. Inc. v. Clark, 901 F.2d 1568 (11th Cir.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-56B0-003B-54FW-00000-00&context=1530671) (experienced trader directed broker to execute trade in violation of stock exchange's margin regulations; broker's over-extension of credit to customer does not create an implied cause of action or give customer an affirmative defense against broker's breach-of-contract claim). Apparently alone in affording a client an affirmative defense to breach of contract on the basis of a broker's violation of [_Regulation T is Thomson McKinnon Sec., Inc. v. Hornung, 419 N.Y.S.2d 526 (App.Div.1979)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-CG40-003C-F348-00000-00&context=1530671). See generally Thomas Lee Hazen, 3 Law of Securities Regulation § 14.9 (2002).

Most cases follow the general rule stated in this section and in Restatement Second, Agency § 379(1). See [_Shields v. Cape Fox Corp., 42 P.3d 1083 (Alaska 2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45BB-P290-0039-41DC-00000-00&context=1530671). An exception is [_Brown v. United Cerebral Palsy/Atlantic & Cape May, Inc., 650 A.2d 848 (N.J.Super.App.Div.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W8Y0-003C-P3VP-00000-00&context=1530671), which holds that a standard of ordinary negligence is applicable to employees' nondiscretionary acts but not to acts of bad management that result in lost profits. But see [_Shields, 42 P.3d at 1091, n.29_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45BB-P290-0039-41DC-00000-00&context=1530671) (noting that § 379 leaves some questions unaddressed; it is "not free from doubt" whether § 379 permits recovery of lost profits).

_c. Duty of competence._ The formulation in this section borrows from that in Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 52(1) (lawyer who owes a duty of care "must exercise the competence and diligence normally exercised by lawyers in similar circumstances."). See also Principles of Corporate Governance: Analysis and Recommendations § 4.01(a) (officer's duty to corporation is "to perform the … officer's functions in good faith, in a manner that he or she reasonably believes to be in the best interests of the corporation, and with the care that an ordinarily prudent person would reasonably be expected to exercise in a like position and under similar circumstances.").

For the point that an agent may reasonably be expected to know the rules under which the agent's profession operates, see [_McCurnin v. Kohlmeyer & Co., 347 F.Supp. 573, 578 (E.D.La.1972)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-M2B0-003B-33RJ-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_477 F.2d 113 (5th Cir.1973)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-23H0-0039-X3WT-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[o\]ne dealing with a commodity broker is entitled to expect him to know the rules of the exchange and of the trade"; broker had duty to advise customer correctly when customer inquired about consequences of broker's unauthorized trade, and customer did not have duty to "second-guess" broker).

On an agent's duty to use special skills that the agent has or claims to have, see [_Garbish v. Malvern Fed. Sav. & Loan Ass'n, 517 A.2d 547, 554 (Pa.Super.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-0K60-003C-S24R-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[t\]he standard of care imposed on the fiduciary depends on whether or not the fiduciary is or claims to be an expert in the area to which his fiduciary duty relates. When the fiduciary either has or procures his appointment by asserting that he has skill beyond that of the ordinary prudent person, he will be judged by the level of skill that he has or that he claims to have, whichever is higher").

On specifics of duties of care in particular contexts, see, e.g., [_Gully v. National Credit Union Admin. Bd., 341 F.3d 155, 165 (2d Cir.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:49BS-3YP0-0038-X51Y-00000-00&context=1530671) (board's conclusion that credit-union manager breached fiduciary duties to credit union and engaged in unsafe and unsound practices was not arbitrary and capricious; after discovering consultant's illegal use of credit card, manager "blithely" accepted consultant's promise to repay credit union and stop misusing card; manager had conflict of interest in attempting to police consultant, who was her father, and also knew that credit union completely lacked internal controls); [_DeLeon v. Beneficial Constr. Co., 55 F.Supp.2d 819 (N.D.Ill.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3W0F-NN10-0038-Y371-00000-00&context=1530671) (mortgage brokers allegedly failed to perform service; although plaintiffs eventually obtained a mortgage, brokers did not do comparative shopping they were engaged to do); [_Golden Rule Ins. Corp. v. Greenfield, 786 F.Supp. 914, 916 (D.Colo.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-N130-008H-F16V-00000-00&context=1530671) (denying summary judgment for insurance agent; agent retained to obtain coverage and who assists applicant in completing policy application has duty to use reasonable care so that insurance policy remains effective); [_Das v. Royal Jordanian Airlines, 766 F. Supp. 169 (S.D.N.Y.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-CKG0-001T-73M8-00000-00&context=1530671) (travel agent has duty to traveler on whose behalf ticket is purchased to notify traveler that airline, having cancelled original flight, has demoted traveler from "confirmed" to "wait-listed" status on flight for which ticket is issued); [_Central Nat'l Ins. Co., 426 F. Supp. at 24-25_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-JFC0-0054-638S-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent's reliance on insured's property valuation not reasonable under the circumstances; although insured a highly respected organization, waiver of coinsurance clause in blanket policy left insurer vulnerable to insured's property valuation, even when error in valuation is inadvertent); [_Peter v. Schumacher Enters., Inc., 22 P.3d 481, 486 (Alaska 2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4320-J9C0-0039-41DJ-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurance agent ordinarily fulfills duty to client by providing requested coverage; agent may assume duty of selecting appropriate policy and, if agent has a special advisory relationship with client, is subject to duty to advise client, for example of advisability of choosing higher levels of under-insured motorist coverage); [_F.W. Myers & Co. v. Hunter Farms, 319 N.W.2d 186, 187-188 (Iowa 1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2SX0-003G-51NR-00000-00&context=1530671) (import broker does not have duty to client to explain widely known nature of tentative customs assessment); [_Brown v. Roth, 514 S.E.2d 294, 297 (N.C.App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3W9C-DSD0-0039-42K2-00000-00&context=1530671) (reversing grant of summary judgment for real-estate agent who represented both vendor and purchaser of large and unusually configured house; some evidence that agent fulfilled duty to disclose square footage of house to purchaser by relying on square-footage estimate made by vendor's appraiser not conclusive as matter of law in light of expectation articulated by state real-estate commission that agent personally determine square footage); [_Fleet Constr. Co. v. Aetna Life & Cas. Co., 746 A.2d 1247, 1250-1251 (R.I.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YTT-M8C0-0039-43SH-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurer and insurance agent do not have duty to classify construction project to provide insured with lowest possible rate, especially in arm's-length relationship with business customer); [_First Nat'l Bank v. Wernhart, 555 N.W.2d 819, 822-823 (Wis.App. 1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-X410-003G-34HT-00000-00&context=1530671) (as mortgagor's agent, mortgagee bank has duty of due care to mortgagor to assure funds paid for work actually done, mandating that bank inspect property or take other reasonable measures to assure compliance with construction contract prior to releasing funds; court notes split in authority among jurisdictions on scope of duty).

_d. Duty of diligence._ For the point that a securities broker's ordinary duty to a client with a nondiscretionary account is one of diligence and competence in executing orders received from the client, see de [_Kwiatkowski v. Bear, Stearns & Co., 306 F.3d 1293, 1302 (2d Cir.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:46T4-TV30-0038-X4KM-00000-00&context=1530671). In _de Kwiatkowski,_ the court held that the client's relationship with the broker was not converted into a relationship of special trust and confidence on the basis of the magnitude of the customer's trades; the customer, who held foreign-currency-exchange futures accounts, had holdings with magnitudes comparable to those of some sovereigns and was a glutton for risk, in the court's assessment. [_Id. at 1308_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:46T4-TV30-0038-X4KM-00000-00&context=1530671). After realizing gains in U.S. dollar trading of \$ 228 million over three months, the customer then suffered a single-day loss of \$ 112 million, [_id. at 1300_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:46T4-TV30-0038-X4KM-00000-00&context=1530671), but continued to trade aggressively over the next few months, losing \$ 116 million in a final weekend of trading, [_id. at 1301-1302_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:46T4-TV30-0038-X4KM-00000-00&context=1530671).

On the circumstances under which a broker may owe additional duties to a client on the basis of the client's trust and confidence, see James D. Cox et al., Securities Regulation 1093-1095 (2d ed. 1997) (discussing, inter alia, [_Hughes v. SEC, 174 F.2d 969 (D.C.Cir.1949)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-2CT0-003B-02M1-00000-00&context=1530671) (broker's style of doing business involved eliciting trust of clients in broker's recommended stock purchases) and [_Charles Hughes & Co. v. SEC, 139 F.2d 434 (2d Cir. 1943)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-T4R0-003B-T0CK-00000-00&context=1530671) (by going into securities business, broker-dealer impliedly represents that customers will be dealt with fairly, termed "shingle theory" of broker's obligation)). See also [_Associated Randall Bank v. Griffin, Kubik, Stephens & Thompson, Inc., 3 F.3d 208, 212 (7th Cir.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-DG70-003B-P1BP-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Wisconsin law; broker-dealer's duty of reasonable care is distinct from duty of reasonable investigation owed by securities underwriter).

For the point that, absent agreement otherwise, an agent's duty is to use reasonable effort, see, e.g., [_Carrier v. McLlarky, 693 A.2d 76, 78 (N.H. 1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-4F00-003G-B01P-00000-00&context=1530671) (installer of replacement water heater acted as agent of homeowner in undertaking to obtain credit from manufacturer against cost of new heater; installer made reasonable effort to obtain credit by returning old heater to supplier to return to manufacturer, did not fail to remit any refund actually received from manufacturer, and did not guarantee that such a refund would be received). See also [_Rude v. Larson, 207 N.W.2d 709, 711 (Minn.1973)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-3JT0-003G-V1RP-00000-00&context=1530671) (absent agreement to contrary, broker owes customer duty only to exercise due care in executing instructions and is not a guarantor or insurer against losses in customer's account).

_e. Gratuitous agents._ Illustration 5 is based on a simplified version of the facts of Chaudhry v. Prabhakar \[1989\] 1 W.L.R. (Ct. App.). For discussion, see Francis M.B. Reynolds, Bowstead & Reynolds on Agency 164 (17th ed. 2001). On the liability of gratuitous agents, see also [_Faith v. Keefer, 736 A.2d 422, 442 (Md.App. 1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XBW-6FN0-0039-42C6-00000-00&context=1530671) (passenger's claim against driver of passenger's own car; existence of agency relationship does not preclude passenger's claim against driver); [_Lee Hawkins Realty, Inc. v. Moss, 724 So. 2d 1116, 1119 (Miss. App.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3VCC-T5Y0-0039-43V7-00000-00&context=1530671) (vendor's agent may have gratuitously also become agent of purchaser by placing phone call for purchaser to confirm that house was covered by warranty; no evidence before trier of fact that agent breached any duty owed to purchaser); [_Delk v. Markel Am. Ins. Co., 81 P.3d 629, 640-641 (Okla.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:49V0-9M50-0039-40V0-00000-00&context=1530671) (cotenant who undertakes to insure premises is obligated to use reasonable care; fact that cotenant is subject to liability provides an insurable interest for purposes of property insurance).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 8.09_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8Y0-00YG-4010-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 8- Duties of Agent and Principal to Each Other_ > _Topic 1- Agent's Duties to Principal_ > _Title C- Duties of Performance_**

**§ 8.09 Duty to Act Only Within Scope of Actual Authority and to Comply with Principal's Lawful Instructions**

**(1) An agent has a duty to take action only within the scope of the agent's actual authority.**

**(2) An agent has a duty to comply with all lawful instructions received from the principal and persons designated by the principal concerning the agent's actions on behalf of the principal.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section states the basic rule that an agent's duty requires that the agent act only within the scope of actual authority, whether established through manifestations of the principal at the commencement of the agency relationship or through interim instructions from the principal. Comment _b_ discusses an agent's duty to act only with actual authority, that is, consistently with a reasonable interpretation of manifestations of assent made by the principal to the agent. Comment _c_ discusses an agent's duty to comply with lawful instructions furnished by a principal. Comment _d_ discusses the analogous duty of an escrow holder to comply with the terms of the escrow agreement.

Section 2.01 defines "actual authority." Section 2.02 states how the scope of an agent's actual authority is determined.

_b. Duty to act only within scope of actual authority._ The existence of a relationship of agency is premised on a manifestation of assent by a principal that an agent shall act on the principal's behalf. An agent acts with actual authority as stated in § 2.01 "when, at the time of taking action that has legal consequences for the principal, the agent reasonably believes, in accordance with the principal's manifestations to the agent, that the principal wishes the agent so to act." When an agent acts with actual authority, the agent's power to affect the principal's legal relations and the agent's right to do so are coextensive. Id., Comment _c._ In the context of the agent's relationship with the principal, the boundary of an agent's rightful action is the scope of the agent's actual authority. As stated in § 2.02(1), an agent has actual authority to take action for which the principal's assent is designated or implied in the principal's manifestations to the agent and to take actions that are necessary or incidental to achieving the principal's objectives as the agent reasonably understands them when the agent determines how to act. An agent's interpretation of a principal's manifestations and understanding of a principal's objectives must be reasonable. See § 2.02(2) and (3).

If an agent takes action beyond the scope of the agent's actual authority, the agent is subject to liability to the principal for loss caused the principal. The principal's loss may stem from actions taken by the agent with apparent authority, on the basis of which the principal became subject to liability to third parties. The principal's loss may include costs the principal incurs in defending against lawsuits brought against the principal by third parties. By taking action beyond the scope of actual authority, an agent may become subject to a duty to provide information to the principal that is material to the principal. See § 8.11. It will often be material to the principal to know that the agent has taken unauthorized action and to know what measures may be required to repudiate any transaction stemming from the agent's unauthorized action. See id., Comment _b._ An agent's duty to take action only within the scope of actual authority survives termination of the agent's actual authority and of the agency relationship. On the continuation of an agent's apparent authority following termination, see § 3.11.

**Illustration:**

1\. A acts as P Insurance Co.'s agent for purposes of issuing policies of workers'-compensation insurance. A issues a binder policy to T. A acts with apparent authority in issuing the policy but A issues it in a manner clearly prohibited by A's agency agreement with P Insurance Co. E, an employee of T, suffers a job-related injury. P Insurance Company must pay the claim even though A acted without actual authority in issuing the policy. A exceeded A's actual authority in issuing the insurance policy to T. A is subject to liability to P Insurance Co. for the amount of E's claim against P Insurance Co. A is also subject to liability for any costs P Insurance Co. incurred in opposing T's claim.

If an agent's action beyond the scope of the agent's actual authority causes loss to the principal, the agent is subject to liability to the principal for that loss even though the loss would have been greater had the agent acted consistently with the agent's actual authority. Were the rule otherwise, an agent might be tempted to act or to continue to act without actual authority in the hope that matters will turn out sufficiently well that the principal will not suffer loss. Moreover, the underlying premise of a relationship of agency is action by the agent that is consistent with the principal's manifestation of assent, not whether an agent's action is in fact beneficial to the principal.

However, an agent's departure from the scope of actual authority does not impose on the agent the risk of all losses that the principal may subsequently suffer. Upon learning that an agent has acted without actual authority or has failed to act as the principal directed or wished, the principal's right to be indemnified against subsequent losses by the agent does not extend to loss that the principal could have avoided. Subjecting an agent to liability for all losses suffered by a principal once the principal has notice that the agent has acted without authority would make the agent a guarantor of subsequent speculation by the principal.

**Illustrations:**

2\. P, an investor, maintains an account with A Bank for the purpose of trading in foreign currency. A Bank assigns E to P's account as P's account executive. P places an order to liquidate a short position that P holds. Had P's order been executed that day, P would have realized a profit of \$ 8000. E relays P's order to a foreign bank for execution, as is customary in A Bank's relationship with P. The following day, E realizes that P's order has not been executed, so informs P, and tells P that the order can be executed immediately. Had P's order been executed then, P would have realized a profit of \$ 4700. However, P tells E not to execute the order at the then-current price, telling E, "Don't worry, I am bullish." P waits a month to direct that the order be executed and realizes a loss of over \$ 400,000. A Bank's liability to P is limited to the amount of profit P lost because P's order was not executed promptly, measured by the difference between the price at which P could have executed the transaction within a reasonable time after learning that it had not been effected earlier and the price at which the transaction should have been executed when P placed the order to sell.

3\. P opens an account with A Bank for the purpose of trading in pork-belly futures in the commodities market. A Bank assigns E to P's account as P's account executive. E, who disagrees with the wisdom of P's trading strategy, makes several trades contrary to P's directions. P reprimands E but does not complain to E's superiors. When the pork-belly market is dropping rapidly, P learns that E has made additional trades in direct contravention of P's orders. Having lost confidence in E and in A Bank, P demands that A Bank close P's account immediately and remit the balance to P. A Bank delays in so doing. P does not have sufficient assets otherwise to minimize P's losses without the funds in P's account at A Bank. A Bank is subject to liability to P for the loss P suffered due to E's last set of unauthorized trades. P was unable to avoid the losses caused by E's last set of unauthorized trades due to A Bank's delay in closing P's account. A Bank is not subject to liability for losses suffered by P due to E's earlier set of unauthorized trades because, although P reprimanded E, P did not repudiate E's unauthorized trades. Alternatively, P's failure to manifest objection to E's earlier set of unauthorized trades may ratify them as stated in § 4.01(2)(b) if P had notice that others were likely to infer P's assent to the trades from P's failure to repudiate them. See § 4.01, Comment _d._

Mitigation does not require that a principal undertake measures beyond those that are reasonable to avoid subsequent losses. A principal's ability to mitigate loss caused by an agent's unauthorized activity may, as in Illustration 3, be stymied when the agent continues to hold the assets needed to mitigate. Moreover, it is unreasonable to expect a principal to attempt to mitigate by permitting the agent to continue to deal on the principal's behalf when, as in Illustration 3, the agent's disregard for the scope of authorized action causes the principal to lose confidence in the agent's fidelity to the principal's wishes.

If an agent takes action beyond the scope of the agent's actual authority, the agent is not subject to liability to the principal if the principal ratifies the agent's action. A principal may ratify an unauthorized action by failing to repudiate it once the principal knows the material facts about the agent's action. See § 4.01, Comment _f_ (discussing when a principal's failure to act effects ratification) and § 4.06 (knowledge required for ratification).

Once a relationship of agency has terminated, the former agent has a duty to the former principal to cease acting or purporting to act as that principal's agent. A former agent who remains in possession of property of the principal holds it on the principal's behalf and is subject to duties stated in §§ 8.05 and 8.12 in using and handling the property.

_c. Duty to comply with lawful instructions._ Within a relationship of agency, a principal always has power to provide an agent with interim instructions concerning action to be taken on the principal's behalf. The power to give interim instructions is an integral part of a principal's control over an agent and a defining element in a relationship of common-law agency. See § 1.01, Comment _f(1)._ A principal and an agent may agree that the principal will not give interim instructions or otherwise interfere with the agent's exercise of discretion. If so, the principal's subsequent statement of an interim instruction may breach contractual duties owed by the principal to the agent and subject the principal to liability to the agent. See id. Even though principal and agent have previously agreed otherwise, an agent has a duty to comply with lawful instructions received from the principal. This is so although the agent believes that doing otherwise would be better for the principal. Moreover, regardless of the discretion that an agent exercises or the agent's relative status within an organizational hierarchy, an agent has a duty to comply with lawful instructions provided by the principal or the agent's superior within the organization.

In interpreting instructions received from a principal, the agent's interpretation must be reasonable, determined from the standpoint of a reasonable person in the agent's position under all the circumstances of which the agent has notice, including the principal's current circumstances. See § 2.02, Comment _e._ If an instruction is unambiguous, unless an agent has reason to believe that the principal does not intend the instruction as stated or wishes the agent to reconfirm it, the agent has no duty to press the principal for additional confirmation of the principal's intentions.

**Illustration:**

4\. P Bank employs A as a loan officer. P Bank gives A substantial discretion to determine whether to make any particular loan. P Bank also tells A not to exceed a limit of \$ 500,000 in commercial loans to any one customer. A makes a loan on P Bank's behalf to T in the amount of \$ 1 million, honestly believing that T is a good credit risk and will be a good source of additional business for P Bank. A believes that P Bank does not intend A's lending limit to restrict A's lending authority when A harbors such beliefs about a particular borrower. A is subject to liability to P Bank for loss P Bank suffers as a consequence of A's loan to T in an amount that greatly exceeds A's lending limit. A did not comply with P Bank's unambiguous instructions. It is not a defense for A that A believed in good faith that P Bank's instructions should be disregarded.

In general, when an agent breaches the duties to the principal stated in this section, the agent has not breached duties of loyalty owed to the principal. However, an agent's breach of the duties stated in this section may be closely coupled with breaches of the agent's duties of loyalty.

**Illustration:**

5\. P, who owns Blackacre, furnishes A with a power of attorney that authorizes A to sell Blackacre but does not authorize A to make a gift of Blackacre, whether to A or anyone else. Having learned that P has agreed to sell Blackacre to T, A creates an irrevocable trust with an instrument that names A as sole trustee and P as income beneficiary and further provides that the trust corpus upon P's death shall be divided among A and A's siblings. A then conveys ownership of Blackacre to A as trustee of the trust. When P learns what A has done, P demands that A reconvey Blackacre to P. A refuses. A is subject to liability to P. A violated P's instructions. Additionally, A's conveyance of Blackacre to the trust breached A's duty of loyalty to P as stated in § 8.02 because, without P's consent, A acquired a material benefit through A's use of A's position. Separately, A's conveyance of Blackacre and subsequent refusal to reconvey Blackacre to P breached A's duty of loyalty to P as stated in § 8.05 because, without P's consent, A has used P's property for A's own purposes.

At some level of excess, unauthorized transactions that confer large benefits on a third party without a corresponding prospect of benefit for the principal may resemble gifts and thus call into question whether the agent has used the agent's position to deal with the principal on behalf of the third party, see § 8.03, has made unconsented-to use of the principal's assets for the third party's purposes, see § 8.05(1), or has failed to act loyally for the principal's benefit, see § 8.01. The terms of the transaction and the degree to which it exceeds the scope of the agent's actual authority are relevant to this determination.

An agent's duty to comply with instructions is not absolute. An agent has no duty to comply with instructions that may subject the agent to criminal, civil, or administrative sanctions or that exceed legal limits on the principal's right to direct action taken by the agent. Thus, an agent has no duty to comply with a directive to commit a crime or an act the agent has reason to know will be tortious. An agent who is a member of a profession does not have a duty to follow instructions given by the principal that expose the agent to discipline for violating professional rules. On the right of a lawyer to refuse to perform an act the lawyer reasonably believes to be unlawful, see Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 23, Comment _c_ (defining unlawful acts to be those that expose a lawyer to "civil or criminal liability, including procedural sanctions, or discipline for violation of professional rules"). A contract provision in which an agent promises to perform an unlawful act is unenforceable.

When an agent determines not to comply with an instruction, the agent has a duty to so inform the principal. See § 8.11, Comment _d._

_d. Duty of escrow holder to comply with terms of escrow agreement._ A holder of an escrow contracts to hold money, a deed, or some other asset until the occurrence or nonoccurrence of a specified event before a specified date; the escrow contract states the holder's duties to deliver or return the escrow upon the occurrence or nonoccurrence of the specified event. An escrow arrangement places the escrowed deed or other property beyond the control of parties to a transaction for a specified time and determines when the holder may properly make delivery. Prior to the occurrence or nonoccurrence of the specified event, an escrow holder is not an agent as defined in § 1.01 because the holder does not assent to acting subject to the control of the parties to the escrow contract. An escrow holder, although often termed an "escrow agent," is more precisely comparable to a holder of a power given as security for the benefit of a third party other than the power holder. See § 3.12, Comment _b._

To achieve the purposes for which an escrow is created, the escrow holder has a duty to adhere to the terms of the escrow agreement. If an escrow holder breaches its duty to adhere to the terms of the agreement, the holder is subject to liability to parties for whose benefit the escrow was created for loss caused by the holder's failure to adhere to the terms of the agreement. The stringency of an escrow holder's duty of compliance provides security to parties who structure transactions that involve a risk of nonconsummation. For example, in an "all or nothing" offering of stock, subscribers to the offering make payment into an escrow account, to be held for the corporation that will issue its stock to them if subscriptions are received for the number of shares set in the escrow agreement. If subscriptions are not received for that number of shares by a date set in the escrow agreement, the escrow holder has a duty to return the funds to the subscribers. The escrow structure provides investors with an assurance that the issuer will receive capital in a sufficient amount and that their money will be returned by a certain date if the conditions stated in the escrow agreement are not met.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency, and codifications._ This section is the counterpart to Restatement Second, Agency §§ 383 and 385.

Ga. Code § 10-6-21 (2000 & Supp. 2005) provides that "\[t\]he agent shall act within the authority granted to him, reasonably interpreted; if he shall exceed or violate his instructions, he does it at his own risk, the principal having the privilege of affirming or dissenting, as his interest may dictate. In cases where the power is coupled with an interest in the agent, unreasonable instructions, detrimental to the agent's interest, may be disregarded."

Under La. Civ. Code art. 3008 (2005), "\[i\]f the mandatary exceeds his authority, he is answerable to the principal for resulting loss that the principal sustains. The principal is not answerable to the mandatary for loss that the mandatary sustains because of acts that exceed his authority unless the principal ratifies those acts."

_b. Duty to act only within scope of actual authority._ For the basic point that an agent has a duty not to exceed the scope of actual authority, see, e.g., [_Thropp v. Bache Halsey Stuart Shields, Inc., 650 F.2d 817, 820-821 (6th Cir.1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-1WN0-0039-W2JY-00000-00&context=1530671) (stockbroker negligently liquidated assets in client's account on authority of forged power of attorney; transactions not authorized by client); [_Securities Investor Prot. Corp. v. R.D. Kushnir & Co., 267 B.R. 819, 824 (Bankr.N.D.Ill. 2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:444H-1ST0-0039-03B7-00000-00&context=1530671) (broker subject to liability to clients for loss caused by effecting unauthorized transactions in their account); [_Goss v. Hill, 149 A.2d 10, 12 (Md.1959)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S12-2P50-003D-Y2MG-00000-00&context=1530671) (vendors' agent returned forfeited deposit to purchaser without any instruction from sellers to do so; agent not entitled to recover commission from vendors); [_McCrory v. Spigel, 740 A.2d 1274, 1276-1277 (R.I. 1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XYG-XVX0-0039-453Y-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent of owners of car dealership, without their authority, used their sales license to sell a stolen car; owners entitled to recover from agent the amount they refunded to purchaser of stolen car).

On the duty of an agent to inform the principal of the agent's unauthorized actions and of reasonable courses of action open to the principal, see [_Merrill Lynch, Pierce, Fenner & Smith, Inc. v. Cheng, 901 F.2d 1124 (D.C.Cir.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-5NX0-003B-517P-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Ward v. Atlantic Sec. Bank, 777 So. 2d 1144, 1146 (Fla.Dist.App.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:42C6-W2Y0-0039-4393-00000-00&context=1530671) (reversing summary judgment on behalf of bank that managed mutual fund in which plaintiff invested; bank failed to sell shares as instructed, and then took "proactive step" of contacting plaintiff to stop plaintiff from selling while concealing negative information concerning fund to keep it afloat).

Illustration 1 is based on [_Hartford Cas. Ins. Co. v. Walker County Agency, Inc., 808 S.W.2d 681 (Tex.App. 1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3K-2320-003C-24CM-00000-00&context=1530671).

On an agent's duty to indemnify the principal, see [_Putnam v. DeRosa, 963 F.2d 480, 485 (1st Cir.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-4000-008H-V2MV-00000-00&context=1530671) (when salesman made misrepresentations to purchasers of apartments that finder of fact could find may have been authorized by principal, principal not entitled to indemnity because principal is not blameless); [_Securities Investor Prot. Corp., 267 B.R. at 824_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:444H-1ST0-0039-03B7-00000-00&context=1530671); [_McCrory, 740 A.2d at 1277_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3XYG-XVX0-0039-453Y-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 2 is based on [_Saboundjian v. Bank Audi (USA), 556 N.Y.S. 2d 258 (App.Div.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S2R-9C70-003V-B2S6-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 3 is based on [_Theis v. duPont, Glore Forgan Inc., 510 P.2d 1212 (Kan.1973)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-X320-003F-F0B1-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_McCurnin v. Kohlmeyer & Co., 347 F. Supp. 573, 578 (E.D.La.1972)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-M2B0-003B-33RJ-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_477 F.2d 113 (5th Cir.1973)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-23H0-0039-X3WT-00000-00&context=1530671) (customer of cotton broker, who violated customer's instructions and entered into unauthorized contract, "manifested his displeasure" to broker after learning of transaction but was told by broker there was nothing he could do; customer did not ratify unauthorized transaction).

On the consequences of a principal's ratification for the agent's liability, see [_National Inspection & Repair, Inc. v. Valley Forge Life Ins. Co., 56 P.3d 807, 821-823 (Kan.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:474W-S560-0039-42CG-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurer ratified agent's unauthorized acceptance of application for key-man life-insurance policy and initial premium when, knowing of action, insurer failed to repudiate it and retained premium; agent did not have duty to reimburse insurer for any loss suffered due to his unauthorized conduct); [_Barta v. Kindschuh, 518 N.W.2d 98, 100-101 (Neb.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-4DS0-003D-B0YJ-00000-00&context=1530671) (vendors' agent failed to correct statements regarding condition of roof; even if agent exceeded authority by failing to make corrections, vendors acquiesced in agent's failure and ratified agent's acts, thereby releasing agent from liability to them for damages resulting from suit brought by purchasers). See also [_Apple Glen Crossing, LLC v. Trademark Retail, Inc., 784 N.E.2d 484, 488-489 (Ind. 2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4840-2GG0-0039-4345-00000-00&context=1530671) (ratification of agent's unauthorized action does not preclude claim against agent when necessary to protect principal's interests; fact that owner of shopping center honored obligations to building contractor did not cure alleged default by managing agent that, under terms of operating agreement, entitled owner to remove manager; default arose when, contrary to terms of agreement, manager approved change orders).

_c. Duty to comply with lawful instructions._ On an agent's interpretation of instructions received from the principal, see [_Thompson v. Federico, 324 F.Supp.2d 1152, 1168-1169 (D.Or. 2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4CVX-67P0-0038-Y091-00000-00&context=1530671) (material issue of fact whether client with nondiscretionary account instructed stockbroker to sell trust's equity holdings; client instructed broker by letter to sell "'some positions,'" which client understood to refer to all of trust's equity holdings as opposed to cash and bonds but broker characterized as ambiguous). For the point that an agent has a duty to comply with lawful instructions although another course of conduct appears preferable, see [_William Stevens, Ltd. v. Kings Vill. Corp., 650 N.Y.S.2d 307, 308 (App.Div.1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RWS-NN00-003V-B54H-00000-00&context=1530671) (owner of apartment cooperative had cause to terminate managing agent when managing agent, contrary to directive of cooperative's board of directors, filed involuntary bankruptcy petition against sponsor of cooperative's sponsor).

On the basic point that an agent has a duty to comply with lawful instructions, see [_Cecka v. Beckman & Co., 104 Cal.Rptr. 374 (Cal.App.1972)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-8S50-003C-J1WW-00000-00&context=1530671) (brokerage firm failed to carry out client's instruction to maintain same relative position in futures contracts for cattle in margin account); [_Jarnagin v. Terry, 807 S.W.2d 190, 194 (Mo. App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-87M0-003F-C4FP-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[a\]n agent is bound to obey the specific instructions of the principal and to follow them faithfully, where the agent has undertaken to perform them, unless prevented by unavoidable accident or unless the instructions require the doing of an immoral or illegal act"; lawyer subject to liability for loss caused divorced spouse by lawyer's failure to follow instructions given by divorcing couple on distribution of property and assumption of debt).

For the point that the duty to comply with lawful instructions is applicable to a highly placed agent within an organization, see [_Reilly v. Polychrome Corp., 872 F.Supp. 1265, 1269 (S.D.N.Y.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-VDC0-001T-5561-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _71 F.3d 405 (2d Cir.1995)_ (corporation's vice-president committed material breach of employment contract by disobeying president's order that he report to work; fact that vice-president claimed he believed his absence was unimportant to corporation at time does not justify his absence: "\[vice-president\] was well aware that his superior had instructed him to report to work, and his absence continued for three days despite \[employer's\] letters notifying him of his impending dismissal if he failed to report to work"). For an example of an agreement relieving an agent of the duty, see [_Reale v. Sotheby's, Inc., 718 N.Y.S.2d 37, 38 (Div. 2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:422F-MTC0-0039-40CT-00000-00&context=1530671) (consignment agreement gave auction house "'absolute discretion'" to consult with any expert concerning consigned property; auction house did not breach duty to consignor of coin collection by consulting with expert after consignor stated he wanted no involvement with particular coin expert due to consignor's personal animus toward expert).

On the lack of a duty to reconfirm unambiguous instructions, see [_Holtz v. J.J.B. Hilliard W.L. Lyons, Inc., 185 F.3d 732, 741-742 (7th Cir.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WYF-4V70-0038-X082-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Indiana law; broker had no duty to inquire about customer's intentions in completing form that changed retirement account without designating a beneficiary when customer controlled discretionary account and did not turn to broker for advice or guidance).

Illustration 4 is based on a simplified version of the facts of [_OmniBank v. United S. Bank, 607 So. 2d 76 (Miss.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-4GB0-003G-745K-00000-00&context=1530671) (loan officer's subjective good faith in making loan that greatly exceeded lending limit is not a defense to officer's substantial deviation from instructions and officer's incompetence; no defense that superior officer in bank also breached duty to bank through negligent supervision of loan officer).

Illustration 5 is based on [_Gagnon v. Coombs, 654 N.E.2d 54 (Mass.App. 1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-9420-003C-V0Y7-00000-00&context=1530671). For another illustration of the same underlying point, see [_IPSCO Steel, Inc. v. Blaine Constr. Corp., 371 F.3d 141, 148 (3d Cir.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4CKG-HDH0-0038-X2MB-00000-00&context=1530671) (construction manager's duty of loyalty to project owner obliged it to effectuate owner's manifested wish to effect settlement with insurer; manager instead refused to consent to settlement when its self-interest was to ensure that funds remained available under "wasting" insurance policy to pay for its own future defense costs).

For the point that breaches of duties not to exceed authority and to comply with instructions are not breaches of an agent's duty of loyalty, but that "gift-like" transactions breach the duty of loyalty, see [_Omni-Bank, 607 So. 2d at 91_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-4GB0-003G-745K-00000-00&context=1530671).

Compliance is not required when a principal's instructions or demands are not lawful. See, e.g., [_Ford v. Wisconsin Real Estate Examining Bd., 179 N.W.2d 786, 790 (Wis.1970)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-WPH0-003G-313K-00000-00&context=1530671) (real-estate agent complied with instruction to engage in racially discriminatory acts in showing principal's property; agent has no duty to perform illegal acts); cf. [_Caesar Elec. Inc. v. Andrews, 905 F.2d 287, 289-290 (9th Cir.1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-4V50-003B-539D-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent immune from civil liability when agent cooperated in good faith with federal authorities in criminal investigation of principal). It is not a defense to an agent that the agent followed an instruction to take action known by the agent to be criminal or tortious. See Carol M. Rice, The Superior Orders Defense in Legal Ethics: Sending the Wrong Message to Young Lawyers, [_32 Wake Forest L. Rev. 887, 906-908 (1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:3S3T-W8B0-00CW-40H4-00000-00&context=1530671).

_d. Duty of escrow holder to comply with terms of escrow agreement._ For the basic point that administration of funds in escrow does not by itself create an agency relationship, see [_Telfair v. First Union Mortgage Corp., 216 F.3d 1333, 1342 (11th Cir. 2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40SM-6560-0038-X45R-00000-00&context=1530671). For general discussion of escrow relationships, see J. Dennis Hynes & Mark Loewenstein, Agency, Partnership, and the LLC: The Law of Unincorporated Business Enterprises 40-45 (6th ed. 2003). An escrow agent has been characterized as an "agent to the terms of the escrow contract." See [_Fasciana v. Electronic Data Sys. Corp., 829 A.2d 160, 173 n.44 (Del.Ch.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4864-KNN0-0039-4525-00000-00&context=1530671). An escrow holder's duties are defined by the escrow instructions. See [_Denaxas v. Sandstone Court, L.L.C., 63 P.3d 125 (Wash.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:47VY-VB60-0039-418H-00000-00&context=1530671) (escrow holder did not breach duty by failing to detect and point out discrepancies in documents by comparing them when escrow instructions did not require comparison).

On escrow arrangements in "all or nothing" stock offerings, see [_Gomez v. Huntington Trust Co., N.A., 129 F.Supp.2d 1116 (N.D.Ohio 2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4281-GCR0-0038-Y2NR-00000-00&context=1530671) (escrow holder breached duty of adherence to terms of escrow agreement by retaining subscribers' funds past termination date stated in agreement; stock purchasers after termination date had claims against holder as third-party beneficiaries of agreement when they were told that their investments were made subject to terms of agreement, unless escrow holder establishes waiver or ratification by purchasers of its breaches of duty). A court may, in appropriate circumstances, recharacterize a party termed an "escrow agent" as the agent of one party to a transaction. See [_Paul v. Kennedy, 102 A.2d 158 (Pa.1954)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-VH30-003C-M4KK-00000-00&context=1530671) (business broker engaged by owner to sell business who embezzled purchase money deposited with broker characterized by court as seller's agent although sale agreement called for "deposit in escrow" with broker; court grants specific performance of sales agreement to buyer on basis that buyer made full payment to seller's agent).
