[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 8.10_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8Y0-00YG-4011-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 8- Duties of Agent and Principal to Each Other_ > _Topic 1- Agent's Duties to Principal_ > _Title C- Duties of Performance_**

**§ 8.10 Duty of Good Conduct**

**An agent has a duty, within the scope of the agency relationship, to act reasonably and to refrain from conduct that is likely to damage the principal's enterprise.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope._ This section states the duty of good conduct owed by an agent to the principal. Section 2.02(2) states when an agent's interpretation of a principal's manifestation is reasonable. For further discussion of interpretation by an agent, see § 2.02, Comments _f_ and _g._

_b. Good conduct--in general._ An agent acts as a principal's representative or otherwise acts on behalf of a principal with power to affect the principal's legal relations with third parties. See § 1.01, Comment _c._ The conduct of agents appointed by a principal may constitute the basis on which third parties assess the principal's reputation; when a principal is an organization and not an individual person, its agents may largely define its character. Regardless of its size, power, or wealth, a principal is always vulnerable to the impact that its agents' actions may carry for its reputation.

The nature of the principal's activities and the agent's position, as well as manifestations of the principal, are relevant to whether an agent is subject to a duty to refrain from particular conduct. An agent's duty to refrain from engaging in conduct may extend to conduct that, although it is beyond the scope of activity encompassed by the agency relationship itself, is nonetheless closely connected to the principal or the principal's enterprise and is likely to bring the principal or the principal's enterprise into disrepute. If an agent breaches the duty of good conduct, it is not a defense that the agent's performance has otherwise been satisfactory to the principal.

**Illustrations:**

1\. P Van Lines, Inc., an interstate shipper, enters into a contract with A Warehouse, Inc., providing that A Warehouse, Inc., shall act as an agent of P Van Lines, Inc., in furnishing storage for household and commercial goods shipped by other agents of P Van Lines, Inc. C, the Chief Executive Officer of A Warehouse, Inc., pleads guilty to an indictment charging C with submitting false bids to a federal agency for the intrastate moving of companies dislocated by federal urban-renewal projects. C submitted the false bids on stationery bearing the name of P Van Lines, Inc. C's conduct as an agent of A Warehouse, Inc., breaches A Warehouse's duty of good conduct to P Van Lines, Inc. Although C submitted the false bids in connection with intrastate moving, C did so using stationery bearing the name of P Van Lines, Inc. C's conduct also breaches C's duty of good conduct to A Warehouse, Inc.

2\. P Church enters into a contract to employ A for one year as its music director. A, a bodybuilder, is arrested in P Church's parking lot after A illegally receives a shipment of anabolic steroids on church premises. A newspaper article about A's arrest mentions that A is employed by P Church. A's conduct breaches A's duty of good conduct to P Church. The publicity that followed A's illegal acts indirectly involved P Church in them.

3\. Same facts as Illustration 2, except that A's arrest goes unnoticed by news media. Same result. A used P Church's premises in A's commission of illegal acts.

In Illustrations 1, 2, and 3, the agent's conduct is illegal and in close proximity to the principal's business. Conduct that is not illegal may breach an agent's duty of good conduct when it is likely to damage the principal's business reputation.

**Illustration:**

4\. P operates a health-food store, "Veggie Delight," located in a shopping mall. P's store markets itself as an enterprise dedicated to promoting vegan eating habits. P requires its employees to wear T-shirts throughout the workday that bear the insignia: "Veggie Delight. The Vegan Way." P employs A as manager of Veggie Delight. Wearing the T-shirt provided by P, A regularly eats lunch at the steak house, "Cattle Call," opposite Veggie Delight in the mall, publicly consuming large orders of meat. A's conduct breaches A's duty of good conduct to P. The nature of P's business requires A to refrain from public consumption of meat and other substances inconsistent with vegan eating habits. By publicly eating meat on a regular basis, A reflects adversely on the credibility of P's enterprise. The close proximity of Cattle Call to Veggie Delight makes it more likely that prospective customers of Veggie Delight will notice A's lack of adherence to vegan eating habits and infer that A does not endorse P's message.

To be sure, A in Illustration 4 has a right to eat meat, but A's right to eat meat is qualified by A's duties to P. The manner and context of A's conduct are also significant. The connotations of A's public consumption of meat, in close physical proximity to P's vegan premises and while garbed in a promotional T-shirt furnished by P, are in conflict with P's reasonable interest that a high-level representative not deliberately flaunt a challenge to a basic premise underlying P's business. Similarly, a lawyer's right freely to express the lawyer's opinions on matters of public concern "is modified by the lawyer's duties to clients." See Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 125, Comment _e._

A principal may know that a prospective agent has a propensity for particular conduct that the principal views as undesirable but nonetheless engage the agent. For example, P in Illustration 4 may hire A to manage Veggie Delight notwithstanding P's knowledge that A regularly eats meat. Subsequent consumption of meat by A does not breach A's duty of good conduct to P unless and until A knows or has reason to know that P regards it as a breach of A's duties. This is because it is likely to be reasonable for A to assume that P's decision to hire A, knowing of A's carnivorous tastes, reflected P's assessment that A's strengths as a manager outweighed A's eating habits in their importance to P.

A contract between a principal and an agent may prescribe standards for the agent's conduct that expand, contract, or coincide with the limits established by common-law agency. An agent who breaches the agent's duty of good conduct is subject to liability to the principal, including liability for breach of contract to the extent that the breach contravenes an express or implied term of the contract and causes any damages to the principal.

An agent may have a right to engage in some forms of conduct that a principal views as objectionable. For example, some statutes protect whistleblowing employees against reprisals when the agent believes in good faith that the principal or other agents of the principal are engaged in wrongdoing. See § 8.05, Comment _c._

The typical remedy for breach of the duty stated in this section is discharge or termination. Even when an agent has a contract that assures the agent of employment or appointment for a fixed term of employment, the agent's breach of the duty of good conduct privileges the principal to discharge the agent.

_c. Amicable relations._ Many agency relationships will not achieve the principal's objectives unless the course of the relationship between agent and principal, or between an agent and the principal's other agents, is sufficiently smooth to be functional. It has been said of an agent, "\[h\]e need not render cheerful obedience, but he must not be insubordinate in speech or by other manifestation, either to the principal or to third persons." Restatement Second, Agency § 380, Comment _b._ However, conduct by a principal that provokes an intemperate response from an agent may justify the agent's response and preclude the principal's right to treat the agent's conduct as a breach of duty.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency._ This section is the counterpart of Restatement Second, Agency § 380.

_b. Good conduct--in general._ Illustration 1 is based on [_Jarboe Bros. Storage Warehouses, Inc. v. Allied Van Lines, Inc., 400 F.2d 743 (4th Cir.1968)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-V8G0-0039-Y3C9-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 2 is based on [_McGarry v. St. Anthony of Padua Roman Catholic Church, 704 A.2d 1353 (N.J.Super.App.Div.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S41-MG00-0039-43MH-00000-00&context=1530671). Misconduct by an agent inconsistent with the principal's interests justifies discharge of the agent, notwithstanding satisfactory service in other respects. See [_id. at 1357_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S41-MG00-0039-43MH-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[e\]ven where, as here, the employee performs the duties contracted for satisfactorily, criminal activity by the employee can justify his discharge for breach of an employment contract. . . . '\[m\]isconduct of an employee which is inconsistent with the relation of employer and employee justifies discharge,'" quoting 30 C.J.S. § 64). See also Murray v. Bragge (1889) 7 N.Z.L.R. 252 (discharge justified when schoolmaster's horse seen tied up outside house of ill repute).

On conduct that may embarrass a principal, see [_Griep v. Yamaha Motor Corp., 120 F.Supp.2d 1196, 1201 (D.Minn.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:41SS-BFK0-0038-Y3KH-00000-00&context=1530671) (mechanic for snowmobile-racing team sponsored by defendant, in response to dare at awards banquet, opened trousers; granting summary judgment for defendant in mechanic's action for breach of contract requiring notice prior to discharge, court holds mechanic's behavior breached "an implied contractual obligation … to observe rudimentary principles of appropriate behavior" because it "might well jeopardize \[defendant's\] business interests and reputation"); [_Marsh v. Delta Air Lines, Inc., 952 F.Supp. 1458 (D.Colo.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-H080-00B1-F1RY-00000-00&context=1530671) (upholding discharge of employee who wrote letter critical of employer's customer service for publication in newspaper; employee's public communication not shielded by Colo. Rev. Stat. § 24-34-402.5, which protects employees from discharge for private activity conducted off of employer's premises, of which employer disapproves). See also Clouston & Co., Ltd. v. Corry \[1906\] A.C. 122, 129 (P.C.) ("there is no fixed rule of law defining the degree of misconduct which will justify dismissal"; question for jury whether employee's indulgence in weekend drunkenness and loutish behavior in small New Zealand town, which led to criminal conviction, warranted dismissal in light of position as manager of grain and produce department).

For the point that a principal may engage an agent despite knowledge of conduct that the principal views as objectionable, see [_Kippen v. American Automatic Typewriter Co., 324 F.2d 742, 744-745 (9th Cir.1963)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-28R0-0039-Y061-00000-00&context=1530671) (manufacturer engaged distributor known to be an alcoholic; manufacturer not "entitled to call to an end to \[distributor's\] contract, without affording him an opportunity to correct his conduct, unless \[distributor\] knew, or should have known, that \[manufacturer\] regarded his drinking conduct as a breach of his implied duties under the contract"; distributorship relationship comparable to agency on this point).

_c. Amicable relations._ On conduct by a principal that provokes an otherwise objectionable response from an agent, see [_Helsby v. St. Paul Hosp. & Cas. Co., 195 F.Supp. 385, 394 (D.Minn.1961)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-JYV0-003B-20DJ-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, [_304 F.2d 758 (8th Cir.1962)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-3PV0-0039-Y09Y-00000-00&context=1530671) (affirming trial court's finding that insurer did not have cause to terminate agent although at "gathering of insurance people," insurance agent accused vice-president of insurance company of "undertaking to 'steal'" the insurance program that agent had developed; trial court properly left to jury to determine whether cause was present for termination and jury "may well have determined that \[agent\] was not without provocation and some justification in making his charge"). See also [_Griffin Grocery Co. v. Thaxton, 11 S.W.2d 473, 473-474 (Ark.1928)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YWF-WHX0-00KR-D30X-00000-00&context=1530671) (relationship between employee and president of corporation, friendly for years, became characterized by "reciprocal caustic criticisms in frequent and lengthy letters written by each to the other"; court would not have been justified in directing a verdict for employer following discharge of employee when employee's alleged insubordination and insolence were "provoked, in a measure by letters from \[employer's\] president").

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 8.11_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T8Y0-00YG-4012-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 8- Duties of Agent and Principal to Each Other_ > _Topic 1- Agent's Duties to Principal_ > _Title C- Duties of Performance_**

**§ 8.11 Duty to Provide Information**

**An agent has a duty to use reasonable effort to provide the principal with facts that the agent knows, has reason to know, or should know when**

**(1) subject to any manifestation by the principal, the agent knows or has reason to know that the principal would wish to have the facts or the facts are material to the agent's duties to the principal; and**

**(2) the facts can be provided to the principal without violating a superior duty owed by the agent to another person.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section states an agent's duty to furnish information to the principal. Comment _b_ discusses the general operation of this duty and the basis for its imposition. Comment _c_ discusses the scope of the duty, including its temporal limits. Comment _d_ examines the relationship between this duty and others to which an agent is subject, including the agent's duty of care stated in § 8.08 and the duties of loyalty stated in §§ 8.01 to 8.05.

Notice of a fact that an agent knows or has reason to know is imputed to the principal as stated in §§ 5.03 and 5.04 for purposes of determining a principal's legal relations with third parties.

_b. In general._ A principal's agents link the principal to the external world for purposes of acquiring information as well as for purposes of taking action. See § 5.03, Comment _b._ An agent owes the principal a duty to provide information to the principal that the agent knows or has reason to know the principal would wish to have. An agent also owes the principal a duty, subject to any manifestation by the principal, to provide information to the principal that is material to the agent's duties to the principal. The principal may direct that information be furnished to another agent or another person designated by the principal. The agent's duty is satisfied if the agent uses reasonable effort to provide the information, acting reasonably and consistently with any directions furnished by the principal. The principal has the burden of establishing that the agent's conduct breached the duty. However, the agent's duty does not extend to information when the agent owes a superior duty to another not to disclose it to the principal. If an agent knows or has reason to know a fact, notice of the fact is imputed to the principal for purposes of determining the principal's legal relations with third parties as stated in §§ 5.03 and 5.04, regardless of whether the agent fulfills the duty stated in this section.

An agent's duty to provide information to the principal facilitates the principal's exercise of control over the agent. Within an agency relationship, an agent assents to act subject to the principal's control. See § 1.01. A principal may exercise control by providing the agent with interim instructions directing action that the agent may properly take on the principal's behalf. See id., Comment _f(1)._ Information that the agent provides to the principal may enable the principal to reconsider a course of action that the principal has previously decided upon, leading the principal to revise or rescind prior instructions given to the agent and thereby enabling the principal to shape how the agent's actions may affect the principal's legal relations with third parties in light of developments reported by the agent.

**Illustrations:**

1\. A represents P Insurance Co. and solicits applications for insurance policies to be issued by it. A submits an application for life insurance completed by T to P Insurance Co. After submitting the application but before P Insurance Co. issues a policy to T, A learns that T's health has deteriorated substantially. As A knows, this is information that P Insurance Co. would desire to have in determining whether to issue a policy of life insurance to T. A has a duty to use reasonable effort to provide this information to P Insurance Co.

2\. Same facts as Illustration 1, except that A sends a fax containing the information about the deterioration in T's health to the underwriting department of P Insurance Co., as P Insurance Co. has directed A to do. A's fax is mistakenly destroyed upon receipt by a clerk in the department. A has complied with A's duty to use reasonable effort to provide the information to P Insurance Co.

3\. P, who owns a house, retains A as a rental agent. A learns that T, the tenant who occupies the house, has vacated it and that the water pipes in the house have frozen and then thawed, causing damage to the house's flooring. As A knows, P would desire to have this information to determine what steps to take. A has a duty to use reasonable effort to provide this information to P.

4\. P, who owns Blackacre, lists it for sale with A. T makes an offer to buy Blackacre for \$ 100,000. Before T's offer is accepted, A learns that S is willing to pay \$ 120,000 for Blackacre. As A knows, P would desire to have this information. A has a duty to use reasonable effort to provide the information to P.

A principal's receipt of information from an agent may be beneficial to the principal in many ways, for example by enabling the principal to deal on terms that will be advantageous to the principal, as in Illustration 4. Moreover, information provided to the principal by an agent may prove beneficial to third parties because it may enable the principal to take action to avoid harm that otherwise would be inflicted on third parties. Information provided by an agent may also serve to alert the principal to exercise caution in dealings with third parties.

**Illustrations:**

5\. P, who wishes to sell stock, opens an account at B Brokerage Co. All of P's dealings are handled by A, the account executive assigned to P. At A's suggestion, P opens a central asset-management account with B Brokerage Co. that features a securities-margin account and a no-load money-market mutual fund. The money-market fund enables a customer to draft checks against assets in the fund. At P's direction, A sells the stock, deposits the proceeds in the money-market fund, and reinvests most of the proceeds in low-risk debt instruments. A learns that P's spouse, S, is forging checks on P's account. A has a duty to use reasonable effort to provide this information to P. If A does not provide the information, A is subject to liability to P, as is B Brokerage Co.

6\. P, who owns Blackacre, lists it for sale with A, telling A that P would be willing to sell either for cash or on an installment basis. P agrees to pay A a commission of six percent upon execution of a contract for sale of Blackacre. T offers to buy Blackacre at a price acceptable to T and to pay the price in 10 annual installments. A learns that T has experienced financial reverses that call into question T's ability to make the installment payments. A has a duty to use reasonable effort to provide this information to P.

A principal's decisions may also be affected by information about an agent and the agent's conduct once the agent has been retained by the principal. An agent's duty to provide information extends to information about the agent when it is material to decisions the principal may wish to make. In some circumstances, the principal may determine that it is wise to make alternate arrangements or to take action to protect the principal's interests. For example, if an agent provides the principal with notice that the agent will be unable to perform as previously directed, the principal may choose another agent or take action directly. If an agent determines that an instruction received from the principal or a person designated by the principal would require unlawful action and declines to comply with the instruction, the agent should notify the principal. See § 8.09(2) on an agent's duty to comply with lawful instructions. Moreover, if an agent has acted beyond the scope of the agent's authority, the agent's duty to the principal may require the agent to inform the principal of the unauthorized action and the courses of action reasonably open to the principal.

**Illustrations:**

7\. P opens an account with B Brokerage Co. B Brokerage Co. assigns A as P's account executive. After engaging in several transactions in options on T Corp. stock, P contacts A to discuss the possibility of purchasing more options on T Corp. stock. P instructs A to buy additional options but only if "the account can afford it." Due to a computer malfunction, A overbuys P's account by \$ 100,000. A has a duty to tell P about the unauthorized transactions and to inform P of all reasonable courses of action open to P, including rejecting the unauthorized purchases.

8\. Same facts as Illustration 7, except that, after overbuying P's account, A calls P and tells P that A has overbought the account and that the price of the options has declined since A bought them. A then says: "Look, we still like T Corp. We still think T Corp. is going to do well. There are two things we can do. We can sell out of these things or you can go ahead and keep the positions and send more money in." In effect, A tells P that P may choose between selling the options at a loss or providing more cash to cover the debit in P's account in the hope that the price of the options will rise. A is subject to liability to P, as is B Brokerage Co. A breached A's duty to tell P about all courses of action open to P by omitting to tell P that P may reject A's unauthorized purchases.

9\. P opens an account with B Brokerage Co. A, the account executive assigned to P's account, encourages P to buy shares in T Corp., at the time traded over-the-counter at \$ 13. P gives A an order, without any time or price limitation, to buy 20,000 shares of T Corp. stock. Due to the persuasiveness of A and its other brokers, B Brokerage Co. accumulates a large backlog of buy orders for T Corp. stock. To handle the imbalance, B Brokerage Co. becomes a market maker in the stock, buying and selling for its own account, which will foreseeably cause a delay in filling P's order for 20,000 shares. P's order is filled one week later at a price of \$ 15 per share. A had a duty to tell P that due to the order imbalance, B Brokerage Co. had become a market maker in T Corp. stock and that the execution of P's order would be delayed. A and B Brokerage Co. are subject to liability to P for the additional cost the delayed purchase imposed on P, measured by the difference between \$ 13 per share and \$ 15 per share.

A principal may manifest a lack of interest in receiving some or all information from the agent. If so, and if the agent complies with the principal's manifested desire not to receive information, the principal bears the risk that action taken by the agent will not represent the course of conduct that the principal would have wished, had the principal received the information from the agent.

If an agent breaches the duty stated in this section, the agent is subject to liability to the principal for loss caused the principal by the agent's breach. The agent's breach may also be a basis on which to terminate the agent's authority to act on the principal's behalf. Additionally, if the agent's breach of duty breaches an express or implied term of a contract between the agent and the principal, the agent is subject to liability for breach of contract.

_c. Scope of duty._ Unless a manifestation by the principal specifies the extent of the agent's duty to provide information to the principal, the scope of the agent's duty to provide information to the principal is defined by the extent of their relationship. One element of the relationship is whether the agent is on notice that the principal would wish to have more information provided by the agent than is customary in a given type of relationship. If an agent lacks notice that a principal wishes to receive advice or information beyond the information customarily provided by an agent in a particular position, the agent is not subject to a duty to provide such advice or information.

**Illustration:**

10\. P, an importer, wishes to purchase for resale a large quantity of a farm herbicide not readily available in the area. P locates a foreign supplier for the herbicide, S, and retains A, an import broker, to assist movement of the herbicide through customs. S contacts C, a representative of the customs service, to determine the import duty. C tells S that the rate will be five percent but that the final rate can only be determined when the herbicide is examined at the time of importation. A learns of C's initial assessment but does not tell P that the final rate may be different. Importers generally know that initial duty assessments are subject to change. A, knowing that P is an importer, is unaware that P needs to be told that the initial duty assessment is subject to change. A has not breached A's duty to P.

Another relevant dimension of an agency relationship concerns the nature of the agent's discretion. Some agency relationships are confined, on the one hand, to the agent's execution of a single transaction or a series of transactions, as specifically directed by the principal. On the other hand, some are ongoing relationships in which an agent provides advice about particular transactions or may exercise discretion to commit the principal to particular transactions without the principal's specific assent to each. To the extent that the agent's relationship with the principal involves more than the execution of specific orders on terms directed by the principal, the agent's duty to furnish information will be wider in scope. However, even a relationship confined to a single transaction may impose a duty on the agent to furnish information to the principal. For example, in Illustration 9, A and B Brokerage Co. have a duty to tell P, their customer, that B Brokerage Co. has determined to make a market in T Corp. stock to resolve the imbalance in orders and that, as a consequence, execution of P's order will most likely be delayed.

An agreement that limits the scope of an agent's duties to a principal may be implicit in the relationship between an agent and a principal. For example, it may be evident that a particular type of agent may contemporaneously represent several principals with comparable interests, as would a real-estate agent who accepts listings for similar properties from more than one owner. The agent owes duties of loyalty and confidentiality to each owner, making it unreasonable for any one owner to expect that the agent will reveal confidential information provided by other owners or that the agent will forbear from showing other owners' properties to prospective buyers. In such settings, only by explicit agreement with the agent may a principal obtain the agent's exclusive commitment. See § 8.06, Comment _e,_ for further discussion.

However, unless an agent owes a superior duty to a person to keep information confidential, how the agent came to know or have reason to know the information is not relevant to the agent's duty to furnish the information to the principal. The agent's duty, that is, is operative without regard to the provenance of the information. See § 5.03, Comment _e._

A relationship between an agent and a principal also has temporal dimensions that define the scope of the agent's duty to provide information to the principal. Ordinarily, an agent does not have a duty to furnish information to the principal until an agency relationship as defined in § 1.01 has been formed. As a consequence, a prospective agent ordinarily may negotiate contract terms with a prospective principal without furnishing the prospective principal with all information possessed by the agent that may be of use to the prospective principal. Moreover, a prospective agent is not subject to duties of disclosure that stem from an agent's duties of loyalty as stated in §§ 8.02-8.05, and, in particular, is not treated as an agent dealing as an adverse party with the principal as stated in § 8.03. On the other hand, it may be open to question precisely when an agency relationship has been formed, especially when the prospective principal and agent have interactions extending over some period of time, followed by execution of a formal contract.

Moreover, if the creation of the relationship between an agent and a principal involves peculiar trust and confidence, and the prospective principal relies on the prospective agent to deal fairly with the prospective principal, the prospective agent is subject to a duty to deal fairly in arranging the terms of the agency relationship, as if the prospective agent were an agent dealing as an adverse party with a principal, with the principal's knowledge. See § 8.06. The duty to deal fairly may require a prospective agent to furnish the prospective principal with information that is not otherwise reasonably available to the prospective principal and that is material to the principal's decision whether to engage the agent.

**Illustration:**

11\. P opens an account with A for the purpose of trading in a complex and unusual type of overseas commodities option contract. A provides P with a document stating that each option contract has three components: (1) a premium for the option; (2) a commission; and (3) a foreign service fee of 20 percent of the premium. A does not tell P that the "foreign service fee" does not represent any additional expense that A must incur to execute a transaction but, instead, is an additional commission to be retained by A. Few investors at the time understand the complexities of such transactions and P could not know what expenses a broker like A would incur in executing them. A has a duty to P to inform P that the "foreign service fee" represents monies that A will retain. P is uniquely dependent on A for this information, which was material to P's decision whether engaging in such transactions could be to P's advantage.

Ordinarily, the end of an agency relationship ends a now-former agent's duty to provide information to a former principal. However, a former agent may be subject to a duty to continue to furnish material information to a now-former principal when it is foreseeable to the agent that the principal will continue to rely on the agent for information and the agent does not inform the principal that no further information will be provided. For example, if an agent arranges a transaction on behalf of a principal that is ongoing at the time their agency relationship ends, it may be foreseeable to the agent that the former principal will continue to rely on the agent to provide information relevant to the ongoing transaction. Moreover, if a person, once an agent, continues to purport to act as agent in dealings with third parties and to bind the principal, the purported agent may be subject to a duty to continue to provide information to the principal. For discussion of the extent of a lawyer's duty to convey material communications and other information to a former client, see Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 33 and Comment _h._

_d. Relationship to agent's other duties._ The duty stated in this section is closely related to other duties that an agent owes a principal. An agent's duty to furnish information to the principal facilitates the principal's exercise of control over the agent's conduct, as discussed in Comment _b._ By fulfilling the duty to furnish information, an agent enables the principal to update and sharpen instructions provided to the agent. The agent's duty stated in § 8.09(2) is then to comply with lawful instructions received from the principal. Moreover, an agent has a duty not to take action affecting the principal's legal relations except within the scope of the agent's actual authority. See § 8.09(1). If an agent acts without actual authority, or fails to take action that the principal has directed, the agent's duty to provide information to the principal will require the agent to give timely notice to the principal of the agent's unauthorized action and the responses reasonably available to the principal. See Illustrations 7 and 8.

An agent's duty to furnish information is often an important component of the agent's duty of care. For general discussion of an agent's duty of care, see § 8.08. If an agent fails to provide information to the principal that is material to decisions that the principal will make, the agent may not have acted with the diligence and care reasonably to be expected of an agent in a particular position. An agent's duty of care may require the agent to obtain information that is material to the principal's interests. If an agent's inquiry or investigation has been limited in some respect, the agent has a duty so to inform the principal. Moreover, an agent's duty of care requires that the agent use care to transmit information accurately to the principal.

**Illustrations:**

12\. P, who manufactures windows with aluminum frames, negotiates the terms of a large overseas order for custom-sized windows with B, a prospective buyer. B agrees to pay \$ 375,000 for the order. B agrees to pay by sight draft upon delivery. P determines not to accept B's order unless P obtains "export insurance" with regard to B's order, providing full payment if B defaults in payment. P retains A to arrange export insurance on its behalf. A contacts F, an export insurer. F's standard master policy provides \$ 200,000 in coverage. P completes an application to increase this limit to \$ 375,000. However, the insurance policy that F issues to P provides only F's basic coverage amount of \$ 200,000. A misinterprets documents received on P's behalf from F and erroneously tells P that F has issued a policy for the entire amount. A's misinterpretation resulted because A did not act with the competence and diligence reasonably expected of agents in A's position. Relying on A's statement, P ships the order to B. The windows are delivered to B, who defaults in payment and does not respond to P's subsequent efforts to collect the purchase price. A is subject to liability to P. A breached A's duty to use care in furnishing material information to P.

13\. P, who owns a farm, retains A, an auctioneer, to sell the farm at auction. P and A agree that A will advertise the sale and that the sale will be for "cash," meaning currency and good checks. A and P do not discuss the specifics of how proceeds will be distributed and how any checks might be handled by A. In the locale, there is no standard practice on these matters. Without informing P, A deposits the auction proceeds in an account in a bank, B. As A knows, deposits in B are not insured. B becomes insolvent before P receives payment of the auction proceeds. A is subject to liability to P. A had a duty to inform P how A proposed to handle the auction proceeds before depositing them in B.

A principal has a right to rely on advice given by an agent and on the agent's accurate transmission of material information to the principal. It is not a defense to an agent's breach of duty to transmit material information that the principal could, through investigation, have ascertained the truth independently.

When an agent has an interest in a matter that diverges from the principal's interest, the likelihood that the agent will fulfill the duty to furnish material information to the principal may be lessened if the agent believes that furnishing the information may jeopardize achieving the agent's divergent interest. This prospect connects an agent's duty to furnish information to the agent's duties of loyalty stated in §§ 8.01 to 8.05. Moreover, it is possible that the benefit that an agent anticipates receiving if a principal completes a transaction may tempt the agent not to furnish information to the principal when the agent believes that the principal might on that basis reconsider the transaction or its terms, with results unfavorable for the agent. For example, in Illustration 1, A may not provide the information concerning the deterioration in T's health to P Insurance Co. if A will receive a sales commission only when P Insurance Co. issues a policy to T. In Illustration 6, A may not provide P with information about T's financial reverses because A does not wish to jeopardize A's prospective commission. In Illustrations 7 and 8, A and B Brokerage Co. may not inform P that P may order that A's unauthorized trades be reversed because it is more beneficial to A and B Brokerage Co. that P deal with A's unauthorized trades in some other manner. This connection may explain why courts occasionally refer to the duty stated in this section as a "fiduciary" duty, although the agent's position was not adverse to that of the principal as stated in § 8.03 nor was the agent acting as competitor of the principal as stated in § 8.04. When an agent deliberately withholds material information from the principal to further the agent's own purposes, the agent's conduct is inconsistent with the agent's fiduciary duty to act loyally for the principal's benefit as stated in § 8.01.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency, and codifications._ This section is the counterpart to Restatement Second, Agency § 381, which states that "\[u\]nless otherwise agreed, an agent is subject to a duty to use reasonable efforts to give his principal information which is relevant to affairs entrusted to him and which, as the agent has notice, the principal would desire to have and which can be communicated without violating a superior duty to a third person."

Codified treatments of agency law vary in how and whether this duty is expressly articulated. Under Cal. Civ. Code § 2020 (1985 & Supp. 2005), "\[a\]n agent must use ordinary diligence to keep his principal informed of his acts in the course of the agency." Accord, Mont. Code Ann. § 28-10-302 (2005); N.D. Cent. Code § 3-02-12 (1997 & Supp. 2005); S.D. Codified Laws § 59-4-1 (2004 & Supp. 2005). In contrast, under La. Civ. Code art. 3003 (2005), "\[a\]t the request of the principal, or when the circumstances so require, the mandatary is bound to provide information and render an account of his performance of the mandate. The mandatary is bound to notify the principal, without delay, of the fulfillment of the mandate."

_b. In general._ It is the principal's burden to establish an agent's breach of the duty. See [_Olsen v. Vail Assocs. Real Estate, Inc., 935 P.2d 975, 978 (Colo.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-YSJ0-003D-90HN-00000-00&context=1530671) (burden on principal to demonstrate that "the agent was aware of the nondisclosed fact, that the nondisclosed fact is material, and that the agent breached his or her fiduciary duty"); [_Lindland v. United Bus. Invs., Inc., 693 P.2d 20 (Or.1984)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-X3H0-003F-Y29J-00000-00&context=1530671) (when principal alleges that agent breached duty to disclose material facts but no breach of duty of loyalty, principal bears burden of proving breach).

For the point made by Illustration 1, see [_United States v. Schwab, 88 F.Supp.2d 1275, 1286-1287 (D.Wyo. 2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YV5-F2W0-0038-Y244-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurance agents "had a duty to disclose material information to the insurance company that would be relevant to the insurers' decisions to issue insurance policies to life insurance policy applicants"; court denies agents' motion to strike indictment charging taking of property by fraud in violation of [_18 U.S.C. § 2314_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:8SG9-5042-D6RV-H0WH-00000-00&context=1530671), when indictment alleges agents did not inform insurer that policy applicants intended to cancel or reduce policy after its first year when insurer expected to receive premium payments beyond the first year); [_Jackson Nat'l Life Ins. Co. v. Receconi, 827 P.2d 118, 133 (N.M.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-W320-003D-D2R3-00000-00&context=1530671) (in failing to tell insurer that applicant for life insurance had been hospitalized for viral pneumonia following application but nonetheless collecting first premium for policy, agent breached duty of disclosure to insurer "to disclose any fact that might affect his principal's interests").

A contract between an agent and a principal may enlarge or reduce the agent's duty to use reasonable effort to provide information. See [_Jones v. Jackson Nat'l Life Ins. Co., 819 F.Supp. 1372, 1381 (W.D.Mich.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-THN0-001T-64KK-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _27 F.3d 566 (6th Cir.1994)_ (agent did not breach tort-law duty of disclosure to insurer although agent did not tell insurer that, after completion of application, applicant had been scheduled to receive an endoscopy of the esophagus; agent's agreement with insurer provided that agent had a duty "'not to make delivery of a policy on behalf of the Company at any time after the Agent has knowledge, directly or indirectly, that the health of the proposed insured covered by such policy has materially changed since the time the application for such policy was made,'" which did not require disclosure of "any information that \[agent\] knew or should have known signalled a change in an application answer that might bear on \[insurer's\] assessment of insurability").

Illustration 3 is based on [_Muller v. Wise, 295 A.2d 361 (N.J.Super.App.Div.1972)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRR-2H20-003C-N32J-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 4 is based on the observation in Restatement Second, Agency § 381, Comment _a,_ that "an agent who is appointed to sell property at a fixed price to a particular person may learn that another person is willing to pay a higher price. Unless he has reason to believe that his employer desires to sell at a fixed price to the particular person, or unless he is to receive as commission all above a fixed price, it is his duty to inform the principal of the facts, if this can be done without violating a confidence." For support for a broad formulation of an agent's duty to provide information about market appreciation, see [_Iriart v. Johnson, 411 P.2d 226, 227-228 (N.M.1965)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S3J-WDP0-003D-D01G-00000-00&context=1530671) (broker failed to inform principal of facts within his knowledge concerning future values subject to liability; broker breached duty to communicate to his principal "all facts within his knowledge … which might affect his principal's rights and interest or influence his action relative to the disposition of the property"). But see [_Musselman v. Southwinds Realty, Inc., 704 P.2d 814, 818 (Ariz.App.1985)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-Y6N0-003F-T3JS-00000-00&context=1530671) (burden on principals to establish that agent breached duty; jury found for agent although trial court admitted "all the evidence concerning the appreciation in land values in northwest Tucson … the information which the \[principals\] contend the \[agent\] should have related to them.").

For an example of information that, if provided to the principal by the agent, would have enabled the principal to take measures that would have prevented injury to a third party, see [_Sulik v. Central Valley Farms, Inc., 521 P.2d 144 (Idaho 1974)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-23K0-003D-34X8-00000-00&context=1530671) (electrician injured by falling from defective power-line pole; employee of power company, who was tenant in nearby structure, warned of defective condition of pole by crew that made temporary repairs but power company did not replace pole). See also [_In re Allegheny Int'l, Inc., 954 F.2d 167, 179-180 (3d Cir.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-67T0-008H-V4CW-00000-00&context=1530671) (employee of corporation who knew of serious violations of sound accounting practices had duty to bring them to attention of corporation's management, especially if employee knew problems were likely to continue; failure to make disclosure constitutes breach of fiduciary duty).

Illustration 5 is based on [_Lichtenstein v. Kidder, Peabody & Co., 840 F.Supp. 374 (W.D.Pa.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-9F60-001T-613T-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 6 is based on [_Miller v. Berkoski, 297 N.W.2d 334, 338 (Iowa 1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2X70-003G-5294-00000-00&context=1530671) (principal would have wanted to know that purchaser would not provide a written property statement, was "'thin' on liquidity" and had only \$ 2500 to pay down on \$ 210,000 purchase price; agent additionally had duty to seller to tell seller that agent was lending purchaser remainder of down payment). See also _Hardy v. Davis, 164 A.2d 281 (Md.1960)_ (broker's relationship with vendor terminated when sale contract signed; broker had no duty thereafter to disclose to vendor loans made to purchaser; broker had duty to disclose loans to purchaser, or agreements to lend to purchaser, made before sale contract signed). An agent's duty of disclosure extends to information about the principal's inability to finance or otherwise complete a contemplated transaction. See [_Hurney v. Locke, 308 N.W.2d 764 (S.D.1981)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S71-3820-003G-D3BB-00000-00&context=1530671) (reversing grant of summary judgment for broker and agent when agent knew that vendor who listed residence for sale with broker would be ineligible for specific form of financing vendor believed would be requisite to vendor's ability to purchase another residence).

On an agent's awareness that another party's financial circumstances have deteriorated, Restatement Second, Agency § 381, Comment _a,_ observes, "a person whose authority is only to collect a debt and not to bring action upon it may obtain information that in order to collect the debt it is necessary to take immediate legal steps, as by attachment. In this situation, the agent's duty is to communicate the information in order that the principal may choose what he will do." In some jurisdictions, an agent who represents a purchaser has a duty to disclose the purchaser's financial difficulties to the vendor. See [_Lombardo v. Albu, 14 P.3d 288, 290 (Ariz.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:426R-G330-0039-405X-00000-00&context=1530671) (vendor lost home to trustee's sale after purchaser defaulted and vendor was unable to continue making loan payments; both purchaser and agent had duty to disclose to vendor that purchaser might be unable to perform contract when regulation applicable to agent required disclosure of "'any information which the licensee possesses which materially and adversely affects the consideration to be paid by any party to the transaction, including … \[a\]ny information that the buyer or lessee is, or may be, unable to perform due to insolvency or otherwise'").

Information about an agent that the agent may have a duty to provide to the principal may include the fact that the agent has breached duties owed to the principal. On an agent's duty to make such disclosure when agent and principal engage in negotiations to modify terms of a prior contract between them, see [_Hadden v. Consolidated Edison Co., 382 N.E.2d 1136, 1139 (N.Y.1978)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-B150-003C-F340-00000-00&context=1530671) (court holds that corporation may rescind severance agreement negotiated with vice president because vice president defrauded corporation by falsely stating that he had not taken bribes; dictum that a "failure to disclose material facts" would also entitle corporation to rescind). See also Principles of Corporate Governance: Analysis and Recommendations § 5.03, Comment _d_ ("there may be unusual circumstances in which directors or senior executives will be obligated to disclose facts concerning their personal circumstances that would clearly be of material importance to the corporation (for example, knowledge of a terminal illness when an executive seeks a fixed benefit to survivors in the event of death)."). On an agent's duty to make such disclosure outside the context of contractual renegotiations, see Item Software (UK) Ltd. v. Fassihi, 2004 WL 2077421 P 41 (Cal.App.2004) (fiduciary's fundamental duty to act in good faith required corporation's director of sales and marketing to disclose actions previously taken to divert corporation's business to his own business and to sabotage corporation's business).

Illustration 8 is based on [_Merrill Lynch, Pierce, Fenner & Smith, Inc. v. Cheng, 901 F.2d 1124 (D.C.Cir. 1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-5NX0-003B-517P-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Conway v. Icahn & Co., 16 F.3d 504 (2d Cir.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-8J70-003B-P0G7-00000-00&context=1530671) (broker had duty to notify customer of margin call; broker lacked authority to meet margin call in customer's non-discretionary account by selling off securities in account without customer's consent, and lack of notice left customer unable to determine whether to meet margin call by providing other assets or even to designate securities to be sold from account); [_Ward v. Atlantic Sec. Bank, 777 So. 2d 1144, 1146 (Fla.Dist.App.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:42C6-W2Y0-0039-4393-00000-00&context=1530671) (bank that managed mutual fund did not execute customer's order to sell shares in fund, then took "proactive step" of contacting customer to persuade customer not to sell, while concealing negative information about fund in order to keep it afloat). See also [_Fesseha v. TD Waterhouse Investor Servs., Inc., 305 A.D.2d 268 (N.Y.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:48MX-WGS0-0039-41NV-00000-00&context=1530671) (provision in margin account agreement gave broker the right to liquidate customer's position; court declines to imply requirement of notice and opportunity to cure when agreement is silent on questions and is not ambiguous).

Illustration 9 is based on [_Magnum Corp. v. Lehman Bros. Kuhn Loeb, Inc., 794 F.2d 198 (5th Cir.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-3FR0-0039-P4NB-00000-00&context=1530671) (brokerage firm liable to customer for difference in price between date order placed and price when order executed).

_c. Scope of duty._ Illustration 10 is based on [_F.W. Myers & Co. v. Hunter Farms, 319 N.W.2d 186 (Iowa 1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2SX0-003G-51NR-00000-00&context=1530671).

For the distinction between relationships in which an agent's duty is limited to executing specific transactions and relationships that create ongoing duties, see, e.g., in the context of securities brokers, de [_Kwiatkowski v. Bear, Stearns & Co., 306 F.3d 1293 (2d Cir.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:46T4-TV30-0038-X4KM-00000-00&context=1530671) (brokerage firm did not owe ongoing duty to provide advice and risk warnings to customer with nondiscretionary account, despite size of account and magnitude of trading risks undertaken by customer in foreign-currency futures); [_Securities Settlement Corp. v. Jachera, 772 F.Supp. 770, 775-776 (S.D.N.Y.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-BH30-001T-73YH-00000-00&context=1530671) (broker's duty limited to completion of transaction and does not encompass a warranty that customer owns securities found in customer's account; broker not required to make "extraordinary efforts" to discover genealogy of stock in customer's account); [_Robinson v. Merrill Lynch, Pierce, Fenner & Smith, Inc., 337 F.Supp. 107, 112 (N.D.Ala.1971)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-PWG0-003B-326P-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _453 F.2d 417 (5th Cir.1972)_ (even when principal-agent relationship is prolonged because principal's order cannot be executed for some time, "the broker has no duty to relay news of political, economic, weather or price changes to his customer, absent an express contract to furnish such information"; any investment advice provided by broker was incidental to brokerage services and did not make broker an investment advisor); _Walston & Co. v. Miller, 410 P.2d 658, 661 (Ariz.1966)_ (agency relationship between broker and customer "normally terminates with the execution of the order because the broker's duties, unlike those of an investment advisor or those of a manager of a discretionary account are only to fulfill the mechanical, ministerial requirements of the purchase or sale of the security or future contracts on the market"; by providing customer with credit through a margin account, broker does not extend duration of agency relationship); in the context of insurance brokerage, American Envtl., Inc. v. 3-[_J Co., 583 N.E.2d 649, 655 (Ill.App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-3TX0-003D-H2XC-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurance broker's engagement is to accomplish single objective, and same broker and customer may have discrete series of agency relationships; factual question when agency relationship began anew for particular project).

On the duties of travel agents to provide information to travelers, see [_Rookard v. Mexicoach, 680 F.2d 1257, 1261-1263 (9th Cir.1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-2HN0-003B-G14J-00000-00&context=1530671) (travel agent who is not "a mere ticket agent" has duty to customer to "warn of those dangers of which he is aware, or should be aware in the exercise of due care"; scope of duty of disclosure is limited to what is reasonable in given circumstances); [_Sachs v. TWA Getaway Vacations, Inc., 125 F.Supp.2d 1368, 1374 (S.D.Fla. 2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:422M-3VM0-0038-Y0VY-00000-00&context=1530671) (travel agent has duty to disclose "reasonably obtainable information"; no duty to warn customer of conditions that are "obvious and readily observable by a traveler," including risk of descending steps of motorcoach); [_Stafford v. Intrav, Inc., 841 F.Supp. 284, 288 (E.D.Mo.1993)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-XX60-001T-64XM-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _16 F.3d 1228 (8th Cir.1994)_ (travel agent who arranges traveler's plans and thus acts as more than ticket agent "has a duty to disclose reasonably obtainable information that is material to the object of the agency"; agent did not breach duty by failing to tell passenger on cruise ship of obvious danger of falling presented by gangplank); [_United Airlines, Inc. v. Lerner, 410 N.E.2d 225, 228 (Ill. App.1980)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S11-W910-003C-B4J5-00000-00&context=1530671) (travel agent's duty to disclose material information does not require agent to be "prescient," as opposed to loyal; agent did not have duty to tell client of "possible hindrances to his vacation" that were uncertain).

On reasonable expectations of owners who list property for sale with agents who represent other owners as well, see Kelly v. Cooper, \[1993\] A.C. 205, 214-215 (P.C.) (agent not under duty to disclose to one owner material information that is confidential to another owner; when principal has notice that nature of agent's business involves acting for numerous principals, "the terms to be implied into such agency contract must differ from those to be implied when an agent is not carrying on such general agency business," and agency contract between plaintiff and defendants "cannot have included either (a) a term requiring the defendants to disclose … confidential information \[concerning another owner\] to the plaintiff or (b) a term precluding the defendants from acting from rival vendors or (c) a term precluding the defendants from seeking to earn commission on the sale of the property of a rival vendor.").

On agreements between principal and agent, see [_Jackson Nat'l Life Ins. Co. v. Gofen & Glossberg, Inc., 882 F.Supp. 713 (N.D.Ill.1995)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-W260-001T-50TN-00000-00&context=1530671) (agreement between agent and principal specified circumstances in which agent had duty to furnish information to principal; agent did not guarantee to pass along information about reorganization of corporation whose stock was held in principal's investment portfolio); [_Louisiana Hand & Upper Extremity Inst., Inc. v. City of Shreveport, 781 So. 2d 695, 699 (La. App.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:42G1-FXB0-0039-415G-00000-00&context=1530671) (agreement between purchaser and buyer's agent provided that purchaser had burden of confirming that his intended use of building conformed with zoning requirements); [_Holzman v. Fiola Blum, Inc., 726 A.2d 818, 831 n.4 (Md.App.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3W6C-V5S0-0039-42MB-00000-00&context=1530671) (broker not subject to duty to remind principal of terms of agreement regarding commissions; court notes that it assumes broker considered "the impact \[its\] suit \[against principal\] might have on its reputation as an advocate for its customers" when agreement entitled broker to entire commission on first sales contract, which principal cancelled to enter into second contract with another buyer that provided for split commission).

For the point that the provenance of an agent's knowledge is irrelevant to the duty to inform the principal, see [_Aronoff v. Lenkin Co., 618 A.2d 669, 687 (D.C.App.1992)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRT-8VC0-003G-11NK-00000-00&context=1530671) (settlement agent had duty to inform vendors that title was not merchantable when agent knew or should have known that vendors did not possess this information; settlement agent not free to withhold information learned as purchaser's title insurer, which it knew to be vital to completing transaction).

An agent's duty to inform the principal does not extend to information beyond the scope of the account that the agent manages for the principal. See [_Anton v. Merrill Lynch, 36 S.W.3d 251 (Tex.App.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:423S-15M0-0039-435C-00000-00&context=1530671) (broker who managed discretionary brokerage account did not have duty to tell client that late spouse's change in designated beneficiary for retirement account was adverse to client when retirement account was not account managed for client).

Illustration 11 is based on [_Martin v. Heinold Commodities, Inc., 643 N.E.2d 734 (Ill.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-1S30-003D-H2NT-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_United States v. Szur, 289 F.3d 200, 212 (2d Cir.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45R8-1DR0-0038-X1MS-00000-00&context=1530671) (broker's duty to use reasonable efforts to give client information relevant to affairs entrusted to broker encompassed information that all sales of particular stock carried 45 or 55% commissions, which "plainly had no relationship to the commissions that would be charged in a competitive market … even in the absence of any general fiduciary duty resulting from discretionary authority, we hold that \[broker\] was under a duty to disclose these exorbitant commissions because the information would have been relevant to a customer's decision to purchase the stock"); [_Press v. Chemical Inv. Servs. Corp., 166 F.3d 529 (2d Cir.1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3VR6-FFY0-0038-X4DS-00000-00&context=1530671) (no per se disclosure rule concerning disclosure of mark-ups; broker did not breach fiduciary duty to client by failing to disclose markup that was not excessive).

It may, of course, be open to question when an agency relationship begins for purposes of the applicability of this duty, among others. See [_Maksym v. Loesch, 937 F.2d 1237, 1242 (7th Cir.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-B2K0-008H-V17R-00000-00&context=1530671) (noting that creation of lawyer-client relationship and of expectations it engenders "is not instantaneous"; relationship may be formed well prior to execution of retainer agreement, at which "point the lawyer or other fiduciary has an ascendancy over the client, a position of trust, that may enable him to drive too hard a bargain"); [_Kearney v. J.P. King Auction Co., 265 F.3d 27, 39 (1st Cir.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4401-R1M0-0038-X1R6-00000-00&context=1530671) (statements made prior to commencement of agency relationship were not relevant to any breach of duty to principal that agent owed as a fiduciary).

If the parties to an agreement have a fiduciary relationship, the relationship may impose a duty on one party to explain terms of the agreement to the other. See [_Kloss v. Edward D. Jones & Co., 54 P.3d 1, 9 (Mont.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4637-V3J0-0039-4538-00000-00&context=1530671) (broker with discretion to buy and sell securities in customer's account had duty to explain consequences of arbitration clause in new agreement to customer, a 95-year-old widow "with no bargaining power and a relative lack of sophistication in such matters").

On post-termination duties to provide information, see _In re Cooper Mfg. Corp., 131 F.Supp.2d 1230, 1237 (N.D.Okla.2001)_ (insurance broker may owe duty to furnish information to insurer after termination of formal agency relationship; relevant that "transaction about which \[insurer\] wishes to impose a duty to inform was ongoing both before and after the termination of the parties' formal agency relationship").

On the consequences of purporting to act as an agent following termination, see Letsos v. Century 21-[_New West Realty, 675 N.E.2d 217 (Ill.App. 1996)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-23D0-003D-H485-00000-00&context=1530671) (following termination of listing agreement, broker continued to attempt to find buyer for property, as owner expected, and broker received commission when he purchased property himself; broker breached fiduciary duty to owner by failing to disclose that broker had located purchaser willing to pay price for property in excess of price broker agreed to pay, although listing agreement had expired at time broker located purchaser, to whom broker eventually sold property).

On the translation of agency-based duties of disclosure within a complex corporate context, see Donald C. Langevoort, Agency Law Inside the Corporation: Problems of Candor and Knowledge, [_71 U. Cin. L. Rev. 1187 (2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4B36-HHB0-00CW-50KC-00000-00&context=1530671).

_d. Relationship to agent's other duties._ For the point that an agent has a duty to inform the principal that the agent has failed to accomplish what the principal directed, see [_Baseball Office of the Comm'r v. Marsh & McLennan, Inc., 742 N.Y.S.2d 40, 46 (App.Div.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45V6-T340-0039-400H-00000-00&context=1530671) (insurance broker neglected to tell principal that insurer deleted its personal-injury coverage; if insurance broker retained to place coverage for principal, broker must exercise reasonable diligence to do so and if unable to make placement "must timely notify customer to afford it the opportunity to procure the insurance elsewhere"). An insured also has the right to rely on its broker's expertise on insurance questions and is not obliged to read insurance policies; "it is precisely to perform this service as well as others that the insured pays a commission to the broker." [_Id. at 48_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45V6-T340-0039-400H-00000-00&context=1530671). See also [_Investitions-Und Handels-Bank A.G. v. United Cal. Bank Int'l, 277 F.Supp. 1005, 1007-1008 (S.D.N.Y.1968)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4V-PNM0-0054-84YB-00000-00&context=1530671) (defendant-bank with which plaintiff-bank opened line of credit did not follow plaintiffs instructions to notify customer that credit had been opened only through a particular third bank; material issue of fact whether failure to follow instructions was proximate cause of loss to plaintiff when customer and export service proved to be financially irresponsible; defendant-bank may have breached duty to inform plaintiff of manner in which it effected notification).

On the circumstances under which an agent has a duty of investigation, beyond the duty to provide facts that an agent knows or has reason to know, see [_Fling v. Hollywood Travel & Tours, 765 F.Supp. 1302, 1305 (N.D.Ohio 1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-7HF0-0054-42DC-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd, _933 F.2d 1008 (6th Cir.1991)_ (travel agent's duty does not extend to investigation "unless the travel agent knew or should have known of the potential harm that occurred").

For the point that an agent has a duty to disclose limitations on any inquiry made by the agent, see [_Salahutdin v. Valley of California, Inc., 29 Cal.Rptr.2d 463, 467 (Cal.App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-G4X0-003D-J13F-00000-00&context=1530671) (broker misrepresented lot size to purchasers, his principals; although broker "was not required to investigate the sellers' representations or the truth of the description contained in the \[MLS\] sheet before showing the property to \[principals\], \[Broker\] was at least required to tell \[principals\] that he had not verified the information he was passing on to them").

On the relationship between an agent's duty of care and the agent's duty to furnish information to the principal, see, e.g., [_Martha Graham School & Dance Found., Inc. v. Martha Graham Ctr. of Contemporary Dance, Inc., 224 F.Supp.2d 567, 609 (S.D.N.Y.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:46M5-8FT0-0038-Y0D8-00000-00&context=1530671), aff'd in part and vacated in part on other grounds, [_380 F.3d 624 (2d Cir.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4D4C-HKG0-0038-X4C5-00000-00&context=1530671) (executive director of dance center concealed from center's directors that his attorneys had advised him to investigate copyrights owned by founder's estate, instead representing to directors that he owned everything; executive director's failure to undertake investigation into copyrights and his concealment of uncertainty concerning copyright ownership breached duties of care, skill, and diligence owed to center); [_Saiz v. Horn, 668 N.W.2d 332, 336 (S.D.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:497Y-P780-0039-41KX-00000-00&context=1530671) (buyer's broker in residential-property transaction has duty to inform buyer that seller has legal obligation under S.D. Codified L. § 43-4-38 to furnish buyer with a disclosure statement that may reveal matters material to buyer's decision to purchase property because "real estate agents are expected to advise their principals on the rules and procedures involved in a real estate transaction. Why have realty agents for buyers if agents, supposedly knowledgeable in real estate transactions, have no obligation to tell clients that sellers are required by law to give a disclosure statement?"); [_Salt Lake County v. Western Dairymen Co-op., Inc., 48 P.3d 910, 916 (Utah 2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45N3-G3F0-0039-4486-00000-00&context=1530671) (real-estate agent's duty of care requires agent to disclose latent defects of which it is aware to principal).

Illustration 12 is based on a somewhat simplified version of [_Nu-Air Mfg. Co. v. Frank B. Hall & Co., 822 F.2d 987 (11th Cir.1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-8P50-001B-K142-00000-00&context=1530671). On an agent's duty to relay information accurately, see [_Whipple v. Haberle, 36 Cal.Rptr. 9, 15 (Cal.App.1963)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRS-B6Y0-003C-J2DR-00000-00&context=1530671) (loan broker falsely informed purchasers that their FHA loan had been approved; although broker acted without fraudulent intent, having assumed that loan would be approved, "the duty of an agent in the circumstances is broader and more positive than merely to refrain from actual fraud; she owed an obligation not to be negligent and a particular duty not to give false information.").

Illustration 13 is based on [_Cruikshank v. Horn, 386 N.W.2d 134 (Iowa App.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-4N30-003G-548J-00000-00&context=1530671).
