[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 8.14_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-4016-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 8- Duties of Agent and Principal to Each Other_ > _Topic 2- Principal's Duties to Agent_**

**§ 8.14 Duty to Indemnify**

**A principal has a duty to indemnify an agent**

**(1) in accordance with the terms of any contract between them; and**

**(2) unless otherwise agreed,**

**(a) when the agent makes a payment**

**(i) within the scope of the agent's actual authority, or**

**(ii) that is beneficial to the principal, unless the agent acts officiously in making the payment; or**

**(b) when the agent suffers a loss that fairly should be borne by the principal in light of their relationship.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section states the circumstances under which a principal has a duty to hold an agent harmless against pecuniary loss suffered by the agent in connection with an agency relationship. Comment _b_ discusses general principles applicable to indemnification rights of agents against principals. Comment _c_ contrasts these principles with agents' rights to indemnification created by or pursuant to statutes, including corporation and other organizational statutes. Comment _d_ discusses agents' rights to indemnification in connection with litigation.

An agent's right to be indemnified by a principal is distinct from any right of the agent to receive compensation for the agent's services. On compensation, see § 8.13.

Section 2.01 defines "actual authority." Section 2.02 states when an agent acts within the scope of actual authority.

For the bases from which rights to indemnity may stem, see Restatement Third, Restitution and Unjust Enrichment § 25, Comment _a_ (Tentative Draft No. 2, 2002). For a definition of "officious" action, see Restatement Third, Restitution and Unjust Enrichment § 2(4) (Discussion Draft, 2000).

On a client's duty to indemnify a lawyer, see Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 17(2).

_b. Agent's right to indemnification--in general._ In general, a principal's obligation to indemnify an agent arises when the agent makes a payment or incurs an expense or other loss while acting on behalf of the principal. An agent's actions on behalf of a principal may result in pecuniary loss for the agent. For example, an agent may be required to make payments to third parties to carry out the agent's work for the principal. Actions taken by an agent may also result in litigation against the agent brought by third parties with whom the agent has interacted on the principal's behalf. A contract between a principal and an agent may anticipate the possibility that the agent will incur pecuniary losses, specify when and to what extent the principal has a duty to indemnify the agent, and prescribe procedures to be followed by the agent in claiming rights to indemnity under the contract.

In the absence of such a contract, a principal has duties to indemnify the agent as stated in subsection (2). If an agent acts with actual authority in making a payment to a third party, the principal has a duty to indemnify the agent unless otherwise agreed.

**Illustration:**

1\. P retains A, an import broker, to handle importation of a large quantity of herbicide. A learns that the amount of duty payable on the herbicide will exceed a prior estimate given by the customs service because the herbicide contains various chemicals not listed on its label. Fearing forfeiture of the security bond A has posted for the duty, A pays the additional amount under protest and seeks indemnity from P. P has a duty to indemnify A. A acted with actual authority in making the payment.

An agent and a principal may agree that expenses incurred by the agent will be borne by the agent as an expense of the agent's business. Such a contract may be based on practices that are well-established and well-known in particular industries or professions. For example, a real-estate agent customarily bears the expense of advertising property listed for sale with the agent. In contrast, the generally prevailing practice in relationships between lawyers and clients is that disbursements a lawyer makes on the client's behalf to persons outside the lawyer's office, such as consultants and printers, are charged to the client in addition to the lawyer's fee. See Restatement Third, The Law Governing Lawyers § 38, Comment _e._

An agent's right to be indemnified against payments the agent has made to third parties does not encompass compensation the agent pays to its employees and other subagents, unless the principal has agreed otherwise. On the principal's duty to pay compensation and subagents, see § 8.13, Comment _d._

An agent's right to be indemnified by the principal for payments made to third parties is not unlimited. If the agent acted without actual authority in making the payment, the principal has a duty to indemnify the agent only if the principal benefited from the payment and the agent did not act officiously in making it. An agent acts officiously in making a payment without actual authority when, under the circumstances, the agent's action was not excusable. See Restatement Third, Restitution and Unjust Enrichment § 2(4) (Discussion Draft, 2000).

Additionally, in appropriate circumstances, a principal has a duty to indemnify an agent against pecuniary losses incurred by the agent in connection with the agency relationship. Such losses include but are not limited to litigation-related expenses. A principal's duty to indemnify does not extend to losses that result from the agent's own negligence, illegal acts, or other wrongful conduct. For discussion of this basic point in connection with litigation against an agent, see Comment _d._ Additionally, a principal may have a duty to indemnify an agent against a loss when the loss foreseeably resulted from conduct of the principal and the agent was without fault. See § 8.15, Comment _b._

A principal's duty to indemnify extends to subagents, although the principal is not subject to liability on an appointing agent's promise to pay compensation to a subagent. See § 8.13, Comment _d._ Although a subagent has fiduciary duties to both the appointing agent and the principal, the interests of the principal are paramount. See § 3.15, Comments _b_ and _d._ Unless a subagent agrees otherwise, a subagent has claims to indemnity against both the appointing agent and the principal.

**Illustration:**

2\. P, owner of the ship "Elkton," engages A as its agent under an operating contract. A engages S to fit out the Elkton in the Philippines. The Elkton and all hands aboard are lost in a typhoon, as is cargo on board owned by T. T sues S for its loss. S defends successfully against T's suit. P has a duty to indemnify S against costs incurred by S in its defense of T's suit. A also has a duty to indemnify S. If A reimburses S for the costs of S's defense against T's suit, P has a duty to indemnify A.

A principal's duty to indemnify an agent may arise after the agency relationship is terminated, whether or not the duty is based on a contract between principal and agent.

_c. Rights to indemnification created by or pursuant to statute._ Organizational and other statutes may contain provisions that mandate indemnification or permit the creation of rights to indemnification. Within a partnership, unless the partnership agreement provides otherwise, a general partner has a right to be indemnified by the partnership for payments made by the partner and liabilities incurred in the ordinary course of partnership business or for the preservation of the partnership's business or property. See Unif. Partnership Act § 18(b); Rev. Unif. Partnership Act § 401(c). Some limited-liability company (LLC) statutes provide a comparable right of indemnity to a member or manager of an LLC. See Unif. Limited Liability Co. Act § 403(a).

In contrast, indemnification provisions in corporation statutes are limited to litigation-related expenses, including attorney's fees, and judgments, fines, and amounts paid in settlement. Some statutory provisions are applicable only to a corporation's officers or directors; others cover a corporation's officers, directors, employees, and other agents; in others, some components apply only to officers and directors while other components apply to officers, directors, employees, and other agents. All, however, cover only losses sustained as a consequence of actions taken in the would-be indemnitee's capacity as a director or agent of the corporation or of another corporation in which the person served in a fiduciary capacity at the request of the indemnifying corporation.

All statutory provisions mandate that a corporation indemnify when the would-be indemnitee has been successful in connection with a legal proceeding. Statutes differ on whether a corporation's duty to indemnify is triggered only by complete success and only by success achieved on the merits. All statutory provisions also empower a corporation, acting through a mechanism authorized by the statute, to indemnify in a particular case when indemnification is not mandatory. Most statutory provisions also permit a corporation to create additional rights to indemnity, whether on behalf of specific individuals or a class of individuals, thereby obligating itself to indemnify when the statute's requirements for mandatory indemnification are not present.

If a corporation refuses to indemnify when it has a duty to do so and a court orders that payment be made, the plaintiff may also seek recovery of litigation expenses incurred to recover the indemnification amount, colloquially known as "fees on fees." Some statutes explicitly require that the court award "fees on fees" when the corporation has a duty to indemnify, whether created directly by the statute or by the corporation itself. See Model Bus. Corp. Act § 8.54(b). Other statutes limit "fees on fees" to awards of indemnification mandated directly by the statute itself or by the court. See Cal. Corp. Code § 317(a). Cases are split on whether, in the absence of such statutory direction, an award of "fees on fees" should be made to a plaintiff who succeeds in enforcing a right to be indemnified. The better view is that, absent an explicit statutory directive to the contrary, awarding "fees on fees" is consistent with the statutory policy reflected in indemnification statutes of securing the service of personnel whose loyal focus on serving the corporation's interests will not be clouded by the fear of litigation expenses. Awarding "fees on fees" also reflects the contemporary reality that a corporation may seek strategic advantage in requiring an agent to vault the expensive rigors of litigation to recover what is due the agent. See also Principles of Corporate Governance: Analysis and Recommendations § 7.20(c)(1) (in ordering payment of mandatory indemnification, court should also order corporation to pay the reasonable expenses incurred by an officer or a director in obtaining court-ordered indemnification).

Some issues raised by indemnification provisions in organizational statutes can usefully be examined against the backdrop of common-law agency. For example, some statutes require indemnification to the extent that a present or former "director, officer, employee, or other agent" has been successful in the defense of a legal proceeding in which the person was involved _"as a consequence_ of the fact that" the person "is or was a director, officer, employee, or other agent." See Del. Code Ann. tit. 8, § 145(a) (emphasis added). The "as a consequence" requirement excludes legal proceedings unrelated to actions taken by a person in the person's corporate capacity, just as a principal has no duty to indemnify an agent against pecuniary losses unrelated to the agency relationship. Moreover, common-law agency principles suggest that statutory indemnification provisions should not be interpreted to create a duty to indemnify a nonemployee agent of the corporation when the legal proceeding does not concern actions taken as an agent in the corporation's interactions with third parties.

_d. Rights to indemnification in connection with litigation._ In the absence of an express contractual provision that requires the principal to indemnify an agent in connection with litigation against the agent, a principal has a duty to indemnify the agent against expenses and other losses incurred by the agent in defending against actions brought by third parties if the agent acted with actual authority in taking the action challenged by the third party's suit. The agent's right is limited to recovery of expenses and losses in amounts that are reasonable. A principal does not have a duty to indemnify an agent against losses caused by unauthorized action taken by the agent that did not benefit the principal or losses caused solely by wrongful acts committed by the agent. Unproven allegations in the third party's complaint do not determine whether the agent is entitled to indemnification from the principal.

An agent seeking indemnification has a prior duty to give timely notice to the principal of the third party's suit so that the principal may provide the agent with a defense. Because the principal will be subject to liability for any payment due the third party, the principal has the primary interest in defending against the third party's claim. Providing the agent with a defense permits the principal to assume control of the defense of the litigation, which may be advantageous to the principal. An agent's failure to give notice to the principal should not automatically bar the agent's right to indemnification, especially if the agent's defense was reasonable and the principal did not suffer a loss as a consequence of losing its opportunity to control the defense of the suit. Moreover, the principal may have notice of the suit from sources other than the agent.

If an agent gives timely notice of a third party's suit to the principal and the principal declines to provide the agent with a defense, the agent's right to indemnification should not thereafter turn on whether the agent litigated the suit to the bitter end, as opposed to settling it, so long as the agent acted reasonably and in good faith. A few cases instead hold that, by settling, an agent cuts off the right to receive indemnity from the principal unless the agent's indemnity right is protected by an express contract, reasoning that by only through a final adjudication on the merits can it be determined whether the agent was compelled to make payment to the third party or did so voluntarily. This view ignores the fact that the principal, having received timely notification of the suit and having rejected the opportunity to control its defense, has placed the agent in a position in which it is foreseeable that the agent, acting reasonably to protect its own interests as well as those of the principal, may find it advantageous to settle. A principal's decision to remain uninvolved in defending the suit should not deprive the agent of indemnity rights if the agent reasonably exercises its discretion in good faith.

General indemnity law does not recognize an extracontractual right to recover the expenses requisite to establishing an underlying right to indemnity, as opposed to legal fees expended in defending against a suit. When an agent seeks indemnity from a principal, it is open to question whether a court should consider departing from the general rule in appropriate circumstances. Agents' indemnity claims involve a wider range of payments and losses than the litigation-related expenses covered by the indemnification provisions in corporation statutes discussed in Comment _c._ In that context, as discussed, some courts make awards of "fees on fees" and, in so doing, mitigate the risk that a corporate principal will make strategic use of its resources against an agent who has a valid claim to receive indemnity. In the broader context of agents' common-law claims for indemnity, a court should, at a minimum, consider whether a "fees on fees" award, unless clearly contrary to a statute, is warranted by the principal's behavior.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency, and codifications._ This section is the counterpart to Restatement Second, Agency §§ 438, 439, and 440.

Ga. Code § 10-6-31 (2000 & Supp. 2005) provides that "\[a\]n agent who shall have discharged his duty shall be entitled to his commission and all necessary expenses incurred about the business of his principal. If he has violated his engagement, he shall be entitled to no commission."

Under La. Civ. Code art. 3012 (2005), "\[t\]he principal is bound to reimburse the mandatary for the expenses and charges he has incurred and to pay him the remuneration to which he is entitled." Art. 3013 requires the principal to "compensate the mandatary for loss the mandatary sustains as a result of the mandate, but not for loss caused by the fault of the mandatary."

_b. Agent's right to indemnification--in general._ Illustration 1 is based on [_F.W. Myers & Co. v. Hunter Farms, 319 N.W.2d 186 (Iowa 1982)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-2SX0-003G-51NR-00000-00&context=1530671) (customs broker did not breach duty of care to principal by failing to tell principal that initial estimate of duty by customs service was only tentative; standard of care for import brokers did not include providing such advice to customers unless specifically requested to do so).

For the basic point that a principal has a duty to indemnify an agent when the agent's liability arose solely from actions taken with actual authority, see [_Marie Deonier & Assocs. v. Paul Revere Life Ins. Co., 9 P.3d 622, 634 (Mont.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:413M-RF00-0039-44MB-00000-00&context=1530671). A principal's duty to indemnify does not encompass losses that result from the agent's own fault. See [_Society of Roman Catholic Church v. Interstate Fire & Cas. Co., 126 F.3d 727, 740 (5th Cir.1997)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RR4-V840-00B1-D4BF-00000-00&context=1530671); [_GAB Bus. Servs., Inc. v. Syndicate 627, 809 F.2d 755, 758 (11th Cir.1987)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-CJ80-001B-K0SX-00000-00&context=1530671); [_Allen v. Great Am. Reserve Ins. Co., 766 N.E.2d 1157, 1169-1170 (Ind.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45H8-98Y0-0039-454G-00000-00&context=1530671).

Illustration 2 is based on [_Admiral Oriental Line v. United States, 86 F.2d 201 (2d Cir.1936)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-TNC0-003B-K4DY-00000-00&context=1530671). On the underlying rationale for a principal's duty to indemnify an agent, the court observes, "\[t\]he doctrine stands upon the fact that the venture is the principal's, and that, as the profits will be his, so should be the expenses. Since by hypothesis the agent's outlay is not due to his mismanagement, it should be regarded only as a loss, unexpected it is true, but inextricably interwoven with the enterprise." [_Id. at 202_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-TNC0-003B-K4DY-00000-00&context=1530671). The duty reaches a subagent's losses: "\[h\]ad the \[agent\] paid the \[subagent\], it would therefore have been in a position to recover of the \[principal\], for there is no difference between payment by an agent of his sub-agent's recover\[y\] against him, and payment of his own expenses. Obviously the same considerations apply to the suit of the \[subagent\] against the \[principal\]." [_Id. at 203_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4W-TNC0-003B-K4DY-00000-00&context=1530671).

_c. Rights to indemnification created by or pursuant to statute._ When a right to indemnity is stated in a contract, a question that may arise is whether a prospective indemnitee is a party to the contract or otherwise entitled to the benefit of the provision. For examples, see [_Manley v. AmBase Corp., 337 F.3d 237 (2d Cir. 2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:495N-R4W0-0038-X2CH-00000-00&context=1530671) (former president of corporation, also a partner in law firm that became insolvent, claims indemnity rights for payments made pursuant to settlement agreement with law firm's bankruptcy trustee to resolve law-firm-related claims under indemnification agreement between corporation and former president; no right to indemnity when only partner's professional corporation, not covered by indemnity agreement, was partner in law firm); [_Gordon v. CRS Consulting Eng'rs, Inc., 820 P.2d 492 (Utah App.1991)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RXP-7510-003G-F463-00000-00&context=1530671) (construction contract with engineering firm requiring state to indemnify its agents covered only agencies and instrumentalities of state itself).

If a partnership becomes a limited-liability partnership, a partner's right to indemnity remains unaffected. See Rev. Unif. Partnership Act § 401, Comment 4.

For examples of statutory indemnity rights for agents outside the context of organizational statutes, see Cal. Labor Code § 2802(a) (employer must indemnify employee "for all necessary expenditures or losses incurred by the employee in direct consequence of the discharge of his or her duties"); prior language held in [_Douglas v. Los Angeles Herald-Examiner, 123 Cal.Rptr. 683, 692 (Cal. App.1975)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S11-V5S0-003C-R0S1-00000-00&context=1530671) (to require employer to indemnify employee against attorney's fees and costs of defense "when an employer refuses to defend an employee in an action which may or may not be unfounded for conduct which may or may not have been within the course and scope of his employment and it is ultimately established that the action was unfounded and the employee acted within the course and scope of his employment"); Minn. Stat. § 466.07 (2001 & Supp. 2005) (municipality required to "defend and indemnify any of its officers and employees" for damage resulting from performance of duties of office; held inapplicable to loss covered by employee's personal insurance in [_Grinnell Mut. Reinsurance Co. v. City of Cokato, 603 N.W.2d 665 (Minn.App. 1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3Y72-B2Y0-0039-44SP-00000-00&context=1530671); Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 17.555 (2002 & Supp. 2005); "\[a\] person against whom an action has been brought under this subchapter may seek contribution or indemnity from one who, under the statute law or at common law, may have liability for the damaging event of which the consumer complains"; held in [_Miller v. Keyser, 90 S.W.3d 712, 718 (Tex. 2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:475B-HDJ0-0039-446V-00000-00&context=1530671), to enable innocent employee who merely passes along information to seek indemnity from employer).

On differences among indemnification provisions in corporation statutes, see generally James D. Cox & Thomas L. Hazen, Cox & Hazen on Corporations § 15.21 (2d ed. 2003).

For differences among statutes in coverage of corporate personnel, compare Model Bus. Corp. Act, Subch. E, Introductory Comment 1 (all provisions applicable only to directors and officers) with Cal. Corp. Code § 317(a) (all components of provision applicable to "agent" of corporation, defined as "any person who is or was a director, officer, employee or other agent of the corporation") and Del. Code Ann. tit. 8, § 145(a) and (b) (2001 & Supp. 2004) (corporation has power to indemnify any person made or threatened to be made party to action or proceeding "by reason of the fact that the person is or was a director, officer, employee or agent of the corporation"), § 145(c) (corporation has duty to indemnify "a present or former director or officer" to the extent person "has been successful on the merits or otherwise in defense") and § 145(e) (corporation has power to advance defense expenses to "an officer or director" subject to compliance with stated requirements).

On whether a person acted as an "agent" for purposes of indemnification rights under a corporation statute, see [_Channel Lumber Co. v. Porter Simon, 93 Cal.Rptr.2d 482 (Cal. App.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3YS2-WHK0-0039-43P7-00000-00&context=1530671) (lawyer who prevailed in defense of malpractice action brought by corporate client was not acting as corporation's "agent" within meaning of indemnification statute); [_APSB Bancorp v. Thornton Grant, 31 Cal. Rptr.2d 736, 740-741 (Cal.App.1994)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-G2B0-003D-J0R8-00000-00&context=1530671) (accounting firm did not act as corporation's "agent" in conducting audit); [_Fasciana v. Electronic Data Sys. Corp., 829 A.2d 160 (Del.Ch.2003)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4864-KNN0-0039-4525-00000-00&context=1530671) (lawyer retained by corporation was its "agent" for purpose of determining entitlement to advancement of expenses under bylaw as to claims involving conduct in dealings with third parties on corporation's behalf but not its "agent" as to claims stemming from confidential advice given to corporation not intended for use by third parties).

On whether an indemnification claim stems from activity taken in an individual capacity and outside the scope of corporate activity, see, e.g., [_In re Miller, 290 F.3d 263, 267 (5th Cir.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45PV-2JR0-0038-X11C-00000-00&context=1530671) (debtor not sued by former employer by reason of fact that debtor was an officer of employer when claims in suit focused on debtor's postemployment competition with corporation and alleged theft of trade secrets; present employer has no duty to indemnify when debtor's status as corporate officer and former employer's "motivation to sue him because he jumped ship to work for a competitor" do not establish that debtor was sued in official, not personal, capacity); [_Harris v. Howard Univ., 28 F.Supp.2d 1 (D.D.C.1998)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3V6W-K930-0038-Y14S-00000-00&context=1530671) (former vice-president sued by FDIC in connection with service as director of bank entitled to indemnification for period of service overlapping with tenure as vice-president because service on bank board was at employer's request but not for postretirement service); [_Plate v. Sun-Diamond Growers, 275 Cal.Rptr. 667, 673 (Cal.App. 1990)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX6-HH70-003D-J1KX-00000-00&context=1530671) (indemnity claim fails when former officers took actions, leading to lawsuit against them, to establish their own business independent of corporation).

On outcomes in "fees on fees" cases, compare [_Stifel Fin. Corp. v. Cochran, 809 A.2d 555, 560-561 (Del. 2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:463H-MDM0-0039-412B-00000-00&context=1530671) (awarding fees on fees, noting remedial purpose of indemnification statute and that such an award "prevents a corporation from using its 'deep pockets' to wear down a former director, with a valid claim to indemnification, through expensive litigation"; plaintiffs claim to indemnification based on bylaw provision obligating corporation to indemnify to full extent authorized by law) with [_Baker v. Health Mgmt. Sys., Inc., 772 N.E.2d 1099 (N.Y.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:45NS-74T0-0039-41PT-00000-00&context=1530671) (court lacks power to award fees on fees under N.Y. Bus. Corp. Law § 722(a); corporation's duty to indemnify plaintiff based on plaintiff's success in underlying litigation).

_d. Rights to indemnification in connection with litigation._ For statements of the general rule, see, e.g., [_Johnston v. Suckow, 370 N.E.2d 650, 653-654 (Ill.App.1977)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S11-Y7C0-003C-B105-00000-00&context=1530671) ("an agent who himself is guilty of no illegal conduct may be indemnified by his principal for the expenses incurred in defending actions by third persons brought because of the agent's authorized conduct … an agent is not obliged to let judgment go against him; rather he may defend and recover from his principal the expenses of a defense bona fide made … \[a\]n agent should not be required to suffer a loss from the doing of an act undertaken by him at the direction, and for the benefit of his principal"; agent entitled to recover attorney's fees and expenses of investigation from principal).

For the point that unproven allegations in a third party's complaint do not determine whether an agent has a right to indemnification, see [_INA Ins. Co. v. Valley Forge Ins. Co., 722 P.2d 975, 981 (Ariz.App.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-Y2S0-003F-T2WF-00000-00&context=1530671) (noting that under contrary rule, "a candidate for possible indemnity loses the right even if the plaintiffs complaint against that defendant is groundless"); [_Peters v. Lyons, 168 N.W.2d 759, 770 (Iowa 1969)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-3JY0-003G-5150-00000-00&context=1530671) (indemnitee's rights should not rest on pleadings "by a third party, who through an overabundance of caution or optimism alleges more (or less) than he can prove"). In _INA,_ the plaintiff had issued a policy of errors and omissions insurance to an insurance agent. The agent was sued by a customer who purchased a policy of homeowners insurance, issued by another insurer; the customer sued both the agent and the homeowners insurer when it refused to cover loss on the basis that its cause was arson by the customer's spouse. The agent tendered the defense of the suit to the insurer pursuant to an indemnification clause in an agreement between them but, when the insurer refused to provide a defense, the agent's defense was provided by the plaintiff.

On the requirement that an agent give timely notice to the principal, see [_Sunset-Sternau Food Co. v. Bonzi, 389 P.2d 133 (Cal.1964)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRK-MS30-003C-H2B3-00000-00&context=1530671) (failure to request defense from principal does not bar indemnity claim if agent's defense of suit is reasonable); [_Johnson Realty v. Bender, 39 P.3d 1215, 1219-1220 (Colo.App.2001)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:443C-F0W0-0039-44RK-00000-00&context=1530671) (failure to give notice to principal does not invariably bar claim; claim will be diminished to the extent agent's defense was unreasonable or "to the extent the principal suffered a loss because it was deprived of the opportunity to settle or to mitigate defense costs").

For the point that an agent's good-faith decision to settle a case does not terminate the agent's indemnity claims against the principal, see Warren A. Seavey, Agency 267 (1964) ("\[i\]f the principal, having been notified, fails to act, the agent is entitled to use his own discretion in settling or defending the claim and can recover from the principal his expenses in connection with it … If he defends the action and loses, the matter is res judicata between him and the principal, if he had offered to let the principal defend or to share in the defense."); [_Long v. Vlasic Food Prods. Co., 439 F.2d 229, 231 (4th Cir.1971)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4X-DSP0-0039-X35Y-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent has burden to show reasonableness and good faith; agent settled action following trial); [_First Real Estate Corp. v. Winters, 551 So. 2d 417, 420 (Ala.Civ.App.1989)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX4-9MW0-003C-94Y2-00000-00&context=1530671) (agent not entitled to indemnity from principal when settlement not reasonable). For a recent application of the minority rule, see [_Willard v. Interpool, Ltd., 758 A.2d 684, 687 (Pa.Super.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4101-6PM0-0039-450W-00000-00&context=1530671), appeal den., _796 A.2d 985 (Pa.2001)_ (noting that authority from other jurisdictions supports majority rule, court holds that agent's settlement of suit bars indemnity claim against principal in absence of written indemnity agreement; "one who has ample opportunity to have his rights litigated and can use all processes of law to protect himself against an unwarranted claim, but chooses to compromise the claim, is not entitled to a right of recovery over by way of subrogation or indemnity, since payment thus made is not compulsory."). _Willard_ relies on [_Tugboat Indian Co. v. A/S Ivarans Rederi, 5 A.2d 153, 156 (Pa. 1939)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RRM-WBM0-003C-M4N7-00000-00&context=1530671), in which the court held that "\[t\]o recover indemnity when there has been … a voluntary payment … it must appear that the party paying the settlement was himself legally liable and could have been compelled to satisfy the claim."

On the general rule that judgment amounts on indemnity claims do not encompass the expenses of establishing the right to indemnification, see 41 Am. Jur. 2d Indemnity § 36; [_INA Ins. Co., 722 P.2d at 983_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3RX3-Y2S0-003F-T2WF-00000-00&context=1530671) (right to indemnity includes right to attorney's fees in defense of underlying claim but not fees incurred in establishing right to indemnity).

Some state statutes have the effect of making "fees on fees" recoverable in some circumstances. See, e.g., Ariz. Rev. Stat. § 12-341.01(A) (successful party in contested action arising out of contract entitled to recover attorney's fees); see generally Douglas A. Laycock, Modern American Remedies 913 (3d ed. 2002). See also [_Gurary v. Nu-Tech Bio-Med, Inc., 303 F.3d 212, 225-226 (2d Cir.2002)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:46M0-MPV0-0038-X2JT-00000-00&context=1530671), cert. denied, _538 U.S. 923 (2003)_ (award of fees and expenses incurred by defendant to recover award of sanctions against plaintiff who brought baseless securities-fraud action consistent with statutory purpose of Private Securities Litigation Reform Act).

[**_Restat 3d of Agency, § 8.15_**](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=analytical-materials&id=urn:contentItem:4P41-T900-00YG-4017-00000-00&context=1530671)

**_Restatement of the Law, Agency 3d - Official Text_ > _Chapter 8- Duties of Agent and Principal to Each Other_ > _Topic 2- Principal's Duties to Agent_**

**§ 8.15 Principal's Duty to Deal Fairly and in Good Faith**

**A principal has a duty to deal with the agent fairly and in good faith, including a duty to provide the agent with information about risks of physical harm or pecuniary loss that the principal knows, has reason to know, or should know are present in the agent's work but unknown to the agent.**

**COMMENTS & ILLUSTRATIONS**

**Comment:**

_a. Scope and cross-references._ This section sets forth duties owed by a principal to an agent that are distinct from duties created by contract, see § 8.13, and from a principal's duties to indemnify an agent, see § 8.14. Comment _b_ discusses a principal's general duty to deal with an agent fairly and in good faith. Comment _c_ discusses a principal's duty to furnish information to an agent. Comment _d_ discusses a principal's duty to refrain from conduct likely to harm an agent's business reputation.

_b. Duty to deal with agent fairly and in good faith._ A principal has a duty to deal fairly and in good faith with an agent. This duty does not supersede the principal's power to terminate the agent's authority as stated in § 3.10(1). This general duty encompasses more specific duties. The general duty obliges the principal to refrain from engaging in conduct that will foreseeably result in loss for the agent when the agent's own conduct is without fault.

**Illustration:**

1\. P Corporation, wishing to do business in Taiwan, engages A as its general manager in Taiwan. P Corporation designates A as its "responsible person" or legal representative in Taiwan, which requires such a designation to conduct business in the country. As P Corporation's designated "responsible person," A affixes A's "chop" or signature-equivalent, to tax returns that P Corporation prepares and files in Taiwan. Having decided to cease doing business in Taiwan, P Corporation terminates A's engagement but does not remove its designation of A as its "responsible person," although A requests several times that P Corporation do so and tells P Corporation that A is concerned that A may be subject to liability in the event of tax-related disputes between P Corporation and Taiwan. Taiwan assesses a tax liability against P Corporation that P Corporation contests and then, following an adverse final determination, does not pay. Taiwanese authorities notify A that A is forbidden to leave the country until its tax dispute with P Corporation is resolved. P Corporation has 14 breached its duty of good faith. P Corporation is subject to liability for loss suffered by A, including attorney's fees incurred by A to resolve A's predicament.

_c. Duty to provide information to agent._ A principal's general duty to deal with an agent fairly and in good faith requires that the principal furnish information to the agent, in particular, information about circumstances of which the agent is unaware that might subject the agent to physical or pecuniary loss in acting on the principal's behalf. The duty does not require the principal to provide an agent with all information known to the principal about a product that the agent sells on the principal's behalf; the principal's duty is limited to information material to the agent's professional reputation or risk of pecuniary loss to third parties with whom the agent deals on the principal's behalf.

**Illustrations:**

2\. A is an insurance agent who represents P Insurance Co. in selling policies of disability insurance. P Insurance Co. receives complaints from insureds concerning, inter alia, how the policies it issues define disability and state exclusions from coverage. P Insurance Co. does not have a duty to furnish A with such complaints.

3\. A is engaged as a sales representative by P Corporation, a manufacturer of air-handling equipment. As P Corporation knows, it is customary for purchasers of air-handling equipment to receive a warranty concerning a system's capacity before the system is purchased. P Corporation permits A to give such warranties on the basis of information it furnishes to A. On P Corporation's behalf, A negotiates the sale of a large air-handling system to T University and, at T University's request and relying on information furnished by P Corporation, warrants that the system has the capacity requisite for a building of 100,000 square feet. Unbeknownst to A, this statement is false because the system's maximum capacity is 50,000 square feet. The information furnished to A by P Corporation, on the basis of which A gave the warranty to T University, was known by P Corporation to be erroneous. P Corporation has breached its duty to A.

_d. Duty to refrain from conduct likely to injure agent's business reputation or reasonable self-respect._ This duty is the reciprocal of an agent's duty of good conduct as stated in § 8.10. Although a principal is not subject to a duty of loyalty to an agent, the principal's duty to deal with an agent fairly and in good faith requires that the principal refrain from conduct that is likely to injure the agent's business reputation through the agent's association with the principal. Likewise, a principal has a duty to refrain from conduct that will injure the agent's reasonable self-respect if the association continues.

The nature of the agent's work and other circumstances of the relationship between principal and agent are relevant to whether a principal's conduct breaches this duty.

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

**REPORTER'S NOTES**

_a. Relationship to Restatement Second, Agency._ This section corresponds to Restatement Second, Agency §§ 435 and 437. Restatement Second, Agency, does not state a general duty to deal fairly and in good faith comparable to this section.

_b. Duty to deal with agent fairly and in good faith._ For the proposition that a principal has a duty to deal with an agent fairly and in good faith, see [_Metropolitan Enter. Corp. v. United Techs. Int'l Corp., 2005 WL 2300382, at \*9-10 (D.Conn.2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4KXF-R260-TVT2-22M1-00000-00&context=1530671) (principal owes agent duty of good faith, which is not a fiduciary duty); [_Taylor v. Cordis Corp., 634 F.Supp. 1242 (S.D.Miss.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-9XJ0-0039-R30Y-00000-00&context=1530671). For recognition of the possibility that the relationship between a principal and an agent may have special characteristics that subject the principal to duties inherent in a fiduciary relationship, see [_Madey v. Duke Univ., 336 F.Supp.2d 583, 602 (M.D.N.C.2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4DDW-1960-0038-Y35V-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying North Carolina law; genuine issue of fact whether university employer owed fiduciary duties to faculty member in connection with control of grant-funded research project and free-electron laser); [_Cagin v. McFarland Clinic, P.C., 317 F.Supp.2d 964, 969-970 (S.D.Iowa 2004)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4CCW-04X0-0038-Y48S-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Iowa law; complaint did not allege facts describing relationship between physician and clinic as other than "a typical arms-length negotiation and a standard employment arrangement").

Illustration 1 is based on [_Shen v. Leo A. Daly Co., 222 F.3d 472 (8th Cir.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40W6-NYP0-0038-X04R-00000-00&context=1530671) (applying Nebraska law). _Shen_ also holds that the principal's conduct did not constitute false imprisonment of the agent because, although the former agent's liberty was restrained, he was nonetheless "free to move about Taiwan, and was not restrained in any way in his daily activities. . . . The country of Taiwan is clearly too great an area within which to be falsely imprisoned." [_Id. at 478_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40W6-NYP0-0038-X04R-00000-00&context=1530671). The court's rationale for the principal's liability in _Shen_ is that the principal's breach of its duty of good faith and fair dealing breached a fiduciary duty that the principal owed its agent, which, under Nebraska law, constituted a tort that entitled the agent to recover attorney's fees paid in Taiwan. See [_id. at 479_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40W6-NYP0-0038-X04R-00000-00&context=1530671). The court's basis for characterizing the principal's duty as "fiduciary" is simply that "\[a\] principal and an agent are in a fiduciary relationship. . . . Because of the fiduciary relationship, the principal owes the agent a duty of good faith and fair dealing in the incidents of their relationship." [_Id. at 477_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:40W6-NYP0-0038-X04R-00000-00&context=1530671).

_c. Duty to provide information to agent._ For general statement of the duty and its relationship to a principal's duty to deal fairly and in good faith with an agent, see [_Metropolitan Enter. Corp. v. United Techs. Int'l Corp, 2005 WL 2300382, at \*8-9 (D.Conn.2005)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:4KXF-R260-TVT2-22M1-00000-00&context=1530671) (duty to disclose known financial risks); [_Taylor v. Cordis Corp., 634 F.Supp. 1242, 1247 (D.Miss.1986)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-9XJ0-0039-R30Y-00000-00&context=1530671).

For application of the duty in an insurance context, see [_Marie Deonier & Assocs. v. Paul Revere Life Ins. Co., 9 P.3d 622 (Mont.2000)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:413M-RF00-0039-44MB-00000-00&context=1530671). In _Marie Deonier,_ a disability insurer did not inform an insurance broker who represented it that it had newly determined to assert the defense that, beyond a policy's two-year contestability period, an insured's sickness was not covered because it manifested itself prior to the date the policy was issued. Not knowing this, the broker specifically recommended the insurer's disability policy to a customer who disclosed prior episodes of insomnia and who, two years after issuance of the policy, submitted a claim for disability following a diagnosis of major depression. The court held that the insurer was subject to liability to the broker. Along the same lines, Restatement Second, Agency § 435, Comment _a,_ observes: "The duty may require not only warning the agent against physical dangers, as when the principal directs an agent to go to a place which is dangerous, but also disclosing facts which, if unknown, would be likely to subject the agent to pecuniary loss. Thus, a principal employing a factor to sell goods which appear to be but are not sound is subject to liability to the agent if the agent is led to incur personal liability through mistaken statements concerning the condition of the goods."

For the point made in Illustration 2, see [_Taylor, 634 F.Supp. at 1247_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-9XJ0-0039-R30Y-00000-00&context=1530671) (manufacturer of pacemakers does not have duty to salesman to inform him of "product problems" or provide copies of "consumer complaints"; "\[t\]he duty to inform its sales agents of product problems attached only when the company, in the exercise of reasonable diligence, knew that specific product defects posed a threat of harm to consumers and a concomitant threat to the professional reputation of the sales agent."). A principal's misrepresentation to an agent may not be actionable as a tort by the agent. See [_Dougherty v. Azko Nobel Salt Inc., 47 F.Supp.2d 989 (N.D.Ill. 1999)_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3WG8-S1H0-0038-Y401-00000-00&context=1530671) (supervisor's statements about plaintiff's future employment not actionable under Illinois law when plaintiff does not allege physical injury and employer not in the business of supplying information to employees to guide their business transactions).

_d. Duty to refrain from conduct likely to injure agent's business reputation or reasonable self-respect._ For a statement of the duty, see [_Taylor, 634 F.Supp. at 1246_](https://plus.lexis.com/api/document?collection=cases&id=urn:contentItem:3S4N-9XJ0-0039-R30Y-00000-00&context=1530671) ("\[t\]he principal's good faith duty to the agent also demands that the principal must maintain a standard of conduct which will not harm the agent's business reputation or reasonable self-respect."). See also Malik v. Bank of Credit & Commerce Int'l S.A. \[1998\] A.C. 20 (H.L.) (innocent managers of bank that was operated in dishonest and corrupt fashion stated claim for breach of principal's implied obligation not so to conduct bank; innocent managers may claim compensation for loss stemming from stigma due to association with bank, based on difficulty in finding employment subsequent to bank's notorious collapse). For discussion of the principle articulated in _Malik_ and related English and Commonwealth cases, see Kelly Godfrey, Contracts of Employment: Renaissance of the Implied Term of Trust and Confidence, (2003) 77 Aust. L.J. 764.
